The problem wasn't our technical competence—our partners were sharp. It was our structure. Auditors worked in silos. Advisory teams didn't talk to tax specialists. Clients saw us as transactional, not strategic.

I was managing senior auditor on a $340K engagement with Consolidated Logistics, a regional shipping company. Their CFO mentioned she was shopping for a new tax advisor because our tax team had never even called. That's when it clicked: we weren't failing because of talent. We were failing because of plumbing.

I proposed something our 85-person office had never tried. Instead of waiting for formal client requests, I created what I called "integrated pods"—small, cross-functional teams assigned to the same clients. An auditor, a tax specialist, and an advisory consultant worked together from kickoff. No permission layers. No "that's not in my scope." They sat together, literally, and met every two weeks to discuss client needs beyond their specific engagement.

The firm's managing partner thought it was too messy, too informal. But I pushed for a six-month pilot with four mid-market clients. I did the legwork to train the teams on communication protocols, ensured the partner didn't feel threatened, and built dashboards showing not just billable hours but cross-service revenue.

Six months in, those four clients had generated an additional $560K in advisory and tax work we wouldn't have otherwise captured. More importantly, our Net Promoter Score with them jumped from 42 to 76. The CFO at Consolidated Logistics? She became one of our strongest advocates.

The firm rolled it out firm-wide. We restructured the entire office around this model, and revenue grew 18% that year.

What I learned wasn't just about organizational design. It was that the best solutions aren't always the most complicated ones. It required naming the actual problem, listening to people closer to it than the executive team, and being willing to run something unproven. Said's EMBA structure—integrating finance, strategy, and operations—appeals to me precisely because I've seen firsthand how integration unlocks value. I want to deepen my ability to design systems that work in complex markets, especially in MENA where relationships matter as much as spreadsheets.

school_name: Poznan University of Economics MSc Management
prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
During a logistics coordination meeting at the Spanish Army's Joint Command in Madrid, I watched our director dismiss an idea from our youngest lieutenant—not because it was flawed, but because he'd never "done things this way before." The lieutenant had suggested a supply chain optimization that could have saved 40,000 euros monthly. I realized then that organizations don't suffer from lack of ideas. They suffer from cultures where people stop sharing them.

That moment fundamentally shifted how I approach collaboration. In a military environment where hierarchy is both necessary and ingrained, I've spent the last four years figuring out how to be someone who creates space for good ideas, regardless of rank or tenure. I plan to bring this commitment to Poznan.

In my current role managing operations for 120+ personnel, I've learned that contribution isn't always about speaking loudest. When my team proposed rotating project assignments to build cross-functional skills, I didn't have all the answers about implementation. Instead of defaulting to the standard two-year rotations we'd always used, I facilitated a working group. A junior coordinator who'd suggested the idea ended up designing the framework we eventually adopted. It wasn't that my analytical input wasn't valuable—it was that the solution was *better* because it incorporated perspectives I wouldn't have generated alone.

I know this matters at Poznan because I've watched MBA programs work. My supervisor completed his degree at a business school in Germany, and I watched how he'd return from classes with new frameworks that immediately changed how we approached problems. But more than that, I watched how his cohort became his professional network. Two years later, he'd hire classmates. They'd meet for strategy projects. Those relationships outlasted the program itself. I want that—but I also want to be intentional about what kind of peer I am within it.

This means several things practically. First, I commit to preparing thoroughly for seminars. Coming from a military background where briefing materials are exhaustive and decisions carry material consequences, I approach case studies the same way. I'll do the work so that when I'm in the room, I can contribute substantively rather than filling air. Second, I'll push back thoughtfully. In military culture, there's often pressure toward consensus that can mask real disagreement. At Poznan, where the cohort will be international and diverse, I want to voice alternative perspectives respectfully but clearly. That lieutenant's idea deserved pushback if it was weak—but it deserved real engagement first, not dismissal.

Third, I'm genuinely curious about how management operates outside the public sector. I've spent four years optimizing within constraints—budget caps, regulatory requirements, political cycles I can't control. I want to understand how private companies think differently. And honestly, I have things to offer in return. Military logistics isn't sexy, but we've solved problems around resource scarcity and cross-organizational coordination that companies face too. I'd like to share that perspective.

Finally, I want to contribute to student life beyond the classroom. I'm not someone who needs to be the president of everything, but I do believe in showing up. In Madrid, I helped organize our Command's professional development program for women in leadership—nothing revolutionary, but consistent mentoring and visibility matter. At Poznan, I'd likely gravitate toward that kind of work. Perhaps a study group focused on operations management. Or connecting Spanish students with international classmates. Those smaller contributions to community are where I actually add value.

I'm choosing Poznan specifically because I want to study in Europe while being genuinely far from home—somewhere the diversity of thought will challenge my assumptions. A Spanish person studying management in Poland with classmates from Turkey, Nigeria, Brazil, and China isn't accidental for me. That's exactly the environment where I want to learn.

I won't be the loudest voice in your program. But I'll be the one who listens carefully, prepares thoroughly, and genuinely wants to make the people around me think differently.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
When I joined Crescent Capital Partners in Riyadh in 2014, the firm's investment committee followed a rigid sector-based allocation model. We would assign a fixed percentage to retail, another to manufacturing, another to real estate—the same playbook that every other PE firm in the region was using. The problem was that this approach missed opportunities. In 2016, we passed on a stake in a mid-market logistics company because it didn't fit neatly into our "core sectors."

Six months later, a competitor acquired that same company for $12 million and exited it three years later for $47 million.

That miss haunted me. I started studying how the best international PE firms—Apollo, Blackstone, KKR—made investment decisions. They weren't wedded to predefined categories. They looked at unit economics, management quality, and market dynamics first, then decided if it made sense.

I proposed something radical for our firm: a "thesis-driven" investment framework instead of sector buckets. Rather than asking "Is this in our retail bucket?" we'd ask "Does this business have strong unit economics? Can we improve operations? Is the founder coachable?" I created a scoring matrix and ran it against our historical deals to show that this approach would have flagged better opportunities earlier.

The investment committee was skeptical. In conservative Saudi business culture, deviation from established process isn't celebrated. But I had data. I had historical examples. After three months of pushback, they agreed to a pilot program.

We used the new framework for eighteen months. We invested in three companies that wouldn't have qualified under the old model—a B2B software platform, a specialty manufacturing business, and an e-commerce logistics operator. Two of them we're still holding; one we exited for a 2.8x multiple.

What I learned wasn't just that challenging convention can work—it's that doing so requires patience and evidence, not just conviction. I had to listen to legitimate concerns about operational risk and portfolio concentration. I had to adjust the framework based on feedback. The process was messier than I expected.

This experience shaped how I now approach problems. I question inherited assumptions, but I don't do it carelessly. Bilkent's emphasis on both analytical rigor and entrepreneurial thinking appeals to me precisely because I believe that combination matters. I want to bring that mindset to your program and develop it further.

school_name: University of Auckland Business School Executive MBA
prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
# Ethical Dilemma Essay

Three years ago, I was leading a team of eight engineers at a FAANG company's Singapore office, and we faced a situation that forced me to choose between what was easy and what was right.

We were building a recommendation algorithm for a consumer app that would eventually reach millions of Southeast Asian users. About six months into the project, one of my engineers flagged something in our data pipeline: the training dataset had a significant bias toward urban, higher-income users. Our algorithm was essentially optimizing for a demographic that represented maybe 15% of our actual user base.

The practical problem was clear—we'd need to rebuild our dataset, re-train the model, and push back our launch by at least two months. But here's where it got complicated. Our VP had already committed to a hard launch date to investors. Senior leadership had been briefing analysts on our rollout timeline. Missing that date would trigger difficult conversations with finance and potentially affect our quarterly numbers.

I remember sitting in my apartment that night, honestly wrestling with this. The business case for delaying was weak on paper. Our algorithm would still perform well for the majority of users. The bias would disadvantage a minority, but it wouldn't break anything—it would just perpetuate existing inequalities by steering recommendation traffic toward already-privileged segments. Easy to rationalize. Easy to ship.

But I kept thinking about what that meant for the users we were systematically deprioritizing. A student in the Philippines trying to find educational content. A small business owner in rural Vietnam trying to discover suppliers. These aren't abstract ethical concerns—they're real people who would get an objectively worse product because we cut corners.

I decided to escalate this to my director, but not before doing the homework. I spent a week quantifying the actual impact: what percentage of users were affected, what the performance gap looked like, what the cost of remediation actually was. I also modeled a phased launch—we could go live on schedule in tier-one cities with cleaned data, then expand regionally as we expanded our dataset. It wasn't perfect, but it was honest.

My director's first reaction was frustration with the timeline. But I came prepared with specifics, not just hand-wringing. I showed her that phased approach would actually let us gather better real-world data faster than a global launch anyway. The engineering case became practical, not just moral.

It took three weeks of conversations to get buy-in, but we eventually did. We soft-launched in Singapore and Hong Kong first with better data, then expanded to Southeast Asia month by month as we improved our datasets for each region. We still hit a meaningful launch date, just staggered it. Our quarterly numbers didn't take the hit leadership feared.

What I learned wasn't that doing the right thing always works out neatly—sometimes it does, sometimes it doesn't. What I learned was that ethical problems usually aren't binary. There's almost always a way to be principled without being reckless, if you're willing to put in the work to find it. And credibility matters. Because I came with data, not just moral outrage, people listened.

This experience has shaped how I've approached my role since. Before escalating anything, I ask myself: Have I done the work to understand the full picture? Can I propose a solution, not just a problem? Am I being fair to the business constraints people are operating under?

As I think about an Executive MBA, I'm drawn to the University of Auckland program because it sits at the intersection of serious technical rigor and grounded business thinking. I want to deepen my understanding of how ethical frameworks actually apply in organizations—not as theoretical exercises, but as practical leadership challenges. That's where I want to grow.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I started my career in 2019 at Sambhav Foundation in Delhi, a mid-sized NGO focused on educational access in underserved communities. I was hired as a Program Coordinator for what was supposed to be a support role—administrative tasks, liaison work between our field teams and central office. My first project involved coordinating our scholarship distribution across three districts, managing spreadsheets and ensuring documentation. It was necessary work, but honestly, I found myself frustrated. I'd see the same administrative bottlenecks causing delays in student disbursements, and nobody seemed to think it was worth fixing.

By my second year, I pushed to redesign our entire scholarship process. I taught myself basic SQL and built a simple database to replace our paper-based system. The director was skeptical—we weren't a tech organization—but gave me three months. Within six weeks, we'd reduced processing time from 45 days to 12 days and caught a ₹2.8 lakh discrepancy in an existing ledger. After that, I was promoted to Operations Manager overseeing all program delivery across four states.

That role taught me the difference between solving isolated problems and understanding systems. I expanded our team from 8 to 22 people, implemented real-time reporting dashboards, and worked directly with our Finance Director to establish cost-per-outcome metrics. We increased student reach by 34% while reducing overhead costs by 18% in two years. But I also made mistakes. I hired too quickly in one state and had to let three people go after six months. I pushed for a data integration project that took twice as long as planned and frustrated our program teams.

What's changed is how I think about these failures. They're not just setbacks—they forced me to understand that operations exists to serve mission, not the other way around. I became fixated on efficiency at the expense of stakeholder relationships.

Now, at 27, I'm running strategy projects for Sambhav's expansion into three new states. But I feel the ceiling of what I can contribute without deeper expertise in organizational strategy, financial planning, and leadership theory. ESPAE's Executive MBA appeals to me because you balance rigorous business training with Latin American development contexts. That specificity matters—I don't want generic business school tools.

I want to return to India with frameworks I can actually apply.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
# A Time I Demonstrated Leadership

Three years ago, I made a decision that cost my company $400,000 in sunk costs and got me called into the CEO's office—not for praise.

I was Operations Director at Nexo, a mid-sized Peruvian e-commerce platform competing against Amazon and Mercado Libre. We'd invested heavily in a proprietary logistics network, building our own warehouse management system from scratch. The software was buggy, expensive to maintain, and slower than third-party solutions. My team of eight logistics engineers had spent eighteen months on it. My boss, the CTO, considered it his legacy project.

Six months in, our fulfillment times lagged competitors by two days. Customer complaints were climbing. My team was exhausted—they were debugging at midnight while rivals were shipping orders in 24 hours. I knew the system was fundamentally broken, but saying so meant admitting failure publicly and shelving thousands of hours of work.

I was terrified of being the person who killed the project.

Instead of ignoring the problem, I did something harder: I built the case methodically. I spent three weeks analyzing our system against Shopify's third-party logistics integrations. I tracked cost-per-shipment, error rates, and delivery times. The numbers were brutal. Our system cost $2.8 per shipment; competitors charged $0.90. Our error rate was 3.2%. Industry standard was 0.4%.

Then I did something I'm not proud of: I tried to soften the blow first. I met individually with the CTO, with department heads, with the finance team. I acknowledged what we'd built, what we'd learned, and why it mattered that we'd tried. I wasn't trying to be political—I genuinely respected the effort. But I was also afraid of being blamed.

What actually changed things was sitting with my team. I told them: "I think we need to recommend stopping this project. I know you've invested everything into it. I want to hear if I'm wrong." Two of my engineers cried. One had turned down another job to stay and see this through. But they also said: "We know it's not working. We're just relieved someone finally said it."

Their honesty shamed me out of my cowardice.

I presented the recommendation to leadership without hedging. I didn't bury the bad news or layer it with jargon. I said: our system is slower and more expensive than solutions that already exist. We're burning cash and team morale to maintain a sunk cost. Our customers are suffering. We should migrate to Shopify's logistics infrastructure and redeploy our engineers toward higher-impact work.

The CEO was quiet for a long time. Then he said something I didn't expect: "Why didn't anyone tell us this six months ago?"

Because I was too scared. Because I wanted to protect people. Because I thought leadership meant having all the answers.

What happened next surprised me. The CTO was hurt, but he also seemed relieved. We spent the next two months planning a careful migration. My team helped audit the transition, and three of them led our integration with the new system. Two of them got promoted within a year because they'd proven they could deliver results under pressure.

The $400,000 loss hurt. But staying on that path would have cost us millions more and probably would have tanked the company during the pandemic—which hit three months later.

I learned that leadership isn't about protecting people from hard truths. It's about having the integrity to name them, even when it costs you. And it's about trusting your team enough to be vulnerable with them.

That's the kind of leader I want to become at Kellogg—someone who helps others see clearly, even when the view is uncomfortable.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
I have always been passionate about making a difference in the manufacturing sector. With five years of experience in operations and supply chain management, I have developed a strong foundation in understanding how businesses work. Now, I am ready to take my career to the next level, and I believe that an MBA from ISCTE Business School will be the perfect catalyst to help me achieve my goals.

Throughout my career, I have worked in various roles that have exposed me to different aspects of manufacturing. I started as a junior operations coordinator and have since grown in responsibility. I have learned a lot about how to manage teams, optimize processes, and think strategically about business challenges. However, I recognize that I need more formal business education to advance further in my career.

My main career aspiration is to become a general manager or operations director at a major manufacturing firm. I want to lead large teams and make decisions that impact the company's bottom line. I believe that with an MBA, I will have the knowledge and credentials necessary to compete for these senior positions. The manufacturing industry is evolving rapidly with new technologies like automation and Industry 4.0, and I want to be prepared to lead through these changes.

ISCTE Business School's MBA program appeals to me because it is well-regarded and will enhance my professional credentials. The curriculum seems comprehensive and will give me the business acumen I need. I am particularly interested in operations management and strategy courses, which align with my career goals. Additionally, studying at ISCTE in Lisbon will give me international exposure and help me build a global network, which I think is important for my career development.

I am also interested in exploring different markets, potentially moving into consulting or starting my own business venture in the future. An MBA will open doors and provide me with the analytical skills and business knowledge to pursue these opportunities. The program will help me understand finance, marketing, and organizational behavior at a deeper level, which will make me a more well-rounded business leader.

I am confident that I will be a good fit for ISCTE's MBA program. I am a hard worker and have consistently performed well in my roles. I am eager to learn and am committed to making the most of the program. My experience in manufacturing, combined with the rigorous training at ISCTE, will position me to achieve my career goals and make a meaningful impact in the business world.

In conclusion, an MBA from ISCTE Business School represents an important step in my career journey. It will provide me with the tools, knowledge, and network I need to advance to senior management positions and potentially explore other opportunities in business. I am excited about the possibility of joining ISCTE and contributing to the program's diverse community.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
In five years, I want to lead the commercial operations for a major renewable energy developer across Asia. Not as someone who manages projects from a corporate office in Stockholm, but as someone embedded in the region, making real decisions about where capital flows and how we structure deals that actually move markets toward decarbonization.

Right now, I'm a project manager at Nordwind Energy, managing the development pipeline for utility-scale wind projects across Europe. I know the technology cold. But I've hit a ceiling—my technical expertise doesn't translate into the business acumen needed to move up. Last year, we lost a €40 million contract in Portugal because our bid structure didn't account for the local financing landscape. I sat in that debrief knowing I could have contributed more if I understood corporate finance, international business models, and how to navigate stakeholder dynamics across different markets. That's when I realized the MBA wasn't optional for where I want to go.

Asia is where the growth is. China installed more renewable capacity last year than Europe's entire fleet. India is mobilizing billions. But European companies are struggling to scale there because we don't understand the regulatory frameworks, the partnership models, or how to price deals in markets with different cost structures and risk profiles. I want to be the bridge—someone who can take Nordwind's operational excellence and adapt it to markets where that kind of discipline is a genuine competitive advantage.

HKUST fits this vision in specific ways. The program's emphasis on Asia-Pacific business strategy is exactly what I need. I'm not looking for a general MBA; I need one rooted in this region. The case studies, the faculty expertise, the student network built into Hong Kong's finance and energy sector—that's my real classroom. When I look at your curriculum, the Asia business strategy courses and the emerging markets finance electives aren't nice-to-haves. They're essential.

The executive format also matters. I can't step away from my role for two years. Nordwind has committed me to leading our Southeast Asia feasibility study starting in six months, and I need to be on the ground for that while I'm studying. The flexibility to do both—managing real projects while developing the strategic thinking to scale—is what makes HKUST's EMBA model perfect for my timeline.

I'm also honest about what I don't know yet. I've never worked in M&A, never managed a P&L, never negotiated a major contract. The executive MBA will give me those frameworks, but more importantly, it'll give me a cohort of practitioners who are solving similar problems. That peer learning—sitting next to someone who's actually navigating business in Vietnam or building utility relationships in India—is something I can't replicate reading case studies at home.

In five years, I see myself running a business unit. HKUST is how I get there.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
Three years ago, I sat across from our fund's largest institutional investor—a pension fund representing 50,000 teachers in Lima—and told them something they didn't want to hear. Their $45 million allocation into our emerging markets equity fund was underperforming, and I was recommending we reallocate 60% of it into fixed income, which would generate lower fees for our firm.

That conversation crystallized something I'd been grappling with for months. At Artemis Capital, I oversee client relationships and portfolio strategy for our Latin American funds. The conflict was real: our investment committee wanted to maintain aggressive equity exposure (good for AUM growth and my bonus structure), our risk team was flagging concentration risk in our commodity holdings, and our clients—mostly pension funds and family offices—were getting nervous about volatility before key disbursement periods.

I had to choose whose interests I'd actually serve.

The easy path was the one I was trained to take. Asset management runs on fee momentum. More assets, higher fees. Bigger funds mean better economics. Our CFO had made it clear that Q4 targets depended on fund inflows, not outflows. My compensation is tied to AUM growth. The investment committee believed in our strategy. Moving $27 million out of equities would signal weakness to the market.

But I couldn't unsee what the data showed. We had 68% of the fund in commodity-linked equities—concentrated, cyclical, dangerous. The teachers' pension fund was supposed to fund retirements, not spike returns. They had a 20-year horizon, but they were asking for liquidity within 5. Our equity-heavy portfolio was structurally misaligned with their needs.

I spent two weeks modeling scenarios and building a transition plan that felt honest. I met with the investment committee first, not to ask permission but to present the rationale in terms they'd respect—downside protection actually attracts institutional capital long-term, reputation matters more than one quarter of fees. I met with risk. I ran the numbers with our operations team on execution costs.

Then I had that difficult conversation with the pension fund. I walked them through why their capital should move, knowing it would hurt our fund's headline AUM. I also recommended they keep $18 million in our Asian equity fund, which actually had a better risk-adjusted profile for their needs. The conversation lasted three hours. There was skepticism, some frustration that we were "suggesting" capital reduction.

What I didn't expect was their response after they verified the analysis independently. They reallocated exactly as recommended—$27 million to fixed income, $18 million to Asia equities. But more importantly, they increased their overall allocation with us by $35 million the following year. The CFO was confused. "How did we grow AUM after shrinking the fund?"

Our CEO understood immediately. The pension fund told their peers that we'd prioritized their interests over ours. In a industry built on trust and opacity, that conversation became our differentiator.

That experience taught me something uncomfortable about myself: I'm someone who will choose the right answer over the comfortable one, but I'm not naturally heroic about it. I had to work through the discomfort, the fear that I'd look weak, the worry about my compensation. I still felt those pressures. I just decided they were less important than being someone I respect.

That's why I'm applying to EPGP now. I'm five years into asset management and I've realized that the question isn't whether I can balance competing interests—it's whether I have the strategic frameworks and global perspective to do it consistently, across industries and markets I don't yet know. I want to understand the fundamentals of business strategy beyond portfolio management. Colombia needs leaders who can navigate these tensions in local markets, and I want to be equipped to contribute there eventually.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
# Creative Solution Essay

Three years ago, I was managing a 12-person education team at a nonprofit in New Mexico called Bright Futures Education Alliance. We were hemorrhaging money on a contract with a learning management platform—$15,000 annually—that only 40% of our partner schools actually used. Worse, the platform wasn't built for rural schools with spotty internet, which was most of our network.

I could've just negotiated a lower price. That felt like admitting defeat. Instead, I spent a Friday afternoon doing something I'd never done before: I actually asked the teachers what they needed.

What emerged was messy and specific. Teachers wanted offline access to lesson plans. They wanted to track which students had actually completed assignments without clicking through ten screens. They wanted something that worked on their aging school laptops. They definitely didn't want another password to remember.

So we built one. Not built, exactly—I cobbled together two open-source platforms that weren't designed to work together, learned just enough Python to write a custom integration script, and convinced our IT person to believe in the project even when it seemed ridiculous. We created something hybrid: a lightweight lesson repository that teachers could download monthly, plus a simple paper-based tracking system that students could fill out and we'd photograph for our records.

It wasn't elegant. It definitely wasn't scalable in the way venture capitalists would understand. But it worked. Within six months, 89% of partner schools were using our system. We redirected that $15,000 toward actual teacher training. And when COVID hit two years later, those schools weren't scrambling—they already had offline-capable infrastructure.

What surprised me about this wasn't the technical part. It was realizing how much of the problem existed because I hadn't actually asked the people using the system what they needed. I'd relied on industry assumptions: bigger platform, more features, must be better. The creative solution wasn't fancy. It was listening first.

I'm applying to XLRI's Executive MBA because I'm at a point where I see systemic problems everywhere—in nonprofit operations, in how we measure impact, in how we scale programs without losing what makes them work. I have the instinct to solve them, but I'm missing the framework. I don't have formal training in finance or organizational strategy. I've been managing through pattern recognition and reading every business book I could find.

The PGDM-GMP program appeals to me specifically because it's designed for leaders already in the field who need to sharpen their analytical toolkit without leaving their work. I want to understand how to model solutions financially, how to think about scaling in a structured way, and how to bring more people into the problem-solving process so I'm not the bottleneck.

The nonprofit world has taught me that the best solutions are often ones nobody's tried because they require asking uncomfortable questions. I want to bring that instinct into an MBA program, and I think XLRI would actually expect that.

prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
Facing Setback and Growing as a Professional

Throughout my twelve years in consulting at a Big 4 firm, I have experienced many challenges that have shaped my career and personal development. One particular setback stands out as a defining moment that taught me valuable lessons about resilience and leadership.

About five years ago, I was leading a digital transformation project for a major client in the financial services sector. The project was significant—it involved implementing new systems across multiple departments and required coordination among teams of 20+ people. I was responsible for the overall delivery and outcomes. Unfortunately, the project fell behind schedule due to poor communication between my team and the client's IT department. We missed a critical deadline by three weeks, which disappointed the client and reflected poorly on our consulting firm.

This was a difficult moment for me. I felt personally responsible for the failure, as the project lead. The client was unhappy, and I worried about how this would affect my reputation and career progression at the firm. At first, I was discouraged and questioned whether I was suited for senior leadership roles.

However, I decided to take a step back and analyze what went wrong. I conducted a thorough review of the project timeline, team communications, and client interactions. I realized that the main issue was inadequate communication protocols between our team and their technical team. We had assumed that regular meetings were sufficient, but we had not established clear escalation procedures or defined roles and responsibilities with enough clarity.

After this realization, I took several actions to recover from this setback. First, I created a detailed post-mortem report and presented it to both my firm's leadership and the client. I did not make excuses but instead took full responsibility and outlined specific steps we would take to prevent similar issues in the future. This showed accountability and professionalism.

Second, I implemented new project management systems and communication frameworks that improved how we tracked deliverables and communicated with clients. These frameworks became best practices that my firm later adopted across other projects.

Third, I rebuilt the relationship with the client by dedicating extra time and resources to completing the remaining phases of the project flawlessly. Over the next six months, we successfully delivered all remaining components without any further delays. The client eventually became satisfied with our work and even engaged us for additional projects.

What I learned from this experience is that setbacks are not failures—they are learning opportunities. I learned that effective leadership requires clear communication, defined processes, and the ability to take responsibility when things go wrong. I also learned the importance of not getting discouraged when facing challenges, but instead using them as motivation to improve.

This experience has made me a better manager and consultant. I now prioritize communication and stakeholder management in every project I work on. I have also become more empathetic to the challenges my team members face, as I understand the pressure of delivering results under difficult circumstances.

As I consider pursuing an MBA at Waseda Business School, I believe this experience demonstrates my capacity for growth and learning. I want to deepen my strategic thinking and leadership skills so I can handle even more complex challenges in my future career. The setback I experienced five years ago was difficult at the time, but it ultimately made me stronger and more resilient.

school_name: NHH Norwegian School of Economics (Executive MBA)
prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
Two years ago, I discovered that one of our school administrators at Lighthouse Academy in Lagos was falsifying attendance records. Students were being marked present when they hadn't attended class for weeks. The principal had been using inflated numbers to secure government funding based on enrollment metrics.

I faced a genuine conflict. The principal had hired me, mentored me through my transition into educational leadership, and was respected in our community. Reporting him meant risking my job, damaging his reputation, and potentially destabilizing the school. But the falsified records directly harmed students—especially the poorest families who relied on government oversight to ensure quality education.

I spent a week wrestling with this. I considered reporting anonymously, but that felt cowardly. I considered staying quiet, but I kept thinking about the students whose educational needs were being obscured by these numbers. I even considered whether there was some charitable interpretation of what he was doing—maybe he genuinely believed the inflated numbers reflected the school's true capacity?

Eventually, I scheduled a private meeting with him. I came with documentation and specific instances, but I also came with respect. I told him I knew what was happening, and I explained clearly why I couldn't be part of it. I didn't accuse or shame him. I said: "You've built something good here. But this undermines it. You need to fix this, and I need you to know that I will escalate this if the records aren't corrected within two weeks."

He was defensive at first, then quiet. He asked for a week. The records were corrected. He later told me he had convinced himself that the government wasn't actually using those numbers seriously—that everyone inflated figures. Confronting him directly forced him to see the real impact.

I kept my job. He faced no formal consequences, though our relationship changed. The school's actual enrollment numbers were lower than we'd claimed, which meant reduced funding, but it meant our remaining resources actually matched our student population.

This experience shaped how I lead: ethical decisions rarely have clean outcomes, but they require you to be direct about your values while remaining human toward others. As I pursue the Executive MBA at NHH, I'm drawn to deeper study in corporate governance and organizational ethics—contexts where these tensions play out with much higher stakes.

prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
Throughout my professional career in medical devices, I have been driven by a passion to make a positive impact in the healthcare industry. My work experience has taught me the importance of teamwork, leadership, and innovation. I believe that an MBA from Cranfield will help me achieve my career goals and become a more effective leader.

In my current role, I have gained valuable experience in various aspects of business operations. I work with cross-functional teams to develop strategies and implement solutions that benefit the company. I have learned how to communicate effectively with colleagues from different backgrounds and cultures, which has been an important skill in my professional growth.

My journey has not always been straightforward. I come from a unique background as someone from the UAE, and I have had to navigate different cultural expectations and professional environments. Being non-binary has also presented certain challenges in traditional corporate settings, but these experiences have made me more resilient and adaptable. I believe diversity is important in business, and I want to contribute to creating more inclusive workplaces.

I am particularly interested in studying at Cranfield because it is a respected institution with strong connections to the business world. I am excited about the opportunity to learn from experienced faculty and network with peers from around the globe. The Executive MBA program aligns with my goal to transition into a leadership position within the next five years.

Outside of work, I enjoy reading about business and technology trends. I follow industry publications and try to stay updated on the latest developments in my field. I also believe in maintaining work-life balance, which is why I dedicate time to personal interests and spending time with family and friends.

My technical background in the medical devices sector has given me insights into how products are developed and brought to market. Understanding the regulatory environment and quality standards has been crucial in my role. I am confident that the analytical and strategic skills I will gain from the MBA program will complement my industry knowledge and prepare me for senior leadership roles.

In conclusion, I am a dedicated professional with a strong work ethic and commitment to continuous learning. I am excited about the prospect of joining the Cranfield MBA cohort and contributing my unique perspective and experiences to the community. I am confident that this program will provide me with the tools and knowledge necessary to achieve my career aspirations and make a meaningful impact in the healthcare industry.

prompt: Describe your mid-term and long-term visions for your post-MBA career path and how will the Desautels MBA program add value to your future career?
content:
When I tell people I want to leave law, they usually ask "why?" as if practicing law is the obvious default career path. After ten years as an in-house counsel at a mid-sized Nigerian oil services firm, I've realized the question I should be asking is different: why am I not building something myself?

My legal background has given me a particular skill set. I'm good at spotting risk, drafting contracts, managing regulatory relationships. But I've watched colleagues in business development and operations move faster, make bigger decisions, and honestly—earn more money while taking on interesting problems. Three years ago, our company launched a new division targeting renewable energy. I handled the legal structure, but I watched the operations director shape the entire strategy. She was making $40,000 more than me and reporting directly to the CEO. That's when I started paying attention.

What I want in five to seven years is to move into business development or corporate strategy roles in the energy sector—likely managing a portfolio of projects or leading a function at a company doing the transition to cleaner energy. I'm not interested in leaving Nigeria or the continent, which narrows my options but makes them clearer. The market here is moving toward renewables faster than people realize, and the companies that understand both the regulatory environment and how to actually execute projects will win.

To get there, I need three things the MBA will give me. First, I need financial literacy I don't have. I can read a balance sheet, but I can't build one. I can't model cash flows or understand capital structures deeply enough. In my current role, I'm constantly asking the finance team questions that I should already know how to answer. That gap holds me back from credibility in business development conversations.

Second, I need a network outside of law. My entire professional circle is either lawyers or people I've met through legal work. Desautels would connect me with people in operations, finance, entrepreneurship—people who think about problems differently than I do. Montreal's proximity to the US and Europe also matters for my long-term plans; I want exposure to how energy companies operate at larger scales.

Third, I need to understand strategy frameworks that I've never formally studied. I read business cases, but that's different from working through them with experienced professors and classmates who challenge your assumptions. The energy sector is moving in directions that require strategic thinking about technology adoption, political risk, and market timing. I'm currently thinking about these things reactively, through the lens of contracts and compliance.

I'm aware that an MBA won't automatically solve these gaps—I'll have to do the work. But I'm also realistic about what I don't know. I'm 32, I've been successful in law, but I've hit a ceiling where domain expertise alone isn't enough. Desautels feels like the right timing and place, particularly because the curriculum has flexibility to go deeper in sustainability and operations, which is where I want to build competency.

By 2032, I'd like to be leading a business function at a major African energy company, making strategic decisions about portfolio allocation and market entry. Law was the foundation. The MBA is how I actually get to build.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
When my team at Bain was tasked with improving operational efficiency for a retail client, we followed the standard playbook: analyze the current state, benchmark against competitors, identify cost-cutting opportunities. We were three months into the engagement when the numbers just felt incomplete.

The client had a 15% variance in store profitability across their 200-location portfolio, and everyone assumed it was a labor productivity issue. Our preliminary analysis pointed toward scheduling inefficiencies and staffing costs. But during a store visit in suburban Melbourne, I noticed something that didn't fit the model: the highest-performing location had the same labor structure as their worst performer, yet generated 40% higher revenue.

I flagged this with my project lead, who essentially told me to focus on the roadmap we'd already committed to. The data was clear, the framework was sound. But the anomaly bothered me enough that I spent a weekend diving deeper. I pulled transaction-level data instead of just the P&L summaries we'd been using, and cross-referenced it with store characteristics—foot traffic, demographic density, local competition.

What emerged was that the high performers weren't special because of what they did operationally. They were special because the store managers had autonomy to adjust their product mix and pricing based on local demand. The struggling stores were locked into a centralized assortment that worked in affluent areas but flopped in price-sensitive neighborhoods.

I went back to my lead and essentially said: we're solving the wrong problem. We presented this finding to the client separately—not as part of the standard efficiency analysis, but as a strategic insight. Instead of cutting labor by 8%, they could unlock revenue growth of 12-15% by decentralizing merchandising decisions while maintaining centralized supply chain controls.

The client didn't love it initially. Decentralization meant letting go of control, which felt risky to their head office. But we modeled the downside scenarios, and they eventually bought in. Twelve months later, the portfolio improved by $11M in EBIT, with most of that coming from revenue uplift rather than cost cuts.

What I learned wasn't earth-shattering, but it shifted how I approach consulting. The standard frameworks are standard because they work 80% of the time. But that 80% can blind you to the 20% that matters. Since then, I've made it a habit to spend time looking for what the model isn't capturing—the store visits, the conversations with frontline staff, the outliers in the data.

I've brought this mindset to my subsequent cases: questioning whether we should be benchmarking efficiency when the real opportunity is revenue expansion, or pushing back when we're using lagged industry data that might not reflect current market dynamics. I'm not always right—I've definitely wasted time chasing theories that led nowhere. But I've noticed that when I resist the temptation to plug everything into the established frameworks, that's when my team and I find something genuinely useful.

I know this probably sounds obvious in retrospect. But in consulting, the pressure to move quickly and follow the roadmap is real. Challenging the approach takes more time upfront and carries real risk if you're wrong. I did it partly because I was frustrated with an incomplete answer, and partly because I had a lead who, despite initial skepticism, was willing to test a different hypothesis.

As I look toward an MBA, I'm interested in environments where this kind of thinking is cultivated rather than just tolerated. I want to deepen my understanding of when to trust the conventional wisdom and when to question it—and ideally, study that in a place where I'm surrounded by people who are also wrestling with that question.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I started my career in 2018 as a junior operations manager at Al Baraka International, my family's import-export business based in Dubai. My father built the company from nothing—literally from a small warehouse in Al Quoz—and by the time I joined, we were doing about $45 million in annual revenue with a team of 120 across three locations in the UAE. I thought I knew what I was walking into. I was wrong.

My first assignment was managing our Al Ain facility, which handled logistics for textile imports from India and Bangladesh. I was 24, fresh from university, and I had the credentials to prove I belonged there. What I didn't have was any real understanding of operational complexity. Within my first three months, I missed two major shipment deadlines because I didn't realize our customs clearance process was broken. My father didn't fire me—he made me sit with the customs broker for two weeks to understand exactly where the system was failing. That humbled me quickly.

By 2020, I'd worked through the supply chain issues and started looking at our bigger problem: we had zero centralized data infrastructure. Everything was in spreadsheets and paper records. I proposed—probably too confidently—that we implement an ERP system. My father listened but told me to first prove I understood our current costs well enough to justify the investment. I spent four months doing a real financial audit of our three facilities. The number was shocking: we were bleeding roughly 8% of revenue through inefficiency, redundancy, and lost inventory tracking.

That's when I got serious about the business side. I led the ERP implementation from 2021 to 2022, which meant learning procurement, negotiating with vendors, managing the technical team, and dealing with the resistance from our older staff who'd done things the same way for 20 years. We went live in early 2023. Within a year, we'd cut that 8% waste down to 2.3%, and our inventory accuracy went from 76% to 94%.

Last year, I was promoted to operations director for all three facilities. My team expanded to 35 people. But I realized something: I was strong at solving specific operational problems, but I didn't have a coherent framework for thinking about strategy, organizational design, or scaling. My father could do it intuitively, but I needed actual training. I also knew I wanted to eventually modernize other aspects of the business—maybe diversify into logistics or e-commerce—but I felt like I was working with half the toolkit I needed.

That's why I'm applying to Waseda. I want to spend a year understanding management from first principles: how to think about organizational change, financial strategy, and international expansion. I've proven I can solve problems inside a family business. Now I need to build the broader perspective to actually lead one into its next phase.

prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
I'd planned to modernize our entire procurement system in eighteen months. We were in year two of a three-year contract with the Saudi Ministry of Defense, managing inventory across four major facilities near Riyadh, and the manual tracking processes were costing us nearly 2 million SAR annually in redundancies and errors.

I was confident. Too confident, maybe. I'd seen similar system implementations work at previous postings, and I'd pushed hard in meetings to accelerate the timeline. My team—about twelve people, mostly veteran logistics officers—was ready. The software vendor seemed solid. We had budget approval. What could go wrong?

Everything, as it turned out.

The vendor severely underestimated integration complexity with our existing security protocols. Three months in, we realized their architecture didn't meet our classified data requirements. Meanwhile, my team was split between maintaining the old system and testing the new one, and people were burning out. I remember my operations manager, Captain Al-Otaibi, sitting in my office looking exhausted, essentially telling me we'd hit a wall. I'd failed to account for how rigid government systems actually are—I'd relied on my private sector experience and assumed flexibility we simply didn't have.

The worst part was that I'd oversold the project internally. I'd been so focused on demonstrating initiative and delivering results that I hadn't built in adequate buffer time or communicated the risks honestly. When delays mounted, credibility eroded.

I had to do something I wasn't good at: admit I'd miscalculated. I went to my commanding officer and laid out what had gone wrong. Not defensively. Not with excuses. I explained that I'd prioritized speed over sustainability and hadn't accounted for our bureaucratic constraints—which weren't failures of the system but realities I should have anticipated.

Here's what I did differently: We renegotiated with the vendor, extended the timeline to 24 months, and—this was crucial—I pulled back from driving it myself. I put Captain Al-Otaibi in charge of day-to-day implementation while I shifted to removing institutional obstacles and managing upward. I also started monthly all-hands updates where I was genuinely transparent about progress and setbacks, not just wins. People needed to know we'd miscalculated and that we were being realistic now.

We completed the implementation in 26 months. Not perfect. But we stayed within revised budget, the system's been stable for over a year now, and we've recovered about 1.8 million SAR annually. More importantly, my relationship with the team shifted. I stopped being the person with all the answers and became someone they could trust to handle hard conversations.

That experience taught me that leadership isn't about flawless execution—it's about judgment, which includes knowing what you don't know. Coming to UM's Executive MBA, I'm looking to formalize what I've learned through experience and exposure to leaders who've had to navigate complex environments. The program's emphasis on organizational behavior and strategic decision-making directly addresses what I'm trying to strengthen. I'm ready to contribute my operational background while becoming the more thoughtful leader this region needs.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
# Ethical Dilemma in Private Equity

During my three years working in private equity, I have encountered many situations that required me to think deeply about ethics and values. One particular experience stands out as a moment where I had to make a difficult choice between what was easy and what was right.

At my firm, we were evaluating a potential investment in a manufacturing company. This company had strong financials and good growth potential, which made it attractive to our investors. However, during our due diligence process, I discovered that the company was not fully compliant with environmental regulations in their production facilities.

When I brought this issue to my team lead, I was told that many companies in this sector operate in similar grey areas, and that addressing these concerns would significantly reduce the deal's attractiveness to our partners. The pressure was to move forward with the investment anyway. This put me in a difficult position because I knew that ignoring environmental violations was not aligned with my personal values.

I faced an ethical dilemma. Should I prioritize the financial returns that my firm and our investors were expecting, or should I advocate for addressing the environmental compliance issues? This was not an easy decision because I was still relatively junior and worried about how pushing back might affect my career trajectory.

After thinking about this carefully, I decided to do more research on the company's environmental violations. I gathered detailed information about the specific regulations they were breaking and the potential costs of remediation. I prepared a comprehensive analysis showing that while addressing these issues would require upfront investment, it would actually protect the firm from future regulatory fines and reputational damage.

I presented this analysis to my team lead and the investment committee. I emphasized that addressing the environmental concerns was not just ethically important, but also made financial sense. The committee appreciated the thorough research and decided to include environmental remediation requirements in our investment terms. The company agreed to these conditions, and we proceeded with the investment.

This experience taught me that doing the right thing does not always mean sacrificing financial performance. In fact, thorough analysis often shows that ethical practices and good business outcomes can align. It also showed me that speaking up, even when it is uncomfortable, is important. I learned that I have a responsibility to voice concerns when I see them, and to back them up with solid evidence.

This situation reinforced my commitment to ethical business practices and my desire to work in an environment where values like integrity and sustainability are taken seriously. I believe that MBA education will help me develop stronger frameworks for navigating complex ethical decisions and will prepare me to be a leader who can drive positive change while also creating value.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
I am writing to express my strong interest in ISCTE Business School's MBA program. With five years of experience in military logistics and a clear vision for transitioning into the private sector, I believe your program is the ideal platform to bridge this gap and accelerate my career development.

My background is unconventional. I started as a procurement officer with the Kenya Defence Force in 2019, managing supply chains for a 2,000-person logistics unit based in Nairobi. I quickly realized that my aptitude lay not just in executing orders, but in understanding the systems behind them. Over five years, I progressed to senior analyst, overseeing inventory management for equipment worth approximately 8 million USD. I streamlined our procurement processes, reducing procurement cycle time from 90 days to 45 days—a change that freed up capital and improved operational readiness. This achievement taught me that efficiency and strategic thinking matter more than hierarchy or tenure.

However, I reached a ceiling in the military environment. The organization moves slowly, and decisions are bound by protocol rather than market logic. I realized that my problem-solving skills were being constrained by the nature of the institution. I wanted to work where metrics matter, where ROI is the language, and where innovation isn't just encouraged—it's expected.

An MBA from ISCTE appeals to me because I need rigorous grounding in core business functions I've only glimpsed tangentially: finance, strategy, and organizational behavior in competitive markets. My military training taught me discipline and systems thinking, but it did not teach me how to build a business, allocate capital, or lead in uncertain environments outside of crisis management.

I am particularly drawn to ISCTE's location in Lisbon and its strong connections to European business. Portugal's economy is expanding, and I see opportunity in the African-European trade corridor—an area where my East African perspective combined with European business education could be valuable. I'm also interested in your program's emphasis on management challenges in emerging markets, which resonates deeply with my background.

I recognize that my GMAT score of 629 does not reflect my analytical capability. I am a strong performer under pressure—military environments demand it—but standardized testing is not my strength. My academic performance and professional progression demonstrate my capacity for rigorous thinking. I learn by doing, and I have consistently delivered results in complex, high-stakes environments.

My candidacy may seem unconventional. I am not coming from investment banking or consulting. I am a woman from Kenya seeking a mid-career pivot into business leadership. What I bring is resilience, clarity about my limitations, and genuine hunger to build competence in areas where I currently lack depth. I am ready to contribute fully to your cohort and to emerge as a leader who understands both African context and global business standards.

Thank you for considering my application.

prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
I had to lead without formal authority when my family business faced a critical challenge during the pandemic. My father, who runs our textile export company in Bangkok, asked me to help coordinate with our suppliers while he focused on major clients. Although I didn't have an official title, I took on this responsibility.

The situation was difficult. Many suppliers were struggling financially and considering stopping shipments. Our production was at risk. I realized that to solve this problem, I needed to communicate with people who didn't report to me and convince them to continue working with us despite their difficulties.

I started by calling all our major suppliers individually. I listened to their concerns about cash flow and payment delays. I understood that they were worried about their own businesses. Instead of just asking them to continue production, I proposed a new payment plan that would help them while also helping us. I worked with my father to arrange earlier payments and smaller batches so they could manage their cash flow better.

This experience taught me several important lessons. First, I learned that leadership is not about having authority over people. It's about building relationships and understanding what matters to others. When I took time to listen to the suppliers' problems, they became more willing to work with us because they felt heard and respected.

Second, I learned the importance of finding creative solutions. Instead of just demanding that suppliers meet our deadlines, I had to think about what would benefit both sides. This required me to be more flexible and collaborative than I might have been if I had formal authority.

Third, this experience showed me that I could handle responsibility and solve complex problems, even when I wasn't officially in charge. I realized that I have skills in communication and problem-solving that can be useful in leadership roles.

During this time, I also had to coordinate with our internal team to adjust our production schedule. I worked with the warehouse manager and production staff to reorganize our workflow. Although they were older than me and had more experience, they responded well when I explained the reasons for the changes and asked for their input.

By the end of the first year of the pandemic, we had maintained all our key supplier relationships and even increased production by 15%. My father was pleased with my work and asked me to take on more responsibilities in our company.

This experience has made me think about my future career. I realized that I want to develop stronger business skills and learn more about management and strategy. This is why I am interested in pursuing an MBA. I believe that business education will help me become a better leader and eventually help my family business grow internationally.

In conclusion, leading without formal authority taught me that true leadership comes from relationships, creativity, and the ability to understand others' perspectives. I am confident that this experience, combined with the knowledge I will gain from an MBA program, will help me become an effective leader in any organization.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
I want to pursue an MBA to advance my career in healthcare management and to become a leader who can make a positive impact on the healthcare system in Africa. Throughout my 12 years working in the healthcare sector, I have developed a deep understanding of the challenges that African healthcare systems face, and I believe that with the right education and training, I can help address some of these issues.

Currently, I work as a senior manager at a private hospital group in Nairobi, where I am responsible for overseeing operations and strategy. In this role, I have learned about the complexities of managing healthcare facilities, including financial constraints, staffing challenges, and the need to balance quality care with sustainability. I have seen firsthand how poor management practices can negatively impact patient outcomes and employee satisfaction. This experience has motivated me to pursue further education in management.

An MBA will provide me with the theoretical knowledge and practical skills that I currently lack. I know that I need to understand financial management better, as well as strategic planning and organizational leadership. These are areas where I feel I have gaps in my education. I also want to learn about international best practices in healthcare management so that I can bring these back to Kenya.

I am interested in the University of Bern because it is a prestigious institution with a strong reputation. The program seems to offer relevant courses in healthcare management and strategy, which align with my career goals. I believe that studying in Switzerland will also expose me to a different business environment and allow me to network with professionals from around the world.

After completing the MBA, I plan to move into a more senior leadership position, possibly a CEO role at a hospital group or health insurance company. I would also like to eventually start my own healthcare consulting firm that helps smaller hospitals improve their operations and financial performance. My long-term vision is to contribute to improving healthcare delivery across East Africa.

The skills I will develop at the University of Bern, combined with my practical experience in the sector, will position me well to achieve these goals. I am confident that this program will give me the foundation I need to transition from operational management to strategic leadership. I am excited about the opportunity to study in Switzerland and to become part of the University of Bern community.

I believe that with an MBA from the University of Bern, I will be able to make meaningful contributions to healthcare in Africa, and I am committed to using my education to create positive change in the lives of patients and healthcare workers.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
# Competing Interests, Committed Choices

The spreadsheet on my screen showed two irreconcilable truths: our portfolio company couldn't afford both the operational turnaround my team had planned and the severance packages our investors demanded. I was the deal manager, which meant I had to figure out how to navigate between them.

This was 2021, and Meridian Manufacturing—a mid-market industrial components supplier we'd acquired for $180 million—was bleeding cash. The previous owner had let things decay. Inventory management was prehistoric. Production lines ran at 60% capacity. When we completed our 100-day assessment, the numbers were worse than we'd modeled.

My first instinct was to push the operational fix. I'd built relationships with the plant managers in Osaka and the purchasing team in Chicago over those hundred days. I understood their problems. They knew I was trying to help. The COO had sketched out a $4.2 million investment in new planning software and process retraining that could unlock another 20% capacity within nine months. The upside was real—we'd modeled the exit multiple improving from 5.8x to 7.1x.

But my firm's partners had different math. Two of our LPs were in their distribution windows. They wanted cash now. The efficient move, from their perspective, was to cut $3.8 million in annual overhead, which meant consolidating the Chicago purchasing operation into Ohio and reducing headcount by roughly 35 people. This would give them their distributions and provide immediate relief to our cash position. It would also destroy the turnaround momentum I'd spent months building.

I spent a week doing something I'm not proud of: I avoided the conversation. I kept working on the operational scenario, knowing it wasn't the one I'd have to present. I told myself the data would speak. It didn't. In investment decisions, emotion and incentive structures speak louder.

Then I attended the plant visit in Osaka. The purchasing director, Takeshi, asked me directly—not in a meeting, but over dinner—whether he should encourage his team to look for other positions. He'd sensed the uncertainty. I realized my avoidance had created anxiety for people whose livelihoods depended on my decisions. That's when I stopped hedging.

I scheduled a meeting with the two partners and prepared three scenarios, not one. I showed them the operational scenario with our 7.1x exit. I showed them the efficiency scenario with their immediate distributions. But I also showed them a hybrid approach: a more modest $2.1 million investment focused only on the planning software, plus a structured reduction of 15 people—mostly through attrition and early retirement offers—instead of 35. This would cost us maybe 0.2x on the multiple but would preserve continuity and give us a credible path to 6.8x.

What mattered, I told them, was that we needed to make a choice and communicate it clearly. The operational team couldn't execute if they were preparing for two different futures.

The partners chose the hybrid approach. Not because it was the most profitable—it wasn't—but because I'd shown them it was defensible to both the LP base and the management team. By explicitly naming the trade-offs instead of pretending they didn't exist, I'd made it easier for them to choose something other than the most aggressive option.

We kept 20 of the 35 people we'd planned to cut. The Chicago operation retrained rather than relocated. Takeshi stayed. We hit 7.2x on exit last year.

This experience fundamentally changed how I approach conflict between stakeholder interests. I don't believe compromise always means splitting the difference. It means understanding what each party actually needs—not just what they're asking for—and finding a solution where real interests align, even if it doesn't maximize any single stakeholder's gain. That's what I want to study at Michigan Ross: not just the mechanics of business, but how to make better decisions when the right answer isn't obvious and the people involved have real stakes.
