prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
Leadership has always been important to me. In my role at a law firm in Riyadh, I was given the opportunity to lead a small team and I realized that being a leader means inspiring others to do their best work.

When I joined the firm four years ago, I was excited to contribute to the legal field. I worked on various cases and gradually took on more responsibility. My supervisors noticed my dedication and soon I was assigned to oversee a team of three junior associates. This was a turning point in my career because it showed me that I could handle leadership responsibilities.

As a leader, I tried to create an environment where my team felt motivated. I held regular meetings with them to discuss their progress and provide feedback. I believed that good communication was key to success. I also made sure they understood the importance of our work and how it contributed to the firm's goals. When team members faced challenges, I tried to be supportive and guide them toward solutions.

One specific example was when we were working on a complex case with tight deadlines. The team was stressed and some members felt overwhelmed. I recognized that morale was low and took action. I organized a team lunch to help everyone relax and reconnect. I also redistributed the workload more fairly so no one person was carrying too much burden. After these changes, I noticed the team's productivity improved and they seemed happier coming to work.

Through this leadership experience, I learned that leaders need to be aware of their team's emotional and professional needs. I also learned that small gestures can have big impacts. My team members told me they appreciated my efforts to support them, which made me feel that I was making a difference.

I believe that an MBA from Kellogg will help me develop these leadership skills further and prepare me for bigger challenges. The school's focus on educating brave leaders aligns with my own values and goals. I am excited about the possibility of learning from experienced professors and talented classmates who share my passion for making an impact in the world.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
# Ethical Dilemma Essay

Throughout my career in healthcare, I have faced many challenging situations that have tested my values and character. One particular experience stands out as a defining moment in my professional journey, where I had to navigate a complex ethical dilemma that ultimately shaped who I am as a leader.

During my time working in the pharmaceutical industry, I was confronted with a situation that forced me to choose between personal ambition and doing what was right. This experience taught me the importance of integrity in business.

At my previous company, I was part of a team responsible for managing relationships with hospital networks across Latin America. My manager was pressuring me to sign off on a contract with a hospital that had some questionable billing practices. The commission would have been significant, and my promotion was dependent on closing deals. However, I had concerns about whether the hospital's practices aligned with our company's ethical standards.

This was a difficult position to be in. On one hand, I wanted to advance my career and achieve my goals. On the other hand, I knew that something didn't feel right about the arrangement. I spent several days thinking about what to do.

I decided to raise my concerns with my manager. I explained that while I understood the importance of closing deals, I had reservations about this particular hospital's practices. My manager was not receptive to my concerns and reminded me that my performance evaluation depended on hitting my targets.

I then made the decision to escalate the issue to our compliance department. I documented my concerns and submitted a formal report. This was risky because I knew my manager would not be happy about it, and it could impact my career trajectory at that company. However, I felt it was the right thing to do.

The compliance team investigated the hospital and found several issues with their billing practices that violated our policies. The contract was ultimately not signed. My manager was upset with me for going around him, but the company's leadership recognized that I had acted with integrity. While I didn't receive the promotion I was hoping for, I was recognized internally for my ethical stance.

This experience was a turning point for me. It taught me that doing the right thing sometimes comes at a personal cost, but that integrity is more valuable than short-term gains. I learned that ethical leadership means being willing to speak up even when it's uncomfortable, and being willing to accept the consequences of your choices.

Since this experience, I have tried to embody these principles in all aspects of my work. I believe that businesses have a responsibility to operate with integrity, and that leaders must model this behavior. In the healthcare industry particularly, where decisions directly impact people's lives and wellbeing, ethical conduct is non-negotiable.

This dilemma helped me understand what kind of leader and professional I want to be. I want to work for companies that value integrity as much as I do, and I want to create teams and cultures where people feel empowered to raise concerns without fear of retaliation.

Going forward, I am committed to continuing to navigate complex situations with honesty and transparency. An MBA from Hult will help me develop the skills and knowledge I need to be an even more effective leader who can drive positive change in my industry while maintaining the highest ethical standards. I am excited about the opportunity to contribute to Hult's diverse community and to learn from peers who share a commitment to ethical business practice.

school_name: University of Nairobi Business School Executive MBA
prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
When I was promoted to Senior Accountant at Deloitte Hanoi three years ago, I thought I had the formula figured out. Keep your numbers clean, your deadlines met, your clients happy. I'd done that for two years as a junior, earned my promotion, and was ready to lead.

Then I nearly destroyed a client relationship worth $800,000 in annual fees.

I was managing the audit for Vinafood, a major agricultural trading company. The client had given us six weeks to complete our preliminary work before their fiscal year close. Straightforward timeline, or so I thought. I divided the work among my four team members and set weekly check-ins. I was confident. Too confident.

By week three, I realized our progress was falling behind—but only because I wasn't actually looking. Our team member Linh, who was handling inventory reconciliation, had been flagging issues in her status updates that I'd glossed over. She'd found significant discrepancies between Vinafood's warehouse counts and their system records. Instead of treating this as the serious problem it was, I saw it as a scheduling problem. I told her to expedite the work and move forward. I didn't ask her for details. I didn't involve our senior manager. I just wanted the timeline to stay green.

Two weeks before the deadline, Vinafood's CFO discovered what Linh had found and panicked. They called our partner in charge before I even knew there was an escalation. Suddenly I was in a room with my partner, the client, and a problem that could have been caught and resolved three weeks earlier. The CFO was furious—rightfully so. He told us we'd missed the entire point of having an external auditor.

I've replayed this moment many times. What I did wrong wasn't a technical error. I had the right process in place. I failed because I wasn't actually leading. I was managing a timeline instead of managing people and information. I heard Linh's concerns but didn't listen to them. I chose speed over understanding.

Here's what came next, though: I had to rebuild that relationship. I asked Linh to walk me through everything she'd found, exactly as she'd wanted to do three weeks before. We spent two days on inventory analysis together. I brought in our senior manager to help validate her findings and develop a real solution. More importantly, I changed how we communicated. Instead of status updates I skimmed, I started having real conversations about what was actually happening in the audit.

Vinafood's CFO was skeptical at first, but the work was thorough. We got the audit done—late, but right. The client didn't leave, partly because of how we recovered, but mostly because Linh's original instinct was correct and now they knew we'd caught it.

The difference in me now: I don't optimize for metrics that don't matter. When I see a flag in someone's update, I ask for a conversation, not a solution. I've learned that managing people means understanding what they're actually seeing, not just tracking what they're delivering.

This experience is why I'm applying to the University of Nairobi Executive MBA. I'm at a point where I can do the accounting work—the technical skills are there. But I want to develop into someone who can lead teams across different markets and business challenges. East Africa's business environment is complex and fast-moving, and I want to understand it deeply. I want to learn from practitioners who've led through uncertainty here. I want to build a network that helps me think beyond compliance and control, toward strategy and impact.

The Nairobi MBA appeals to me specifically because of that regional focus. I'm not looking for a generic business education. I'm looking to understand how to lead effectively in this part of the world.

school_name: ESSEC Business School Master in Management
prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
The achievement I am most proud of is my promotion to Senior Account Manager at my telecommunications company after eight years of dedication and hard work. This represents the culmination of my efforts to become a better professional and leader.

Throughout my career, I have always believed in the importance of working hard and giving my best effort. When I joined the telecommunications sector, I started as a junior representative with limited experience. I was determined to prove myself and show my value to the organization. Over the years, I have worked on many different projects and accounts, learning new skills and developing my expertise in customer relationship management.

My promotion came after I successfully managed a large portfolio of corporate clients. I was responsible for maintaining these relationships and ensuring their satisfaction with our services. Through consistent dedication, I was able to increase customer retention rates and generate positive feedback from my clients. The management team recognized my contributions and decided to promote me to a senior position.

What makes me proud of this achievement is not just the promotion itself, but what it represents. It shows that hard work and persistence do lead to success. In the telecommunications industry, which is highly competitive, it is important to stand out and demonstrate your value. I believe I did this by always being reliable, responsive to client needs, and willing to go above and beyond my job description.

This promotion has also given me more responsibility and the opportunity to mentor junior staff members. I now oversee a team and help guide their professional development. I find this aspect of my role very rewarding because I can share what I have learned and help others grow in their careers.

However, I recognize that there are still many things I need to learn. The business world is constantly changing, and I need to adapt to new technologies and market trends. This is one of the reasons why I am interested in pursuing an MBA. I want to gain a deeper understanding of business fundamentals, finance, and strategy so that I can become a more effective leader. My promotion has shown me that I have the capability to succeed, but I also understand that formal business education will help me reach higher levels in my career.

I am grateful for the opportunities I have had at my company and the trust my managers have placed in me. Moving forward, I want to continue developing myself and taking on new challenges. An MBA from ESSEC will provide me with the tools and knowledge I need to advance further in the telecommunications industry or potentially explore other sectors. I am committed to making the most of this opportunity and contributing meaningfully to whatever organization I join after graduation.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
When I think about creative problem-solving, I immediately think of my time at TechVenture Solutions, where I worked in product development. During my tenure there, we faced a significant challenge that required thinking outside the box and approaching the problem from a different angle.

Our company was struggling with inefficiencies in our workflow management system. The team was spending too much time on manual processes, and this was affecting our productivity. Everyone knew something needed to change, but there wasn't a clear path forward. I realized that what we needed was a fresh perspective on how we approached our daily tasks.

I decided to implement a new system that would streamline our operations. I looked at what other companies were doing in our space and adapted their best practices to fit our needs. I worked with my team to identify the key pain points in our existing process, and then we brainstormed solutions together. This collaborative approach meant that everyone had a voice in the decision-making process, which was important for buy-in.

The solution we came up with involved reorganizing how we structured our workflow and implementing new software tools to automate certain tasks. I led the implementation process, making sure everyone understood the changes and had the training they needed to use the new system effectively. We rolled it out over a period of several weeks, and I monitored the results closely to ensure it was working as intended.

The results were positive. We saw improvements in our efficiency metrics, and the team reported that they had more time to focus on high-value work. This was a success for everyone involved, and it demonstrated my ability to lead change within an organization.

I think this experience is relevant to an MBA programme because it shows that I can identify problems and develop solutions. It also shows that I value teamwork and collaboration. Creative problem-solving is something I'm passionate about, and I believe that an MBA would give me the skills and knowledge to tackle even more complex challenges in the future.

Throughout my career, I've learned that the best solutions often come from listening to the people who are directly affected by a problem. In this case, it was my team members who provided valuable insights that helped shape the final solution. I'm committed to continuing to develop my leadership abilities and to making a real impact in whatever organization I work for.

An MBA from Ulster University would be a natural next step in my professional development. I'm excited about the opportunity to study alongside experienced professionals and to learn from faculty who have real-world experience in business. I believe that this programme will equip me with the tools I need to take on greater responsibilities and to lead more effectively in my career going forward.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I first saw the University of Bayreuth program, I was drawn to its focus on general management combined with international perspective. After seven years in consulting at Deloitte, working across EMEA offices and managing teams of 8-15 people, I've realized that my path forward requires something different than what I can get from executive education or online courses. I need structured time to step back, think strategically about my career, and build a genuinely international network.

My consulting background has been valuable but also limiting in certain ways. I spent the first four years in Cairo and then Munich, mostly in operations and supply chain optimization for manufacturing and retail clients. The work was solid — I helped a German automotive supplier reduce logistics costs by 18% and led a team through a challenging organizational restructuring. But I notice that much of my expertise is quite technical. I know process improvement deeply, I understand P&Ls, I can manage complex projects. What I lack is broader strategic thinking and exposure to different business models and industries. Most of my network is either consultants or my clients' operations teams. I want to understand how companies think about strategy, innovation, and growth from different angles.

I'm applying to Bayreuth specifically because of its location and reputation in Germany. I've now spent four years living in Munich, and I'm considering staying in Germany longer-term rather than returning to Egypt or moving to London like many of my peers. The German business environment interests me — the emphasis on engineering, the Mittelstand model, the way companies think about sustainable growth rather than just quarterly returns. Bayreuth is smaller than Munich or Frankfurt programs, which appeals to me. I want an environment where I can actually know my classmates and where discussion happens in seminars, not just lectures to 200 people.

I'm also being honest that I'm still uncertain about what comes next. I don't have a clear five-year plan, which some applicants probably do. I know I want to move into a general management role eventually, possibly in corporate strategy or business development. I know I want to stay in Europe. I know I want to work with teams I respect. But I'm genuinely unsure whether that means joining a corporation, starting something, or moving into a different type of organization. An MBA feels like the right time to explore these questions rather than figure them out alone.

What I'll bring to Bayreuth: perspective from both consulting and the Arabic business world. I grew up in Cairo, worked there, and still have connections across the region. I'm used to working in matrix organizations and across cultural differences. In group projects, I tend to drive toward practical solutions and timelines — something I learned from consulting that isn't always valued in academia but matters in real business. I'm a careful listener, which means I usually understand what's actually being asked rather than what I think is being asked.

I know this essay might sound less polished than others you receive. I'm choosing to be straightforward rather than craft something that sounds more impressive. Bayreuth seems like a place where that authenticity might be valued.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
When I first visited LBS's website, I'll admit I scrolled past the glossy homepage photos of the Thames and the glass atrium like everyone else does. But what stopped me was a video from a student panel on the Africa Initiative. One woman—I think she was from Kenya—was describing how she'd used her summer internship to help restructure a microfinance NGO's operations. She didn't sound like she was reading from a script. She sounded genuinely frustrated about the inefficiencies she'd found and excited about the real changes she'd made. That felt different. That felt possible.

So I did something probably most applicants don't do: I emailed her. I found her LinkedIn profile and reached out asking about her experience with the initiative, how accessible it really was to students with African backgrounds. She responded within 48 hours. We ended up chatting for 45 minutes on Zoom, and she walked me through not just the Africa Initiative itself, but how the school's location in London—a global hub with serious African financial infrastructure—gave her access to real projects and real stakeholders, not case studies about development work.

That conversation shifted something for me. I've spent the last decade building education systems. First as a teacher in Lagos, then moving into curriculum design and program management at a mid-size EdTech company serving West Africa. I've built something—we grew from 40 schools to 240 schools across Nigeria and Ghana in six years—but I've hit a ceiling. I don't have a finance framework to understand unit economics at scale. I don't have operational strategy language for what I'm doing intuitively. And honestly, I don't have the network to move from regional to continental.

After that conversation, I attended one of LBS's webinars on entrepreneurship in emerging markets. I asked a pointed question about how MBA programs actually balance theory with practice in the African context—I was a bit skeptical, I think obviously—and afterward the admissions officer stayed behind to chat. She didn't try to oversell me. She was direct: "Look, you'll get the strategy and finance toolkit you need. But what you'll really get from London is access. Your cohort will include people raising Series A for African startups, people from global investment firms looking at Sub-Saharan Africa, people who've actually worked in your sector." That honesty mattered.

Now here's what I want to do at LBS: I want to join the Africa Initiative, and I want to structure a project around sustainable scaling models for EdTech in emerging markets. Not as charity—I'm over that frame—but as a real commercial problem that needs rigor and network. I want to spend my core classes obsessing over the finance gaps I haven't been able to fill: how to model growth with inconsistent data, how to manage unit economics across dispersed operations. And I want the summer to be abroad. Not in London. Back in Lagos or Accra, connected to someone in the investment community, actually testing whether what I'm learning transfers.

Being part of your community means I'm not seeking inspiration or feel-good stories. I'm showing up for the unglamorous work of building business sense in a sector I already understand. That's what gets me to LBS, not the prestige. That's what I'll use it for once I'm there.

school_name: The University of Chicago Booth School of Business
prompt: Is there any unclear information in your application that needs further explanation?
content:
I came to the United States from South Korea when I was sixteen years old to pursue higher education and build a better future for myself. This decision has shaped everything about my journey, which I believe is important context for understanding my academic record.

During my undergraduate years at a state university, I struggled significantly with the transition to American education. I was working part-time to support myself while maintaining full-time student status, and I was still adjusting to living in a new country. My GPA of 2.7 reflects this difficult period. I was not focused on academics the way I should have been, and I made mistakes in time management that I deeply regret.

Since graduation, I have worked in the government sector for three years, and this experience has given me the maturity and discipline I lacked in college. In my role, I have taken on increasing responsibility and have learned the value of hard work and dedication. My supervisor has been supportive of my professional growth, and I have developed strong skills in project management and team collaboration.

I am taking the GMAT seriously and understand that my score of 625 does not reflect my current capabilities as a professional. I was managing a demanding job while studying, and I did not prepare as thoroughly as I should have. I know this score is below Booth's average, and I understand that it raises questions about my quantitative abilities.

I believe that my military background and diverse international perspective are valuable, and I am committed to succeeding in an MBA program. I have matured significantly since my undergraduate years, and I am ready to dedicate myself fully to my studies. I hope you will consider my application holistically and give me the opportunity to prove myself at Booth.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
Two years into my engineering role at Rashid Petroleum, I was asked to lead a cross-functional team tasked with reducing methane emissions at our Suez facility. I was 24, the only woman on a team of eight senior technicians, and I'd never managed anyone before.

My first instinct was to prove myself through sheer technical competence. I spent weeks analyzing data, building models, pushing hard on deadlines. Within a month, I'd alienated half the team. One of my senior technicians, Mahmoud, pulled me aside after a tense meeting and said something I needed to hear: "You're smart, but you're not listening. We've been here longer than you've been alive. Ask us what we think."

That stung. But he was right.

I completely changed my approach. Instead of presenting solutions, I started asking questions. I brought the team together every Friday morning with coffee and told them explicitly: I don't have all the answers, and I need your experience. We spent those sessions brainstorming, and something shifted. Mahmoud suggested a modification to our flare system that I'd never considered. Another technician proposed phasing in changes rather than implementing everything at once—much smarter than my aggressive timeline.

Within five months, we reduced methane emissions by 18%, exceeding our target by 3 percentage points. But the real learning wasn't in the numbers.

I realized that leadership isn't about being the smartest person in the room. It's about creating space for people to contribute their best thinking. The team didn't suddenly become more competent when I started listening—they were always capable. What changed was that they felt genuinely heard.

That experience fundamentally shaped how I think about management. I now believe a leader's job is to unlock what's already there in her team, not to impose solutions from above. I've carried this into every project since: genuine curiosity about people's perspectives, transparency about my own limitations, and creating psychological safety so people actually speak up.

It's also made me acutely aware of how much I still have to learn about leading at scale. I want to deepen my understanding of organizational dynamics and strategic leadership in an Executive MBA environment where I can learn from practitioners with far more experience than I have.

prompt: Reflect on a core value that defines you. Share a specific example of how it has guided your decisions and actions, and explain how it will shape your contributions and growth as a McCombs MBA student. Highlight how this value has influenced your leadership style and life path, and how it will shape your MBA experience.
content:
I've always believed that integrity means doing the right thing even when it costs you something.

Three years ago at Deloitte, I was staffed on a financial services project where we were helping a mid-sized bank streamline their lending process. About halfway through the engagement, I discovered that our recommendation would inadvertently make it harder for small business owners in lower-income neighborhoods to qualify for loans—the opposite of what the client claimed to want. The partner leading the work didn't see it as our problem to solve. He said we were hired to optimize efficiency, not to rewrite their lending criteria.

I spent a weekend mapping out the issue and brought a revised recommendation to him on Monday. It meant scrapping two weeks of work and pushing the delivery timeline back. He wasn't happy, but I couldn't present findings I knew would harm the people our client said they wanted to serve. We ended up implementing a hybrid approach that achieved most of the efficiency gains while protecting access for underserved borrowers.

I didn't get the promotion I was expecting that year. My manager told me I'd been "too idealistic." But the client eventually thanked us, and more importantly, I could actually stand behind the work.

That experience shaped how I lead now. I push my teams to think beyond the obvious answer. I ask uncomfortable questions. I've learned that integrity isn't about being self-righteous—it's about taking responsibility for the full weight of your decisions.

At McCombs, I want to deepen my strategic thinking so I can advocate more effectively for outcomes that matter. An EMBA will help me move into leadership roles where I can embed these values into how organizations operate, not just how I operate personally.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
When I started at Goldman Sachs in 2013, I thought leadership meant knowing all the answers. I was 22, freshly graduated from LSE, and convinced that my job was to be the smartest person in every meeting. I spent my first year in the technology banking division working 80-hour weeks, obsessing over pitch books and financial models, treating every interaction like a test I might fail.

By year three, I'd made MD, had a reasonable bonus, and felt hollow. I was good at the mechanics—the valuation analysis, the client management, the deal closing. But I wasn't learning anything real about how businesses actually worked. I was watching from the sidelines, packaging other people's ambitions into PowerPoint slides.

That's when I called my uncle, who'd been investing in tech startups since the dot-com days. He told me there was a junior analyst role at a London seed fund, Boundary Ventures. The pay cut was significant—nearly 60%. My parents thought I'd lost my mind. But I took it.

The first two years felt like learning to see. I wasn't building models; I was sitting across from founders asking why their product mattered. I visited factories in Manchester, sat through product demos that crashed mid-pitch, flew to Berlin to watch a portfolio company pivot completely. I learned that capital deployment is actually about conviction, intuition, and the willingness to be wrong. I had plenty of practice with that last part.

By 2018, I was promoted to investment partner. The fund was growing—we had about £45 million in AUM then—and I took point on our health tech thesis. This was where things got harder in ways I hadn't anticipated. I backed three companies that I genuinely believed in. One of them, a platform for remote patient monitoring, raised a Series A and everything seemed to be tracking. Then the founder turned out to be embezzling money. Not a little. Over £2 million. I was the partner who'd vouched for him. I'd missed basic red flags because I'd fallen in love with the thesis.

That failure—and it was genuinely a failure, not the sanitized version you tell investors—changed how I approached this work. I became obsessed with founder due diligence. I started taking on earlier-stage deals, pre-Series A, where I could watch teams actually execute rather than just theoretically evaluate them. By 2020, when COVID hit, I was managing a £12 million allocation and had shifted our focus entirely toward founders solving healthcare access problems in developing markets.

The last three years have been about building conviction on a bigger stage. I've led or co-led eleven investments, eight of which have progressed to follow-on rounds. Two have found meaningful product-market fit. One is almost certainly going to be a substantial exit. I'm also doing something I never did at Goldman: I'm mentoring junior analysts, trying to teach them that good investing is about humility, not ego.

But I've hit another ceiling. I'm 33, I've been in venture for eight years, and I can feel that the best investors—the ones making 10-year decisions with serious capital—they have something else. Some combination of pattern recognition, strategic thinking, and international perspective that I'm still assembling.

I'm applying to Hebrew University's Executive MBA because Israel has become the epicenter of the innovation I'm trying to understand. I want to study the intersection of venture, innovation policy, and how capital shapes entire economies. I want to learn from people who are building things in a market that proves daily that constraint drives creativity. And honestly, I want to be pushed by a program that takes itself seriously—one that forces me to think structurally about what I've learned intuitively.

I'm not looking for validation. I'm looking for the next skill I don't know I'm missing.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
When I decided to pursue an Executive MBA, Rotterdam School of Management immediately stood out to me as a top choice. I have done extensive research on your program and I am excited about the possibility of joining your community.

I first learned about RSM through various online rankings and websites. I visited the school's website multiple times and reviewed the curriculum, faculty profiles, and program structure. I watched some videos of current students discussing their experiences, which gave me a good sense of the program's rigor and international focus. I also looked at the location in Rotterdam and found it to be an interesting and vibrant city in Europe, which appeals to me as someone seeking a global perspective.

What impressed me most about RSM is its strong reputation and diverse student body. I read that the program brings together professionals from many different countries and industries, which I believe creates a dynamic learning environment. This diversity is something I value greatly because in my current role at a real estate company in São Paulo, I work with colleagues from various backgrounds, and I have learned how much this enriches problem-solving and innovation.

I am particularly interested in RSM's focus on international business and global leadership. As a Brazilian professional working in real estate, I see enormous potential for growth in emerging markets, and I believe an Executive MBA from a European institution will give me the tools and network to expand my career globally. Real estate is becoming increasingly international, and I want to be prepared for that future.

The Executive MBA format is also very appealing to me. Since I am currently working full-time, the program's flexibility allows me to continue my professional responsibilities while advancing my education. This is crucial for my development at this stage of my career.

Additionally, I am drawn to the school's emphasis on sustainability and responsible business practices. In real estate, we are beginning to focus more on green buildings and sustainable development, and I want to deepen my understanding of how to integrate these principles into business strategy. I believe RSM's curriculum addresses these contemporary issues, which is exactly what I need.

During my time at RSM, I plan to take full advantage of the community by actively participating in class discussions, joining relevant clubs focused on real estate and international business, and networking with my peers. I want to learn from professionals who have different experiences and perspectives. I also hope to participate in case competitions and consulting projects if available, as these will help me apply theoretical knowledge to practical business challenges.

Furthermore, I am interested in the alumni network that RSM offers. Building relationships with graduates who work in international real estate and global markets will be invaluable for my career trajectory. I see the program not just as an educational experience but as the beginning of a lifelong professional community that will support my growth.

In conclusion, Rotterdam School of Management offers exactly what I am looking for at this point in my career. The combination of academic excellence, international perspective, and practical focus on real estate and sustainability makes it the ideal choice for me. I am confident that I will be a positive contributor to the RSM community while also gaining immensely from the experience.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
Throughout my career in consulting, I have encountered many complex problems that required creative thinking. One situation that particularly stands out demonstrates my ability to innovate under pressure.

At my firm, we were working with a major manufacturing client facing significant operational inefficiencies. The client had multiple production facilities across different regions, and they were struggling to coordinate between departments. This was causing delays and increased costs. The problem was multifaceted and seemed difficult to solve at first glance.

I was assigned to lead a small team to address this challenge. We conducted extensive interviews and gathered data from various stakeholders. Through this process, I realized that the core issue wasn't just a lack of communication—it was that different departments were using incompatible systems and processes. They were essentially speaking different languages.

Rather than recommending expensive software implementation, which is the typical consulting approach, I thought about a simpler solution. I proposed creating a standardized digital dashboard that could pull data from their existing systems. This dashboard would give all departments visibility into production schedules, inventory levels, and bottlenecks in real time.

The implementation took about three months. We worked closely with the IT team and department heads to ensure the dashboard met everyone's needs. Once it was launched, the results were impressive. The client saw a 25% reduction in production delays and estimated cost savings of approximately $2 million annually.

What made this solution creative was that it didn't require the client to overhaul their entire infrastructure. Instead, it worked within their existing constraints and used the resources they already had. The key was understanding that sometimes the best solutions are not the most complex ones.

This experience taught me that problem-solving in business isn't always about implementing the newest technology or the most sophisticated strategy. Sometimes it's about stepping back and seeing what really matters to your stakeholder. This mindset has stayed with me throughout my career, and I believe it will be valuable as I pursue my MBA. I'm excited to learn more advanced problem-solving frameworks that can help me tackle even more complex business challenges in the future.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I think about the next chapter of my career, I know it has to happen in Asia. The region represents the future of manufacturing innovation, and Japan especially fascinates me because of its commitment to quality and continuous improvement. Nagoya University of Commerce's Master in Management program seems like the ideal place to build the skills I need to succeed in this market.

I have spent the last five years working in manufacturing operations in Israel, managing production schedules and supply chain logistics for a mid-sized factory. My role has given me solid technical knowledge, but I realize now that I need a more comprehensive business education to move into strategic positions. An MBA will help me understand finance, marketing, and organizational leadership at a deeper level. I believe your program is well-suited for someone like me who wants to transition into international management roles.

Japan has always represented something special to the manufacturing world. The discipline, the attention to detail, the philosophy of kaizen—these concepts have shaped how I think about operations. By studying at Nagoya University of Commerce, I would be immersed in that culture while learning from professors and classmates who understand the Asian business landscape intimately. I am particularly interested in the program's focus on global business strategy, as this aligns with my goal of eventually leading international operations teams.

What I can bring to your program is perspective from outside Japan. My background in manufacturing, combined with my Israeli work ethic and direct communication style, would add diversity to classroom discussions. I am used to solving problems creatively under pressure, and I believe this mindset would benefit my cohort. Additionally, I am someone who works hard and takes my education seriously. I plan to be fully engaged in group projects and case competitions.

I am also drawn to Nagoya specifically because of its industrial heritage. The city is a genuine manufacturing hub, and studying there would allow me to network with industry professionals and understand how leading Japanese companies actually operate. This is much more valuable than attending a program in Tokyo, where the focus tends to be more financial rather than industrial.

Finally, I am committed to becoming fluent in Japanese during and after the program. I have already begun studying the language, and I see this as essential for building genuine relationships and deepening my understanding of Japanese business culture. This will set me apart when I return to the region for work.

Your program represents a strategic investment in my future, and I do not take that lightly. I am ready to commit fully to the experience and to emerge as a more globally minded, technically skilled manager who can bridge Israeli, Japanese, and broader Western business practices.

prompt: Reflect on a core value that defines you. Share a specific example of how it has guided your decisions and actions, and explain how it will shape your contributions and growth as a McCombs MBA student. Highlight how this value has influenced your leadership style and life path, and how it will shape your MBA experience.
content:
Integrity is the core value that defines who I am. In my career in accounting, I have learned that integrity is not just about following rules, but about doing the right thing even when no one is watching.

Three years ago, I worked at a mid-sized accounting firm in Bangkok. My supervisor asked me to overlook a discrepancy in a client's financial records that would have resulted in a significant tax benefit for the client. The partner was pushing for approval because the client was very important to the firm. I was nervous about refusing because I did not want to upset my supervisor or lose the client relationship. However, I knew it was wrong. I told my supervisor that I could not approve the records as they were, and I explained why the discrepancy needed to be corrected.

My supervisor was upset with me at first. But after I showed her the specific issue, she agreed with me and we corrected the records together. The client was actually relieved because they understood the importance of accuracy. We kept the client relationship and everyone benefited from doing things the right way.

This experience shaped my leadership style. I believe that leaders must model integrity and create environments where doing the right thing is valued. I want to be someone that people can trust.

At McCombs, I plan to apply this value by being honest in my group work and contributing fully to my team. I will also use my accounting background to help other students understand financial concepts. I believe that integrity will help me grow as a leader and make meaningful contributions to the business community. McCombs' focus on ethics aligns with my values, and I am excited to develop my skills in an environment that prioritizes doing the right thing.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
Why Keio Business School

I have always been passionate about business and making a positive impact in the world. This is why I am excited to apply to Keio Business School's Master of Management program. I believe that this program will help me achieve my career goals and become a better leader.

Throughout my five years of work experience in the manufacturing sector, I have learned a lot about operations, supply chain management, and team leadership. I started as an assistant manager at a mid-sized manufacturing company in the Philippines and have since grown into a more senior role. During this time, I have managed teams, improved processes, and contributed to the company's success. These experiences have shown me that I need formal business education to take the next step in my career.

Keio Business School appeals to me because of its strong reputation in Asia and its focus on developing global business leaders. The program's curriculum seems comprehensive and will give me the tools I need to succeed in an increasingly competitive business environment. I am particularly interested in the operations management and supply chain courses, as these align with my professional background. Additionally, I know that Keio is located in Tokyo, which is a major business hub, and studying there will expose me to different business cultures and perspectives.

I will be a valuable member of the Keio community. As someone from the Philippines working in manufacturing, I bring a unique perspective from a developing economy. I can share my experiences with classmates and contribute to class discussions about emerging markets and manufacturing challenges in Asia. My work experience means I am not just a student—I have practical knowledge that I can apply to case studies and group projects. I am also a hard-working person who takes my studies seriously, and I believe I will be a positive presence in the classroom.

Furthermore, I am committed to building a strong professional network at Keio. I understand that one of the most valuable aspects of business school is the connections you make with your classmates and professors. I plan to actively participate in student clubs and networking events to maximize this opportunity. I also hope to connect with other Filipino alumni and professionals in Japan to strengthen ties between the Philippines and Japan in the business world.

After graduation, I plan to return to the Philippines and apply what I have learned at Keio to help improve manufacturing practices in my country. I believe that with an MBA from a prestigious international institution, I can take on more leadership responsibilities and potentially move into senior management or start my own business ventures in the manufacturing or logistics sectors.

In conclusion, Keio Business School's Master of Management program is the right choice for me at this stage of my career. The program will provide me with the education, skills, and network I need to achieve my goals. I am confident that I will be a dedicated and engaged student who contributes meaningfully to the Keio community. I am excited about the opportunity to study at Keio and become part of your alumni network.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
Dear Selection Committee,

I am writing to express my strong interest in the MSc Management program at the University of Bologna. With over ten years of experience in the non-profit sector and a deep commitment to creating positive change in the world, I believe I am an ideal candidate for your program.

Throughout my career, I have worked in various roles within international development organizations, primarily focused on education and community development across Southeast Asia. I have always been passionate about making a difference in people's lives, and the non-profit sector has allowed me to pursue this passion while developing valuable skills in project management, team leadership, and strategic planning.

As I have progressed in my career, I have realized that to have greater impact and take on more senior leadership positions, I need to deepen my understanding of business management and organizational strategy. An MBA will provide me with the essential knowledge and tools necessary to become a more effective leader and manager. The University of Bologna's MSc Management program is particularly appealing because of its strong reputation and global perspective.

I believe my background brings a unique perspective to the program. Coming from Vietnam and having worked across different countries and cultures, I have developed strong cross-cultural communication skills and adaptability. My experience managing teams and implementing programs in resource-constrained environments has taught me to be creative, resilient, and focused on results.

Additionally, I am excited about the opportunity to study in Italy and be part of a diverse international student body. I am committed to contributing meaningfully to classroom discussions and learning from my peers while also sharing my own experiences and insights.

My professional journey in the non-profit sector has been fulfilling, but I recognize that I need formal training in management and business principles to advance further. The MSc Management program will equip me with the strategic and analytical skills I lack, and I am confident that this education will enable me to return to my field with greater capacity to drive organizational growth and impact.

Thank you for considering my application. I look forward to the possibility of joining your program and contributing to the University of Bologna community.

Sincerely,
[Applicant Name]

prompt: A personal statement offers you the chance to explain why you want to study your chosen course, your academic and relevant work experience, and how a master's will help you with your future career aspirations.
content:
When I first arrived at the Johannesburg office of Sasol Energy in 2015, I was given a simple task: help the operations team understand why their downstream production line was losing 2-3% efficiency each quarter. I wasn't a process engineer—I'd just finished my commerce degree. But I had a spreadsheet, curiosity, and access to nine years of maintenance data.

What I found wasn't complicated, but it required looking at the problem from outside the usual channels. The team had been optimizing individual equipment assets without considering how their maintenance schedules cascaded across the entire system. When one unit went down for servicing, a downstream bottleneck rippled through the whole operation. Over three months, I helped redesign their maintenance calendar. The result was a 1.8% recovery in efficiency—roughly $2.4 million in annual production value from better scheduling alone.

That project changed how I thought about my career in energy. I realized I wasn't just interested in operations or engineering or finance in isolation. I wanted to understand how these systems connect, where inefficiencies hide in the space between departments, and how to translate technical problems into business decisions that matter.

Over the past nine years, I've held three roles at Sasol: operations analyst, process improvement coordinator, and most recently, supply chain analyst. Each role gave me practical experience in different parts of the value chain, but I've hit a ceiling. My managers are supportive, and I could probably move into a senior operations role if I stayed. But I've realized that the gap holding me back isn't technical knowledge—it's the ability to think strategically about business systems as a whole. I can diagnose problems in a production line or supply network. What I struggle with is translating that diagnostic work into long-term strategic thinking for the organization.

The energy sector is changing rapidly. The company is investing heavily in renewable energy projects and transitioning away from pure coal dependence. There's real opportunity for someone who understands both the legacy operations and can think clearly about where the business needs to go. But to do that at scale, I need training in how to approach strategy, risk, and organizational decision-making with more rigor. My experience is deep but narrow. I've built intuition about problems in my sector, but I haven't learned the frameworks that would let me apply that intuition across industries or in a consulting context—which is actually where I'm interested in working eventually.

Southampton's Master in Management appeals to me for a few reasons. The program's emphasis on strategy and organizational change aligns with what I'm trying to develop. The first year core courses in economics, finance, and organizational behavior would fill genuine gaps in my education. I didn't study finance at undergrad, and I've been frustrated by how much business conversation I miss because I don't have that fluency. But beyond the curriculum, I'm drawn to studying somewhere outside South Africa. I've been here for my entire career, and I think I need exposure to different ways of thinking about business problems. The UK's management education approach feels like it would challenge some of my assumptions.

I'm realistic about what an MBA won't do. It won't make me an engineer, and it won't solve every problem at my company. But I do think it would make me significantly more useful—both to my organization in the short term, and to a consulting firm or renewable energy company down the road where I'd eventually like to work.

The timing feels right now. I'm at a point where I have enough industry credibility to make the transition, but not so deep in the system that moving would feel irresponsible. And I'm genuinely curious again about learning, which I hadn't felt in a few years. That matters.

prompt: If there is an important part of your story missing from your MBA application (e.g., unexplained gaps in work experience, choice of recommenders, past academic challenges), please use this section to provide a brief explanation. Please use bullets if you need to address more than one topic.
content:
I wanted to address a few things that don't fully represent my capability as a candidate.

My undergraduate GPA of 2.9 doesn't reflect my actual academic ability. I struggled initially with the transition from secondary school and wasn't as focused as I should have been. Since then, I've completed multiple professional certifications including my RICS qualification, which demonstrates my commitment to continuous learning. I'm confident that my performance in the EMBA programme will be significantly stronger, as I'm now much more mature and motivated.

Regarding my GMAT score of 638, I found the standardised testing format quite challenging. I sat it once and felt the timing was rushed. While I recognise this isn't a strong score, I believe it doesn't capture my analytical skills, which I use daily in my role managing property portfolios worth over £50m.

My recommenders are both from my current company because I wanted to ensure they had direct visibility of my recent work. I haven't worked elsewhere for the past five years, so it made sense to choose people who know me well professionally.

Finally, there was a six-month gap in my CV in 2017 when I left a role due to management disagreements. I've learned from that experience and have maintained stability in my current position.

I'm excited about the EMBA and confident I can succeed in the programme.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
I was hired as a regulatory affairs associate at Syntora Pharmaceuticals in 2020, right when they were pushing their diabetes drug through Phase 3 trials. Three years in, I'd become lead on the regulatory strategy team—the person who knew the data inside out, who stakeholders trusted, who could predict what the FDA would ask before they asked it.

In early 2023, I noticed something in our dossier submission that felt wrong.

Our drug showed efficacy at 8 weeks. The primary endpoint was clear. But the data at 16 weeks told a different story—the effect was diminishing. Not dramatically. Just enough that it mattered. Our biostatisticians had flagged it, quietly, in a memo that got buried under fifty other documents. No one was lying. Everyone had just... moved past it.

I sat with this for two weeks. I told myself I was overthinking. The 8-week data was solid. Patients would benefit. Our company had already invested $200 million. Three hundred people worked on this program. I had a mortgage.

I also had emails in my inbox from diabetic patients asking when this drug would be available.

The thing about being Pakistani and non-binary in pharma is that you learn early to avoid rocking the boat. I'd already navigated enough conversations where people got my identity wrong or questioned my technical credibility. This was different, though. This was about people taking a medication thinking it would work one way when our own data suggested something else.

I scheduled a meeting with our chief medical officer. Not in front of the whole team. Just us.

I told him about the 16-week data. I said we needed to present it—not hide it, not spin it, present it as part of the complete picture. I expected pushback. I expected talk about market timing, about competitive landscape, about how competitors would use this against us.

He listened. Then he said, "You're right. Thank you for saying it out loud."

We presented the complete data set. The FDA asked harder questions. The timeline extended by eight months. Our stock dipped briefly. But we got approval with a label that was honest about what the data showed. Not perfect, but real.

Would I have made the same choice if I'd been less secure in my role? I honestly don't know. That's what bothers me still. I had leverage—the technical knowledge, the relationships, the track record. Someone earlier in their career might have been too afraid to speak up, and I need to carry that awareness forward.

Hebrew University's emphasis on ethics in business, especially in healthcare, resonates deeply. I want to work in an environment where these conversations aren't exceptional. They're expected.

school_name: University of Hamburg MSc Business Administration
prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
I am excited about the opportunity to contribute meaningfully to the classroom and campus community at University of Hamburg. Throughout my career in healthcare, I have learned that diverse perspectives and collaborative environments are essential for growth, and I believe I can bring valuable insights to both classroom discussions and student life.

In my current role as a Healthcare Coordinator at a mid-sized clinic in Chicago, I have gained exposure to various aspects of the healthcare industry. This experience has given me practical knowledge about operations, patient management, and organizational structure. I believe these real-world experiences will enhance classroom discussions by providing concrete examples that complement theoretical concepts. When discussing supply chain management or organizational efficiency, I will be able to reference actual challenges I have encountered and how different solutions might impact workflow in practice.

I am also passionate about making a difference in student communities. I care deeply about creating an inclusive environment where everyone feels welcome and heard. During my time at my current organization, I have made efforts to be supportive of my colleagues, and I intend to carry this forward at University of Hamburg. I understand that diversity of thought and background strengthens teams, and I am committed to ensuring that all perspectives are valued.

Beyond the classroom, I see myself as someone who can contribute to campus activities and student organizations. I enjoy meeting new people and building relationships, which I believe will help me engage authentically with the student body. I am interested in getting involved in clubs or groups that align with my interests, whether that involves professional development, cultural exchange, or wellness initiatives. I am flexible and willing to participate in various aspects of student life as needed.

Additionally, I recognize that my identity as a non-binary person may offer a unique perspective in discussions around workplace culture, diversity and inclusion, and organizational behavior. I am aware that my academic background may be different from some peers, and this makes me even more committed to being an engaged and thoughtful participant.
