I was a Senior Regulatory Affairs Manager at Medtronic's facility in Bangalore, and our team had been struggling for two years to get approval for our cardiac monitoring device in the Indian market. We were stuck at the testing phase—regulators kept asking for additional data, and our timelines kept slipping.

The device itself was good. It could genuinely help Indian cardiac patients. But the regulatory environment was complicated, and our internal team was fragmented. Regulatory Affairs was waiting on R&D, R&D was waiting on Clinical Operations, and nobody was really talking to each other. We were losing time and momentum.

I decided to take ownership. I scheduled weekly sync meetings—not the usual status update calls, but actual working sessions where we mapped out every single requirement the regulators had raised. I made a matrix showing which team owned what, what the blockers were, and realistic deadlines. It wasn't complicated, but it was clear.

More importantly, I started visiting the regulatory offices myself. Instead of sending emails, I sat down with the officials and asked them directly what they needed to move forward. I learned that one of the reasons for the delays wasn't missing data—it was confusion about our manufacturing standards versus Indian standards. We'd been collecting the wrong benchmarks.

Over six months, we restructured our data submission, got buy-in from all teams, and submitted a revised application. In March 2019, we received approval. The device launched later that year, and in the first 18 months, we had around 2,500 installations across India. That meant 2,500 patients with better cardiac monitoring.

I'm proud of this for a few reasons. First, I saw a problem and didn't wait for someone else to solve it. Second, I realized the issue wasn't technical—it was organizational and interpersonal. Third, the outcome was tangible. I could measure it.

But I also want to be honest about what this achievement taught me about my limitations. I'm good at fixing broken processes, but I'm not naturally strategic. I was so focused on getting approval that I didn't think about what came after. When the device launched, I wasn't involved in commercial strategy or market planning. I realized I'd been operating tactically within a narrow lane, and I didn't fully understand how my work connected to the larger business picture. That's where I felt the gap.

This is actually why an MBA matters for me now. I've spent 11 years in medical devices, and I've done well operationally. But I want to understand strategy, P&L, and how to think about scaling businesses globally—not just solving the next regulatory problem. I want to move from being really good at one function to understanding how all the pieces fit together.

An Executive MBA appeals to me because of its focus on integrating across functions. I don't want to start over in a classroom with people fresh out of undergrad. I want to learn alongside people who've done things, who understand operations, who can teach me what they've learned.

school_name: Vlerick Business School Master in General Management
prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
Five years after completing the Master in General Management at Vlerick Business School, I see myself in a leadership position within the consumer goods industry. I envision myself managing a team of professionals, driving business strategy, and making a positive impact in my organization. This is the natural next step in my career trajectory, and I believe Vlerick's program will provide me with the skills and knowledge necessary to achieve this goal.

Throughout my nine years in the CPG sector, I have developed a strong foundation in operations, supply chain management, and cross-functional collaboration. I have worked on various projects that have taught me the importance of strategic thinking and data-driven decision making. However, I recognize that to move into senior management roles, I need to broaden my business acumen beyond my functional expertise. This is where an MBA becomes essential.

The Vlerick Master in General Management appeals to me because of its comprehensive curriculum. The program's focus on general management rather than specialization aligns with my desire to develop a 360-degree view of business. I am particularly interested in courses that will strengthen my understanding of finance, marketing strategy, and organizational leadership. These areas are currently gaps in my knowledge, and I believe addressing them will make me a more well-rounded leader.

Additionally, I appreciate that Vlerick is located in Belgium, a strategic hub in Europe. The international exposure and diverse cohort will be invaluable. Working alongside professionals from different backgrounds and industries will push me to think globally and consider perspectives I might not encounter in Pakistan. This diversity of thought is crucial for developing the kind of strategic mindset that senior leaders need today.

After graduation, my goal is to secure a position as a Regional Manager or Category Director at a major CPG company. In five years, I want to be someone who is known for driving innovation, developing talent, and delivering business results. I want to lead teams that are motivated and engaged, and I want to contribute to organizational strategy at a meaningful level.

I am also interested in eventually leveraging my experience to make a difference in emerging markets. Pakistan has a growing consumer goods industry, and I believe I can play a role in its development. Whether this means eventually returning to Pakistan or working for a multinational company operating in South Asia, I want to contribute to business growth in my region.

Vlerick's strong alumni network and connections within the European business community will be instrumental in building the professional relationships I need to advance my career. The school's reputation for producing effective general managers is well-known in the industry, and I am confident that a degree from Vlerick will open doors for me both in Europe and beyond.

In conclusion, the Master in General Management at Vlerick is the right program for me at this stage of my career. It will provide the business education I need to transition from a functional specialist to a strategic leader. Five years after graduation, I envision myself leading a successful team, driving business impact, and preparing for even greater responsibilities. I am committed to making the most of the Vlerick experience and contributing positively to the cohort and the school's community.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
Dear Admissions Committee,

I am writing to express my strong interest in Durham University Business School's MSc Management program. Over the past two years working in e-commerce, I've come to realize that my technical knowledge of operations and supply chain is incomplete without the broader strategic framework an MBA provides. Your program, with its emphasis on both core business disciplines and practical application, aligns perfectly with where I need to be in my career.

My professional journey began at Daraz Pakistan, where I started as an Operations Analyst in the warehouse management division. In my first year, I helped optimize our Lahore fulfillment center's sorting process, reducing average processing time from 8 hours to 5.5 hours through process mapping and staff retraining. This wasn't groundbreaking, but it showed me how operational improvements directly impact customer satisfaction and costs. The experience gave me confidence that I could drive results, but also exposed my limitations.

I was promoted to Senior Operations Coordinator managing a team of twelve last year. Almost immediately, I made a mistake. I implemented a new inventory categorization system without properly consulting the warehouse floor team. It looked good on paper—I'd benchmarked it against three competitors—but the execution was clumsy. We lost two weeks to confusion and nearly missed our peak season targets. My manager pulled me aside and asked why I hadn't involved the people who actually lived with the system daily. The honest answer was that I didn't know how to lead that kind of collaborative planning. I could analyze data and identify problems, but I couldn't navigate the human side of change management.

That failure pushed me to seek better tools. I read Kotter on change management and watched case studies of failed implementations. But I realized quickly that reading about leadership isn't the same as developing it. I need structured learning from experienced faculty and peers who've navigated similar situations. I also need to understand the financial and strategic dimensions of decisions I was making intuitively. Why should we invest in automation over hiring? How do we calculate the true cost of inventory holding? What are the tax implications of different supply chain structures? These questions go beyond operations—they require business acumen I simply don't have yet.

Durham's MSc Management program appeals to me for several reasons. The core curriculum—finance, organizational behavior, strategy—hits the gaps I've identified. I'm particularly drawn to the Operations and Supply Chain electives, where I can deepen my technical knowledge while learning to align it with broader business objectives. The emphasis on case-based learning mirrors real decision-making, which matters more to me than pure theory.

I'm also drawn to Durham's location and culture. Studying in the UK will expose me to European business practices and regulatory environments. Given that Daraz operates across South Asia and has discussed expansion into European markets, this international perspective feels directly relevant to my trajectory. I'm hoping to return to Pakistan after my degree in a more senior role, potentially leading supply chain strategy rather than just operations.

I recognize my profile isn't exceptional by traditional metrics. My GMAT score of 705 reflects solid quantitative skills but not genius-level testing ability. My two years of experience is relatively modest. But I also know that I'm self-aware about what I don't know, I learn from failures, and I'm committed to the unglamorous work of building real expertise. The MSc Management program feels like the right next step, not a box to check.

I'm excited about the possibility of joining Durham's cohort and bringing my emerging operational experience to classroom discussions while absorbing the strategic thinking I'm still building.

Thank you for considering my application.

Sincerely,
[Applicant Name]

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
Two years ago, I discovered our largest retail partner in Pakistan was systematically underreporting sales volumes to avoid tiered pricing agreements. We're talking about nearly $2 million in unaccounted product annually. My team had stumbled on it during a routine audit—discrepancies between distributor records and POS data that didn't add up.

The obvious move was to report it up the chain and let legal handle it. That's what the compliance manual said to do. But I knew what would actually happen: we'd terminate the relationship, lose 18% of our regional revenue, and probably trigger a lawsuit that would consume the next two years. I also knew the partner's finance director—a guy named Asad who'd been honest with me for five years and had genuinely believed his accounting team was handling things correctly. He was in his mid-50s, approaching his daughter's wedding.

I went to him first, which was technically the wrong move.

I showed him the numbers and gave him three options: come clean to our auditors, restate their records, and we'd work out a settlement; involve our legal teams and escalate it; or he could deny everything and we'd go through formal dispute resolution. I wasn't trying to be noble. I was trying to figure out what was actually right, not just what was procedurally safe.

Asad was quiet for a long time. He told me his accounting manager had been padding numbers to hit bonus targets without his knowledge. He was furious and embarrassed. We talked for two hours. He asked if I could give him two weeks to inform his board and sort it internally before escalating to us.

I said no. I told him I needed to inform my CFO by end of week, but I'd frame it as a joint discovery and a willingness to work together on resolution rather than litigation. I also said—and this felt important—that I didn't think he was the villain here. The system that incentivized people to cheat was the problem.

I reported it to my CFO on Thursday. By Friday, our teams were working together on a restatement. Asad's company paid back the $1.8 million in outstanding amounts plus a penalty. We kept the relationship intact. Their accounting manager was fired, and they restructured their bonus system. Ours too, actually. We realized our pricing model was essentially designed to reward dishonesty.

Was it the right call? I genuinely don't know. What I do know is that I couldn't pretend I was protecting the company by following procedures that would have destroyed a relationship and probably generated more legal liability. I also couldn't pretend I didn't know Asad as a person.

At CUHK, I want to study how companies can build ethical systems that don't rely on individuals having a come-to-Jesus moment in an audit. That's not sustainable. I've spent eight years in CPG watching good people make bad choices because the incentives point that direction. That's the problem I want to solve.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
When I joined SãoPaulo Retail Co. in 2016, the company was hemorrhaging customers to digital-native competitors. Our leadership had one response: copy everything Amazon was doing. Build a bigger website. Offer faster shipping. Match their prices. I was working in operations, managing our five distribution centers, and I kept asking questions nobody wanted to hear: Why are we trying to beat Amazon at their own game? What do we actually have that they don't?

For two years, I watched us pour R$40 million into infrastructure that still couldn't compete on speed or scale. Our margins were collapsing. Then I got frustrated enough to do something about it.

I started spending time on the warehouse floor, talking to our pickers and packers—people who'd been there for 5+ years. They told me something interesting: customers kept calling asking if they could inspect products before buying. Not just clothing, but appliances, furniture, everything. Online reviews weren't enough. We were trying to solve for convenience when our actual advantage was trust and tangibility.

I wrote a proposal that essentially said: stop trying to be Amazon. Instead, become the anti-Amazon. Create a hybrid model where customers can order online but pick up and inspect items at our physical stores. We'd staff stores differently—fewer cashiers, more product specialists. Sellers could set up small showrooms inside our locations. It sounds obvious now, but in 2018, this was considered backwards.

The CFO rejected it. The CEO said we were already too far down the digital path. But I had support from our head of retail, and we got approval to pilot it in two stores in Rio. I managed the rollout myself—negotiating with mall owners, redesigning 2,000 square meters of floor space, training fifty staff members on a completely different sales approach.

Within four months, those two stores generated 35% higher revenue per square meter than our traditional locations. More importantly, our customer retention increased by 18%. We weren't competing on price anymore. We were competing on experience.

We scaled to twelve stores. Then the pandemic hit, and those physical locations became a liability overnight. But the underlying insight—that we had a real advantage if we leaned into what made us different—changed how leadership thought about strategy. We're now testing a B2B wholesale model leveraging our distribution network, something we never would have considered if we'd kept chasing Amazon.

I learned that innovation isn't always about doing something entirely new. Sometimes it's about having the courage to say no to what everyone else is doing and building something that actually fits who you are. That lesson will matter in business school and beyond.

prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
# Professional Setback Essay

I was the only one in my cohort not staffed on the Africa expansion project.

That stung more than I expected. McKinsey had just launched a major initiative across the continent, and everyone around me was preparing for three-month client deployments. I'd been at the firm for two years, consistently earned solid reviews, and assumed I'd be one of the first names called. Instead, I watched colleagues pack their bags while I remained in Stockholm on smaller Nordic accounts.

I spiraled a bit, if I'm honest. I told myself the decision was arbitrary, that it reflected politics rather than capability. I considered leaving the firm. I definitely complained to my friends, who were tired of hearing about it by week two. But somewhere around day ten of sulking, I realized I was doing what I always do when I don't get what I want: I was making excuses instead of doing the harder thing, which is looking at myself.

So I scheduled a coffee with my project leader, Petra, and asked her directly why I wasn't on the list.

Her answer wasn't what I expected. She said I had good analytical skills and strong client instincts, but my communication—particularly in large team settings—was inconsistent. She told me I sometimes shut down in meetings, that I'd contributed interesting ideas but rarely pushed back when others disagreed, and that the Africa team needed people who could hold their own in complex discussions. "You're easy to work with," she said, "but sometimes too easy. They need people who own their perspective."

I wanted to argue. I'm Swedish; directness isn't my cultural default. I'm also non-binary in a fairly homogeneous industry, and I'd learned early on that standing out too much meant standing out in uncomfortable ways. Keeping my head down felt safer. But Petra was right, and the discomfort of hearing it was actually the useful part.

I spent the next six months deliberately changing how I showed up. I started preparing one substantive opinion before every meeting, whether I felt confident or not. I volunteered for smaller presentations first—pitches to internal stakeholders, not clients—where I could practice defending a position without the same stakes. I noticed when I was staying silent and asked myself why. Sometimes the answer was "good call, let it go." Often it was fear, which was useful information.

By month four, a client project gave me the chance to lead a workstream. It was a mid-market reorganization in Copenhagen, nothing glamorous, but I owned the diagnostic phase. I pushed back on the client's initial assumptions. I disagreed with a partner in front of the room and had to defend why. My hands shook, but I did it.

Three months later, I was staffed on a follow-on engagement in South Africa.

The Africa experience was meaningful, but honestly, the bigger gain was understanding how my own hesitation had been limiting me. I spent the years before MBA applications thinking I was constrained by external factors—the firm structure, my cultural background, the consulting monoculture. Some of that was real. But a lot of it was me choosing safety over growth, and then calling it strategy.

I came to MBA programs because I want to work differently. I want to build things rather than optimize them, lead teams rather than support them, and do that in places where my perspective actually matters. I know I won't be the loudest person in the room at IAE, but I'm no longer interested in being invisible in it either. That coffee with Petra was the start of believing I actually have something to contribute.

school_name: ICN Business School Master in Management
prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
I've spent most of my career fixing things that other people had decided were broken beyond repair. Not machines—people.

In 2018, I joined Carraro SpA's manufacturing division in Padova as a production supervisor. The facility was hemorrhaging talent. Annual turnover was 34%, absenteeism hit 18% on Mondays, and morale was so low that people stopped showing up to safety briefings. The previous supervisor had relied on enforcement—stricter schedules, public callouts for mistakes, threats of dismissal. It had made everything worse.

My instinct was to do the opposite. I started eating lunch on the production floor instead of in the office. I learned the names of everyone's kids. I asked operators what they actually wanted from their jobs instead of assuming I knew. Most said the same thing: they felt invisible. Carraro treated them as interchangeable units, not people with ambitions, problems, capabilities.

So I pushed back on some things my management didn't want to hear. I proposed letting operators choose their shift preferences within operational constraints. I created a skills development program where people could learn new machines and get certified, unlocking higher pay grades. I insisted we publicly acknowledge good work—literally printing photos of operators with their monthly targets met and posting them in the break room. My boss thought this was soft.

Six months in, turnover dropped to 12%. Absenteeism fell to 8%. But the real change was less quantifiable. People started staying late voluntarily to help troubleshoot quality issues. Operators trained new hires without being asked. We implemented three process improvements that came from the floor—suggestions that would have been ignored two years earlier.

Here's where I had to be brave, which is another word for stubborn: I kept advocating for things that didn't fit the typical manufacturing playbook. When the company wanted to cut training budgets during a market downturn, I fought for it. When HR proposed reverting to the old shift system for "efficiency," I presented data showing that flexibility had actually improved punctuality. I wasn't always right—sometimes I pushed too hard and had to backtrack—but I stayed consistent about the principle: people perform when they're treated with dignity.

By 2022, that facility had become a model site. We won the company's operational excellence award. More importantly, I'd learned something about leadership that I couldn't have understood from a textbook: it requires giving up control to gain it. The best results came when I stopped trying to drive outcomes and started creating conditions where people could drive them themselves.

Kellogg's purpose to "educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders" resonates with me because I've seen what happens when leaders choose courage over convenience. I want to deepen my understanding of how to scale these principles—how to build systems and cultures that develop brave leaders at every level, not just at the top. An MBA will give me the frameworks to articulate what I've learned intuitively and the toolkit to apply it across industries and contexts. I'm ready for that next chapter.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
What matters most to me is the ability to build something that lasts—not just in concrete and square meters, but in the relationships and trust I create along the way.

I've spent the last decade in real estate development in Madrid, moving from project manager to development director at a mid-sized firm. On the surface, my job is about land acquisition, financing, and construction timelines. But what actually keeps me up at night is something different: how do I lead teams through uncertainty, make decisions that balance profit with responsibility, and create spaces where people want to live?

Three years ago, I made a choice that clarified this for me. Our company was developing a residential project in a less affluent neighborhood outside Madrid. My CFO pushed hard to cut corners on materials and increase density to maximize returns. The numbers made sense. But I'd spent months talking to residents about what they needed, and I knew cheap finishes would mean the building would deteriorate in five years. I pushed back, and we lost roughly €200k in margin by using better materials and creating more green space.

That project now has a waiting list. More importantly, residents talk about it differently than they talk about other developments. My team fought for something, and it mattered.

But here's the honest part: I'm not naturally good at everything this requires. I'm a decent numbers person, but I struggle with patience in negotiations. I can see the long-term vision but sometimes miss the operational details. And I know my limitations are holding me back. At 32, I'm managing €50 million in projects, but I sense there's a level of strategic thinking and influence I haven't yet developed.

That's why an Executive MBA appeals to me now. I don't need an MBA to move up—I could probably get promoted without it. I need it to understand what I don't know, to learn from people who've navigated similar questions about impact and profit, and to sharpen the parts of myself that are still rough.

Stellenbosch particularly interests me because of your emphasis on responsible leadership and African context. Spain and Africa face different challenges, but they share something: the need for development that actually serves people, not just investors. I want to spend two years thinking seriously about that tension, and I want to do it somewhere that won't let me hide behind spreadsheets.

school_name: University of Porto FEP MSc Management
prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
# How I Plan to Contribute to Classroom Discussions and Student Life

I believe that education is about more than just learning theory—it is about building a community where different perspectives come together to create meaningful conversations. Throughout my ten years in the energy sector, I have learned the value of diverse viewpoints, and I am excited to bring this mindset to the University of Porto FEP classroom.

In my current role, I work with teams across multiple countries and backgrounds. This experience has taught me that good discussions require active listening and respect for different opinions. I plan to contribute to classroom conversations by sharing real-world examples from my work experience and asking thoughtful questions that push my classmates to think deeper about course material. I believe peer learning is just as important as professor-led instruction, and I want to be someone who elevates those conversations.

Beyond the classroom, I hope to get involved in student clubs and events. I enjoy meeting new people and learning about different cultures. Portugal is a new country for me, so I am eager to explore the local community and share my perspectives as someone from the Middle East. I think cultural exchange is important in today's global world, and I would like to participate in activities that bring students together from various backgrounds.

I also understand the importance of supporting my peers. In my experience, many people struggle with the transition back to school after working for several years. I would be happy to mentor younger students or classmates who are navigating this change, as I have been through it myself. Additionally, I am willing to take on leadership roles in group projects and help organize study groups if there is interest among my classmates.

My goal is to be a positive presence both inside and outside the classroom. I recognize that I may not be the strongest student academically, but I believe my maturity, work experience, and genuine interest in connecting with others will allow me to make valuable contributions to student life at FEP. I am committed to giving back to the program and the community, and I look forward to making the most of this opportunity.

prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
I was the senior manager leading a $4.2M transformation project for a retail client in Tokyo. We were restructuring their supply chain across 47 stores. Halfway through, I realized we were about to recommend something that would work beautifully on paper but would devastate the warehouse staff—people I'd actually spent time with, understanding their routines, their constraints.

I had to tell my engagement manager, our partner-in-charge, and ultimately the client that we needed to slow down and revisit our recommendations. For a consulting project already eight weeks in, this was uncomfortable. Cost overruns were real. The team was tired. But proceeding would have meant implementing changes that workers couldn't realistically execute, which meant the whole thing would fail.

The partner's first reaction was skeptical. "We have three weeks to finalize the plan," he said. I remember standing in that conference room in Iidabashi, saying something like, "Yes, and we can do that. But not with this design." I had to show him that the margin we thought we'd built was illusory—that we were seeing what we wanted to see in the data, not what was actually there.

We went back to the warehouse. I had my team walk the night shift, talk to the team leads about seasonal demand patterns we'd overlooked. What we found was that the original plan required workers to process 18% more volume per hour than the current peak load. Impossible. We redesigned it over ten days—a leaner warehouse footprint, yes, but with realistic handling volumes and a phased rollout instead of a hard cutover.

The client actually appreciated this. Not initially—the revised plan cost an additional $180K in consulting fees. But three months into implementation, adoption was 94% instead of the 60% we'd probably have seen otherwise. They've asked us back for the next phase.

What I learned wasn't a nice abstraction about "listening to stakeholders." It was more uncomfortable than that: I learned that I have a bias toward elegant solutions, toward being the person in the room who solved the puzzle. I was willing to gloss over how a solution would actually feel to the people executing it. I was optimizing for my professional credibility rather than client success.

At Aalto, I want to understand the human side of operations and strategy more deeply. The program's emphasis on organizational behavior alongside P&L logic appeals to me specifically because I know I need to build this muscle more systematically. I'm looking for classmates and faculty who will push me to account for the human dimension from the beginning of problem-solving, not as an afterthought when something breaks.

This setback taught me that the best transformations aren't the ones that look best in PowerPoint. They're the ones that actually work.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
Leadership has always been important to me, and I believe that being a good leader means inspiring others to achieve their goals. Throughout my ten years working in tech, I have had many opportunities to lead teams and develop my own management philosophy.

One experience that shaped my thinking was when I was asked to lead a team at my startup. This was a challenging time because the team was not performing well and morale was low. I knew I needed to make changes to turn things around. I decided to focus on communication and transparency, as I believe these are key to any successful organization.

I started having weekly meetings with my team members to discuss their concerns and ideas. I also made sure to give them feedback on their work and recognize their achievements. This helped create a more positive environment where people felt valued. Over time, I noticed that the team's productivity improved and people seemed happier coming to work.

Through this experience, I learned that leadership is not just about telling people what to do. It's about listening to them and understanding their needs. I also learned that being a good leader means being vulnerable and admitting when you don't have all the answers. This has become central to my management philosophy.

My approach now is to focus on creating an inclusive workplace where everyone feels heard and respected. I believe that diversity of thought leads to better solutions, and as someone from Nigeria who identifies as non-binary, I understand firsthand how important it is to feel included. I want to create teams where people from different backgrounds can thrive.

I also believe in empowering my team members to take ownership of their work. Rather than micromanaging, I prefer to set clear goals and give people the autonomy to figure out how to achieve them. This builds trust and helps people develop their own leadership skills.

At Nagoya University of Commerce, I hope to deepen my understanding of management philosophy and learn new approaches to leadership. The MiM program seems like a great fit for my goals because it will give me the opportunity to study business in an international context. I am excited to learn from professors and classmates from different countries and backgrounds.

My ten years in tech have taught me a lot, but I know there is still much to learn. I am committed to becoming an even better leader and manager, and I believe that pursuing a Master's in Management will help me achieve this goal. I am eager to contribute my experiences and perspectives to the Nagoya community.

school_name: EM Lyon Business School MSc in Management
prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
My most significant accomplishment isn't one of the obvious ones—it's not closing a $50 million development deal or getting promoted ahead of schedule. It's what happened after I made a colossal mistake.

Three years ago, I was a junior analyst at Westmount Properties in Montreal. We were competing for a mixed-use project in the Griffintown redevelopment zone—a 120,000 square-foot site that would anchor our commercial portfolio. I was tasked with running the financial models for the proposal, and I was eager to prove myself. The deadline was tight, and I worked nights for two weeks straight, convinced that effort alone would compensate for my inexperience.

It didn't. I made an error in my yield assumptions—I'd used comparable market data from 2019 instead of 2023, inflating our projected returns by nearly 8%. The senior team, trusting my numbers, pitched the project to investors with these flawed projections. We won the bid.

Then reality crashed in. Within two months of breaking ground, our leasing velocity fell short. The market had shifted. Our financial cushion, which my models said was comfortable, turned out to be non-existent. My mistake was costing the firm real money—and it was publicly visible within the company.

I remember sitting in my director's office, expecting to be reassigned or worse. Instead, he asked me what I'd learned. I gave him the obvious answer at first—be more careful, check your assumptions. He pushed back: "What will you actually do differently?"

That question changed how I approach my work. Rather than internalizing this as just a cautionary tale, I spent the next six months building a systematic peer-review process for all quantitative analysis in our department. I created templates that forced people to document data sources, explicitly state assumptions, and flag sensitivity ranges. I didn't do this to rehabilitate my reputation—though I won't pretend that wasn't partly true. I did it because I realized that one person's blind spot shouldn't be able to derail a project.

What matters most about this accomplishment is that it taught me the difference between being smart and being rigorous. My GMAT scores and academic background had always made certain things feel easy. But professional rigor—catching your own mistakes, thinking through unintended consequences, building systems that help others avoid your failures—that takes something harder. That takes actual character.

The peer-review process is still in use at Westmount. I know this sounds small, but last year it caught a critical error in a commercial lease analysis that would have cost us $2.3 million in overvalued revenue projections. The analyst who made that mistake wasn't embarrassed; he was grateful. That's what I'm proud of—not that I was smart enough to eventually fix my problem, but that I was thoughtful enough to create something that helps everyone.

This is why I'm pursuing the MSc in Management at EM Lyon. I want to deepen my understanding of how organizations function, how systems prevent failure, and how to lead teams through complexity. Real estate has been an excellent teacher, but I'm ready to expand my perspective beyond one industry. A rigorous, internationally-focused program will help me develop the leadership maturity to move into strategy roles where I can shape how firms make decisions. I want to build organizations, not just buildings.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
Why Insper MBA

prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
# Failure and Learning

Failure is an important part of life. Everyone experiences it at some point, and I am no exception. In my career, I have learned that failure can be a valuable teacher if we are willing to listen to what it has to say.

One significant failure in my professional life occurred about three years ago when I was working in the family business. We had a product that we believed would be very successful in the market. I was confident about this product, and I convinced my family members to invest significant resources into its development and marketing.

Unfortunately, the product did not perform as expected. Sales were lower than our projections, and we lost money on the investment. This was a difficult time for me because I had encouraged my family to pursue this direction, and I felt responsible for the failure. My confidence had not been justified by the results.

This experience taught me several important lessons. First, I learned that confidence alone is not enough. You need to back up your ideas with research and data. Before recommending a major investment, I should have conducted more thorough market research and consulted with experts who could have provided valuable insights.

Second, I learned the importance of listening to different perspectives. Some family members had expressed concerns about the product, but I did not listen carefully enough to their doubts. I was too focused on my own vision. If I had been more open to hearing their concerns and discussing them seriously, perhaps we could have identified potential problems before investing so much money.

Third, this failure made me realize that making decisions in a family business context is complex. The stakes are not just financial—they affect relationships and family dynamics. I needed to develop better judgment about when to push forward with an idea and when to step back and reconsider.

After this experience, I became more careful and thoughtful in my approach to business decisions. I started to seek input from people with different expertise and perspectives. I learned to analyze data more critically rather than relying on intuition. I also became more humble about my own knowledge and abilities.

This failure was painful, but it has made me a better professional. It showed me that I need to balance confidence with humility, vision with data analysis, and personal conviction with openness to other viewpoints. These are skills that I believe will be essential for success in business school and in my future career.

Going forward, I am committed to continuing to learn from my failures and mistakes. I know that I still have much to learn, and I hope that studying at Korea University Business School will provide me with the knowledge and tools to make better decisions in the future.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
When I think about challenging conventional thinking, I immediately recall my experience at Apex Capital Partners, where I worked in the investment analysis team. During my two years there, I became passionate about finding new ways to approach deal evaluation and create value for our portfolio companies.

One particular situation stands out. Our firm was looking at a potential acquisition in the manufacturing sector. The traditional approach would have been to focus primarily on EBITDA multiples and comparable company analysis, which is what most firms in private equity do. However, I believed there was a better way to think about this opportunity.

I proposed that we should also consider the operational efficiency metrics of the target company in greater detail. Rather than just looking at the numbers on the spreadsheet, I suggested we conduct a more thorough analysis of the company's supply chain, production processes, and workforce management. This was different from how deals were typically evaluated at our firm.

My colleagues were skeptical at first. The conventional wisdom in private equity is that you identify targets with strong fundamentals and then improve them post-acquisition. But I pushed back and argued that understanding operational bottlenecks before closing could help us identify which value creation levers would be most effective. I created a framework that incorporated operational metrics alongside financial metrics.

The partner overseeing the deal gave me the opportunity to present my framework to the investment committee. I prepared a detailed presentation showing how this approach could potentially unlock an additional 15-20% in value creation through operational improvements. The committee found this analysis compelling and decided to pursue the deal using this enhanced evaluation methodology.

In the end, the deal closed successfully, and the operational improvements I had identified became central to the value creation strategy. The company improved its production efficiency by 18% in the first year post-acquisition, which directly contributed to EBITDA growth. This success led to my team incorporating this approach into future deals.

What I learned from this experience is that challenging the status quo requires both courage and preparation. I had to be willing to speak up, but I also had to back my ideas with solid analysis. Not every unconventional idea is good, but not every conventional approach is optimal either.

This experience has shaped how I approach problems. I want to continue developing this skill of questioning assumptions while remaining grounded in analytical rigor. An MBA from Nirma would help me strengthen my strategic thinking and learn frameworks for innovation that I can apply throughout my career in finance and beyond.

I believe that the business world needs people who are willing to challenge conventional thinking, and I see myself as someone who can do this effectively. I'm excited about the opportunity to study at Nirma and further develop as a business leader who can drive meaningful innovation.

prompt: Imagine yourself at the completion of your MBA journey. Why was pursuing your MBA at Texas McCombs the right decision for your personal and professional growth? Reflect on how you made the most of your time in the program—academically, through hands-on learning opportunities, and within the McCombs community. Highlight how you contributed to the McCombs community and used its resources to grow personally and professionally. Consider sharing how you applied what you learned in the program to your work.
content:
Looking back at my time in the program, I realize the McCombs Executive MBA wasn't just about acquiring new skills—it forced me to confront how I define success in my career.

Nine years in consulting at McKinsey taught me to optimize for the next promotion, the next case win. But by year eight, I was running strategy projects for Fortune 500 clients while feeling disconnected from the actual impact. The program gave me space to ask harder questions: What kind of leader do I want to be? How do I build organizations where people like me—non-binary professionals navigating corporate structures that weren't designed with us in mind—can actually thrive?

The Operations Management course was where this crystallized. Working on a capstone project analyzing supply chain inefficiencies at a regional manufacturing company, I realized I wanted to move toward operational leadership where decisions ripple through entire organizations, not just boardrooms. I started applying frameworks from class directly to how my consulting firm structured project teams, even pushing back on staffing decisions that perpetuated homogeneity.

What distinguished McCombs for me was the cohort model. Thirty professionals from different industries meant I wasn't surrounded by consulting clones reinforcing the same assumptions. A peer from healthcare challenged how I framed "efficiency" in a way that a roomful of McKinsey colleagues never would have. Those conversations—uncomfortable at times—mattered more than any case study.

I also took on a peer mentorship role, specifically working with younger consultants navigating identity in a traditional industry. It wasn't glamorous, but it felt like the work I actually want to do.

The MBA didn't redirect my career path dramatically. It redirected me toward myself.

school_name: Montpellier Business School Master in Management
prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
# Career Progression Essay

Since I started my professional career six years ago, I have been on an exciting journey in the world of entrepreneurship. I am passionate about building businesses and creating value through innovation. My career has been defined by a strong desire to make a difference in the business world.

I began my career as a junior associate at a consulting firm in Madrid, where I worked for two years. During this time, I developed key skills in business analysis, project management, and client relations. I learned how to work effectively in teams and how to deliver results under pressure. This experience was very important for my professional development.

After consulting, I decided to pursue my entrepreneurial dreams and started my first venture. This was a technology startup focused on the e-commerce sector. We had a team of five people, and we worked very hard to establish our presence in the market. The startup operated for approximately three years, and although we faced many challenges, it was an incredible learning experience. I learned about the difficulties of running a business, managing finances, and dealing with market competition. Even though the company did not ultimately succeed, I gained invaluable insights into what it takes to be an entrepreneur.

Following this experience, I joined another startup as a business development manager. In this role, I was responsible for expanding our client base and identifying new market opportunities. I worked in this position for one year and helped the company grow significantly. I was focused on achieving our business objectives and working with different departments to ensure success.

Most recently, I have been working on developing my own ideas and exploring potential business opportunities. I am currently in a transitional phase where I am considering what direction my career should take next. I believe that an MBA from Montpellier Business School would be a logical next step for me. The program will help me develop the strategic and financial skills I need to become a more effective business leader.

Throughout my six years of work experience, I have developed strong entrepreneurial instincts and a deep passion for business. I have learned that success requires dedication, hard work, and the ability to adapt to changing circumstances. I am excited about the opportunity to pursue my MBA and continue my professional growth. I believe that Montpellier Business School is an excellent institution that can help me achieve my goals and prepare me for future challenges in the business world.

An MBA will allow me to consolidate my experience and gain new perspectives on business strategy and management. I am confident that this program will be a valuable investment in my future.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# Challenging the Roadmap

When I joined Vertex as a Product Manager in 2019, our enterprise clients were churning at 18% annually—a figure the executive team had basically accepted as "industry standard." They'd built the entire go-to-market strategy around it: invest heavily in customer acquisition, expect 5-6 years of revenue per customer, move on. The unit economics worked on paper.

But I kept noticing something odd. Our customers weren't leaving because the product was bad. They were leaving because they'd solved their initial problem and couldn't figure out what to do next.

I spent a month doing something no one had really done: I actually called departed customers and asked why they left. Not a satisfaction survey—proper conversations. One CFO told me, "Your tool got us from chaos to stable. But now we don't know how to scale it without hiring ten more people." Another said they'd moved to a competitor because that competitor's onboarding actually taught them how to use advanced features.

The conventional wisdom at Vertex was: "Our product is for small teams. Once companies grow, they naturally outgrow us." This narrative was embedded everywhere—our pricing tiers capped at 100 users, our training was minimal, our in-app guidance was sparse. We were essentially designing for churn.

I proposed something controversial: what if we treated customer success as a growth lever instead of a cost center? What if we built features that *enabled* our users to scale rather than just sold them the base product? I got pushback immediately. The VP of Sales said we'd cannibalize upsells. Finance wanted to cut support headcount, not expand it. The narrative was too comfortable to challenge.

I didn't ask for permission. I partnered with three of our most valuable customers and built an internal pilot: enhanced onboarding modules, a feature adoption playbook, and quarterly success reviews. Cost to run it: £80K over six months. Potential upside: keeping customers we'd otherwise lose.

Six months later, our pilot customers had expanded usage to 340 users on average—up from 78. More importantly, zero of them were at risk of churning. We'd actually increased their contract values by 42% through better feature adoption, not aggressive upselling.

That became the foundation of our new customer success program. Over the next two years, churn dropped to 6%. Revenue per customer nearly tripled. We weren't reinventing the product—we were just stopping ourselves from designing our customers toward the door.

What I learned wasn't just about product strategy. It was that "the way things are done" is often just inertia. At Ivey, I want to challenge this instinct in myself and others—to ask harder questions before accepting conventional wisdom as truth. That intellectual restlessness is what will make me valuable in whatever industry I move into next.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
Three years into teaching high school economics in South Jersey, I faced a choice that I couldn't talk my way around.

Our district implemented a new standardized test prep curriculum that consumed about 40% of class time. The materials were comprehensive and test-focused, designed to boost our school's numbers, which had slipped below state averages. My principal made it clear: use the curriculum as written, or face performance reviews reflecting "insufficient alignment with district priorities." I'd been teaching for three years, had a good reputation, and wanted to stay.

But the curriculum was hollowing out the actual learning. When we got to the 2008 financial crisis unit, I had maybe 45 minutes to cover it, most of which was dedicated to multiple-choice formats rather than analyzing real decisions, competing incentives, or the moral dimensions of risk-taking. My students could identify a liquidity crisis but couldn't articulate why it mattered or who paid the price.

I spent a week wrestling with this. The easy path was compliance. Our test scores would improve, I'd stay in good standing, and I could tell myself that teaching is about pragmatism. But I kept thinking about the students I was short-changing—particularly the ones who didn't have parents who could supplement their education with dinner table conversations about economics.

So I asked my principal for a meeting. I didn't come with complaints; I came with a proposal. I offered to maintain all the test prep benchmarks but to restructure the sequencing: front-load the curriculum heavy, which meant hitting 80% of test prep in the first semester, then use the second semester more flexibly. Scores wouldn't suffer. I'd have time to do real case study work on financial crises, recession policy, and the choices that real people—including low-income families—faced.

He pushed back initially. But I showed him the data from another district that had tried a similar approach. More importantly, I committed to being accountable: if our test scores dipped, we'd revert to full scripted curriculum. They didn't dip. They rose 8%.

That experience taught me something I didn't expect: ethical problems at work aren't usually binary. They're usually about finding ways to align your principles with institutional constraints. That skill—seeing past false choices—is exactly why I need an MBA. I want to move into education policy and administration, where these decisions happen at scale.

school_name: University of Amsterdam Business School MSc
prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
Last year, I had to tell my co-founder we couldn't hire his brother.

We were twelve months into building our e-commerce logistics platform in Riyadh, and we'd just closed a Series A round of $2.3 million. My co-founder, Ahmed, wanted to bring his brother Khalid into the operations team. On paper, it made sense—Khalid had relevant experience, we were understaffed, and Ahmed argued that family connections meant Khalid would be fully committed. For Ahmed personally, this was important. He'd been telling Khalid about the company for months, painting a vision of what we were building. There was genuine excitement there.

But I couldn't say yes.

Our investors—three Saudi venture funds and one from Dubai—had made it clear during due diligence that governance and hiring practices were key concerns. They'd seen enough local startups collapse because of nepotism decisions that created resentment, tanked culture, and eventually alienated talent. One investor, Faisal from Jadwa Venture Capital, had explicitly flagged this in our term sheet conversations. Beyond that, our two strongest product engineers had recently joined us partly because they'd worked at a company that had imploded after the founder hired underqualified family members into leadership roles. They were vocal about wanting a meritocratic environment. And then there was Khalid himself—I'd actually worked with him years ago in a consulting context, and while he was capable, he wasn't the operations person we needed. We needed someone with supply chain experience, and Khalid's background was more general management.

I sat with Ahmed for nearly two hours over coffee near the Olaya district. I didn't lead with "no." I walked through what we actually needed in that role: specific expertise in freight optimization, which Ahmed himself understood we didn't have internally. I acknowledged that this was about more than just skills—that I understood the personal disappointment, that I was asking something genuinely hard of him. I showed him the investor feedback (anonymized) about governance concerns. I made clear that if Khalid had the right experience, the answer would be different, but that wasn't the case here.

Ahmed pushed back initially. He said I was overcomplicating it, that Khalid would learn on the job, that family loyalty mattered. At one point, he said, "You're not even from here. You don't understand how business works in Saudi Arabia."

That stung, but it also clarified something for me. He was right that I needed to understand the cultural context we were operating in—family networks genuinely matter here, and dismissing that would have been tone-deaf. But he was wrong that cultural context meant abandoning our own standards. I told him that: "I respect that this matters to you culturally, and I hear you. But what I'm not going to do is compromise the company's integrity because I'm afraid of being seen as culturally insensitive. That's actually the easier path, and it would hurt us both in the long run."

Two weeks later, Ahmed and I went to dinner with a talented operations manager we'd been courting—she had seven years in logistics and was considering another offer. We hired her, and Ahmed was part of the process. He saw how rigorously we evaluated her experience and how seriously we took fit. He also, I think, felt respected in the decision-making process rather than overruled.

Six months later, Ahmed told me that watching us hire right had changed his thinking. His brother had ended up joining a different startup, one where he actually was a better fit for the role.

That decision taught me something crucial about stakeholder management: sometimes honoring different interests doesn't mean splitting the difference. It means understanding what each person actually needs—not just what they're asking for—and being willing to have uncomfortable conversations about why. For Ahmed, what he needed wasn't his brother hired; it was to feel heard and to see that the company's standards mattered.

It's a lesson I'll carry into Amsterdam and into whatever I build next.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
What matters most to me is making a positive impact on society while building a successful career in finance. Growing up in Peru, I witnessed firsthand how lack of access to capital limited opportunities for talented entrepreneurs and business leaders. This experience shaped my values and my desire to work in investment banking, where I could help companies grow and create jobs.

Throughout my eight years in banking, I have worked with diverse teams and clients across multiple sectors. I have learned that success in finance requires not just analytical skills but also emotional intelligence and the ability to connect with people from different backgrounds. These experiences have taught me that what truly matters is using your position and platform to help others succeed.

I became interested in pursuing an MBA because I want to deepen my understanding of business strategy and leadership. In banking, I have focused primarily on execution and client management, but I feel I lack a broader perspective on how to lead organizations and drive meaningful change. An MBA from Bradford would give me the tools and knowledge to transition into a leadership role where I can have greater impact.

The financial industry can sometimes feel disconnected from real human needs. I have seen deals that were profitable but had negative social implications, and I want to be the kind of leader who thinks about stakeholders beyond shareholders. I believe business should be a force for good, and I want to work for companies that align with this philosophy.

My background as a Peruvian woman in a male-dominated industry has also shaped what matters to me. Breaking barriers and proving myself in spaces where I was underrepresented has made me passionate about supporting other women in finance and business. I would like to use my MBA to expand my network and find opportunities to mentor younger women entering the field.

What matters most to me is ultimately about legacy. I want to look back on my career and know that I made a difference—not just in terms of revenue or deals closed, but in the lives of people and communities I touched. An MBA from a respected institution like Bradford will help me develop the strategic thinking and leadership capabilities needed to achieve this vision.

I am excited about the possibility of joining the Bradford community and contributing my perspective as an international student with real-world banking experience. I believe the program would be transformative for me and would position me to become a leader who brings both financial acumen and genuine concern for human impact to whatever organization I lead.

prompt: Describe a time when you led a team through a significant challenge. What was the outcome?
content:
When I started working at my family's textile distribution company four years ago, I knew I wanted to make a real difference. Our business had been operating the same way for twenty years, and I could see that things needed to change. This is when I decided to lead my team through a major digital transformation.

Our company employed about thirty people across two locations in Tel Aviv and Ramat Gan. The main challenge we faced was that everything was done manually—orders were written in notebooks, inventory was tracked on spreadsheets, and communication between departments was slow and inefficient. I recognized that if we didn't modernize, we would fall behind our competitors. I became passionate about finding a solution that would help us grow.

I proposed implementing a new enterprise resource planning system to the management team, which included my father and uncle. They were initially resistant because they were concerned about the cost and disruption to daily operations. However, I was determined to show them that this investment would pay off in the long run. I gathered data on how other textile companies had benefited from digital transformation and presented a comprehensive business case to convince them.

Once I got approval, I assembled a team of eight people from different departments—warehouse staff, sales representatives, and administrative personnel. Most of them had never used such a system before, and they were skeptical. I organized training sessions and workshops to help everyone understand the new platform. I made sure to listen to their concerns and address them patiently. I also appointed team leaders within each department who could serve as peer mentors and help their colleagues during the transition.

The implementation took about six months, and there were definitely obstacles along the way. The software had some compatibility issues with our existing systems, and we had to bring in external consultants to help resolve these problems. There were also times when people wanted to go back to the old ways because they found the new system confusing. But I remained committed to the vision and kept the team motivated even when things got difficult.

In the end, the transformation was successful. After the system was fully operational, we saw a 35% reduction in order processing time and a 20% improvement in inventory accuracy. Our sales team could now respond faster to customer inquiries, which helped us win some new accounts. The company was able to reduce administrative costs and reinvest that money into other areas of growth.

This experience taught me that being a good leader means having a clear vision, communicating effectively, and supporting your team through change. I learned that even when facing resistance, it's important to back up your ideas with data and to bring people along on the journey rather than forcing change upon them. It also showed me the value of patience and understanding different perspectives.

I believe that an MBA from UTM would help me develop my leadership and strategic thinking skills even further so that I can take on greater responsibilities in my family business and eventually contribute to the growth and success of the organization in new ways.

prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
I learned a valuable lesson about the importance of communication and collaboration when I led a product launch that fell short of expectations at my current company.

In 2019, I was tasked with overseeing the launch of a new feature for our enterprise software platform. I was excited about the opportunity and felt confident in my ability to deliver results. However, I quickly realized that I had not communicated my vision clearly enough to the cross-functional teams involved. The engineering team was working on a different timeline than the sales team expected, and the marketing materials were not aligned with what the product actually delivered.

When launch day arrived, the feature was incomplete and the messaging was inconsistent. Our customers were confused, and we received negative feedback. The launch was ultimately unsuccessful, and I felt responsible for the failure.

Reflecting on this experience, I understood that I had made several mistakes. First, I did not establish clear communication channels or regular check-ins with all stakeholders. Second, I assumed that everyone shared my understanding of the project goals without verifying this assumption. Third, I focused too much on my own vision rather than listening to feedback from the team.

This setback taught me the importance of being a better leader. I realized that success is not just about having a good idea, but about ensuring that everyone involved understands the vision and is aligned on the path forward. I also learned that as a leader, I need to be more open to feedback and willing to adjust my approach when necessary.

In the future, if I were to face a similar situation, I would handle it differently. I would start by establishing a clear communication plan and ensuring that all stakeholders were on the same page from the beginning. I would schedule regular sync meetings to discuss progress and address any concerns. I would also make sure to listen actively to feedback from my team and be willing to adjust my approach if needed.

This experience has made me a stronger and more self-aware leader. I am now more mindful of the importance of communication and collaboration in achieving success. I believe this lesson will serve me well as I continue my career and pursue further education at Durham University Business School.

prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
When I joined Abbott's Cardiovascular division as a senior analyst in Lahore, I was 24, fresh out of university, and reporting to a director who had zero interest in mentoring anyone. Three months in, I noticed something broken in how we were handling compliance documentation for our hospital partnerships across Pakistan. It was costing us roughly 15-20% of our operational bandwidth, and hospitals were getting frustrated.

The problem was that sales, quality assurance, and hospital relations were operating completely separately. No one owned the issue because it technically fell between departments. I could have documented it, escaled it, and moved on. Instead, I decided to fix it myself—even though nobody had asked me to and I had no authority to tell anyone what to do.

I started by actually sitting down with people. I spent two weeks meeting informally with the quality lead (Fatima, who reported to a different director), the hospital liaison manager, and three hospital administrators. I just asked questions: What's frustrating you? Where are things breaking down? I didn't come with solutions. I came with curiosity, and people responded to that.

What I discovered was that hospitals needed compliance confirmation before signing any contract, but they were sending requests to sales, who didn't have the documents. Sales would ask quality, quality would ask hospitals for clarifications, and the cycle repeated. It was absurd—the documents existed, but no one had built a simple process.

I created a one-page intake form and a submission checklist. Nothing fancy. Then I did something probably annoying: I volunteered to be the person who collected requests and routed them to the right team. For six weeks, I was basically an unpaid coordinator. But I used those six weeks to prove the concept worked and to build relationships with the people actually doing the work.

Once the data showed the process was reducing turnaround time from 3-4 weeks to 5-7 days, I documented everything and presented it to my director and Fatima's director together. By then, they weren't rejecting some random analyst's proposal—they were seeing a working system that their teams had already started relying on.

The process eventually got formalized, and within six months, what I started was handed to a newly hired coordinator. That actually felt better than if I'd kept it.

I learned that authority isn't what gets things done—attention and consistency do. I learned that I could influence without a title, but only if I was willing to do unglamorous work first. And I learned that people trust you more when you ask for their input than when you tell them you have the answer.

That's why I'm drawn to Universidad Adolfo Ibáñez. I want to be in an environment that values leadership as something you build through relationships and impact, not just hierarchy. Your case-based approach and emphasis on real business problems appeals to how I actually work.

prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
When I joined AlNoor Retail as a Junior Manager in 2019, I was confident that my education and theoretical knowledge would be sufficient to succeed. I was assigned to lead a team of 12 people responsible for the online inventory management system across our three stores in Riyadh.

During my first six months, I implemented a new inventory tracking process that I had learned about in my undergraduate coursework. I believed this would increase efficiency by 40% based on case studies I had read. However, after three months of implementation, we discovered that the system was actually creating more problems than solutions. The manual data entry requirements were too high, our team was spending more time on administrative work, and customer orders were being delayed.

When my manager asked me to explain the failures, I realized I had not properly consulted with my team before rolling out the system. I had not considered the ground realities of our operations or asked the people who were actually doing the work what challenges they faced daily. The case studies had worked in different contexts, and I had failed to adapt them to our specific situation.

This was a difficult moment for me. I had to admit that my approach was wrong and that I needed to change how I worked. I decided to take a different approach. I spent two weeks meeting with each team member individually to understand their workflow, the bottlenecks they faced, and what tools would actually help them. I also consulted with the store managers about their needs.

Based on this feedback, I developed a simpler system that required less manual data entry and integrated better with our existing processes. The new system was implemented gradually with proper training and support. Within four months, we reduced inventory discrepancies by 32% and improved order fulfillment time by 25%. More importantly, my team's satisfaction increased significantly.

This experience taught me the importance of listening and collaboration. I learned that success in business is not just about having good ideas or strong theoretical knowledge. It is about understanding people, context, and being willing to change your approach when something is not working.

After this experience, I have tried to be more humble in my approach to problem-solving. Before implementing any new initiative, I now always spend time gathering input from stakeholders and considering multiple perspectives. I have also become more comfortable admitting when I do not have all the answers.

This lesson has remained with me throughout my career. As I move toward an MBA, I understand that leadership is about continuous learning and adaptation. I want to develop stronger skills in strategic thinking and data analysis, which I believe an IIM Calcutta education would provide. The experience has shaped how I approach challenges and how I plan to lead in the future.

school_name: Peking Guanghua School of Management (Master in Management)
prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
What matters most to me is making a positive impact on society through my work in real estate development.

Since I was young, I have always believed that we have a responsibility to contribute to our communities and to help build a better world. In my current role as a junior project manager at a real estate company in Istanbul, I have come to realize that the built environment is one of the most important ways we can create lasting change. Every building we develop, every neighborhood we transform, touches thousands of lives.

Throughout my career, I have seen firsthand how real estate shapes the way people live, work, and interact with each other. A well-designed office building can increase productivity. A thoughtfully developed residential community can strengthen social bonds. These are the kinds of impacts that matter to me. When I walk through a neighborhood that we have developed, and I see families enjoying the parks, or people working in the offices we built, I feel a deep sense of fulfillment knowing that my work contributed to their lives.

Real estate has given me the opportunity to think about how we can build more sustainably and responsibly. Climate change is one of the biggest challenges facing our generation, and I believe that the real estate and construction industry has a crucial role to play in addressing this crisis. More efficient buildings, green spaces, renewable energy—these are all things that can make a real difference.

What also matters to me is professional growth and continuous learning. I am passionate about developing my skills and becoming a better leader. An MBA from Peking Guanghua will help me understand business at a deeper level and will prepare me to take on more senior positions in the future. I want to lead projects that are not just profitable, but that also create value for society.

Family is also very important to me. My parents have always taught me the importance of hard work and integrity. They came from humble backgrounds and built a good life for our family through determination and honesty. These values guide everything I do, including how I approach my work in real estate.

Finally, I believe that what matters most is being authentic and true to yourself. In a world where there is so much pressure to be like everyone else, I think it is important to stay true to your values and your vision. For me, that vision is to build developments that are not only economically successful but also contribute positively to society and the environment.

An MBA will help me achieve this vision. I am excited about the possibility of studying at Peking Guanghua and becoming part of a community of leaders who share these values and are committed to making a positive impact in the world.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
When I took the GMAT for the first time last year, I was honestly shocked at my 629. After spending fifteen years in education—first as a secondary teacher in Manchester, then building curriculum frameworks for three international schools across Asia—I'd grown comfortable with analytical thinking. But standardized tests? A completely different animal. I retook it twice and landed at 665, which still isn't stellar. Here's what I want you to know: that score doesn't reflect how I actually work.

I mention this because it matters for context. My academic journey has always been unconventional. I graduated with a 3.2 from Sheffield because I was working twenty hours a week as a residential advisor and genuinely uninterested in performing well on exams. I was more interested in why my residents were struggling than in memorizing content for finals. That pattern has defined my career.

For the past five years, I've led the pedagogical transformation at Wellington International School in Bangkok—a 2,000-student institution. I designed our entire teacher development program, restructured how we assess learning, and most critically, managed a budget of approximately $800,000 while overseeing a team that grew from 4 to 12 people. We increased our IB pass rates from 68% to 84% and won the Asia-Pacific Education Award in 2022. Not because I'm naturally brilliant at systems—I actually struggle with spreadsheets—but because I obsess over understanding what's broken and why.

What's missing from my profile is the reason I'm applying now. I'm thirty-eight, and I've realized that sustainable impact in education requires deeper business acumen than I currently possess. I can redesign pedagogy and lead teams, but I can't confidently engage with financial strategy, operations scaling, or market dynamics the way I need to. I've built something meaningful at Wellington, and I want the next chapter—whether that's leading an educational institution, launching a social enterprise, or consulting for EdTech organizations—to be even more strategic.

IIM Jammu's EPGP appeals to me specifically because you prioritize experienced leaders who need business literacy, not MBA rookies. Your focus on strategic management and your cohort of professionals in their late thirties gives me confidence that the program understands my stage. I'm ready to be stretched intellectually in ways I haven't been since university, and I'm committed to earning your investment in my growth.

prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
I have always believed that failure is the stepping stone to success. This philosophy was tested when I led a major warehouse consolidation project at my company, TruckLogic Solutions, where I failed to deliver on my initial timeline and budget.

In 2018, I was assigned to consolidate three distribution centers in North India into one central facility. The goal was to reduce operating costs by 30% and improve delivery times. I was confident about this project because I had five years of supply chain experience at that time. I created a detailed project plan and presented it to senior management with high optimism.
