However, things did not go as planned. The actual consolidation took eight months instead of the planned four months. We exceeded the budget by nearly 25%. The failure was due to multiple reasons: I underestimated the complexity of integrating legacy systems, I did not account for resistance from the existing warehouse staff, and I failed to communicate effectively with different stakeholders including IT, HR, and operations teams.

When the project finally completed, I felt disappointed and ashamed. My manager called me for a review, and I was worried about the consequences. But instead of blaming me, he asked me to reflect on what went wrong and what I learned.

This failure taught me three important lessons. First, I realized the importance of stakeholder management. I had focused only on the technical aspects of the consolidation and had ignored the human side. The warehouse staff were worried about their jobs and were not cooperative. If I had engaged them early and addressed their concerns, the transition would have been smoother.

Second, I learned that estimation is crucial in project management. I had been too optimistic about timelines and had not built in buffer time for unexpected issues. Now I always add contingency buffers and regularly update my estimates based on actual progress.

Third, this experience taught me humility. I realized that having experience does not mean I know everything. I should be willing to learn from others, ask for help, and seek advice from team members and seniors.

After this failure, I became a better project manager. I have successfully led three other consolidation projects since then, all of which were completed on time and within budget. I also mentored junior managers on the importance of stakeholder engagement and realistic planning.

This failure was painful, but it shaped my approach to work. I now understand that success is not just about technical knowledge but also about people management, communication, and humility. I am confident that my experience at TruckLogic Solutions has prepared me well for the challenges of an MBA program, where I hope to deepen my understanding of supply chain management and leadership.

prompt: Select one prompt from group B and respond to your selected prompt using 100 words or fewer. I learned from a colleague (or someone I supervised) when... A practical way in which I have shown compassion towards a colleague or colleagues was... I have empowered a colleague (or teammate) to succeed in their own career/project by...
content: I learned from a colleague when our department faced a difficult transition during the pandemic. My coworker showed me the importance of being flexible and adapting to change. She taught me that communication is key when leading a team through uncertainty. I realized that listening to my team members and understanding their concerns helps build trust. This experience changed how I approach my leadership style. I now make sure to be more open to feedback and to support my colleagues during challenging times. Her example of resilience inspired me to become a better leader and manager.

prompt: What qualities would you look for in an MBA classmate when working on a team project and describe how your background and leadership skills will enhance the experience of others?
content:
When I think about the teammates I actually want to work with, I don't picture the person with the highest SAT score. I picture Priya, a fixed income analyst at Hokuto Capital where I worked for two years. She'd challenge my analysis, but she'd do it in my office with the door closed, not in front of the portfolio committee. She read financial papers the way most people read novels—not to extract one thesis, but to understand all the tensions within them.

That's the first quality I'd want in an MBA classmate: intellectual humility paired with genuine curiosity. Not false modesty, but the actual willingness to say "I don't know that" or "I think you're missing something." I've noticed that people who work well in teams aren't the ones who arrive with all the answers pre-loaded. They're the ones asking better questions.

The second is reliability about small things. I've seen brilliant ideas die because someone didn't bother reading the prior week's work before showing up to the meeting. At Hokuto, I managed a three-person team analyzing Asian fixed income markets for institutional clients. The work was technically straightforward—duration analysis, curve positioning, that sort of thing—but it only mattered if we were honest about our assumptions and didn't cut corners on due diligence. The teammates I trusted weren't necessarily the smartest; they were the ones who showed up prepared and admitted when they'd missed something.

What I'll bring to a team at Desautels is probably not what some might expect. I don't walk into rooms as the loudest voice. I'm non-binary, I'm Japanese, I grew up shuttling between Tokyo and Toronto—being invisible or being "the person explaining their background" is something I've gotten used to. But that's actually shaped how I work. I listen first. I notice when someone isn't speaking. I've learned to translate between different professional cultures, which matters when your team includes a geologist, an engineer, and someone from a regulated industry.

I'm also stubborn about data. In 2021, I noticed our team was projecting Asian bond issuance based on historical patterns that no longer applied post-COVID. Everyone wanted to use the model we'd built the year before. I spent three weeks pulling granular issuer-level data from central banks to argue for a different approach. It wasn't popular. But the actual issuance came in within 2% of my revised forecast, versus 18% off for the consensus. That matters not because I was right, but because I was willing to be wrong in front of people who outranked me to make sure we got better information.

I'm looking for classmates who want to think harder, not think faster. Someone who knows that a good team project isn't about dividing work into four equal pieces. It's about creating something none of us could have made alone—which means being willing to have your thinking changed, to admit gaps in your expertise, and to do the unglamorous work of actually understanding what everyone else is trying to say.

Desautels' cohort-based model appeals to me precisely because it's built for that kind of interaction. I want to work with people who take that seriously.

prompt: What are your short-term and long-term professional goals? How will our MBA program help you achieve them?
content:
# What are your short-term and long-term professional goals? How will our SKEMA MBA help you achieve them?

I have always been passionate about making a difference in the healthcare industry. Throughout my 10 years of working in this sector, I have realized that to truly impact the lives of patients and contribute meaningfully to healthcare organizations, I need to develop stronger business and leadership skills.

My short-term goal is to transition into a management position within the next two years. Currently, I work as a healthcare coordinator, but I want to move into a supervisory or middle management role where I can lead teams and drive operational improvements. I believe the SKEMA MBA will equip me with the necessary tools to understand business strategy, financial management, and team dynamics. These are areas where I feel I lack formal training, and I know an MBA will help me bridge this gap.

My long-term vision is to eventually become a director-level executive in a healthcare organization, ideally working in hospital administration or healthcare management consulting. I want to be in a position where I can influence organizational strategy and help hospitals operate more efficiently. The healthcare industry is changing rapidly, and I think having an MBA from a respected institution like SKEMA will give me the credibility and knowledge I need to advance.

I chose SKEMA because of its global reputation and executive focus. As an international program, I believe it will give me exposure to different healthcare systems and business practices from around the world. Since healthcare varies significantly between countries, understanding global perspectives will be valuable for my career.

The curriculum appears to offer courses in general management, finance, and leadership, which directly align with my needs. I am particularly interested in learning about healthcare finance and strategic management. Additionally, the networking opportunities with other executives from different industries will help me build connections that could support my career development.

I am also drawn to SKEMA's location and international student body. Being part of a diverse cohort will expose me to different viewpoints and help me develop a global mindset, which I think is increasingly important in today's business environment.

Over the past decade, I have gained valuable experience in healthcare operations, but I recognize that my background is somewhat narrow. An MBA will broaden my perspective and help me transition from a specialist to a more well-rounded business leader. I am confident that SKEMA's program will provide the foundation I need to reach my goals and contribute more effectively to the healthcare industry.

I am ready to commit fully to this program and believe it represents the next logical step in my professional journey.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
Working in the pharmaceutical industry has taught me that success requires managing multiple competing interests. Throughout my eleven years in this field, I have encountered many situations where stakeholders wanted different outcomes, and I had to find solutions that worked for everyone.

One significant example occurred when I was leading a product launch team at a mid-sized biotech company in Istanbul. The sales department wanted to launch the drug as quickly as possible to capture market share. The regulatory team, however, needed more time to ensure all compliance requirements were met. The clinical team was concerned about gathering additional safety data, while finance wanted to minimize spending on market research.

In this situation, I had to balance these competing interests carefully. I scheduled meetings with each department and listened to their concerns. I understood that sales wanted revenue, but I also understood that regulatory compliance was non-negotiable. I realized that we could not rush the process just to beat competitors.

I proposed a solution where we would proceed with the regulatory pathway as planned while simultaneously preparing our marketing materials and training sales staff. This meant that once regulatory approval came through, we could launch immediately. For the finance team, I suggested we allocate resources to the most critical market research areas rather than cutting the entire budget. This satisfied their concerns about cost while maintaining the quality needed for a successful launch.

The launch ultimately happened on schedule, and the product achieved strong market performance in the first quarter. All stakeholders felt their core needs were addressed. The sales team got their timely launch, regulatory remained compliant, clinical was satisfied with safety protocols, and finance kept costs reasonable.

This experience showed me the importance of communication and compromise in leadership. I learned that when stakeholders have different goals, listening is the first step. It is not always possible to give everyone everything they want, but by understanding their real needs, you can often find creative solutions.

I believe that an MBA will help me develop stronger skills in stakeholder management and strategic decision-making. At Edinburgh Business School, I hope to study how leaders in global organizations handle these complex situations and develop my ability to navigate them effectively.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
I am most proud of my achievement in helping my family's business grow over the past decade. This is something that has shaped who I am today and made me realize the importance of hard work and dedication.

When I joined the company in 2014, we were a mid-sized operation in the manufacturing sector. The business was stable but not growing as much as it could have been. I saw potential for improvement and decided to take action. Over the years, I implemented various changes that helped the company become more efficient and profitable.

One of my main contributions was improving our operational processes. I looked at how we were doing things and realized there were inefficiencies that were costing us time and money. By streamlining certain procedures and introducing new systems, we were able to reduce costs and improve productivity. This was a significant step forward for the business.

I also worked on expanding our client base. I believed that if we could reach more customers, we would see better revenue growth. I worked with the sales team to develop new strategies and identify new market opportunities. Through these efforts, we were able to attract new clients and increase our market presence.

Another important achievement was building a stronger team. I focused on hiring talented people and creating a better work environment. I believe that a good team is essential for business success, and investing in people is one of the best decisions a company can make.

Through all of this work, the company's revenue increased significantly. We also became more competitive in our industry and earned a better reputation. My family was proud of what we accomplished together, and I felt a real sense of fulfillment knowing that my ideas and hard work had contributed to the company's success.

This achievement is important to me because it showed me that with determination and strategic thinking, you can create real change. It also taught me valuable lessons about business, leadership, and working with people. I learned that success is not just about making more money, but about building something meaningful that can last.

Now I want to take my career to the next level, and I believe an MSc in Management from Heriot-Watt will provide me with the formal education and skills I need to become a better leader and manager. I am excited about the possibility of studying at your institution and applying what I learn to my future career, whether that is continuing in the family business or pursuing new opportunities.

school_name: Stockholm School of Economics Executive MBA
prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
# Ethical Dilemma Essay

During my time working at my family's textile manufacturing company in Como, I have faced many challenging situations that tested my values and professional ethics. One particular dilemma stands out as a defining moment in my career that forced me to reconsider what it means to do the right thing in business.

Last year, our company was approached by a major international retailer who wanted to place a significant order. This was a huge opportunity for our business, and the potential revenue would have helped us expand our operations and hire more staff. However, during the negotiation process, I discovered that the retailer was asking us to compromise on our environmental standards in order to reduce production costs and meet their aggressive pricing demands.

Specifically, they wanted us to use cheaper materials and cut corners on our waste management processes. This would have increased our profit margin substantially, but it would also have meant polluting the local river that supplies water to our community. I faced a real conflict between my responsibility to my family and the business versus my responsibility to the environment and society.

I discussed this situation with my father, who is the CEO, and explained my concerns. At first, he was focused on the financial benefits and was hesitant to turn down such a large order. However, I pushed for us to maintain our ethical standards. I explained that in the long term, damaging our reputation and the environment would be worse for the business than losing one order.

After several meetings and discussions with our team, we decided to decline the order and instead focus on finding clients who shared our values regarding sustainability. This was difficult because the financial impact was real, but it was the right decision.

Looking back, I realize this experience taught me that ethical decisions are rarely simple. There are often competing interests and pressures that make doing the right thing challenging. However, I learned that compromising on core values for short-term gains is never worth it. Our family business is built on trust and integrity, and maintaining those principles is essential to our long-term success.

Since this incident, I have become more committed to ensuring that our company operates in an ethical and sustainable way. I have started researching ways to improve our environmental practices further, and I have also begun looking into certifications that would demonstrate our commitment to sustainability to potential clients.

This experience has also made me realize that I need to develop my leadership and business skills further in order to make better decisions in the future. An MBA from Stockholm School of Economics would help me gain the knowledge and tools I need to navigate complex ethical situations and lead my family business into a more sustainable future.

The values of the school, particularly its focus on business ethics and social responsibility, align perfectly with what I believe in. I am excited about the opportunity to study at SSE and to contribute to the school's community with my unique perspective and experiences.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
# Leadership Experience Essay

During my second year at the American University in Cairo, I was assigned to lead a student mentorship program that was, frankly, a mess. Twenty-three high school students matched with twenty-eight university mentors, inconsistent meeting schedules, zero tracking system, and a faculty advisor who'd checked out emotionally. The whole thing existed on a shared Google Drive that nobody accessed. Three months in, seven mentee-mentor pairs had already dissolved.

I remember sitting in the office thinking, "This is either going to be my most humbling failure or the place I actually learn how to lead." I chose the latter, though not because I had some grand vision—I just couldn't accept that these teenagers wouldn't get what they deserved.

The first thing I did was stop trying to fix everything at once. I met individually with the remaining fifteen mentors and asked a question that sounds simple but changed everything: "What would actually make this work for you?" Not "what's wrong with the program" but "what do you need?" A physics student said she wanted structured lesson plans. An engineering mentor said he wanted fewer meetings but longer ones. One mentor confessed she didn't know how to talk to a fifteen-year-old about anything other than academics. These weren't program failures; they were design failures.

I implemented a flexible framework instead of rigid structure. Mentors could choose between weekly 90-minute sessions or bi-weekly two-hour sessions. I created a simple curriculum scaffold—not a script—with space for mentors to adjust based on each student's needs. For the mentor who felt out of depth with soft skills, I organized a two-hour training on active listening. I also built an actual tracking system using simple spreadsheets that took maybe fifteen minutes a month to maintain.

But here's what I learned that mattered most: I had to lead by making myself the bottleneck, not the solution. I became the person who responded within 24 hours to any question, who remembered which mentee was applying to universities, who flagged when a pair hadn't met in three weeks. I showed up. That predictability changed the culture more than any initiative did.

By the end of the year, all fifteen remaining pairs met consistently. Nine mentees got into competitive Egyptian universities; three of them were first-generation applicants. One mentor, Layla, pulled me aside and said she'd decided to pursue education policy because she'd seen how much a structured mentorship changed one teenager's trajectory. That probably sounds corny, but it shattered something in me—the idea that leadership is about moving people toward your vision. It's actually about moving toward theirs.

That experience shaped two core beliefs I hold about management. First, I believe in diagnosis before prescription. I've watched so many initiatives fail because leaders skip the part where they actually listen to the people doing the work. When I took over a struggling tutoring program last year at my current organization, I spent two weeks interviewing tutors before making a single change. That discipline saves months of wasted effort.

Second, I learned that consistency and reliability are underrated leadership tools. You don't need charisma or perfect strategic insights. You need to show up, follow through, and care enough to get the details right. In my current role managing a team of seven curriculum developers, I block 30 minutes every Friday for each person—no agenda, they lead. Half the time nothing significant comes up. But sometimes that's when someone tells me they're struggling or sees a better way to solve a problem we've been stuck on.

Both of these philosophies—listening deeply before acting, and building trust through reliability—feel more important now than they did at twenty-two. As I prepare for an MBA, I'm excited to test them against more complex systems and larger teams, to see if they scale or if they're just habits that worked in specific contexts.

I think KAIST's emphasis on ethics and pragmatic leadership resonates with where I've landed.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
In five years, I want to be the person my team comes to when they're stuck between what's commercially viable and what's medically necessary. Right now, I'm a Senior Product Manager at Medtronic, and I spend a lot of time translating between surgeons who want to push the boundaries of what's possible and finance teams worried about unit economics. That gap—between innovation and scale—is where I want to build my career.

The honest version: I'm not sure I'm ready for that gap yet. I can manage a product roadmap. I can analyze market data. But when our VP asked me last year to lead a cross-functional initiative to expand our robotic surgery platform into emerging markets, I froze. I had the tactical skills but not the strategic language to think about market entry, regulatory pathways, and organizational change simultaneously. I spent three weeks sketching things out before I realized I was missing a framework—not just a process, but an actual way of thinking about complexity.

That's when I knew I needed an MBA.

But not just any program. I need something that fits around my job, because I can't afford to step back right now. We're in the middle of launching a new device in Southeast Asia, and my team depends on me. Columbia's Executive MBA is built for people like me—people who are already doing real work and need to level up while doing it. The Friday-Saturday format means I don't have to choose between ambition and responsibility.

Five years out, I see myself leading product strategy for a med-tech company, probably at the director or VP level. Not because I'm climbing for titles, but because I want autonomy over the decisions that affect patient outcomes. Right now, I can influence those decisions. But I'm still asking permission. I want to be the one giving the strategic direction.

Columbia specifically helps me get there in ways I've researched carefully. Your healthcare management track is actually structured around real business cases—I've read the syllabi—not just theory. And the location matters more than people think. I work with surgeons and hospital networks in New York and New Jersey constantly. Being in the same city as my customers and classmates who work in healthcare means the learning doesn't stop in the classroom. I could actually pilot ideas with my colleagues and bring that back to my projects.

I'm also drawn to your emphasis on global health and emerging markets. China is where I'm from, and Southeast Asia is where I'm building my expertise. But I don't want to stay in a regional bubble. The EMBA curriculum—especially the international consulting project—seems designed to push people like me to think bigger than their current geography.

Honestly, the biggest risk I see is whether I'll actually have the bandwidth to do this well. I work long hours. I travel maybe one week a month. Part of me worries I'll phone it in. But I've thought about this enough to know that risk is worth taking. The alternative—staying comfortable in my current role for another five years—feels worse.

I want to lead with conviction, not just competence. Columbia's EMBA is where I build that conviction.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
# Essay Response

My most significant accomplishment is leading a team of 15 people to implement a new quality management system at our manufacturing facility in Lagos. This achievement has shaped my understanding of what it means to be a leader and has instilled in me a deep passion for operational excellence.

When I joined the company in 2016 as a Production Supervisor, the facility was struggling with quality issues. Our defect rate was around 8%, which was significantly higher than industry standards. Management decided to implement a comprehensive quality management system, and I was chosen to lead this initiative. This was a turning point in my career because I had never managed such a large-scale project before.

The project took 18 months to complete. I had to coordinate with different departments, manage stakeholders, and ensure that all 200 employees on the factory floor were trained on the new processes. It was challenging because many people were resistant to change. Some of the older workers felt that the new system was unnecessary and that the old ways had worked fine for years. I had to be patient and empathetic while also maintaining the urgency of the project.

Throughout the implementation, I faced many obstacles. There were budget constraints, equipment delays, and resistance from the workforce. However, I persevered because I believed in the importance of the project. I conducted regular training sessions, created visual aids to help workers understand the new processes, and celebrated small wins along the way. By the end of the project, the defect rate had dropped to 2.5%, and we achieved ISO 9001 certification.

This accomplishment matters to me because it taught me that real leadership is not about having all the answers. It is about listening to your team, understanding their concerns, and finding ways to bring everyone together toward a common goal. I learned that change management is just as important as the technical aspects of a project. I also learned that persistence and empathy can overcome even the most stubborn resistance to change.

Beyond the numbers, what I am most proud of is the relationships I built with my team. Many of the supervisors who initially resisted the new system became champions of the initiative. This showed me that people are willing to embrace change when they feel heard and supported. This experience has made me more confident in my ability to lead larger teams and manage more complex projects.

The reason this accomplishment matters to me is that it represents my commitment to continuous improvement and my desire to make a positive impact on the organizations I work for. Manufacturing is an industry that is constantly evolving, and I want to be at the forefront of that evolution. I believe that an MBA from IIM Indore will provide me with the tools and knowledge to take on more strategic roles and to drive innovation in the manufacturing sector.

In conclusion, implementing this quality management system was the most significant accomplishment of my career so far. It has shaped who I am as a leader and has motivated me to pursue further education and advancement in my field. I am confident that an MBA will help me achieve my goals and make an even greater impact in the manufacturing industry.

school_name: University of Bergen NHH MSc (additional track)
prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
In five years, I see myself as an investment professional with deep expertise in emerging market tech ecosystems, ideally working with a fund that invests across Africa and Southeast Asia. My goal is to lead deal sourcing and portfolio support for early-stage companies that are solving real problems in underserved markets.

The NHH MSc program appeals to me because of its rigorous finance curriculum and strong European network. I believe studying in Norway will expose me to different perspectives on venture capital and help me develop the analytical foundations I need to move into a senior investing role.

Currently, I work as an Associate at a venture capital firm in Johannesburg, where I've been involved in evaluating tech startups and managing post-investment relationships. My background in commerce and finance has prepared me well for the quantitative side of investing, but I recognize gaps in my knowledge around corporate strategy and international market dynamics. An MBA will help me fill these gaps.

My path to venture capital began somewhat organically. After completing my undergraduate degree, I worked in corporate finance roles at two companies, which taught me how businesses operate from the inside. I was drawn to VC because I wanted to work with founders earlier in their journey and have more influence over company strategy. At my current firm, I've been part of investment decisions totaling over $50 million across our portfolio. I've also mentored two portfolio companies through scaling challenges, which reinforced my belief that I want to take on more operational involvement in future roles.

However, I'm realistic about where I currently stand. I'm not yet equipped to lead investment decisions independently, and I lack formal training in areas like financial modeling at an institutional level and strategic planning. The NHH program's focus on applied finance and business strategy directly addresses these weaknesses. Additionally, the opportunity to study in Europe—a different market from where I've worked—will broaden my perspective on how venture ecosystems develop and how capital flows across regions.

Beyond coursework, I'm interested in NHH's alumni network in the venture and private equity space. I've researched several alumni working in European VC firms, and I believe their insights could be valuable as I build my career. I'm also drawn to the program's emphasis on sustainability and impact investing, which aligns with my increasing interest in venture models that generate both financial and social returns.

Five years from now, I hope to be an investment director at a fund focused on African tech. I want to be someone founders trust and seek out, not just for capital but for strategic guidance. The NHH MSc will give me the analytical rigor, strategic mindset, and international perspective I need to reach this goal. I'm committed to making the most of this program and returning to my market with strengthened capabilities.

prompt: Describe a time when you led a team through a significant challenge. What was the outcome?
content:
When I became the operations manager at my family's manufacturing company in Bangkok, I knew I had to make significant changes. The company had been operating the same way for over 20 years, and it was struggling to compete with newer competitors. I saw this as my moment to lead.

One of the biggest challenges was implementing a new inventory management system. The existing system was completely manual, using spreadsheets and paper records. This caused numerous problems: we often ran out of stock, we had excess inventory in other areas, and we were losing money. I decided that we needed to move to a digital system.

I gathered my team of about 15 people and explained the situation. I told them that change was necessary for our survival. Many of them were resistant at first because they had been doing things the old way for so long. But I believed in the importance of this change, and I worked hard to help them understand why it mattered.

I worked closely with a software vendor to customize a system that would fit our specific needs. During the implementation, there were many obstacles. Some team members did not want to learn the new system. There were technical issues that needed to be solved. But I stayed committed to the process.

I held regular training sessions with my team. I also created a transition period where people could still use the old system while learning the new one. This helped reduce their anxiety and made them more comfortable with the change. I was patient and understanding of their concerns.

After about six months, the new system was fully implemented. The results were very positive. We reduced inventory carrying costs by 25%, and our stock-out situations decreased significantly. Lead times improved, and customers were more satisfied with on-time deliveries. The team was proud of what they had accomplished.

This experience taught me valuable lessons about leadership and change management. I learned that change is difficult for people, but with proper communication, patience, and support, it can be achieved. I also learned that listening to your team's concerns is important.

This is why I want to pursue an MBA at Durham. I need to develop my leadership and management skills further. I want to learn more about business strategy and operations so that I can help my family business grow and compete globally. An MBA will give me the knowledge and credentials to take on bigger challenges in the future.

prompt: What are your short-term career goals and how will the Full-Time MBA program at Michigan State University's Broad College of Business help you achieve them?
content:
When I started as a junior associate at Adeyemi & Partners in Lagos eight years ago, I thought my trajectory was set: make partner, build a practice, retire wealthy. The problem was that I was solving the same problems for the same clients on repeat. A contract dispute in 2019 changed that. Our client, a mid-sized agricultural exporter, was losing millions because they didn't understand their own supply chain risks. I spent two weeks mapping their operations, identified five critical vulnerabilities, and proposed solutions that had nothing to do with litigation. It saved them $1.2 million and cost them nothing. That moment crystallized something I'd been avoiding: I was good at law, but I was better at solving business problems.

The challenge is that Nigeria's legal market hasn't caught up to this shift. There's no real market for lawyer-consultants here. So I've decided my short-term goal is to transition into a hybrid business advisory role within five years—ideally helping medium-sized African companies navigate growth, regulatory expansion, and operational scaling. I want to work for a firm that advises on strategy and operations, not one that just handles disputes. But I can't get there from Lagos.

This is where Michigan State's MBA becomes essential, not optional.

I'm not looking for a program that teaches me business basics—I've learned plenty on the ground. I need a program that teaches me how to think like an operator, not a lawyer. That means real exposure to finance, strategy, and operations in a way that goes beyond what I can teach myself through case studies at night. More importantly, I need a network that bridges Africa and North America. The businesses I want to advise will increasingly operate across both continents. Broad's African business focus and its students from across the continent give me access to people who are already building those bridges.

What makes Michigan State specific to my needs isn't just curriculum. It's the concentration in business analytics and your partnerships with firms doing real advisory work in emerging markets. I've looked at the consulting club's case competition results—you're placing people at firms like Deloitte and EY who have substantial African operations. That's my pipeline. I also know three alumni working at Dalberg Advisors, which does exactly the type of advisory work I want to do. That's not coincidence; it's alignment.

I also need to be honest: my GPA and GMAT aren't exceptional. I'm not coming in as the strongest applicant academically. But I'm coming in with eight years of real experience managing complexity, leading teams, and making decisions that affected millions of dollars. I've failed publicly (a case we should have won), learned from it, and adjusted my approach. I know how to work hard and solve problems under pressure. The MBA isn't a credential I'm collecting—it's a deliberate pivot point in my career.

In two years, I want to have completed the program, built relationships with classmates and alumni in the advisory space, and have offers from strategy or operations firms with African exposure. In five years, I want to be advising African companies on the moves that keep them awake at night. Michigan State's program gives me the tools, the network, and the international perspective to make that transition credible and concrete.

school_name: University of Auckland Business School MiM
prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
Failing Forward: A Lesson in Humility

Throughout my career, I have learned that failure is not the opposite of success—it is a stepping stone toward it. One experience in particular has shaped my understanding of leadership and collaboration.

During my second year at Google, I was assigned to lead a cross-functional project aimed at optimizing our internal data pipeline. I was excited about the opportunity and confident in my technical abilities. I had spent months developing what I believed was an innovative solution, working late nights and weekends to perfect every detail.

When I presented my work to the team and stakeholders, I expected praise. Instead, I received critical feedback. The senior engineer on my team pointed out that I had overlooked several key requirements from the product team, and my solution would actually create more problems downstream. My approach had been too narrow—I had focused so heavily on the technical implementation that I failed to consider the broader business implications and user needs.

At first, I was defensive. I had worked so hard, and hearing that my work was flawed was difficult to accept. However, I realized that my failure stemmed from a fundamental mistake: I had not collaborated effectively with my teammates from the beginning. I had worked in isolation, assuming I knew what the right solution was without truly listening to other perspectives.

This experience taught me the importance of humility and communication. I learned that the strongest solutions come from diverse viewpoints, not from individual brilliance. I went back to the drawing board, this time scheduling regular sync meetings with the product team, the data scientists, and the engineers. We rebuilt the solution together, and it ultimately became one of the most successful projects I've worked on at Google.

This failure has fundamentally changed how I approach problems. I now prioritize understanding different stakeholder needs before diving into solutions. I have become a better listener and a more collaborative team member.

As I consider pursuing an MBA, I recognize that this lesson will be invaluable. Business school will expose me to diverse perspectives from people with different backgrounds and experiences. I am committed to bringing the same collaborative mindset I developed after this failure to my MBA experience and beyond.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
When I think about my most significant accomplishment, I am reminded of the work I have done over the past five years in the non-profit sector. I have always been passionate about making a difference in the world, and I believe that my contributions to sustainable development have been meaningful and important.

During my time at a development organization in Peru, I worked on various projects aimed at improving the lives of underserved communities. One project in particular stands out as being especially significant to me. We implemented an initiative focused on education and community development in rural areas. This work was challenging but rewarding, and I learned a great deal about myself and about the communities we served.

What made this accomplishment significant is that it combined my interest in social impact with my growing skills in project management. I was responsible for coordinating between different stakeholders, managing timelines, and ensuring that our objectives were met. The project involved working with local government officials, community leaders, and international partners. Through this experience, I developed stronger communication skills and a deeper understanding of how to navigate complex organizational environments.

The project was successful because of our collaborative approach. We brought together people from different backgrounds and perspectives, and this diversity of thought led to better solutions. I took on a leadership role, even though it was not officially my title, and I found that I was able to motivate my team and guide them toward our shared goals. This was an important realization for me—that I have the capacity to lead others and inspire them toward a common purpose.

Beyond the professional skills I gained, this work matters to me on a personal level. Growing up in Peru, I saw firsthand the inequalities that exist in my country, and I have always felt a responsibility to contribute to positive change. This project represented my commitment to that goal. It allowed me to be part of something larger than myself, which is something I value deeply.

The accomplishment also matters because it has shaped my decision to pursue an MBA. I realized that while my passion for social impact is genuine, I need stronger business and financial management skills to be more effective in my field. I want to be able to manage budgets more strategically, understand organizational finance, and develop sustainable business models for non-profits. An MBA will give me the tools I need to scale my impact and take on more senior leadership roles in the development sector.

Looking back, I am proud of what I accomplished, but I also recognize that there is much more I can learn and achieve. This project was a stepping stone toward becoming a more skilled and impactful leader. I believe that Nottingham University Business School will be an important part of my journey, providing me with the knowledge and network I need to advance my career in sustainable development.

In conclusion, my most significant accomplishment is not just about the project itself, but about what it taught me about my own capabilities and my commitment to creating positive change in the world.

school_name: EDHEC Business School Master in Management
prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
Last year, I failed to meet a critical deadline at my manufacturing company that resulted in losing a major client contract worth approximately 2 million baht. This experience taught me a valuable lesson about responsibility and communication.

I was assigned to manage a production schedule for a client in the automotive sector. The project seemed straightforward, so I did not think it was necessary to communicate frequently with the team. I assumed everything was on track. However, two weeks before the deadline, I discovered that our supplier had delayed delivering raw materials. At that point, it was too late to find alternative suppliers or adjust the timeline.

When I informed my manager about the delay, he was very disappointed. The client cancelled the contract because we could not deliver on time. My manager told me that I should have been monitoring the supply chain more carefully and updating him regularly. I realized that I made a serious mistake by not staying in touch with all the people involved in the project.

This failure taught me that communication is essential in any business environment. If I had checked with the suppliers earlier and reported the risks to my manager, we could have solved the problem before it became a crisis. I learned that even if a project seems simple, it is important to maintain constant contact with all stakeholders.

After this experience, I changed my approach completely. Now I always communicate with my team members and suppliers on a weekly basis. I use project management tools to track progress and identify risks early. I also learned to report any potential issues to my manager immediately, not waiting until the last moment.

This failure was painful, but it made me understand the importance of proactive communication and responsibility. I believe this lesson will help me succeed in the MBA program and in my future career. I want to study at EDHEC because I know that the program will teach me more about how to manage teams and projects effectively. I am committed to learning from my mistakes and becoming a better leader and manager in the future.

school_name: African Leadership University School of Business MiM
prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
# Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.

Throughout my seven years working in the energy sector, I have encountered many challenges that required creative thinking and innovative problem-solving. One situation stands out as particularly impactful and demonstrates my ability to approach difficult problems with a fresh perspective.

At my previous company, an international oil and gas firm, our team faced a significant challenge with project delays and miscommunication between departments. The exploration team, production team, and logistics team were not working efficiently together, which resulted in missed deadlines and increased costs. This was a complex problem because it involved multiple stakeholders with different priorities and objectives.

I recognized that the root cause of the issue was that each department was working in silos without proper communication channels. To address this, I decided to implement a solution that would bring everyone together and improve collaboration.

My creative solution was to establish a weekly cross-functional meeting that included representatives from all three departments. During these meetings, we would discuss ongoing projects, identify potential problems before they became critical issues, and align on timelines and expectations. I created a simple shared spreadsheet that tracked all major milestones and deadlines so that everyone could see what others were working on.

The results were quite positive. Within three months, we saw a noticeable improvement in project timelines and team morale. The delays that had been plaguing us for months began to decrease, and people seemed more engaged because they understood how their work connected to the larger picture. By the end of the year, we had reduced project delays by approximately 20%, which had a positive impact on our budget and company reputation.

What I learned from this experience is that sometimes the most creative solutions are not necessarily complicated. Sometimes what people need most is simply a forum to communicate and collaborate. This taught me valuable lessons about leadership, teamwork, and the importance of listening to others.

This experience has made me more passionate about understanding how organizations can work more effectively. I believe that an MBA will help me develop stronger skills in organizational management and leadership so that I can tackle even more complex business problems in the future.

My time in the energy sector has been rewarding, but I am looking forward to expanding my knowledge and perspectives through business education. The energy industry is undergoing significant transformation with the shift toward renewable energy, and I want to be equipped with the tools and knowledge to lead this change. I am excited about the possibility of studying at African Leadership University because I believe the program will provide me with the education and network I need to make a meaningful impact.

Thank you for considering my application.

school_name: SKOLKOVO Moscow School of Management EMBA
prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
I've spent the last five years building businesses in Cairo and the Middle East, which means I've learned to operate without a map. When something isn't documented, you figure it out. When there's no "right way," you create one. This has made me useful in messy situations, but it's also made me realize how much I don't know—and how much I need to learn from people who've solved problems at scale that I'm only beginning to understand.

At Skolkovo, I want to be the person who asks the question that makes everyone reconsider their assumption. I don't mean contrarian for its own sake. I mean the kind of question that comes from actually doing something different, that challenges the frame without dismissing the analysis.

I started a B2B logistics platform in 2019 that connected regional suppliers with retailers. We grew to about $2.3 million in annual revenue, served 40+ clients across Egypt and Jordan, and then failed spectacularly in 2022. The technology worked. The unit economics made sense on paper. But I didn't understand regulatory compliance across markets, I underestimated the relationship-based nature of regional business, and I moved too fast into expansion without building a real go-to-market strategy. When discussing case studies about emerging market entry or scaling challenges, I can bring the perspective of someone who got humbled by this exact problem.

What I'm really interested in at Skolkovo is learning from EMBA peers who've already done what I'm attempting. I know the program attracts executives from across Europe and Asia with real P&L responsibility, successful exits, deep operational experience. I want to sit next to someone who scaled a manufacturing operation across multiple countries and ask them what I should have done differently. I want to understand how experienced operators think about organizational design in ways that my scrappy startup experience hasn't taught me.

I'll also bring something useful: the perspective of someone building in emerging markets where capital is scarce and every assumption gets tested. When discussions turn to strategy, operations, or go-to-market challenges, I have real data points from markets that most MBA case studies ignore. I'm interested in the operational side of business more than the theoretical side, so I'll probably ask uncomfortable questions about implementation and trade-offs—the stuff between the PowerPoint slides.

Outside the classroom, I'm genuinely interested in connecting with people. I've lived in three countries, I speak Arabic, English, and some French badly. I'm the kind of person who stays late to have the real conversation about what didn't work, not just what did. I imagine there will be classmates starting ventures in new markets or trying to expand regionally—I'd be useful as a sounding board there, someone who's already made the mistakes they're about to make.

Skolkovo is the right place for me at the right time because I'm past the stage of thinking I have all the answers, and I need to be around people who've actually solved the hard problems I'm facing. I'm ready to both contribute and be genuinely changed by what I learn.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
When I turned down the promotion to Regional Marketing Manager at PepsiCo's Middle East division two years ago, my boss thought I'd made a terrible mistake. The role came with a 40% salary increase, a corner office in Dubai, and everything my younger self would have wanted. But I knew something had shifted in how I measured success.

Over nine years in CPG, I've climbed the ladder steadily—brand manager at Mondelēz, senior marketing roles at local distributors, eventually leading a team of twelve at PepsiCo. I'm good at executing campaigns and reading markets. My last initiative, a regional rebranding campaign for our youth-focused beverage line, increased market share by 3.2% across four countries and generated $8.7 million in incremental revenue. But somewhere between the PowerPoints and the quarterly earnings calls, I realized I could recite every metric from our last launch and barely describe the actual people we were selling to.

That's when I asked to move laterally into consumer insights, taking a pay cut to do it. My colleagues thought I was crazy. I wanted to understand not just what consumers buy, but why—and more importantly, what responsibility we have in shaping those choices. In CPG, we spend billions trying to make people crave things. That's powerful. And for years, I'd been wielding that power without really interrogating it.

The next eighteen months changed my perspective completely. I led research across Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Egypt on how digital connectivity was reshaping family consumption patterns. We discovered that middle-class mothers in these markets were increasingly skeptical of Western brands' understanding of their values—not because the products were inferior, but because the messaging felt imported, tone-deaf. This insight led us to completely restructure our go-to-market strategy for three product lines. Revenue growth accelerated to 5.8%, but what mattered more was that we weren't just selling—we were actually listening.

I want to go deeper into this work, but I've hit a ceiling. To drive systemic change in how my industry makes decisions—to move from insights to strategy to organizational transformation—I need different tools. At PepsiCo, I can influence product teams and marketing departments. But I can't influence capital allocation decisions, I can't shape corporate strategy, and I can't mentor the next generation of leaders to think differently about business and responsibility.

King Saud University's Executive MBA appeals to me precisely because it's rooted here, where I'm rooted. I'm not interested in a program that treats the Middle East as a case study or an emerging market opportunity. I want to learn alongside Saudi executives who are building businesses in this context, who understand the complexity of balancing global standards with local values. The program's focus on ethical leadership and sustainable business practices aligns with where my own thinking has evolved. More practically, I want to understand capital markets and corporate governance from a regional perspective—to prepare myself for board-level conversations and C-suite roles where I can shape how large organizations think about purpose alongside profit.

I know this essay might raise questions about why I'm stepping back from aggressive career growth. I'm not. I'm just redirecting the ambition toward something that keeps me awake at night for the right reasons.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
When I joined Catalyst Ventures as an investment analyst three years ago, I inherited a portfolio review process that was fundamentally broken. Our team of six people was spending roughly 15 hours per week manually updating spreadsheets to track 47 portfolio companies across three fund vehicles. The spreadsheets were fragmented across different folders, different versions, and inevitably, different numbers.

Our managing partner would call Monday morning meetings asking simple questions—"What's our burn rate across consumer companies?"—and I'd spend two hours cobbling together answers from misaligned data sources. We were losing credibility with LPs and, worse, missing signals about which portfolio companies actually needed intervention.

I could have asked for budget to buy an expensive enterprise software solution. Instead, I spent a weekend learning Airtable and building a central database that pulled data from our existing systems through API connections. The system automatically categorized our companies by stage, sector, and risk level. It flagged when monthly burn exceeded thresholds. It created real-time dashboards showing portfolio health metrics.

The technical part wasn't that difficult, honestly. What actually took months was the social engineering.

Our CFO didn't trust cloud-based systems. Our operations manager saw it as threatening her spreadsheet expertise. Two senior analysts just wanted everything to stay the way it was. I had to rebuild this three times based on feedback, not because the original design was wrong, but because people needed to feel ownership over the change.

I started by asking the CFO to help me design the data validation rules. I had the operations manager teach me her manual reconciliation process so I could ensure the new system matched her logic, not replaced it. I ran weekly testing sessions with actual users before going live, not to collect feedback but to give them agency in the solution.

Six months after launch, our team was spending maybe three hours per week on portfolio administration. More importantly, our managing partner could ask that burn rate question and have an answer in 30 seconds. The system became the source of truth that everyone referenced.

What I learned from this wasn't about technology—it was about the gap between solving a problem and solving it in a way that actually works in a human organization. Implementing the smartest solution means nothing if people don't use it.

This feels relevant as I think about business school and eventually about joining a growth-stage company or potentially moving into strategy roles. I recognize that the problems I'll face at Galway and beyond won't just be analytical. They'll require me to understand organizational dynamics, stakeholder incentives, and how to build consensus around change.

I'm drawn to your program's emphasis on real-world problem solving and your focus on practical application. The integrated project work, particularly the strategy consulting module, aligns with how I actually want to develop—not just as someone who can identify problems, but as someone who can move organizations forward through them.

school_name: Asian Institute of Management Executive MBA
prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
Five years after completing the AIM Executive MBA, I see myself in a leadership position within a major media organization, driving innovation and managing large teams. I am passionate about making a difference in the entertainment industry, and I believe this program will help me achieve my goals.

Throughout my career in media, I have always been drawn to the challenge of building things from the ground up. I started at a production company in Germany and worked my way up through various roles in content development and project management. Over the past 12 years, I have learned that success in this industry requires both creative vision and solid business fundamentals. An MBA from AIM will provide me with the business knowledge I currently lack.

My vision for the future is clear: I want to be in a C-level position or senior management role where I can oversee operations and strategy. The media landscape is changing rapidly, and companies need leaders who understand both the creative side and the business side. I believe that having an MBA will make me more competitive for these positions and give me credibility in the marketplace.

The AIM Executive MBA appeals to me because of its strong reputation and its location in Southeast Asia. This region is experiencing significant growth in the media and entertainment sector, and I want to be part of this expansion. The program's curriculum in strategy and general management aligns well with my career trajectory. Additionally, I am looking forward to the international cohort and the networking opportunities that AIM provides. Meeting people from different backgrounds and industries will broaden my perspective and help me build valuable connections.

Specifically, I am interested in the courses on operations management and corporate strategy, as these are areas where I feel I need to strengthen my skills. My current role involves some strategic thinking, but I realize that I lack formal training in frameworks and methodologies that would help me be more effective. The AIM program seems well-designed to address these gaps.

In five years, I hope to have moved into a senior role at a media company—ideally overseeing multiple departments or a regional operation. I would like to be earning a competitive salary and managing a team of at least 10-15 people. The MBA will be instrumental in achieving this because it will give me the credentials and knowledge that companies look for when promoting people to senior positions.

I am committed to making the most of this program. I understand that success depends not only on the quality of instruction but also on my own effort and engagement. I am ready to take on this challenge and believe that AIM is the right choice for my career development.

school_name: The University of Chicago Booth School of Business
prompt: How will a Booth MBA help you achieve your immediate and long-term post-MBA career goals?
content:
In my role as a program coordinator at EduVenture, a nonprofit serving low-income students in Houston, I've spent the last two years designing curriculum and managing partnerships with local schools. It's meaningful work, but I've hit a ceiling. I can design a better program, but I can't scale it. I can't convince a school district to adopt our model across 50 schools instead of five. I can't navigate the business side of education innovation, and that's become the biggest limitation in what I can accomplish.

An MBA will give me the toolkit I'm missing. Right now, I make decisions based on intuition and what I've read online. I need rigor—in financial modeling, in market analysis, in understanding how to build sustainable operations. When I looked at schools, Booth stood out because of its analytical reputation and the way your program balances quantitative skills with real-world application.

Immediately after graduation, I want to move into a strategy or operations role at a larger education nonprofit or social enterprise. Organizations like Room to Read or The Luminos Fund work at the scale I'm interested in, and they need people who understand both the mission and the business mechanics. I'd specifically focus on program expansion—figuring out which initiatives work in one market and how to replicate them efficiently. My two years at EduVenture have taught me what effective programming looks like on the ground. An MBA would teach me how to think about it systematically.

Long-term, I'm considering two paths. The first is staying in nonprofit education and eventually running my own organization or leading operations for a large one. The second—and I'm less certain about this—is exploring edtech. I've watched startups try to solve education problems with zero understanding of how schools actually function. I think there's opportunity for someone with nonprofit experience to work in education technology, whether that's starting something or joining a mission-driven company. I'm honestly not sure which direction yet, but Booth's entrepreneurship and venture capital courses would help me explore both before committing.

What makes me ready for an MBA now is less about where I've been and more about what I'm trying to do. Two years ago, I wouldn't have been ready—I didn't know the right questions to ask. Now I do. I know the gaps in my thinking. I know I need to understand financial sustainability, strategy, and how to work with boards and funders. That clarity matters.

I came to the U.S. from Hanoi for college and stayed because the education work felt urgent. But urgency without strategy doesn't change systems. Booth would give me the credibility and framework to push beyond good intentions. That's what I'm after.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
# Ethical Dilemma in Tech

Throughout my career at Google, I have been faced with numerous challenges that have tested my values and character. One particular situation stands out as a defining moment in my professional life and helped shape who I am today.

Two years ago, I was working on a project where my team was tasked with improving our data collection processes. During our development cycle, I discovered that we were collecting user data in a way that, while technically legal, felt morally questionable. The data was being used to build profiles that could potentially be misused by third parties, even though our terms of service technically permitted it.

I faced a difficult choice. On one hand, the project was a priority for leadership, and pushing back could have implications for my career advancement and team reputation. On the other hand, I felt uncomfortable with the direction we were heading. It was an ethical dilemma that many in tech face today.

I decided to speak with my manager about my concerns. I explained that while the practice was legal, it might not align with our company values around privacy and user trust. I suggested we consider alternative approaches that would achieve our business goals while maintaining higher ethical standards. My manager was receptive and agreed that my concerns were valid.

We restructured the project to implement stronger data privacy measures. This meant more work for the team and a slight delay in the timeline, but we felt better about the final product. The new approach was ultimately approved by leadership, and the project went forward successfully.

This experience taught me that speaking up about ethical concerns is important, even when it's uncomfortable. It also showed me that most people in leadership positions want to do the right thing—they sometimes just need someone to point out the issue. I learned that you don't have to choose between being ethical and being effective; often the right thing and the smart thing align.

Since then, I have tried to maintain this principle in my work. Whether it's questions about how we handle user data, hiring practices, or how we represent our products to customers, I believe it's my responsibility to raise concerns when I see them.

I believe this kind of ethical thinking is crucial in business, and an MBA from IMD would help me develop a deeper understanding of how to navigate complex business situations while maintaining integrity. The school's focus on responsible leadership resonates with my values, and I'm excited to engage with a community that takes ethics seriously.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
When I joined Meridian Capital Partners as an analyst in 2019, I was tasked with leading due diligence on a €180 million acquisition of a Nordic manufacturing company. I was 25, fresh from my undergraduate degree, and honestly out of my depth.

The deal had a tight six-week timeline. I was responsible for building financial models, coordinating with external auditors, and presenting findings to the investment committee. Two weeks in, I discovered a significant inventory discrepancy that could have meant a €3 million write-down. Rather than flag it immediately and create panic, I spent the next week retracing the seller's accounting records with their CFO. What I found was actually more complicated than a simple error—the previous accounting system had misclassified components across product lines, inflating apparent margins.

I presented this to my deal team not as a problem, but as an opportunity. Instead of demanding a price reduction, I proposed that we could actually restructure their supply chain post-acquisition to unlock €2.1 million in annual savings. Our thesis shifted from "this business is riskier than we thought" to "we can create value if we address this systematically."

The deal closed at the original price. Within eighteen months, the operational improvements I'd outlined were implemented, and the company was generating those projected savings. When we exited that holding, it was our strongest performer that year.

I'm proud of this achievement because it required moving beyond pure technical competence. The financial modeling and due diligence were straightforward—that's what you're hired to do. What mattered was the judgment to reframe a problem, the initiative to dig deeper rather than escalate immediately, and the credibility to influence how senior partners viewed risk. It taught me that value creation in PE doesn't just come from finding the right price; it comes from having the conviction to challenge your own initial thesis.

This experience has shaped how I approach problems since. I've become the person my colleagues ask to untangle messy situations. I want to understand the architecture of why things work—or don't—rather than just react to surface-level issues.

As I consider the next chapter, I recognize that five years in deal work has given me deep financial and analytical skills, but I want to develop a broader perspective on organizational leadership and strategy that goes beyond transaction mechanics. Nottingham's curriculum, particularly the operations and strategic management modules, appeals to me because I want to think more systematically about these questions.

prompt: What are your short-term and long-term professional goals? How will our MBA program help you achieve them?
content:
# Professional Goals and Career Development

Throughout my five years working in supply chain and logistics, I have come to realize that this field is incredibly important for businesses around the world. Every day, I see how crucial it is to have efficient operations, and I am passionate about making a difference in this industry. This is why I have decided to pursue an MBA, and I believe that EAE Business School's program is the perfect fit for my goals.

My short-term goal is to move into a managerial position within the next two to three years. Currently, I work as a supply chain coordinator, and I want to develop my leadership skills and business acumen so that I can lead a team. An MBA will give me the technical knowledge and the credibility that I need to advance in my career. I believe that with this degree, companies will take me more seriously when I apply for management roles.

In the long term, I want to become a supply chain director or operations manager at a large multinational corporation. I am interested in working for companies that value innovation and efficiency, and I want to be in a position where I can influence strategic decisions. Eventually, I would like to work internationally, possibly returning to opportunities in Israel or expanding to other markets in Europe and beyond.

EAE Business School is a great choice for me because of its strong reputation and its focus on business excellence. The program offers courses in operations management and supply chain, which are directly relevant to my field. I know that the faculty at EAE are experts in their domains, and learning from them will help me gain the skills I need.

Additionally, the international environment at EAE appeals to me. Coming from Israel, I appreciate the opportunity to study at a school with students from many different countries. This will help me develop a global mindset and build a network of professionals from around the world. I believe that networking is crucial for career advancement, and EAE provides excellent opportunities for this through its alumni network and student cohort.

Another reason I am drawn to EAE is the program's emphasis on practical learning. I want to apply the theories I learn in the classroom to real business situations. The case study method and team projects at EAE will allow me to do this, and I think this hands-on approach will make me a more effective leader.

In conclusion, I am very excited about the prospect of joining EAE Business School. I am confident that this MBA will provide me with the knowledge, skills, and network I need to achieve my professional goals. I am committed to my career development and believe that an MBA is the right next step for me. I am eager to contribute to the EAE community and to learn from my classmates and professors.

school_name: University of Birmingham Business School MSc
prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
My most significant accomplishment is leading a renewable energy transition project at my current company. This project was important because it represented a shift toward sustainability in the oil and gas sector.

In my role as Senior Analyst at an energy company in Tokyo, I was tasked with evaluating the feasibility of integrating solar and wind technologies into our existing operations. I worked with a team of engineers and project managers to develop a comprehensive strategy. Over the course of eighteen months, we created a roadmap that outlined how the company could reduce its carbon footprint by investing in renewable sources alongside traditional energy production.

This accomplishment matters to me for several reasons. First, it demonstrated my ability to manage complex projects and lead cross-functional teams. Second, it showed that change is possible even in traditional industries. Working in oil and gas, I often felt conflicted about the environmental impact of our operations. This project gave me an opportunity to make a positive contribution and to feel that my work had purpose beyond just generating profits.

The project was successful in that it received approval from senior management and we began initial implementation phases. This validation was crucial for my confidence and professional growth. It proved that I could think strategically about business challenges and propose solutions that benefit both the company and society.

Beyond the professional achievements, this experience shaped my perspective on career and values. I realized that I want to work in an industry where my personal values align with what I do every day. The renewable energy sector appeals to me because it offers this alignment. I also learned that I am most effective when I have autonomy to drive meaningful projects and when I can collaborate with talented colleagues.

This is why I am interested in pursuing an MSc at University of Birmingham Business School. I believe that an advanced business degree will prepare me to take on leadership roles in the renewable energy transition. The program's focus on sustainability and business strategy aligns with my career goals. I am committed to leveraging my technical background in energy with stronger business acumen to become a leader who can drive positive change.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I started at Kanto Asset Management eleven years ago, I was the only person in the Tokyo office under 30. The firm was traditional, hierarchical, maybe even a bit rigid about how things should be done. I was brought in to help modernize our investment analysis processes, and I remember my manager saying, very politely, that "new ideas are welcome, but we should be careful not to break what already works."

That tension—between respecting institutional knowledge and pushing for necessary change—has stayed with me throughout my career. I've spent the last decade learning how to navigate it, not always successfully.

At Kanto, I moved from analyst to senior analyst to portfolio manager overseeing about $180 million in mid-cap equities. I built a small team of four analysts and introduced new screening methodologies that reduced our research time by roughly 30%. But I also made mistakes. I was impatient with colleagues who questioned new approaches, and I didn't always explain the "why" behind decisions clearly enough. There were moments I felt frustrated being a woman in a field that, even in 2024, defaults to assuming leadership looks a certain way. There were also moments I wasn't sure I belonged.

Three years ago, I began to realize that what I actually wanted to do wasn't just manage money better—it was to help shape how the next generation of investment professionals thinks about their work. That realization is why I'm considering leaving asset management. I'm not certain yet what comes next, but it will involve more direct involvement in education or developing talent.

Nagoya's Master in Management program appeals to me for specific reasons. Your requirement for an international project resonates because I've seen how parochial Japanese asset management can be, and I believe our industry needs people who understand how investment philosophy differs across regions. Your curriculum structure—moving from foundations to specialization to strategic projects—feels right for someone like me who needs to think more broadly before deciding on a particular direction. And frankly, being in Nagoya rather than Tokyo or Osaka matters. It's a chance to step outside the bubble, to think differently.

I also notice your program intentionally builds cohorts of professionals from different industries and countries. I think that's where real learning happens. In asset management, we're often siloed within finance, talking only to other finance people about finance problems. I've found that some of my best insights have come from conversations with people in completely different fields—someone from manufacturing, someone from non-profits—who asked questions I'd never thought to ask.

What I can contribute is probably not glamorous. I can bring perspective on how established institutions actually change (slowly, with friction, but it's possible). I can be honest about the difference between having an idea and being able to convince a team to execute it. I can also bring a certain humility. I've learned that the most confident people in the room aren't always right, and the quietest voices sometimes have the most important things to say.
