In conclusion, my twelve years of experience combined with an MBA from Broad will position me to take on more strategic roles and make a greater impact in the technology industry. I am eager to join your program and contribute to the diverse community of students at Broad.

prompt: Why do you intend on pursuing an MBA at this point in your life?
content:
I have always been passionate about business and technology. Working at Google for the past four years has shown me that I need an MBA to advance my career and reach my full potential as a leader in the tech industry.

My journey started in Colombia, where I grew up in a middle-class family. My parents always emphasized the importance of education, which inspired me to pursue computer science. I moved to Canada for my undergraduate degree and eventually landed at Google as a software engineer. It has been a great experience, and I have learned a lot about how large companies operate.

However, I have realized that to move into management and strategy roles, I need the credentials and knowledge that an MBA provides. At Google, I see many of my peers getting promoted into leadership positions, and many of them have MBAs. I want to be competitive in that space and develop the business skills that are necessary to succeed at the executive level.

An MBA will help me understand finance, accounting, operations, and strategy in a more formal way. Right now, I learn these things on the job, but I think having structured education in these areas will make me a better leader. I also want to network with other ambitious professionals and learn from experienced professors who have worked in the industry.

I am specifically interested in Desautels because it is a well-respected program in Canada, and the location in Montreal makes sense for me personally. I also like that Desautels has a strong reputation in the tech and finance sectors. The Executive MBA format appeals to me because I can continue working while pursuing the degree, which is important for my current situation at Google.

Additionally, I come from an underrepresented background in tech as a non-binary person from Colombia. I believe that bringing my perspective to Desautels will contribute to the diversity of the cohort. I am committed to helping create an inclusive learning environment for all students.

After my MBA, I see myself moving into a product management or strategy role at a major tech company, or potentially starting my own company. I have some ideas about building a SaaS platform that addresses inefficiencies in how companies manage remote teams, but I want to develop my business acumen first before diving into entrepreneurship.

The MBA at this point in my life is the natural next step. I have four years of solid experience at a top company, I have demonstrated my technical abilities, and now I need to build my business foundation. Desautels Executive MBA will provide me with the tools, knowledge, and network that I need to achieve my long-term goals in business and technology leadership.

I am excited about the opportunity to join the Desautels community and contribute to the program's culture of excellence and innovation.

prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
In my seven years at Meridian Manufacturing in Singapore, I've learned that the best solutions emerge when people from different backgrounds challenge each other. That's what I want to bring to Bradford.

Most of my experience has been in operations—managing production schedules, optimizing supply chains, that sort of thing. What I've noticed is that engineers and finance people often talk past each other. Engineers see inefficiencies that could be solved with new equipment; finance sees only the cost line. Neither is wrong, but neither really listens to understand the other's constraints. In 2022, I was part of a project to reduce our factory's carbon footprint. The engineering team had proposed new machinery worth $2.8 million. Before everyone just accepted it, I pushed back on the assumptions—not to kill the project, but to actually understand it. Why this vendor? Had we modeled scenarios where we phased implementation? That conversation moved us to a hybrid approach that cut costs by 40% while still hitting our emissions targets.

I'm coming to Bradford because I realize how much I don't know. I can optimize a production line, but I haven't studied strategy at a theoretical level. I don't really understand market dynamics or how pricing decisions work at the corporate level. I want to sit in classes where people are asking questions I haven't thought to ask yet.

More than that, though, I think I can actually contribute something. Not everyone in your cohort will have spent time on a factory floor or worked through supply chain crises. I've lived through the pandemic's disruptions to global manufacturing. I've seen how quickly things can break down and how much it matters to have people who actually think about implementation, not just theory. When classmates are discussing strategy cases, I want to be the person who says, "That sounds good in theory, but here's what actually happens when you try to implement that in a real business."

I won't pretend to be the loudest voice in the room. I'm more likely to ask clarifying questions and build on others' ideas than to dominate discussions. But I think Bradford needs people who listen carefully and think practically. That's what I'm offering.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
My career has been unconventional, which is probably the kindest way to describe dropping out of an engineering degree to join a software startup in Bangalore.

I was three months into my second year at BITS Pilani when I realized I was studying computer science because it made sense on paper, not because I actually wanted to spend four years in a classroom. My parents were horrified. I got a job at Wistron, a logistics optimization platform, as a Product Operations Associate in early 2022, making about ₹3.8 lakhs annually—roughly half what my engineering graduate friends were expecting. I remember my mom asking if I'd made a terrible mistake.

The first six months were humbling. I was doing a mix of everything: data entry for customer onboarding, coordinating between the engineering and sales teams, and occasionally jumping into customer support calls because we had maybe twelve corporate clients and everyone did everything. Our founder, Aditya, was chaotic in that way early-stage founders are—brilliant on strategy, completely unreliable on logistics. There was this moment where I realized that all the process problems we were complaining about weren't going to fix themselves. Someone had to build systems for a company that, up until that point, operated on Slack messages and hope.

By mid-2023, I'd moved into a Product Operations Manager role managing a team of three. My responsibility was mapping out workflows for our expansion into Southeast Asia, which was theoretically exciting until I realized we had almost no standardized processes. I spent three months building documentation, customer journey maps, and process frameworks. It was tedious, often frustrating, and exactly what the company needed. We brought on two new enterprise customers in Malaysia and Singapore—our first revenue outside India, totaling about $120K in the first year.

What's interesting is that I didn't feel particularly accomplished during this work. I remember thinking that building spreadsheets and creating Notion databases wasn't the startup romance I'd imagined. But I was also learning something critical: that operational efficiency isn't boring—it's the difference between a company that scales and one that collapses under its own complexity. I was learning to think like a founder, not an employee.

Last year, I led a major operational restructuring. Our team had grown to twenty-five people, and we were hitting coordination problems constantly. I interviewed maybe thirty percent of the company, documented their pain points, and proposed a reorganization that included splitting our operations into two teams: customer operations and product operations. It was uncomfortable recommending changes that meant I'd be managing fewer people, but it was right for the company. That restructuring happened, and our product cycle time dropped from eight weeks to five. We closed three more enterprise deals that year.

But here's what I'm being honest about: I'm still relatively junior, and there's a lot I don't know. I can't evaluate whether my operational decisions are truly scalable, I haven't worked through a major downturn or a failed product pivot, and I haven't managed large budgets or made hiring decisions with real strategic weight. I've been incredibly fortunate to work in a company where growth was rapid and my contributions were visible, but luck and favorable market conditions accelerate learning in ways that other startups might not experience.

That's actually why I'm applying to Lagos Business School right now. I'm interested in West Africa because the operating environment is messier—more constraints, more ambiguity, more room for operational leverage to actually matter. I want to understand how businesses scale in markets where the playbook doesn't exist yet. An MBA, specifically one grounded in African markets and taught by faculty who understand these contexts, would let me move from "person who built processes at a growing startup" to someone who can architect organizational systems in emerging markets. I'm not sure if that's my final answer, but right now, that's the direction I want to move.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
I still remember the moment I realized I had no idea what I was doing.

It was 2017, and I'd just been promoted to VP of Customer Success at Pinnacle, a mid-market SaaS platform in Lagos. We had maybe 40 customers, $2.1M ARR, and I was suddenly responsible for keeping them happy. The problem was that my predecessor had built the team in his image—reactive, transactional, focused on putting out fires. When I walked in, we had a 28% annual churn rate. One of our largest clients, a logistics company generating $150k in annual recurring revenue, was leaving because they felt like a ticket number, not a partnership.

My first instinct was to bring in a new process framework. I spent weeks designing what I thought was the perfect customer success model—automated touchpoints, standardized cadences, a tiered engagement strategy. I presented it to the team with real confidence. Two months in, nothing had changed. Churn was still 28%. My team was going through the motions but seemed disconnected from the work.

Then I had a conversation with Chioma, one of our most senior CSMs, that broke something open. I asked her why she wasn't excited about the new process. She said something I didn't expect: "Because you never asked us what success looked like to us. You told us what success looked like to you."

That landed hard. I realized I'd walked into leadership with a fundamental assumption—that I knew better because of my title. But Chioma and her colleagues had been doing this work for years. They understood our customers in ways I simply didn't yet. I'd treated them like executors of my vision instead of collaborators in building something better.

So I stopped. I shelved the framework I'd spent weeks building.

Instead, I started having one-on-one conversations with each CSM. I asked them: What makes you feel good about this job? What frustrates you about how we're currently handling things? What would you do if you were in my position? Over three weeks, patterns emerged. They wanted autonomy to customize their approach based on each customer's actual needs rather than following a script. They needed clearer visibility into which customers were at risk. And they needed me to actually trust them.

We rebuilt the strategy together. The "process" this time came from them. We created a modified framework that gave clear guardrails but serious flexibility. We introduced weekly customer health reviews where the team itself decided which accounts needed intervention. We changed how I spent my time—less designing systems, more sitting with them on customer calls to understand what they were seeing.

Within six months, churn dropped to 18%. Within a year, 12%. Customer NPS moved from 31 to 67. The shift wasn't about a better methodology. It was about recognizing that leadership isn't about being the smartest person in the room—it's about creating the conditions for smart people to do their best work.

That experience fundamentally changed how I approach management. I've carried it through every role since: Listen first. Build with your team, not for them. Trust is earned by making decisions transparently and explaining the why, even—especially—when you change your mind.

Rotterdam's Executive MBA appeals to me because I'm at a point where I want to deepen this philosophy. I've spent eight years learning through doing. Now I want exposure to different frameworks, different leadership traditions, and perspectives from executives across industries and geographies. The program's emphasis on reflective practice and leadership development feels like the natural next step—not replacing what I've learned, but building rigor around it.

prompt: What are your short-term and long-term professional goals? How will our MBA program help you achieve them?
content:
I came to consulting to understand how businesses really work. Eight years at McKinsey later, I've realized I understand the mechanics far better than I understand the context—and context is everything, especially in emerging markets.

Right now, I'm leading operations transformation for a pan-African financial services client across five countries. The work is technically solid: we've cut processing time by 40%, identified $8M in cost savings, we present quarterly to the board. But three months in, I noticed we were optimizing for a market structure that doesn't exist yet. We were building processes for customers who don't have smartphones. We were assuming regulatory frameworks that Lagos and Nairobi will probably never adopt exactly as written.

That's when it clicked: I've been solving problems from the outside, with frameworks designed elsewhere. If I want to actually build something that works here—whether that's scaling fintech infrastructure or restructuring regional supply chains—I need to understand African markets from the inside. Not as a McKinsey project, but as someone building.

In five years, I want to lead operations for a growing pan-African tech company or financial services firm. I'm not interested in the startup energy for its own sake; I want to apply operational discipline to challenges that actually matter for the continent. Cost optimization is valuable, but so is building systems that work when electricity is unreliable, when customers pay in cash, when borders are inefficient.

In fifteen years, I want to either be running operations at a company that's meaningfully scaled across multiple African countries, or advising governments on economic infrastructure—probably both, somehow.

Insper is exactly right for this. I'm not looking for another school that teaches Harvard Business School cases. I need a program rooted in emerging market reality, taught by faculty who've actually navigated scale in these contexts. Your curriculum emphasizes entrepreneurship and sustainability in growth markets, not as abstractions but as operational problems. Your network is specifically built across Latin America and Africa—the geographies where I want to work.

More practically, I want to fill the gaps in my background. I've never run a P&L. I've never had to make trade-offs between growth and profitability without a consulting framework to hide behind. I need both the technical skills and the permission to be uncertain.

I'm not coming to Insper to change careers. I'm coming to deepen the one I want to build.

prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
Over the past nine years in accounting, I've learned that the best solutions come from conversations that cross functional boundaries. This is what I hope to bring to Macquarie's classroom—not just technical knowledge, but a willingness to challenge assumptions and listen carefully to perspectives different from my own.

In my current role as Senior Accountant at a mid-sized firm in Milan, I manage compliance and financial reporting for clients across manufacturing, retail, and tech. What strikes me most is how differently these industries approach the same problems. A manufacturing client once asked why they should invest in automated accounting systems when their current process worked fine. Most of my team saw inefficiency. I saw a real constraint: their CFO had just retired, and the team was understaffed. The technical argument for automation wasn't wrong, but it missed the human reality. We redesigned the proposal around training and change management instead. It got approved.

This experience shapes how I want to contribute at Macquarie. I tend to ask questions like "but why do we believe that?" or "what are we not seeing here?" Sometimes these are useful. Sometimes they're annoying. I'm aware of this about myself. I've worked with people who prefer decisiveness over debate, and I've had to learn when to push and when to step back. But I think business schools benefit from people who genuinely want to understand how others think, even when—or especially when—they disagree.

As a non-binary person from Italy studying in Australia, I already sit at the margins of my classroom experience in some ways. I've found this makes me curious about how others navigate their own outsider positions, whether that's first-generation students, people from different career paths, or those who've taken unconventional routes to business. I'm interested in how those different starting points shape the way we approach problems. I want to actively create space for people to share those perspectives, even the uncomfortable ones.

I'm also realistic about my strengths and gaps. My quantitative background is solid, but my work has been primarily in compliance and tax. I'll likely be quieter in sessions on derivatives or advanced finance—at least initially. But I'm genuinely interested in learning those areas, and I plan to prepare thoroughly so I can at least ask intelligent questions. I'd rather be thoughtful and selective in my contributions than constantly perform knowledge I don't actually have.

Outside the classroom, I'm interested in Macquarie's Italian alumni network. My family's accounting firm is growing, and I eventually plan to move back to Italy to work there. I'd like to help build connections between the Sydney and Milan business communities, even if it's small. I've already reached out to a few alumni on LinkedIn, and I think there's potential for genuine collaboration.

I know that strong classroom contributions require both preparation and presence. I'm committing to both. I don't expect to be the loudest voice in the room, but I hope to be the person who listens well, asks the right questions, and genuinely wants to understand how my classmates think.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
Since graduating with my degree in Business Administration, I have been passionate about building a successful career in the financial services sector. My journey has been defined by a commitment to excellence and a desire to grow professionally.

I started my career as an Analyst at a mid-sized investment firm in Stockholm, where I gained foundational knowledge in financial modeling and valuation techniques. During my three years in this role, I developed strong technical skills and learned the importance of attention to detail when analyzing potential investments. I worked on several deals ranging from €50 million to €200 million and contributed to due diligence processes.

After this initial phase, I was promoted to Senior Analyst at the same firm. In this position, I took on more responsibility and began leading smaller projects. I worked more closely with portfolio companies and developed my understanding of operational improvements and value creation strategies. This role gave me exposure to the broader aspects of private equity beyond just the numbers.

Four years ago, I made the decision to join another investment firm as an Associate. This move was important because I wanted to experience a different investment approach and expand my network. At this new firm, I have been involved in deal sourcing, financial modeling, and portfolio management. I have gained experience across various sectors including consumer goods, technology, and industrial manufacturing.

Throughout my career, I have developed strong analytical skills and have become proficient in Excel, financial valuation, and investment analysis. I believe I have a good understanding of how to identify investment opportunities and create value for stakeholders.

Looking forward, I am interested in pursuing an MBA to deepen my knowledge of business strategy, finance, and leadership. The skills I have developed so far have been valuable, but I recognize that an MBA will help me transition into more senior roles within private equity or potentially explore other areas of finance. I am particularly interested in learning about international markets and how companies operate in different regions.

I am excited about the opportunity to study at Rotterdam School of Management and believe the program will provide me with the knowledge and network I need to advance my career to the next level.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
Why I Want to Study at Lyon University

I am writing to express my strong interest in the MSc Management Sciences program at Lyon University. After ten years working in the non-profit sector, I have come to realize that I need to deepen my understanding of business management and strategic planning to make a greater impact in my field.

Throughout my career, I have been passionate about making a difference in communities that need support. Working in NGOs has taught me the value of dedication and hard work. I have seen firsthand how proper management can transform organizations and help them reach their goals more effectively. This is why I am interested in pursuing advanced education in management sciences.

Lyon University is an excellent choice for me because it is a reputable institution in Europe with a strong program in management. The curriculum appears comprehensive and will give me the skills I need to advance in my career. I am particularly interested in learning about organizational behavior, strategic management, and operations, as these are areas where I feel I need more formal training.

My background in the non-profit sector will bring a unique perspective to your program. I have worked with diverse teams and managed projects with limited budgets, which has made me creative and resourceful. I believe I can contribute to classroom discussions by sharing real-world examples from my experience. Additionally, as someone from Israel, I can offer an international perspective that will enrich the student community.

I am committed to being an active member of the Lyon community. I plan to participate in group projects and networking events, and I am open to learning from my peers who come from different backgrounds and industries. I believe that collaborative learning is essential for personal growth.

In conclusion, the MSc Management Sciences program at Lyon University aligns well with my career goals and personal values. I am confident that this program will equip me with the necessary tools to become a more effective leader in the non-profit sector. I am excited about the opportunity to study in France and to contribute to the diverse community at Lyon University.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# EM Lyon MBA Application Essay

Throughout my career in accounting, I have always believed that thinking differently is important for success. When I joined Deloitte Turkey in 2017, I quickly realized that many traditional approaches to accounting practices were holding back innovation and efficiency. This experience taught me that challenging conventional thinking can drive meaningful change.

In my role as a senior accountant, I noticed that our team was using spreadsheets and manual processes for financial reconciliation, even though we worked at a large international firm. Every month, my colleagues would spend countless hours on tasks that seemed repetitive and inefficient. I thought about how technology could help us work better.

I proposed implementing an automation tool to streamline our reconciliation process. My manager was skeptical at first because "this is how we have always done things." However, I persisted in my belief that change was possible. I researched different software solutions and presented a detailed proposal to the partner in charge of our department.

The proposal included cost-benefit analysis and implementation timeline. After several meetings, they agreed to let me lead a pilot project with one of our major clients. I worked with the IT department to customize the automation tool to fit our specific needs. Within three months, we reduced the time spent on monthly reconciliation from 80 hours to 25 hours.

This success led to the tool being rolled out across the entire department. Other senior accountants saw the results and became supporters of the change. The firm recognized this initiative and I was given responsibility for overseeing similar automation projects for other processes.

This experience showed me that innovation does not always require completely new ideas. Sometimes it is about having the courage to challenge how things have always been done and to propose practical solutions that benefit both the team and the clients. I learned that when you believe in something, you must communicate that vision clearly and demonstrate the value through concrete results.

I believe that an MBA from EM Lyon will help me develop both my technical knowledge and my leadership capabilities. The program's focus on strategic management and business transformation aligns well with my desire to drive change in the accounting and finance industry. I am particularly interested in the courses related to digital transformation and organizational change management.

My experience at Deloitte has shown me that I have potential to become a leader who can inspire teams to embrace innovation. However, I recognize that I need deeper business knowledge to take on more strategic roles in the future. An MBA will give me the tools and perspective I need to move beyond operational improvements and to drive transformational change at a higher level.

I am excited about the opportunity to study at EM Lyon and to contribute my perspective as someone from a different cultural background and professional experience. I am committed to bringing this same spirit of challenging conventional thinking to the classroom and to my future career.

school_name: University of Queensland Business School Executive MBA
prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
When I was growing up in Cape Town, my grandmother spent her final months in a government hospital ward. The ward was clean enough, but understaffed. One nurse managed forty patients. My grandmother developed a pressure ulcer that could have been prevented with more frequent turning and monitoring. She recovered, but the experience stayed with me—not because of what happened, but because I realized the constraint wasn't money or knowledge. It was people and systems that couldn't scale.

That's what matters most to me: solving problems at scale where the bottleneck isn't resources but organizational capability.

I've spent eight years in pharmaceutical development, and this conviction has only deepened. I started as a quality assurance coordinator at a mid-sized biotech firm in Johannesburg, essentially checking documentation and following procedures. Within three years, I moved into process improvement, then supply chain coordination. By the time I left that role, our manufacturing cycle time had dropped 22%—not because we bought new equipment, but because we reorganized how teams worked and communicated.

That success taught me something crucial: you can't fix systems from the middle. You need to understand strategy, finance, and how to move people. My current role as Operations Manager at a larger biotech firm has reinforced this. We manufacture injectable medications. Last year, a product line I oversee contributed $8.2 million in revenue, but we're operating at 73% capacity because of bottlenecks in validation and release processes. I know what needs to change, but I'm constrained by governance structures and stakeholder management that I don't fully understand.

I'm not looking for an MBA to become a CEO—though I won't pretend that's not appealing. I'm looking for one because I've hit a ceiling. The problems I want to solve require fluency in areas where I'm currently superficial. How do you restructure incentive systems when you don't understand financial modeling? How do you build the business case for operational change when you can't speak the language of strategy? How do you convince a CFO that investing in better processes now protects margin long-term?

I'm drawn to UQ's Executive MBA because it respects my eight years of practical experience while giving me the tools to scale my impact. The program's emphasis on strategy and organizational change resonates with what I'm trying to solve. I'm also keen to learn from peers facing similar constraints in their industries—I suspect the peer cohort will teach me as much as the curriculum.

Here's what I've learned: the most capable people often get frustrated in organizations because they can see what's broken but can't fix it alone. The MBA isn't about confirming that I'm capable. It's about gaining the toolkit to match capability with impact. That gap between seeing the problem and solving it at scale—that's what matters most to me. And that's what I'm ready to address.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
# Kellogg's Purpose and My Leadership Journey

Three years ago, I led the restructuring of our logistics operations at a mid-sized distribution company in São Paulo, managing a team of 14 coordinators and warehouse staff. The company was hemorrhaging money—we had a 23% error rate in shipments and delivery times that frustrated our clients. I was brought in as operations manager with a mandate to fix it, but I quickly realized the real problem wasn't systems. It was that people felt unheard.

I started by doing something simple: I spent two weeks on the warehouse floor, talking to people during their breaks, asking what frustrated them about their jobs. I learned that coordinators were entering data into three different systems because departments didn't communicate. Warehouse staff were moving packages inefficiently because no one had asked them how the layout could work better. People had ideas but no one ever asked.

I reorganized the team into cross-functional groups where warehouse staff and coordinators worked together on specific routes. We consolidated to one system and redesigned the warehouse layout based on employee suggestions. Within four months, our error rate dropped to 8%, and on-time delivery improved by 31%. Revenue impact wasn't dramatic—maybe 5-6% improvement—but what struck me was the shift in how people worked. They started staying late voluntarily when we had backups. They proposed process improvements without being asked.

The experience taught me that leadership isn't about being the smartest person in the room making decisions from above. It's about creating space for people to contribute what they know. I probably spent less time on grand strategy than most managers in my position, and more time listening and facilitating.

What surprised me most was how this approach changed how I think about problems. I became less attached to my own ideas and more curious about what I was missing. That curiosity has become central to how I approach challenges now.

I'm drawn to Kellogg because of its emphasis on equipping leaders, not just crowning them. I want to deepen my understanding of organizational behavior and strategy so I can bring more intentionality to how I develop people and drive change. My analytical background is solid, but I know there's depth I'm missing, and I'm ready to build on that foundation.

prompt: Imagine yourself at the completion of your MBA journey. Why was pursuing your MBA at Texas McCombs the right decision for your personal and professional growth? Reflect on how you made the most of your time in the program—academically, through hands-on learning opportunities, and within the McCombs community. Highlight how you contributed to the McCombs community and used its resources to grow personally and professionally. Consider sharing how you applied what you learned in the program to your work.
content:
# Why McCombs

It's December, and I'm sitting in my apartment in Austin, still wearing my McCombs hoodie. I'm reviewing a contract I just negotiated for a mid-market tech company—work I could never have done with the same confidence two years ago. That's when I know the MBA was right.

I spent nine years in corporate law, climbing a predictable ladder at a Tokyo firm. Competent, yes. Fulfilled? No. I realized I wanted to build things, not just review documents about other people's ambitions. But pivoting at 32 with limited business training felt reckless. McCombs offered a bridge.

What made the difference wasn't the degree itself—it was the deliberate choices I made about how to use my time there. In Strategic Management, I led a consulting project for a B2B SaaS startup, finally understanding unit economics and customer acquisition cost beyond theory. More importantly, in that classroom, I stopped being the quiet person who had all the answers written down and started being someone who asked better questions.

The real growth happened outside classes, though. I joined the Social Impact Club, where my Japanese background became an asset rather than something I managed around. I mentored first-years navigating the career switch that scared me too. I took on a case competition in supply chain—wildly outside my comfort zone—and placed third, which mattered less than discovering I could learn a new domain quickly.

McCombs worked because it aligned with where I was actually trying to go. The case method clicked with my legal training. Austin's startup ecosystem gave me immediate places to apply what I learned. The community valued the perspective someone from outside tech brought, rather than treating it as a liability.

Now, negotiating that contract, I'm using frameworks from Corporate Finance, negotiation skills from my club work, and the confidence that comes from having genuinely changed. That's what I needed an MBA for.

prompt: If there is an important part of your story missing from your MBA application (e.g., unexplained gaps in work experience, choice of recommenders, past academic challenges), please use this section to provide a brief explanation. Please use bullets if you need to address more than one topic.
content:
I want to address a few areas in my application that may raise questions.

**Academic Performance:** My undergraduate GPA of 2.8 reflects a difficult period in my life. I was working full-time while completing my degree, and I struggled to balance both responsibilities effectively. I did not have strong study habits and made poor choices about time management. However, I have demonstrated consistent growth in my professional career and I believe my work experience speaks to my ability to learn and improve.

**GMAT Score:** I scored 595 on the GMAT, which is below the average. I prepared for about two months but found it challenging to focus on test prep while managing my job. I understand this score may be a concern, but I am confident in my ability to succeed in an MBA program through dedication and hard work.

**Recommender Selection:** I chose my current manager and a former colleague because they know my work best. They can speak to my abilities and potential.

I come from a background where higher education was not prioritized, and I am the first in my family pursuing an MBA. This program is incredibly important to me. I am ready to commit fully to my studies and I believe I can contribute meaningfully to the program. Thank you for considering my application.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
# Leadership Experience and Management Philosophy

Throughout my career in supply chain and logistics, I have learned that leadership is about making people feel valued and motivated. One experience in particular has shaped how I approach management and team dynamics.

Two years ago at my company, I was promoted to a team lead position overseeing a warehouse operations team of about 12 people. This was my first real leadership role, and I wanted to make a positive impact. The team was struggling with low morale and high turnover rates. Many employees were leaving after just a few months, which created more problems for everyone.

I realized that the previous manager had been very focused on targets and KPIs without considering the people. My philosophy became: if you take care of your people, the numbers will follow. I started implementing weekly one-on-one meetings with each team member to understand their challenges and career aspirations. I also created a more flexible shift system that allowed people to have better work-life balance. Additionally, I pushed for better safety training because I believed that a safe workplace is a happy workplace.

Within six months, we saw improvements. Turnover decreased, and our on-time delivery rates actually improved by around 8-10%. The team seemed more engaged, and people were willing to stay longer. This proved to me that investing in people is not just good for morale—it's good for business.

This experience taught me that good leadership is about empathy and understanding that people have lives outside of work. It's not just about giving orders and expecting results. I learned that you need to listen more than you talk, and you need to show genuine interest in your team members' wellbeing.

Since then, I have tried to carry this philosophy into everything I do. Whether it's a simple conversation with a colleague or making bigger decisions that affect the team, I always think about the human element. I believe that people perform better when they feel respected and heard.

However, I also understand that there is a balance. Being empathetic doesn't mean being weak or avoiding difficult decisions. Sometimes you need to hold people accountable and make tough calls. But I think the key is doing it in a way that maintains trust and respect.

As I progress in my career, I want to continue developing my leadership skills and understanding how to be an effective manager at higher levels. The EMBA at Macquarie Business School would help me formalize my management philosophy and learn from other experienced professionals in the program. I am excited about the opportunity to grow as a leader and bring my values into practice in more challenging roles.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
When I look back at my nine years in tech, I see a clear pattern: I've been good at executing, but I've never fully understood the bigger picture of *why* things work.

I started as an engineer at Samsung, building infrastructure for mobile platforms. I was competent—promoted twice, managed a team of four, shipped products to millions of users. But I sat in meetings where business leaders discussed market expansion, pricing strategy, competitive positioning, and I realized I was missing an entire language. I could optimize a system, but I couldn't optimize a business.

Three years ago, I moved to a startup in Seoul as a technical lead. The chaos was instructive. We had product-market fit in Korea, but expanding to Southeast Asia meant confronting questions I'd never seriously asked: How do we price differently by region? What does our unit economics actually tell us? Why did our approach work here and fail there? I found myself reading random business books at night, asking our CEO questions that probably should have been in my MBA application five years earlier.

That's when I knew I needed to step back and build a real foundation in management and business strategy. Not as a technical person learning some business—I wanted to understand how organizations actually function, how decisions get made under uncertainty, how to think about markets and competitive advantage.

ITAM appeals to me specifically because of your location and your focus on strategic management in the Latin American context. I've spent my career in Asia-Pacific markets, and I recognize that my knowledge of business dynamics is deeply regional. Mexico and Latin America represent fundamentally different market structures, consumer behaviors, and institutional contexts. Learning to think strategically about emerging markets beyond Asia would genuinely expand my perspective, not just check a box on my resume.

I'm also drawn to your program's emphasis on practical application—case method, consulting projects, industry immersion. I don't need another year of theory. I need to study business rigorously while staying grounded in real problems.

I'm at a point where I can contribute immediately in group work and class discussions because of my operational experience, but I'm also humble enough to know what I don't know. That combination feels right for your program.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
# Why London Business School?

From an early age, I have been passionate about business and making a difference in the world. Growing up in Italy, I was inspired by the entrepreneurial spirit and innovation that drives global business. This passion led me to pursue a career in consulting, where I have had the opportunity to work with diverse clients and develop my skillset.

After two years at a Big 4 consulting firm, I have come to realize that I need to take the next step in my career development. An MBA would provide me with the knowledge and credentials necessary to advance to senior leadership positions. I have always admired London Business School's reputation for excellence and innovation, and I believe that an Executive MBA from LBS would be the perfect fit for my career aspirations.

London Business School is known for its rigorous curriculum and world-class faculty. The program offers courses in strategy, finance, and operations management, which are all areas I am interested in developing further. Additionally, the school's location in London provides access to a vibrant business community and networking opportunities that are unparalleled in Europe.

During my time in consulting, I have worked on projects across multiple industries including financial services, retail, and technology. These experiences have given me exposure to different business models and challenged me to think critically about complex problems. However, I recognize that my current role has limitations in terms of breadth of knowledge required for senior leadership. An MBA will help me fill these gaps and develop a more comprehensive understanding of business.

Furthermore, I am excited about the diverse cohort at London Business School. The program attracts talented professionals from around the world, and I believe that learning from peers with different backgrounds and experiences would be invaluable. I am committed to contributing to this community by bringing my own perspective as an Italian woman in consulting and sharing my experiences with my classmates.

The Executive MBA format appeals to me because it allows working professionals like myself to continue developing their careers while pursuing advanced education. The flexibility of the program means I can continue working while studying, which is important for my financial stability and career continuity. I am confident that I can balance the demands of the program with my professional responsibilities.

In conclusion, London Business School represents an opportunity to achieve my career goals while joining a community of exceptional professionals. I am convinced that an Executive MBA from LBS will equip me with the tools and knowledge necessary to make a meaningful impact in the business world. I look forward to contributing to the school's community and taking advantage of all the opportunities that the program has to offer.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
I came to Tokyo three years ago expecting a two-year assignment. Staying longer was never the plan—I'm Swedish, I have family in Stockholm, and I'd built a comfortable career in Stockholm at Telia. But something happened that changed my trajectory entirely.

I was brought in to lead digital transformation for a regional telecom operator here, essentially a startup inside a legacy organization. The mandate was vague—"modernize the customer experience"—but the challenge became clear immediately. Our churn rate was 8% annually, our systems couldn't talk to each other, and our team was split between those who'd been there twenty years and those who'd been there six months. I had about €2.5M and twelve months to prove this could work.

The temptation was to build something parallel—create a shiny new division separate from the "old guard." Instead, I decided to do something harder: embed myself in the existing organization and actually change how it worked. This meant listening to legacy staff whose jobs felt threatened, understanding why they did things the way they did, and finding ways to leverage their knowledge rather than replace it. It also meant admitting when I was wrong—my first attempt at process redesign was too aggressive, and I had to walk it back publicly.

What emerged over eighteen months was a hybrid operating model. We integrated our old infrastructure teams with new cloud engineers, built an API-first architecture, and redesigned workflows around customer needs rather than departmental silos. Churn dropped to 4.2%. Revenue per customer increased 18%. More importantly, the team stopped seeing this as "my" project and started owning it themselves.

That experience taught me something crucial: I can execute well in my current role, but I'm hitting the edges of what I can achieve without deeper business literacy. I know how to drive operational change, but I don't fully understand how to structure deals, how to think about portfolio optimization, or how to translate customer insights into financial strategy. I manage a P&L of roughly €12M, but I'm making educated guesses when I should be making calculated decisions.

Waseda's Executive MBA appeals to me precisely because it acknowledges that experience alone isn't enough—that rigor and frameworks matter. The cohort-based model matters too. In telecommunications, I've learned that the best insights come from people outside my industry. I've built my professional network here in Asia, and I want to deepen it with leaders from different sectors.

I'm not coming to business school because I'm dissatisfied with technology or operations. I'm coming because I want to lead more strategically in an increasingly complex industry. I want to understand how the decisions I make cascade through organizations. I want to stay in Asia—this region has become home—and I want the credential and network that allows me to take on broader leadership roles here.

prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
**Professional Setback**

Two years into my time at Deloitte's Stockholm office, I led my first major client engagement—a digital transformation project for a 500-person manufacturing firm in Värmland. I was 28, hungry to prove myself, and convinced that my technical skills and work ethic could overcome anything. Looking back, I was dangerously overconfident.

The project was a complete failure. Within four months, the client fired us.

We'd been tasked with implementing a new ERP system across their supply chain. I'd designed what I thought was a comprehensive change management strategy, but I'd made a critical mistake: I'd done most of the thinking in Stockholm, with the client team. When we arrived for implementation, the plant floor operators—the people who actually used the system daily—actively resisted. They hadn't been meaningfully consulted. A 62-year-old foreman named Sven told me bluntly during week two that we didn't understand their business and never would if we kept talking at them rather than listening.

He was right. I'd been so focused on the elegance of our solution that I'd skipped the harder work of understanding how people actually worked. I'd assumed that a slick presentation and a detailed implementation plan would carry us. When it didn't, I got defensive. I blamed the client for being "resistant to change." I blamed my team for not executing perfectly. What I didn't do was take responsibility.

The moment everything unraveled was in a debrief meeting with my senior partner. She asked me what I would do differently. I started with the usual consultant deflection: "In retrospect, we could have allocated more resources to—" She stopped me. "No. What would *you* do differently? Not the team. You."

I sat with that question for days. The honest answer was painful: I'd been more interested in delivering the solution I'd designed than in actually solving their problem. I'd treated them like a case study rather than a real business with real constraints and real people. I hadn't asked enough questions. I hadn't listened carefully enough. I'd relied on frameworks and past wins instead of doing the difficult work of truly understanding their context.

I asked my partner if I could stay on the project—unpaid—to see if we could salvage it. She agreed, skeptically. I spent the next three weeks doing something I should have done from the start: I worked on the plant floor. I shadowed operators. I had coffee with supervisors. I asked Sven what we'd gotten wrong and, more importantly, what we'd gotten right about the parts they *were* using. I stopped presenting and started listening.

We didn't win the client back, but we did identify what would have worked. More importantly, I rebuilt the foundation of how I approach client work. Every project since, I've invested heavily in understanding the human and operational reality before designing solutions. This meant longer discovery phases, more time with frontline teams, and frankly, being comfortable with ambiguity earlier in projects.

This setback changed how I work and how I lead. When my team struggles, I ask them what they'd do differently themselves—not because I'm avoiding responsibility, but because that's when real learning happens. I've learned to see resistance as information, not failure. And I've learned that the most sophisticated strategy means nothing if the people implementing it haven't been brought along.

I'm applying to Nanjing EMBA now because I want to deepen this understanding of organizational change and human-centered leadership across different cultural and business contexts. I know that growth—both organizational and personal—requires sitting with uncomfortable truths. That Värmland manufacturing plant taught me that in the most expensive way possible. I don't plan to make that mistake again.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
When I think about creative problem-solving, I realize that in my seven years working in venture capital, I've had to approach challenges from different angles. One situation that stands out is when our fund faced a significant hurdle in sourcing quality deal flow.

Our firm manages approximately $200 million in assets, and we focus primarily on early-stage technology companies in the Middle East and North Africa region. About three years ago, we noticed that our pipeline was becoming stale. We were seeing the same founders repeatedly, and emerging talent wasn't reaching us. The traditional approach would have been to hire more associates or attend more pitch events, but both options seemed costly and inefficient given our size.

I decided to implement what I called an "ambassador program." The idea was relatively straightforward: identify successful founders we had previously invested in and ask them to refer promising companies they encountered. In exchange, we offered a small referral fee—typically between $5,000 and $15,000—if we invested in a company they introduced.

What made this creative, I think, was that we weren't just paying for introductions. We created a structured mentorship component where our portfolio companies could formally advise emerging founders. This solved multiple problems simultaneously. Founders got access to experienced mentors. We built stronger community ties. And we generated a consistent stream of pre-screened opportunities.

The results were tangible. Within the first year, approximately 35% of our new investments came through this ambassador network, compared to less than 10% previously. More importantly, these referred companies had a higher success rate—our data showed they achieved Series A funding at roughly double the rate of companies from other sources.

However, I want to be honest about the limitations of this solution. The program did introduce some complications. Managing relationships with dozens of ambassadors required more administrative overhead than I initially anticipated. Some founders became frustrated with the compensation structure or felt that we were asking too much of their time. A few referrals were clearly unsuitable, and declining them sometimes created awkward moments with people we otherwise respected.

Additionally, while the program worked well within our specific geographic focus, I'm not certain it would scale to larger funds or different regions without modification. Each market has different dynamics, and what worked in the MENA region might not work in Southeast Asia or Europe.

Looking back, I'm proud that I identified a real problem and tested a hypothesis rather than defaulting to standard industry practices. But I'm also aware that this solution, while effective, had real trade-offs. I spent considerable time managing the program that I could have spent on other activities.

This experience taught me that creative problem-solving isn't just about novelty—it's about understanding your constraints, knowing what you're optimizing for, and being willing to accept imperfect outcomes. It's also taught me that I want to develop deeper expertise in how venture capital markets operate across different regions, which is one reason I'm interested in INSEAD's diverse cohort and international perspective.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
I started my career thinking I understood risk. At a Manila-based VC fund with $150M AUM, I spent three years evaluating pitch decks and spreadsheets, comfortable in the belief that due diligence was mostly pattern-matching and financial modeling. Then I backed a logistics startup that looked perfect on paper—strong founding team, $8M TAM, 40% month-over-month growth. Within fourteen months, they collapsed because the founders couldn't navigate the political complexity of port relationships in Mindanao. The lesson wasn't about missing red flags in the financials. It was that I had no framework for understanding the human and institutional dynamics that actually determine outcomes in emerging markets.

That realization led me to a second fund, where I moved into the sourcing and partnership side. Over the past three years, I've built relationships across the Southeast Asian startup ecosystem—not as a capital provider visiting CEOs for thirty minutes, but as someone embedded in the market. I've sat through founder council meetings in Bangkok, watched how Singapore-based investors navigate regulatory hurdles that Filipino founders don't anticipate, and learned why the same business model succeeds in Hanoi but fails in Ho Chi Minh City. I've also learned how much I don't know.

This is precisely why IIM Ahmedabad makes sense to me now. India is where many of the Southeast Asian patterns repeat at scale—the emerging market dynamics, the infrastructure constraints, the regulatory unpredictability. But India is also where many investors make different choices. I want to understand why. I want to learn from faculty and classmates who have navigated these complexities successfully, who can teach me frameworks that go beyond financial engineering.

My GMAT 735 and my work experience give me competence. But IIM Ahmedabad isn't admitting me because I'm competent. You're admitting me because I'm aware of what I don't know, and I'm serious about closing those gaps. I'm at a point where passive learning through deal flow won't cut it anymore. I need the rigor of a classroom, the diversity of a cohort, and the structure of a curriculum that challenges how I think about emerging market investing.

I'm applying to IIM Ahmedabad because it's the place where I can actually learn something that matters.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
My most significant accomplishment is leading the transition to renewable energy initiatives at my previous company in Germany. I am proud of this achievement because it represents my commitment to sustainability and my ability to drive meaningful change in a traditionally fossil fuel-dependent industry.

Throughout my nine years in the energy sector, I have witnessed firsthand the urgent need for companies to adapt to a changing global landscape. The oil and gas industry is at a crossroads, and I wanted to be part of the solution. When I was promoted to Senior Project Manager, I saw an opportunity to champion renewable energy projects alongside our traditional operations.

I assembled a cross-functional team and developed a comprehensive strategy to integrate solar and wind initiatives into our portfolio. Over two years, we successfully launched three renewable projects, which generated significant interest from investors and stakeholders. This work was challenging because many colleagues were skeptical about the business case for renewables. However, I persisted and demonstrated that sustainability and profitability are not mutually exclusive.

What makes this accomplishment matter to me goes beyond the projects themselves. It represents personal growth. Early in my career, I was uncomfortable challenging the status quo. I focused on executing tasks efficiently without questioning the bigger picture. Through this renewable energy initiative, I learned to think strategically and advocate for ideas that align with my values. I realized that being a good engineer or manager is not enough—I want to be a leader who influences organizational direction.

This journey also taught me humility. Not all our renewable projects succeeded as planned. One solar installation in Bavaria fell behind schedule due to permitting delays, and we had to revise our timelines. Rather than view this as a failure, I treated it as a learning opportunity and improved our regulatory compliance processes.

Pursuing an MBA at Wits Business School is the natural next step in my career. I want to deepen my understanding of business strategy, particularly in emerging markets where energy transition is critical. South Africa's energy challenges present a compelling context for me to apply my technical expertise while developing broader business acumen. I am excited to contribute my international perspective and learn from a diverse student body.

school_name: KAIST College of Business (MSc Management Engineering)
prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
# Professional Setback and Recovery

In my nine years working in my family's textile business in São Paulo, I have faced many challenges. The biggest setback came in 2019 when I was promoted to Operations Manager and had to oversee a major restructuring project. This experience was difficult but taught me important lessons about leadership and resilience.

When I took the position, I was excited about the opportunity to improve our processes. The company had been using outdated systems for many years, and I believed modernization was necessary. I proposed implementing new ERP software across all departments and convinced the senior management team to approve a budget of approximately R$ 800,000 for this project.

However, I did not plan well enough. I underestimated the resistance from employees who were comfortable with the old systems. I also did not allocate enough time for proper training. The implementation took much longer than expected, and productivity decreased during the transition period. After six months, the project was behind schedule and over budget. My manager told me that I had failed to manage expectations and communicate effectively with the team.

This was a very difficult moment for me. I felt disappointed in myself because I had made mistakes that affected the company's performance. For a few weeks, I questioned whether I was ready for a management position.

But I decided to take responsibility and learn from this experience. I requested a meeting with my team and apologized for the poor planning. I created a new timeline and provided better training sessions to help employees adapt to the new system. I started having weekly meetings with department heads to gather feedback and make adjustments. I also learned that listening to people who have been in the company for a long time is very important, because they understand the business in ways that I did not.

After three more months of focused effort, the ERP system was fully implemented. Productivity returned to normal levels, and the system actually helped us reduce costs and improve efficiency. Looking back, I am grateful for this setback because it taught me about humility and the importance of change management.

This experience has shaped how I approach problems now. I understand that technical solutions alone are not enough—you need to consider the human side of business. I have become a better listener and more patient with people who are resistant to change.

I believe that an MBA will help me develop the management skills I need to lead larger teams and more complex projects in the future. I am particularly interested in studying organizational behavior and strategic management at KAIST to deepen my understanding of these areas. I am ready to grow from this experience and become a more effective leader.

prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
# Essay Response

When I tell people I work in management consulting, their eyes usually light up. They ask about the travel, the prestige, the clear path to partnership. What they don't ask about is the part that keeps me up at night: knowing exactly how good I am at building a spreadsheet but being genuinely uncertain whether I'm building anything that matters.

I've spent the last eight years at one of the top consulting firms, and I'm good at what I do. My utilization rates are solid, I've staffed larger engagements, and I've been told multiple times that I'm "on track" for the next promotion cycle. But somewhere around year five, I started noticing something uncomfortable. I could diagnose a company's supply chain inefficiency in my sleep. I could present a 80-slide deck and make it feel like gospel. What I couldn't do was look back at a project and know for certain that my recommendations actually changed how the client operated six months later.

Last year, I worked on a transformation project for a mid-sized manufacturing company in Ohio. The engagement was textbook consulting: three months, a team of four, a final recommendation to restructure their operations. I was proud of the work—really proud. But I stayed in touch with my primary contact there, which I don't usually do. A year later, she told me that maybe 40% of what we recommended actually got implemented. The rest hit organizational inertia, competing priorities, leadership turnover. Our analysis was sound. The execution was someone else's problem.

That conversation broke something open in me. It made me realize I don't actually know if I want to climb the consulting ladder anymore. I might. But I also might want to work inside an organization where I can see the second and third-order effects of my decisions. Where I'm not leaving at the project closure. Where I'm responsible not just for the diagnosis but for whether the patient actually gets better.

The honest part is that I don't have a clean answer about what comes next. I'm not someone who discovered their passion and now everything aligns neatly. I'm someone who realized that prestige and promotion aren't the same as impact, and I need some time and space to figure out what actually drives me. An MBA makes sense right now because it buys me time to explore without derailing my career entirely. It also means learning from people in different industries who might have grappled with similar questions.

Wisconsin appeals to me partly because it's not Harvard or Wharton, and I think that matters for what I'm trying to figure out. I want to be around people building real businesses in real regions, not just optimizing for the next job title. I want to learn from folks who know what it feels like to own a decision from start to finish.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
# Ivey MBA Application – Personal Accomplishment Essay

Throughout my twelve years in venture capital, I have had many opportunities to grow both professionally and personally. My most significant accomplishment, however, came when I successfully led a Series B investment round for a promising fintech startup in Southeast Asia. This experience has shaped my understanding of what it means to be a true leader and investor.

When I first encountered this company, it was a relatively small operation with a talented team but limited market traction. The founders had a vision to disrupt the payments space across the region, and I believed in their potential. I decided to take a hands-on approach to the investment, not just committing capital but also providing strategic guidance throughout the fundraising process.

The Series B round was challenging. We needed to raise $10 million in a competitive landscape where many other investors were skeptical about the market opportunity. I spent countless hours preparing pitch decks, managing investor relations, and conducting due diligence. My ability to understand both the technology and the business model helped me communicate the investment thesis to potential partners. Eventually, we secured the funding from a combination of new and existing investors, bringing the total capital raised to over $25 million.

What makes this accomplishment significant is that it demonstrated my capacity to drive results in a complex environment. The success of this round opened doors for the company to expand into new markets and hire top talent. Today, the company has grown substantially and has become one of the leading fintech players in the region. Knowing that my involvement contributed to this success is deeply fulfilling.

This experience matters to me for several reasons. First, it reinforced my belief that having the right support system – whether through capital, advice, or networks – can make a tremendous difference in a startup's trajectory. Second, it showed me that success in venture capital is not just about picking winners, but about actively contributing to their growth. This hands-on mentality has become central to my investment philosophy.

Beyond the financial returns, what truly resonates with me is the human element. I developed a strong relationship with the founders, and I was able to mentor them through critical business decisions. Seeing them grow as leaders and witnessing their team's dedication to solving a real problem in the market has been incredibly rewarding. This is why I believe venture capital is more than just a financial pursuit – it is about building and nurturing innovation.

The fintech space in Southeast Asia is evolving rapidly, and I want to deepen my impact in this ecosystem. An MBA from Ivey will equip me with the strategic frameworks and business acumen to take my career to the next level. I am particularly interested in understanding how companies scale globally and how to identify emerging market opportunities more systematically. The curriculum at Ivey, combined with its strong network of entrepreneurs and business leaders, aligns perfectly with my goals.

Reflecting on this accomplishment, I realize that I still have much to learn. The complexities of international expansion, regulatory challenges, and talent management are areas where I want to develop greater expertise. An MBA will provide me with the rigorous training and perspective needed to navigate these challenges more effectively.

In conclusion, my most significant accomplishment has been instrumental in shaping my career and my vision for the future. It has taught me that success is not measured solely in dollars, but in the lasting impact you have on people and businesses. I am excited about the opportunity to bring my experience and learning mindset to Ivey, and to contribute meaningfully to my classmates' perspectives on venture capital and entrepreneurship.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
# Creative Solution Essay

When Hakan and I started our digital marketing agency in Istanbul in 2017 with about $8,000, neither of us knew we'd end up solving a problem that would eventually let us scale to 35 employees across three countries. We certainly didn't know it would change how I think about running a business.

The problem hit us in late 2019. We were growing fast—around 40% year-over-year—but we were losing clients at nearly the same rate. Not because our work was bad. Our campaign performance metrics were solid. The issue was that our clients couldn't see the value. We'd deliver a detailed monthly report with conversion data, impressions, CTR—all the right metrics—but by the third month, clients would either leave or demand steep discounts.

I blamed ourselves. I thought our team wasn't executing well enough. Hakan thought we needed to hire more senior strategists. We were both wrong, but it took talking to a client over coffee to realize it.

Aslı, the CMO at a mid-sized B2B SaaS company in Ankara, was leaving us. When I asked why, she didn't complain about our work. She said, "I can't explain what you're doing to my CEO. You send me this massive PDF every month, but I don't know if I should be happy or worried." That sentence stuck with me.

The insight was uncomfortable: we were experts talking to experts, but our experts weren't talking to *their* decision-makers. The CEO doesn't care about bounce rate. The CFO doesn't care about impressions. They care about revenue impact, because they're the ones accountable for the marketing budget.

Here's what we built: Instead of a traditional report, we created a one-page "Executive Brief" that led with a single number—revenue attributed to our campaigns, calculated conservatively based on their own conversion data. Below that, we showed three contextual insights: one about market conditions, one about what their competitors were doing, and one about what we'd recommend changing next month. That's it. No charts with 15 data points. No jargon.

We kept the detailed reports, but made them optional—for the actual marketing people who wanted to dig into the numbers.

When we started sending these to clients, the first tangible sign that something was working came when Aslı forwarded me an email she'd sent to her CEO: "I'm including the marketing brief with the financials this month. Take a look at the bottom line." Three days later, she called to expand our contract.

It wasn't magic. We were delivering the same value. We'd just finally translated it into a language the person signing the check could understand. Over the next 18 months, our client retention improved from 68% to 89%, and our average account value went up 23%. More importantly, referrals became our primary growth channel because satisfied CFOs and CEOs were talking about us.

The reason I'm telling you this is because it taught me something I didn't expect: technical skill isn't enough. The most sophisticated marketing strategy fails if you can't communicate its value to someone who doesn't share your expertise. That lesson has shaped how I lead. I've started pushing our account managers to understand their clients' business goals, not just their marketing goals. I've gotten better at asking "why does this matter?" before I start explaining the how.

I'm applying to Temple Fox's Executive MBA because I'm ready to deepen that instinct. I want to understand business structure, finance, and operations in a more formal way. Running a growing company has taught me what I don't know. The EMBA program seems built for people like me—we've done some things right, we've learned from failing, and we need to get more strategic.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
# Kellogg's Purpose: My Leadership Journey

Leadership has always been important to me. Throughout my career, I have demonstrated leadership in many ways and situations. I believe that a brave leader is someone who is not afraid to take risks and make difficult decisions for the greater good.

In my current role as founder of a technology startup, I have had the opportunity to lead a team of talented individuals. When I started the company three years ago, I knew that I wanted to create a workplace culture that valued innovation and collaboration. I worked hard to build a strong team and we have achieved many successes together.

One example of my leadership was when our company faced a difficult decision about our product strategy. The team was divided on which direction to take, and I had to make a tough call. I listened to everyone's opinions, considered the data, and ultimately decided to pivot our business model. This was a brave decision because it was risky, but I believed it was the right thing to do for the company's future. In the end, the pivot was successful and we were able to grow our revenue.

Another way I have demonstrated leadership is by mentoring junior employees and helping them develop their careers. I am passionate about helping others grow and succeed. I have coached several employees who have gone on to promotions and new opportunities. This is very rewarding for me.

I also believe that brave leaders must be willing to admit when they are wrong and learn from their mistakes. I have made many mistakes in my entrepreneurial journey, and I have learned valuable lessons from each one. These experiences have made me a better leader.

Kellogg's mission to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders resonates deeply with me. I am excited about the opportunity to learn from world-class professors and talented peers. I know that an MBA from Kellogg will help me become an even braver and more effective leader. I am committed to using my education to make a positive impact on the world and to lead with purpose and integrity.

school_name: IESE Business School Master in Management
prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
During my time at Maison Legale, a mid-sized law firm in Paris, I encountered a situation where I had to manage the interests of multiple stakeholders with competing priorities. This experience taught me valuable lessons about leadership and compromise.

Our firm had a major corporate client, a manufacturing company, who needed assistance with a contract negotiation. At the same time, the firm's partners wanted to maximize billable hours and profitability. The client, however, was concerned about costs and wanted us to work efficiently. Additionally, my team members were already working overtime and wanted more reasonable hours.

I realized that all these different needs had to be addressed. The partners wanted revenue, the client wanted good service at a fair price, and my team wanted work-life balance. This was a delicate situation that required me to think carefully about what to do.

I decided to take action. First, I met with the partners and explained that by working more efficiently, we could actually improve client satisfaction and potentially win more business through referrals. I suggested that we focus on quality over quantity of hours. Then I worked with my team to create a more organized workflow that would reduce unnecessary work and allow them to leave at reasonable times.

For the client, I proposed a fixed-fee arrangement instead of hourly billing. This was good for them because they could predict costs, and it was good for us because it motivated us to be efficient. Everyone seemed happy with this solution.

The negotiation itself went well. Our client got their contract sorted out, and they were satisfied with our service. The firm still made a decent profit, and my team was able to work normal hours. By the end of the project, the client said they would recommend us to other companies, which was very positive feedback.

This experience showed me that sometimes you don't have to choose between different stakeholder interests—you can find solutions that work for everyone. I learned that communication is key, and that efficiency and quality can go hand in hand. I also learned that good leadership means listening to different perspectives and trying to find the best path forward.

This situation reinforced my belief that business is not just about making money or winning at all costs. It's about creating value for different groups and building relationships that last. In the future, I want to work in roles where I can balance the needs of different stakeholders and help organizations make decisions that are good for everyone involved.
