I believe this experience demonstrates the kind of thinking that will be valuable in an MBA program. Understanding how to manage competing interests is crucial in any business environment. The Master in Management at IESE will help me develop these skills further and prepare me for leadership positions where I can apply this kind of balanced thinking to bigger challenges and different industries.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
I have always been impressed by ESADE's reputation as a leading business school in Europe. Throughout my career in management consulting at Deloitte, I have worked with many companies that value the kind of education that ESADE provides. When I began researching MBA programs, ESADE was naturally one of the first schools I considered due to its strong rankings and global network.

My interest in ESADE grew after I visited the website and learned more about the program structure. The Master in Management is an excellent program that offers a comprehensive curriculum covering all aspects of business. I was particularly interested in learning that the program includes courses in strategy, finance, and operations, which are all areas that I believe are important for developing business leaders.

I have also attended several webinars hosted by ESADE admissions, which gave me insight into the student experience and the faculty expertise. These sessions reinforced my belief that ESADE is the right fit for my career goals. The speakers discussed how the school emphasizes teamwork and collaboration, which resonates deeply with my experience in consulting, where teamwork is essential for success.

One thing I particularly appreciated was learning about the Barcelona location and the diverse student body. Europe is an important market, and being based in Spain would give me access to opportunities across the continent. Additionally, the international student composition means I would be learning alongside peers from different backgrounds and cultures, which I believe would enhance my business education.

In terms of how I would contribute to the ESADE community, I bring six years of experience in management consulting, where I have worked on various projects across multiple industries. This experience would allow me to contribute meaningfully to classroom discussions and group work. I am confident that my perspective as someone who has worked in the field would add value to my cohort.

Furthermore, I am passionate about making a difference in the business world, and I believe that an ESADE education would provide me with the tools and knowledge necessary to achieve my goals. The program's focus on developing ethical business leaders aligns with my personal values and career aspirations. I am committed to being an active member of the ESADE community, whether through participating in clubs, attending networking events, or engaging with the alumni network.

Upon graduation, I plan to leverage my ESADE degree to pursue a role in strategy consulting or move into a corporate strategy position, potentially in a tech company. The skills I would gain from the program, combined with my existing consulting background, would position me well for these opportunities.

In conclusion, ESADE represents an ideal opportunity for me to advance my career and personal development. The program's rigorous curriculum, excellent faculty, and vibrant community make it the perfect place for me to grow as a business leader. I am excited about the prospect of joining the ESADE community and contributing to its continued excellence.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I started my career in the pharmaceutical industry with the goal of making a meaningful impact on healthcare. After completing my undergraduate degree in Life Sciences, I joined a major pharmaceutical company as a Junior Regulatory Affairs Specialist.

In this role, I was responsible for ensuring compliance with regulatory requirements across different markets. I worked with cross-functional teams to prepare documentation for product submissions and liaised with regulatory agencies. This experience taught me the importance of attention to detail and understanding the regulatory landscape. I realized that regulatory affairs was a critical function in bringing medicines to patients.

After two years, I was promoted to Senior Regulatory Affairs Specialist, where I took on more responsibility. I managed a portfolio of products and worked on submissions for the ASEAN region. During this time, I developed stronger project management skills and learned how to navigate complex bureaucratic processes. I became more confident in my ability to lead initiatives and coordinate with multiple stakeholders.

Recognizing the need to broaden my expertise, I made a lateral move to the Quality Assurance department. This transition was challenging because I had to learn new systems and processes, but I was determined to grow beyond my functional expertise. In this role, I oversaw quality control operations and implemented several process improvements that increased efficiency. I also gained exposure to manufacturing, which gave me a better understanding of the entire product lifecycle.

Most recently, I have been working as a Senior Quality Manager, where I manage a team of five people and oversee quality assurance for multiple product lines. My responsibilities include developing quality strategies, managing audits, and ensuring compliance with international standards. I have successfully led initiatives to reduce non-conformances by 30% and improved our audit performance metrics.

Throughout my career, I have been driven by the desire to contribute to the pharmaceutical industry and improve healthcare outcomes. I have taken on diverse roles that have expanded my technical knowledge and leadership capabilities. Each position has provided valuable lessons about working in a regulated industry and managing teams effectively.

However, I recognize that my career progression has been relatively linear within the same company. I believe an MBA from Stellenbosch Business School will help me develop a more strategic perspective on business and prepare me for senior leadership roles. The program will give me exposure to different industries and business functions, which will be invaluable as I move forward in my career. I am particularly interested in understanding how business strategy and operations intersect, as this will enhance my ability to drive meaningful change in the pharmaceutical sector.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
My career in consumer goods has been a journey of continuous growth and learning. I started as a junior marketing executive at a mid-sized FMCG company and have progressed through various roles, developing my skills in brand management, product development, and team leadership.

When I first entered the industry in 2013, I was eager to make an impact. I took on responsibilities across marketing campaigns and worked on several successful product launches. During this time, I learned the fundamentals of how consumer brands operate and developed a strong foundation in understanding market dynamics and consumer behavior. This experience was invaluable as it gave me insight into the fast-paced nature of the industry.

Over the next few years, I moved into brand management roles where I had more strategic responsibility. I worked on portfolio optimization and helped develop go-to-market strategies for new products. I collaborated with teams across sales, operations, and supply chain to ensure our brands were competitive in the marketplace. These roles taught me the importance of cross-functional collaboration and understanding how different departments contribute to overall business success.

In my mid-career, I transitioned into a senior marketing position where I managed a team of four people. Leading a team was challenging but rewarding. I learned that effective communication and clear goal-setting are crucial for team performance. I also gained experience working with external agencies and managing budgets in the millions of pounds range. This role gave me confidence in my ability to manage complex projects and lead people toward shared objectives.

Most recently, I have been working in a strategic commercial role that combines elements of my previous experience. I have been involved in market analysis, pricing strategy, and exploring new market opportunities for our brands. This position has allowed me to think more broadly about business strategy rather than just focusing on individual brand campaigns.

Throughout my career, I have encountered various challenges and setbacks. There have been times when product launches did not perform as expected or when I had to navigate difficult team dynamics. These experiences have taught me resilience and the importance of learning from failure. I have come to understand that not every strategy works, and being able to adapt and pivot is essential in a competitive industry.

What I have realized through my 11 years in the industry is that while I have gained considerable experience, I feel I lack a more formal understanding of international business, global strategy, and the broader frameworks that govern how multinational organizations operate. My background has been somewhat concentrated in the UK market, and I want to expand my perspective on how consumer goods companies operate across different regions and cultural contexts.

An MSc in International Management would provide me with the theoretical knowledge and global perspective I currently lack. I believe Exeter Business School would be an excellent environment for this next phase of my development. The program's focus on international business and management would complement my practical industry experience and help me transition into more strategic, globally-focused roles.

I am keen to challenge myself academically and to gain qualifications that will support my career progression. I believe that combining my industry experience with formal business education will position me well for more senior roles in international management, potentially in multinational organizations or consulting.

In conclusion, my career has been defined by gradual progression and growing responsibility. I am now at a point where I recognize the value of structured education to complement my practical experience. I am committed to making the most of the opportunity that an MBA or MSc program would provide.

prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
When I joined Deloitte's Southeast Asia consulting practice six years ago, I quickly realized that the best insights came not from the smartest person in the room, but from the person willing to ask the uncomfortable question. I want to bring that same energy to Ivey's classroom.

In my current role at Deloitte, I work across supply chain and organizational design projects. A few years in, I noticed something: junior consultants often held back their perspectives in client meetings because they assumed their ideas weren't "polished enough." I started organizing informal lunch sessions where we'd debate case studies without the pressure of hierarchy. Those conversations often surfaced the most creative solutions. I realized that speaking up—even imperfectly—matters more than waiting for the perfect moment.

This is directly relevant to classroom participation. I don't expect to have all the answers, especially coming into an MBA with quantitative gaps (my GMAT score reflects this). But I'm comfortable sitting with uncertainty and pushing my own thinking out loud. In consulting, I've learned that the willingness to be wrong publicly often accelerates everyone's learning. I plan to ask the clarifying questions that others might skip, flag assumptions that seem off, and build on classmates' ideas even when they're half-formed.

I'm also aware that I bring a specific lens to discussions. As a non-binary person from Jakarta now based in Sydney, I've navigated organizations where my identity didn't fit neatly into existing boxes. In consulting, this has made me attuned to inclusion issues—who gets invited to which meetings, whose voice shapes strategy, what perspectives we're missing. During a recent organizational redesign project, I pushed the team to interview frontline workers, not just managers. That became the turning point in our recommendations. I expect I'll notice and name these dynamics in class too, and I hope that contributes something.

Beyond the classroom, I'm interested in Ivey's case competition culture. I've led case competitions internally at Deloitte, and I know the value of pulling together diverse problem-solving approaches. I'd want to participate, and I'd bring the real-world context of how cases actually play out (spoiler: implementation is messier than the case suggests). I'm also keen on connecting with international students—India, Southeast Asia, Latin America particularly. The consulting work has made me comfortable in cross-cultural settings, and I think there's real value in Ivey students understanding how business actually works outside North America and Europe.

I won't pretend to be the person who raises their hand in every class or dominates group projects. That's not my style. But I show up prepared, I listen carefully, and I contribute when it counts. I push back on surface-level thinking, and I try to make space for quieter voices. That's the kind of peer I want to be at Ivey.

prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
# Professional Setback Essay

Two years ago, I made a decision that cost our startup nearly $200,000 and damaged my credibility with the leadership team. I was the Product Lead at a Tokyo-based B2B SaaS company called Synapse, and I pushed hard to build a new feature for our enterprise clients—a custom reporting module that I believed would differentiate us from competitors.

The problem was I didn't listen. Our Head of Engineering warned me the timeline was unrealistic given our team size of six developers. Our only customer success manager flagged that three of our top-five accounts hadn't requested this feature. I dismissed both concerns because I was convinced I understood the market better than anyone else. I was 24, promoted quickly, and frankly, I was arrogant.

We spent four months building it. The feature launched with bugs, and worse, only two customers actually used it. We eventually sunset the module. The financial impact was brutal—it set back our product roadmap by two quarters, and it meant we couldn't hire the additional support staff we desperately needed. But the real damage was relational. My team stopped volunteering ideas in meetings. People defaulted to doing exactly what I asked, no more, no less. I could feel the trust had eroded.

For about three weeks, I was genuinely depressed about it. I considered leaving the company entirely. I remember sitting in a coffee shop near Shibuya, thinking that maybe I wasn't suited for this kind of responsibility.

Then I did something uncomfortable. I asked my manager for brutally honest feedback. Not the HR-sanitized version, but what she actually thought. She told me: "You're talented, but you mistake conviction for competence. You need to learn the difference between having a strong opinion and being right."

That landed. Hard.

I started rebuilding, and it was deliberate. First, I implemented a decision-making framework where major product decisions required written input from at least three functions—engineering, customer success, and finance. Not to stifle my thinking, but to stress-test it. Second, I created what I called "red team sessions" where I explicitly asked people to poke holes in my proposals. Initially it felt defensive. Gradually, it became natural.

The shift wasn't instant. It took maybe six months before I noticed people starting to volunteer ideas again without prompting. But it happened. And honestly, our product got better. By the time I left Synapse last year, the three products I overseen in my final year had much stronger adoption rates because they were genuinely informed by what customers needed, not what I thought was clever.

What I learned goes beyond project management methodology. I learned that the people around me have real expertise I don't have, and ignoring it isn't confidence—it's blindness. I learned that being wrong is actually useful data if you're willing to sit with the discomfort. I learned that recovery isn't about proving everyone wrong next time; it's about building systems where you're less likely to make the same mistake.

I'm applying to Nanjing University's EMBA because I'm at a point where I want to formalize what I've learned through failure and broaden my perspective beyond pure product thinking. My next role will likely be General Manager of a division or Head of Strategy, and I know I need deeper financial literacy and exposure to how senior leaders make decisions. More importantly, I want to study alongside experienced professionals who've navigated their own setbacks. The kind of humility I developed the hard way should be part of my leadership foundation going forward, and an EMBA program in an environment outside Japan feels like the right place to deepen that.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
# Creative Solution Essay – CUHK Business School

When I started at Villanueva & Associates in Manila two years ago, I was assigned to work on contract disputes for our SME clients—mostly manufacturers and logistics companies. The problem seemed straightforward: clients would come to us with payment disputes, we'd review documents, and send a demand letter. It was textbook legal work.

But I noticed something that bothered me. About 60% of our clients came back within six months with the same vendor in a new dispute. We were solving their immediate problem but not their underlying issue. They were signing contracts they didn't fully understand, often rushing through terms because they didn't have time to review dense legal language.

I mentioned this to my supervising partner, Maria Santos, and she basically said: "That's how it works. We get paid when disputes happen." Which was true. But it felt wasteful—both for our clients and for us, since half our time was spent on preventable conflicts.

I decided to build something, though I had no idea if it would work. I created a one-page "contract checklist" for our common contract types—purchase agreements, supplier agreements, freight contracts. Nothing fancy. Just plain questions: "Does this specify payment terms clearly?" "What happens if delivery is late?" "Are penalties defined?" I color-coded it red/yellow/green based on risk level.

I tested it with one client first—a textile manufacturer called Songhe Imports. I walked through the checklist with their operations manager for about 30 minutes before they signed their next agreement. It took time, but when I reviewed the contract afterward, they'd caught a payment clause that would have created a major problem down the line.

Then I proposed something to Maria: let me offer this to all our SME clients for a flat fee of 8,000 pesos ($150) instead of waiting for disputes. She was skeptical. But we had capacity, and if it prevented even a few disputes, the opportunity cost was minimal.

Over the next year, I refined the checklist based on actual contracts we reviewed. I added templates for common amendments. I created a simple Excel tracker so clients could log all their vendor contracts in one place. It wasn't sophisticated, but it was practical.

The numbers surprised both Maria and me. Of the 43 clients who used the service, only 2 came back with related disputes in the next twelve months—down from the 60% rate. The service brought in about 344,000 pesos in additional revenue annually. More importantly, clients started referring us to other companies specifically for this service.

What this taught me isn't revolutionary: that businesses have problems that sit in the gap between what they need and what they're currently paying for. I didn't need complex technology to solve this. I needed to listen, notice a pattern, and build something simple that addressed the real constraint—client time, not lack of legal knowledge.

This is why CUHK appeals to me. I've seen how Hong Kong and Greater China focus on operational excellence and practical solutions in business. I want to deepen my understanding of how to scale what I've started—how to build sustainable models that serve real business problems. An MBA will give me the frameworks to think bigger about my work, and CUHK's emphasis on business across Asia specifically matches where I want to apply this thinking.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
# Creative Solution Essay – INSEAD Abu Dhabi

Three years ago, my unit faced a problem that our standard operating procedures couldn't solve. We were managing security logistics for a multinational naval task force operating across the Eastern Mediterranean and Gulf regions. Nine different nations, nine different communication systems, nine different interpretations of the same safety protocols. When a potential threat developed, we'd lose critical minutes—sometimes longer—translating requests between allied partners. This wasn't theoretical inefficiency. It cost us operational tempo and created genuine safety gaps.

The traditional solution would have been to push the decision up the chain of command: write a report, wait for interagency working groups, request funding. But we didn't have that timeline. I proposed something unconventional instead.

I created what we called the "Tactical Harmony Protocol"—essentially a standardized decision tree with visual icons rather than language-dependent text. Instead of writing "All vessels maintain 500-meter separation unless authorized by commanding officer," we used a simple diagram: a circle with distance markers and color-coded approval levels. A Spanish frigate captain and a Greek officer could understand it instantly, without translation.

But the real insight came from involving the people who actually used the system. I brought together junior officers and enlisted personnel from each nation—the people who lived with the problem daily—and made them core to the design process. We held informal working sessions in the ships' cafeterias, not in conference rooms. A French petty officer suggested we build in redundancy using hand signals for critical messages. An American ensign flagged that our color scheme didn't work for colorblind crew members. A Turkish lieutenant realized we needed different protocols for nighttime operations.

It took six weeks to design something that actually worked, because we iterated constantly based on real feedback. Then came the harder part: implementation. I knew I couldn't just hand it down and expect adoption. Instead, we ran low-pressure simulation exercises where the protocol was optional. Crews that used it completed scenarios faster. Crews that didn't made more mistakes. The data did the convincing for me.

Within four months, all nine navies had integrated the protocol into their operations. It sounds simple now, but the outcome was measurable: communication delays in urgent situations dropped from an average of four minutes to under thirty seconds. We actually saved a crew during a medical emergency when faster coordination enabled helicopter deployment.

What I learned—and what changed how I lead—is that the best solution often isn't the most technically sophisticated one. It's the one designed with the people who'll implement it, not for them. I was trained to give orders and expect compliance. That works in a crisis, but for sustainable change, you need buy-in built on understanding why something matters.

This is part of why I'm drawn to INSEAD Abu Dhabi specifically. The program's emphasis on diversity and real-time problem-solving resonates with what I've learned works. I've spent nine years in environments where decisions have immediate consequences and diversity isn't a checkbox—it's operational necessity. I want to study in a setting that mirrors that reality while pushing me into different sectors and perspectives. Abu Dhabi's location and international cohort align with how I want to evolve as a leader.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# Essay: Challenging Convention to Drive Innovation

When I started my first company in Berlin three years ago, everyone told me I was approaching the problem wrong. I was building a B2B SaaS platform for managing inventory in small retail shops—a crowded market with dozens of established competitors. The conventional wisdom was clear: either you build enterprise software for large corporations, or you build something flashy for consumers. The middle market was considered too small to be profitable.

My co-founders and I disagreed. We had worked in retail operations before starting the company, and we noticed something nobody seemed to be addressing properly: small shop owners didn't actually want complicated software. They wanted something simple enough that they could train employees in an afternoon, not something that required a dedicated IT person.

The industry standard was to build feature-rich platforms with endless customization options. Every competitor was doing this. Every pitch deck we saw emphasized the breadth of functionality. So we did the opposite. We built the simplest version possible—basically what you could do in a spreadsheet, but online and with automatic alerts. That was it. No advanced analytics. No predictive modeling. No integrations with seventeen different payment systems.

Our investors hated this. "You need more features," they said. "The market expects enterprise-grade functionality." But we stuck with our instinct and started talking directly to shop owners. We found that they would actually pay more for simplicity than for features they'd never use. A 45-year-old woman running a bookshop in Hamburg didn't care about machine learning; she wanted to know when to reorder stock without calling her supplier.

We launched with 12 shops in Berlin and Munich. Within eighteen months, we had 2,400 customers across Germany and Austria, generating €180,000 in monthly recurring revenue. We never added those advanced features everyone said we needed.

What I learned from this experience is that challenging convention requires more than just being contrarian. It requires actually listening to your customers instead of assuming the industry knows what's best. Most of my competitors never talked to their customers this way. They were too busy matching each other's feature sets.

The second lesson was harder: knowing when to stay stubborn and when to admit you're wrong. After about two years, we realized that our simplicity had limits. Larger retailers wanted more features, and we were losing deals to Shopify and other platforms. So we built a modular system where basic features stayed simple, but power users could add complexity if they needed it. We challenged convention again, but differently—by refusing to believe that "simple" and "scalable" had to be mutually exclusive.

This experience shaped how I think about business problems. I'm naturally skeptical of industry consensus. I ask why things are done the way they are. But I've also learned that innovation isn't just about being different; it's about being right in ways that matter to real people.

I'm applying to IIM Calcutta because I want to deepen my understanding of business strategy and scaling. I've learned a lot through doing, but I recognize the gaps in my knowledge. I want to study frameworks for market analysis, operations management, and growth that can complement my entrepreneurial experience. India's startup ecosystem is booming, and I'm interested in understanding how businesses scale differently across markets.

The innovation I'm most proud of wasn't flashy or technically impressive. It was the simple decision to make something useful instead of something impressive. I think that perspective—that sometimes the most valuable innovation is about clarity and focus—is something I'll bring to your program, and something I hope to develop further.

prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
# Essay Response

Three years ago, I made a decision that cost my real estate development firm nearly $2.3 million and taught me more about leadership than any business school case study could.

I was leading a mixed-use development project in Lagos—a 45,000 square meter commercial and residential complex aimed at the rapidly growing Lekki business district. The project had strong fundamentals: solid investor backing, proven market demand, and what I believed was a bulletproof timeline. I had overseen two successful projects before this, and I was confident. Perhaps too confident.

The trouble started with contractor selection. We received four bids, and I chose the lowest-cost proposal without conducting the thorough due diligence I should have. My reasoning was straightforward: we needed to hit our budget targets, and the contractor had decent references. I didn't dig deeper. I didn't visit their previous sites extensively. I didn't talk to their past clients about their actual experience working with them, only read their formal testimonials. I was moving fast, and I convinced myself that speed was more important than caution.

Six months into construction, cracks appeared. Literally and figuratively. The contractor was cutting corners on materials, missing deadlines consistently, and their communication was sporadic at best. By the time we realized the severity of the issues, we were already eight months behind schedule. The structural defects were significant enough that we couldn't simply push forward. We had to stop, bring in a new contractor, and essentially restart major sections of the work.

The financial impact was brutal. Beyond the $2.3 million in additional costs, we lost investor confidence, faced legal complications, and I personally experienced something I hadn't felt before in my career: real doubt about my judgment. My team was demoralized. One of my senior project managers left the company. I remember sitting alone in my office one evening, thinking about how my overconfidence had created a mess that affected not just numbers on a spreadsheet, but people's livelihoods and my investors' trust.

I had to own it completely. I called our main investor and explained exactly what happened, where I had failed, and what we would do differently. I didn't make excuses. I then brought the team together and we rebuilt the project plan from scratch—this time with proper scrutiny at every step.

What changed: First, I implemented a mandatory vendor review process that includes site visits and conversations with at least three previous clients. It takes time, but it's non-negotiable now. Second, I stopped treating budget constraints as a reason to skip due diligence. If anything, tighter budgets demand more careful vetting, not less. Third, I learned to actively listen to my team's concerns. My project manager had flagged concerns early, but I had dismissed them because I was locked into my initial decision.

The project eventually completed successfully, 18 months later than planned. We delivered a quality product, but the experience humbled me significantly. I realized that 11 years of relative success had made me overconfident in my instincts. I was making decisions based on what had worked before, not based on the specific conditions in front of me.

This experience has shaped how I lead now. I'm more systematic about risk assessment. I'm more willing to slow down to move forward correctly. I'm also more aware of my biases and more intentional about seeking contrary views before making major decisions.

Looking forward, this is part of why an MBA appeals to me. I want to strengthen my analytical foundation and learn frameworks that prevent emotional decision-making from clouding judgment. I believe Executive MBA programs, particularly one focused on real-world application, would help me build the disciplined thinking I need for the next stage of my career.

school_name: Yonsei University School of Business MiM
prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
I grew up in Ho Chi Minh City in a household where my parents ran a small textiles trading business. By "small," I mean my father worked from a desk in our living room, and my mother managed inventory in a room adjacent to where I did my homework. The business was profitable enough to send me to a decent international school, but it never felt stable. Some years were good; others, my parents would argue about cash flow at dinner.

When I was thirteen, my father made a decision that scared him: he turned down a lucrative contract from a large buyer because the terms were unsustainable for our factory partners. My mother thought he was insane. I remember him explaining to me afterward that short-term gains weren't worth relationships built over years. He took less money to restructure the deal fairly. That supplier stayed with us for another decade.

I mention this because it shaped how I approach problems, but I want to be honest—I didn't become some principled crusader. I became someone obsessed with data and frameworks. At McKinsey, I've spent two years building models, running regressions, and presenting decks to C-suite executives. I'm good at it. I've been staffed on interesting projects: helping a fintech company in Bangkok pivot their payment processing, optimizing a manufacturing supply chain across Vietnam and Thailand. My counselor tells me my growth trajectory is strong.

But here's what doesn't fit neatly on my resume: I often feel like I'm playing chess with other people's futures. I sit in strategy sessions where we recommend workforce reductions that will save a client $15 million annually. The number is clean. The impact is abstract. I understand the business logic—if you don't optimize, your competitor will, and then everyone loses their job. But my father's textile business taught me something different: that sustainable decisions require holding multiple truths at once. That efficiency and relationships both matter.

I'm applying to Yonsei MiM because I want to think differently about how businesses create value. I'm drawn to your program's emphasis on responsible management and stakeholder capitalism—not as a buzzword, but because I genuinely believe the next phase of my career needs to ground my analytical skills in something more grounded. Your curriculum on sustainable supply chains, your partnerships with Korean conglomerates navigating family business transitions, and frankly, your location in Korea where I want to deepen my understanding of East Asian business practices—these are specific reasons, not generic ones.

I don't know yet if I want to return to consulting at a higher level, move into corporate strategy, or eventually do something with my family's business. What I do know is that I'm at a point where pure optimization feels incomplete. I need exposure to frameworks that don't reduce human systems to unit economics.

My GMAT score isn't elite, and my work experience is brief. But I learn quickly, and I'm genuinely uncertain about the right direction. That uncertainty is actually my strength—it means I'm not coming to Yonsei with answers, but with real questions. I want to spend eighteen months wrestling with how to be a good strategist and a good human simultaneously.

prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
# How I'll Contribute to Classroom Discussions and Student Life

During my seven years at Goldman Sachs in Madrid, I've learned that the best deals happen when people from different backgrounds challenge each other. I spent most of my time in the Financial Institutions Group, closing €200-300 million transactions, but the moments I remember most aren't the closed deals—they're the conversations in the hallway afterward where someone from Operations would poke a hole in our strategy, or a junior analyst would ask the question nobody wanted to ask.

I plan to bring that mentality to the EAFIT MBA classroom.

My banking background naturally positions me to contribute to finance-focused discussions. I can walk through how we structured a cross-border acquisition, what drove credit spread decisions during the 2020 crisis, or why a particular valuation method made sense in context. But honestly, that feels like the obvious contribution. What I'm more interested in is bringing the perspective of someone who's lived in a very narrow ecosystem—investment banking is insular, competitive, and risk-averse in ways that don't translate cleanly to how business actually works in the real world.

I spent two years managing relationships with large Spanish industrial companies as a client, and I was shocked by how differently they operated compared to what we discussed in my undergraduate economics classes. The CEO of a €500 million manufacturing firm wasn't optimizing for quarterly earnings; she was thinking about family legacy, employee relationships, and regional stability. Our models completely missed that. That gap—between what we assume about how businesses operate and how they actually operate—is something I want to understand better, and it's something I want to bring up when we're discussing strategy or organizational behavior.

I'm also interested in how the EAFIT community approaches problems differently than the Spanish banking environment I come from. Spain is traditionally hierarchical; decisions flow down from senior partners. But I've noticed in my recent conversations with EAFIT alumni that the culture here seems to value more direct collaboration, even across seniority levels. I want to learn how that actually works, and I want to test my assumptions about whether that's genuinely more effective or if it's just a different trade-off.

Beyond the classroom, I'm realistic about what I can contribute. I'm not the kind of person who'll organize every social event—my work schedule in banking has made me someone who shows up, participates fully, and then needs to catch up on sleep. But in smaller settings, I think I add value. I'm genuinely curious about how people from different industries think, and I ask questions that might seem obvious but often aren't. I'm also someone who will say "I disagree" when I do, which some groups need.

What I don't want to do is show up as the banker guy who has all the answers about finance and nothing else. That feels like a waste of everyone's time, including my own.

The EAFIT MBA appeals to me partly because I think I need to break out of the investment banking bubble. I want to be pushed by people who've built things, who've failed, who've worked outside of Spain. I'm committing to being the person who listens first, contributes specifically, and stays humble about how much I don't know.

prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
I am excited about the opportunity to join EDHEC's Executive MBA program and contribute meaningfully to the classroom and broader student community.

Throughout my nine years in the pharmaceutical industry, I have developed a deep passion for learning and sharing knowledge with colleagues. I believe this mindset will translate well to the MBA environment. In my current role at a biotech firm in Milan, I manage cross-functional teams and am exposed to diverse perspectives daily. This experience has taught me the value of collaboration and open dialogue, skills that are essential for enriching classroom discussions.

I am particularly interested in how business strategy intersects with innovation in the life sciences sector. During classroom discussions, I plan to bring real-world examples from my experience in drug development and regulatory affairs. I will contribute thoughtfully to case studies and group projects, drawing on my background to offer unique insights that may benefit my peers. I believe that by sharing what I have learned over nearly a decade in this field, I can help other students understand the complexities of the pharmaceutical landscape.

Beyond the classroom, I am committed to being an active member of the student community. I would be interested in participating in clubs and events, particularly those focused on entrepreneurship and innovation. As a non-binary individual, I also value diversity and inclusion, and I hope to support initiatives that foster a welcoming environment for all students. I think it is important that business schools reflect the diversity of the world we live in.

I am also eager to learn from my classmates. I recognize that an MBA program brings together individuals from various industries and backgrounds, and I am genuinely curious about their experiences and perspectives. I plan to build meaningful relationships with my peers and contribute to a collaborative learning environment where everyone feels valued.

In summary, I am committed to making a positive impact on EDHEC's community through active participation, the sharing of my professional experience, and a genuine commitment to diversity and inclusion. I look forward to growing alongside my classmates and contributing to the richness of the program.

prompt: What are your short-term career goals and how will the Full-Time MBA program at Michigan State University's Broad College of Business help you achieve them?
content:
# What are your short-term career goals and how will the Full-Time MBA program at Michigan State University's Broad College of Business help you achieve them?

When I tell people I'm Swedish and I work in accounting, they usually nod politely and change the subject. It's not the most glamorous starting point. But what they don't see is the problem I became obsessed with while working as a senior auditor at Deloitte Stockholm for the past four years: why do so many companies treat financial controls like a compliance checkbox instead of a competitive advantage?

I've sat through countless control implementations where the CFO just wanted to check a box for the auditors. I've watched companies hemorrhage money through process inefficiencies that a thoughtful financial systems strategy could have prevented. And I realized that my technical accounting knowledge—while solid—wasn't enough to drive real change. I could identify the problems, but I didn't have the broader business acumen to propose solutions that actually stuck.

My short-term goal is to transition into financial planning and analysis for a mid-sized manufacturing or industrial company. Not because it's prestigious, but because that's where I think I can have the most immediate impact. These companies typically have less sophisticated FP&A functions than tech companies or banks. They need someone who understands both the accounting foundations and the strategic business questions: How do we optimize our capital allocation? What's driving margin compression in our largest business unit? How should we structure our financial reporting to give leaders better visibility?

The reason I'm applying to Broad—specifically—is because I've researched your manufacturing and supply chain concentration heavily, and it's genuinely where my interest is pointing. I'm not applying to fifteen schools hoping one says yes. Your program's partnerships with companies like the ones I want to work for, combined with the integrated core curriculum, felt like the right fit for what I actually need.

Here's what I know I need: I need to strengthen my general business strategy skills, which are my weakest area. I spent four years getting very good at identifying risks; I need training in identifying opportunities. I also need the network and recruiting access that an MBA provides—I'm realistic about that. Moving from audit to FP&A is possible internally, but it's harder. An MBA from Broad gives me credibility in the U.S. market, which matters if I eventually want to move back to Europe in an FP&A role at a larger company.

What I don't need is another deep dive into accounting theory. What I need is your operations course, your strategic finance course, and the ability to actually test my ideas in a capstone project working with a real company.

I'm twenty-eight, and I've spent four years learning that I'm good at detailed technical work but that I want to build something bigger. The MBA is a reset button that lets me lean into the strategy side while keeping the financial rigor I've developed. Broad's culture around practical application—not just theory—is what drew me to Michigan State. Your graduates go to work with real tools and real confidence, not just textbook knowledge.

I'm ready for that next step.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
# Leadership in Private Equity

Leadership is about making tough decisions and inspiring your team to achieve great results. Throughout my three years in private equity, I have had the opportunity to lead in various capacities, and one experience stands out as particularly meaningful.

During my time at Meridian Capital Partners in Madrid, I was assigned to oversee a portfolio company in the manufacturing sector. The company was struggling with operational inefficiencies, and the team morale was quite low. As someone relatively new to the role, I knew I had to make an impact quickly.

I began by meeting with each department head individually to understand their concerns and ideas for improvement. This listening approach helped me identify that the main issues stemmed from poor communication between teams and outdated processes. I then organized a company-wide meeting where I presented a vision for change that was ambitious but achievable.

Over the next six months, I implemented new project management systems, established regular cross-functional meetings, and created clear KPIs for each department. I made sure to celebrate small wins along the way, which helped boost morale. The results were impressive—we increased operational efficiency by 22% and reduced costs by approximately €1.2 million annually.

What made this experience meaningful was not just the financial results, but the personal growth of the team members. Several people who were previously disengaged became more motivated and took on leadership roles themselves. One team member was eventually promoted to a senior position, which I believe would not have happened without the supportive environment I helped create.

This experience taught me that brave leadership sometimes means taking risks and trusting your team, even when you don't have all the answers. It also showed me the importance of empathy and communication in driving meaningful change.

I believe an MBA from a top institution will further develop my leadership capabilities and help me tackle even more complex business challenges in the future. I am excited about the opportunity to learn from experienced faculty and accomplished peers.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
# Toulouse Business School Executive MBA Application

I am most proud of leading the expansion of our organization's education program across Southeast Asia. When I joined the NGO seven years ago, we operated in only three countries with limited reach. Through my efforts, we have now expanded to eight countries and impacted over 50,000 students annually.

This achievement is significant because it demonstrates my ability to scale operations and create lasting change. Education is fundamental to breaking cycles of poverty, and being able to extend our programs to more regions meant more children could access quality learning opportunities. I took initiative in identifying new markets, building partnerships with local governments, and training regional teams to implement our curriculum standards.

The expansion process required strong planning and coordination. I conducted market research in potential target countries, assessed feasibility, and created implementation roadmaps. I worked with our finance team to secure additional funding and with our program directors to ensure quality standards were maintained across all locations. Managing a team of thirty people across different time zones and cultural contexts taught me the importance of clear communication and flexibility.

One challenge we faced was maintaining program quality while expanding rapidly. Different regions had different needs and constraints. I established monitoring systems and regular feedback mechanisms to ensure consistency. We also created training programs for local staff so they could adapt our core curriculum to their specific contexts while maintaining our educational values.

This expansion has been transformative for the organization. Our annual budget increased significantly, we attracted more international donors, and our reputation grew substantially. More importantly, we are now helping many more students than before. The ripple effects extend to teachers who receive better training, schools that gain resources, and entire communities that benefit from improved education systems.

Why am I proud of this achievement? Because it combines operational excellence with meaningful impact. It required me to master multiple skills—strategic planning, financial management, team leadership, and cross-cultural communication. It also challenged me personally. There were moments of doubt, especially when dealing with unexpected obstacles or cultural misunderstandings. But I persevered because I believed in the mission.

This achievement has prepared me well for an executive MBA. I have gained substantial professional experience and demonstrated my ability to execute complex projects. However, I recognize that to advance further in the non-profit sector and potentially move into leadership roles, I need deeper knowledge in business strategy, organizational development, and financial management. An executive MBA will provide the analytical frameworks and business acumen I need to become a more effective leader.

Toulouse Business School's Executive MBA appeals to me because of its focus on global business and its strong community of international professionals. I am eager to learn from faculty and peers who bring diverse perspectives and to contribute my own experience from the non-profit sector.

prompt: Describe a difficult decision you made and the process that you went through to make it.
content:
# A Difficult Decision

Throughout my career, I have faced many challenging decisions that have shaped me as a professional and as a person. One decision in particular stands out as being especially difficult and transformative.

During my time at a pharmaceutical company in Lima, I was working as a junior analyst in the regulatory affairs department. After two years with the company, I was offered a promotion to a senior analyst role. However, at the same time, I received a job offer from a biotech startup in Santiago, Chile. This startup was working on innovative drug delivery systems and seemed like an exciting opportunity for growth.

The decision was difficult because both paths had their merits. The promotion at my current company offered stability, a higher salary, and the security of working at an established organization. On the other hand, the startup offered the chance to work on cutting-edge technology and potentially make a bigger impact on the industry. I was torn between these two options.

To make this decision, I did several things. First, I made a list of pros and cons for each opportunity. I thought about my long-term career goals and what would be best for my professional development. I also spoke with mentors and colleagues who gave me valuable advice. They helped me see different perspectives on the situation.

After careful consideration, I decided to stay at my current company and accept the promotion. I realized that I still had much to learn from my current role and that the stability would allow me to build a stronger foundation for my career. I also felt a sense of loyalty to my company and my team, who had invested in my development.

However, I do sometimes wonder what would have happened if I had chosen the startup path instead. But I believe I made the right choice, and I have been happy with my decision. The promotion gave me new responsibilities and allowed me to grow as a professional. I learned valuable skills in project management and cross-functional collaboration that I believe will serve me well in business school and beyond.

This experience taught me the importance of carefully considering difficult decisions and seeking advice from others. It also showed me that there is no single "right" answer to difficult questions. Sometimes you have to make a choice and commit to it, even if you are not entirely certain.

I believe this experience demonstrates my ability to think critically and make thoughtful decisions. It shows that I am capable of weighing complex options and considering different perspectives. These are skills that I will need in business school and in my future career, and I am excited to develop them further at your institution.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
I have always been passionate about making a difference in the world through business and innovation. Growing up in Peru, I saw firsthand how media can shape society, and I wanted to be part of that change. This is why I am excited to apply to IE Business School and pursue my MBA.

My career in media and entertainment has been fulfilling. I have worked in various roles where I learned about the industry and developed my skills. I believe that studying at IE will open doors for me and help me achieve my ambitions. The location in Madrid is also appealing because Europe is an important market for media and entertainment.

Regarding my GMAT and GPA, I want to acknowledge that my test scores are not as strong as they could be. I faced some personal challenges during my undergraduate years that affected my academic performance.
