prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
Three years ago, I made the worst decision of my management career, and it taught me everything I needed to know about leadership.

I was a coordinator at Logística Ibérica, managing a team of four warehouse staff in our Barcelona facility. We handled distribution for a mid-sized consumer goods company—about 200 shipments daily. When our supervisor left unexpectedly, I was promoted into her role. I was 26, ambitious, and convinced that efficiency was the only metric that mattered.

For the first six months, I drove the team hard. I implemented new tracking software, tightened procedures, cut overtime by 23%, and increased throughput. On paper, I looked like a star. In reality, I was destroying morale.

Then one Monday, two of my best people gave notice on the same day. María, who'd been with the company for eight years, told me bluntly: "You treat us like machines, not people. You don't even know that my daughter started school this year." I felt sick. I genuinely didn't know. I'd been so focused on optimizing processes that I'd stopped seeing the humans executing them.

That week, I did something I'd never done before: I asked for help from my manager, not as a confession of failure, but as a genuine conversation. She was honest. She said I had strong analytical skills but was missing basic emotional intelligence. She assigned me to mentor under our regional operations director, Javier, who managed three facilities with wildly different demographics and challenges.

Javier taught me that consistency isn't cruelty. He showed me that one fifteen-minute conversation with an employee about their family, their goals, their frustrations, could yield more than three months of directives. He had me observe how he managed his team—he asked questions before giving answers. He admitted uncertainty. When a major contract was lost due to a planning error on his watch, he didn't blame the staff; he took responsibility and then involved them in solutions.

I spent the next eight months deliberately changing how I led. I started weekly one-on-ones with each team member. I asked about their lives. I explained the "why" behind decisions, not just the "what." When someone made a mistake, I focused on teaching rather than punishing. I learned to say "I don't know, let's figure this out together" instead of pretending to have all answers.

Within a year, I'd rebuilt trust. We didn't lose anyone else. Productivity remained strong—we actually improved it—but now people wanted to come to work. I got promoted to manage a second facility with this approach.

That experience crystallized my management philosophy: **People execute strategy. Efficiency without buy-in is temporary. Real leadership means creating conditions where your team feels valued and trusted enough to bring their best work.**

I'm drawn to KAIST's MBA because South Korea's approach to operations and process improvement is globally respected, but I want to combine that rigor with the human-centered management I've learned to value. I'm specifically interested in your supply chain management track, which I believe could deepen both my analytical capabilities and my understanding of how to lead diverse, distributed teams—something I know I still need to improve. I want to lead in an industry that demands both precision and humanity, and I believe KAIST can help me integrate those seemingly opposing forces into a coherent leadership approach.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
When I took over as Director of Curriculum Development at Bangkok International Institute three years ago, I inherited a team that was fractured. The previous director had managed through command-and-control—weekly meetings where she'd announce decisions already made, teachers nodded, and nothing changed. Enrollment was declining. Teachers were disengaged. The institution was coasting.

My first instinct was to shake things up immediately. I wanted to restructure the entire curriculum, bring in new teaching methodologies, everything. But I made a mistake in my first month that stopped me. I announced a major change to our assessment system without consulting anyone. One of my senior teachers, who had been with the school for twelve years, didn't just disagree—she resigned. That hurt. I realized I was repeating the same pattern I was trying to break.

I regrouped. Instead of top-down change, I started small. I created what we called "curriculum circles"—six people, rotating membership, meeting biweekly to tackle one specific problem. The first group looked at why students weren't engaging with science classes. Rather than me presenting solutions, I asked them to diagnose the problem themselves. What I learned surprised me. It wasn't the content or the teaching method. Students felt disconnected because the curriculum didn't connect to anything in their lives.

Over the next eighteen months, we rebuilt the entire upper school curriculum around real-world applications. Math classes included business case studies. Literature courses examined how media shapes society. I had to resist the urge to direct every decision. Sometimes the solutions the teams proposed weren't what I would have chosen. But they owned the implementation in a way they never had before.

The results were measurable. Enrollment increased by 32 percent over two years. Teacher satisfaction scores rose from 58 to 78 out of 100. But more importantly, I developed a management philosophy: leadership isn't about having all the answers. It's about creating conditions where talented people can contribute their best thinking.

This experience taught me that you can't scale an organization by being the smartest person in the room. You scale by distributing responsibility and trusting people to solve problems within their domain. I still set the vision and hold people accountable. But I've learned that the best insights come from people closer to the work than I am.

I'm applying to IIM Ahmedabad's PGPX program because I want to sharpen this philosophy with more rigorous management frameworks. My current knowledge is mostly experiential—I've learned through trial and error in an educational setting. I want to understand organizational design, change management, and strategy at a deeper level so I can lead larger, more complex institutions effectively. India's education sector is growing rapidly, and I believe IIM Ahmedabad can help me prepare for a bigger role in that space.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
When I was asked to turn around our Newcastle warehouse operations in 2021, I was handed what felt like an impossible brief: reduce picking errors by 40%, cut delivery times in half, and somehow do it with the same budget and headcount. I'd been at the online retailer for three years in a steady role managing logistics for the Midlands region, and honestly, I wasn't sure I was ready for something this large.

The Newcastle facility was hemorrhaging money. Error rates were at 8.2%—nearly triple the company average—and customer complaints were climbing fast. My predecessor had tried to patch things with software updates and stricter policies. Everyone expected the same approach from me. Instead, I spent my first three weeks on the warehouse floor, working actual shifts alongside the pickers, packers, and forklift operators. I wanted to understand the problem from their perspective, not just from spreadsheets.

What I discovered was that the team felt invisible. Targets came down from above, penalties were applied, but no one ever asked them what was actually broken. The real issue wasn't laziness or incompetence—it was that our warehouse layout was designed for a product mix we'd stopped selling two years ago. Pickers were walking an extra 12,000 steps per shift chasing items across the facility.

I convinced leadership to fund a modest redesign—about £45,000—plus a three-month implementation window where we'd actually lose productivity initially. This required pushing back on quarterly targets, which felt genuinely risky. But I presented the data clearly: the long-term mathematics made sense.

What I'm most proud of isn't just the numbers, though they mattered. Within four months, we'd hit 2.1% error rates and dropped average delivery times from 4.8 days to 2.3 days. We got there because I listened first and imposed solutions second. I also created a bonus structure tied to accuracy and speed that benefited the warehouse team directly—nothing dramatic, but meaningful. When people felt ownership, they moved differently.

The achievement wasn't mine alone. Sarah, one of our most experienced pickers, suggested we reorganize by product velocity instead of category. Marcus, a supervisor, redesigned the packing stations. But I think what I'm genuinely proud of is that I created space for their expertise to matter.

This experience fundamentally changed how I approach problems. I've since moved into a broader operations role covering our entire South region, managing £12 million in annual logistics costs across six facilities. But I keep returning to that lesson: the people closest to the work usually understand it best.

An executive MBA appeals to me because I want to deepen my strategic thinking around these human systems questions. I'm not interested in optimizing processes for their own sake—I want to understand how to build organizations where people's best thinking actually gets used. Otago's integration of leadership and business strategy, paired with its emphasis on real-world application, feels like the right next step.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
During my eight years in tech, I have learned that leadership is about inspiring people to achieve great things. One moment that stands out is when I led a team project at my current company.

I was working as a senior analyst at a SaaS firm, and I noticed that our sales team was struggling with productivity. I saw an opportunity to make a difference. I decided to take initiative and proposed a new process to my manager that would help streamline our workflows and improve our overall efficiency.

My manager agreed with my proposal, and I was given the responsibility to lead a cross-functional team of about six people to implement this new system. This was my first real leadership experience, and I was excited but also nervous about the challenge ahead.

I organized weekly meetings where everyone could share their thoughts and concerns. I listened carefully to what people had to say and tried to incorporate their feedback into the process. Some team members were resistant to change at first, but I worked hard to convince them that this new approach would benefit everyone in the long run.

After three months, we successfully implemented the new process. The results were positive – we saw a 20% increase in team productivity, and people seemed happier at work. My manager was impressed with what we accomplished, and I felt proud of what we had achieved as a team.

This experience showed me that leadership is not about being the smartest person in the room. It is about listening to others, being patient, and helping people understand the vision. I learned that when you empower people and make them feel valued, they will work hard to achieve the goals you set together.

Through this project, I realized that I want to continue developing my leadership skills and take on more challenging roles in the future. An MBA from Kellogg's would help me become a better leader and equip me with the tools and knowledge I need to succeed in a more senior position. I am excited about the opportunity to learn from experienced professors and network with talented peers who share my passion for business and leadership.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# Vlerick MBA Essay: Challenging Convention

When I joined EY's Manila office as an audit associate two years ago, I expected to spend my days checking spreadsheets and flagging compliance issues. Instead, I found myself asking a question that got me pulled into a conference room with senior partners: "Why are we still doing this manually?"

The "this" was our client relationship management system for mid-market manufacturing firms. EY had been using the same client intake process for seven years—a hybrid of email questionnaires, printed forms, and Excel files that got passed between audit teams. It took roughly 40 hours to organize materials for each new client and another 15 hours to identify red flags. For a portfolio of 60 active clients, that's roughly 3,300 hours annually. When I calculated the cost, it came to around ₱2.6 million just sitting in inefficiency.

But here's the thing: everyone knew this was inefficient. Senior managers joked about it in the hallway. My supervisor had even mentioned wanting to "fix it someday." The conventional wisdom, though, was that EY's centralized IT department would never prioritize a single office's workflow problem, and besides, "that's just how client onboarding works." The system worked. It was slow, but it worked.

I decided to not accept that.

Rather than escalate through formal channels—which would have meant waiting months for IT review—I spent three weekends learning Airtable and building a prototype. I didn't ask permission because I knew what the answer would be. Instead, I created a system that let clients input their own financial documents through a simple portal, automatically categorized risk factors, and flagged anomalies using conditional logic. It wasn't sophisticated AI. It was frankly basic automation. But it worked.

When I showed my supervisor the prototype, I expected skepticism. Instead, she pushed me to present it to the engagement manager for our largest client—a family-owned textile manufacturer processing ₱450 million in annual transactions. I was terrified. I was a two-year-in junior accountant showing a half-built tool to someone who'd been doing this for 20 years.

That manager ran my prototype on their next onboarding cycle. Intake time dropped to 12 hours. The risk-flagging accuracy improved because the system was catching things human eyes had missed. She asked me to scale it.

Here's where I learned something important: challenging convention doesn't just mean having a good idea. It means being willing to look foolish and to persist when people are skeptical. It means understanding that "this is how we've always done it" usually means "nobody's asked this question before," not "this is the optimal way."

Over the next eight months, we refined the system with input from five different client teams. I learned to present technical ideas to people who weren't technical. I learned to push back on requests that would overcomplicate the process. By my second anniversary at EY, the system was running onboarding for 18 clients and saving approximately 1,800 hours annually—almost halving our original spend.

What made this moment stick with me wasn't getting credit or recognition. It was realizing that innovation often doesn't require brilliance or resources. It requires someone willing to question why things are the way they are and then actually do something about it instead of just complaining.

This is why I'm drawn to Vlerick. I want to work in an environment where asking "why" isn't seen as friction—where you're encouraged to challenge frameworks and build solutions. The strong emphasis on entrepreneurship and real-world case studies in your curriculum appeals to me because I've learned that the most valuable business insights come from actually building things, not just analyzing them.

I'm ready to bring that same relentless questioning to my MBA. I just need the space to ask better questions.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
# Career Aspirations and the Role of an Executive MBA

I have always been passionate about making a difference in the business world. Throughout my career, I have developed a strong interest in venture capital, and I believe that pursuing an MBA from London Business School will be transformative for my professional journey.

My career aspiration is to become a successful venture capital investor and eventually lead my own investment fund. I am drawn to the world of startups and innovation because I believe that entrepreneurship is the key to solving global problems. Working in venture capital for the past two years has shown me the incredible potential of early-stage companies to create positive impact while generating returns for investors.

At my current role in a venture capital firm in Manila, I have been exposed to deal analysis, portfolio management, and investor relations. These experiences have given me valuable insight into how investment decisions are made and what makes certain companies succeed while others fail. I am passionate about identifying promising founders and helping them scale their businesses to achieve their vision.

London Business School's Executive MBA will be a crucial catalyst for my career advancement. The program's focus on global business perspectives aligns perfectly with my goal to eventually expand into international markets and develop a more comprehensive understanding of the global venture ecosystem. I am particularly interested in how entrepreneurship differs across regions and how investors can better support founders in navigating these differences.

The curriculum at LBS offers courses in corporate finance, strategy, and entrepreneurship that will deepen my technical knowledge in areas where I currently have gaps. Additionally, the diverse cohort of experienced professionals will provide me with a network that spans multiple industries and geographies. This network will be invaluable as I work towards establishing myself as a respected investor in the venture capital space.

Beyond academics, I am drawn to London as a global hub for innovation and finance. Being based in one of the world's most dynamic business centers will expose me to cutting-edge trends in technology and entrepreneurship. I will have the opportunity to engage with leading entrepreneurs, investors, and thought leaders who are shaping the future of business.

My background as a Filipino professional brings a unique perspective to the program. I come from a country with an emerging startup ecosystem and deep entrepreneurial spirit. I believe I can contribute meaningfully to class discussions about emerging markets, Southeast Asian business practices, and the unique challenges faced by founders in developing economies. At the same time, I am eager to learn from my peers about how business is conducted in mature markets.

The Executive MBA format is particularly appealing to me because it allows me to continue working while pursuing my degree. This balance will enable me to apply concepts learned in the classroom immediately to my current role, creating a feedback loop that enhances both my professional contributions and my learning experience. I am committed to maximizing every aspect of the program, from core courses to electives to networking events.

In five years, I see myself having expanded my deal flow beyond the Philippines, possibly working with cross-border investments between Southeast Asia and Europe. In ten years, I hope to be leading a fund that specifically focuses on emerging market founders with global ambitions. An MBA from London Business School will equip me with the strategic thinking, financial acumen, and international network necessary to achieve these goals.

I am confident that LBS will serve as the catalyst I need to elevate my career to the next level and make a meaningful impact in the venture capital industry.

prompt: What are your short-term and long-term professional goals? How will our MBA program help you achieve them?
content:
When I walked into McKinsey's Cairo office in 2016, I told myself I'd stay for two years. Eight years later, I'm still here—but not because I'm stuck. I've stayed because I've learned that impact in emerging markets doesn't follow the typical partner-track timeline that consulting firms optimize for.

My short-term goal is straightforward: transition into a leadership role at a growth-stage company operating across North Africa and Europe. I've spent the last three years leading transformation projects for energy and fintech clients, watching firsthand how European firms enter African markets—often with brilliant strategies and terrible execution. I want to build something different. Within two years, I want to be running operations or strategy for a company that actually understands both contexts because its leadership lives in both contexts.

The longer-term goal is messier, and I'm honestly still figuring it out. I don't want to start my own firm (I've seen what that costs). I don't want to stay in pure consulting forever (I need to build something concrete). What I know is that I want to be the person who bridges the European playbook with emerging market realities—someone who can speak the language of Basel III compliance and also knows why a fintech app won't work in rural Egypt without offline functionality.

Grenoble specifically appeals to me because of your geographic position and your program's focus on European business with real emerging market engagement. I've read about your Africa initiative and your faculty's research on emerging market strategy. That's not a bolt-on addition to your curriculum; it seems woven into how you think about business. I also need a program that won't ask me to start from scratch professionally. At eight years in, I need peers who bring real experience and instructors who won't insult our intelligence with generic case studies.

The MBA I need isn't a credential reset or a brand name on my LinkedIn. It's a structured space to think strategically about the next chapter instead of just reacting to the next client project. Grenoble's one-year format and European network offer exactly that—intensity without interrupting the trajectory I've built, and access to perspectives I don't have sitting in Cairo.

prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
Failure is something that everyone experiences in life, and it is important to learn from these experiences. Throughout my career in manufacturing, I have faced several challenges that have taught me valuable lessons.

One of the most significant failures in my professional journey occurred about three years ago when I was working as a production supervisor at a factory. I was responsible for managing a team of about 20 workers, and we were tasked with meeting a tight deadline for a major client. I believed that I could push the team hard and we would achieve the goals without any problems.

However, due to poor planning and lack of communication, the project failed. We missed the deadline by two weeks, and the client was very disappointed. The company lost money, and my reputation was damaged. At first, I was angry and blamed the team for not working hard enough. But after some reflection, I realized that the problem was with my leadership style and my inability to communicate effectively with my team members.

This failure taught me that success requires more than just hard work and determination. I learned that as a leader, I need to listen to my team, plan better, and communicate clearly about expectations and deadlines. I also learned that blaming others is not productive and that taking responsibility for mistakes is essential.

After this experience, I decided to improve myself. I read books about leadership and management, and I attended some training programs to develop better communication skills. I also started having regular meetings with my team to discuss progress and challenges. These changes made a significant difference in our performance. In the following year, our team became one of the most productive in the company, and we successfully completed all projects on time.

This experience has shaped who I am today as a professional. It taught me humility, the importance of self-reflection, and the value of continuous learning. These lessons are not only important for my professional development but also for my personal growth. I believe that an MBA program like Lyon University will help me further develop these skills and prepare me for greater challenges in my career.

prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
When our portfolio company's operations director left suddenly in June 2019, I was a senior associate at the fund, not the operations leader. The CEO asked if I could "stabilize things" for three months until we hired a replacement. It became eighteen months.

The company manufactured industrial valves—about €8 million in annual revenue, 35 people spread across Lyon and two smaller facilities. The operations team barely knew me; I'd visited four times to assess the investment case. They had no reason to listen to me, and frankly, I had every reason to stay in my lane and let someone else own the mess.

But the fund's capital was at risk, and I understood that better than anyone else in the room.

I didn't storm in with authority I didn't have. Instead, I spent the first two weeks shadowing—actually standing on the factory floor watching molding cycles, sitting in on supplier calls, understanding what kept people up at night. I learned that the previous director had been hoarding knowledge; nobody knew which vendors were reliable, how pricing actually worked, why certain machines kept breaking. The operations team felt incompetent because they'd been starved of information.

My first move wasn't to restructure. I created a simple shared spreadsheet documenting every critical process—vendor contacts, maintenance schedules, supplier terms, customer quirks. This took longer than it should have because I had to extract this from emails and people's heads, but it was the foundation. I wasn't imposing a system; I was making visible what was already there.

Then I did something that surprised people: I admitted what I didn't know. In a meeting with the production manager, I said directly, "I don't understand why we're tolerating 14-day lead times when you mentioned your best customer needs 7 days. Is this a capacity issue or something about our suppliers?" He'd actually been frustrated that nobody asked. We spent an afternoon reworking the supplier contract. Lead times dropped to 9 days.

The harder part came around month four, when I realized the plant manager—capable, well-liked—was protective of the old ways and resistant to change. I could have forced things from the PE firm's authority. Instead, I asked him to lead a cost reduction initiative with me. Gave him ownership. Turns out he had excellent instincts about where we were bleeding money; he'd just never been asked. We reduced waste by 23% together, and he became an advocate for other improvements.

By the end, we'd improved on-time delivery from 71% to 89%, reduced working capital by €400K, and hired a strong operations director I'd helped recruit. More importantly, the team went from demoralized to engaged.

What I learned isn't revolutionary: authority is often less about position than about competence, transparency, and genuine curiosity about what others know. I learned I'm most effective when I'm humble about what I don't know and clear about what I'm trying to accomplish. And I learned that the people closest to the problem—the ones actually doing the work—want to solve it. They're not waiting for a title to give them permission.

This experience changed how I think about leadership entirely. It's why I'm drawn to ESSEC's Executive MBA. At this stage in my career, I want to sharpen these instincts with rigorous frameworks, to understand what made things click, and to build a network of leaders who operate the same way. I'm not looking for a credential. I'm looking to become significantly better at this work.

The energy sector is evolving rapidly, and professionals must adapt to new challenges and opportunities. I want to be part of this change. school_name: Stockholm School of Economics Executive MBA
prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
# Essay: The Supply Chain Decision

Three years ago, I was responsible for sourcing components for a cardiac monitoring device at Axxion Medical in Stockholm. We were under pressure to launch in Q2, and our primary supplier—a factory in Izmir producing circuit boards—had quoted us at €8.40 per unit. With volumes of 50,000 units annually, that was the difference between hitting margin targets or missing them by several points.

At 2 AM on a Thursday, I received an email from my contact at the supplier. He wanted to schedule a call. When we spoke, he told me the factory had discovered they could meet our price only by moving final assembly to a facility in a different region—one where labor costs were lower and, more importantly, where regulations around worker safety and environmental standards were less strictly enforced. He didn't say this directly. He said, "We've found a way to make your numbers work."

I asked the right questions. I got the real answers.

The facility had documented violations from a local NGO report. Workers were logging 54-hour weeks. There were no proper ventilation systems for the soldering area. When I dug deeper, I found out this supplier's decision hinged entirely on our volume commitment. Without our order, they wouldn't expand to this facility.

I remember sitting in my apartment in Södermalm, actually sick about it. The cost difference was roughly €210,000 annually—real money that would affect our bonus structure and how leadership viewed my sourcing performance. I'd been hired to solve this problem. Here was the solution, and it was legal, at least technically.

I considered a few paths. I could have accepted the quote and never mentioned where the work was actually happening. I could have asked for documentation confirming the facility met certain standards, then chosen not to verify it closely. These weren't even unconscious options—I knew exactly what I was doing in considering them, and I hated myself a little for it.

Instead, I did what was probably the dumbest thing for my career at that moment. I told my boss, and I told him I couldn't recommend we move forward without visiting the facility and having it independently audited. I also told him the audit would cost approximately €15,000 and add six weeks to our timeline.

The response surprised me. My boss didn't think I was naive. He said, "Yeah, okay. Let's do it."

We ran the audit. The findings were worse than the NGO report suggested. The facility wasn't compliant with basic EU standards, let alone the standards we claim to uphold. When we presented the results to the supplier, they had a choice: invest in proper infrastructure or lose our business.

They invested. It cost them more than the original margin savings they'd hoped to extract. Our price moved to €11.20 per unit. We missed our margin target by exactly what we'd projected. I took some internal heat for it, though less than I expected.

Here's what I didn't expect: eighteen months later, that supplier became one of our most reliable partners. Their equipment modernization made them more efficient overall. Their safety improvements actually reduced defects in the final product. And when we needed to scale to 80,000 units during the pandemic, they were the only supplier who could flex without quality degradation.

The ethical resolution wasn't really about being a good person—though I like to think I am one. It was about understanding that the short-term costs of doing things right are often smaller than they appear, and the long-term benefits are larger. I didn't learn this from a business school case study. I learned it by having to actually live with the consequences of my choices.

That's partly why I'm applying to Stockholm School of Economics. I want to deepen how I think about these decisions, not just reactively navigate them.

school_name: University of Porto FEP MSc Management
prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
I was twenty-six when I realized I'd been helping to dismantle a company I actually believed in.

At Marcos Capital, a mid-market PE firm in Manila, I worked as an analyst on a healthcare services portfolio. We acquired PhilCare Systems, a clinic operator serving provincial areas where private healthcare was still a luxury. The founder, Dr. Reyes, had spent fifteen years building trust in these communities. When I first visited a clinic, I saw it—an elderly woman paying in installments because she couldn't afford dialysis upfront, and Dr. Reyes personally negotiating a flexible payment plan. That's not how private equity usually works.

Our fund's thesis was straightforward: consolidate clinic operations, improve margins, then exit in five years. On paper, this made sense. We could standardize procurement, reduce administrative overhead, maybe even improve service quality through better management systems. I modeled everything—headcount reductions, supply chain optimization, fee increases across the network. The spreadsheet projected 28% EBITDA growth.

The problem emerged during operational due diligence. Our COO wanted to eliminate the "non-revenue generating" financing flexibility Dr. Reyes offered. It was technically sound: if patients paid upfront, working capital improved. We'd also reduce bad debt provisions. But eliminating flexible payments meant fewer people would access care. The math worked. The ethics didn't.

I brought this to my Managing Partner, Marcus. Not as a complaint, but as a genuine question: "If we implement this, are we actually improving the business or just extracting value from patients who can't afford better options?" I expected him to say I was being naive. Instead, he said something I didn't expect: "That's a fair question. What do you want to do?"

That moment terrified me because it was actually on me.

I spent two weeks researching alternatives. I looked at what other PE firms had done in similar situations. I found a fund in India that had redesigned their clinic economics around volume and retention rather than per-transaction revenue. I modeled a hybrid approach: keep flexible payments but incentivize early payment through discounts, invest in digital systems to reduce administrative costs of managing payments, and train staff on upselling preventive services (which patients actually valued).

I presented this to Marcus with full transparency: yes, this would reduce our exit multiple by an estimated 1-2%, maybe $800K in fund returns. But it would also mean we could actually tell Dr. Reyes we'd grown his company without making it inaccessible. We'd built something defensible, not just extractive.

What surprised me was that Marcus agreed to pilot the approach in two clinics. The results were messy but ultimately positive. Patient volume grew 12% over eighteen months. Margins were lower than the COO's original plan, but healthier than baseline. When we exited, the new ownership team wanted to expand the model because it actually worked.

But here's what I need to be honest about: I didn't do this because I'm some ethical purist. I did it because I realized I couldn't work somewhere that asked me to ignore the human impact of my decisions. That's selfish in its own way—protecting my own integrity rather than purely protecting patients.

This experience taught me that ethics in business isn't about being noble. It's about asking harder questions about who bears the costs of your decisions, and whether that's defensible. It's also taught me that the best outcomes usually aren't binary—pure profit or pure mission. They're the harder middle path that requires creativity.

I'm drawn to FEP's MSc Management partly because I want to build these skills more systematically. Europe's stronger stakeholder model interests me more than the pure shareholder primacy I grew up around. I want to return to Southeast Asia eventually, but with frameworks for thinking about business that aren't just extraction.

prompt: Reflect on a core value that defines you. Share a specific example of how it has guided your decisions and actions, and explain how it will shape your contributions and growth as a McCombs MBA student. Highlight how this value has influenced your leadership style and life path, and how it will shape your MBA experience.
content:
# Core Value Essay: Responsibility

Responsibility isn't just something I believe in—it's how I've learned to make sense of difficult choices.

I first felt the weight of this value when I was promoted to Operations Manager at a mid-sized solar installation company in Perth. I inherited a team that had been cutting corners on safety protocols to hit aggressive timelines. On my first site visit, I watched our crews skip harness checks. It was faster. It got us closer to bonuses.

I could have looked the other way. The previous manager had. But I realized that responsibility meant more than hitting targets—it meant protecting the people in front of me, even when it was inconvenient.

I redesigned our safety process, which added roughly 90 minutes per installation. Our completion rates dropped 15% in the first quarter. Our director was frustrated. I considered reverting back. Instead, I presented the data: zero incidents in twelve months (we'd had three the previous year), and improved crew morale that actually reduced turnover costs by 22%. Within eighteen months, we'd regained our timeline efficiency while maintaining safety.

That experience taught me something crucial: responsibility isn't just a personal virtue—it's a leadership multiplier. When a team knows their manager will prioritize their wellbeing over expediency, they're more engaged and more careful with their own decisions.

I want to bring this same approach to McCombs. In group projects, I'll push teams toward solutions that are ethically sound, not just expedient. I'll ask harder questions about the businesses we analyze and the decisions we justify.

I know an MBA can teach me frameworks and financial models. But I'm coming to develop a deeper understanding of how to lead responsibly in complex situations—where the right choice and the profitable choice aren't always aligned.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I started at McKinsey in Cairo in 2012, I remember my engagement manager asking me why I wanted to work in strategy consulting. I gave the standard answer about solving complex problems and driving impact. She smiled and said, "That's true, but figure out what impact actually means to you." Twelve years later, I'm finally ready to answer that question seriously—which is why I'm applying to Insper.

My career has been unconventional. I spent the first six years in traditional MBB strategy work—market entries, operational improvements, digital transformations. I was good at it. I made partner track, led teams of 15+ consultants, managed projects worth $2M+. But somewhere around 2018, I started noticing that the firms paying us to optimize their supply chains or enter new markets were almost entirely run by people who looked and thought like each other. I wasn't one of them. As someone who is non-binary and from Egypt, I realized I'd been trying to fit into a mold that would never quite fit me.

That's when things shifted. I moved into a consulting role focused specifically on emerging markets and diversity strategy—helping companies understand why their expansion into Latin America was failing, or why their DEI initiatives weren't sticking. This work felt different. I was still using the same analytical toolkit, but now I was asking questions about culture, power structures, and whose voices were being heard. I realized that impact, for me, means helping organizations become more intentional about who they include and why.

Here's what I don't know: how to build that into a sustainable career. Consulting firms talk about impact, but the incentive structures still reward billable hours and client satisfaction above all else. I can see the ceiling. So I'm looking for a place where I can deepen my understanding of how organizations actually change—not just strategically, but culturally.

Insper appeals to me specifically because of your focus on ethical leadership and your position in Brazil. I've watched how you approach business education differently than the traditional models—emphasizing entrepreneurship alongside responsibility, pushing students to think about their role in society, not just their salaries. Brazil's complexity—its economic dynamism alongside deep inequality—feels like the right place to study how leadership actually gets forged. I want to work with classmates who are building businesses that serve more than just shareholders, and I think Insper's ecosystem would push me to be more serious about that.

What I'd bring: perspective from two continents and twelve years in high-stakes environments, combined with a genuine intellectual curiosity about why organizations fail at their stated values. I'm not looking to optimize something. I'm looking to understand, and then to help others do the same. I think that's what Insper is looking for too.

Coming from Indonesia, I believe that an international MBA experience will help me build connections across different markets and cultures. Through the program, I hope to meet classmates from Europe, Asia, and other regions who can become lifelong professional partners.

The curriculum at IE also attracted my attention. I have read about courses in international finance and emerging markets, which are directly relevant to my work in asset management. My three years of experience in portfolio management at PT Mitra Investasi have taught me the importance of understanding global market dynamics. What I plan to do as part of the IE community is to actively participate in learning. I will attend classes regularly, complete assignments thoughtfully, and contribute to group discussions. I believe that diversity of thought makes education better, and I am committed to being an active and thoughtful member of the community.

After graduation, I hope to leverage my IE degree to advance my career in asset management, possibly moving into a leadership role or exploring new markets. The combination of technical skills and global network will help me achieve these goals.

Right now, I feel that my background is primarily in teaching and curriculum development, but I want to broaden my skillset to include business operations and management.

My long-term vision is to eventually lead an educational institution or start my own educational venture that serves underprivileged communities. I believe that education is the key to breaking cycles of poverty, and I want to be at the forefront of this mission. I see myself running either a school, a training center, or perhaps a tech-enabled learning platform that makes quality education accessible to everyone. The Shanghai Jiao Tong Antai MSc Management program is an excellent fit for my goals. This program will provide me with the business knowledge I need to complement my education background. I have read that there are many interesting developments happening in EdTech across China and the broader region, and being based in Shanghai would give me the opportunity to understand these markets better. I am also drawn to the diverse cohort at Antai. Learning from classmates from different countries and backgrounds will expose me to different perspectives on education and management. An MBA will give me the credibility and knowledge base I need to be taken seriously as a business leader, not just an educator.

I am excited about the prospect of joining the Antai community and becoming part of a network of global professionals.
