I am confident that with the skills and knowledge I gain from this program, I will be able to make a meaningful impact in the education sector and contribute to the mission of making education accessible to all.

prompt: What qualities would you look for in an MBA classmate when working on a team project and describe how your background and leadership skills will enhance the experience of others?
content:
# What I Look For in Teammates

When I'm evaluating potential team members for a project, I've learned that technical competence is just the entry fee. What actually matters is someone who can sit in discomfort without trying to fix it immediately.

I learned this the hard way at Momentum Capital in Johannesburg. Our portfolio company was hemorrhaging cash, and we brought in a new analyst—technically brilliant, had built financial models that made the rest of us look amateur. But in our first investment committee meeting, when our partner raised a concern about the founder's track record, the analyst immediately pivoted to defend the numbers. He couldn't sit with the ambiguity. He needed to prove he was right rather than understand what we were actually worried about.

I look for the opposite. I want teammates who ask clarifying questions before diving in. Who acknowledge when they don't know something. Who can disagree with me in a meeting and then implement a decision they initially opposed without resentment.

Beyond that, I value people with genuine intellectual humility. In venture capital, you're making bets on incomplete information constantly. The best analysts I've worked with aren't the ones who sound most confident—they're the ones who can hold competing hypotheses simultaneously. My colleague Priya at Momentum could present three different scenarios for a company's growth trajectory, flag the assumptions in each one, and say honestly: "I think scenario two is most likely, but here's what would change my mind." That's the person I want in my corner.

I also need teammates who bring perspective I don't have. I grew up in Cape Town, worked in Johannesburg, and moved to London for this role. It's given me a certain worldview, but it's limited. I want people who challenge my blind spots. Someone from a different socioeconomic background, or who worked in an industry completely foreign to me, or who approached problems from a totally different angle—that's valuable.

Here's what I contribute to that dynamic: I listen more than I talk, which isn't always seen as a leadership strength but I think it is. In my first year at Momentum, I was quieter than colleagues, and I worried it made me seem junior. But after about six months, the partners started asking for my input specifically because I'd usually absorbed enough context to ask the right question rather than the obvious one. I don't need to be heard constantly to feel like I'm leading.

I'm also reliable in a way that isn't glamorous. If I commit to something, it gets done. If I say I'll have analysis by Thursday, you'll have it Wednesday. That reliability means other people can trust me to own a piece of work without constant checking in, which frees them to focus on their own responsibilities.

I think where I sometimes struggle is patience with people who are slower to make decisions than I am. I've moved relatively quickly in my career—I was a deal analyst within six months of joining Momentum—and I can get frustrated with deliberation. But I'm aware of it now. I know that speed isn't always an asset, and sometimes the person who wants to slow down is actually protecting us from a bad decision.

For an MBA program like Desautels, where I'm coming in with some professional experience, I imagine I'll be most valuable not as the person driving every project forward, but as someone who creates space for better thinking to happen.

school_name: Burgundy School of Business Master in Management
prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
When I started at Morrison & Associates, a mid-sized law firm with about 40 attorneys, I quickly realized that our document management system was inefficient. Cases were being delayed because paralegals and lawyers couldn't find the files they needed. This was a complex problem that was affecting our entire workflow.

I decided to take initiative and find a creative solution. I knew that if I could improve this process, it would help everyone at the firm work better and serve our clients more effectively. I spent time observing how people were searching for documents and talking to team members about their frustrations.

After researching different options, I proposed implementing a new cloud-based filing system that would allow attorneys to access documents remotely and search by multiple criteria. This was creative because instead of just suggesting we buy new software, I also created a detailed transition plan that would minimize disruption. I worked with the IT department to set up the system and trained all the staff on how to use it.

The results were impressive. We reduced the time it took to locate documents by about 50%, and this freed up paralegals to focus on more meaningful legal work. Attorneys said they were happier because they could access files from home or the courtroom, which made them more productive. The firm ended up saving money too because we weren't paying for as many duplicate files and storage.

This experience taught me that creative problem-solving isn't just about coming up with a big idea. It's about understanding the root of the problem, listening to the people affected, and then implementing a solution that actually works in the real world. I learned that I'm good at seeing inefficiencies and thinking of practical ways to fix them.

I think this skill will be valuable in my MBA studies and future career. At Burgundy, I want to deepen my understanding of how technology and business strategy connect. I'm interested in learning more about operations and change management so I can continue solving problems in bigger and more complex ways.

school_name: The University of Chicago Booth School of Business
prompt: How will a Booth MBA help you achieve your immediate and long-term post-MBA career goals?
content:
When I joined Qursh as a sales engineer in 2016, the company was processing about $2 million in monthly transactions. Today, that number is north of $150 million. I didn't single-handedly drive that growth—I've been part of a team that went from 40 people to 280. But I also know exactly where my gaps are. I can close deals and solve technical problems on the fly, but I struggle with the strategic thinking required to scale beyond my current role. That's why I need an MBA, and specifically, why Booth is the right place.

My immediate goal after graduation is to move into a product leadership role—either VP of Product or Senior Director. At Qursh, I've spent the last three years increasingly involved in product decisions, working closely with our product team on feature prioritization, customer feedback loops, and go-to-market strategy. The problem is that I'm doing this informally, learning from my VP of Product through osmosis. I need structured frameworks: how to think about roadmaps, how to balance technical debt with feature velocity, how to build conviction in a data-driven way. Booth's product management curriculum—especially courses like "Strategy" and "Product Design and Management"—will give me that rigor.

But there's a longer-term play here too. I'm 31 now, and I want to eventually become a Chief Product Officer at a scaling enterprise SaaS company, ideally back in the Middle East where I can contribute to the region's digital transformation. The Middle East software market is booming, but it's still relatively immature. Most successful products here are either direct imports from the West or poorly executed local imitations. There's a real opportunity to build something meaningful—products designed for how Middle Eastern businesses actually work.

To get there, I need more than just product knowledge. I need to understand finance and unit economics deeply enough that I can make capital allocation decisions. I need to think about organizational design and how to build world-class teams across geographies. I need exposure to scaling challenges that I haven't faced yet. And honestly, I need the credential. Right now, I'm often the only non-MBA in senior strategy meetings, and while my results speak for themselves, I've hit an invisible ceiling. That's frustrating because I know I can do this job well—I just need the institutional backing.

Booth specifically appeals to me because of your emphasis on analytical rigor and practical problem-solving. Your MBA isn't purely theoretical. The integrated core, the emphasis on real-world consulting projects, the access to practicing executives—these matter to me because I learn by doing, not by memorizing frameworks. I also value the diversity of your cohort. I'll spend two years learning from people with very different backgrounds than mine, and I think that diversity will sharpen my thinking in ways a homogeneous program simply can't.

This MBA is an investment in becoming the leader I know I can be.

school_name: SP Jain School of Global Management EMBA
prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
When my father handed me the operational keys to our family's textile manufacturing business seven years ago, I didn't understand what I'd actually accepted. I thought he was giving me a job. What he was really giving me was a decades-long lesson in the gap between good intentions and sustainable growth.

Textiles Medellín—our 180-person operation—was profitable but stagnant. We'd been doing things the same way for 25 years: strong relationships with four major Colombian retailers, decent margins, zero international presence. My father had built something solid. But solid wasn't going to survive the next decade of Asian competition and global supply chain disruption.

I spent the first two years making mistakes that sound obvious in retrospect. I hired a logistics consultant who proposed expensive automation that our facilities couldn't support. I launched an e-commerce platform that nobody wanted. But around year three, something shifted. I started asking better questions: not "How do we do this?" but "Why are we doing this at all?" I began tracking unit economics more carefully. I visited our retailers instead of just calling them. I realized 40% of our margin actually came from a single, underexploited product line—our cotton-linen blend for corporate uniforms.

I pivoted hard. We repositioned toward B2B corporate apparel, invested in a small quality lab, and started building direct relationships with manufacturing companies in Peru and Ecuador. Over the last four years, we've grown export revenue from near-zero to $2.8 million annually. We now employ 220 people. But I've also learned that I'm operating at the edge of my capabilities.

Here's what keeps me up at night: I can run operations in Spanish, in a context I understand deeply. What I cannot do—not yet—is navigate the complexity of scaling a family business beyond the Andean region. I don't have frameworks for thinking about capital structure as the business grows. I haven't systematically learned how to build management teams that work across cultures and time zones. I'm making these decisions partly by instinct and partly by calling people I know, and that's not a sustainable way to lead if we're going to double again.

SP Jain specifically appeals to me because of its global structure. An EMBA from an Indian business school is not the obvious choice for a Colombian manufacturer—which is exactly why I want it. The executive program's focus on emerging markets makes sense for my context, but the delivery model is what actually excites me. I need to study alongside people building businesses in different markets: India, Singapore, the Middle East. I need that perspective challenge. A traditional MBA would be the safe choice. An EMBA with SP Jain feels like the useful one.

The specific courses that matter to me are clearer than they've ever been. I need corporate finance and capital structure—real understanding, not just spreadsheet literacy. I need strategy, specifically how to think about competitive positioning when you're operating across multiple geographies with different regulations. And I need something most MBAs don't emphasize: organizational design for family businesses. That might sound niche, but it's where my leverage is. We have something most startups don't—institutional relationships and capital. We have something most multinationals don't—deep market knowledge. I need to learn how to combine those advantages.

In five years, I see Textiles Medellín as a regional player with sustainable margins, not just growing revenue. I see a business that could support a second generation—my own or someone else's—with professional management that isn't just my decisions. And beyond that? That's where I'm honest: I'm not sure. Maybe I build something even bigger. Maybe I move into other sectors. Maybe I help other Colombian family businesses scale professionally.

What I know for certain is that the next phase requires different tools than the last one. SP Jain's EMBA will give me those tools, but more importantly, it will give me the framework to use them intelligently in a context that actually matters to me.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
Throughout my career at Google, I have become passionate about technology and its ability to change the world. I believe that pursuing an MBA at Durham University Business School will help me achieve my career aspirations and become a leader in the tech industry.

My journey in tech began six years ago when I joined Google as a junior analyst. Since then, I have worked my way up to a senior position, managing teams and driving projects that have impacted millions of users globally. I am proud of what I have accomplished, but I know there is more I can learn and more I can achieve.

The tech industry is evolving rapidly, and I want to ensure I have the skills necessary to lead in this changing landscape. An MBA will provide me with a comprehensive understanding of business strategy, finance, and operations—areas where I feel I lack formal training. Currently, my knowledge is primarily technical, and I believe a business education will round out my skillset and prepare me for executive roles.

My career aspiration is to become a CEO or Chief Technology Officer at a leading tech company. I have always been interested in innovation and problem-solving, and I believe these are key traits of successful leaders. I want to build a career where I can make a strategic impact and guide teams toward ambitious goals. An MBA will give me the credibility and knowledge base needed to pursue these senior leadership positions.

Durham University Business School is an excellent choice for me because it is a respected institution with a strong reputation. The program's focus on management and business strategy aligns well with my goals, and I am confident that the curriculum will prepare me adequately for my future endeavors. Additionally, I am looking forward to networking with peers from around the world and building relationships that will support my career growth.

Outside of work, I am interested in mentoring junior employees and giving back to the community. I believe in using my position to help others develop their skills and grow professionally. I also enjoy staying updated on industry trends and attending conferences where I can learn from thought leaders in the field.

In conclusion, an MBA from Durham University Business School will serve as a crucial step toward achieving my ambitious career goals. The program will provide me with the business acumen I need to transition into senior leadership, while also building a network of professionals who will support me throughout my career. I am excited about the opportunity to study at Durham and am confident that this experience will be transformative for my professional development. Thank you for considering my application.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
# Essay Response

My most significant accomplishment is founding my own startup, TechVenture Solutions, which I started two years ago with the goal of disrupting the tech industry through innovative solutions. This accomplishment matters to me because it represents my commitment to building something from nothing and making a positive impact in the world.

When I graduated from university, I knew that I wanted to pursue entrepreneurship rather than work for a large corporation. I believed that I had good ideas and the drive to execute them. I spent several months planning my business model and eventually launched TechVenture Solutions with a focus on providing software services to small and medium enterprises. My vision was to create a company that could help businesses streamline their operations and increase productivity.

During the first year, I worked incredibly hard. I spent countless hours developing our product, managing finances, and trying to acquire customers. The experience taught me a lot about dedication and perseverance. I faced many challenges, including finding initial customers, managing cash flow, and competing against larger established companies. However, I remained focused on my goals and continued to push forward despite these obstacles.

By the end of year one, we had acquired around 15 customers and generated approximately $120,000 in revenue. While this may not seem like a huge number compared to larger companies, it represented significant progress for a startup at that stage. I was proud of what we had accomplished, and I felt that we were on the right track toward building a successful business.

Starting this company has been meaningful to me for several reasons. First, it gave me the opportunity to take control of my own destiny rather than relying on someone else to provide me with a career path. Second, it taught me valuable lessons about business, management, and personal growth. Third, I was able to create job opportunities for other people by hiring a small team to help me run the company.

However, I also recognize that running a startup is not easy, and there have been moments where I questioned whether I made the right decision. The financial uncertainty, the long working hours, and the pressure to constantly innovate can be overwhelming at times. Despite these challenges, I remain committed to my entrepreneurial journey and believe that the experience has made me a stronger and more resilient person.

I believe that this accomplishment demonstrates my ability to take initiative, work independently, and pursue my passions. These are qualities that will be valuable to me in my studies at Peking Guanghua and in my future career. An MBA will help me develop the strategic thinking and business knowledge that I need to take my company to the next level and to better understand the complexities of global business.

My startup journey has been my most significant accomplishment because it represents who I am as a person and what I hope to achieve in my career. It has shaped my perspectives on business and given me clarity about my goals. I am excited about the opportunity to further develop my skills through the MSc Management program at Peking Guanghua and to apply these lessons in a structured academic setting.

prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
When I joined Tecno Medical as a Clinical Sales Manager in 2018, I was convinced I understood the market. I had worked in sales for four years, hit my targets consistently, and felt ready to expand into clinical device territory. My manager gave me the Colombian territory—a market I knew well, speaking the language fluently, familiar with the hospital networks in Bogotá and Cali.

Six months in, I realized my success had been built on selling transactional products to procurement teams. Clinical devices are different. Surgeons care about outcomes, not price lists. Hospital directors needed evidence of cost-effectiveness over three years, not quarterly savings. I was pitching like I sold before, and it wasn't working.

My numbers for the first year fell 30% short of target. Not catastrophically, but enough to matter. My manager suggested I was "still learning the vertical." But I knew the problem wasn't learning—it was stubbornness. I had assumed my experience transferred directly, and I'd been too proud to ask for help early enough.

What I did next was harder than admitting failure. I spent two weeks shadowing our top clinical sales rep in Mexico, watching how he spoke to surgeons about complication rates, how he built relationships with hospital procurement by understanding their P&Ls. I realized I needed to start over, almost. I restructured my whole approach: I identified the five key teaching hospitals in my territory, scheduled one-on-one meetings with surgical teams rather than procurement, and began investing in outcome-based conversations instead of price comparisons.

The second year, I exceeded my target by 12%. More importantly, I built a pipeline that actually sustained—my retention numbers went from 68% to 82% because I was solving actual problems instead of just moving units.

What would I do differently? The obvious answer is "ask for help sooner." But honestly, it's deeper than that. I would have been more humble about the limits of my experience. I conflated knowing a market with understanding a customer. I let early success in sales convince me that fundamentals were optional. That's a dangerous lie in any business.

This taught me I need environments where I can challenge my own assumptions. It's why I'm drawn to SMU's integrated curriculum and the emphasis on cross-functional problems. In medical devices, I've seen how narrow functional expertise creates blind spots. I want to study alongside people from different industries, to practice thinking like someone I'm not yet.

I'm a better manager now because I failed. I understand when confidence is earned and when it's just habit. That distinction feels important for whatever comes next.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
# What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?

Two years ago, I was sitting in a São Paulo café with our VP of Engineering, staring at a spreadsheet that showed our conversion rate had dropped 34% in three weeks. We had no idea why. Our product—a logistics optimization platform for mid-market distribution companies across Latin America—was hemorrhaging users, and our founding team was starting to panic.

I was hired as the first person on our Business Operations team when the company had about $1.2 million in ARR. My job was vaguely defined: "figure out what's broken in our operations." Within my first month, I realized nobody actually understood our customer cohorts. We were treating every client the same, but a 50-person operation in Mexico City had completely different needs than a 500-person company in São Paulo. We had no segmentation, no targeted retention strategy, and frankly, no real data infrastructure to support decision-making.

When that conversion crisis hit, I had the data to diagnose it. The drop was concentrated entirely in companies with fewer than 100 employees—our smallest segment. They were churning because we'd released a feature that actually made their workflows more complicated, not simpler. Our largest clients loved it. Nobody had noticed because we weren't tracking things by segment.

This was the moment I realized something crucial: I could build the analytical infrastructure that our company desperately needed, or I could watch us make decisions in the dark while blaming product, sales, or bad luck.

I spent the next four months building a customer segmentation model from scratch. I worked with our data engineer to pull historical data from three years back. I created dashboards that broke down revenue, churn, NPS, and feature adoption by company size, geography, and industry vertical. I trained our sales and success teams to use these tools. I built monthly business reviews with our founders where we actually talked about what was working and what wasn't.

The segmentation work changed everything. Within six months, our churn in the small-company segment dropped by 28%. We started building features specifically for that cohort. We repositioned our pricing to reflect the real value we were delivering to different customer sizes. Over the next 18 months, our ARR grew from $1.2 million to $6.7 million.

But what matters more to me than the revenue growth is what it taught me about my own leadership. I'm Mexican; I grew up in Monterrey and moved to São Paulo for this job. I wasn't the loudest voice in the room. I wasn't the founder. But I had something to contribute, and I had to figure out how to make that contribution matter.

I realized I was most effective when I stopped waiting for permission to have impact. Instead of asking "Is this my job?", I started asking "What does this company actually need right now?" That shift—from protecting my lane to expanding my impact—made me better at everything else.

This accomplishment matters to me because it wasn't about being the smartest person in the room. It was about being willing to do the unglamorous work of building systems, training people, and pushing for accountability. It was about understanding that operations and analytics aren't overhead—they're competitive advantages.

That's why I'm pursuing an MBA. I want to formalize what I've learned through experience. I want to understand how to scale these principles across larger organizations, different industries, maybe different continents. At IIM Raipur, I'm particularly drawn to your focus on entrepreneurship and real-world business problems. That case method approach—where we're solving actual business challenges—is how I've always learned best.

school_name: KAIST College of Business (MSc Management Engineering)
prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
When I joined Seriti Logistics as a junior coordinator in 2021, I was eager to prove myself. Within six months, I was promoted to manage a regional distribution center with 15 staff members. I thought I was ready, but I wasn't.

The major setback came when our client, a large retail company, threatened to pull their contract after we missed delivery deadlines for three consecutive weeks. This represented 40% of our facility's revenue. My manager pulled me into a meeting where I had to explain what went wrong. I froze. The truth was, I had implemented a new inventory system without properly training my team, and I hadn't communicated the changes to our transport partners. I was overconfident and it cost us.

I realized I had made critical mistakes in my approach to leadership. I focused on implementing changes quickly rather than ensuring my team understood and could execute them properly. I also didn't maintain regular communication channels with our partners, which created confusion in the supply chain.

To recover, I took responsibility immediately. I apologized to my manager and the client. Then I created an action plan. First, I conducted comprehensive training sessions for my team on the new system, allowing time for questions and feedback. Second, I established weekly sync calls with our transport partners to ensure alignment. Third, I implemented a backup system to catch errors before they affected client deliveries.

Within two months, we were back on track. We successfully delivered all orders on time for eight consecutive weeks, and the client renewed their contract. More importantly, my team's confidence in me was restored.

This experience taught me that leadership isn't about being the smartest person in the room. It's about ensuring others have the tools, knowledge, and communication they need to succeed. I learned that setbacks are often failures in execution, not in vision.

Since then, I have been more deliberate about change management. I communicate earlier, listen more, and move slower when necessary. I believe this experience makes me a better candidate for KAIST's program because I understand the importance of systematic thinking and operational excellence in management engineering.

prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
# Essay Response

When I think about a time things didn't go as planned, the first situation that comes to mind is during my role as a logistics coordinator at a freight forwarding company in Medellín. This experience taught me many valuable lessons about the importance of planning and communication.

In 2018, I was responsible for managing a shipment of automotive parts destined for a major client in Mexico. The shipment was supposed to arrive within a 10-day window, which was critical because our client needed the parts for their assembly line. Unfortunately, due to some miscommunication within our team about the customs documentation, the shipment was delayed at the border for nearly two weeks. This caused significant problems for our client and resulted in a serious conversation with my manager about accountability.

What I learned from this experience was that things don't always go according to plan in logistics. There are many variables that are outside of our control, such as weather, customs regulations, and transportation delays. However, I realized that I could have done more to prevent this particular problem. If I had been more proactive in double-checking the documentation with my team members, this delay could have been avoided.

Looking back, I would handle this situation differently in several ways. First, I would implement a more rigorous checklist system to ensure that all customs documents are properly prepared and reviewed before shipment. Second, I would communicate more frequently with my team throughout the process, rather than assuming that everyone understands their responsibilities. Third, I would maintain closer contact with the customs broker to stay informed about any potential delays or issues.

Since that time, I have tried to apply these lessons to my work. In my current position, I have taken on more responsibility for quality control and have implemented several new procedures to minimize delays. I have also become more aware of the importance of clear communication and teamwork in a logistics environment.

This experience has made me more humble and more willing to accept responsibility for my mistakes. It has also shown me that even when things go wrong, there are always opportunities to learn and improve. I believe that the skills I have developed through overcoming this challenge will be valuable assets in an MBA program at NUS Business School, where I hope to deepen my understanding of supply chain management and advance my career in this field.

I am committed to continuous improvement and to becoming a better leader and professional. I believe that pursuing an MBA will help me achieve these goals and prepare me for greater responsibilities in the future.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
When I was 26, I managed a team of three people at Google's Moscow office. It felt like the peak of my career at the time. Eleven years later, I'm managing forty engineers across product, infrastructure, and data teams—and I've realized that what got me here won't get me where I want to go.

My title is Senior Engineering Manager, but what I actually do is translate between business strategy and technical execution. I've shipped features that reached 200 million users. I've also killed projects that were technically brilliant but commercially pointless. That friction—between "can we?" and "should we?"—is where I want to spend the next decade of my career. Not as an IC engineer, not as a people manager, but as someone who builds products that matter from a business perspective.

The problem is that my background has a blind spot. I came up through engineering. I can read a balance sheet, but I don't think in financial systems. I understand unit economics intuitively, but I've never built a P&L from scratch. Last year, when I proposed a new internal tool platform, I couldn't articulate the business case clearly enough. A finance manager with an MBA made the same argument a month later—using the same logic I'd used—and got the greenlight. That stung.

I'm not interested in becoming a PM just to chase a job title. I want to move into product leadership—possibly founding something eventually—because I genuinely believe engineers should build the next wave of consumer products. We understand constraints, we understand what's actually possible, and we're less likely to chase pure hype. But to do that credibly, I need business acumen that goes beyond "I've worked with business people."

EM Lyon appeals to me specifically because of your approach to entrepreneurship and your European network. I've spent most of my career at American tech companies optimizing for US markets. Europe is executing on different problems: more regulated, more fragmented, more focused on sustainability and privacy-first design. Your location in Lyon, plus your partnerships with companies across France and the broader EU, gives me access to founders and markets I couldn't easily reach from California.

The practical things matter too. Your program is 16 months, not two years—I can't afford to step away from my industry for longer than that. Your curriculum seems designed for people like me: experienced professionals who need finance and strategy, not someone teaching us how to write a resume.

Here's the honest part: my GMAT was 700, my undergrad GPA was 3.1. I'm not the student who glides through standardized tests. But I've shipped products that millions of people use, and I've learned to lead through ambiguity and disagreement. I want to apply that experience to building something bigger. EM Lyon is the partner I need to make that transition credible.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# Challenging Conventional Thinking in Media

Throughout my eleven years working in the media and entertainment industry, I have learned that true innovation comes from questioning the status quo. One experience in particular stands out as a moment when I challenged conventional thinking to drive meaningful change.

At my previous company, a mid-sized media production firm in Mexico City, we were producing content for television and digital platforms. Like most traditional media companies, we followed a very rigid production process that had been in place for many years. The workflow was: write script, get approval from management, film, edit, and publish. This process took approximately three months from conception to final output.

I noticed that this approach was inefficient and did not align with how audiences were consuming content anymore. People were watching shorter, more personalized content on their phones and social media platforms, not traditional television. So I thought, why are we still using a process designed for TV when the world has changed?

I proposed a new approach to my team. Instead of following the old method, I suggested we try a more agile methodology. We would work in shorter cycles, get feedback from the audience early, and iterate quickly. This was unconventional at the time in our company because management believed that quality control required multiple approval stages and that faster was not better.

I advocated for a pilot project with a small team of five people. We selected one series to test the new process. We reduced the approval stages from four to two, implemented weekly feedback loops with viewers, and published content every two weeks instead of every three months. The results were significant. We were able to produce more content, the audience engagement metrics improved by about 40%, and the team reported higher job satisfaction because they saw their work reach audiences much faster.

After seeing the success of this pilot, senior management approved rolling out the new process across the entire company. Within a year, we had restructured how three different departments worked, and we became known internally as the innovation team. This experience was important to me because it showed that challenging the conventional way of doing things, even when it is uncomfortable, can lead to real improvements.

However, I also learned some valuable lessons from this experience. Not everything we tried in the new process worked perfectly. Some of our early published content had quality issues because we were moving so quickly. We had to find a balance between speed and excellence. This taught me that innovation is not about completely abandoning all previous methods, but rather thoughtfully combining the best of old and new approaches.

This experience has shaped how I approach problems in my career. I have come to believe that the best leaders are those who can think critically about why things are done a certain way, and who have the courage to propose alternatives even when they might face resistance.

I am interested in pursuing an MBA at KAIST College of Business because I want to develop a deeper understanding of innovation management and organizational change. I believe the rigorous curriculum will help me become more strategic in how I approach these challenges, and the international environment will expose me to different perspectives and ways of thinking. I am excited about the possibility of joining a community of ambitious professionals who share a commitment to challenging conventional thinking and driving positive change in their industries.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# Essay Response

Innovation is something I feel passionate about. Throughout my career, I have always tried to think outside the box and challenge the status quo. I believe that in today's fast-paced world, companies that don't innovate will fall behind their competitors.

At my current role at a major tech company, I have been exposed to many different perspectives on how we can improve our products and services. I work in a team of about fifteen people, and we are responsible for various aspects of product development. One day, I realized that the way we were approaching a particular project was perhaps not the most effective method.

The conventional approach was to follow the standard process that had been used for many years. However, I thought that we could do things differently. I suggested to my manager that we try a new approach that would be more efficient. My manager was initially skeptical, but after I explained my reasoning, he agreed to let me give it a try.

I worked with my team to implement this new approach, and we found that it actually did save time. We were able to complete the project faster than expected, and the results were positive. This showed me that challenging conventional thinking can lead to real results.

This experience taught me that it's important to speak up when you see an opportunity for improvement. It also taught me that not everyone will agree with you at first, but if you have confidence in your ideas and can back them up with solid reasoning, people will be more willing to listen.

I believe that an MBA program like Insper would help me develop my skills further and give me the tools I need to drive innovation in whatever industry I work in. I am excited about the prospect of learning from experienced professors and my peers, and I believe this will help me become a better leader and innovator.

prompt: Why do you intend on pursuing an MBA at this point in your life?
content:
Why an MBA at Desautels

I am writing to express my strong interest in pursuing an MBA at Desautels Executive MBA program. At this point in my career, I believe that an advanced business degree is the natural next step to accelerate my professional growth and achieve my long-term goals.

I currently work as a Senior Consultant at a Big 4 consulting firm, where I have spent the last three years advising clients across various industries. My role involves analyzing business problems, developing solutions, and presenting recommendations to senior leadership. Through this experience, I have developed a deep appreciation for the complexity of modern business and the importance of strategic thinking.

An MBA will provide me with the comprehensive business knowledge I need to transition into senior management roles. In consulting, I have gained exposure to different functional areas—operations, finance, strategy—but I feel that a formal program will help me integrate this knowledge more systematically. I want to develop a deeper understanding of financial modeling, organizational behavior, and global business strategy. These skills are essential for the leadership positions I aspire to reach in the coming years.

Beyond technical skills, I am eager to expand my professional network. Consulting has given me valuable connections, but an MBA cohort will expose me to professionals from diverse industries and geographies. I believe that the network I build at Desautels will be instrumental in my future career transitions.

I am also interested in the international dimension of business. As a German national working in a global consulting firm, I have worked with clients in multiple countries and across different business contexts. However, I want to deepen my understanding of international business strategy and how companies navigate different regulatory environments and market dynamics. The Executive MBA format appeals to me because it allows me to continue working while I study, and Montreal's position as a bridge between North American and European markets makes Desautels particularly relevant for my goals.

Why Desautels specifically? Desautels has an excellent reputation for developing business leaders, and the program's focus on strategic management aligns with my career aspirations. I am impressed by the caliber of the faculty and the strong alumni network in both Europe and North America. Additionally, the opportunity to study in Canada appeals to me personally, as it offers a different perspective from my experience in the German and broader European markets.

I recognize that my academic background is not as strong as some candidates, but I am confident that I have the maturity and professional experience to excel in the program. My consulting work has taught me how to learn quickly, adapt to new situations, and work under pressure. These are skills that will serve me well in an intensive MBA program.

In conclusion, pursuing an MBA at Desautels at this stage of my career represents the logical progression of my professional journey. I am committed to leveraging this opportunity to develop the strategic and financial expertise needed to assume senior leadership roles, while building relationships that will shape my career trajectory for decades to come.

prompt: Three essays as specified
content:
# Essay 1: Why MBA, Why Now

I didn't plan to take over my family's distribution business. At 22, I wanted to work in investment banking—something that felt more "impressive" than managing a Lagos-based FMCG supply chain that my grandfather had started in 1987. But when my father suffered a stroke in 2016, I came home for what I thought would be three months. Seven years later, I'm still here, and I've learned that running a business with 140 employees and ₦2.3 billion in annual revenue is infinitely more complex than any pitch deck.

The problem is that I'm operating at the ceiling of what I know. We've plateaued at about 18% market share in our segment, and I can see the opportunities we're missing—better data analytics for demand forecasting, systematic route optimization, formal supply chain risk management. But I'm largely self-taught, pulling from online courses and late-night calls with friends in logistics. I make decisions based on intuition and past experience, which works until it doesn't.

Two years ago, I tried to implement a new distribution model. I had data suggesting it would work. I didn't consult anyone. It failed spectacularly, cost us three months and roughly ₦80 million, and damaged relationships with two key clients. My team was frustrated with me. I was furious at myself. That failure crystallized something: I need structured frameworks, not just hustle and gut feel.

An MBA from Koç appeals to me specifically because of your emphasis on family business management and your connections across emerging markets. But honestly, it's also because I'm tired of being the smartest person in every room about my own business. I've built something, but I haven't built it *well*. I want to learn from professors and classmates who've navigated similar challenges in different contexts.

I'm also realistic about what an MBA isn't—it won't make the hard decisions easier or guarantee success. But it will give me the decision-making tools I should have had five years ago. I'm ready to invest two years in myself because my team, my family, and frankly my competitive market, deserve a leader who operates from knowledge, not just determination.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# IIM Calcutta Application Essay

When I joined Nestlé's Southeast Asia innovation team in 2018, instant noodles were a commodity. Competitors fought over shelf space with identical products—same packaging, same flavors, same price points. Our category was shrinking, especially among younger consumers who saw the category as outdated and unhealthy.

I suggested something that made my manager visibly uncomfortable: what if we stopped trying to improve the product and instead changed what "instant noodles" meant?

Most of my colleagues dismissed this immediately. The argument was straightforward: our supply chain was built for speed and cost. Our factories weren't equipped for premium ingredients. Our retail partners had relationships with three competitors already. Why would we compete against ourselves in a space with lower margins?

But I'd spent time in Bangkok and Ho Chi Minh City grocery stores, watching consumers. I noticed young professionals grabbing instant noodles not because they wanted noodles, but because they wanted something fast, portable, and under SGD 3. What if we kept that convenience but positioned the product differently?

I pushed internally for a test launch. We developed a version with shiitake mushrooms, ginger, and bone broth—genuinely better ingredients sourced from our existing suppliers at only 40% premium cost. The packaging shifted from the bright, cartoonish design to something more minimal. We called it "Heritage Bowl" and priced it at SGD 2.80, barely above the original line.

Here's where it got interesting: we didn't market it as noodles. We marketed it as a five-minute meal replacement positioned against convenience foods. That distinction mattered. We tested it in three neighborhoods in Singapore—Tiong Bahru, Katong, and Clementi—with young professionals as the target segment.

The first four weeks were discouraging. Sales were 35% below projection. I remember sitting in a performance review where my manager said, "I think we've learned our lesson here." But two things gave me confidence to push forward. First, repeat purchase rate was 58%—nearly three times higher than our core range. Second, we got unsolicited positive feedback. Women particularly appreciated that the product didn't make their kitchens smell like the traditional noodles.

We extended the pilot another eight weeks and adjusted our positioning in week five to emphasize "meals that matter" rather than convenience. By week twelve, we'd hit our volume targets. Within eighteen months, Heritage Bowl was our third-highest revenue generator in Singapore, with expansion into Malaysia and Thailand underway.

The broader impact was cultural. Once this succeeded, the innovation team's mandate shifted. We started questioning other "commodity" categories in our portfolio—snacks, beverages, frozen foods. We launched three subsequent lines using similar thinking: same category, reframed positioning, premium ingredients, different consumer experience.

This taught me something important about innovation: it's not always about technology or transformation. Sometimes it's about permission—giving yourself and your organization permission to redefine what's possible within existing constraints.

But I'll be honest—this success hasn't made me overconfident about innovation. I know the test market conditions weren't representative of how the product would perform at national scale. I know we got lucky with timing, as premiumization trends were emerging. And I know I could have communicated better internally early on instead of waiting for data to validate my instinct.

I'm drawn to IIM Calcutta because I want to understand the frameworks behind what I did intuitively. I want to study how organizations systematize innovation rather than relying on individuals pushing back. And I'm particularly interested in how these principles apply in India's market, where I see similar opportunities to reframe categories entirely.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
During my third year at Hassan & Partners, a mid-sized law firm in Cairo with about 80 lawyers, I was staffed on a contract negotiation between a major Egyptian textile manufacturer and a European retail chain. The deal was worth roughly €2.8 million—significant for both parties, but especially for our client who was trying to break into the European market.

The challenge emerged during the final negotiation phase. Our client, the textile manufacturer, wanted aggressive payment terms—30% upfront, the rest within 60 days of delivery. They were worried about cash flow, having invested heavily in new equipment that quarter. The European retailer, however, wanted 90-day terms to align with their own payment cycles. Both were reasonable positions.

But there was a third stakeholder I hadn't initially weighted heavily: my own firm's managing partner. Hassan, the founder, pulled me aside and explained that he'd promised the retail chain's legal counsel—an old friend—that we'd be "reasonable and flexible" to close the deal quickly. Hassan had his own incentives: this was a potential gateway to more European clients, and he wanted the relationship smooth and quick.

So I was caught between three legitimate interests: our client's financial security, the retailer's operational needs, and my firm's business development goals. I spent a weekend working through scenarios. I realized the issue wasn't really the payment terms in isolation—it was risk distribution.

I proposed a structure: 40% upfront, 30% at delivery, 30% at 60 days, with a performance bond covering the retailer's downside if delivery was late. This wasn't my original idea, actually. I borrowed it from a similar deal I'd read about in a journal article, but I adapted it for this situation.

When I presented it, I was honest about the trade-offs with each party. To our client, I explained that while they weren't getting their full upfront payment, the performance bond actually protected them by ensuring the retailer wouldn't delay. To the retailer, I showed that the phased structure reduced their immediate exposure compared to a standard upfront model. And to Hassan, I emphasized that solving the problem creatively—rather than just capitulating—would actually impress his contact more.

They all accepted. The deal closed within two weeks.

What struck me afterward was that I'd almost defaulted to just splitting the difference or deferring to Hassan's preference. Instead, I had to actually understand what each party fundamentally needed—not what they initially asked for. That distinction matters.

This experience shaped how I approach problems now. I think business school, particularly at a school with IE's emphasis on stakeholder-inclusive leadership, would help me develop this instinct more systematically. Right now, it feels somewhat intuitive. I want to understand the frameworks better.

school_name: Fudan University School of Management (Master in Management)
prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
**Essay: Challenging Convention in Renewable Energy**

Throughout my career in renewable energy, I have always believed that challenging conventional thinking is essential to innovation. This is something I have tried to do consistently, and I would like to share an example that illustrates this philosophy.

In my current position, I work in the renewable energy sector, where I have been responsible for various projects. A few years ago, our company was facing challenges with how we approached our business model. The traditional approach in our industry had always been to focus on large-scale installations and major infrastructure projects. However, I began to think differently about this.

I noticed that the market was changing, and consumers wanted more flexible, innovative solutions. I proposed to my team that we should explore new market segments that had not been fully developed. This was unconventional because most of our competitors were still pursuing the traditional model. My idea was to look at emerging opportunities and think about how we could serve different customer needs.

After conducting some research and analysis, I presented my findings to management. I showed that there was significant potential in markets that were previously overlooked. The data I gathered demonstrated that if we shifted our approach, we could capture market share in new areas. My manager was initially skeptical, as this went against the established way of doing business in our industry.

However, I persisted with my proposal. I worked with my team to develop a detailed plan that showed how we could implement this new strategy without abandoning our core business. We outlined the potential benefits, the risks involved, and a timeline for execution. I also made sure to address the concerns that management had raised.

Eventually, my proposal was accepted, and we began to move forward with this new approach. The results were positive. We were able to expand into new market segments and increase our revenue. This experience taught me that sometimes it is necessary to question the way things have always been done and look for better solutions.

This example demonstrates my commitment to innovation and my willingness to challenge conventional thinking. I believe that in business, it is important to constantly evaluate whether your current approach is the best one. By being open to new ideas and willing to take calculated risks, organizations can find new opportunities for growth.

However, I recognize that this was just one example, and there is still much more I need to learn about business strategy and innovation. I believe that an MBA program will help me develop the skills and knowledge needed to become a more effective leader and innovator. I am particularly interested in studying strategy and business development, as these areas relate directly to my experience and future aspirations.

I chose to apply to Fudan University School of Management because I believe the program will provide me with a strong foundation in business fundamentals while also allowing me to develop my leadership capabilities. I am excited about the opportunity to learn from experienced faculty and work with classmates from diverse backgrounds.

In conclusion, my experience challenging conventional thinking in the renewable energy industry has shaped my approach to business and my desire to pursue further education. I am confident that an MBA will help me achieve my career goals and make a meaningful contribution to my field.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I started my career at a crossroads. After graduating from NTU in 2015, I had offers from a local bank and a small B2B software company in Singapore called CloudSync. Everyone advised the bank—stability, brand name, clear path to management. But I took CloudSync. The salary was 15% lower, and I'd be employee number 23. My parents were skeptical.

That decision shaped everything that followed.

For the first three years, I wore every hat imaginable. I sold to mid-market logistics companies, implemented their systems, and built basic features when our product team was overwhelmed. I learned that enterprise sales isn't about closing deals—it's about understanding a customer's broken processes and convincing them that fixing it matters more than the cost. By 2018, I'd brought in $1.2M in ARR, and we promoted me to Sales Director.

But here's what I didn't anticipate: scaling taught me my limits. Leading a team of five felt completely different from individual contribution. I micromanaged early deals, second-guessed my hires, and burned out my team. A sales rep named Deepak left in 2019 and was honest in his exit interview: he felt like I didn't trust his judgment. That stung. I realized I was leading the way I'd been led—by example and intensity—without actually developing people. I brought in a sales coach, read Radical Candor, and completely shifted my approach. The team's performance improved, but more importantly, I stopped losing good people.

By 2021, CloudSync had grown to 80 employees, and I was overseeing four sales managers and $8M in revenue. We expanded into the Philippines and Indonesia, which forced me to think about localization, hiring practices, and managing across very different business cultures. That expansion didn't go smoothly. We lost two key deals in Manila because we assumed the same playbook would work. We had to rebuild our approach, hire locally, and listen more than we pitched.

The pandemic accelerated everything. Customers froze budgets. We pivoted to self-serve models. My sales playbook became almost useless overnight. I had to admit that I didn't have the answers—that none of us did. We brought in a fractional CMO, collaborated with Product in ways we never had before, and rebuilt our growth strategy from first principles. It was humbling and necessary.

That's when I realized I'd hit another ceiling. I was strong at building and managing sales organizations, but I lacked the finance, operations, and strategic framework to contribute at the executive level. Our CEO encouraged me to consider an MBA, and I resisted for two years because it felt like admitting I wasn't enough as I was.

But I'm enough *and* incomplete. An MBA at ESSEC appeals to me precisely because of its emphasis on both technical rigor and international business perspective. I've built something in Southeast Asia, and I want to build it again—but with the tools to do it better, faster, and more strategically. I'm ready to learn.

prompt: Use this essay to share information that is not presented elsewhere in your application, e.g. explanation of employment gaps, academic aberrations, quantitative abilities, etc.
content:
When I look back at my undergraduate transcript, I see a story of someone still figuring out who they were. My first two years at FAST-NUCES in Islamabad show grades that don't reflect my abilities—a 2.8 GPA that haunted me when I applied to graduate programs. I had just come out to my family and was processing what that meant while managing a full course load in a conservative engineering program. I wasn't ready to talk about this then, but it's worth explaining now.

By my junior year, something shifted. My GPA climbed to 3.6 in my final two years as I found stability and clarity about my identity. This wasn't just academic recovery; it was evidence that I could navigate complexity and bounce back. I've carried that lesson into my career.

My quantitative foundation has been consistently strong. My undergraduate degree in computer engineering required rigorous mathematics and systems thinking. In my decade at Zong Pakistan—where I started as a junior network engineer and now manage technical operations for enterprise clients—I've applied these skills daily. I've analyzed network performance metrics, managed budgets exceeding $4.2 million, and led infrastructure projects serving over 2 million users. My GMAT score of 716 reflects this comfort with quantitative reasoning, though I recognize the quant section (46th percentile) is a gap I'll address in the program's quantitative courses.

I'm also aware that my work history shows consistent growth within one company, which some might view as a limitation. But this wasn't about stagnation. It was about building something meaningful. I grew from managing five people to overseeing 23 direct reports. I led the migration of our legacy systems to cloud infrastructure—a $1.8 million initiative that reduced costs by 34% and improved uptime. These weren't impressive titles on paper; they were real problems with measurable impact.

My reasons for pursuing an MBA are professional—I want to transition into general management, and I know my technical background alone won't take me there. But they're also personal. As a non-binary professional in Pakistan's conservative telecom industry, I've navigated spaces where I'm often the only person like me. I believe an MBA from a diverse, urban program will help me develop the business acumen I need while also connecting me with leaders who understand the importance of inclusion.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I decided to apply for an MBA, I knew I wanted a program that would challenge me in a different way than law school did. Yonsei School of Business stood out to me because of its reputation in Asia and its focus on international business, which directly aligns with my career trajectory.

My background is in corporate law at a mid-size firm in Moscow. For the past two years, I have worked on cross-border transactions, primarily M&A deals between Russian and European companies. The work has been intellectually stimulating, but I've noticed a gap in my understanding. I can draft contracts and identify legal risks, but I struggle with the broader business strategy. When a client decides to acquire a company, I understand the regulatory implications. I don't fully understand the financial modeling, market analysis, or operational integration that should drive that decision in the first place.

This realization came during a transaction last year involving a European manufacturing firm. Our team spent weeks on due diligence, but I realized I couldn't meaningfully contribute to discussions about post-merger synergies or valuation multiples. The partner leading the deal made decisions that seemed to ignore what I thought were obvious legal concerns, and I later understood those concerns were minor compared to the strategic business issues at play. I felt out of depth, and that bothered me.

I want to transition from being a supporting player to someone who can lead complex international business decisions. An MBA will give me the financial literacy and strategic thinking I currently lack. More importantly, studying at Yonsei specifically will ground me in Asian markets, which I believe will be central to my career. Russia's business environment is becoming increasingly difficult, and I am interested in expanding my focus toward Southeast Asia and China, where many of my current clients are investing anyway.

Yonsei's curriculum appeals to me particularly because of the required international business courses and the opportunity to study in Seoul, a major financial hub. The location matters—I want to be in Asia, not Europe, during my studies. I also value that your program brings together students from across the region, which will give me the network I need to transition into this market effectively.

What I will bring to Yonsei is perspective from emerging markets and a disciplined approach to problem-solving developed through legal training. I know how to research thoroughly, think through edge cases, and communicate complex ideas clearly. I also bring genuine curiosity about how businesses operate beyond the legal function. In group projects, I will ask the difficult questions about implementation and risk, not just strategy.

I recognize my GMAT score is below your median, and my GPA is weaker than ideal. I struggled with undergraduate subjects outside my major and didn't prepare adequately for the GMAT on my first attempt. This MBA represents a fresh start for me—a chance to prove I can excel in a rigorous business program and build the skills I need for the next phase of my career.
