prompt: Describe a time when you led a team through a significant challenge. What was the outcome?
content:
# A Team Divided in Court

Two years into my role as a legal coordinator at Cordova & Associates in Lima, I thought I knew what I was doing. I managed document review for our corporate litigation team—12 paralegals, 3 associates, endless spreadsheets. Then a multinational client sued us for missing a filing deadline on a $2.3 million trademark case. Not our fault technically, but our internal handoff between teams had failed spectacularly. The senior partner called me in and said, "Fix this before it becomes a bigger disaster."

I wasn't officially a manager. I was 26. But suddenly I was responsible for preventing the same mistake from costing us more cases.

The real problem wasn't the deadline—it was that the corporate litigation team and the IP team didn't trust each other. They sat on opposite sides of our office, literally. Corporate thought IP moved too slowly. IP thought corporate was reckless. When a case touched both areas, documents would sit in limbo for days while people argued over who should do what. Nobody wanted to own the responsibility.

I called separate meetings with both teams first, which was probably a mistake. I just got two versions of the same blame game. So I did something different: I invited both teams to the same room, in our small conference area, and I walked through the botched filing timeline chronologically. I didn't accuse anyone. I just said, "Here's what happened. Here's where it slowed down. I need to understand why." For maybe five uncomfortable minutes, nobody spoke.

Then one of the IP associates said the corporate team had been unclear about what documents were urgent. A corporate paralegal responded that IP never confirmed receipt of requests. It escalated from there, but it wasn't hostile—it was honest. People were frustrated because the system was broken, not because they were incompetent.

I proposed we create a shared handoff template. Every case that touched both teams would move through the same documented process: one person would formally assign work, the other would formally confirm receipt and deadlines, and we'd have weekly 15-minute syncs instead of sending emails into the void. I asked for volunteers from both teams to build it together. Three people volunteered. We spent two weeks refining it, and honestly, most of my input was just saying "yes, that makes sense" and getting out of their way.

The hard part was getting buy-in from the associates who didn't want to add meetings to their already full schedules. I had to sit with each one individually and explain that the 15 minutes of weekly coordination would save them hours of back-and-forth later. I showed them data from our mistakes—how much time we actually lost. One senior associate asked me flat out, "Are you going to make us do this?" I told her the truth: I couldn't make anyone do anything. But if we kept operating the way we had been, cases would keep slipping through the cracks, and that was worse for everyone.

Three months in, we had zero missed handoffs in cases involving both teams. Within six months, the template had caught on informally with other practice areas too.

What struck me most was how quickly things improved once I stopped trying to solve it alone. I thought my job was to come up with the answer. It actually was to create space for the people doing the work to solve it together. I was also humbled by how much I didn't know—I had opinions about how legal operations should work, but I'd never managed anything before. That experience taught me I need to understand how global organizations actually function, where friction happens across teams and borders, and how to navigate that.

That's why I'm drawn to Thunderbird's Executive MBA. I've spent two years in a single office. I want to understand how to scale these insights across countries, cultures, and completely different industries. The global network and the focus on cross-functional leadership are exactly what I need next.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
When I learned about Indian School of Business, I was immediately impressed by its world-class reputation and commitment to developing future business leaders. I knew this was the right place for my MBA journey.

To learn more about ISB, I visited the website and read through the program curriculum. I was particularly interested to see that the school offers courses in healthcare management, which aligns perfectly with my background in the pharmaceutical industry. I also watched some YouTube videos of student testimonials and current students speaking about their experiences. They all seemed very happy and motivated, which gave me confidence that ISB would be a good fit.

I attended one of the virtual information sessions last month, which was helpful in understanding the application process and what the school is looking for. The admissions officer explained the importance of strong academics and work experience, both of which I am proud of. I also learned that ISB has a strong network of alumni across Asia, which would be beneficial for my career goals.

What excites me most about joining ISB is the opportunity to study alongside talented peers from diverse backgrounds. In my current role at a healthcare startup in Bangkok, I have worked with people from many different countries, and I have learned that diversity leads to better problem-solving and innovation. I believe the ISB community would enhance this experience further.

Additionally, I am interested in the school's focus on ethics and sustainability in business. These values are important to me, and I would like to contribute to discussions around how businesses can operate responsibly while still being profitable. I hope to take advantage of clubs and case competitions at ISB to deepen my understanding of these topics.

After graduation, I plan to move into a leadership position in healthcare management, possibly in operations or strategy. An MBA from ISB would give me the analytical skills and international perspective I need to succeed in this role. The alumni network would also open doors for job opportunities across Asia.

I am excited about the possibility of joining the ISB community and contributing my unique perspective as a healthcare professional with experience in emerging markets.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
# Balancing Stakeholder Interests in My Career

Throughout my eight years working in the renewable energy sector, I have encountered many situations where I needed to balance competing interests from different stakeholders. One experience that stands out is when I was managing a solar project implementation at my previous company, and I had to deal with conflicting demands from the client, my team, and senior management.

The project involved installing solar panels at a large commercial facility in São Paulo. The client wanted the installation to be completed as quickly as possible so they could start saving on electricity costs. My team, on the other hand, was concerned about the tight timeline and worried about quality issues if we rushed the work. Meanwhile, senior management was focused on maintaining the profit margin and keeping costs down.

This was a challenging situation because each stakeholder had legitimate concerns. I needed to find a way to satisfy everyone while also ensuring the project's success. I decided to take a more collaborative approach by organizing meetings with all parties involved. I listened to each group's concerns and tried to understand what was really important to them.

After analyzing the situation, I realized that what we actually needed was better planning and communication. I worked with my team to create a detailed project timeline that was ambitious but realistic. I also negotiated with the client to understand their flexibility on the deadline. I explained to senior management that investing in better planning upfront would actually save money in the long run by preventing costly mistakes.

Through these discussions, we were able to reach an agreement that worked for everyone. The client accepted a slightly extended timeline in exchange for guaranteed quality. My team felt more confident about meeting the deadline with the revised schedule. Senior management was satisfied because we found cost efficiencies in other areas of the project that actually improved the profit margin.

The project was completed successfully, and the client was very happy with the results. We finished only one week behind the original deadline, and there were no major quality issues. This experience taught me the importance of listening to all stakeholders and finding creative solutions that address their core concerns rather than just their initial demands.

This situation also showed me that good leadership is about understanding different perspectives and helping people see how working together benefits everyone. It's not about making the loudest voice happy or just splitting the difference – it's about really understanding what matters to each group and finding solutions where people can move forward together.

I believe this skill will be valuable in an MBA program and in my future career. Business is always about managing different interests and perspectives, whether you're dealing with employees, customers, investors, or partners. The ability to balance these interests while maintaining integrity and achieving results is something I want to continue developing.

Going forward, I want to apply these lessons to larger and more complex business challenges. The renewable energy industry is growing rapidly in Latin America, and there will be many opportunities to manage stakeholder interests at a higher level. I believe an MBA from ESPAE-ESPOL will give me the tools and knowledge to take on these bigger challenges and make a greater impact in my field.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
My professional journey has been defined by my passion for solving complex business problems and driving meaningful impact across diverse industries and geographies.

I began my career at McKinsey & Company in our Lima office, where I was hired as an Associate after completing my undergraduate degree. During my time there, I worked on strategy projects spanning sectors like telecommunications, financial services, and energy. This experience taught me the fundamentals of business analysis, client management, and how to think critically about organizational challenges. I was promoted to Senior Associate after two years, which reflected my growing expertise and ability to lead smaller project teams.

In my current role at Bain & Company, which I've held for the past three years, I've continued to develop as a consultant while taking on increasing responsibility. I've managed larger engagements with bigger budgets and have begun mentoring junior consultants. The work has been fulfilling—I've helped clients with digital transformation initiatives, operational efficiency improvements, and go-to-market strategies. I've learned to be a better problem-solver and have developed strong relationships with clients across multiple regions.

Throughout my consulting career, I've been exposed to different business models, industries, and management philosophies. I've worked with C-level executives, learned how to present to boards, and understood what it takes to implement recommendations in complex organizational environments. Each project has added to my toolkit and broadened my perspective on business.

However, I've come to realize that consulting, while intellectually stimulating, is not where I want to spend the rest of my career. I've learned a lot about strategy and operations, but I want to move into a more hands-on operational role where I can see the direct impact of my work. I want to build something, to be accountable for results over a longer time horizon, and to have deeper relationships with teams and organizations. An MBA will help me make this transition by providing me with the business fundamentals I may be missing and by expanding my professional network to include operating executives, not just other consultants.

I'm also interested in eventually returning to Peru or the Latin American region to work on business challenges that are relevant to my home country. I believe an MBA will give me the credibility and the network to make that transition successfully.

The Hebrew University Executive MBA appeals to me because of its strong reputation and international focus. I'm excited about the opportunity to learn alongside experienced professionals from different backgrounds and to gain new perspectives on global business challenges.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
What matters most to me is knowing that the work I do directly improves someone's life—not in theory, but in a way I can actually trace.

I realized this three years ago when I was managing customer operations for Vodafone's rural expansion program in southern Italy. We were rolling out broadband to villages that had been without reliable internet for decades. I spent months optimizing service delivery, cutting installation times, reducing churn. The metrics were solid—cost per user down 22%, satisfaction scores up. But what stuck with me was visiting a small town in Calabria where a woman told me she'd finally been able to help her daughter with homework without making a two-hour trip to the city library. That conversation changed how I see my work.

Since then, everything I do professionally gets filtered through this lens: Am I creating value that matters beyond the quarterly report?

When I moved to leading a team managing network infrastructure projects for a major European operator, I pushed for something unusual. We built an internal program that paired our technical staff with small businesses in underserved regions, helping them understand and optimize their connectivity needs. It wasn't in my job description. It added work. But I believed our expertise shouldn't just sit in enterprise contracts—it should benefit the businesses that were actually trying to grow their local economies. We ended up supporting forty-three small companies. Most trivial to a telecom company. Absolutely critical to those forty-three owners.

The reason this matters to me so deeply is partly my background. My parents came from limited means and understood the compound effect of access—access to education, to opportunity, to information. But it's also about how I want to spend my limited working years. I've realized I don't want to optimize systems for optimization's sake. I want to solve problems where the human impact is visible and real.

An MBA from ISB represents a deliberate pivot toward this. India's scale, complexity, and growth trajectory present problems where management expertise and sector knowledge directly translate into broader impact. The school's emphasis on leadership with social consciousness aligns with how I've come to define success. I'm ready to deepen my strategic thinking and expand my toolkit so that the work I do—whoever I work for—genuinely improves lives in measurable ways.

That's what matters most to me.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
In five years, I see myself leading investment strategy at a mid-market VC fund, managing a portfolio of 15-20 companies across Europe and potentially overseeing $200-300M in assets. I want to move beyond individual deal sourcing and due diligence into the strategic direction of fund deployment, particularly in deep tech and climate tech sectors where I believe capital allocation will matter most.

The EMBA at Toulouse Business School appeals to me because I recognize a real gap in my background. I've spent eight years in venture capital—first as an analyst at a US-based fund, then as a Senior Associate at a mid-market firm in Boston. I'm comfortable reading cap tables and spotting technical founders with potential. What I've lacked is formal training in international expansion, European market dynamics, and the operational side of fund management. Every time my team discusses European expansion, I'm relying on pattern-matching rather than framework. That's not sustainable when I'm making decisions worth millions.

Toulouse specifically makes sense for where I want to go. The executive program's focus on European business and cross-border investment aligns with my goal of launching or joining a fund that's genuinely pan-European, not just US money with European outposts. Most of my career has been US-centric. I need to understand regulatory environments in France, Germany, and Scandinavia in a structured way—not just through conversations with founders.

What appeals to me, honestly, is the cohort model at your school. I've read that many EMBA participants are founders and executives building businesses across borders. That's different from an MBA cohort. I want to learn from people actually operating in Europe, not just studying about it. The core curriculum on international business strategy and the option to focus on innovation and entrepreneurship felt designed for someone in my position.

I know an EMBA is a significant commitment. Working full-time in venture capital while finishing a program won't be simple. But I've hit a ceiling where my instincts can only take me. At 33, with the relationships I've built and the conviction around European tech, this is the right time. The program would give me the language and frameworks to step into a leadership role at a fund in a way that feels earned, not lucky.

school_name: The University of Chicago Booth School of Business
prompt: Chicago Booth appreciates the individual experiences and perspectives that all of our students bring to our community. This respect for different viewpoints creates an open-minded environment that supports curiosity, inspires us to think more broadly, and take risks. At Booth, community is about collaborative thinking and learning from one another to better ourselves, our ideas, and the world around us. The photos below represent some of the values described above that we uphold at Chicago Booth. Select one and share how it resonates with one of your own values.
content:
# Essay Response

I selected the image showing a diverse group of students engaged in what appears to be a heated debate around a conference table. What struck me immediately was that no one looked uncomfortable—they looked alive.

Growing up in Lyon, I learned early that French culture values intellectual rigor and argumentation. Disagreement isn't personal; it's how you sharpen your thinking. But when I moved to Boston for my first biotech role at Vertex Pharmaceuticals seven years ago, I quickly realized this wasn't universal. During my first team meeting, I challenged a colleague's market analysis with what I considered straightforward criticism. The room went silent. Later, my manager pulled me aside and said I needed to "soften my approach."

That moment could have made me retreat. Instead, it made me curious about the gap between my communication style and how it landed. I started watching how the most effective leaders in our Boston office operated. They weren't less direct—they were more intentional. They built trust first, then brought the hard questions.

This became especially relevant when I moved into a senior scientist role managing a seven-person research team. We were developing a biomarker panel for a Phase 3 trial, and two of my team members had fundamentally different approaches to the statistical validation. Instead of deciding between them, I created space for both perspectives. We spent three weeks working through both methodologies, letting the data speak. The process was slower and messier than me just choosing. But it caught an error the faster path would have missed, and my team members felt genuinely heard.

What I've come to value—and what that Booth image represents—isn't just diversity of thought. It's the infrastructure that makes people feel safe enough to voice unpopular ideas. At Vertex, we talk about "psychological safety" in our team charters, but I've seen how easily it becomes a box checked on a form rather than something lived.

I'm applying to Booth specifically because I want to sharpen how I think about scaling this. After seven years in R&D, I'm moving into business development next year, and I'll be navigating more complex stakeholder dynamics across commercial, scientific, and regulatory functions. An MBA feels like the right moment to learn from people who've built cultures that actually work—not just theoretically, but in practice.

What resonates with me about that image is that disagreement is treated as an asset, not a liability. That's the environment where I do my best thinking, and it's also where I help others do theirs. I'm looking for a program that doesn't just talk about collaborative thinking but models it. From what I know of Booth, that seems to be the case.

school_name: Rennes School of Business Master in Management
prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
In my current role as a Product Manager at a major technology company, I am often faced with complex situations where I must balance the needs and expectations of different stakeholders. One particular situation stands out as a defining moment in my career where I had to navigate competing interests effectively.

Last year, our team was tasked with launching a new feature for our mobile application. The engineering team wanted to delay the launch by three months to ensure the code was perfect and there were no bugs. Meanwhile, the sales team was pushing for an immediate launch because they had promised the feature to our biggest client by the end of the quarter. The marketing team had already begun their campaign, and delaying would cost us significant resources. Additionally, our users were expecting this feature based on our roadmap announcements.

I realized that this was a situation where everyone had valid points, and I needed to find a solution that would satisfy all parties. I organized several meetings with the key stakeholders to understand their concerns and priorities more deeply. Through these conversations, I learned that the engineering team's main concern was quality, the sales team's concern was revenue, and the marketing team's concern was brand reputation.

After careful analysis, I proposed a phased launch approach. We would launch the feature on schedule with the core functionality that was ready, while the engineering team could continue working on additional features and optimizations in the background. This allowed us to meet the sales commitment, keep the marketing campaign on track, and ensure that we delivered quality to our users.

The launch was successful, and all stakeholders were satisfied with the outcome. The client was happy because they received the feature on time, the sales team achieved their targets, the marketing team maintained the campaign schedule, and the engineering team was able to continue improving the product without the pressure of a hard deadline.

This experience taught me that balancing competing interests is not about finding a compromise where everyone loses a little. Instead, it is about understanding what truly matters to each stakeholder and finding creative solutions that address their core concerns. Good communication, active listening, and analytical thinking are essential skills for any manager.

Moving forward, I am confident that this experience will help me navigate similar situations in my career. I believe that pursuing an MBA from Rennes School of Business will further develop these skills and prepare me for more complex leadership challenges. The program's focus on management and stakeholder engagement aligns well with my professional goals.

prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
# Leading Without the Title

Three years ago, I was brought into a struggling audit engagement at Deloitte where the client—a mid-sized manufacturing company in Ontario—was on the verge of changing firms. The relationship had deteriorated under the previous senior manager, and our team of six junior staff felt the tension every time we walked into their offices. I wasn't the manager on the file. I was a senior auditor with no formal authority over anyone, but I realized quickly that someone needed to stabilize things or we'd lose the account.

The first thing I did was stop trying to be the previous manager. Instead, I started having actual conversations with our team about what was going wrong. It turned out that the juniors felt micromanaged, the client felt ignored between site visits, and nobody had a clear picture of what success looked like. I called an informal huddle—just coffee in the break room—and asked people what they needed from me. No hierarchy, no agenda. Just listening.

What emerged was that everyone wanted clarity and autonomy. So I restructured how we communicated with the client. Rather than waiting for the manager to approve everything, I started weekly check-in calls where I'd walk through what we'd completed and what was next. I gave our junior staff clear ownership of their sections and let them know I'd back them up if questions came in. This sounds simple in retrospect, but it required me to step into a space that wasn't technically mine to step into.

The risk was real. The manager could have seen it as overstepping. The client could have resisted my direct contact. My team could have seen it as performative. But I decided the downside of not acting was worse than the downside of overreaching slightly.

Within two months, things shifted. Our team started joking again. The client stopped calling with complaints and started asking our opinion on process improvements. We actually finished the audit ahead of schedule, which almost never happens. The following year, we won a new engagement from them.

Looking back, I learned that leading without authority isn't about convincing people you should be in charge. It's about being genuinely useful to the people around you. I was useful because I removed friction points—I communicated upward and downward, I gave people autonomy, and I made sure no one felt stuck. The formal title would have come later, but the leadership actually started by ignoring the absence of one.

This also taught me something about my own limits, though. I spent that engagement running at about 110%, trying to manage personalities and processes from a position that didn't technically allow for it. It worked, but it wasn't sustainable. I eventually moved into a manager role where I had the authority to make some of these changes systematically, which felt like progress. But I also realized I don't always need to be the one solving problems, and that's been harder to internalize than you'd think.

For my MBA, I'm looking to build on this foundation with more structured training in how organizations actually work—how incentives align, how to diagnose problems at a systems level rather than just firefighting, and frankly, how to be more strategic about when to step in and when to step back. I think Vlerick's emphasis on practical business problems appeals to me partly because I've spent over a decade in the field and I need something that connects to the messiness of actual work, not just case studies.

The accounting world has given me valuable experience in risk and detail, but I've realized I want to move into a broader business leadership role where I can apply these instincts about people and process on a larger scale.

prompt: Please use this space to share with the Admissions Committee how you have reflected and grown since your previous application and discuss any relevant updates to your candidacy (e.g., changes in your professional life, additional coursework, and extracurricular/volunteer engagements).
content:

Over the past year, I completed an online financial modeling course from Coursera, which helped strengthen my quantitative skills ahead of a potential reapplication. Additionally, I have become more involved in my local community. I volunteer with an organization that focuses on energy access in underserved regions of Kenya, work that feels particularly important given my background. I am now clearer about my goals: to eventually transition into energy sector management consulting, where I can help companies navigate the transition to renewable energy. I am genuinely excited about the prospect of joining the Wharton community and bringing my twelve years of practical industry experience to contribute to classroom discussions.

school_name: University of Zagreb Faculty of Economics MSc
prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
# Leadership Essay

Throughout my career as an entrepreneur, I have come to understand that leadership is about inspiring others to achieve greatness. This belief has guided me in my professional journey and has helped me become the leader I am today.

During my time building my startup, I faced a significant challenge that tested my leadership abilities. My team was struggling with motivation and productivity had declined. As the founder, I realized that I needed to step up and take charge of the situation. I decided to implement new systems and processes that would help the team work more efficiently.

I started by having one-on-one conversations with my team members to understand their concerns and challenges. I listened to their feedback and made adjustments based on what I heard. This showed them that I valued their input and cared about their wellbeing. I also organized team-building activities to strengthen our relationships and create a more positive work environment.

The results were remarkable. Within a few months, productivity increased significantly and team morale improved. My team members felt more engaged and motivated, and they were willing to go the extra mile for the company. This experience taught me the importance of listening, empathy, and communication in leadership.

I believe that brave leaders are those who are willing to take risks and make difficult decisions. During another period in my business, we had to pivot our business model because the market was changing. This was a difficult decision that could have failed. However, I communicated the vision clearly to my team and we worked together to execute the new strategy. Although there were challenges along the way, we emerged stronger.

Leadership is not just about making decisions from the top down. It's about creating an environment where everyone feels empowered to contribute and make a difference. I have always believed in empowering my team members to take ownership of their work and to pursue their own goals within the company.

Through my entrepreneurial journey, I have learned that leadership requires continuous learning and growth. I have made mistakes, and I have learned from them. I know that an MBA will help me develop my leadership skills further and prepare me to lead with greater confidence and competence. I am excited about the opportunity to learn from experienced professors and peers at Kellogg.

In conclusion, my experiences as an entrepreneur have shaped me into a leader who values listening, empathy, and empowerment. I am confident that I will bring these qualities to the Kellogg community and contribute positively to the program.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
In five years, I want to be running content operations for a major streaming platform across Asia-Pacific, responsible for everything from production strategy to distribution logistics. More specifically, I see myself leading a team of 20-30 people, managing a budget in the $15-20 million range, and having a seat at the table where executives decide which markets to enter and which content to greenlight.

This isn't entirely new territory for me. At my current company, a mid-sized production house in Tokyo, I oversee localization for five regional markets and manage relationships with broadcast partners. I've grown the division from three people to twelve over four years, increased revenue by roughly 40%, and learned how to navigate the messy reality of translating creative vision into operational reality across different cultures and regulatory environments. But I've hit a ceiling. To move into that regional leadership role I'm chasing, I need to understand the business side more rigorously—how streaming economics actually work, how to build scalable operations in emerging markets, how to make decisions when data conflicts with intuition.

HKUST's EMBA appeals to me specifically because of how its curriculum treats Asia as the central market, not a secondary consideration. Most business schools teach frameworks built for American or European contexts, then add "what about Asia?" as an afterthought. That doesn't work anymore. The streaming wars are being decided in Southeast Asia and India, not just in California. Your program's focus on Asian business ecosystems, combined with the concentration options in technology and operations, directly addresses what I need to learn.

What's equally important is the cohort experience. I've worked in this industry long enough to know that my biggest blind spots aren't technical—they're about understanding how other industries actually operate. I need to learn from classmates in finance, tech, and supply chain management. At HKUST, I'd be surrounded by working professionals from across Asia with deep expertise in their respective fields. That environment is hard to replicate, and it's something I need right now.

I'm also realistic about what the MBA won't do. It won't instantly unlock a regional VP role. What it will do is give me the vocabulary, frameworks, and network to be credible in rooms where those decisions are made. It will help me think systematically about market entry strategies, operational scaling, and financial modeling in ways I currently approach intuitively. After the program, I plan to move into a regional content role—probably with one of the major streamers—where I can apply what I've learned while continuing to prove myself operationally before eventually stepping into that leadership position.

The timing feels right. I'm not running away from something; I'm running toward the next level of what I'm already building. HKUST feels like the bridge between where I am and where I need to be.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
What matters most to me is understanding the gap between what a business says it values and what it actually does.

I noticed this first at Beiersdorf, where I spent two years in brand management. We had this mission statement about sustainability that lived on the company intranet, printed on posters in the break room. Meanwhile, in my team's quarterly planning sessions, sustainability initiatives were always the first things cut when we needed to hit margin targets. I watched a proposal for reducing plastic packaging get shelved because it cost 0.3% more per unit. No one said it directly, but the message was clear: sustainability mattered until it didn't.

That contradiction bothered me more than it probably should have. Most people seemed fine with the gap. I wasn't.

So when I moved to a mid-market CPG company in Hamburg last year, I was more skeptical. I expected the same thing. But what I found was different. Our CEO actually turned down a distribution deal with a major retailer because their labor practices didn't align with what we claimed to believe. The decision cost us probably €800k in projected revenue. I watched the CFO nearly have a heart attack in the board meeting. But we did it anyway.

That's when I realized what actually matters to me: organizations where the difficult choice and the right choice are the same thing. Or at least, where the leadership honestly tries to make them the same thing.

It's not about being naive or self-righteous. It's not about perfection. I know every company makes compromises. But there's a difference between a compromise you can defend and a compromise you have to hide from yourself. I want to work somewhere that's honest about which is which.

This matters to me in the small moments too. In my current role, I inherited a team of four people who had been told for two years that a promotion restructure was coming. It never came. When I arrived, they were cynical and disengaged. Instead of repeating the same empty promise, I told them the truth: restructures take time, and I didn't want to set expectations I couldn't meet. Some of them were disappointed. But then we started being more transparent about where the business was going, what performance actually meant, and what was actually possible. Two of them are now genuinely excited about their career progression because they know what they're actually working toward.

What I'm searching for in an MBA is clarity about what I want to build. Right now, I work in marketing and brand management, and I'm reasonably good at it. But I don't think that's where my energy actually goes. My energy goes to questions about how organizations actually function — why they succeed and fail, why people choose to stay or leave, why some cultures are resilient and others aren't. I want to be the person in the room who understands both the spreadsheet and the human side, so I can eventually lead a company where those two things aren't in conflict.

Insper appeals to me because of its emphasis on critical thinking and entrepreneurial action. I want to study somewhere that doesn't just teach best practices but interrogates them. Brazil's business culture is different from Germany's, and I want to understand those differences. I also want to build a network of people who are similarly restless with the gap between aspiration and reality.

I don't expect business school to solve this for me. But I need the space to think more deeply about it, to test my assumptions, and to meet people who are equally serious about this question. That's what matters most — finding the gap and figuring out how not to widen it.

prompt: Why do you intend on pursuing an MBA at this point in your life?
content:
Why MBA at Desautels

I am applying to the Executive MBA program at Desautels because I want to advance my career in real estate and become a better leader. Over the past seven years, I have worked in various roles in the real estate industry, and I believe an MBA will help me achieve my goals.

Real estate is a dynamic and growing field, and I have always been interested in business and finance. During my time working in this industry, I have realized that to move forward and take on more senior positions, I need to strengthen my business acumen and strategic thinking skills. An MBA from a reputable program like Desautels will give me the knowledge and credentials necessary to succeed.

I chose Desautels because it is a well-respected program located in Montreal, and I am looking to study in Canada. The Executive MBA format appeals to me because I can continue working while pursuing my degree, which is important given my current responsibilities. I have researched the program and believe the curriculum aligns with my career objectives.

Throughout my career in real estate, I have worked with different teams and clients. I have learned the importance of communication, problem-solving, and understanding market dynamics. However, I recognize that my background in real estate operations has not exposed me deeply to areas like financial analysis, corporate strategy, and organizational management. These are critical skills that I lack and that an MBA will provide.

Furthermore, as someone with an Italian background, I see value in building a network in North America. An MBA at Desautels would connect me with professionals from different industries and backgrounds, which could open doors for future opportunities. Networking is essential in business, and I believe this program will provide valuable connections.

Looking ahead, I see myself potentially moving into a development or investment role within real estate, possibly managing larger projects or even starting my own venture. To do this successfully, I need the strategic framework and financial expertise that an MBA offers. I want to understand how to evaluate deals from multiple angles, manage complex projects, and lead teams effectively.

In conclusion, pursuing an MBA at Desautels is the logical next step in my career. It will equip me with the necessary skills, knowledge, and network to advance in real estate and become a more effective leader. I am committed to the program and believe it will be transformative for my professional future.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# A Better Way to Say No

At the Abu Dhabi National Oil Company, I managed a team of fifteen engineers tasked with optimizing our upstream asset performance. By 2019, our performance metrics had plateaued for three consecutive quarters. Leadership wanted incremental improvements—maybe 2-3% efficiency gains through better maintenance scheduling and equipment upgrades. Everyone was satisfied with this approach. Everyone except me.

I kept thinking about the data we were collecting but not using. For years, we'd gathered operational information from hundreds of sensors across our fields—pressure readings, temperature fluctuations, flow rates—but our analysis was siloed by discipline. Reservoir engineers didn't talk to production engineers. Equipment specialists worked independently from logistics teams. We had real-time information we were essentially ignoring.

I proposed something that made my boss visibly uncomfortable: we should reorganize around integrated data analysis rather than functional expertise. Instead of each discipline optimizing their piece, we'd create cross-functional pods that analyzed problems together. More meetings, more coordination, more messiness in the short term.

The reaction was predictable. "That's how we've always done it," people said. "You'll just create confusion." One senior engineer told me I was trying to fix what wasn't broken. The hierarchy was entrenched—people's authority came from their specialized expertise, and this threatened that.

I spent three months building the case with actual numbers. I pulled data showing communication gaps that had cost us approximately $2.3 million in preventive maintenance on equipment that didn't actually need it. I documented seventeen instances where problems identified by one discipline should have informed decisions made by another. I modeled what integrated analysis could have caught.

More importantly, I didn't push this as a permanent restructure. I proposed a three-month pilot with one producing asset and volunteer team members. Lower stakes. Reversible.

The pilot worked. Within two months, our integrated team identified a compressor efficiency issue that no single discipline had flagged independently. The fix generated an additional $890,000 in quarterly production value with almost no capital investment. By month four, we'd implemented the approach across four additional assets.

What I learned wasn't revolutionary, but it shifted how I lead. Conventional thinking exists for reasons—usually good ones, at some point. But when circumstances change, when you have new information or tools, defending the status quo becomes an active choice, not a default. The hard part isn't having the idea. It's actually persuading people to try it, and doing so respectfully without dismissing why they preferred the old way.

As I move toward an MBA, I'm interested in how organizations can institutionalize this kind of thinking without creating chaos. Syracuse Whitman's emphasis on integrated business problem-solving and your collaborations with local manufacturing firms particularly appeal to me. I want to understand how to scale this across more complex systems, and your curriculum on organizational strategy and change management aligns exactly with that goal. I'm ready to push on complacency—but smarter, with better frameworks, and with people alongside me rather than against me.

school_name: Stockholm School of Economics Executive MBA
prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
# Failure: A Stepping Stone to Success

Throughout my career in the medical devices industry, I have learned that failure is not something to be feared, but rather an opportunity for growth and self-improvement. One particular failure stands out as a turning point in my professional journey.

Three years ago, I was leading a project at my company to launch a new diagnostic device in the South Asian market. I was excited about this opportunity and believed that my understanding of the region would be enough to ensure success. However, I made several critical mistakes that ultimately led to the project's failure.

First, I did not conduct thorough market research before launching the product. I assumed that what worked in one country would automatically work in another, which proved to be a costly mistake. I also failed to build a strong team around me, instead relying heavily on my own knowledge and experience. This meant that I was not open to different perspectives and ideas from my colleagues.

The project ultimately failed because the product did not meet local regulatory requirements and customer expectations. We had to withdraw from the market after only six months, and the company lost significant resources. I felt disappointed and ashamed, not only about the financial loss but also because I had let down my team and the company.

However, this failure taught me invaluable lessons that have shaped how I approach my work today. First, I learned the importance of thorough market research and due diligence before entering new markets. I realized that assumptions, no matter how confident I felt, could lead to serious consequences. Second, I learned the value of building diverse teams and listening to different perspectives. I understood that my way of thinking was not the only way, and that collaboration and open communication are essential to success.

After this experience, I made significant changes to how I worked. I became more humble and willing to listen to others. I invested time in understanding local regulations, customer needs, and competitive landscapes before making strategic decisions. I also built stronger relationships with my colleagues and created an environment where people felt comfortable sharing their ideas and concerns.

Since then, I have successfully led several product launches in emerging markets. These successes would not have been possible without the lessons I learned from my earlier failure. I now approach challenges with a more thoughtful and collaborative mindset.

This experience has reinforced my belief that failure is not the end of the road, but rather a valuable lesson that can help us become better professionals and leaders. I am grateful for this failure because it has made me more resilient, humble, and aware of my limitations.

As I pursue an MBA at Stockholm School of Economics, I am committed to bringing this learning mindset to the classroom. I believe that an MBA will help me develop the strategic and analytical skills needed to succeed in global markets. I am particularly interested in learning about international business strategy and how to navigate complex regulatory environments.

In conclusion, my failure in the South Asian market was a humbling experience that taught me the importance of thorough research, teamwork, and humility. These lessons have shaped my professional approach and will continue to guide me as I pursue my MBA and advance in my career.

prompt: What are your short-term and long-term professional goals? How will our MBA program help you achieve them?
content:
**What are your short-term and long-term professional goals? How will our MBA program help you achieve them?**

When I started at Ripley, Peru's largest department store chain, as an associate in visual merchandising eleven years ago, I had no idea I'd still be there today—or that I'd need an MBA to take the next step. That's the honest version. The ambitious version says I always knew I wanted to lead, but the truth is more complicated. I was good at what I did, got promoted, and kept getting promoted, until one day I realized I'd hit a ceiling made of spreadsheets and business terminology I didn't fully understand.

Currently, I manage the e-commerce operations team for Ripley's online division across three countries. I oversee about fifteen people, manage a budget of roughly $2.8 million annually, and we've grown online sales by 34% year-over-year. But here's what keeps me up at night: I can manage operations, but I can't model a P&L with confidence. I can lead a team, but I struggle to articulate strategic business cases to our C-suite. I've learned through doing, and that's gotten me far, but it's also made me aware of what I'm missing.

My short-term goal is straightforward: I want to move into a regional operations director role within two years, ideally managing across multiple business units—not just e-commerce. This requires me to understand financial analysis, supply chain strategy, and how to think about organizational structure in a way that creates actual value. Right now, I rely on my CFO to translate numbers into meaning. That needs to change.

Long-term, I want to lead a retail or e-commerce operation at the C-level. Not to check a box. I've seen too many leaders who just wanted the title and the salary. I want it because I understand the impact—how inventory management decisions ripple through supply chains, how pricing strategy affects margins and customer acquisition, how team culture actually drives business results. I've seen those connections happen in my current role, and I want to see them at scale.

I'm choosing IMT Ghaziabad specifically because of the Executive PGDM's focus on South Asian business contexts. Retail in Peru and retail across India and Southeast Asia aren't identical, but they share real similarities—emerging middle classes, varying levels of digital adoption by region, complex logistics challenges, and the need to balance growth with margin discipline. The faculty expertise in this region actually matters to my career because I'm thinking about whether Ripley expands more aggressively into other Latin American markets, and understanding how other emerging market retailers have scaled regionally could be directly applicable.

The program's structure also appeals to me. I can't leave my job—logistically it's difficult, and Ripley has supported my development. The flexibility to study while working means I won't be starting over; I'll be building on what I already know. That's not lazy thinking; it's practical. I don't need an MBA to learn what ambition is. I need one to develop the analytical and strategic frameworks I'm currently borrowing from others.

I'm not going to claim this MBA will transform me. I'll get out what I put in. But after eleven years of learning in real time, I'm ready for the structured foundation that will let me actually lead—not just manage.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
# Ethical Dilemma in My Professional Journey

Throughout my seven years working in the oil and gas industry, I have encountered numerous situations that required me to balance business objectives with ethical considerations. One particular incident stands out as a defining moment in my career, where I had to make a difficult decision that challenged my professional integrity.

During my tenure as a senior operations coordinator at an international energy company, I was tasked with overseeing a project in Southeast Asia. The project involved managing supplier relationships and ensuring compliance with local environmental regulations. One of our key suppliers was operating below the environmental standards set by the local government, but their pricing was significantly lower than competitors, which would have helped us meet our budget targets.

My supervisor indicated that many companies in the region operated this way, and that overlooking these minor violations was common practice. The pressure to reduce costs was immense, as our project was already facing budget constraints. However, I knew that this approach was wrong. The environmental standards existed for a reason, and compromising on them would not only harm the environment but also put the company at legal and reputational risk.

I decided to escalate the issue to the compliance department. I documented all the violations I had observed and presented my findings to the ethics committee. This was a challenging moment because it meant potentially delaying the project and increasing costs, which my supervisor was not happy about. There was tension in our working relationship for several weeks.

However, I remained firm in my conviction that doing the right thing was more important than short-term financial gains. The compliance team investigated the situation and confirmed my observations. The company ultimately decided to change suppliers, which did increase project costs by approximately 15%, but ensured full compliance with local regulations.

This experience taught me that integrity is not negotiable, regardless of the business pressures we face. The decision was difficult, and there were consequences, but the outcome demonstrated that ethical behavior and sound business practices can coexist. The company avoided potential legal issues and protected its reputation in the region.

This incident has shaped my approach to leadership and decision-making. I now understand that true professional success is not measured solely by meeting financial targets, but by how we conduct ourselves when facing difficult choices. As I transition into a more strategic leadership role, I believe it is crucial to foster a culture where ethical considerations are prioritized alongside business objectives.

The Executive MBA program at IIM Udaipur appeals to me because of its emphasis on responsible leadership and business ethics. I am eager to deepen my understanding of how organizations can balance profit with purpose, and how leaders can drive sustainable value creation while maintaining high ethical standards.

school_name: Nanyang Business School MSc in Management
prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
When my VP told the team we were shutting down our Israeli operations, I wasn't thinking about leadership. I was thinking about whether I'd just become unemployed.

It was December 2021. Our SaaS company, CloudVault, had burned through runway faster than expected. The decision came down from HQ in Boston: consolidate everything in the US, let the Tel Aviv office go. My VP called me into his office and said, "We need someone to manage the wind-down. It has to be done right." Then he left it to me.

I was a Senior Account Executive. Managing accounts, not people. Definitely not managing the dissolution of an entire office. But that afternoon, I had 23 people on my team—engineers, salespeople, operations staff—who didn't know their jobs were disappearing in six weeks.

The temptation was to just execute efficiently: notify everyone, help them find new jobs, tie up loose ends. Instead, I decided something that felt risky at the time. I would be honest.

I scheduled individual meetings with everyone. Not group announcements, not just an email. I sat with each person, told them exactly what was happening, answered their questions directly, and then asked what they needed from me. Some people were relieved—they'd been worried about the company's direction anyway. Others were devastated. One engineer cried in my office. I didn't try to fix that or rush through it.

What emerged surprised me. People didn't leave immediately. Instead, they actually cared about finishing what they'd started. One developer told me, "If you're going to stick around and help us, I'll stick around and help the company." So we made a pact: we'd give the remaining time our best effort. We'd ensure customer handoffs were clean, documentation was complete, no one was left in the lurch.

Over six weeks, we managed a complex transition. We helped twelve people land jobs at other Israeli tech companies. For the remaining eleven who wanted to relocate to the US, I advocated hard with the Boston office for visa sponsorship, and we secured it for ten of them. We lost zero customers to angry departures. The handoff code was documented so thoroughly that the new team finished the migration in half the time they'd planned.

What I learned wasn't about productivity or execution. It was that leadership isn't a title—it's the choice to care about people even when the situation is uncomfortable. It's being transparent when silence would be easier. It's advocating upward when your team needs something.

I came out the other side unemployed for about two months, but I also came out of it knowing I could lead in hard circumstances. I'm looking for an MBA program that will help me apply that instinct more systematically, not just in crisis moments. Kellogg's focus on developing brave leaders—ones who do the right thing even when it's complicated—resonates deeply with who I want to become.

prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
Leading Without Authority at TechStart

Throughout my ten years working in the tech industry, I have learned that leadership is not about the title you hold, but about the influence you can create. One experience that truly shaped my understanding of this concept happened at my current startup, where I work as a Senior Developer.

Our company was struggling with communication between the engineering and product teams. The two departments were not aligned on project goals, which was causing delays and frustration. As a developer without any management position, I realized that I could take initiative to solve this problem.

I organized weekly sync meetings between the two teams and created a shared document where everyone could write their priorities and concerns. Through these meetings, I helped both teams understand each other's perspectives and constraints. I also suggested that we implement a new project management tool to improve transparency. The teams agreed with my suggestion, and we started using this tool to track progress and communicate more effectively.

After several months, the communication between engineering and product improved significantly. Project delays decreased by about 30%, and the teams reported feeling more satisfied with their collaboration. This was a great success for the company and showed me that I could make a real difference without having formal authority.

From this experience, I learned that leadership comes from taking initiative and caring about the success of the team. I realized that by being proactive and listening to people's concerns, I could influence others and create positive change. I also learned that good communication is the foundation of any successful team, and that sometimes the best solutions come from listening to what people actually need.

This experience has made me more confident in my ability to lead and influence others. It has shown me that I don't need a management title to be a leader. I believe that an MBA from Keio Business School will help me develop these leadership skills further and prepare me for more challenging roles in the future.

prompt: Describe your professional goals, how you expect the Broad Executive MBA to impact these goals, and why this is the ideal time to pursue an MBA.
content:
When I joined Novartis as a senior research scientist ten years ago, I thought I had my career trajectory figured out. I'd climb the R&D ladder, maybe lead a therapeutic program by my mid-40s. But three years ago, when I took on a cross-functional role managing a $40M clinical pipeline rebuild after a failed Phase 3 study, something shifted. I wasn't just excited about the chemistry anymore—I was energized by the decisions about resource allocation, team dynamics, and strategic pivots. That's when I realized: I wanted to move from being a deep technical expert to being the person making decisions that shape the organization itself.

My goal over the next five years is to transition into a general management role—ideally heading a therapeutic area or a smaller biotech subsidiary. But I've hit a ceiling in my current organization. The jump from senior scientist to VP typically requires an MBA or similar credential, and more importantly, it requires the broader business acumen I simply don't have. I can tell you the IC50 of our lead compound with precision. I cannot construct a coherent financial model or speak confidently about M&A strategy in a board meeting. That gap frustrates me because I know I have the leadership instinct and the strategic thinking—I just need the actual toolkit.

The Broad Executive MBA appeals to me specifically because of its density and format. I don't have the luxury of stepping away from Novartis for two years; I have three direct reports who depend on me and active programs that can't lose continuity. Broad's weekend and module-based structure lets me stay embedded in my role while still getting real rigor. More importantly, Broad has historically placed people like me—scientists with strong technical backgrounds who want to broaden into management. That's not coincidence.

The timing is now because I've been recruited twice in the past year for general management roles at mid-cap biotech firms, and both times I've had to decline because I wasn't confident I could credibly step into those positions without stronger business training. I'm at the inflection point where the investment in an MBA directly unblocks my next chapter. I'm ready to stop being the smartest person in the lab about molecules, and start being competent at the things that actually determine whether molecules ever reach patients.

prompt: Goizueta Business School is a values-driven community with a mission to prepare principled leaders to have an influence on business and society. Please tell us which of the Goizueta Core Values (Courage, Accountability, Integrity, Rigor, Diversity, Team, Community) resonates most with you and share a personal account of demonstrating leadership in this area within your current or previous company or service organization. The committee is interested in a personal story of leadership.
content:
I am drawn to the value of Courage because I believe it is essential for any leader who wants to make a meaningful impact. Throughout my experience in entrepreneurship, I have learned that courage means taking risks and standing up for what you believe in, even when it is difficult.

At my startup, we were developing a new product that I was very passionate about. However, about six months into the project, I realized that the initial market research was flawed and we were heading in the wrong direction. I knew that telling my team and investors this would be challenging because we had already invested significant time and resources.

I decided to be courageous and honest. I organized a meeting with the leadership team and presented my findings. I explained why I thought we needed to pivot our approach, even though it meant admitting that my original vision was not as solid as I had thought. To my surprise, everyone appreciated my honesty and we were able to adjust our strategy together.

This experience taught me that courage is not about being fearless—it is about doing the right thing even when you are afraid. As a leader, I want to continue developing this quality because I know it will help me make better decisions and lead with integrity.

I believe Goizueta's focus on courage aligns with my personal values and goals. I am excited about the possibility of learning from a community that prioritizes principled leadership.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
# Kellogg Essay

Three years ago, I was sitting in our Dubai office when my director asked me to lead a project that had already failed twice. The NGO's women's entrepreneurship program was supposed to connect female business owners across the UAE with mentors and funding. Sounds straightforward, right? Except our first two attempts ended with scattered mentors, no real relationships forming, and maybe 30% of the women actually completing the program.

My director was honest: "I'm not sure this is fixable." Part of me agreed with her.

But I'd seen something in those incomplete cohorts. The women who *had* connected with mentors weren't just getting business advice—they were changing. One woman came back after six months and said her mentor had helped her finally ask for a business loan. Another told me she'd never spoken to someone who'd actually built a company before. The relationships mattered more than the curriculum.

So I rebuilt the whole thing. First, I spent two weeks interviewing women from the first cohort—not asking what went wrong with the program, but what they actually needed. I learned that the mentorship model was too formal, meeting quarterly felt impersonal, and mentors weren't giving honest feedback because they didn't think it was their place to criticize. Everyone was being too polite.

I then had a harder conversation: I told our mentor pool that we were changing expectations. Mentors would now commit to monthly calls, not quarterly. I'd give them a simple framework—talk about your biggest business failure first—to break the ice and make feedback feel normal. Some mentors pushed back. One said this was too much time. I didn't force anyone to stay. I'd rather have ten committed mentors than fifty who were just checking a box.

For the third cohort, we also changed who we recruited. Instead of a broad call for "women entrepreneurs," we focused on women already running businesses—even if they were small. They were more likely to take mentorship seriously and could relate to each other's problems.

When the third cohort graduated nine months later, 87% completed the program. More importantly, six months after graduation, we surveyed the women. 62% said they'd raised capital or increased their business revenue. Some of the mentor relationships were still going strong. One pair told us they'd become actual business partners.

Here's what that experience taught me: leadership isn't about having all the answers. It's about being honest enough to say something isn't working, curious enough to understand why, and brave enough to make changes even when people are uncomfortable. It's also about recognizing that when you do change something, people will leave—and that's okay.

I'm drawn to Kellogg because I see that same philosophy embedded in how you frame leadership. "Equip and inspire" suggests leadership isn't about command-and-control; it's about creating conditions where people can actually grow. That's what I'm trying to do in the nonprofit space, and I want to bring that more intentionally to my next chapter. An Executive MBA from Kellogg feels like the right place to deepen that thinking, especially around how to scale impact when you're leading across cultures and competing interests.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
A few years ago, I faced a challenging situation at my manufacturing company that required me to think outside the box. Our production line was experiencing frequent delays due to inefficient scheduling, and this was affecting our ability to meet customer deadlines.

The problem was complex because we had multiple variables to consider: machine capacity, labor availability, raw material supply, and customer orders. The traditional approach would have been to hire more staff or invest in new equipment, but both options were expensive and not feasible given our budget constraints at the time.

I decided to implement a creative solution by developing a new scheduling system. Instead of using the old manual method, I introduced a more systematic approach that prioritized orders based on deadlines and machine availability. This involved collaborating with different departments to understand their constraints and requirements.

The implementation process took about three months. I worked with the operations team to map out the workflow and identify bottlenecks. Then, I created a simple tracking system that helped us visualize production schedules more effectively. The key was to get buy-in from the team, so I held several meetings to explain the benefits and address concerns.

The results were positive. We were able to reduce production delays by approximately 25% within the first quarter of implementation. Customer satisfaction improved, and we were able to maintain our schedule without hiring additional staff. This solution also helped us save costs, which was a significant benefit for the company.

This experience taught me that creativity is not always about inventing something completely new. Sometimes it's about combining existing elements in a different way to solve a problem. I learned the importance of listening to team members, understanding their perspectives, and working collaboratively toward a common goal.

This is why I believe an MBA from Curtin Business School would be valuable for my career. The program will give me the tools and frameworks to approach complex business problems more strategically and develop solutions that create value for organizations.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
In five years, I want to be running content strategy for a major streaming platform in Europe, ideally leading a team that's responsible for original programming acquisition across 3-4 markets. That's specific because it's what I actually see myself doing—not some sanitized version of ambition, but the actual work that excites me.

Right now at MBC Group in Cairo, I oversee content partnerships for our digital platforms. We've grown the subscriber base from 400K to 1.2M in two years, partly because I started negotiating directly with international producers instead of relying on legacy distribution partnerships. But here's what I'm realizing: I'm hitting the ceiling of what I can accomplish without deeper financial modeling, strategic pricing frameworks, and—honestly—a stronger European network. The Middle East streaming market is nascent. Europe is where the competitive game is actually being played.

That's why SDA Bocconi specifically matters to me. Not just because it's ranked well, but because of its location and its specific strengths in media and entertainment strategy. Milan is the cultural hub of Southern Europe, and your school's relationships with companies like Netflix, Amazon, and Sky are exactly the ecosystem I need to enter. I've looked at programs in London and Amsterdam, but Bocconi's curriculum—particularly the Strategic Management and Digital Strategy modules—speaks directly to what I need to learn. I need to understand how European streaming companies make acquisition decisions differently than we do in the Middle East. Why does Sky prioritize different content than Netflix in Italy? How do you price in saturated markets?

More than coursework, though, I want your network. I'm Egyptian in a male-dominated media industry in a region where women rarely move into strategic leadership. Europe won't be easier exactly, but it'll be different in ways that actually matter to my career trajectory. Your alumni network in streaming and entertainment is genuinely formidable—I've seen their names on the deals I'm trying to replicate.

In five years, I don't just want a bigger title. I want to have built something—a team, a strategy, maybe even helped launch a platform in a new market. SDA Bocconi isn't just the path to that. It's the only path that makes sense given where I am now and where I'm trying to go.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
My career journey has been a valuable learning experience that has shaped my professional development in meaningful ways. Since graduating, I have worked in the retail and e-commerce sector, which has provided me with exposure to different aspects of business operations and management.

I started my career at a large retail company in France where I worked as an Assistant Manager for approximately two years. In this role, I was responsible for managing daily operations, supporting the store manager, and helping to ensure that customers received good service. I worked with a team of around 15 people and learned about inventory management, staff scheduling, and customer service best practices. This position gave me a foundation in understanding how retail businesses work and the importance of operational efficiency.

After this, I moved to an e-commerce company based in Paris where I have been working for the past two years as a Senior Associate in the Operations department. My responsibilities have included coordinating logistics, managing supplier relationships, and analyzing performance metrics to improve efficiency. I have been involved in several projects aimed at optimizing our warehouse processes and reducing delivery times. Working in e-commerce has taught me about the fast-paced nature of online retail and the importance of technology in modern business operations.

Throughout my career, I have developed skills in problem-solving, team management, and data analysis. I have learned to work well with colleagues from different backgrounds and departments, which I believe is essential in today's business environment. I have also become more confident in my ability to handle multiple tasks and meet deadlines under pressure.

One of the key lessons I have learned is that business is fundamentally about understanding what customers want and finding ways to deliver value to them efficiently. Both in retail and e-commerce, I have seen how important it is to focus on the customer experience while also managing costs and maintaining profitability.

I believe that my experience in both traditional retail and modern e-commerce gives me a unique perspective on the evolving nature of commerce. However, I recognize that my current knowledge has limitations, and there is much more I need to learn to advance further in my career. An MBA from Deakin would provide me with the strategic and analytical skills necessary to move into management positions where I can have a greater impact.

I am interested in the Deakin Executive MBA program because it is recognized and will help me develop the competencies I need. The flexibility of the program is also appealing given my current work commitments. I am excited about the opportunity to study alongside experienced professionals and learn from their perspectives.

In conclusion, my career so far has been a steady progression from an Assistant Manager role to a Senior Associate in operations. I have developed practical skills and gained industry experience, but I feel I need formal business education to take the next step in my career. The MBA will help me transition from operational roles to strategic management positions where I can contribute more significantly to business success. I am committed to my professional development and believe that Deakin's program will be a valuable investment in my future.
