I am committed to the program and to making the most of the opportunity if I am accepted. I am ready to challenge myself and to grow as a business leader and entrepreneur.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
When I joined Nestlé Philippines as a junior sales executive in 2015, I thought the job would be straightforward—move product, hit targets, climb the ladder. Nine years later, I realize what actually changed wasn't the job description, but my relationship with failure and what I'm willing to learn from it.

My GMAT score of 666 sits below where I'd like it to be, and I'm not going to dress that up. I scored a 710 on my first attempt eight months ago, then a 666 on my second. I wanted to retake it again, but my boss—Maria, our regional marketing director—asked me something that stopped me cold: "Are you preparing for the test, or preparing for the degree?" She was right. I'd spent three months grinding through practice problems when what I actually needed was clarity on why I was doing this at all.

That conversation led me to apply to EPGP, and I want to tell you why.

In 2019, I was promoted to Trade Marketing Manager for our biscuits and confectionery portfolio. On paper, it looked like a promotion. In reality, I was managing a portfolio that had lost 12% market share in two years, with a team of four people and a budget that hadn't increased since 2017. I thought the problem was execution—we just needed to work harder, run smarter campaigns, optimize the distributor network. I was wrong.

What I didn't see was that our entire category strategy was built on assumptions about Filipino consumer behavior that were no longer true. We were chasing growth in volume-driven channels when our target consumer—young professionals in Metro Manila and Cebu—had shifted to premiumization. Our pricing was stuck in 2016. Our product line was eight years old. I kept rearranging deck chairs while the ship was moving in a different direction.

It took me eighteen months of flat performance before I actually asked the right questions. I brought in two of our district managers to help me conduct informal consumer interviews. We did this on weekends, visiting small sari-sari stores, malls, homes. One conversation I remember clearly: a 28-year-old marketing manager told us she'd stopped buying our flagship brand because it felt like what her mother bought, not what she wanted. That sentence broke something open in me.

We rebuilt that portfolio from the ground up. New positioning, a premium sub-brand, different packaging, refreshed pricing. It took fourteen months to launch, and when it did, the new premium line captured 8% of the total category value in the first year. Our overall share recovered to 2017 levels. It wasn't a home run, but it was the first time I'd actually led something that required me to challenge my own assumptions rather than optimize around them.

That experience is why I need an MBA now, and specifically why EPGP makes sense for me. I'm nine years into consumer goods, which is far enough in to know what I don't know. I've managed teams, managed budgets, launched products. But I've also felt the ceiling of what I can do with instinct and hard work alone. I need frameworks for strategic thinking. I need to understand how to read a balance sheet and build a financial model. I need exposure to leaders who've navigated the kind of paradigm shifts my industry is going through right now—e-commerce adoption, direct-to-consumer models, shifting retail dynamics in Southeast Asia.

EPGP appeals to me because I'm not looking for a full reset. I'm working, I know my industry, and I want a degree that acknowledges that while pushing me to think differently. The executive format means I can bring my current experience into the classroom instead of boxing it up for two years.

My GMAT score isn't where I wanted it to be. But my motivation is clear, and my conviction about what I need is as certain as anything I've felt in my career.

school_name: University of Leeds Business School MSc Management
prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
When I am not working in my role at the Japan Self-Defense Force, I enjoy spending time with my family and exploring new cultures. Throughout my career, I have developed a passion for learning and understanding different perspectives, which is something that may not be evident from my academic scores alone.

My journey in the military has taught me the importance of discipline, teamwork, and leadership. These values are fundamental to who I am as a person. Working in a structured environment for the past twelve years has shaped my character and given me a strong foundation for future success. I believe that an MBA from University of Leeds will help me build on these experiences and transition into a career in business management.

One aspect of my personality that stands out is my commitment to continuous improvement. I am always looking for ways to enhance my skills and knowledge. Whether it is through reading books, attending seminars, or engaging in discussions with colleagues, I dedicate myself to personal growth. This mindset has served me well throughout my military career and will undoubtedly benefit me in the business world.

Additionally, I have a strong interest in cross-cultural communication. Growing up in Japan and working in an international military environment has exposed me to diverse viewpoints and work styles. I have learned to adapt to different contexts and build relationships with people from various backgrounds. This ability to navigate cultural differences is valuable in today's globalized business landscape and something that I believe will make me a strong candidate for an MBA program.

I also want to mention that I am resilient. The military has tested my resolve on numerous occasions, and each challenge has made me stronger. Whether facing operational difficulties or personal setbacks, I have consistently demonstrated the ability to overcome obstacles and maintain focus on my objectives. This resilience will serve me well as I pursue further education and transition into a new career phase.

Finally, I consider myself to be a team player. In the military, success depends on every member of the team performing their role effectively. I have learned to collaborate, support my colleagues, and contribute to a positive team environment. These skills are essential in any business setting, and I am confident they will help me thrive in an MBA program alongside my peers.

In conclusion, while my GMAT score and undergraduate GPA may not reflect my full potential or dedication to learning, my twelve years of military experience have shaped me into a disciplined, resilient, and culturally aware individual. I am excited about the prospect of bringing these qualities to University of Leeds Business School and contributing to the classroom experience.

school_name: LUISS Business School Master in Management
prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
When I first walked into the Cairo offices of Heliopolis Properties, I was given a spreadsheet and told to "make it useful." The spreadsheet tracked 47 residential projects across Egypt, but nobody—not the VP of Operations, not the finance team, not even the founder—could extract anything meaningful from it. Three years later, I've transformed how our company thinks about real estate portfolio management, but more importantly, I've realized what I actually want to do next.

My role started as Associate Project Manager. I was supposed to monitor construction timelines and budgets. Instead, I became obsessed with why we couldn't predict which projects would hit their milestones. I taught myself SQL over a weekend, pulled data from our four different systems, and built a dashboard that showed project health in real time. Within six months, the Operations VP started scheduling his week around my reports. Within a year, I was presenting to our board.

By year two, I was managing a team of three analysts and overseeing a portfolio worth $180 million. I implemented a risk-scoring system that caught cost overruns before they spiraled—we avoided approximately $2.4 million in preventable expenses on one flagship project in New Cairo. I thought I'd found my place.

But then I hit a wall. I realized I could optimize processes until I was 60, but I'd never truly understand *why* real estate development matters—or doesn't. I couldn't articulate the difference between good strategy and good luck. When our founder asked me to present market analysis for potential expansion into the UAE, I fumbled. I had data. I didn't have frameworks.

More honestly: I'd been promoted based on hustle and technical competence, not strategic thinking. There's a ceiling to how much a talented spreadsheet person can contribute to real business decisions, and I was starting to bump against it.

That's when I knew I needed an MBA, but not just any program. I've watched plenty of colleagues do MBAs at distant universities while working full-time—they come back with certificates but no real transformation. I need something different: an intensive, cohort-based program in a city where I can actually live and study, with peers from different industries who'll challenge how I think.

LUISS appeals to me specifically because of your emphasis on general management fundamentals combined with Italian business culture. Real estate in Egypt has taught me operational excellence, but I've lived most of my professional life inside one industry. LUISS's broad curriculum—particularly courses in strategy and corporate finance—will force me to think beyond real estate. And your location in Rome matters. I want to be in Europe for two years, building perspective from outside my market.

I'm not applying to LUISS because I'm "passionate about business" or I want to "make an impact." I'm applying because I know exactly what I don't know, and I'm ready to fix it. I've spent three years getting very good at execution. I want to learn to see the board.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
When I left my position at a mid-sized Swedish food company last year, my manager asked if I was making a mistake. I had a stable role managing category strategy for our dairy division—decent salary, secure path forward. But I realized I was solving the same problem repeatedly without really understanding why the business worked the way it did.

That question stuck with me. Three years into my career, I could optimize a product line or negotiate with retailers, but I couldn't read a balance sheet with confidence. I couldn't model scenarios or understand what actually drove shareholder value. I was executing tactics without owning strategy.

So I took a step back. For the past eight months, I've been working as a business analyst at a consulting firm in Stockholm, taking on projects across different industries—retail, manufacturing, logistics. It's been humbling. I've realized how much context I was missing in my previous role. But it's also shown me what I need to learn properly.

I'm applying to Technion's MBA specifically because I want to study in an environment where innovation and practical problem-solving aren't separated. Coming from CPG, I'm used to industries where optimization is valued, but I've also seen how quickly companies fail when they don't understand structural change. Technion's focus on technology and entrepreneurship appeals to me because I think the future of consumer goods will be defined by companies that can build, not just market.

I should be honest about my profile: my GMAT score is solid but not exceptional, and I know my work experience is relatively short for executive programs. But I've changed roles deliberately, not randomly. My three years at the food company taught me what I don't want to be—a functional expert who never sees the bigger picture. My current work has shown me what I need to learn.

I'm not sure I'll start a company after the MBA. I might go into corporate strategy or private equity. But I know I need the analytical foundation and the network to think strategically about complex businesses. That's what I'm looking for in an MBA, and that's why I'm interested in studying at Technion.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
I spent three years telling people what was wrong with their financial statements before I learned how to help them fix it. That shift in perspective, which happened almost accidentally, became the foundation of how I lead today.

In 2016, I was a senior auditor at one of the Big Four firms in Mumbai, managing a team of six junior auditors. I was good at my job—thorough, detail-oriented, and generally respected. But I was also exhausted. I'd spend weeks on client sites, document every compliance failure we found, write damning audit reports, and then... leave. The client would acknowledge the issues, promise to remediate, and we'd see the same problems again next year. I felt like I was running on a treadmill, effective but stalled.

Everything changed when I was assigned to manage the audit for a mid-sized construction company facing serious cash flow problems. Our initial findings were brutal: poor receivables management, inconsistent revenue recognition, basically a financial mess. My standard approach would have been to flag everything, file the report, and move on.

But the CFO of that company, a woman named Priya who was only five years older than me, asked me something that caught me off guard. After reviewing our preliminary findings, she said, "You clearly see what we're doing wrong. But do you actually understand why we're doing it?" 

I didn't. I'd identified the problems but had zero insight into the operational constraints, the client relationship dynamics, or the resource limitations that created those problems in the first place. I realized I'd been operating as a critic, not a partner.

Over the next few weeks, instead of just documenting issues, I started asking questions. Why was revenue recognized that way? Because the sales team used that method and Finance didn't have time to validate every transaction. Why were receivables so high? Because the company's largest client had been delaying payments, and the operations team didn't want to jeopardize the relationship. These weren't failures of intent—they were failures of communication and process design.

So I did something I'd never done in my auditing career: I spent time redesigning their processes with them. I didn't just point out that their spreadsheet-based receivables tracking was broken. I helped Priya's team implement a simple system, trained their staff, and created a monthly reconciliation protocol. I rewrote their revenue recognition policy so it actually matched their business model instead of just matching the textbook.

When I look back, what strikes me is how much more value I created by shifting from auditor to advisor. The audit was technically the same—we still identified all the issues. But because we'd solved the problems together, the remediation actually stuck. Two years later, the same company was still using those systems.

That experience showed me something about myself: I'm energized by helping people get better, not by being the smartest person in the room. It made me realize that the best leaders don't hoard expertise—they build capacity in others. Now, when I manage teams, I'm looking for ways to develop people, not just to check boxes.

At my current role in accounting advisory, I've built a team structure that reflects this. We pair junior staff with clients on process improvement projects, not just compliance work. It's slower initially, but the retention rates are better, client satisfaction is higher, and people actually learn something they can carry forward.

For an Executive MBA at CUHK, I'm looking to deepen this philosophy with a global lens. Accounting standards differ across Asia. Client needs vary by market. I want to understand how to lead across these complexities—how to build teams and cultures that adapt locally but stay grounded in solid principles. CUHK's emphasis on Asian business dynamics and your cohort diversity seem designed exactly for someone trying to figure out how to lead beyond the textbook.

prompt: What are your short-term and long-term professional goals? How will our MBA program help you achieve them?
content:
# Short-Term and Long-Term Goals Essay

Over the past ten years, I've built my career in supply chain management, progressing from a logistics coordinator at a mid-sized distributor in Riyadh to operations manager at a regional 3PL firm. I've optimized warehouse layouts, reduced shipping costs by 18%, and managed teams of 15-20 people. But somewhere around year eight, I realized I was becoming very good at executing someone else's strategy—and I wanted to build my own.

My short-term goal, over the next three to four years, is to transition into supply chain consulting or business development. I want to work on strategic projects: evaluating market entry opportunities, designing supply networks for expansion, helping companies think about digitization. The work I do now is tactical—solving immediate problems. I want to work on problems that matter to the company's direction. I see myself at a firm like McKinsey or a specialized logistics consultancy, doing engagements across different geographies and industries.

Long-term, in seven to ten years, I want to lead supply chain transformation at a regional company—either through consulting experience or by joining a logistics operator or manufacturing firm in a VP-level role. I'm specifically interested in the Middle East and Southeast Asia. There's massive inefficiency in how goods move through these regions, and I think there's real opportunity to modernize supply networks while creating jobs and improving margins. I might eventually start something of my own, but I'm not there yet. I need to learn how to think bigger than I currently do.

The MBA is the obvious credential gap. I have the operational experience, but consulting firms and serious business development roles expect an MBA. Beyond that, I need exposure to finance, strategy, and how to think about problems at a portfolio level rather than single-function level. I also need a network. In logistics, most people I know are people I've worked with directly. That's fine for operational roles, but building strategic relationships in consulting and business development is different.

I'm considering Universidad de los Andes specifically because of its regional focus and the strength of its corporate network in Latin America and Spain. I understand MiM programs are designed for people like me—experienced professionals looking to shift career direction, not people coming straight from undergraduate. The program's emphasis on general management and international strategy appeals to me more than a traditional MBA program that tries to be everything to everyone.

What I need from an MBA program is: strong strategy and finance coursework; exposure to people running companies or consulting firms; time to understand business models beyond supply chain; and classmates from different functions and geographies. I'm aware the program is one year, which is actually appealing—I've been out of structured learning for a while, but I don't want to pause my career for two years. An intensive program pushes you to focus on what matters.

There are honestly things I'm uncertain about. I don't know if I'll prefer consulting or corporate strategy once I experience both. I don't know if my career will eventually lead to entrepreneurship or stay in operations. But I do know I can't make an informed decision from where I sit now. I know I need to close the MBA box, partly for credibility and partly because I genuinely have gaps in my understanding of finance and organizational strategy.

I'm coming to business school relatively late in my career, with clear operational expertise but narrow exposure. That's a strength in some ways—I know my baseline competence—and a weakness in others. An MBA is the vehicle to expand that baseline. Universidad de los Andes seems like the right fit: serious academics, global perspective, a cohort of people reinventing themselves mid-career.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
When I joined Al-Noor Foundation in Dubai six years ago, we had a team of four people running youth literacy programs across three neighborhoods. Today, we reach 2,400 students annually. I'm proud of helping build that, but the achievement I'm actually most proud of happened in year three, and it taught me something uncomfortable about myself.

We launched a scholarship program in 2019 to support high-achieving students from low-income families. On paper, it was straightforward: identify talented kids, fund their secondary education, track outcomes. I designed the selection criteria, managed the budget of 1.2 million AED, and oversaw a small team. The first cohort of 45 students did well. Most completed their programs. We reported strong retention rates to our donors.

Then we did a proper impact evaluation in year two.

The data showed something I'd missed: our selection process had inadvertently filtered for students whose parents could already support supplementary tutoring. Kids without that safety net—the ones who actually needed our help most—weren't making it through. We were funding promising students, not lifting up struggling ones. The program was comfortable, and it was failing the people we claimed to serve.

I felt defensive at first. The numbers looked good. The donor reports were positive. But one of our program officers, Mariam, pushed back during a team meeting. She'd noticed patterns I'd rationalized away: students dropping out because they were working evenings, families moving to cheaper neighborhoods mid-term, cultural expectations around girls' education that our one-size criteria couldn't address.

I had to admit I'd optimized the wrong variable. I'd been solving for "scalable selection" rather than "actual support." That's a meaningful difference, and I'd made it because the first option was easier to manage and report on.

We redesigned everything that year. We extended the program to eighteen months with embedded tutoring. We hired two mentors who came from similar backgrounds as our students. We built flexibility into the enrollment process—if a family moved, the student could continue remotely. The cohort started with fewer students (32 instead of 45) and more intensive investment per person. Our donor conversations became harder because the metrics looked smaller.

But the real outcome changed. Completion rates actually improved. More importantly, we started identifying students we'd previously filtered out—students with real potential but real constraints. One student, Fatima, had been working at a supermarket during our original selection period. Under the old system, she would have been rejected for inconsistent exam attendance. She's now in her second year of a business degree and works part-time with our foundation.

What made me proud wasn't the program I designed. It was the willingness to dismantle something I'd built because it wasn't working. That required admitting I'd been wrong about what "success" meant.

That's the achievement that actually changed how I think about leadership. It's made me more careful about metrics, more skeptical of my own assumptions, and more honest about where my blind spots might be. As I move toward an MBA, I know I'll have to unlearn some things again. This experience has made me okay with that.

school_name: Montpellier Business School Master in Management
prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
# What My Resume Doesn't Say

My GMAT score was 678. I took it three times. The first two attempts, I convinced myself that test-taking anxiety was the culprit—that the real me was somewhere above that number, waiting to be unleashed by a quieter testing center or better sleep. By attempt three, I'd stopped making excuses. I sat with the reality that standardized testing simply isn't my strength, and honestly, that's revealed something more useful about myself than any perfect score could.

I've spent eight years in private equity, most recently as a senior associate at Amreya Capital in Riyadh. On paper, I've completed twelve deals worth roughly $280 million in aggregate. I've built models, pitched investors, negotiated with bank syndicates. I'm competent at this work. But what the deal list doesn't capture is that I've spent the last two years increasingly uncomfortable with how I operate.

In 2022, we were managing a portfolio company—a retail group with about 800 employees across the Gulf. My team and I had identified a target for acquisition that would consolidate three smaller competitors. The financial thesis was sound. We'd modeled 35% EBITDA growth within three years through operational synergies and store rationalization. That last part is where I started noticing something: I was personally disconnected from what rationalization meant. It meant closures. It meant people—shopkeepers I'd met during diligence, store managers I'd interviewed—losing jobs.

I didn't pause the process. I didn't even really voice the discomfort internally. Instead, I compartmentalized it, moved forward, and justified it the way I'd been taught: this is how value creation works. The deal closed. The operational improvements happened. The economics worked exactly as planned.

But it bothered me for months afterward in a way that surprised me. Not guilt exactly, but a recognition that I'd made a decision that affected real lives while treating it as a math problem. The discomfort didn't change what happened, but it made me realize I didn't want to keep operating that way.

That's when I started questioning whether PE was my long-term path, and whether I even understood what I actually wanted from my career beyond competence and compensation. I began reading outside my field—organizational psychology, social enterprise models, impact investing frameworks. I attended a conference on responsible business practices in Dubai. I had coffees with people building businesses focused on problems rather than just returns.

This is what pushed me toward an MBA now, at this particular moment in my career. I have enough operational experience to contribute meaningfully in a cohort. What I need is permission and space to fundamentally reconsider what success looks like for me—and the frameworks to build a career where financial performance and genuine social responsibility aren't competing priorities.

An MBA isn't going to erase the deals I've done. But it might help me do the next ones differently.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
When I took over as program director at Seoul Learning Academy three years ago, our online platform was hemorrhaging students. We'd launched it two years prior with the assumption that if we simply moved our in-person curriculum online, students would enroll. They didn't. By mid-2021, we had maybe 200 active users across 15 courses, and our investor was asking hard questions about ROI.

The obvious solution was to hire a consultant or rebuild the platform from scratch. But we didn't have the budget for either. So I did something unconventional: I spent six weeks sitting in on live classes, but not as an administrator. I attended as a student. I took notes. I complained about the interface. I experienced the friction firsthand.

What I discovered surprised me. The problem wasn't the platform's technical features—most students said the video quality and course materials were fine. The real problem was loneliness. Students were isolated. They'd watch a lecture, complete assignments in a vacuum, and see grades pop up without any human feedback. For younger learners especially, the asynchronous model felt cold and disconnected.

So I proposed something that would normally seem to add cost rather than cut it: I built a peer mentorship system that paired advanced students with newer ones, trained them with a simple framework, and gave them 30 minutes of structured time each week to meet via video. No elaborate technology—just Google Meet scheduled in advance. I also pushed our instructors to record brief, personalized video feedback on assignments instead of just leaving written comments. This took them maybe two extra minutes per assignment.

Here's what happened: within four months, student retention improved from 34% to 71%. Enrollment started growing again. By the end of year two, we'd reached 2,400 active users. The cost was minimal because I reallocated existing instructor hours and didn't need to build any new infrastructure.

The solution worked because it was rooted in the actual problem rather than the assumed one. I think many organizations jump to high-tech, expensive fixes when the real issue is something more human. In education, especially, we sometimes forget that learning is fundamentally a social act.

This experience shifted how I approach my work. I've since applied similar thinking to other challenges—like when our corporate training division struggled with low completion rates, and we found it wasn't the content but the lack of accountability structures. We added monthly cohort reviews with peer sharing, and completion jumped from 52% to 84%.

I'm applying to KAIST's Executive MBA because Korea's education sector is at an inflection point. Digital transformation is accelerating, but many institutions are still thinking about it the wrong way. They're investing in learning management systems when they should be thinking about learning ecosystems. I want to develop a deeper strategic framework for how traditional education organizations can innovate without losing what makes education meaningful—the human connection.

I know my profile isn't the strongest numerically. But I've learned that solving real problems requires more than optimization; it requires curiosity and humility about what you don't know. I want to bring that perspective to KAIST, learn from faculty and classmates who are pushing education forward, and return to build solutions that actually stick.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
When I walk past the production floor at our manufacturing facility in Jurong, I still think about the afternoon in 2016 when I nearly shut it down.

We were bleeding money. Our plant was running at 62% efficiency while competitors operated at 85%. I'd been operations manager for three years, and honestly, I'd become comfortable. We had steady orders, reliable customers, and a team that showed up. The problem was precisely that nothing forced us to change. Then our largest customer—representing 35% of our revenue—gave us twelve months to improve or they'd move production to Vietnam.

That ultimatum terrified and liberated me in equal measure.

I could have blamed the aging equipment, the rigid union contracts, or the lack of capital investment. All of those were real obstacles. Instead, I chose to own the problem completely. I spent two weeks on the production floor—not visiting, but actually working shifts. I operated the CNC machines, stacked pallets, watched bottlenecks happen in real time. I asked people questions that probably made me sound naive: "Why do we do it this way?" Most times, the honest answer was, "Because that's how we've always done it."

What I discovered was that our efficiency problem wasn't technological; it was cultural. Our team had stopped believing improvements were possible.

I launched what I called the "Floor-Up Initiative." Rather than imposing top-down changes, I formed small working groups with frontline operators, supervisors, and maintenance staff. I committed to implementing one validated suggestion per month—no bureaucracy, no justification forms. I learned to listen more than I spoke. A machine operator named Rajesh suggested we reorder our raw materials staging area. It saved forty minutes per shift. A supervisor named Maria identified a preventative maintenance schedule that reduced downtime by 18%. These weren't revolutionary changes individually, but cumulatively they were.

Within eight months, we hit 81% efficiency. Within sixteen months, we exceeded 87%. We kept the customer. We actually won two additional contracts.

But here's what I'm most proud of: the culture shift was real. People started proposing ideas without waiting to be invited. We went from 3 suggestions per year to 34 in year two. The manufacturing floor became a place where people felt their observations mattered. Turnover dropped from 28% to 11%. Somewhere in that process, people stopped showing up and started caring.

The personal lesson was harder to absorb than the operational metrics. I learned that my role wasn't to be the smartest person in the room—it was to create conditions where smart things could happen. I learned that being a good leader sometimes means admitting uncertainty and asking for help. I also learned humility. I'd spent years thinking efficiency was about optimization. It was actually about trust.

That experience redirected my entire career. I was promoted to plant manager, then operations director for our Southeast Asia division. But more importantly, it changed how I approach problems. I'm no longer interested in incremental improvements or playing it safe. I want to work at a scale where decisions affect hundreds of people and millions in value.

This is precisely why Sasin's Executive MBA appeals to me. I'm at a stage where I need to sharpen my strategic thinking, understand financial decision-making at the board level, and connect with peers who've navigated similar transitions in their careers. I've hit the ceiling of what operational excellence alone can teach me. I need broader business acumen.

The Jurong plant experience showed me I can drive change. Now I need to understand how to drive it across organizations.

prompt: What qualities would you look for in an MBA classmate when working on a team project and describe how your background and leadership skills will enhance the experience of others?
content:
# Team Dynamics and Leadership in Business

When I think about the ideal MBA classmate, several qualities come to mind. First and foremost, I believe someone who is collaborative and willing to listen to different perspectives is essential. In a team setting, it's important that everyone feels heard and valued. Additionally, I look for people who are hard-working, reliable, and committed to excellence. These are the kinds of people who will push the team forward and help us achieve our goals.

In my current role at an investment banking firm, I work with teams on a daily basis. We analyze financial data, prepare presentations, and work with clients to structure deals. Through this experience, I have learned that strong communication is the foundation of any successful team. When team members can articulate their ideas clearly and listen to feedback, projects run more smoothly and the outcomes are better. I have also learned that diversity of thought leads to better problem-solving, which is why I value working with people from different backgrounds and with different skill sets.

As for my background, I come from Colombia and I am the first in my family to pursue an MBA. This gives me a unique perspective that I believe I can bring to classroom discussions and group projects. I have experienced different cultures and ways of thinking, and I believe this helps me be more open-minded when working with others. Additionally, my experience in investment banking has given me technical skills and an understanding of financial markets that could be valuable in case studies and business simulations.

In terms of leadership, I would describe myself as someone who naturally takes initiative. When I see something that needs to be done, I step up and do it. At my current firm, I have taken on responsibilities beyond my job description, such as mentoring junior analysts and organizing team meetings. I believe that leadership is not just about being in charge—it's about serving your team and helping them succeed. When working on MBA projects, I would try to create an environment where everyone feels comfortable contributing and where we can all learn from each other.

I also believe it's important to be adaptable and flexible. In investment banking, situations change quickly and you have to be able to pivot and adjust your strategy. This adaptability is something I think would be valuable in an MBA program where we may face unexpected challenges or changing circumstances in our group work.

Overall, I am looking for classmates who are passionate about learning and who want to work hard to achieve excellence. I would bring my technical knowledge, my willingness to listen and adapt, and my commitment to making sure everyone on the team feels valued and heard. I think that together, we could create an amazing learning community at Desautels and push each other to achieve great things.

school_name: Asian Institute of Management Executive MBA
prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
In five years, I see myself leading a strategy team at a top African consulting firm, driving digital transformation for Fortune 500 companies across East Africa. I am passionate about making a difference in my region, and I believe the AIM Executive MBA is the perfect platform to accelerate my journey.

My career began at McKinsey, where I worked on supply chain optimization for a major retailer. This experience showed me the power of data-driven decision making and the importance of strategic thinking. I've realized that to truly impact African businesses, I need a deeper understanding of business fundamentals, particularly finance and operations management. The AIM curriculum addresses these gaps perfectly.

What excites me about AIM is the focus on Asian business contexts, which I believe will be invaluable as African markets become increasingly interconnected with Asian markets. The emphasis on entrepreneurship and innovation aligns with my personal interest in eventually starting my own consulting boutique. I am also drawn to the diversity of the cohort—learning alongside professionals from across Asia will broaden my perspective and expand my network significantly.

Over the next five years, I want to transition from pure consulting into a leadership role where I can shape strategy for an organization. The Executive MBA format appeals to me because I can continue working while studying, allowing me to apply classroom learnings immediately. I'm particularly interested in the courses on Financial Analysis and Strategic Management, as these will strengthen my ability to evaluate business opportunities and make sound investments.

Beyond the classroom, I'm excited about the experiential learning opportunities. The international immersion component resonates with me deeply—I want to understand how businesses operate in different cultural and economic contexts across Asia. This will prepare me to advise African companies on expanding into Asian markets.

My five-year vision is clear: lead a team of 15-20 consultants, work on transformation projects with significant revenue impact (ideally $50M+), and build a reputation as a trusted advisor in the East African consulting space. AIM's program will equip me with the technical knowledge, strategic perspective, and professional network necessary to achieve this vision and contribute meaningfully to African economic development.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
# Essay: My Interest in AUD and the Value I'll Bring

When I first looked into American University in Dubai's MBA program, I was curious about how a well-established American institution had built such a strong presence in the UAE. I spent time reviewing the program structure, faculty backgrounds, and student testimonials on your website. I also reached out to two alumni on LinkedIn—both working in education and real estate respectively—who gave me valuable perspective on the curriculum and the networking opportunities available.

What struck me most was how the program balances Western business education with practical exposure to the Middle Eastern market. As someone who has worked in international education for eleven years, primarily managing curriculum development and faculty coordination across four different institutions in Southeast Asia, I understand how crucial it is to have that global lens while maintaining local relevance.

I've spent the last decade building educational programs, but I recognize I've stayed somewhat within my comfort zone. I've managed budgets, led small teams, and created partnerships with schools across the Philippines, Vietnam, and Thailand. However, I've rarely stepped back to understand the business strategy underlying these decisions. I attended one of your webinars on emerging markets in the Gulf region, and the discussion about education sector growth in the UAE genuinely changed how I'm thinking about my career. I realized I want to move beyond program management into strategic roles where I can understand market dynamics and make bigger decisions about expansion and investment.

Being part of the AUD community would help me fill these gaps. The faculty diversity—professors with experience in both American and Gulf markets—appeals to me because I need mentors who've navigated the exact intersection I'm trying to enter. Your emphasis on experiential learning through case competitions and consulting projects aligns with how I actually learn best. When I've been most effective at work, it's because I was solving real problems, not sitting through lectures.

I'm also drawn to the student body composition. Having classmates from across Asia, the Middle East, and Africa means I won't just be learning business theory—I'll be learning how different markets operate from people living in them. My background in Southeast Asian education can contribute there, and I want to exchange ideas with people building their careers in markets I don't know well yet.

Beyond the classroom, I'd like to contribute to AUD's education-focused initiatives. I noticed your school supports youth entrepreneurship programs in the emirate. I've mentored young professionals in my current role, and I'd be interested in bringing that to your community, particularly in helping students from non-business backgrounds feel confident pursuing business careers.

Honestly, I'm not sure I'm a perfect MBA candidate on paper. But I know I'm serious about growth, and I know that AUD's specific environment—with its American rigor and Middle Eastern context—is where I need to be to reach the next level of my career.

school_name: Fudan University School of Management (Master in Management)
prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# Challenging the Billing Box

Three years into my role at Grant Thornton's Toronto office, I was drowning in spreadsheets—and I knew something had to give.

I worked in the audit practice, managing engagements for mid-market manufacturing clients. The standard process was predictable: we'd arrive on-site in January, spend two weeks in client offices, manually extract financial data into our templates, and send preliminary findings by March. Efficient enough, maybe. But it was also rigid, labor-intensive, and honestly felt like we were solving 2024 problems with 2014 tools.

The breaking point came during a $40 million revenue client audit. Their accounting team was still using Excel as their GL system—everything was being manually reconciled. We identified a significant revenue timing issue, but our standard sampling approach meant we'd only tested 5% of transactions. I spent an entire weekend digging through backup files to find the root cause, and it hit me: we were being reactive auditors when we could be strategic consultants. We had access to their data. We had the technical skills. We were just... choosing not to use them.

I pitched my manager an unconventional idea: what if we automated our preliminary data extraction using Python scripts and deployed our analysis remotely before arriving on-site? We'd still conduct fieldwork, but we'd be walking in with preliminary findings rather than blank notebooks. I wasn't a programmer—I'd taken a coding elective in my undergrad commerce degree and hadn't touched it since—so this felt genuinely risky. My manager was skeptical. "That's IT's job," he said. "We're auditors."

I pushed back. Not aggressively—just persistently. I showed him a rough prototype I'd built over a weekend, connecting to the client's accounting software and flagging unusual patterns. He agreed to a pilot: we'd test it on two smaller clients.

The first one was messy. The script broke because their data format was different than I expected. The second client's IT team was paranoid about security and delayed my access for two weeks. But by the third implementation, something shifted. When we arrived for fieldwork, the client was genuinely impressed that we'd already mapped their journal entry patterns. We caught an $80,000 misstatement on day two that would have taken us a week to find under the old method.

I realized the resistance wasn't really about capability—it was about mindset. My team had been trained to follow a process, and the process had never required them to question whether it was the best way. Once we had proof of concept, others started asking to use the tool.

By my third year, I'd refined the approach and documented it as a replicable methodology. The Toronto office was using it on 12 clients. It didn't revolutionize the firm, but it did change how my team thought about technology and client service.

What interests me about the Master in Management at Fudan is exactly this tension—between respecting foundational disciplines and questioning whether current practices serve current realities. I know China's business environment moves faster than North America's, and I'm drawn to being in a place where challenging the status quo is expected, not unusual.

I'm not a brilliant coder or a visionary entrepreneur. But I've learned that innovation doesn't require genius—it requires a willingness to feel incompetent for a while, and the persistence to improve something even when people tell you to stay in your lane. I want to develop that further, and I believe studying at Fudan—where I'll be learning from people building businesses in the fastest-growing market in the world—is where I'll actually learn to think bigger.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
When I think about leadership, I think about my role at my family's business in Dubai. I have been working here for five years, and during this time, I have learned many valuable lessons about what it takes to be a leader.

One situation that really defined my leadership journey was when our team was struggling with productivity. We had about thirty employees, and morale was low. I realized that as a leader, I needed to do something to improve the situation. I decided to implement new processes and strategies to help our team work better together.

I called a meeting with the department heads and explained my vision for the company. I told them that we needed to be brave and try new things. I also explained that leadership means inspiring people to believe in themselves and their work. I gave them examples of successful companies that had transformed their organizations by focusing on people first.

After this meeting, I introduced several initiatives. We started having weekly check-ins with employees to understand their concerns and challenges. We also created a mentorship program where senior staff could work with junior employees. Additionally, I worked to improve communication across departments so that everyone felt heard and valued.

The results were positive. Within three months, our productivity increased by 20% and employee satisfaction scores went up. People were more engaged and motivated to come to work. This experience taught me that true leadership is not just about making decisions from the top down, but about listening to your team and helping them grow.

This is why I want to attend Kellogg and pursue an MBA. I know that an MBA will give me the tools and knowledge to become an even better leader. I want to learn more about business strategy, finance, and how to manage larger organizations. I believe that Kellogg's focus on educating and inspiring brave leaders aligns perfectly with my goals and values.

Leadership is a journey, not a destination. I am committed to continuing to grow as a leader, and I believe that an MBA from Kellogg will help me achieve this goal. I am excited about the opportunity to learn from professors and classmates who share my passion for leadership and business excellence.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
When I started in medical devices eight years ago at a Lima-based distributor, I thought I understood the market. I was wrong. My first major project—launching a new surgical robotics line—failed because I approached it like an engineer, not a business leader. I focused on technical specifications while ignoring hospital procurement timelines, reimbursement structures, and the actual decision-makers in the OR. We lost the contract to a competitor who understood the ecosystem.

That failure taught me something crucial: to lead in this industry, I need to think beyond products. I need to understand how healthcare systems work across markets, how to build sustainable business models in emerging economies, and how to navigate the regulatory and financial complexities that determine success.

Over the next seven years, I've rebuilt my approach. I moved from distributor operations to direct sales leadership at a multinational device company, managing a team of twelve across four countries. I've learned to translate between engineers and hospital administrators, and I've started to see patterns in what works. But I've also realized the ceiling of what I can accomplish without formal business training. I can execute strategy, but I struggle to architect it. I understand China is the fastest-growing medical device market globally, yet I've never worked there—I've only observed from the periphery.

Tsinghua SEM is the obvious inflection point. Not because I want an MBA on my resume, but because China's healthcare system is where the next decade of innovation will happen, and I want to lead there. Your program's strength in understanding how Chinese enterprises operate, combined with exposure to global business frameworks, is precisely what I'm missing. The health economics course appeals to me specifically—understanding reimbursement policy has become my obsession after losing that robotics deal. Your network in Chinese hospitals and device manufacturers would let me translate my seven years of experience into something applicable here.

I don't expect the MBA to fix what I got wrong eight years ago. But it will let me move from being someone who understands problems to someone who can actually shape solutions in the market that matters most. I'm ready to stop reacting to the industry and start leading it.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
Dear Admissions Committee,

I am writing to express my strong interest in the Executive MBA program at Imperial College Business School. With nine years of progressive experience in the pharmaceutical and biotech sectors, I believe I am an ideal candidate for your program.

Throughout my career, I have developed a deep passion for the life sciences industry. Working in roles ranging from research to commercial operations, I have gained comprehensive knowledge of how pharmaceutical companies operate. I am excited about the opportunity to deepen my understanding of business fundamentals through formal education at a world-class institution like Imperial College.

My experience has taught me the importance of leadership and strategic thinking. In my current role, I manage cross-functional teams and contribute to key business decisions. I have seen firsthand how strong business acumen can drive innovation and growth in the biotech space. The Executive MBA program will equip me with the tools and frameworks needed to accelerate my career trajectory and make a greater impact in the industry.

I have always believed in continuous learning and professional development. An MBA from Imperial College will provide me with access to cutting-edge curriculum, renowned faculty, and a diverse network of professionals from around the world. These elements are crucial for anyone looking to advance in today's competitive business environment.

Furthermore, I am drawn to Imperial College's reputation for academic excellence and innovation. The program's strong focus on sustainability and responsible business practices aligns well with my values. I am particularly interested in how businesses can leverage technology to create positive change, not only in pharmaceuticals but across industries.

Beyond academics, I am confident that I will be an engaged member of the Imperial College community. I am eager to contribute my industry insights to classroom discussions and collaborate with classmates from diverse backgrounds. The networking opportunities alone would be invaluable for my professional growth.

In conclusion, I possess the work experience, motivation, and intellectual curiosity necessary to succeed in your program. I am confident that Imperial College's Executive MBA will serve as a catalyst for my career advancement. I look forward to the opportunity to discuss how I can contribute to your cohort and how your program can help me achieve my long-term goals.

Sindays Regards,

[Name]

prompt: Why do you intend on pursuing an MBA at this point in your life? Describe your mid-term and long-term visions for your post-MBA career path and how will the Desautels MBA program add value to your future career?
content:
# Why MBA at Desautels

Throughout my four years in government service, I have come to realize that I need to develop my business acumen to progress in my career. An MBA will provide me with the foundational knowledge and skills necessary to transition into a leadership role, and the Desautels MBA program seems like an excellent fit for my aspirations.

My career began in the Ministry of Defence, where I worked in various administrative and operational capacities. During this time, I discovered that I was genuinely interested in how organizations function and make decisions. I noticed that many of my senior colleagues had MBAs, and they seemed to have a deeper understanding of strategy and finance. This realization prompted me to consider pursuing an MBA myself.

In my mid-term vision, I see myself moving into a senior management position within a government agency or a defense contractor. I would like to lead teams, manage budgets, and contribute to strategic planning. An MBA will equip me with critical thinking skills and a comprehensive understanding of business principles that will help me transition from my current role into management. The specific courses in finance, strategy, and organizational behavior at Desautels will be particularly valuable.

For my long-term vision, I am interested in potentially moving into the private sector, perhaps working for a consulting firm or a large corporation. I believe that the business experience gained through an MBA will make me more competitive in the job market and open doors that would otherwise remain closed. Additionally, the connections I make at Desautels will be invaluable as I navigate this transition.

I have chosen Desautels for several reasons. The program is well-regarded and offers a strong curriculum that aligns with my professional interests. The location in Montreal is appealing, and I appreciate the program's focus on practical learning. I also understand that Desautels has a diverse student body, which I believe will enhance my educational experience through exposure to different perspectives and cultures.

Furthermore, I recognize that my background in government differs from many MBA applicants, and I believe this diversity will add value to the classroom discussions. My experience managing projects and working with stakeholders from various backgrounds has taught me collaboration and problem-solving skills that extend beyond typical business education.

The Desautels MBA will serve as a bridge between my current career and my future ambitions. It will validate my professional competencies, provide me with a structured framework for understanding business, and connect me with a network of professionals who share similar goals. I am confident that this program will be instrumental in achieving my career objectives and positioning myself for success in leadership roles.

I am ready to commit to the rigorous demands of the MBA program and believe that Desautels is the right institution to help me achieve my career aspirations.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
In five years, I want to be running operations for a mid-sized manufacturing business in Southern Africa, ideally one looking to expand internationally or transform its supply chain. I've spent a decade in my family's business—we distribute industrial equipment across South Africa and Botswana—and I've realized that staying comfortable here means becoming complacent. An MBA is the bridge between knowing my industry deeply and understanding how to scale it globally.

Right now, I'm the operations manager, which sounds straightforward until you consider that "operations" in a family business means everything: logistics, HR, vendor relations, financial planning, and somehow still answering phones when the receptionist is sick. I've optimized our warehouse processes and reduced procurement costs by about 18%, but I hit a wall around strategy. I can fix what's broken, but I struggle to see five years ahead. That's what I need to develop.

Bocconi appeals to me specifically because of its European network and focus on international business. My family business is regional, but Europe represents the next frontier—not as a market to enter, but as a place to understand best practices in supply chain and operations management. If I'm going to take our company global or help another business do the same, I need to understand how companies operate at that scale. The program's emphasis on financial management and operations, combined with access to alumni networks across Europe and Africa, directly addresses what I'm missing.

I'm also drawn to the diversity here. Growing up in a family business, my perspective has been somewhat narrow—I've learned from my parents and a small management team. A classroom with people from 80+ countries, studying operations and strategy in an international context, will challenge my assumptions about how business gets done.

In five years, I picture myself leading a larger operation, possibly for a different company, but applying what I learn here. Maybe that's a supply chain director role at a logistics company. Maybe I return to family business but with an international perspective. Either way, I want to move beyond firefighting into genuine strategic thinking. That's what Bocconi offers me—not just technical skills, but exposure to how serious businesses think and operate at scale.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
When I took over the data engineering team at Lattice (a Series B fintech startup in Toronto), we had a serious problem. The previous manager had run things like a dictatorship—smart people getting told what to do without being asked for input. Turnover was high. The team was burned out. And we were shipping bugs because people were afraid to speak up.

I inherited eight engineers, most of whom had already started looking elsewhere. My first instinct was to overcompensate, to be the "cool manager" who lets everyone do whatever they want. I thought that would fix things fast. It didn't. Without direction, the team became unfocused. Projects slipped. People got frustrated because they didn't know what success looked like.

That's when I realized something obvious but important: leadership isn't about being liked or feared. It's about creating the conditions where smart people can actually do their best work. That meant being clear about what mattered, asking for input on how to get there, and then trusting people to execute.

We started structured bi-weekly syncs where team members led discussions about their own technical decisions. I stopped saying "do this"—I started asking "what do you think?" I was transparent about the constraints we were working under (board pressure on timeline, limited hiring budget) so people understood the tradeoffs they were making. When someone had a better idea than mine, I said so publicly.

It took a few months to feel normal, but it worked. By month six, we'd shipped a major data pipeline redesign that the team had actually proposed. By month ten, three of the five people who'd been interviewing elsewhere decided to stay. We cut our bug rate by 40%. More importantly, when I asked "who wants to lead the Kafka migration project," four people volunteered instead of groaning.

What stuck with me is that none of this was rocket science. It was just treating people like adults with something to contribute. It's made me think differently about what a manager actually does—it's not about having all the answers. It's about creating enough clarity, safety, and trust that the team can figure things out together.

That's the manager I want to be going forward.

prompt: What qualities would you look for in an MBA classmate when working on a team project and describe how your background and leadership skills will enhance the experience of others?
content:
I would look for MBA classmates who are passionate about learning and willing to collaborate. In a team project, it's important to work with people who share your values and are committed to achieving the best results. My background in real estate has taught me that teamwork is essential for success.

Throughout my seven years working in the real estate sector, I have developed strong leadership abilities that would benefit my classmates. I started as an agent and worked my way up to managing a small team of five people. This experience has shown me the importance of communication, responsibility, and dedication. I believe these qualities would help me contribute meaningfully to group projects at Desautels.

In my current role, I am responsible for overseeing sales and ensuring that our office meets its targets. I work with different professionals including architects, contractors, and other agents. This has made me realize that diverse perspectives strengthen decision-making. When working on MBA projects, I would want teammates who bring different backgrounds and expertise to the table.

My leadership style is collaborative and inclusive. I prefer to listen to my team members' ideas before making decisions. I have found that this approach leads to better outcomes and stronger relationships. I think classmates would appreciate working with someone who values their input and respects their contributions.

I also have strong organizational skills. In real estate, managing multiple projects simultaneously is necessary. Each property has its own timeline, budget, and stakeholders. These responsibilities have trained me to be detail-oriented and efficient with time management. I could help my team stay on track and meet deadlines.

One challenge I faced was working with a difficult team member at my previous job. This person was resistant to new ideas and often created conflict. I handled this by having an honest conversation about our goals and finding common ground. This experience taught me how to navigate interpersonal challenges, which I know can happen in any team setting.

I am also someone who is curious and willing to admit when I don't know something. In MBA classes, there will be many topics new to me, particularly in finance and strategy. Rather than pretending to understand, I ask questions and learn from others. I believe this humility and openness would make me a good teammate who others enjoy working with.

The real estate industry has been changing rapidly with technology and new market dynamics. I have had to constantly adapt and learn new skills to stay competitive. This adaptability would serve me well in business school group work, where flexibility and willingness to change approaches are valuable.

In conclusion, I would look for classmates who are committed, reliable, and open-minded. I would contribute reliability, leadership experience, strong organizational abilities, and genuine interest in learning from my peers. I believe my background and work ethic would help create a positive and productive team environment at Desautels Executive MBA.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
I was twenty-six when I realized I had no idea how to lead.

It happened in a cramped office in Lima, where I managed a small team of five educators at an edtech startup. We were building adaptive learning modules for high school students in underserved communities—work I believed in deeply. But three months in, two of my best teachers quit, leaving me a resignation email and nothing else.

I'd been what I thought was a good leader: I set clear targets, tracked metrics obsessively, sent detailed feedback emails. What I didn't do was ask what my team actually needed.

When I finally sat down with the remaining three, the answer was uncomfortable. They felt like delivery mechanisms for my vision, not collaborators in it. One person told me I spent more time analyzing completion rates than understanding why a student struggled with logarithms. Another said I made decisions in a vacuum and announced them as gospel. I remember feeling defensive—wasn't that efficiency? But mostly, I felt ashamed.

That experience cracked something open. I spent the next month doing something radically different: I stopped planning. I showed up to classroom observations and just listened. I asked what made teaching feel meaningful to each person. I admitted when I didn't have answers. It felt inefficient and terrifying.

Slowly, things shifted. One educator, Carmen, who had been quiet in meetings, started proposing features for our modules. Another, Diego, began mentoring newer teachers without me asking. The team started staying late not because of deadlines, but because they were invested. We actually improved our student outcomes—not because I managed the metrics better, but because teachers were thinking like owners.

Over the next five years, I've carried that lesson into bigger roles. When I became Regional Education Director for twelve schools across three cities, I deliberately built a model around what I learned. I implemented monthly "reverse mentoring" sessions where teachers teach me—sometimes about pedagogy, sometimes about their lives. I made it standard that every major decision includes someone who disagrees with me. I publicly acknowledge mistakes.

This approach has concrete costs. It's slower. It requires real vulnerability. I've had to accept that the best ideas often come from people I supervise, which means my ego takes hits regularly.

But it's also generated measurable results: our teacher retention improved from 71% to 89% over three years. Student learning gains increased by 23%. And perhaps more importantly, I've built teams where people tell me hard truths instead of what they think I want to hear.

My management philosophy now centers on a simple idea: the people closest to the work usually see it most clearly. My job isn't to have all the answers—it's to create conditions where others feel safe sharing theirs, and brave enough to act on them.

I'm drawn to IIM Ahmedabad's PGPX program because the curriculum's emphasis on organizational behavior and human-centered leadership aligns with how I've learned to lead. I want to deepen my understanding of why systems work the way they do, and learn frameworks that help me scale this approach beyond education. I want to study alongside practitioners who care about more than optimization—who want their leadership to actually mean something.

school_name: University of Queensland Business School Executive MBA
prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
What matters most to me is the ability to build something that lasts—not just profitable, but sustainable in a way that actually improves how people work.

I've spent six years in retail and e-commerce, and I've seen firsthand how quickly things fall apart when you're chasing quarterly targets instead of thinking three years ahead. At my current company, a mid-sized online retailer in South Africa, I manage supply chain operations across the Southern African region. We hit €12 million in revenue last year, which sounds impressive until you realize we've burned through three operations managers in eighteen months because the systems were fundamentally broken. People were drowning, and instead of fixing the root problems, leadership just hired faster.

That frustrated me enough to actually do something about it. I spent eight months mapping our entire fulfillment process and identified about 40% of our delays were self-inflicted—warehouse layout issues, redundant approval steps, terrible communication between teams. I built a case for restructuring, got buy-in from the CFO, and implemented changes that cut our average delivery time from 4.2 days to 2.8 days without hiring additional staff. The team that had been miserable started shipping orders without the constant panic.

That's what stuck with me. It wasn't the efficiency gains, though those mattered. It was watching people actually feel competent again. One of my team leads told me afterwards that she'd been looking for a new job because she thought she was bad at her role. Turns out she was just trying to do an impossible process.

I want to spend my career doing more of this, but at a bigger scale. I need an MBA partly because I don't have formal training in strategic operations, and I can feel the ceiling of what I can accomplish with on-the-job learning alone. But honestly, I also want to understand the business side better—how do you balance operational improvements with financial realities? How do you push for sustainability when the market demands speed?

The UQBS Executive program appeals to me because I can stay working while studying, which means I can keep experimenting with these ideas in real time. I'm not looking for a career pivot. I'm looking for the frameworks to do my current work better, and to eventually lead others through the same kind of systemic thinking that's become second nature to me.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
Throughout my nine years working in technology, I have learned that creative thinking is essential for solving complex problems. At my current company, a large tech firm in the FAANG sector, I faced a challenge that required me to think differently about our approach to customer engagement.

Our team was responsible for managing customer data across multiple platforms. We had been using traditional methods for organizing and categorizing this information, but as our user base grew, we realized that our system was becoming increasingly inefficient. The problem was complex because it involved technical limitations, budget constraints, and the need to maintain data quality and security.

I recognized that we needed a creative solution. After analyzing the situation, I decided to propose a new approach that would leverage existing tools in innovative ways rather than requiring expensive new software. This was important because our budget was limited, and we needed to find a cost-effective solution.

I worked with my team to develop a system that combined several existing platforms in a way they had not been used before. We created workflows that automated certain processes that were previously done manually. This innovation saved time and reduced errors. The results were significant—we were able to process information 40% faster while also improving accuracy.

What made this solution creative was that we did not follow the conventional path of purchasing new technology. Instead, we thought about what resources we already had and how we could use them differently. This required collaboration with different departments, including IT and product management. Everyone had to understand the vision and work together to implement it.

The implementation took about three months. During this time, I learned a lot about change management and how to communicate new ideas to people who might be skeptical. Some team members were concerned about switching from the old system, so I had to explain the benefits clearly and address their concerns. This taught me that technical solutions are only part of the equation—people and communication are equally important.

Beyond the efficiency gains, this project showed me that I enjoy problem-solving and finding creative approaches to challenges. It also demonstrated the value of looking at problems from different angles and not accepting the first solution that comes to mind. I realized that sometimes the best answers are not the most obvious ones.

This experience has shaped how I approach my work. I now always ask myself: What assumptions am I making? What resources do I have that I might be using inefficiently? How can I think about this problem differently? These questions help me continue to find creative solutions.

However, I know that I still have much to learn. The business side of these solutions—understanding ROI, market positioning, and strategic implications—is an area where I want to develop further. I believe that an MBA program like SDA Bocconi would help me become a more well-rounded professional. The program's focus on both analytical and creative thinking appeals to me, as does the opportunity to study in Milan, a city known for innovation and design.

In conclusion, my experience solving this data management challenge has taught me the importance of creative thinking in technology. I am excited to bring this mindset to an MBA program where I can learn from experienced faculty and talented classmates. I am committed to using these new skills to make meaningful contributions in my career going forward.

prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
When I was promoted to Major in 2015, I was convinced that rank and experience were enough to lead effectively. I had risen through the ranks faster than most of my peers in the Turkish military, and I assumed my technical competence would translate automatically into team performance. I was wrong.

I was assigned to lead a procurement and logistics division of 47 people responsible for equipment distribution across three bases. The first month felt like a validation of everything I believed about myself. I reorganized the supply chain, implemented new tracking systems, and cut processing times by 18%. On paper, it looked like a success.

But morale was collapsing. Within six weeks, my best officer submitted a transfer request. Then another. I remember sitting across from Sergeant Aydin, who had been with the division for eight years, asking him why he wanted to leave. His answer was blunt: "You make the decisions. We just execute them."

That conversation broke something open in me. I had optimized processes without building a team. I had been so focused on the metrics—the 18% improvement, the cost savings—that I hadn't asked my people what they actually thought about the work or how we could solve problems together. I realized I had created an environment where people felt like instruments rather than contributors.

I spent the next month doing something uncomfortable: I admitted what I didn't know. I scheduled individual conversations with each team member and asked genuine questions instead of announcing solutions. What frustrated them? Where did they see inefficiencies I was missing? What did they need from their leader? 

It turned out my reorganization had created unexpected bottlenecks that only the people doing the work could see. A junior officer had a completely different approach to inventory management that was actually more efficient than my system. Another team member was struggling with how the new procedures affected his specific responsibilities, but had been too intimidated to tell me.

I reversed some changes. I invited suggestions and implemented several. More importantly, I changed how I operated—moving from directive to collaborative leadership. The same logistics division then went on to achieve a 34% improvement in processing times, not the 18% my individual "genius" had managed. We also reduced turnover to nearly zero. More tellingly, people seemed to actually enjoy coming to work.

The failure taught me something that no leadership seminar could have: expertise in one domain doesn't transfer to leadership across domains. Being smart about supply chains means nothing if you're dumb about how you treat the people managing them. And perhaps more humbling—the best ideas often don't come from the person with the highest rank.

I carried this lesson forward in every role since. When I later led a joint NATO planning committee with officers from five countries, my first instinct was to listen, not to lead with answers. When I transitioned into policy work at the Ministry of Defense, I built small teams that openly disagreed with each other, because I had learned that uniform agreement usually means someone isn't speaking up.

An MBA appeals to me precisely because leadership happens at the intersection of strategy and people. IE's emphasis on both business fundamentals and collaborative learning reflects how I've come to see effectiveness. I'm ready to bring this hard-won humility into a classroom where I can both contribute from ten years of experience and learn from peers with perspectives entirely different from my own.

school_name: Mannheim Business School Master in Management
prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
# Professional Setback and Recovery
