I believe that education is the key to unlocking potential. By obtaining an MBA, I will be better equipped to take on leadership roles and contribute more meaningfully to my organization and industry. The skills and knowledge I gain from Bradford's program will enable me to think strategically about business problems and develop innovative solutions.

My cultural background as a Kenyan has instilled in me values of resilience and community. These values, combined with my professional experience, position me well to contribute to the Bradford cohort. I am eager to learn from my classmates and to share my perspective with them.

Going forward, I am committed to leveraging the tools and frameworks I will learn at Bradford to drive growth and success in the media sector. I am confident that the MBA program will provide me with the competitive advantage I need to move into executive-level positions.

I am excited about the opportunity to join Bradford School of Management and to be part of a community of ambitious professionals. I am certain that the program will be transformative for my career and that I will make valuable contributions to the program itself.

Thank you for considering my application.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
Throughout my career in private equity, I have been driven by a passion for creating value and driving business growth. My experiences have shaped my understanding of investment strategy, financial analysis, and operational excellence. I believe that an MBA from Edinburgh Business School will provide me with the advanced knowledge and skills necessary to achieve my long-term career objectives.

As an Emirati woman working in the male-dominated field of private equity, I have overcome significant cultural and professional challenges. In the UAE market, women are underrepresented in finance, and I take pride in being one of the few women in my investment firm. This has motivated me to work harder and prove my capabilities through strong deal analysis and portfolio management.

Over the past ten years, I have been involved in various investments across different sectors. I have learned about due diligence, valuation, and post-acquisition integration. My financial acumen has grown substantially, and I believe I am well-positioned to take on more senior responsibilities. An MBA will help me develop the strategic thinking skills needed for senior management roles.

I am particularly interested in Edinburgh because of its strong reputation in business education and its location in Europe. Studying in the UK will expose me to different business practices and perspectives, which will be valuable for my career. The program's curriculum appears comprehensive and aligned with my professional goals.

In my current role, I have worked on multiple deals ranging from $10 million to $100 million in enterprise value. I have successfully led due diligence processes and contributed to investment committee presentations. My colleagues have recognized my analytical skills and dedication to detailed work. However, I recognize that I need to develop my soft skills, including leadership and communication, which the MBA program will address.

Additionally, I want to pursue an international career beyond the Middle East. The MBA will provide me with a global network and exposure to international business practices. I believe that studying at Edinburgh will open doors for opportunities in Europe and beyond.

I am also interested in the intersection of technology and traditional business, and I see opportunities for innovation in the private equity space. The program will help me explore how digital transformation is changing the investment landscape and how I can position myself in this evolving market.

Finally, I want to emphasize my commitment to my professional development. I have consistently sought opportunities to expand my knowledge, whether through courses or professional certifications. An MBA from Edinburgh Business School would be a significant milestone in my career journey.

In conclusion, I am confident that I will be a strong addition to your cohort, and I am excited about the prospect of studying at Edinburgh Business School. I believe the program will equip me with the skills and knowledge needed to advance my career in private equity and beyond.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
Eight years into my career at Unilever Pakistan, I've realized something uncomfortable: I've been optimizing for comfort instead of impact.

I manage the home care division across three provinces, overseeing a team of twelve and a portfolio generating roughly 2.3 billion PKR annually. On paper, it's solid. I've delivered consistent growth, launched two successful product lines, and earned promotions. But last year, when I was asked to mentor our supply chain director through a regional expansion, I hit a wall. I could see the problems—fragmented logistics, poor supplier relationships, missed margins—but I couldn't see the systems that created them. I was managing tactics, not strategy.

That's when I knew an MBA wasn't optional anymore; it was necessary.

My goal is to move into general management within a multinational CPG company operating across South Asia within four years. But more specifically, I want to lead the transformation side of businesses—the work of reimagining operations, supply chains, and market strategies when companies enter new regions or face disruption. I've watched too many strong brands fail in Pakistan because they imported Western playbooks without understanding local complexity. I want to be the person who prevents that.

What I need is what Makerere MiM offers in a way other programs don't: a truly regional perspective grounded in African business context.

CPG companies in Pakistan and East Africa face remarkably similar challenges—fragmented distribution networks, regulatory unpredictability, consumers with radically different purchasing power and media consumption than Western markets. Most MBA programs treat emerging markets as a module, a case study. They don't live there. Makerere does. Your faculty works with companies navigating these realities daily. Your alumni network spans the region I want to scale into. Your curriculum wasn't built for consulting case interview preparation; it was built for the actual problems your graduates will face.

The operations and supply chain courses will close my technical gaps—I need formal training in statistical process control and network optimization that my on-the-job experience hasn't provided. But equally important is learning to think like a general manager. I need strategy frameworks, financial modeling for emerging markets, and the ability to lead change through people. I need to understand how to read a balance sheet the way I currently read a market, intuitively.

I'm also drawn to your emphasis on entrepreneurship and innovation. I don't plan to stay in CPG forever. Midway through my career, I want to launch something—whether that's a brand, a supply chain service, or a company solving a specific market gap in South Asia. Your program's approach to new venture creation, combined with mentorship from practitioners who've actually built businesses in this region, directly serves that ambition.

I know what I'm good at. I can execute. What I don't yet know is how to architect. Makerere MiM will teach me that—not in theory, but in context.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# Challenging the Traditional Client Service Model

When I joined Deloitte's Moscow office eight years ago, the audit practice operated exactly as it had for decades. Senior managers reviewed junior auditor work, partners met with CFOs to discuss findings, and clients received a thick binder of observations six weeks after fieldwork ended. It was predictable, standardized, and completely disconnected from how clients actually made decisions.

I noticed this problem while managing an audit at a major Russian manufacturing company. Our team spent three weeks identifying that their accounts payable process had gaps—duplicate invoices weren't being caught, reconciliations were inconsistent. We documented everything meticulously and submitted our findings in the standard format: risk classification, current process description, recommended controls.

The CFO's response was polite silence. He didn't implement a single recommendation.

I asked to meet with him afterward, and he explained something that frustrated me: "Your report is accurate, but I have no idea how this actually affects my business. You found a control weakness. I need to know whether this costs me 50,000 rubles annually or 5 million. And I need to know it now, not in your formal report."

That conversation changed how I approached audits. I began experimenting with an analytics-driven approach. Instead of following our standard procedures, I worked with IT to pull transaction data and quantify the actual financial impact of control gaps. For that manufacturing client, I analyzed three years of vendor payments and identified that duplicate invoices cost them approximately 2.8 million rubles yearly—roughly 4% of their accounts payable volume. I presented this finding verbally, in a one-page summary, the day we finished fieldwork.

The CFO implemented the control within two weeks.

I saw an opportunity to systematize this approach. I proposed to my partner that we pilot a new service model in the Moscow office: real-time analytics reporting during audit fieldwork rather than traditional post-completion findings. The initial reaction was predictable resistance. Partners worried about audit quality, about whether junior staff could perform proper analysis, about whether clients would perceive this as incomplete work.

I built a simple case. I showed that three major clients had implemented recommendations from our standard audits at rates below 30%. When I'd used the analytics approach, implementation rates exceeded 80%. The financial impact was clear too—clients who implemented our recommendations engaged us for larger projects. One of my analytics reports led to a comprehensive operational controls project worth 400,000 euros.

Our Moscow office ran the pilot for six months. I trained a team of eight junior auditors in data analysis techniques and worked with our IT systems to automate data pulls for common audit areas. We delivered preliminary findings within two days of completing fieldwork for four pilot clients. All four implemented major recommendations. Deloitte's Moscow office expanded the model to 12 additional clients the following year, and it eventually became standard practice for our office.

Looking back, the real innovation wasn't particularly complex. It was simply recognizing that our service delivery method—the thing we'd done the same way for twenty years—didn't actually serve our clients' needs. It required questioning whether tradition was actually best practice. It required accepting that I, a mid-level manager, should challenge how senior partners structured client work. It required willingness to be wrong.

The model expanded because the results were undeniable. But it almost didn't happen because the conventional approach felt safe.

I'm drawn to an Executive MBA because I recognize this pattern repeating across every industry now. The accelerating pace of business means that "how we've always done it" becomes obsolete much faster than most organizations admit. I want to deepen my understanding of business strategy and organizational change so I can identify these patterns earlier and drive transformation more effectively, across different industries and contexts.

school_name: Bocconi University MSc in International Management
prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
Three years ago, I was a junior operations analyst at DHL's Southeast Asia hub in Singapore. I wasn't a manager—just someone on the planning team responsible for forecasting shipment volumes. But when our largest pharmaceutical client threatened to move their business to a competitor, I ended up leading the fix.

The problem was a classic disconnect: our sales team had promised the client 48-hour delivery guarantees on all shipments from Bangkok to Singapore, but our actual capability was closer to 72 hours. Sales hadn't consulted operations. No one asked me. I just saw the emails flagged red.

I knew complaining to my manager wouldn't fix it fast enough. The client was reviewing us in two weeks. So I started doing something I probably shouldn't have—I began pulling together data on my own time, mapping every bottleneck in the Bangkok-Singapore corridor. Temperature-controlled goods sat in queues. Documentation was duplicated. The warehouse management system wasn't talking to our transport routing software. I found maybe 16 hours of slack if we moved things around.

Here's where it got awkward. I needed buy-in from people three and four levels above me, plus people in different departments who owed me nothing. I didn't have authority. I had a PowerPoint and a problem.

What I learned was that authority without credibility is useless, but credibility without authority is actually powerful. I spent two days just listening—to the customs broker about his constraints, to the warehouse supervisor about why certain processes existed, to the transport manager about realistic turnaround times. Not to argue, but to understand. Then I rebuilt my proposal around what they actually needed, not what I thought they should do.

I presented it to the operations director, not as "here's what we need to fix," but as "here's what I found, and here's what I need from your team." I was explicit about the limits of what I could influence. He brought in the other managers. Some of their ideas were better than mine. We adjusted.

The client got their 48-hour guarantee. We shuffled some routing, upgraded one warehouse process, and added a transit coordinator in Bangkok. We didn't overhaul everything—we fixed what mattered.

What stuck with me was how much I'd actually underestimated people's willingness to help if you approached them right. They weren't obstacles protecting their turf. They had real constraints and actually useful knowledge. My job was to listen harder than I pushed.

That experience shaped how I think about operations now. I'm not the person with all the answers. But I can ask better questions, listen to what actually matters, and coordinate across silos without needing a title to do it. That feels like the core of what good operational leadership is—not authority, but influence built on credibility and respect.

prompt: Please use this space to share with the Admissions Committee how you have reflected and grown since your previous application and discuss any relevant updates to your candidacy (e.g., changes in your professional life, additional coursework, and extracurricular/volunteer engagements).
content:
When I submitted my previous application last year, I was confident that my manufacturing background and work ethic would speak for themselves. I've since realized that confidence without clarity is not enough.

Over the past twelve months, I've made significant changes to strengthen my candidacy. Most importantly, I've deepened my commitment to business education through online coursework. I completed two courses in financial accounting and business analytics through a reputable online platform, which has given me better tools to understand the quantitative side of business that I know I need to develop.

Professionally, I've taken on increased responsibilities at my current position. My team grew from 8 to 12 people, and I now oversee production scheduling across three facilities. This expanded scope has shown me that I want to pursue strategy and operations management more seriously, and I understand that Wharton's program can help me formalize these skills.

Additionally, I've become more involved in my community. I volunteer with a nonprofit that supports young people interested in engineering and manufacturing careers, which aligns with my passion for giving back and helping the next generation.

Reflecting on my previous application, I see that I didn't explain my goals clearly enough. I was too focused on listing accomplishments without connecting them to a cohesive vision for my career. This time, I want to be transparent: I'm applying because I need structured business training to move from operations management into a strategic consulting role, possibly in supply chain optimization.

I'm a stronger candidate now—not just because of coursework, but because I understand what I'm working toward and why Wharton specifically can help me get there.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I first moved to Hong Kong in 2018, I spent my first week lost. Not just directionally—though I definitely got turned around in Central more times than I care to admit—but existentially. I'd left my co-founding role at a B2B SaaS company in Medellín to chase something I couldn't quite name. Three years later, I know what it was: I needed to understand how to build something that actually worked across borders, not just in my corner of Latin America.

That's when CUHK caught my attention.

I'm applying to your program because Hong Kong is where I've finally stopped being the person who imported one successful model and hoped it would scale. Instead, I've learned to think like someone building for multiple markets simultaneously. Running partnerships across Southeast Asia for my current company—a logistics platform that recently hit $12M ARR—has shown me how differently markets work. A pricing strategy that works in Manila flops in Bangkok. Cultural assumptions I made about how teams should operate in Colombia meant nothing here. I've had to unlearn as much as I've learned.

That's not the origin story most people write about themselves. But it's honest. And I think your program understands the difference between someone who succeeded early and someone who's actually grown.

Here's what I know I need: I need to formalize the pattern recognition I've been building ad hoc. Right now, I'm good at sense-making—reading a market, understanding incentive structures, knowing when to push and when to wait. But I do this intuitively, and intuition doesn't scale past the people who already know me. I need the frameworks to articulate why something works, so I can teach it. I need to understand financial modeling well enough to model markets, not just spreadsheets. And I need peers who've built in different contexts, because my reference point is still too narrow.

Your program is specific for three reasons. First, the Asia-Pacific focus is non-negotiable for my next chapter. I'm not going back to Latin America yet—maybe never. The markets here have density, sophistication, and speed that fascinate me. I want to understand how that translates into building. Second, your emphasis on global teams and emerging market contexts speaks to how I actually work. I read that 40% of your students come from outside greater China, and that matters because the problems worth solving here aren't problems you solve alone. Third—and I'll be direct about this—your program has a reputation for not being afraid of students who don't follow the typical track. I co-founded a company instead of working up the banking ladder. I've never taken a traditional finance role. I'm non-binary in a region where that still reads as unusual. I need a place that sees that as a feature, not something to overcome.

What I'll bring: I'll bring someone who's comfortable with ambiguity, because I've lived in it for five years. I'll bring a perspective from the startup ecosystem that most of your finance-track students won't have—I understand burn rate and unit economics the way people understand oxygen, because we've lived and died by them. I'll bring specific knowledge about building in Southeast Asia, the challenges nobody talks about in case studies because they're too messy to generalize. And I'll bring genuine curiosity. I ask more questions than I answer in most conversations, which either annoys people or intrigues them. In a classroom, I think it builds better discussions.

I'm at a point where I need to step back from execution for a moment and think structurally about what I'm doing and why it's working. CUHK feels like the right place to do that—surrounded by people building across Asia, guided by faculty who understand that emerging markets require different thinking, not just cheaper thinking.

I'm ready to stop being lost and start being intentional.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
Throughout my career in investment banking, I have always believed that challenging the status quo is essential for driving innovation and creating value. One particular experience stands out as a moment where I truly pushed back against conventional thinking with meaningful results.

At my firm, we were working on a merger advisory project for a mid-sized manufacturing client. The traditional approach would have been to focus exclusively on financial synergies and cost-cutting measures. However, I felt that this was too narrow and that we were missing important opportunities.

I decided to challenge this conventional thinking by proposing that we also look at operational synergies and revenue synergies, not just cost synergies. My team was initially skeptical, but I was passionate about this idea and believed it could create significant value for our client.

I worked with the team to develop a comprehensive analysis that examined how the two companies could improve their operations and generate new revenue streams by combining their resources. We looked at their supply chains, their customer bases, and their product portfolios to identify areas where they could collaborate and create synergies.

The results were impressive. Our analysis identified approximately $15 million in additional value that would have been missed using the traditional approach. This represented a significant upside for our client and helped them make a more informed decision about the merger.

What I learned from this experience is that innovation often comes from questioning assumptions and being willing to take a different path than what everyone else is doing. I was not afraid to speak up and suggest an alternative approach, even though it went against the conventional wisdom at the firm.

This experience has shaped how I approach problems in my career. I have come to realize that the most valuable contributions often come from people who are willing to challenge the status quo and think differently about problems. Whether it's in investment banking or in other fields, I believe that this mindset is crucial for driving innovation and creating value.

As I move forward in my career, I want to continue developing this ability to challenge conventional thinking and drive innovation. I believe that an MBA from a reputable institution like IIM Ahmedabad will help me build on these skills and prepare me for greater responsibilities in the future.

The world of business is constantly changing, and those who are able to adapt and innovate will be the ones who succeed. I am committed to being one of those people, and I believe that the PGPX program will provide me with the tools and knowledge I need to achieve this goal.

prompt: Motivation letter (minimum 1.500-2.000 words)
content:
Motivation Letter for Corvinus EMBA Programme

Throughout my career in education, I have come to understand that the world needs leaders who can think critically and make informed decisions that impact communities. This realization has driven me to pursue an MBA, and the Corvinus EMBA programme represents the perfect opportunity for me to develop the skills necessary to become such a leader.

I currently work as an Education Programme Manager at a mid-sized educational institution in Singapore. Over the past 12 years, my career has taken me through various roles in the education sector, and I have gained extensive experience in programme development, stakeholder management, and organizational strategy. However, I recognize that my formal training in business has been limited, and I am eager to fill this gap by pursuing advanced studies in business administration.

The education sector in Singapore is rapidly evolving. With technological advancements and changing societal needs, institutions like mine are constantly seeking ways to innovate and remain relevant. I believe that an MBA will equip me with the tools and frameworks necessary to navigate this complex landscape and contribute meaningfully to my organization's strategic direction.

One of the key reasons I am drawn to the Corvinus EMBA programme is its reputation for excellence and its focus on developing leaders who can operate effectively in international contexts. Singapore, while prosperous, exists within a broader Asian and global context. The skills I gain from an EMBA will enable me to understand and contribute to conversations that extend beyond my immediate organizational boundaries.

Throughout my 12 years in education, I have witnessed firsthand the importance of strong management practices. Whether it has been coordinating large-scale programmes, managing budgets, or leading teams, I have developed a deep appreciation for the nuances of organizational management. Yet I also recognize the limitations of my current knowledge. I have often found myself in situations where I wished I had a more formal understanding of financial analysis, strategic planning, or global business practices.

The Corvinus EMBA appeals to me because it promises to provide these tools while maintaining a focus on real-world applicability. The curriculum, as I understand it, emphasizes case studies and practical scenarios that mirror the challenges faced by contemporary organizations. This approach resonates with my learning philosophy, which prioritizes the ability to apply theoretical knowledge to concrete problems.

Beyond the curriculum, the international cohort that characterizes the EMBA programme is extremely attractive. I believe that learning alongside professionals from diverse backgrounds will expose me to different perspectives and approaches to problem-solving. In Singapore, while we benefit from a multicultural society, my professional circles tend to be relatively homogeneous in terms of industry background. An MBA cohort composed of individuals from various countries and sectors would broaden my horizons and provide me with a global network that could benefit my career in the long term.

I am also motivated by the opportunity to develop my leadership capabilities. While I have held leadership roles during my career, I recognize that leadership is not static. The world has changed dramatically since I began my career 12 years ago, and the qualities that made someone an effective leader a decade ago may not be sufficient today. The Corvinus EMBA, with its emphasis on contemporary business challenges and ethical decision-making, would help me evolve as a leader and prepare me to take on more senior roles within my organization or potentially transition into new sectors.

My family background has always emphasized the importance of education. Both my parents valued learning and saw education as a means of personal development and social contribution. This upbringing has instilled in me a lifelong commitment to learning and self-improvement. Pursuing an MBA is a natural extension of these values, and it represents my dedication to continuous growth.

In terms of my career aspirations, I am interested in moving into senior management within the education sector, potentially taking on roles in organizational strategy or institutional leadership. I am also open to exploring opportunities that leverage my expertise in education but allow me to apply broader business principles. An MBA would provide me with the credibility and knowledge base necessary to pursue these opportunities with confidence.

The timing of pursuing an MBA feels right. I have accumulated sufficient professional experience to bring valuable perspectives to classroom discussions, yet I am still early enough in my career to implement the knowledge and skills I gain. I recognize that MBA graduates often speak of the transformative nature of the programme, and I am prepared to fully engage with the experience, both academically and socially.

Furthermore, I believe that the Corvinus EMBA's location in Budapest and its European perspective would be valuable for someone based in Singapore. Understanding business practices and economic conditions in Europe, a region very different from Asia, would expand my conceptual frameworks and enable me to think more globally. The opportunity to spend time in Europe and immerse myself in a different cultural and business environment is genuinely appealing.

In conclusion, I am applying to the Corvinus EMBA programme because I am committed to developing myself as a business leader and professional. I recognize the gaps in my knowledge, I am motivated to address them, and I believe that Corvinus offers the ideal platform for this development. The combination of rigorous curriculum, international cohort, experienced faculty, and practical approach to learning aligns perfectly with my educational goals and career aspirations. I am confident that I will be a valuable member of the cohort and that the programme will be transformative for my career and personal growth.

I am excited about the prospect of joining the Corvinus community and contributing to the learning experience of my peers while advancing my own professional development. Thank you for considering my application.

school_name: Poznan University of Economics MSc Management

prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
Three years into my role as a project developer at Solaris Energy in Cape Town, I was certain I'd cracked the formula. We were tracking to exceed our renewable energy portfolio targets, and I'd personally shepherded four solar projects through to financial close. When our managing director asked me to lead a team acquisition in Namibia—a market we'd never entered—I accepted immediately.

The deal looked straightforward on paper. A 45 MW wind facility with 80% of power already contracted. I ran conservative financial models, assembled a small team, and we moved quickly. Too quickly, it turned out.

Two months into due diligence, our technical consultant flagged a critical issue with the turbine maintenance contracts. The previous operator had been cutting corners for years. Repairs we'd budgeted at $2.3 million would actually cost closer to $8 million over the first five years. When I recalculated the NPV, the deal margin disappeared entirely. We'd been about to commit $40 million to a project that no longer made sense.

I had to walk into the managing director's office and admit we'd missed something fundamental. The conversation was brutal. We'd burned four months and real money on legal and technical fees. More painful was recognizing that I'd prioritized speed over rigor because I was confident—perhaps overconfident—in my initial analysis.

The recovery wasn't complicated, but it required humility. I spent the next two weeks documenting exactly where our process had failed. We'd skipped a second technical opinion because I trusted the first consultant. We'd rushed the financial modeling without stress-testing maintenance assumptions. I presented this to the leadership team without excuses.

What followed changed my approach entirely. I redesigned our due diligence checklist, built in mandatory independent verification for technical components, and slowed our timeline on deals above $30 million. When we evaluated another Namibian project six months later—this time properly vetted—we negotiated favorable terms and closed it successfully.

The setback hurt my confidence temporarily, but it taught me that being right about the big picture doesn't excuse sloppiness in execution. In renewable energy, where we're managing other people's capital and climate commitments, that distinction matters. An Executive MBA will deepen the financial rigor I now know I need, and Otago's emphasis on decision-making under uncertainty directly addresses where I stumbled.

prompt: Why do you intend on pursuing an MBA at this point in your life? Describe your mid-term and long-term visions for your post-MBA career path and how will the Desautels MBA program add value to your future career?
content:
When I joined Nordmark Capital three years ago as an investment analyst, I thought I understood what I was good at: spotting financial patterns, building models, pitching deals. I was wrong. What I actually discovered was that I'm energized by people—figuring out why a CEO makes decisions, convincing a board to back a turnaround story, helping an operations team execute a transformation. The deal itself became secondary to the human element driving it.

This realization crept up on me gradually. I was deep in a €45 million acquisition of a Nordic manufacturing company when our portfolio company's CFO suddenly resigned mid-restructuring. The deal was technically sound, but without the right leader in place, it would fail. I found myself taking on an unexpected role—less analyst, more advisor. I helped the interim CFO stabilize the finance team, then coached the business development director to pivot the sales strategy. Over 18 months, we turned a struggling operation into a profitable business that successfully exited at a 3.2x MOIC. The financial models I'd built were useful, but the real value came from understanding people and strategy at a deeper level.

That experience shifted something fundamental in how I see my career. I'm 26, and I have maybe 40 years ahead. I don't want to spend them perfecting spreadsheets. I want to lead companies through transformation. I want to move into operations-focused roles—first as an investment director or principal at a growth-focused PE firm, then eventually into a COO or CEO position at one of our portfolio companies within 7-10 years.

The gap I need to close is clear: I lack the structured business acumen to lead at that level. My PE background is strong on deal sourcing and financial mechanics, but weak on strategy, operations, and organizational management. I can spot problems; I can't yet architect solutions at scale. My business school choice is therefore deliberate—I need a program that forces me to think systemically about operations, supply chain, organizational behavior, and strategy.

Desautels appeals to me specifically because of its Montreal location and embedded European perspective. I'm Swedish, and I care deeply about continuing in Nordic and European markets. The school's strong reputation in this region matters, but more importantly, Desautels' curriculum design for operations and management fits my exact needs. The mandatory modules in organizational behavior and operations management directly address the gaps I'm trying to close. The emphasis on integrated strategy—not just financial strategy—is something I haven't seen emphasized the same way at other programs I've considered.

I'm also drawn to how accessible the faculty and current students are. When I visited campus last month and sat in on a strategy class, I was struck by how the professor pushed back on assumptions rather than just accepting analysis. The students debated trade-offs instead of hunting for "the right answer." That's the intellectual environment I need to grow into a leader, not just an investor.

I know that getting into a PE program at 26 with a 780 GMAT puts me ahead on some metrics and behind on others. My GPA isn't strong—I coasted through undergrad. But I've worked harder in three years at Nordmark than I ever did in university, and I'm hungry in a way I wasn't at 22. I'm ready to be challenged, to fill the gaps I see in myself, and to become the operator-leader I want to be.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
I first heard about Vlerick at a legal tech conference in Brussels last year, but I'll be honest—I didn't immediately think it was the right fit. I'd been looking at larger programs in London and Amsterdam, places with bigger brand names in the legal sector. Then I sat down with a Vlerick alumna from my firm's Brussels office, Isabelle, who works in corporate law. She described her experience in ways that made me realize I'd been looking for the wrong things.

What struck me most was when she talked about her consulting project during the program. She'd worked with a mid-sized manufacturing company struggling with supply chain inefficiencies—a problem her legal background alone would never have equipped her to solve. She used frameworks she learned in operations and strategy courses to diagnose the issue, and by the time she finished, she understood not just the legal implications but the business fundamentals. She got hired there six months later. That conversation shifted something for me.

I've spent the last decade in legal practice, mostly in M&A and regulatory compliance. I'm good at it—last year I led the compliance review on a €45 million acquisition—but I've hit a ceiling. I can identify legal risks, but I can't speak the language of CFOs and commercial directors. They listen to me when I say "you can't do this," but they don't listen when I try to explain alternatives. I'm invisible in the strategy conversation. That's not because I'm a woman or because I'm Nigerian, though both of those things shape how I navigate rooms. It's because I don't have the business fluency to sit at that table as an equal.

After that conversation with Isabelle, I visited Vlerick's campus in Gent. I attended a class on corporate strategy, sat in on a session about business valuation, and talked with several current students. What I noticed immediately is that the cohort is small enough that you can't disappear, and the curriculum seems genuinely designed for people trying to transition into business leadership, not just for those who've already arrived there. I noticed that several of your professors have executive experience outside academia—your strategy professor actually ran operations for a logistics company—which means they teach real problems, not abstractions.

I want to use Vlerick's MBA to build the financial and operational literacy I need to eventually move into general management, ideally in a company going through transformation. The consulting projects and case competitions are where I'll get hard business skills. But I also want to join a cohort where my legal background isn't just a credential—it's actually useful. I know from Isabelle's experience that Vlerick puts you on real projects with real companies. That's where I'll learn to bridge the gap between legal thinking and business thinking.

I'm ready to stop being the lawyer in the room who knows what we can't do. I want to be the person who figures out what we can.

prompt: Describe your mid-term and long-term visions for your post-MBA career path and how will the Desautels MBA program add value to your future career?
content:
# Post-MBA Career Vision

Throughout my life, I have always been driven by a desire to make a positive impact in the business world. Working in my family's business for the past five years has given me valuable experience, but I know that an MBA is the next logical step in my professional journey. The Desautels Executive MBA program is the perfect fit for my career aspirations and goals.

In my mid-term career goals, I see myself taking on more leadership responsibilities within our organization. I want to help grow our business by introducing new strategies and modern business practices. Right now, I handle various responsibilities in operations and sales, but I lack the formal business education to take on more senior roles. An MBA will give me the credentials and knowledge I need to step into a director-level position within three to five years.

For my long-term vision, I aspire to eventually become the CEO of our family business. My parents built this company from the ground up, and I want to honor their legacy while also modernizing it for the next generation. I believe I have the work ethic and commitment needed, but I recognize that I need stronger skills in strategic planning, financial analysis, and business management. These are areas where an MBA will be instrumental.

The Desautels Executive MBA program will add tremendous value to my career path in several ways. First, the curriculum will provide me with a solid foundation in core business disciplines. My undergraduate degree was not in business, so having structured courses in finance, marketing, and strategy will fill important gaps in my knowledge. Second, the program's focus on real-world applications means I can immediately implement what I learn in my family business, which is exciting. Third, the cohort model will expose me to peers from different industries and backgrounds, which will broaden my perspective.

Additionally, Desautels' location in Montreal and its reputation in Canada align well with my goals. As someone from Colombia, expanding my professional network in North America is important for my development. The program's executive format is also ideal for me because I can continue working while studying, which means I won't have to step away from the business.

I also recognize that earning an MBA from a respected institution like Desautels will give me credibility with employees, partners, and stakeholders. In a family business context, having formal credentials matters when you're trying to establish yourself as a leader beyond just being a family member.

In conclusion, the Desautels Executive MBA program is the right choice for my career development. It will provide the knowledge, skills, and network I need to become a more effective leader in my family business and eventually step into the CEO role. I am committed to succeeding in this program and applying my learning to drive growth and innovation in our organization. I am confident that Desautels will be a transformative experience in my professional journey.

prompt: Select one prompt from group B and respond to your selected prompt using 100 words or fewer. I learned from a colleague (or someone I supervised) when... A practical way in which I have shown compassion towards a colleague or colleagues was... I have empowered a colleague (or teammate) to succeed in their own career/project by...
content: I have empowered a colleague to succeed by actively mentoring a junior consultant through a challenging client engagement. When he struggled with stakeholder management on his first major project, I could have simply reassigned the work. Instead, I met with him weekly, role-played difficult conversations, and gradually increased his client-facing responsibilities. He eventually led the relationship independently and was promoted six months early. What struck me was how much his confidence grew—he started volunteering for presentations he'd previously avoided. I learned that empowerment isn't about removing obstacles; it's about helping someone build the skills to overcome them. That experience changed how I approach leadership.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
# Leadership Essay - Carlos III Executive MBA

Two years ago, I made a decision that terrified me: I told my CEO we needed to shut down the product that had made us $2.3 million in revenue.

I was VP of Product at Nexus, a Series B logistics platform where I'd worked for seven years. We'd built a real-time tracking system that became our flagship offering—the thing investors loved, the thing customers paid for, the thing that made me look good in board meetings. But by 2022, the data was clear: it was rotting from the inside. Our churn rate had climbed to 34%. Our best customers were staying only because they'd invested engineering resources into our integrations. We were solving yesterday's problem beautifully while the market had moved on.

When I first presented the analysis to leadership, the response was silence. Our founding CEO looked at me like I'd suggested we burn down the office. Our CFO immediately talked about the revenue hit. I understood—this product had sustained us through the pandemic. But as the VP who'd built it and the person closest to customer feedback, I had to be honest about what was happening.

What made this feel like leadership, though, wasn't just identifying the problem. It was what I did next.

Instead of declaring we should kill it, I proposed we transform it. I spent six weeks in one-on-one conversations with our twelve largest customers—not to convince them, but to understand what they actually needed. I talked to our engineers about what they'd learned building the system. I asked our sales team what they heard in rooms we weren't in. I was asking from genuine curiosity, not trying to confirm what I already believed.

Those conversations changed everything. What emerged was that customers didn't want us to build faster or cheaper—they wanted us to integrate more deeply into their operations, which meant going vertical instead of horizontal. It was the opposite of what venture capital usually wants to hear. It meant smaller TAM, lower growth rates, but sustainable competitive advantage.

I then had to convince the team to walk into this smaller, harder market. I won't pretend I used inspiration—I used clarity. I showed them the unit economics. I mapped out a three-year roadmap. I hired a new product lead from a vertical SaaS company who'd done this before. I made sure people understood we were moving toward something, not just away from something broken.

The hardest part was letting go of the ego piece. I'd built that original product. Saying it wasn't working anymore meant saying I wasn't right. It meant being visible about a mistake, which in startup culture can be dangerous.

It worked. Eighteen months into the pivot, we've doubled down with one customer segment in logistics optimization. Our NRR is 127%. We're still small, but we're real.

What I learned is that leadership isn't about having the right answer—it's about being brave enough to ask whether you're asking the right question. It's about listening more than talking, even when your title suggests you should be directing. And it's about understanding that the hardest decisions are often the ones where you have to be wrong about something you built.

That's the kind of leader I want to become. And that's why Carlos III's focus on educating brave leaders resonates so deeply with me.

school_name: Fudan University School of Management MBA
prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
Leadership has always been an important part of my career and personal development. I believe that being a good leader means helping others succeed and creating a positive environment where people can do their best work.

During my time working at an accounting firm in Bangkok, I had the opportunity to lead a team of five junior accountants. This was my first real leadership experience, and I wanted to make sure I did a good job. I organized weekly meetings where we could discuss our work and any challenges we were facing. I also made sure to give feedback to my team members so they could improve their skills.

One of the biggest challenges was when we had a client who was very demanding and wanted their financial reports completed very quickly. My team was stressed because the deadline was very tight. I knew I needed to step up and be a good leader. I worked late nights to help them finish the project on time, and I made sure they knew that I appreciated their hard work. We successfully completed the project and the client was satisfied with our work.

This experience taught me that leadership means being willing to work hard yourself and showing your team that you care about them. I learned that when you support your team members and help them through difficult times, they will respect you and work harder.

I think that an MBA from Kellogg will help me develop my leadership skills further. The program will give me the knowledge and tools I need to become a better leader in my career. I am excited about the possibility of studying at Kellogg and learning from professors and other students who have different experiences and perspectives.

In the future, I want to move into management consulting or become a partner at an accounting firm. Leadership skills will be essential for these goals. I believe that studying at Kellogg will help me achieve my dreams and become the leader I want to be.

school_name: Gordon Institute of Business Science (Executive MBA)
prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
# Ethical Dilemma in My Professional Journey

Throughout my career in telecommunications, I have been faced with many challenges that tested my integrity and values. One situation in particular stands out as a defining moment that shaped how I approach ethical decision-making in business.

Last year, I was working as a senior account manager at a mid-sized telecom company. My team was responsible for managing relationships with corporate clients and meeting quarterly revenue targets. We had been struggling to hit our numbers, and there was significant pressure from management to close deals quickly.

One of our major clients, a manufacturing company, was interested in upgrading their communication infrastructure. During the negotiation process, I realized that the solution we were proposing was more expensive and robust than what they actually needed. The client didn't seem to fully understand the technical specifications, and I knew that if I didn't point this out, we would make the sale easily and exceed our targets.

However, I also knew that recommending an oversized solution would be unethical. The client would be paying for capabilities they didn't need, and when they eventually realized this, they would lose trust in our company. This would damage the long-term relationship, which is more important than any single quarter.

I decided to be honest with the client. I explained the difference between what they needed and what we were proposing. I recommended a more cost-effective solution that would still meet their requirements. I also explained why I was making this recommendation, emphasizing that I wanted to build a lasting partnership based on trust.

The client appreciated my honesty. Although the deal was smaller than originally discussed, they were satisfied with the solution. Over the next year, they continued to work with us and eventually expanded their services as their business grew. They also referred us to other potential clients, which resulted in additional revenue.

When I reported this to my manager, I was uncertain how they would react. To my relief, they supported my decision and praised my integrity. The company later used this case study in our training program to teach others about the importance of ethical selling.

This experience taught me that doing the right thing, even when it comes at a short-term cost, is ultimately better for business. It also showed me that organizations value employees who have strong ethical principles and are willing to stand up for them.

As I pursue my Executive MBA at Gordon, I hope to deepen my understanding of business ethics and how to lead with integrity in complex organizational environments. I believe that this program will help me develop the skills needed to make ethical decisions at a more strategic level and to mentor others in my field.

prompt: Describe a difficult decision you made and the process that you went through to make it.
content:
Three years ago, I had to decide whether to leave my role as a senior analyst at a Seoul-based asset management firm where I'd spent five years building relationships and expertise, or turn down an offer to move to Montreal as a portfolio manager for a smaller, less established fund. On paper, it looked simple: bigger title, new country, higher salary. But the decision paralyzed me for weeks.

The comfortable choice was obvious. I had built real influence at my current firm. My team respected my work on emerging market equities. I understood the political dynamics, knew who to push and who to listen to. My parents were relieved I was finally on a stable career trajectory—they'd already worried enough when I came out as non-binary to them two years earlier. The idea of voluntarily leaving security felt reckless.

But the Montreal offer represented something I couldn't stop thinking about: a chance to actually build something rather than execute someone else's strategy. The fund was founded by two former Goldman partners who wanted to run things differently—less hierarchical, more collaborative, willing to take calculated risks on unconventional ideas. In my current role, I'd become increasingly frustrated watching investment theses I believed in get watered down by committee consensus. I was good at my job, but I wasn't learning anymore.

I created a spreadsheet—which felt absurd even as I did it—comparing factors: career growth, compensation, cost of living, proximity to family. But numbers couldn't capture the real tension: was I choosing growth, or was I choosing to escape? Was I running toward something or away from my discomfort?

What broke the paralysis was actually talking to my manager. I expected to have the conversation as a formality before accepting the Montreal offer, but I decided to be honest instead. I told her I felt stuck, that I wanted more autonomy in decision-making, that I didn't see a path to the kind of role I wanted at our firm given its structure. Her response surprised me: she didn't try to counter-offer or convince me to stay. She said, "You're capable of more than what this place lets you do. You should go."

That conversation didn't make the decision easy, but it reframed it. This wasn't about loyalty or ingratitude. It was about whether I trusted my own judgment about what I needed to grow.

I took the Montreal position. The first two years were harder than I expected—the fund was chaotic, underfunded, and I questioned the move constantly. But last year, a thesis I'd fought for on Japanese healthcare stocks returned 34% while the broader index gained 12%. More importantly, I learned what it felt like to think independently, to own both the wins and the losses, to work alongside people who actually wanted my perspective rather than my compliance.

The decision taught me that discomfort isn't always a warning sign—sometimes it's telling you that you've outgrown your space. Coming to Desautels for my executive MBA feels like a similar moment. I'm not looking for a credential to validate where I am. I'm looking to build the strategic and leadership skills I'll need to run my own fund one day. Montreal already feels like home, and I want to deepen my roots here while deepening my understanding of business strategy and leadership.

prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
When I look back at my career, I realize that failure has been my greatest teacher. In 2019, I was working as a Product Manager at a mid-sized tech startup in Jakarta, and I made a decision that cost the company significant time and resources.

Our team was building a mobile application for e-commerce logistics. I was convinced that our user interface design was innovative and would set us apart from competitors. Without conducting proper user testing or gathering sufficient market feedback, I pushed the team to develop the feature as I had envisioned it. I was confident in my vision and did not want to waste time on what I thought were unnecessary steps.

The feature was launched to the market, and the results were disappointing. Users found the interface confusing and the user experience was poor. We lost approximately three months of development time and had to redesign the entire feature from scratch. The company incurred financial losses, and the team morale was affected.

This experience taught me an important lesson about humility and the value of listening to others. I realized that my overconfidence had blinded me to the perspectives of the team and the users. I should have validated my assumptions before implementing them at such a large scale. I learned that good leadership is not about having all the answers, but about creating an environment where team members feel comfortable sharing their opinions and ideas.

After this failure, I changed my approach to product development. I started conducting regular user testing sessions and created a culture of feedback within the team. I made it a point to listen to my team members and incorporated their suggestions into our product roadmap. This change in approach led to better product outcomes and a more engaged team.

I believe that failure is an integral part of growth, and I am grateful for this experience. It has shaped who I am as a professional and as a person. Going forward, I want to continue learning from my mistakes and developing the skills that will make me a better leader and manager.

In pursuing an MBA at IIM Indore, I hope to further develop my understanding of product management and leadership. The program will provide me with frameworks and tools to make better decisions and lead more effectively. I am committed to being a humble learner and contributing meaningfully to the IIM Indore community.

prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
# Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson

Failure is something everyone experiences in life, and I am no exception. Throughout my entrepreneurial journey, I have faced many challenges and setbacks that have shaped me into the person I am today. One particular failure stands out as a turning point in my understanding of business and leadership.

Three years ago, I decided to launch my own venture in the digital marketing space. I was confident that my previous experience and knowledge would be enough to succeed. I had worked in marketing for several years and believed I understood the market well. I gathered a small team of five people and invested significant personal savings into the business.

The venture started well initially. We landed a few clients and generated some revenue in the first few months. However, as time went on, things began to deteriorate. Our client retention rate was very low, and we struggled to attract new clients. Within six months, the business was failing, and within a year, I had to shut it down completely.

At first, I was devastated. I felt like I had let down my team members and disappointed myself. However, upon reflection, I realized that this failure was actually a valuable learning experience. The main issue was that I did not conduct proper market research before launching the business. I made assumptions about what customers wanted without actually talking to them or understanding their needs deeply. I was focused on my own ideas rather than on solving real problems for real people.

Another key lesson was about teamwork and communication. I realized that I had not effectively communicated my vision and strategy to my team members. They were confused about the direction of the company, and this led to inefficiency and lack of motivation. As a leader, I should have been clearer and more transparent about our goals and our progress.

This experience taught me the importance of proper planning, research, and communication in any business venture. It also taught me humility and the need to listen to others' perspectives. When I started my next venture, I applied these lessons directly. I spent considerable time understanding customer pain points before developing my product. I held regular team meetings and sought input from my team members on strategic decisions.

My second venture has been much more successful because of the lessons I learned from my first failure. We have maintained a client retention rate of over 85% and have grown our revenue significantly year over year. This would not have been possible without the valuable lessons I learned from my earlier mistakes.

I believe that failure is not something to be ashamed of, but rather an opportunity to learn and grow. The experience of failing and recovering has made me more resilient and adaptable. As I pursue an MBA from IIM Calcutta, I want to deepen my understanding of business strategy and leadership so that I can continue to learn from my experiences and become a better entrepreneur and leader.

This failure was a humbling experience, but it has ultimately made me a better businessperson. I am grateful for the opportunity to have learned these important lessons, even though the process was difficult.

school_name: Gordon Institute of Business Science (Executive MBA)
prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
# Failure and Learning

Throughout my career in private equity, I have learned that failure is an important part of the journey to success. One particular experience stands out as a turning point in my professional development and has shaped how I approach challenges today.

Early in my career, I was involved in a deal that did not go as planned. We were evaluating an acquisition target in the retail sector, and I was responsible for conducting due diligence on the financial statements. Looking back, I realize that I was overconfident in my abilities and did not ask for help when I should have. The numbers seemed straightforward to me at the time, and I did not dig deep enough into the details.

When we closed the deal, we discovered that there were significant accounting irregularities that we had missed during the due diligence process. This was a costly mistake that impacted the entire investment. My team was disappointed, and I felt responsible for letting them down. It was a difficult period, but it taught me a valuable lesson about the importance of thoroughness and humility.

This experience changed how I approach my work. I learned that even when you think you know something well, it is critical to verify your assumptions and to be willing to ask questions. I also learned that collaboration is essential in complex situations. No one person has all the answers, and good teams are built on the foundation of open communication and shared responsibility.

After this failure, I became much more diligent in my due diligence processes. I started implementing checklists and frameworks to ensure that nothing was overlooked. I also became more proactive in seeking input from colleagues with different expertise. This approach has served me well in subsequent deals, and I have developed a reputation for being thorough and detail-oriented.

This lesson has extended beyond my professional work. In my personal life, I have become more aware of the areas where I need to improve and have sought out feedback more actively. I believe that the willingness to learn from failure and to adapt is a critical quality for success in business and in life.

The Gordon Executive MBA program appeals to me because I believe it will provide me with the tools and knowledge to continue growing as a leader. The program's emphasis on practical business skills and real-world problem-solving aligns with my values and my approach to learning. I am excited about the opportunity to engage with peers from diverse backgrounds and to gain new perspectives on the challenges we face in business today.

In conclusion, my experience with failure has taught me humility, the importance of collaboration, and the value of continuous learning. These lessons will serve me well in the Executive MBA program and throughout my career.

school_name: EM Lyon Business School MSc in Management
prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
When our plant manager told me we'd lost a 4 million euro contract with Siemens, I wasn't supposed to be the one who fixed it. I was a junior process engineer at Volga Manufacturing, twenty-five years old, working in a mid-sized facility in Penza that made precision hydraulic components. The decision had already been made—we'd failed their quality audits twice, and Siemens was moving to a competitor in Germany. I was angry at first. Not at Siemens, but at us. We'd become complacent.

I asked my manager if I could lead the remediation effort. He hesitated. I'd been there less than a year, and we had senior engineers who technically outranked me. But I had documented every problem during our audits, obsessed over them actually, and I had ideas. He gave me the chance.

I immediately assembled a cross-functional team: two operators who'd been at the plant for years, one quality manager, and a logistics person. Notably, no other engineers. I was terrified, if I'm honest. Leading people who had more experience than me felt like fraud. But I realized quickly that experience at a broken process is still a broken process.

The root problem wasn't one thing—it was culture. We moved fast, but we didn't move carefully. I introduced something simple: a mandatory five-minute huddle every morning where any team member, regardless of rank, could raise issues. The first week, nobody said anything. The second week, an operator mentioned that we were using the wrong calibration standard. It should have been caught by quality control months earlier. By week three, suggestions were coming constantly.

We implemented seventy-three small changes over four months. Not revolutionary, but systematic. I created a visual dashboard showing our progress on the plant floor, updated daily. I also made a personal decision to spend thirty minutes every shift on the factory floor, not in the office. I needed to understand what people were actually doing, not what procedures said they should be doing.

Siemens came back for an audit. We passed with zero non-conformances.

What I learned wasn't that I was a natural leader—I still second-guessed myself constantly. It was that leadership sometimes means stepping into discomfort and refusing to accept that "it's how we've always done things." It meant trusting people who had forgotten they had expertise and permission to speak up. It meant being present, not just in authority.

Winning that contract back meant we kept the plant operating and protected three hundred jobs in a region where other manufacturers were closing. But honestly, the bigger win was seeing that change didn't require a complete overhaul. It required someone willing to listen more than command.

This is why EM Lyon appeals to me. I want to deepen my understanding of organizational behavior and systems thinking. I want to lead larger teams, bigger changes, and I need frameworks beyond intuition. I know I have blind spots. An MBA will help me see them.

prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
I launched my first venture in 2016 with what I thought was foolproof logic. The Chinese e-commerce market was exploding, and I'd identified a gap: affordable, quality skincare for tier-two cities. I'd worked in cosmetics marketing for three years, understood the supply chain, and had a small pool of investors ready to write checks.

By month eight, we'd burned through 40% of our initial capital with almost no revenue to show for it. My three co-founders and I were arguing constantly. The product was good, but our go-to-market strategy was a disaster. We were trying to reach customers through department store partnerships and online marketplaces simultaneously—spreading ourselves impossibly thin. I was also making every decision as if I still had a marketing team behind me, not a lean startup with two part-time developers and one operations person.

What stung most wasn't the money we lost. It was realizing I'd never actually asked our target customers what they wanted. I'd assumed. I'd relied on what I knew from my previous corporate role, which was completely different from what a small-town consumer actually needed or trusted.

The turning point came when I stopped and actually listened. I spent six weeks visiting rural markets in Anhui and Shandong, conducting informal interviews, watching how women in those areas made purchasing decisions. Turns out, community trust mattered more than celebrity endorsements. They wanted local distribution points they could physically visit. The online-first strategy was killing us.

We pivoted entirely. We partnered with local beauty shops instead of big retailers. We trained shop owners to become brand ambassadors. Within twelve months, we'd grown 3x and eventually achieved profitability.

If I could do it again, I'd force myself to validate assumptions before scaling. I'd involve my team in strategic decisions instead of dictating them. And honestly, I'd hire a seasoned operator much earlier—someone who could challenge my instincts.

That failure taught me humility in a way no case study could. It's also why I'm drawn to FORE's emphasis on real-world problem solving and the mentorship model. I want to deepen my understanding of business fundamentals while surrounding myself with people who ask hard questions. I'm ready to learn differently.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
Two years into my job as a content strategist at a Stockholm-based streaming platform, I watched us hemorrhage subscribers to Netflix while we obsessed over what our competitors were doing. We had about 1.2 million subscribers, mostly in Nordic countries, and we kept trying to replicate Netflix's catalog strategy—acquiring more shows, more movies, spending millions on content we didn't understand how to market.

One afternoon, I sat with our data analyst and realized something obvious no one was saying out loud: our competitive advantage wasn't our catalog. It was our community. Our Nordic subscribers weren't just watching content; they were using our platform to discover local artists, independent filmmakers, and niche creators they couldn't find elsewhere. We had this organic, engaged audience that Netflix's algorithm would never prioritize.

I proposed something that made our head of content visibly uncomfortable: dramatically shrink our traditional catalog and double down on a creator marketplace. Let subscribers fund and commission the projects they actually wanted to see. It was the opposite of what the industry was doing. Everyone thought I was insane. Our CFO's first question was, "How do we compete with Netflix if we're smaller?" Fair question.

But I pulled together a small team—three people, basically—and we built a prototype in six weeks. We reached out to 50 independent creators in the Nordics and offered them a rev-share model instead of licensing deals. Within four months, we had 200 projects on the platform. Retention rates went up 34%. Most importantly, subscribers started generating content for us, which meant our marketing costs cratered.

Within a year, that marketplace generated 18% of our revenue while consuming only 6% of our content budget. We eventually sold the company to a larger Nordic media group, and that marketplace model became the blueprint for their entire strategy.

What I learned wasn't just about trusting data over industry consensus. It was that the best innovations often come from admitting what you're not good at. We weren't going to beat Netflix at being Netflix. So we stopped trying. That clarity—that willingness to be smaller and weirder and more specific—changed everything.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I joined Bright Media Group as a Graduate Trainee in Manchester straight after university, thinking I had a decent grasp of how television production worked. I didn't. My first project was coordinating logistics for a six-part documentary series on industrial heritage—essentially managing spreadsheets while trying to look purposeful. But I noticed something: our production schedule kept slipping because no one was talking to the budget team until problems became crises. So I started sitting in on their meetings uninvited. Within three months, I'd rebuilt our project timeline in collaboration with finance, and we brought the series in £40K under budget.

That got me promoted to Production Manager. I managed two documentary series simultaneously and a team of four junior coordinators. The jump felt immense—suddenly I was responsible for not just schedules but people's development. I made mistakes. I hired someone who looked impressive on paper but couldn't handle pressure, and I didn't address it quickly enough. That taught me that hiring is about potential and temperament, not just credentials.

After two years, I wanted to understand the business side more deeply. I moved into Commercial Development, where I negotiated licensing deals and explored new revenue streams. That's where I discovered my real interest wasn't just in making content—it was in understanding why certain content succeeds commercially while equally well-made content fails. I spent six months analyzing our viewership data against profitability, which revealed that our most watched shows weren't our most profitable. That insight led to our first branded content division, generating £300K in year one.

Three years in, I've learned I'm strongest when I'm working across silos—connecting production teams with commercial strategy, finance with creative vision. But I've also realized my technical knowledge is spotty. I can't properly evaluate when we should license versus produce in-house, or how to model ROI across different market segments. 

I'm applying to EPGP because I need structured business fundamentals. At 28, I've done reasonably well by instinct and hustle, but India's media landscape is shifting rapidly, and I want to contribute more strategically. Amritsar's executive format appeals to me because I can't step away from work for two years—I'm in the middle of building something. More importantly, I want to understand the "why" behind decisions, not just make them work.

prompt: What are your short-term and long-term professional goals? How will our MBA program help you achieve them?
content:
Six months ago, I sat across from the founder of a competing Indonesian textile mill and realized I didn't actually know how to negotiate. I mean, I thought I did. I'd spent four years managing production at PT Maju Tekstil, optimized our supply chain to cut costs by 22%, and was promoted to Operations Manager at 28. But sitting there, watching him skillfully reframe a deal that was hemorrhaging our margins, I understood the gap between executing someone else's strategy and creating your own.

That's when I committed to the MBA—not because I want to leave manufacturing, but because I want to lead in it differently.

My short-term goal is straightforward: within two years of graduating, I want to move into a regional business development role—ideally overseeing operations and commercial strategy across Southeast Asia for a mid-sized textile or industrial manufacturer. I'm not interested in jumping ship to consulting or finance. Manufacturing built my foundation, and I've seen firsthand how poorly many Western-trained executives handle the operational complexity and relationship-based decision-making that defines this region. I want to fill that gap.

But my longer vision is less conventional. By my early forties, I want to take a stake in a struggling manufacturing business—either my own company or a family operation—and rebuild it. I've watched my father run his garment factory for thirty years, always reactive, never strategic. He's made money, but he's never built systems that could survive without him. I want to show that you can honor the tradition of hands-on manufacturing leadership while modernizing how you actually think about the business.

Here's where LBS becomes essential, not just helpful.

I'm aware of my blind spots. My analytical skills are strong—my GMAT reflects that—but I need to develop the strategic and financial acumen that separates plant managers from business leaders. LBS's integrated approach to operations and finance will directly address this. More importantly, your faculty brings real African and Asian manufacturing experience. That's not theoretical framework; that's contextual.

But honestly, what matters most is the cohort. I've noticed that successful manufacturers in this region almost always have networks that span countries and industries. My current peer group is tight but narrow—mostly other operations people in Indonesia. At LBS, I'll study alongside CFOs from Lagos, entrepreneurs from Kenya, managers from South Africa. These relationships will be invaluable when I eventually move into cross-border roles or take on my own operations.

The negotiation I walked into six months ago? I lost that deal, but I learned something crucial: gaps in knowledge can be fixed, but gaps in perspective are harder to overcome. I spent four years building execution skills that are solid but limited to one country, one company structure. An MBA won't teach me everything about running a regional business, but it will teach me how to think like someone who actually does.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
Last year, I found myself in the middle of a $280 million acquisition where everyone wanted something different.

Our client—a mid-market industrial manufacturer—was being pursued by three potential buyers. The CFO wanted to maximize sale price and pushed hard for a bidding war. The CEO, who'd built the company for 25 years, cared more about preserving the culture and keeping the workforce intact. The private equity consortium we were advising wanted speed and certainty. And then there was the board, which was split between growth-focused members and those nervous about market timing.

I was one of two analysts leading the process, and my managing director basically told me to make it work.

The easy path would have been to optimize for price—chase the highest bid, close quickly, collect the fee. Instead, I spent two weeks doing something that felt wasteful at first: interviewing stakeholders separately. Not to pitch them, but to listen. I asked the CEO what "preserving culture" actually meant to him—turned out it meant keeping the manufacturing in Quebec and maintaining the management team.
