I asked the PE group which timelines were genuinely possible versus just preferred. I asked the CFO if he'd accept slightly lower price if other conditions protected the business.

What emerged was a framework: we structured the deal to include earnout provisions tied to employee retention, secured commitments that kept operations in Quebec for five years, and achieved a final valuation of $268 million—not the absolute maximum, but solid and defensible to the board.

The CEO told me afterward that this was the outcome he actually wanted but couldn't ask for directly. The CFO's bonus was intact. The PE group closed in under four months.

Here's what I learned: stakeholder conflicts aren't actually about stakeholders. They're about incomplete information and unspoken constraints. My job wasn't to decide whose interests mattered most. It was to understand what people really needed versus what they were saying they wanted, then find the overlap.

That skill—sitting with tension instead of resolving it prematurely—feels increasingly important as I advance. Ivey's case-based approach appeals to me precisely because it doesn't let you hide in analysis. You have to actually negotiate solutions across competing needs, which is the work I want to keep getting better at.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
Three years ago, I made the call to our portfolio company CEO at 11 PM on a Friday night and told him we needed to shut down the fund's largest investment. The company had burned through $8 million of our $45 million fund over 18 months, and the metrics told a story we couldn't ignore anymore.

I'm proud of that decision, though it doesn't feel like a traditional achievement.

When I joined Keio Ventures in 2017, I was the only woman on the investment team and the least experienced analyst. I spent my first two years mostly doing market research and financial modeling—the unglamorous work. But by 2020, I'd earned enough trust to lead due diligence on a Series A logistics software company. On paper, everything looked right: experienced founding team, $2 million in ARR, interest from two strategic acquirers. I recommended we invest $3 million.

Within six months, our CTO started missing board meetings. The monthly growth rate, which should have been 15-20%, plateaued at 3%. The CEO kept saying it was a temporary market slowdown. I kept believing him because I wanted to believe him. I'd staked my credibility on this company. Admitting I'd made a mistake felt impossible.

What changed was having an actual conversation with our COO, Tanaka-san, about why I was avoiding the problem. I told him I was afraid of being seen as incompetent—that a failed investment would confirm what some partners already thought about hiring a woman for this role. Instead of dismissing this, he said something I needed to hear: "Your job isn't to be right all the time. It's to be honest about what you see."

That Friday call was me finally being honest. I walked the CEO through the data: declining velocity, the CTO's departure, the fact that our acquirer friends had gone quiet. We agreed to wind down operations over three months, preserve what cash we could, and try to place the team at other companies.

The outcome wasn't clean. We recovered about 35% of our investment. Some of our other LPs were angry. For six months, I felt the weight of that decision whenever I walked into the office.

But here's why I'm genuinely proud: I learned that making the right call and making the comfortable call are almost never the same thing. I also learned I could survive being wrong. More importantly, I learned to see my perspective as an asset rather than a liability. Bringing a different background into VC, where groupthink runs deep, sometimes means you see problems others won't admit exist.

That decision reshaped how I approach investing entirely. I now spend 40% of my time on what I call "early warning analysis"—systematically questioning the narratives we tell ourselves about portfolio companies. It's been unglamorous work, but it's prevented two other major losses and earned me a promotion to Senior Associate.

I want to study at IIM Bangalore because I'm ready to apply these lessons beyond venture capital. Business decisions require the same honesty about what we're refusing to see. I want to build this capability in different contexts.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
When I started at Goldman Sachs Tokyo three years ago, I noticed that our M&A team followed the same valuation methodology for every deal. We would run comparable company analysis, precedent transactions, and DCF models—always in that order, always with the same assumptions. I realized that this conventional approach wasn't necessarily the best one.

One day, while working on a cross-border acquisition in the automotive sector, I suggested that we challenge our traditional thinking about how we approached client pitches. I proposed that instead of presenting three standard valuation methods, we should customize our analysis based on what the client actually cared about. For a family-owned business, maybe the precedent transactions matter more than theoretical DCF models. For a private equity buyer, maybe we should focus on synergy potential rather than pure financial metrics.

My senior managers were hesitant at first. They said this approach was risky and unconventional. But I was passionate about making a difference, so I pushed forward. I worked late nights to develop a framework that could adapt our standard models to different client profiles and deal types. I leveraged data analytics to identify patterns in what information actually moved the needle in past deals.

The results were impressive. When we presented this new methodology to the client, they were extremely pleased. They said our analysis was more relevant to their specific situation than anything they had seen before. The deal closed successfully, and the client gave us very positive feedback about our innovative approach.

After this experience, I realized that challenging the status quo can lead to better outcomes. I started implementing this customized approach in other deals I worked on. Some of my colleagues began adopting similar methods as well. It showed me that sometimes the best innovations come from questioning why we do things the way we do.

This experience has shaped how I think about problem-solving. I believe that in any organization, there are opportunities to challenge conventional thinking and drive innovation. At business school, I want to develop my strategic thinking skills further and learn how to implement change management in larger organizations. I also want to understand how innovation happens across different industries and contexts.

I am interested in Otago Business School because of its strong reputation and innovative curriculum. I believe the program will prepare me well for a career where I can continue to challenge conventional thinking and create value. I am excited about the possibility of joining your community and contributing my perspective as someone from a different cultural background.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I started my career at the wrong company for the right reasons. In 2018, I joined Zenith Solutions, a mid-market enterprise software firm in Singapore, as a junior account executive. I was 22, fresh out of university, and completely out of my depth. My first quarter, I missed my target by 40%. I remember sitting in my manager's office, convinced I'd be fired within six months.

What saved me wasn't talent—it was stubbornness. I began shadowing our top performer, Rajesh, documenting how he structured client calls, how he positioned our product differently for finance teams versus operations teams. I rewrote my entire pitch deck twice. By quarter three, I hit 92% of target. By year two, I was top performer.

But growth in sales taught me my limitation: I was good at closing deals, not understanding what came after. In 2021, when our VP of customer success left suddenly, I volunteered to move into the role, despite having no formal training. This was mistake number two—but a deliberate one. I managed a team of five, oversaw renewal rates of $4.2M in ARR, and reduced churn from 18% to 11% in eighteen months. More importantly, I learned that I didn't actually enjoy managing accounts. I enjoyed solving operational problems.

By 2023, I shifted into a business operations role at Luminous, a Series B SaaS company focusing on AI-assisted analytics. I was hired to build their revenue operations function from scratch. Over two years, I've designed our go-to-market process, implemented Salesforce infrastructure, and built a team of three. Revenue has grown from $800K to $3.2M ARR.

Yet I've hit a ceiling. I can execute processes, but I lack the strategic framework to think about market positioning, pricing models, or how unit economics drive company decisions. When our CEO discusses discounting strategy or expansion into Southeast Asia, I feel like I'm translating rather than contributing. This is why I'm pursuing the MSc in Management at Waseda.

Singapore is home, but Tokyo's business culture and Waseda's proximity to Japan's enterprise software ecosystem appeals to me. I want to deepen my understanding of organizational strategy while staying connected to the Asia-Pacific tech community. The program's focus on cross-functional leadership and strategic analysis aligns directly with my ambition to move into a head of operations or strategy role within the next five years.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
Three years ago, I faced what seemed like an unsolvable problem at SolarTech Philippines: we were losing 34% of potential customers during the sales process, and no one could figure out why.

I managed the business development team for commercial rooftop installations—we had solid engineering, competitive pricing, and a growing market. But our conversion rate was hemorrhaging, and the pressure from leadership was mounting. The VP wanted traditional solutions: hire more salespeople, run more ads, increase discounts. None of that felt right to me.

Instead of jumping to solutions, I spent two weeks doing something unconventional. I personally called 47 rejected customers and asked them to walk me through their decision-making process. Most of them didn't say no because of price or skepticism about solar—they said they got lost in the paperwork. Our sales team would hand over a 23-page proposal, technical specifications, financing options, and timelines, and customers would disappear.

The insight was humbling: we had built a process for engineers, not for business owners trying to understand ROI in thirty minutes.

So I built something different. With our IT team's help, I created an interactive online tool that walked customers through a personalized three-step story. First, it calculated their exact energy consumption based on their utility bills (which we'd ask them to upload). Second, it showed them a specific savings projection for their building, not generic industry numbers. Third, it mapped out the installation timeline and monthly payment schedule side-by-side with their expected savings—so they could see they'd break even in year six, for example, not year ten.

Critically, it was four pages, not twenty-three.

I knew this might upset our engineering team—simplified might mean we looked unsophisticated. So I didn't just announce it. I brought three engineers into the development process, let them see that we were still capturing all technical data; we were just presenting it differently. They became advocates instead of resistors.

The results came quickly. Within six months, our conversion rate jumped to 58%. More importantly, customer acquisition cost dropped by 26% because rejected customers would often refer us to other building managers—apparently, the process itself was now something they wanted to recommend.

What struck me most was what I learned about myself during this process. I realized I had been defaulting to leadership's suggested playbook without really questioning it. I was competent but not creative. Once I slowed down enough to actually listen to customers—to sit with the discomfort of not knowing the answer—the solution became obvious. That's when I understood the difference between being good at your job and actually solving problems.

This experience changed how I approach challenges. I now instinctively ask people what they actually need instead of what I think they need. At SolarTech, we've since applied this philosophy to three other bottlenecks in our process.

I'm drawn to Deakin's MBA specifically because of its emphasis on sustainable business innovation. I want to deepen my strategic thinking and expand beyond the technical energy sector so I can solve problems at a portfolio level, not just within my team. The collaborative learning environment and industry partnerships are exactly what I need to make that leap.

prompt: Reflect on a core value that defines you. Share a specific example of how it has guided your decisions and actions, and explain how it will shape your contributions and growth as a McCombs MBA student. Highlight how this value has influenced your leadership style and life path, and how it will shape your MBA experience.
content:
Integrity has always been central to who I am. I believe in doing the right thing, even when it's difficult, and this value has shaped my career and personal decisions.

In my current role at a media production company in Sydney, I oversee a team of five and manage client relationships. About a year ago, a major client asked me to exaggerate the viewership numbers for a campaign they had funded. The numbers were good, but not as impressive as they wanted to present to their board. My manager suggested it would be harmless and the client was important to our business.

I decided not to do it. I told the client the actual figures and explained why inflating them would be problematic long-term. I was worried about losing the contract, but I couldn't compromise on honesty. Surprisingly, the client appreciated my transparency and we continued working together. They actually valued having a partner they could trust.

This experience reinforced that integrity isn't just about following rules—it's about building real trust with people. It's made me a better leader because my team knows I won't ask them to do anything unethical, and clients know they can rely on what I tell them.

As an MBA student at McCombs, I want to deepen my business acumen while maintaining these principles. I believe the program will teach me how to scale integrity across larger organizations and more complex situations. In the media industry, there are constant pressures to compromise ethics for growth, and an MBA will give me the tools to lead with both integrity and business success.

I'm excited to bring this commitment to McCombs and to learn from peers who also value doing business the right way.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# Challenging the "Always Have" in Military Procurement

In 2019, I was managing procurement operations for the Ministry of Defense in Hanoi, overseeing contracts worth approximately $12 million annually. We purchased everything from vehicle maintenance supplies to communication equipment through a process that hadn't fundamentally changed in twenty years: vendors submitted bids, we evaluated based on price and established relationships, and we selected whoever had the lowest cost.

The problem wasn't obvious at first. On paper, we were following proper procedure. But after reviewing contracts from the previous five years, I noticed something: we were working with roughly fifteen core suppliers, and our costs kept rising despite "competitive bidding." When I spoke with the procurement team, they explained this was simply how government contracting worked. These were established vendors. They understood our needs. Switching suppliers meant risk.

I pushed back. I asked a question no one seemed to want to answer: why were we assuming the incumbent suppliers were our best option?

So I did something unconventional for our bureaucracy. I spent three weeks mapping our actual supply chain data—not just prices, but delivery times, defect rates, payment terms, and total cost of ownership. I discovered we were paying a 22% premium compared to what private companies paid for similar items. More frustrating, we had zero visibility into why. Our vendor relationships were built on familiarity, not performance metrics.

I proposed we establish a vendor scoring system that weighted price at only 40%, with 30% for reliability, 20% for innovation/responsiveness, and 10% for sustainability practices. My colleagues thought I was naive. One senior officer told me directly: "This is government. We don't work with new suppliers. Too complicated."

I didn't argue. Instead, I piloted the system with one category: office equipment and supplies. I identified three new vendors from outside our traditional network and ran a four-month trial. The results were stark. Our costs dropped 18%, delivery times improved from 14 days to 6 days, and the new vendors were actually more responsive to our specific requests because they were trying to prove themselves.

After seeing those numbers, the resistance faded. We expanded the system across five procurement categories over the next year. By 2021, we'd reduced costs by approximately $2.1 million while improving service quality. More importantly, we'd established a framework that didn't depend on one person pushing—it became the standard process.

What surprised me most wasn't the financial impact. It was realizing how much of our work was done a certain way simply because "that's how we've always done it." In government, this inertia is real. But it's also an opportunity.

This experience taught me that challenging convention requires two things: solid data and patience with bureaucracy. An MBA will deepen my analytical toolkit and expose me to frameworks from other industries that face similar innovation challenges. I'm particularly interested in EM Lyon's focus on responsible innovation—I want to understand how to scale these changes in ways that account for the complexities of large organizations, not just the elegance of the idea.

I'm ready to take what I've learned in procurement and apply it more broadly to organizational strategy and change management.

school_name: UNSW Business School Master of Management
prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
I joined TechVentures Capital in Lima two years ago as an Associate, and I was convinced I would be great at deal sourcing immediately. My manager told me to build relationships with founders in the SaaS space, and I approached it like it was a checkbox—I set up coffee meetings, attended pitch events, sent emails. I thought activity meant success.

After six months, I had met maybe fifty founders but brought zero deals to the investment committee. My pipeline was empty. I was working long hours but producing nothing. It was a difficult time because I felt like I was failing at the one thing the firm hired me to do.

The turning point came when my manager sat me down and was honest about my performance. He said I was making contacts but not making connections. I was approaching founders like I was trying to sell them something, rather than trying to understand what they actually needed. That feedback was hard to hear, but it was true.

I decided to change my approach completely. Instead of trying to meet as many people as possible, I focused on building real relationships with maybe ten founders in the early-stage fintech space. I started asking better questions. I learned their product roadmaps. I understood their burn rates and what they were really worried about at 2 AM. I went to their offices instead of always meeting in coffee shops. I introduced them to other founders I knew, not because I expected a deal but because it was helpful for them.

After three months of this approach, I sourced a deal with a payments startup called Nexo that we ended up investing in at a $8M valuation. The founder later told me he chose us because I was the only investor who actually understood his market and his business model, not just the pitch deck. That deal was $2.4M check, and it was TechVentures' largest investment that year.

What I learned is that in venture capital, you cannot fake relationships. I was trying to be efficient by meeting many people quickly, but efficiency in the wrong direction is just wasting time. Real deal sourcing requires patience and genuine interest in founders' success, not your own metrics. The best investors are the ones founders want to work with, not the ones with the biggest rolodex.

This experience taught me to slow down and go deeper. It is a lesson I am carrying with me as I think about the next chapter of my career. I want to develop a more strategic approach to business—understanding problems deeply before trying to solve them. That is why an MBA is important to me. I need to build analytical and strategic skills that will make me a more thoughtful investor and operator.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
I've spent the last six years convinced I was building something permanent. As a project manager at Mainstreet Properties in Toronto, I oversaw the development of mid-rise residential complexes—coordinating architects, contractors, investors across projects worth $40–80 million each. I was good at it: on-time delivery, under budget, teams that actually wanted to work for me. But somewhere around year five, I realized I was optimizing for the wrong thing.

The realization came during the pandemic shutdown. We had three active sites sitting idle, and I watched our CFO make decisions that affected whether we'd keep people employed or lay them off. These weren't technical decisions. They required understanding our cash position, debt covenants, how different scenarios rippled through the business. I knew the operations side inside out, but I was sitting in meetings suddenly feeling like a translator between my world and his—except I barely spoke his language.

That's when I knew an MBA wasn't optional anymore.

The timing felt awkward at first. I'm not the typical post-undergraduate candidate. Six years in, I've got real projects, real teams, real failures. When our 2019 project in Mississauga ran $3.2 million over budget due to unforeseen soil conditions and my poor risk modeling, I didn't just learn construction—I learned what happens when you guess instead of analyze. That failure has stuck with me more than any case study could.

I've thought about ESADE specifically for a few reasons. First, the Master in Management isn't designed for you to hide from the fact that you're starting fresh in some ways. I like that honesty. I'm not looking for validation of what I already know; I'm looking to actually change how I think about business. Your program's emphasis on leadership and decision-making under uncertainty speaks to what I've lived but haven't formalized.

Second, Barcelona. I know that sounds superficial, but it's not. My family immigrated to Canada when I was four, and I've always worked within North America's real estate ecosystem. I want to understand how development happens differently elsewhere—how Spanish and European regulations, financing models, and labor approaches change the game. Real estate is deeply local, and that forces you to actually understand different contexts, not just import Canadian solutions.

What I'm struggling with now is whether I go deeper into development—maybe eventually launch my own firm—or pivot toward the finance or investment side of real estate. That's not something I can figure out from a Toronto office. I need exposure to different frameworks, different industries, different versions of how business gets done.

My GMAT score isn't exceptional, but my GPA and work history reflect how I actually operate: patient, deliberate, willing to put in the work when something matters. I bring six years of operational experience and a very specific hunger to understand what I've been missing. I'm ready to learn differently.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
# Essay Response

During my nine years in education, I have learned that managing different viewpoints is crucial for success. One situation that stands out involved a conflict between teachers, parents, and administration at the international school where I worked as Academic Coordinator.

The situation was this: the school wanted to implement a new curriculum that was more modern and aligned with international standards. However, the teachers felt this would create extra work, and parents were concerned about their children's transition to the new system. These competing interests made the situation very challenging.

I organized meetings with all stakeholders to understand their concerns. The teachers worried about professional development and training time. The parents wanted to ensure their children would not fall behind academically. The administration wanted to modernize without increasing costs. I realized that each side had valid points, and I needed to find a solution that satisfied everyone.

I developed a phased implementation plan that addressed these concerns. First, I arranged comprehensive training for teachers during the summer break, which gave them time to prepare without disrupting classes. Second, I created detailed communication materials for parents explaining the curriculum changes and how they would benefit the students. Third, I negotiated with administration to allocate a reasonable budget for the transition period. This compromise allowed the school to move forward with modernization while respecting the concerns of all parties.

The new curriculum was successfully implemented, and feedback after one year was positive. Teachers felt more confident, parents saw improvement in student engagement, and the school achieved its modernization goals within budget. This experience taught me the importance of stakeholder management and finding balanced solutions.

I believe this skill is essential for any management role. At Lancaster University Management School, I hope to further develop my abilities in organizational leadership and strategic decision-making. The MBA program will help me understand how to navigate complex business situations where multiple parties have different objectives. I am committed to bringing this collaborative mindset to my future career.

school_name: Singapore Management University Master in Management
prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
Failure is not something I had experienced much in my early career. Growing up in Saudi Arabia, I was used to success coming relatively easily—good grades in school, a comfortable family background, and a clear path forward. But in 2018, when I decided to start my own business, I learned that the real world is much different from the classroom.

My business was in the e-commerce space, selling fashion items online to customers across the Middle East. I had the idea, I had some savings, and I thought I had the skills. In the beginning, things went well. We launched the website, got some initial customers, and I felt like we were on track. However, after about six months, things started to go downhill. Sales were declining, customer retention was low, and I was burning through my capital quickly. I did not have a solid business plan or financial projections. I was just hoping things would work out.

By the end of 2019, I realized I had made serious mistakes. I had not done proper market research. I did not understand my customers well enough. My marketing strategy was unfocused, and I was trying to appeal to everyone instead of a specific niche. I was also managing everything myself, which meant nothing was getting done properly. The business failed, and I lost a significant amount of money—around 200,000 Saudi riyal. This was difficult not just financially but emotionally. I had invested my time, energy, and belief into this venture, and it did not work out.

For several months after the business closed, I felt discouraged. I questioned whether I had what it takes to be an entrepreneur. I wondered if I should just go back to a traditional job and forget about my ambitions. But slowly, I started to think about what I could learn from this experience. I realized that failure, while painful, was actually a valuable teacher.

I decided to spend time analyzing what went wrong. I read books about business strategy and customer psychology. I talked to other entrepreneurs who had failed and succeeded. I understood that my biggest mistake was not validating my business idea before investing so much money. I should have talked to potential customers first, tested my product, and built a minimum viable product with limited resources.

In 2020, I started working at a technology startup as a business development manager. This role was very different from being a founder, but it taught me so much about how successful companies actually operate. I learned about proper financial management, customer segmentation, and strategic planning. I worked with a team of about 15 people and saw how important it is to have clarity in roles and communication.

After two years in this role, I felt ready to try again. In 2022, I started a new business in the same e-commerce space, but this time with a completely different approach. I started small, focused on a specific customer segment, and built the product based on actual customer feedback. I also brought on a co-founder who had strengths in areas where I was weak. This venture has been much more successful. We have grown to serve around 5,000 customers and are now profitable.

Looking back, I understand that my first business failure was not really a failure at all—it was education. It taught me humility, the importance of doing proper due diligence, and the value of learning from others. I also learned that I am resilient. Even when things do not go according to plan, I can pick myself up and try again with a better strategy.

I believe an MBA from Singapore Management University will help me deepen my understanding of business strategy and give me the tools to scale my current venture into something much larger. I am ready to learn and grow, and I am confident that this program will prepare me for the next chapter of my entrepreneurial journey.

school_name: University of Melbourne FBE Master of Management
prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
When I arrived at EY's Stockholm office in 2015, I was the only non-binary person in my consulting cohort. It wasn't a crisis—my colleagues were professional—but I noticed I was performing a version of myself in client meetings. I would sit slightly differently, speak with more certainty than I felt, assume a more conventional presence. It was exhausting, and it was costing me.

By 2019, I'd built enough credibility that I could stop performing. I came out to my team. What followed wasn't a dramatic transformation, but something quieter and more powerful: I became more effective.

My real accomplishment, though, happened in 2022. I was leading a digital transformation engagement for a major Nordic bank—a €8 million project across three countries with twelve consultants and forty client stakeholders. Six months in, our relationship with the client was deteriorating. The CFO felt unheard. The technical teams saw our recommendations as disconnected from reality. Internal politics were fracturing the sponsor coalition.

I did something unconventional. I stopped running structured workshops and started having unscripted conversations. I asked the CFO what she actually wanted the bank to look like in five years, then shut up and listened. I spent afternoons in the development team's office asking stupid questions about their constraints. I acknowledged the political tensions directly rather than pretending they didn't exist.

Within two months, the energy shifted completely. The CFO began advocating for our recommendations internally. The technical teams contributed ideas that actually improved our design. We delivered ahead of schedule and the client renewed their engagement.

My team later told me it was the first time they'd seen me lead without a script. One junior consultant said, "You were actually present instead of performing the role of consultant."

Why does this matter? Because I finally understood that my greatest professional asset isn't my analytical capability or my ability to read a spreadsheet—it's my willingness to show up authentically. And that authenticity makes me a better leader, not despite my complexity, but because of it.

I want an MBA that challenges me to examine these edges more deeply. Melbourne's emphasis on purpose-driven leadership resonates with where I want to grow.

school_name: LUISS Business School Master in Management
prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
Dear LUISS Admissions Committee,

When I started as a warehouse coordinator in Karachi eleven years ago, I was moving boxes and learning the difference between inbound and outbound logistics. Today, I manage supply chain operations for a 2,000-person distribution network across South Asia, but I still remember the frustration of watching inventory inefficiencies cost our company roughly 8% of annual margins—and knowing I lacked the framework to fix it properly.

That's the honest reason I'm applying to your Master in Management program now.

I've built something real in supply chain. At my current role with a multinational FMCG distributor, I oversee logistics for five countries. I reduced last-mile delivery times by 34% through network redesign, cut warehousing costs by €2.3 million annually by consolidating redundant facilities, and led a team through our first SAP implementation—which, frankly, was messy. We went over budget. I made decisions I'd make differently today. But we got it done, and we learned.

The problem is this: I've hit a ceiling that experience alone won't break through. I can manage operations well. I cannot yet think strategically about business—about finance, about market positioning, about how my supply chain decisions ripple through the entire organization's profitability. When our CFO presents quarterly numbers, I understand the operational levers but miss the bigger picture. When our CEO discusses regional expansion into Southeast Asia, I can only contribute tactical logistics thoughts, not business strategy.

I need the tools to become a general manager, not just a functional expert. And I need to do this now, before I'm too senior to go back for it.

Why LUISS specifically? Three reasons.

First, your program's structure—the general management focus with flexibility to specialize—matches where I am. I'm not pivoting careers. I'm deepening what I do. Supply chain is increasingly strategic; I need to learn the economics and strategy language that my peers in finance and commercial teams already speak. Your curriculum seems designed for exactly this.

Second, the location matters. I'm based in Pakistan, but I work across Asia. Many top programs are too Europe or US-centric. LUISS's position in Europe with a genuinely international cohort feels right. I need exposure to how different markets approach business—how European companies think about efficiency differently than we do in South Asia, how that diversity strengthens decision-making.

Third, and I'll be direct: your admissions standards suggest you value potential and track record over just test scores. My GMAT was 623. It's not impressive. I'm not a natural test-taker; I work slowly, methodically. But I've delivered results in a complex, competitive industry. I'm looking for a program that recognizes that an 11-year operational track record might predict graduate success as reliably as standardized tests do.

I'm realistic about what an MBA will and won't do. It won't make me a CFO. It will give me the fluency I need to be a CEO or Chief Operating Officer one day—roles where I actually want to go. It will make me a better partner to finance and strategy leaders. It will give me frameworks I've been building through experience alone, often inefficiently.

I'm ready to work hard in your program. I've spent eleven years learning my craft; I'm asking for two years to learn business.

Thank you for considering my application.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
Three years ago, I walked into Zenith Pharma's Lagos office to find our supply chain in chaos. We'd just lost our primary distributor—no warning, no transition plan—and had 48 hours before critical antimalarial medications would be out of stock across six West African countries. Our operations manager looked defeated. I remember thinking: we can either panic or we can actually solve this.

The obvious route was to scramble for an emergency distributor, but that would cost us 40% more per unit and we'd still have gaps. Instead, I spent that first night mapping out who we actually knew. I started calling—not just distributors, but former colleagues, contacts from my time at GlaxoSmithKline, even people I'd met at conferences years before. What I realized was we had fragments of a solution already within our network; they just weren't connected.

By morning, I'd drafted a plan: partner with three smaller regional distributors simultaneously instead of one large one. Yes, it meant coordinating with three different teams across Lagos, Accra, and Abidjan. Yes, it was messier. But it meant we could leverage existing relationships, split the volume so nobody was overextended, and maintain pricing closer to our standard rates.

The hard part was implementation. I had to convince our CFO this unconventional approach wasn't reckless—I built a spreadsheet showing cost per unit, delivery timeline, and risk assessment for each scenario. I had to negotiate with each distributor individually, offering slightly higher margins to three partners rather than maximizing margins with one. That required me to admit we were in a vulnerable position, which isn't easy.

What made the real difference, though, was how I managed the execution. I physically traveled to each distribution hub in the first two weeks. I didn't send emails or make calls from Lagos. I sat in their warehouses, understood their constraints, and we problem-solved together when shipments had routing issues or documentation delays. One distributor in Accra had capacity but their cold chain wasn't certified for our temperature requirements. Instead of replacing them, I worked with our quality team to get them certified—it took three weeks but strengthened the entire network.

Within six weeks, we weren't just back to normal stock levels. We'd created redundancy in our supply chain we'd never had before. Within eighteen months, we'd formalized all three partnerships. Our cost per unit actually decreased by 8% because the competition between them kept margins reasonable, and our delivery reliability improved from 94% to 98%.

But honestly, what stayed with me most wasn't the financial outcome. It was realizing how often we default to "the way we've always done things" when creative solutions are hiding in plain sight. I'd spent twelve years in pharma thinking I knew how supply chains worked. This taught me that sometimes the best solutions come from being willing to look stupid—admitting you need help, trying something unconventional, and then putting in the unglamorous work to make it actually work.

That's what I'm looking for in the MiM program at Makerere: an environment where I can sharpen not just my technical skills, but also how I think about problems that don't have textbook answers.

school_name: Fudan University School of Management MBA
prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
I have always been passionate about creating value in emerging markets, and my six years in private equity have given me the foundation to pursue my ultimate goal of launching my own investment fund focused on Southeast Asia.

Working at my current firm, I have gained deep experience in deal sourcing, financial modeling, and portfolio management. I have worked on transactions ranging from $5 million to $50 million across various sectors including technology, consumer goods, and manufacturing. This experience has shown me the critical importance of understanding local markets, building strong networks, and having the right cultural insights to succeed in cross-border investments.

My aspiration is to establish a mid-market PE fund that invests in high-growth companies in Indonesia, Vietnam, and Thailand. These markets represent significant opportunities for value creation, yet many emerging entrepreneurs lack access to patient capital and strategic guidance. I want to be the partner who understands their context and can help them scale internationally.

To achieve this goal, I need several things that Fudan's MBA will provide. First, the program's strong network across Asia will be invaluable for building relationships with LPs, entrepreneurs, and other ecosystem players. Second, I need to deepen my understanding of corporate strategy and business development in ways that my PE experience has not fully addressed. Finally, I want to build my skills in negotiation and cross-cultural communication, which will be essential when working with teams and founders across different countries.

I am particularly interested in Fudan's courses on emerging market strategies and regional business dynamics. Being in Shanghai, one of Asia's most important financial centers, will give me access to investors and industry leaders who share my vision for this region.

My background as a Turkish professional working in a primarily European firm has already taught me how to navigate different business cultures. The MBA will allow me to formalize this knowledge and build a more systematic approach to international business. I am committed to leveraging the Fudan community to turn my vision into reality.

prompt: What unique traits, perspectives and experiences will you bring to contribute to Broad's collaborative learning culture?
content:
I grew up watching my parents run a small distribution business in Bogotá, where I learned that success isn't about having all the answers—it's about asking the right people the right questions. That lesson has stayed with me through my four years at Nestlé, where I've moved from a junior role in category management to leading a regional team of eight across three countries.

My perspective is shaped by working in an industry where margins are thin and competition is constant. At Nestlé, I managed the repositioning of our coffee line in Colombia and Ecuador, which required me to navigate not just consumer preferences but also regional supply chain differences and cultural nuances that spreadsheets couldn't capture. I had to rely heavily on my team—on a logistics manager who understood port operations in ways I never would, on a local marketer who could read the pulse of her city. That experience taught me that the best solutions come from people with different vantage points willing to actually listen to each other.

I think that's where I can add value to Broad's classroom. I don't come in with all the answers, and I'm genuinely comfortable with that. I ask a lot of questions, sometimes the uncomfortable ones that force people to defend their assumptions. In meetings, I notice when someone quieter hasn't spoken and I push for their input, because I've seen brilliant ideas come from unexpected places. I also bring real experience with failure—I misread a market expansion into Peru, and the project was scaled back significantly. That humbling moment taught me more about decision-making than any success has.

I'm drawn to an Executive MBA specifically because I want to deepen my strategic thinking while staying engaged with my work. I know I need to understand finance and operations at a level I currently don't, and I need to spend time with people building businesses outside of CPG. But I also think I have something to contribute: a willingness to challenge politely, to stay curious about how others think, and to help create an environment where people feel safe being honest about what they don't know.

My background as a Colombian woman in a traditionally male-dominated industry has meant constantly adapting to different environments. I bring that adaptability to group work. I'm ready to learn from Broad, and I think the class will be better for having someone in the room who asks why before jumping to how.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
When I graduated from Uppsala University in 2012, I thought I understood what I wanted: to build a career in finance. I was wrong, though not in the way you might expect. I did build that career. What I didn't anticipate was how much it would teach me about what I don't know.

I joined Industrivärden as an analyst right after graduation. For three years, I focused on due diligence for industrial companies—valuation models, competitive analysis, the standard toolkit. I was competent. I was also, I realize now, operating at surface level. I could assess a company's balance sheet, but I couldn't articulate why certain leadership teams succeeded while others failed. I couldn't explain the organizational friction I'd witness during post-acquisition integration that no spreadsheet predicted.

At 26, I moved to Segulah as an associate, finally with a seat in investment committee meetings. This is where I started paying attention differently. I began asking questions that made senior partners slightly uncomfortable: Why do we assume this management team can scale? What's the conversation we're not having about culture? I was learning that the best investments weren't about finding the cheapest asset—they were about understanding people and systems in ways I'd never formally studied.

By 2018, I'd been promoted to senior associate and moved into a more strategic role focused on portfolio development and operational improvements. I managed teams. I worked across four countries. I realized I was making decisions daily about organizational structure, talent retention, and transformation—decisions I was essentially learning on the job.

This is where the self-awareness kicks in. At 34, with twelve years in private equity and a technical skill set that's served me well, I also know my limitations. I've never taken a structured approach to understanding organizational behavior, strategy at the enterprise level, or how to formally assess and develop talent. I've learned through osmosis, which has advantages but also gaps.

Sheffield's MSc Management appeals to me because it offers what my career hasn't: a chance to formalize and deepen the thinking I've been doing intuitively. Not to start over—I don't need that. But to develop the strategic and organizational framework that will let me step into the senior principal or partner roles I'm targeting, and to do it with greater intentionality.

I've spent twelve years building instinct. I'm now building insight.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
# Career Aspirations and Makerere's Role in My Next Chapter

Nine years ago, I was managing a single residential project in Tel Aviv—180 units, 15 million shekel budget, and I felt like I was drowning half the time. The banks didn't trust me. The contractors cut corners. The investors called every week asking why their money was tied up. I learned real estate by getting my hands dirty, but I also learned that I was missing something fundamental: I didn't understand how to scale beyond one project.

Today, I run development operations for a regional firm covering Israel, Jordan, and the West Bank. We've completed 2,400 units across eight projects, managing budgets exceeding $200 million. But here's what keeps me up at night: I'm still operating like a project manager who happens to be in charge of a team. I know the detailed mechanics—cost engineering, contractor relationships, permitting—but I lack the strategic frameworks to think about what comes next.

I want to be a developer who can see three moves ahead. Not just "build these 500 units," but "what market conditions support this, how do we structure the financing to de-risk investors, what's the portfolio approach that compounds value?" Right now, I'm making these decisions instinctively, based on patterns I've seen, and that works until it doesn't. I need the financial modeling skills, the strategic planning toolkit, and honestly, the confidence that comes from rigorous training.

The Middle East is changing. Governments are pushing local investors to develop housing, commercial space, and mixed-use developments. There's money available, there's demand, and there's chaos in how it gets deployed. I see an opportunity to build something substantial—not just as a developer, but as someone who helps structure these projects so they actually serve the communities and don't just extract value. That requires different thinking than I currently have.

Makerere attracted me specifically because it's not a Western MBA program teaching me how the world works in New York or London. I need to understand emerging markets, real estate finance in contexts where traditional securitization doesn't exist, and how to operate in environments with different regulatory frameworks and capital structures. Your curriculum on emerging market strategy and infrastructure finance is exactly what I'm looking for.

I also know I need to slow down and think. Running projects non-stop for nine years has made me operationally sharp but strategically thin. A full-time program—not an executive MBA squeezed around my calendar—forces that reset. I need colleagues who are building things in Lagos, Cairo, and Nairobi. I need case studies about emerging market real estate that aren't written by consultants in Geneva.

In five years, I want to be developing flagship projects that get written up in regional publications. In ten years, I want to be advising governments on housing strategy. Makerere isn't just a credential for me. It's the deliberate pause I need to become the kind of developer who builds for impact, not just profit.

school_name: SP Jain School of Global Management EMBA
prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
Since graduating with my degree in Biomedical Engineering, I have been passionate about working in the medical devices industry. My goal has always been to help people improve their health and quality of life, and I believe that medical devices play a crucial role in achieving this mission.

After graduation, I joined a large multinational medical devices company in São Paulo as a Junior Engineer. In this role, I was responsible for various engineering tasks and contributed to different projects. I worked with teams across the organization and gained valuable experience in how a global company operates. This experience was instrumental in shaping my understanding of the industry.

After two years in this position, I was promoted to Senior Engineer. This promotion reflected my hard work and dedication to the company. In my new role, I took on more responsibilities and led several initiatives. I managed cross-functional teams and worked on multiple projects simultaneously. I felt that I was growing professionally and developing new skills that would be valuable for my future career.

Throughout my time at the company, I have worked on different aspects of the medical devices business. I have been involved in product development, quality assurance, and regulatory compliance. These experiences have given me a broad understanding of the industry and the challenges that companies face. I have learned about the importance of innovation and how to bring new products to market efficiently.

One key lesson I learned is the importance of continuous improvement. In my role, I always try to find ways to make processes better and more efficient. I have implemented several improvements that have resulted in cost savings and increased productivity. This mindset of always looking for ways to improve has become a fundamental part of how I approach my work.

I am also interested in the business side of the industry. While my background is primarily technical, I have started to develop an interest in understanding how companies make strategic decisions and how business strategy impacts innovation and growth. I believe that an MBA will help me develop the business skills and knowledge that I need to take on more strategic roles in the future.

My professional journey so far has been rewarding, but I feel that I have reached a point where I need to broaden my horizons. An EMBA from SP Jain would provide me with the opportunity to develop a more comprehensive understanding of business and management. I am excited about the prospect of studying in a global classroom and learning from professors and classmates with diverse backgrounds and experiences.

I am confident that an MBA will help me achieve my career goals and enable me to make a more significant impact in the medical devices industry.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
Last year, I made a decision that cost our company roughly ¥8 million—and I still think it was the right call.

I manage supply chain operations for a mid-sized Japanese e-commerce retailer with about 200 employees. We source from smallholder suppliers across Southeast Asia—farmers and artisans, mostly. Last spring, our largest supplier in Vietnam informed us that to meet our volume demands, they'd need to expand into protected forest land. The financial impact was real: they could lower costs by 18% and we'd hit our margin targets.

But our sustainability-conscious customers—roughly 35% of our base—had explicitly chosen us because of our environmental commitment. Our CEO wanted the cost savings to fund aggressive advertising in new markets. Our sustainability officer wanted a public commitment against deforestation. The Vietnamese supplier needed an answer within two weeks.

I spent a week in Hanoi talking to the supplier face-to-face. They weren't trying to deceive us; they were genuinely caught between market pressures and land limitations. We mapped alternatives together and found three smaller suppliers willing to co-produce at slightly higher costs. It required us to redesign logistics, train new partners, and absorb the margin hit.

Internally, I presented this not as a feel-good choice, but as a risk calculation. I showed our CEO that losing 35% of our customer base—our most engaged segment—would cost far more than ¥8 million. For our sustainability officer, I was honest: this wasn't a complete solution, just the best option available to us right now.

The hardest part wasn't the analysis. It was accepting that I couldn't fully satisfy anyone. The CEO got slower growth than wanted. The supplier lost a major contract. Our sustainability officer had to admit our expanded supply chain made monitoring more complex.

What I learned is that stakeholder balance isn't about finding the perfect compromise. It's about transparency, making a defensible choice, and living with the tradeoffs. Since then, our customer retention has actually improved, and we've built relationships with suppliers genuinely invested in our success.

I'm drawn to SKEMA's Global Executive program specifically because I need to understand these dynamics across different cultural contexts. Working with Japanese suppliers differs fundamentally from Vietnamese partners—trust, communication, risk tolerance all shift. I want to deepen that cross-cultural capability before moving into more senior roles.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
Dear Admissions Committee,

I am writing to express my strong interest in the MBA Program at Renmin University Business School. I am confident that I would be an ideal candidate for your program, and I am excited about the opportunity to pursue advanced business education at your institution.

I am a retail and e-commerce professional with two years of experience working in the fast-paced world of online retail. Throughout my career, I have developed a strong passion for business and a commitment to continuous learning. I believe that an MBA from Renmin would provide me with the knowledge and skills necessary to advance my career and make a meaningful impact in the business world.

My professional background has given me valuable insights into the challenges and opportunities in the retail sector. Working in e-commerce has exposed me to the complexities of managing online operations, customer relations, and business strategy. I have learned the importance of adaptability and innovation in a constantly evolving industry. These experiences have motivated me to pursue further education to deepen my understanding of business principles and develop stronger leadership capabilities.

I chose to apply to Renmin University Business School because of its excellent reputation and commitment to business excellence. Your program's focus on developing well-rounded business professionals aligns perfectly with my career goals. I am particularly interested in the curriculum and the opportunity to learn from experienced faculty and interact with classmates from diverse backgrounds.

Throughout my education and career, I have always strived to be a dedicated and hardworking individual. In university, I maintained a commitment to my studies while balancing other responsibilities. At my current position in retail management, I have consistently demonstrated my ability to work effectively in teams and contribute to company objectives. I take pride in my work ethic and my ability to overcome challenges.

Beyond my professional responsibilities, I am an active individual who enjoys staying engaged with my community. I believe in the importance of continuous self-improvement and personal development. I am excited about the prospect of joining a diverse cohort of students and contributing to the classroom discussions and group projects.

The MBA program at Renmin represents a crucial step in my professional journey. I am committed to making the most of this opportunity and fully engaging with the curriculum, faculty, and my peers. I am confident that my background in retail and e-commerce, combined with my strong work ethic and determination, makes me a valuable addition to your program.

I believe that an MBA will equip me with the strategic thinking and analytical skills needed to progress into senior leadership roles. I am particularly interested in how business strategy and operations management can be applied to drive growth in the retail sector. Your program's emphasis on these areas is one of the key reasons I am applying.

In conclusion, I am a dedicated and motivated professional who is ready to commit fully to the MBA program at Renmin University Business School. My passion for business, combined with my professional experience and my commitment to personal growth, makes me an ideal candidate for your program. I would be grateful for the opportunity to contribute to your institution and to develop the skills necessary for a successful career in business.

Thank you for considering my application. I look forward to the opportunity to discuss how I can contribute to your program.

Sincerely,
[Applicant Name]

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I graduated from Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México in 2014, my father handed me the keys to our family's textile distribution business and said, "Now you'll learn what business really is." The company, which we've operated for 28 years in Mexico City, was generating about $8 million in annual revenue but felt stuck in its ways. My father was a brilliant operator—he knew every client, every supplier, every detail of production. What he didn't know was how to scale systematically or think beyond regional markets.

Over the past decade, I've built what works in family businesses: relationships, operational discipline, and deep industry knowledge. I've streamlined our supply chain, reduced defects by 32%, and helped us enter the Central American market. But I've also hit the ceiling of what accumulated experience can teach me. At 32, running a business with 45 employees and a relatively flat structure, I realize I'm missing fundamental frameworks in finance, strategy, and organizational design. When we tried to formalize our accounting three years ago, we made expensive mistakes. When we considered acquisitions, I felt like I was gambling rather than analyzing.

I'm applying to Keio's Master of Management because I need the tools to take our business to the next level, and because Keio understands the unique challenges of operating in Asia. Mexico and Asia share more than I expected—family business models are dominant, relationships often matter more than contracts, and formal business education isn't as prevalent. Your program's emphasis on practical management rather than pure finance appealed to me more than the theory-heavy programs I researched. I also need the rigor: a structured curriculum where I'll actually have to defend assumptions and learn financial modeling properly.

What I'll bring to Keio is perspective from a market that most of your cohort won't have experienced directly. I've navigated currency fluctuations, worked with suppliers across three countries, and managed the tension between innovation and tradition that defines family businesses throughout Latin America. More importantly, I'm genuinely curious about how management differs across regions. I've read about Japanese manufacturing excellence and kaizen principles, and I want to understand whether these concepts transfer to emerging markets, or if we're importing templates that don't fit our reality.

I'm not applying to Keio to escape Mexico or launch a startup in Tokyo. I'm applying because I want to return to my family business as a better operator. My father is now 68, and within five years, I'll likely be the primary decision-maker rather than his successor learning the trade. That shift requires more than intuition. It requires frameworks for financial planning, talent development, and strategic growth that I don't currently possess.

I recognize my GMAT score might suggest I'm not ready for graduate-level work. The test isn't my strength—I work better with real problems than abstractions. But I have a track record of execution, and I'm disciplined enough to show up and do the work. I'm not the student who will debate theory for theory's sake. I'm the one who will ask, "How do we actually use this?" and push our discussions toward practical application.

I'm ready to learn what my ten years of hands-on experience cannot teach me.

prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
When we launched the remote monitoring system at Medtronic's São Paulo facility in 2019, I wasn't the project manager. That role went to someone with the title. My job was regulatory affairs coordinator—basically making sure we didn't violate Brazilian health codes while everyone else built the product.

Six months in, the project was stalling. The engineering team and clinical team couldn't agree on data transmission protocols. Engineers wanted real-time streaming; clinicians said it created liability nightmares. The PM kept scheduling meetings that went nowhere. People just retreated to their corners.

I didn't have authority to tell anyone what to do. But I had spent four years understanding both sides of the company—I'd worked in clinical before moving to regulatory. So I did something small: I started having individual conversations. Not meetings. Coffees with the lead engineer, lunch with the chief clinician. I asked questions instead of proposing solutions. Why does streaming matter to you? What keeps you up at night?

What emerged was that they weren't actually disagreeing on the technology. The engineer needed to hit a Q4 deadline because investors were watching. The clinician was terrified of liability lawsuits. Once I understood the real constraints, I drafted a hybrid approach—streaming during initial setup and configuration, then switching to batch uploads during normal operation. It satisfied both timelines and risk profiles.

But here's what actually changed things: I brought the proposal to them separately first. I let each one think they'd influenced it. I'm not proud of that small manipulation, but I learned it mattered more than being right. The PM eventually presented the solution in the next steering committee meeting. It went through.

The system launched on schedule and became our second-largest revenue driver in Brazil.

The lesson wasn't about clever negotiation. It was that influence without authority requires something harder than authority itself—genuine curiosity about what others actually need, not what you think they should want. It taught me I'm better at moving things through relationships than through hierarchy.

prompt: Describe a difficult decision you made and the process that you went through to make it.
content:
In 2019, I had to decide whether to leave Doctors Without Borders after eight years, or stay and turn down the role I'd actually been working toward.

I'd joined DWB's UK office as a program coordinator in a fog of purpose—I needed a job, and the mission called to me in that abstract way it calls to a lot of 24-year-olds. But somewhere between logistics in South Sudan and rebuilding supply chains in the Central African Republic, it stopped being abstract. I was good at the operational side. By my seventh year, I'd moved into senior management, overseeing emergency response logistics across four regions with a team of twelve.

The opportunity came in late 2018: head of operations for the entire Africa division. More money, more authority, the title that would look brilliant on a CV. I wanted it desperately.

Then I actually thought about it.

The role would have been 80% desk work in our London office. I'd have traded field trips and real contact with our teams for quarterly reviews and stakeholder management. And here's the bit I'm embarrassed to admit: I realized I'd been convincing myself for years that "moving up" meant moving into management. That the only way to have impact was to climb higher. But sitting in that café in Khartoum, where I'd just spent a week troubleshooting a warehouse collapse with our local team, I felt something I hadn't felt in an office setting in years: like I was actually solving something.

The hardest part wasn't the decision—it was admitting what it meant. It meant I probably wouldn't become the deputy director everyone assumed was my trajectory. It meant slower career progression, less prestige. It meant potentially looking back at this moment as a missed opportunity.

But it also meant I got to spend the next three years deepening expertise in something I actually loved: operational resilience in fragile contexts. I took on projects that smaller organizations would never trust to me. I built methodologies for rapid supply chain assessment that DWB still uses. I mentored younger coordinators instead of managing senior staff. And honestly? I was happier.

By 2022, though, something had shifted again. I realized I'd gone as far as I could without a strategic lens I didn't possess. I could optimize systems brilliantly, but I didn't understand finance well enough to advocate for budgets. I couldn't translate my operational expertise into organizational strategy. The ceiling wasn't glass—I'd just hit it.

That's what's brought me to an Executive MBA. I don't want to manage through crisis anymore. I want to build systems that anticipate it. And I need the financial and strategic frameworks to do that credibly in the NGO sector.

This decision—choosing depth over advancement, then recognizing when depth alone wasn't enough—taught me something that I think a good MBA should reinforce: that career progression isn't a ladder. Sometimes it's staying put. Sometimes it's moving sideways. The only progress that matters is understanding yourself well enough to know the difference.

Desautels' focus on organizational leadership and social impact strategy appeals to me precisely because it bridges that gap. I'm not looking to leave the nonprofit sector. I'm looking to lead it more effectively.

prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
# On Failure and Learning

Failure is often considered a stepping stone to success, and I have learned this lesson firsthand throughout my professional journey. During my time in investment banking, I encountered a significant failure that fundamentally changed how I approach challenges and interact with my team.

Two years ago, I was leading a team of four analysts on a major M&A transaction for a tech company valued at approximately $150 million. This was one of the largest deals our Madrid office had handled, and I was responsible for managing the client relationships, ensuring timely delivery of all due diligence materials, and overseeing the financial modeling. I believed I had the capability to handle everything independently without delegating tasks effectively to my team members.

The project began smoothly, but as we progressed into the intensive phases, I realized that my lack of delegation was creating bottlenecks. I was working late nights, reviewing every document personally, and making all critical decisions without consulting my team. This approach, which I thought demonstrated my commitment and competence, actually undermined the team's potential and morale. My team members felt sidelined and unable to contribute meaningfully.

When we reached the final stages of the transaction, a critical error in the financial model was discovered—an error that could have derailed the entire deal. Upon investigation, I realized that this mistake occurred because I had not properly trained my team or trusted them with important analytical work. If I had delegated effectively from the beginning, multiple team members could have reviewed the model, and this error likely would have been caught earlier.

The client was disappointed, and while the deal ultimately proceeded, we faced reputational damage and the client expressed concerns about our team's reliability. This was a humbling moment for me. I recognized that my inability to trust and delegate had not only failed the immediate objective but had also failed my team members who wanted to grow and contribute meaningfully.

Following this experience, I fundamentally changed my leadership philosophy. I invested time in understanding my team members' strengths and weaknesses, I delegated critical tasks with appropriate oversight, and I created a culture of transparency where team members felt comfortable raising concerns. In subsequent transactions, this approach led to better outcomes—not just in terms of deal quality, but also in team satisfaction and retention.

This failure taught me that true leadership is not about doing everything yourself, but about enabling others to succeed. It reinforced that in investment banking, where pressure is constant and stakes are high, the ability to build and trust a capable team is essential. This lesson will be invaluable in my MBA journey and beyond, as I aim to develop into a more thoughtful and effective leader.

prompt: Statement of Purpose (SOP)
content:
# Statement of Purpose

When I first joined McKinsey ten years ago, I thought my career trajectory was clear: make partner, climb the hierarchy, and eventually lead a practice. I was efficient, analytical, and good at the work. But somewhere between case studies on supply chain optimization and presentations to C-suite executives, I realized I was missing something fundamental about what I actually wanted to build.

The inflection point came three years ago while working on an operational transformation for a financial services client in Lagos. We'd reduced their cost structure by 18%, restructured their teams, and the metrics looked excellent on paper. But sitting in their office after the final handoff, watching them scramble to maintain momentum without our team, I felt hollow. I'd delivered value, yes, but I hadn't built anything that would outlast my presence. I hadn't developed their people or helped them build the capability to solve the next problem themselves.

That realization pushed me to shift how I work. I started staying longer on engagements, spending more time coaching junior consultants, deliberately building frameworks that clients could use independently. On a recent engagement with a West African telecom, I led a post-merger integration project but made it a point to co-develop a playbook with their internal team rather than just hand them recommendations. They've already used that playbook to guide two subsequent integrations. That matters more to me now than the fee we billed.

But here's what I've discovered: my impact is still constrained by the consulting model itself. I'm a trusted advisor to these organizations, but I'm still ultimately an outsider looking in. And the business fundamentals that would let me evaluate whether my recommendations are actually sustainable — whether they'll work in three years, not just three months — those are areas where I lack depth. I understand P&Ls at a surface level from client work, but I've never owned one. I've advised on pricing strategy without managing the trade-offs when you actually implement it. I've recommended organizational structures without living in the political and human dynamics of making them real.

I need to spend two years genuinely understanding how businesses work from the inside, not just how they present themselves to consultants. That's why I'm pursuing an MBA now. I'm not looking to pivot entirely away from consulting — the problem-solving part of it energizes me — but I want to build a more complete toolkit for creating sustainable change. I want to be able to think like an operator, not just an analyst.

Great Lakes appeals to me specifically because of your program structure. The cohort-based approach and the emphasis on peer learning feels right for where I am — I've spent a decade talking to clients, but I haven't invested enough in learning from peers who are grappling with different versions of the same problems. Your EMBA format also lets me keep working, which matters. I'm not ready to step away entirely, and frankly, I think the best learning will come from applying classroom concepts immediately to real situations.

I'm also drawn to your program's focus on leadership and organizational behavior. That's been the gap in my toolkit — I can optimize a process or restructure a team, but I haven't deeply studied how to lead through uncertainty or how to actually shift organizational culture. Those are muscles I need to develop if I want to move from "trusted advisor" to someone who can genuinely lead transformational change.

I know an MBA won't solve everything. But I think two years of structured learning, combined with a cohort of people pushing themselves to grow, is what I need right now to move from giving advice to taking the kind of real accountability for outcomes that will actually fulfill me.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
When I think about leadership, I think about making a difference in people's lives. This is what has shaped my management philosophy over the years. During my time at a mid-sized healthcare organization in Milan, I had the opportunity to lead a team of seven nurses and administrative staff, and this experience taught me the true meaning of being a leader.

At the beginning, our department was struggling. Patients were not satisfied, morale was low, and there were many issues with communication between team members. I realized that something had to change. I decided to implement several new processes and systems to improve how we worked. I introduced weekly team meetings, created a new scheduling system, and tried to be more collaborative with my team members.

Over time, things improved. Patient satisfaction scores went up. The team seemed happier. I felt like we had accomplished something meaningful together. This experience showed me that leadership is about caring for your people and working towards a common goal. It taught me that a good leader should be someone who listens, who is fair, and who wants to help others succeed.

Through this experience, I developed what I am calling my "people-first" management philosophy. I believe that employees are the most important asset of any organization. If you take care of your people, invest in their development, and create a positive work environment, then the business results will follow naturally. This is what I have tried to apply in my subsequent roles at other healthcare facilities.

I am now working as a Senior Operations Coordinator at a larger hospital in Rome, where I oversee quality and compliance issues. In this role, I continue to practice what I learned during my leadership experience in Milan. I try to mentor the younger staff members, I encourage open communication, and I focus on building a culture of continuous improvement.

I believe that management is ultimately about people. It is not about implementing fancy new systems or chasing the latest business trends. Real leadership comes from understanding what motivates people, from being honest and transparent, and from having a genuine interest in their wellbeing and career growth. A good manager should lead by example and should not be afraid to roll up their sleeves and do the work alongside their team.

This is the philosophy that I bring to every role I take on, and it is what I hope to strengthen further through an MBA program. I want to develop my skills in strategic thinking and data-driven decision making, but I never want to lose sight of the human element that is at the core of good leadership.

prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
