When I was promoted to Brand Manager at Henckels Consumer Goods in Chicago, I thought I'd finally cracked the code. I'd spent three years in the role's assistant position, delivered solid campaign results, and had strong relationships with the team. I was ready.

My first major initiative was a product line extension for our best-performing snack brand. We had market research showing demand, competitor analysis pointing to a gap, and I was confident in the strategy. I pushed hard on the timeline—we needed to hit shelves by Q3 to capitalize on back-to-school season. Looking back, I was driving decisions rather than building consensus. I made assumptions about what retailers wanted without doing the work to actually ask them. I even glossed over some supply chain flags my operations contact raised because they didn't fit my narrative.

The product flopped. Hard. We missed sales targets by 40%, and worse, Whole Foods rejected our premium positioning entirely—something my operations colleague had warned could happen if we didn't develop stronger supply partnerships first. The company took an inventory write-down, and I spent weeks in meetings explaining how I'd missed basic strategic elements.

The lesson wasn't revolutionary: I needed to listen more than I talked. But understanding something intellectually and changing your actual behavior are different things. So I did something uncomfortable. I asked my operations contact to essentially shadow me on the next project. Not as my assistant—as my peer reviewer. I sat in strategy meetings and asked questions I didn't know the answers to. I scheduled calls with three key retailers before finalizing the go-to-market plan, not after.

Our next launch hit 89% of forecast, and it was noticeably stronger because I'd built it with input from people I'd initially overlooked. I realized that being a good manager wasn't about having the best ideas—it was about making space for the right ones to surface.

I want to pursue an MBA because I know there's more structural learning ahead. I need frameworks for scaling teams, understanding finance more deeply, and recognizing blind spots earlier. Lyon's management sciences focus appeals to me because I'm looking for rigor, not just case studies. I'm ready to be the person in the room who asks good questions.

prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
I've spent the last two years analyzing market inefficiencies for a living, but the biggest inefficiency I've discovered is my own approach to risk.

At Handelsbanken's asset management division in Stockholm, I managed a Nordic small-cap equity portfolio. The job suited my analytical strengths perfectly—I could spend hours dissecting financial statements, building models, testing hypotheses. My team knew me as meticulous, perhaps annoyingly so. I once delayed a portfolio recommendation by a week because I wanted to verify a single line item in a company's cash flow statement. My manager called it "rigor." Looking back, it was often just fear dressed up as diligence.

The real shift came when we were evaluating Biomeditech, a mid-cap medical device company trading at what I calculated was a 30% discount to intrinsic value. Every model I ran said buy. But the CEO was new, the supply chain was concentrated in one region, and the company had never raised capital in the public markets. Low uncertainty environments are my comfort zone. This was decidedly not.

I spent two weeks analyzing that company. I read every interview the CEO had given. I called three competitors' IR contacts, disguising my questions as market research. I even visited their facility in Västerås, which wasn't standard practice—mostly because I knew my conviction needed to come from something beyond spreadsheets.

What struck me wasn't anything revolutionary. The CEO was transparent about the risks. The supply chain issue wasn't being hidden; it was disclosed in plain language. The business fundamentals were genuinely strong. But I realized I'd been using the absence of certainty as a reason for inaction. Not because the risks were unmanageable, but because managing them required me to hold conviction with incomplete information.

We recommended the position at 4% of the portfolio. It was my recommendation, pushed through despite my lingering hesitation about what I didn't know. That decision—not because I suddenly became confident, but because I decided confidence wasn't the entry requirement—shaped how I think about decision-making now.

That position returned 47% over 18 months. That's the part that gets mentioned in performance reviews. But the more important return was recognizing that my strength in analysis can become a liability when it's paired with an excessive need for certainty. I was using rigor as a shield against the vulnerability of being wrong.

I'm drawn to HKUST because this realization makes your environment particularly relevant to me. Asia's markets move with a velocity and opacity that don't reward paralysis-through-analysis. I've been reading about HKUST's emphasis on emerging markets and entrepreneurial thinking, and I suspect the program would challenge me to operate more comfortably in ambiguity. My Swedish banking background is in markets where information efficiency is relatively high; Hong Kong is not Stockholm.

I also want to spend two years in an environment where I'm not the person who already has all the frameworks figured out. There's comfort in being the analyst everyone trusts. But there's more growth in being the person still learning to trust herself.

The numbers in my application—the GMAT, the GPA, the track record at Handelsbanken—those capture my ability to execute within structure. They don't capture what I'm actually trying to build: the judgment to act decisively when structure runs out. That's what I'm coming to business school to develop. And that's what I believe HKUST's program, with its emphasis on Asian markets and entrepreneurial thinking, is uniquely positioned to teach someone like me.

school_name: University of Ghana Business School Executive MBA
prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
Describe the Achievement You Are Most Proud Of

Throughout my eight years working in the non-profit sector, I have had the privilege of making meaningful contributions to society. However, the achievement I am most proud of is my work in helping to establish a community development program in my region that has positively impacted the lives of thousands of people.

When I started working at my organization, I noticed that there were significant gaps in services being provided to underserved communities. Many people in rural areas lacked access to basic resources and opportunities for growth. I saw this as a problem that needed to be addressed, and I became passionate about making a difference in these communities.

I took the initiative to develop a comprehensive program that would address these needs. Through my leadership and vision, I was able to bring together various stakeholders including government agencies, local leaders, and community members. This collaborative approach was essential in ensuring the success of the program. I worked tirelessly to secure funding, develop program frameworks, and train staff members to implement the initiatives effectively.

The results have been remarkable. Since the program's launch three years ago, we have reached over 5,000 beneficiaries across multiple communities. The program has created employment opportunities, improved access to education, and provided skills training to vulnerable populations. I take great pride in knowing that my efforts have contributed to tangible improvements in people's lives.

What makes this achievement particularly meaningful to me is not just the scale of impact, but the relationships I have built along the way. Working closely with community members has taught me invaluable lessons about resilience, adaptability, and the true meaning of service. I have learned that sustainable development requires more than just good intentions—it requires commitment, strategic thinking, and genuine partnership with the communities we serve.

This experience has reinforced my belief that education and professional development are crucial tools for driving meaningful change. I have witnessed how skilled and knowledgeable leaders can transform organizations and communities. This realization has motivated me to pursue an MBA, as I believe advanced business knowledge will enable me to scale my impact and contribute more effectively to solving complex social problems.

The Executive MBA program at University of Ghana Business School is particularly appealing to me because of its focus on African leadership and context-specific solutions. I am confident that this program will provide me with the strategic and analytical skills necessary to advance my career in the non-profit sector and continue making a positive impact on society.

school_name: Stockholm School of Economics Executive MBA
prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
Two years ago, my father asked me to fire our company's longest-serving employee. After 23 years, Gonzalo had built half of our textile manufacturing operation in Trujillo. He was also stealing from us—systematically, methodically, for at least eight months before the auditors caught it. Around $120,000 USD.

My initial reaction was anger. Not just at the theft, but at the position it put me in. I'd recently taken over operations from my father, and this was supposed to be my moment to prove I could run things differently. Cleanly. Professionally. Instead, I was facing something messier than any Harvard Business School case study.

My instinct was the corporate one: document everything, involve HR, process the termination. My father expected the same. He'd been betrayed and wanted swift justice. But I couldn't stop thinking about Gonzalo's daughter, who worked in our packaging department. About how a single decision from me would reshape someone's entire family.

So I did something that probably would have horrified my business school professors, if I'd had them then. I spent a Saturday afternoon at Gonzalo's home, uninvited. We sat in his living room for three hours. He didn't deny anything. He told me his wife had medical bills. His son had gotten involved with the wrong people and needed money to get out. He was desperate, not malicious. That distinction mattered to me, even though it didn't change what he'd done.

I decided to press charges—but with conditions. The company would recover the money through a repayment plan deducted from his final severance over two years. We wouldn't prosecute if he complied. He'd resign immediately, and I'd help him find work with a supplier we trusted. His daughter would keep her job, and the family's health insurance would continue for six months.

Gonzalo agreed, though I could see the shame in his face. My father disagreed sharply. He saw it as weakness, as dangerous precedent. We didn't speak for three weeks. But eventually, I explained that the precedent I wanted to set wasn't about letting theft slide—it was about understanding that people are more complicated than their worst decisions. That sometimes leadership means holding someone accountable *and* preserving their dignity.

Gonzalo paid back most of the money within eighteen months. His daughter eventually became our shift supervisor. And my father? He came to see that approach as something between naive idealism and hard pragmatism—exactly the tension I think I've been navigating for the last decade in family business.

An Executive MBA program at Stockholm, where I can study governance, ethics, and how to lead across cultures and generations, feels like the natural place to sharpen those instincts.

school_name: Mannheim Business School Master in Management
prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
In my ten years working in retail and e-commerce, I have faced many challenges that tested my resilience and determination. One particular setback stands out as a pivotal moment in my career.

Two years ago, I was promoted to Regional Manager at a mid-sized online retailer where I was responsible for overseeing operations across three states. I was excited about this opportunity and felt confident in my ability to lead the team. However, within six months, my region was underperforming significantly compared to other regions. Sales had dropped by approximately 15%, and customer satisfaction scores were declining. Senior management was concerned about my performance.

This was a difficult time for me. I had invested so much energy into this role, and it felt like I was failing. I remember sitting in my office after a particularly harsh performance review, wondering if I had made a mistake taking on this responsibility. The setback made me question my leadership abilities and whether I was cut out for a management position.

Rather than giving up, I decided to take action. I spent time analyzing what had gone wrong. I realized that I had been too focused on implementing new processes and systems without properly engaging my team. I had not listened to their concerns or valued their input. This lack of communication had created tension and disconnection within the department.

I made several changes. First, I started having regular one-on-one conversations with team members to understand their challenges and gather their feedback. I created a more collaborative environment where people felt heard and valued. I also simplified some of the processes I had implemented too hastily, incorporating suggestions from the team.

Within six months, the situation improved dramatically. Sales increased by 12%, and our customer satisfaction scores rose significantly. More importantly, I noticed the team morale had improved considerably. People seemed more engaged and motivated.

This experience taught me valuable lessons about leadership and humility. I learned that being a good leader is not just about having ideas and implementing them quickly. It requires listening, understanding your team, and being willing to adjust your approach. I learned that setbacks are opportunities to grow and improve.

This experience has shaped how I approach challenges today. I am more reflective and seek feedback regularly. I understand the importance of building strong relationships and creating psychological safety within teams. I also learned that failure is not final – it is a stepping stone to success.

This is why I am interested in pursuing an MBA at Mannheim. I want to deepen my understanding of leadership, strategy, and organizational behavior. I believe the program will provide me with frameworks and tools to become a more effective leader and drive meaningful results in my future roles.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
**What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?**

Seven years ago, I made a decision that still defines my career: I walked away from a comfortable role as Head of Acquisitions at a mid-sized British production company to launch our digital content division from scratch. We had £2.3 million in budget, a skeletal team of three people, and absolutely no track record in the space. My managing director called it "ambitious." I called it terrifying.

The traditional media world doesn't move fast. We greenlight projects eighteen months in advance. We measure success in quarterly earnings. But in 2017, short-form video platforms were exploding, and we were losing younger audiences by the month. The board saw risk. I saw the only path forward.

The first eighteen months were brutal. We produced dozens of test pieces—YouTube shorts, TikTok content, web series pilots—most of which landed with audiences that could generously be called "small." My team was stretched thin. I remember one week where I was simultaneously pitching a YouTube partner program, editing a rough cut at 11 PM, and realizing we'd massively miscalculated production costs on a six-episode series. It would've been easy to declare the experiment a failure and shuffle back to traditional programming.

But we kept iterating. I brought in someone from the digital advertising world who understood creator relationships differently than we did. We started co-producing with emerging creators rather than treating them as distribution channels. We learned to fail faster and cheaper rather than perfectioning everything before launch.

By year three, our digital division was generating 28% of company revenue. More importantly, we'd shifted how the entire company thought about audience engagement. The traditional acquisition team started learning from our metrics. We weren't just producing content—we were building a direct relationship with viewers, understanding their preferences in real-time, and adapting accordingly.

The accomplishment itself is measurable: we grew from £2.3 million to a £12 million revenue stream. We retained most of our initial team, which I'm genuinely proud of given how uncertain things were early on. We've produced content for Netflix, Amazon Prime, and YouTube that's reached over 300 million views collectively.

But honestly, what matters more to me is what it taught me about leadership and uncertainty. I learned that you can't plan your way out of disruption—you have to embrace it. I learned that surrounding yourself with people smarter than you in specific areas (digital marketing, creator strategy, analytics) matters more than being the smartest person in the room. And I learned that vulnerability—admitting when we were wrong, when costs spiraled, when an approach wasn't working—actually built stronger team cohesion than pretending everything was under control.

This is also why I'm seeking an MBA. I've built something meaningful within one company, but I want to understand how to scale this kind of thinking across different industries and markets. I want to lead bigger teams through transformation, not just survive it. Bern's curriculum appeals to me because of its emphasis on strategic management alongside digital innovation—it feels designed for someone trying to bridge the traditional and the new, rather than abandon one for the other.

prompt: Please describe any significant updates to your candidacy since your last application, including changes in your personal or professional life, additional coursework, or extracurricular/volunteer activities.
content:
Since my initial application, I've taken on significantly more responsibility at Perez Manufacturing. I was promoted to Senior Production Supervisor overseeing three shifts and 45 team members across our facility in Phoenix. This role has forced me to develop the management and communication skills that I realize were gaps in my first application.

I've also completed two online courses—Operations Management and Business Analytics—through Arizona State University's continuing education program. My GMAT score improved to 653, which better reflects my analytical ability than my earlier attempt.

On a personal level, moving to the US from Tel Aviv two years ago has shifted my perspective. Initially, I came to gain operational experience in manufacturing. But working here, I've realized I want to return to Israel eventually and help modernize the tech-heavy manufacturing sector there. The US focuses heavily on automation and efficiency metrics, and I want to bring those practices home.

An MBA from Marshall would give me the strategic foundation I'm missing. Right now, I can manage operations effectively, but I struggle with understanding the financial side of major decisions and how to think about supply chain investments across multiple facilities. The program's focus on operations and finance seems aligned with what I need.

I'm more committed to this application because I now have concrete goals rather than just wanting to "advance my career." I understand what I'm missing and why an MBA specifically matters to me.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
When I think about ethical dilemmas in consulting, the first thing that comes to mind is the importance of integrity and doing the right thing. During my five years at Deloitte, I have encountered several situations where my values were tested, and I believe these experiences have shaped who I am today.

One particular situation stands out. I was working on a project for a financial services client, and our team was tasked with analysing their operational efficiency. During the engagement, I discovered that one of our senior consultants had made an error in the data analysis that would significantly impact the client's decision-making. The numbers we presented didn't fully reflect the reality of the situation, and I knew this could lead to poor strategic choices on their part.

This was a challenging moment. On one hand, I was worried about the implications of raising this issue publicly. The senior consultant was well-respected, and I didn't want to create conflict within the team. I was also concerned about how this might affect my standing within the firm. On the other hand, I felt a strong sense of responsibility towards our client and to the profession of consulting itself.

I decided that the right thing to do was to speak up. I approached the senior consultant privately first, explaining what I had found and asking if they had noticed the same thing. It turned out they had not caught the error themselves. Rather than making it confrontational, I framed it as a collaborative opportunity to strengthen our work before presenting to the client. Together, we corrected the analysis and updated our recommendations.

The client ultimately made a different strategic decision based on the corrected data, and they were grateful for our thoroughness. The senior consultant appreciated me bringing this to their attention professionally, and it actually strengthened our working relationship.

This experience reinforced my belief that ethical behaviour isn't just about following rules—it's about having the courage to do what's right, even when it's uncomfortable. In consulting, our credibility is everything, and I would rather face short-term discomfort than compromise my integrity or our client's trust.

I believe that an MBA programme like MIT Sloan would help me develop the leadership skills to navigate these complex situations more effectively in my future career.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
In five years, I want to be advising government leadership on defense procurement strategy at the policy level—not just executing decisions made by others, but shaping them. Right now, I oversee a $180 million budget for tactical equipment across our Lima operations, managing everything from vendor negotiations to end-user training. It's substantial work, and I'm good at it. But I've realized the constraint isn't my capability in this role. It's my framework. I can optimize a supply chain or cut costs by 12 percent, and I do. But I can't answer why we're building the supply chain that way in the first place, or how geopolitical shifts should reshape our entire procurement philosophy. That gap is what's driving me to Columbia.

Five years out, I see myself in a role where economic literacy and strategic business thinking are non-negotiable. Maybe that's in the Office of the Secretary of Defense. Maybe it's a policy think tank. Maybe it's advising a transition administration on how to restructure defense operations for the next decade. What connects those options is the same thing: I need to speak the language of business and strategy—real strategy, not the watered-down kind—fluently enough that I can translate between the operational world I come from and the decision-making world where budgets get approved and priorities get set.

I've spent six years as a military officer moving steadily upward. The trajectory is clear, the competencies are teachable, and I can probably make general officer if that's what I want. But watching my current boss struggle to influence procurement decisions because he didn't understand cost accounting well enough to push back on vendor proposals—that shook me. He was operationally brilliant. He was strategically blind. I don't want to be that person in ten years.

Columbia's Executive MBA is the specific vehicle I need. Not because I need credentials (I don't), but because your program actually teaches business fundamentals to people with real operational experience, which is rare and valuable. Most MBAs teach business to people who've never built anything. Your program teaches it to people who've built things but lack the financial and strategic frameworks to operate at scale in civilian contexts. That's my gap exactly.

The structure appeals to me too. I can't leave my job for two years—that's not realistic given my family situation and my commitment to my unit. But Friday through Sunday, I can do. I can spend my weeks shipping out procurement analyses and my weekends wrestling with case studies about competitive strategy and organizational design. That's actually harder in some ways, but it's honest about what I can commit.

What I'm drawn to specifically is your focus on policy and leadership. The International Business and Policy electives matter to me. My background is Latin American operations, and I've seen firsthand how policy decisions made in Washington ripple through partner nations in ways that create either stability or chaos. I want to understand the business economics underneath those decisions. I also want to build a network of people who understand both worlds—the military mind and the business mind. Columbia's cohort, given its geography and student profile, is full of those people.

Here's what's honest: I'm not coming to Columbia to find myself or discover a passion. I know what I want. I'm coming to sharpen the tools I'll need to do it well. The military taught me discipline, operations, and how to move large organizations. What it didn't teach me—what it deliberately doesn't teach—is how to think like a strategist in a resource-constrained, competitive market environment. That's what these two years are for.

In five years, I want to be the person in the room who can tell leadership what something actually costs, what it should cost, and what we should do about the gap. Columbia teaches you how to think that way. That's why I'm applying.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I began my career in São Paulo as a junior analyst at a regional healthcare distributor, managing inventory systems for a 40-person supply chain team. The work was straightforward—optimizing warehouse logistics, reducing costs—but I realized quickly that I was solving problems without fully understanding the business side. So I moved into operations management at the same company, where I spent four years learning how procurement decisions affected our margins and customer relationships.

That foundation led me to a larger opportunity in 2016 when I joined Grupo Saúde Brasil, a pharmaceutical wholesaler with about 500 employees. I was brought in to lead a regional division serving 15 states in the Northeast. My initial charge was to improve distributor profitability, which was underperforming by roughly 12%. I restructured our sales approach, implemented a new customer tiering system, and renegotiated supplier contracts. Within two years, we'd increased EBITDA by 18% and expanded our customer base by 30%.

But success can be deceptive. By 2019, I'd become comfortable—competent at executing the playbook I'd developed, but not pushing myself intellectually. I realized I was operating in a bubble. I didn't understand healthcare economics at a systemic level, and I had no exposure to digital transformation that was beginning to reshape the industry. Most importantly, I hadn't developed the strategic thinking needed to move into executive leadership.

That recognition prompted a shift. I took on a project leading the company's entry into specialized pharmaceutical distribution—a new market segment requiring partnerships with hospitals and clinics rather than traditional wholesalers. It failed. We underestimated regulatory complexity and overestimated our ability to compete against established players. We pivoted after 18 months and absorbed a 2 million reais loss.

But the failure taught me more than my earlier successes. I learned I needed structured exposure to global business frameworks, data analytics, and strategy—not just operational execution. I've spent the past two years consolidating my role while staying alert to the next challenge. I speak fluent English and some Mandarin. I'm ready to think bigger.

An MBA from ESSEC would bridge what I know and what I need to know—particularly ESSEC's strength in international business and digital transformation in healthcare.

school_name: KEDGE Business School Master in Management
prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
# Leadership Experience

During my six years working in supply chain and logistics, I have had many opportunities to develop as a leader. One experience that shaped my management philosophy was when I led a team during a challenging project.

In my previous role at a large logistics company in Lima, I was promoted to manage a team of eight people. My manager gave me responsibility for overseeing daily operations and ensuring that we met our targets. This was a significant moment in my career because I had never managed people before.

At first, I tried to be a very strict leader. I thought that if I set high expectations and pushed people hard, they would work better. However, I quickly realized that this approach was not working. My team members seemed unhappy, and some of them were making mistakes. I realized that my management style was not effective.

I decided to change my approach. Instead of being very strict, I tried to be more understanding and supportive. I started having one-on-one meetings with each team member to understand their challenges and what they needed from me as a leader. I listened to their feedback and tried to incorporate their ideas into how we worked. I also tried to create a more positive work environment where people felt valued.

This experience taught me that good leadership is about understanding people and creating an environment where they can do their best work. I learned that being a leader is not just about giving orders or pushing people to meet targets. It is about building relationships and trust with your team.

Through this experience, my management philosophy became focused on communication, listening, and creating psychological safety. I believe that when people feel heard and supported, they are more motivated and productive. I also learned the importance of being flexible and adapting your leadership style based on what your team needs.

This understanding has been very important in my career. It has helped me become a better manager and has made me realize that I want to continue developing my leadership skills. I believe that an MBA program like KEDGE will help me deepen my understanding of management and learn new frameworks and tools that I can apply in my career.

In conclusion, my leadership experience at the logistics company shaped my management philosophy in important ways. I learned that good leadership requires understanding people, listening to feedback, and creating a positive work environment. I am excited about the opportunity to study at KEDGE and to continue my journey as a leader and manager.

prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
When I joined Akçay Manufacturing in Istanbul as a Production Coordinator four years ago, I quickly realized that my role didn't give me direct authority over the quality control team. However, I was determined to improve our defect rates, which were affecting customer satisfaction.

I noticed that the QC team was using outdated inspection methods that were missing critical issues. Rather than complaining to management, I decided to take initiative. I started attending their daily meetings and suggested we implement a new checklist system. At first, they were resistant to changes, but I persisted.

I created a comprehensive inspection checklist based on best practices I had researched online. I presented it to the team and explained how it would make their work easier and more efficient. Over time, they saw the value in what I was proposing and agreed to trial it for two weeks.

The results were positive. We reduced defects by 15% in the first month. The QC team appreciated that I had listened to their concerns and incorporated their feedback into the final process. This experience taught me that leadership is not about having a title or authority, but about influencing people through collaboration and trust.

I learned that when you want to make changes, you need to understand the perspective of those affected. The QC team initially resisted because they felt their expertise was not being valued. By showing respect for their knowledge and involving them in the solution, I was able to build a strong relationship with them.

This experience was important for my development as a leader. It showed me that you can create positive change even when you don't have formal power. I realized that the best leaders are those who can inspire others to work toward a common goal.

I believe an MBA will help me develop my leadership skills further and prepare me for roles where I will have more responsibility. I am interested in operations management and want to work for a large manufacturing company eventually. XLRI's strong focus on leadership development and its curriculum in operations management aligns well with my career goals.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
When I started at Moretti & Partners in Milan in 2015, I was the kind of junior associate who stayed until 9 PM just to organize partner schedules. I had my law degree but no real sense of what I wanted to become. I could draft contracts well enough, but I felt like I was following a script that had been written for me—law school, big firm, make partner someday, retire. It felt hollow.

The turning point came in 2017 when a client, a mid-sized manufacturing company, asked if our firm could help them restructure their operations after losing a major supplier. The senior partner assigned the work to me. What surprised me wasn't the legal complexity—it was realizing that the real problem wasn't contracts or compliance. The company needed to understand their cost structure, renegotiate supplier agreements strategically, and figure out which divisions were actually profitable. I was a lawyer trying to solve what was fundamentally a business problem.

I started teaching myself Excel. I asked the client's CFO for their P&Ls. I built models to show them where their margins had eroded. We eventually restructured their supplier agreements and helped them divest a loss-making division. The senior partner noticed the work was unusually thorough. More importantly, I noticed I'd spent sixty hours on something I actually cared about.

From 2017 to 2019, I deliberately took on client work that touched business fundamentals—M&A transactions where I studied the buyer's strategy, employment disputes where I learned about organizational design, regulatory matters where I understood the business impact of compliance costs. My peers thought I was making strange career choices for a lawyer. Maybe I was. But I was building a different skill set.

In 2019, I moved to London to join the legal department of Brenntag Italia—a €3.2 billion FMCG company operating across Europe. The company offered me the role because they wanted a lawyer who understood operations, not just legal risk. I spent three years managing a portfolio of 40+ supplier contracts worth €180 million annually, but I was also in meetings about supply chain optimization, margin pressure, and competitive positioning. I learned that a contract is worthless if the commercial terms don't work. I restructured our supplier portfolio, which saved the company roughly €4.2 million over two years, though honestly, I only understood later that I should have quantified this impact earlier.

By 2022, I realized I'd hit a ceiling. I could become general counsel someday, but that felt like continuing the script in a different font. The business side of what I'd been doing—the P&Ls, the strategic negotiations, the operational challenges—that was what genuinely engaged me. I didn't want to be the lawyer in the corner office. I wanted a seat at the table where strategy gets made.

That clarity has been my north star for the last two years. I've taken on a broader portfolio here at Brenntag—supply chain finance, working capital optimization, and most recently, leading a cross-functional initiative around digital procurement. I still have the title "Senior Legal Counsel," but I'm functioning more like an operations-focused business partner. It's given me real experience in managing budgets, coordinating teams across functions, and seeing how decisions cascade through an organization.

Wisconsin's MBA appeals to me precisely because it treats business as integrated, not siloed. I'm not trying to become someone else—I'm trying to formalize and deepen what I've been learning the hard way. I want to understand finance and operations rigor that I've picked up piecemeal. I want to be around people building strategy, not protecting against risk.

I also want to eventually lead operations or strategy in a mid-cap industrial company, probably back in Europe. I know what the work feels like now. I just need to learn how to lead it.

school_name: Shanghai Jiao Tong Antai MSc Management
prompt: What are your short-term and long-term professional goals? How will our MBA program help you achieve them?
content:
What are my professional goals? I have always been passionate about making a difference in the business world. Throughout my career in consulting, I have gained valuable experience that has shaped my ambition to become a leader in the global business environment.

My short-term goal is to transition into a management consulting role that focuses on digital transformation and operational efficiency. I believe that the modern business landscape requires professionals who can bridge the gap between strategy and execution, and this is where I want to position myself. In the medium term, I hope to lead consulting projects for Fortune 500 companies across different regions and industries.

My long-term vision is to become a senior executive, possibly a Partner at a consulting firm or a Chief Operations Officer at a multinational corporation. I want to leverage my technical skills and business acumen to drive organizational change and help companies achieve their strategic objectives. I am particularly interested in working with companies in Asia, as I see tremendous growth potential in emerging markets.

Throughout my eight years at a Big 4 consulting firm in Mexico, I have worked on various projects involving process improvement, cost optimization, and strategy development. I have led teams, managed client relationships, and delivered results that have impacted the bottom line. However, I recognize that my current skill set has limitations. I lack formal training in financial modeling, advanced data analytics, and international business strategy. Additionally, my experience has been primarily limited to Latin America, and I need to expand my global perspective.

This is where the Shanghai Jiao Tong Antai MSc Management program is the right fit for me. The program offers courses in strategic management, financial analysis, and global business operations that will directly support my goals. The international exposure in Shanghai will allow me to build a network in Asia and understand the nuances of doing business in this region. Additionally, the program's focus on digital transformation aligns perfectly with my professional interests.

Furthermore, studying at an institution in China will give me firsthand experience in one of the world's most dynamic economies. I will be able to understand market trends, consumer behavior, and competitive dynamics in Asia firsthand. This knowledge will be invaluable as I work toward becoming a leader in international business.

The diverse student body at Antai will also enrich my learning experience. I am eager to collaborate with classmates from different backgrounds and industries, exchange ideas, and develop a global network that will support my career aspirations. I believe that the combination of rigorous academics, international environment, and strategic location makes this program an ideal stepping stone toward my goals.

In conclusion, my professional journey has prepared me well for advanced management education, and I am confident that the MSc Management program at Shanghai Jiao Tong Antai will equip me with the skills, knowledge, and network necessary to achieve my short-term and long-term objectives. I am ready to make the most of this opportunity and contribute meaningfully to the program's community.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
When I look at my GMAT score of 588, I know it doesn't reflect my actual capabilities. I spent six weeks preparing, worked with a tutor, and still walked out of that test center frustrated. The truth is that standardized testing has never been my strength — I overthink the logic problems, second-guess myself on verbal reasoning, and my timing management falls apart under pressure. But in my actual work evaluating investment opportunities and analyzing market data, I don't have these constraints. I wanted to acknowledge this gap directly because I think context matters.

My real story is in what I've built over the past four years at Crescent Venture Partners in Riyadh. I started as an analyst and was promoted to senior analyst after eighteen months, managing a portfolio of twelve companies across fintech and enterprise software. Last year, I led due diligence on a Series B investment in a logistics startup that has since grown to a $180 million valuation. That deal required me to dig into unit economics, customer acquisition costs, and competitive positioning — the analytical rigor that matters in business, not test-taking reflexes.

What I've realized is that my academic foundation, while solid (3.0 GPA), doesn't capture how I actually think. I'm someone who learns by doing. I didn't understand cap tables until I had to model them. I didn't grasp SaaS metrics until I had to explain them to LPs. This is both my limitation and my strength — I need direct application to lock in concepts.

An MBA is something I've been thinking about for two years now. Initially, I thought of it as credential-building, something to check off before moving into founder relations or moving back to the US. But the more senior I've become, the more I see gaps. I've never taken a formal finance course beyond what I picked up on the job. I don't have structured thinking around operations or organizational behavior — things that feel increasingly important as our portfolio companies scale and I advise CEOs on hiring and structure.

Yonsei specifically appeals to me because of its position in Asia and the growing venture ecosystem here. The Middle East needs better connections to Asian markets, and I see my future potentially bridging that gap — either through investing or eventually advising companies expanding regionally. Your curriculum seems to give that geographic focus while maintaining rigor.

I also want to be honest: my test scores probably worry you. They should, somewhat. But I'd rather you know that I'm aware of that weakness and honest about it, rather than pretend I'm a perfect profile. I'm the person who gets frustrated with multiple choice but builds something real when given a problem. That's not an excuse. That's just who I am, and I think I can contribute something valuable to your program because of it, not despite it.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
I am writing to provide additional context for my application to UTS Business School.

Throughout my career in venture capital, I have developed a passion for supporting innovative startups and helping them grow. Working at Prestige Capital Partners in Paris for the past nine years, I have been exposed to many different sectors and business models. This experience has given me a strong foundation in understanding how markets work and what makes companies succeed.

I believe that an MBA from UTS Business School will help me transition into a leadership role where I can make a bigger impact. The program's focus on innovation and entrepreneurship aligns well with my career goals. I am particularly interested in learning more about business strategy and how to think about problems from a broader perspective.

My GMAT score of 631 does not fully represent my analytical abilities. I took the exam while managing several portfolio companies simultaneously, which was challenging. However, I am confident that I can succeed in the rigorous MBA curriculum because I have consistently demonstrated strong performance in my professional role.

Additionally, I come from France, which I believe brings a unique international perspective to the classroom. Being non-binary, I also value diversity and inclusion, and I hope to contribute to building a more inclusive community at UTS Business School.

I am excited about the opportunity to join your program and grow as a leader. The MBA will give me the skills and network I need to succeed in my next career move, whether that is launching my own fund or taking on a more senior role at an existing investment firm.

Thank you for considering my application. I look forward to hearing from you.

school_name: University of Amsterdam Business School MSc
prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
I spent three years as a logistics coordinator at the Brazilian Ministry of Defense coordinating humanitarian aid operations, which sounds more glamorous than it actually was. The reality involved spreadsheets, frustrated NGO partners, and me often being the person caught between irreconcilable demands.

In 2019, our ministry partnered with two international organizations—Doctors Without Borders and the Red Cross—to deliver medical supplies and food to communities affected by floods in Bahia state. Simple enough on paper. In practice, it became a masterclass in competing interests.

The Ministry wanted to move quickly to demonstrate government responsiveness before the next news cycle. They pushed for logistics routes through Feira de Santana, which was faster but less reliable—the roads flooded regularly. The Red Cross prioritized reaching the most vulnerable populations, which required slower routes through more damaged areas. Doctors Without Borders needed temperature-controlled transport for vaccines and medications, which meant fewer total shipments but stricter handling protocols. And the local government officials wanted distribution centers in politically visible locations, not necessarily where need was greatest.

I was twenty-six, relatively junior, and responsible for making these pieces fit together. I couldn't just tell the Ministry "no" or dismiss the NGOs' concerns. I also couldn't pretend these trade-offs didn't exist.

What I did was build a model that made the tensions visible rather than hidden. I created a simple matrix showing different route options with metrics that mattered to each stakeholder: delivery speed, population reach, cold chain reliability, and political viability. I didn't hide that Feira de Santana was faster but riskier. I showed that the slow routes through damaged areas reached three times more isolated families but took five days longer.

Then I did something that probably wasn't in my job description: I scheduled a meeting where all four groups presented their priorities directly to each other. Not as abstract demands but as actual consequences. The Ministry heard from a Red Cross field coordinator about what happened when medical supplies arrived too late. The NGOs heard about the political pressure the Ministry faced and why visibility mattered for future funding.

We ended up with a hybrid approach. Three fast routes through Feira de Santana for high-priority medical shipments. Two slower routes for general aid that reached more remote areas. A commitment from the Ministry to fund a second rotation after the initial response—buying time for the NGOs' slower approach without delaying urgent care.

The operation wasn't perfect. We still had heated disagreements. But by making everyone's constraints and reasoning transparent, we moved from positional bargaining to collaborative problem-solving.

That's what drew me toward Amsterdam—your program's emphasis on stakeholder analysis and ethical business practice. I'm not interested in MBA programs that teach me to optimize for a single metric. I want to study how to make decisions when values genuinely conflict, in contexts where people are right to care about different things. That feels like the actual work of leadership.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
# Essay Response

When I first considered pursuing an MBA, SKEMA Business School appeared frequently in my research about global executive programs. I've spent the last several months learning more about what the school offers and why it might be the right fit for my career goals.

My research began online, where I reviewed SKEMA's website, read through the program curriculum, and looked at the school's rankings and accreditations. I found that SKEMA has a strong international reputation and offers courses in various business disciplines. The Global Executive MBA program seems comprehensive and well-structured. I also learned that the school has multiple campuses, which indicates a global presence that appeals to me given the international nature of finance.

Beyond the website, I've tried to connect with the school in other ways. I attended one of the virtual information sessions last month where admissions staff discussed the program. I also looked at some LinkedIn profiles of SKEMA graduates to understand what kinds of careers they've pursued after graduation. This helped me see that people from different backgrounds have successfully completed the program.

I'm interested in attending SKEMA because I believe the MBA will help me advance my career in investment banking. Throughout my ten years in this industry, I've worked on various transactions and built strong analytical skills. However, I recognize that formal business education could strengthen my leadership capabilities and provide a broader perspective on business strategy. An MBA from a respected international school would open doors that might not otherwise be available to me.

Being part of the SKEMA community would benefit me in several ways. First, I would gain access to a rigorous curriculum that covers finance, strategy, and other critical business areas. Second, the international student body would expose me to different perspectives and business practices from around the world. Given that investment banking is increasingly global, this kind of cross-cultural understanding seems valuable. Third, I would build a network of peers and alumni who could help me advance professionally in the years following graduation.

I'm also drawn to SKEMA's emphasis on developing responsible leaders. In my current role, I've seen firsthand how business decisions impact stakeholders beyond shareholders. I'm interested in learning more about ethical decision-making and sustainable business practices, which I believe SKEMA emphasizes.

I recognize that my academic background is not as strong as some applicants. However, I've demonstrated my commitment to professional development throughout my banking career and I'm ready to bring that same dedication to my MBA studies. I'm confident that the SKEMA community would benefit from my experience in the financial sector, and I know that SKEMA's program would provide the education and network I need to reach my next career level.

prompt: Why do you intend on pursuing an MBA at this point in your life? Describe your mid-term and long-term visions for your post-MBA career path and how will the Desautels MBA program add value to your future career?
content:
When I was promoted to Supply Chain Manager at Zhong Long Logistics three years ago, I thought I had figured out my career. I managed a team of twelve people, oversaw operations for our Shanghai distribution hub, and hit our KPIs consistently. But then I realized something uncomfortable: I was very good at my job, and I was also stuck.

I could optimize a warehouse layout or negotiate better rates with vendors. What I couldn't do was understand why the company made certain strategic decisions, or how to influence them. When our CFO presented supply chain restructuring plans that I disagreed with—plans that I believed would create inefficiencies downstream—I didn't have the language or credibility to push back effectively. I had domain expertise but not the broader business fluency to operate at the executive level.

That gap is exactly why I'm pursuing an MBA now. I'm at nine years into my career, which is the right time: I have enough experience to know what I don't know, but enough runway ahead to actually use an MBA differently than someone fresh from undergrad.

In the medium term—the next 3-5 years—I want to move into a Supply Chain Director role, ideally at a larger organization. But I want to get there as someone who understands P&L responsibility, finance, and corporate strategy, not just logistics operations. I've already started taking on some business development work at my current company, looking at how supply chain decisions impact overall profitability. An MBA would legitimize that shift and accelerate it.

Long-term, I see myself in a Chief Operations or VP Supply Chain role within ten years. I'm particularly interested in companies that are scaling operations internationally—there's something appealing about the problem of managing global complexity. I don't know if that will be manufacturing, e-commerce, or something else, but I know I want to lead operations at a strategic level, not just manage day-to-day execution.

Desautels specifically appeals to me for a few reasons. First, Montreal's location as a North American logistics hub is directly relevant to my career. I've worked with several Canadian companies, and I understand that region's supply chain ecosystem well. I also know that Desautels has strong connections with companies in the manufacturing and distribution sectors—the industries where I see my future. Your program's emphasis on operations and business strategy aligns with what I actually need to learn, not just generic MBA skills.

I'm also drawn to the cohort diversity you cultivate. A lot of my learning will come from classmates with different functional backgrounds. I've spent nine years around logistics people; I need exposure to finance, marketing, and technology perspectives to round out my thinking. Desautels' international student population—particularly from China—is actually valuable to me, since I'll eventually want to work in Asia-Pacific operations.

Honestly, I know my GMAT score isn't where it should be for a top-tier program. I'm not a natural test-taker, and that number doesn't fully reflect my analytical capability. But I'm confident that my work experience, clear career vision, and specific reasons for wanting an MBA right now are stronger than what a test score shows. I'm ready for this next chapter, and I think Desautels is the right place to take it.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
When I started working at my first tech company in Cairo eleven years ago, I thought an MBA was for people who wanted to work at McKinsey or Goldman Sachs. I coded, I shipped products, and I thought that was enough. But somewhere between building features and managing teams, I realized that my technical skills alone couldn't answer the questions I was actually wrestling with.

I've worked in three startups across Egypt and the UAE, watching founders make decisions without financial literacy, seeing promising companies collapse because of poor operations, and experiencing the frustration of being in rooms where I had good ideas but lacked the vocabulary to push them forward convincingly. That's when I understood why I needed an MBA—not as a credential, but as a tool to be more effective at what I already care about.

My GMAT score of 591 doesn't reflect how I think about complex problems. I struggled with that test format, the pressure, the clock. But ask me to model financial scenarios for a company, to understand unit economics, to think through a go-to-market strategy—that's where my thinking sharpens. I've spent the last three years as a senior manager at a B2B SaaS startup in Cairo, building a team of twelve across engineering and product, managing a budget of $800K, and helping us grow from $2M to $6M in ARR. Those projects required exactly the kind of systems thinking an MBA teaches. I just learned it on the job instead of in a classroom.

What's harder to explain on an application is that I'm not sure what comes after the degree yet. I could go into founding, consulting, or scaling operations at a growth-stage company. The program appeals to me because the EMBA format lets me stay engaged with real work while finally getting structured exposure to finance, strategy, and the broader business toolkit I'm missing. And honestly, the cohort from the region matters. I want to learn alongside people building things in markets I understand, where problems are different from Silicon Valley case studies.

I know I'm not your typical candidate. My path has been nonlinear, and my test scores aren't exceptional. But I'm serious about this degree because I've seen exactly where I'm limited, and I'm committed to closing those gaps.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
Over the past eight years, I've built my career at the intersection of product development and business strategy—first at a Cairo-based fintech startup, and more recently leading product operations at a Saudi-focused logistics tech company. My GMAT score of 601 doesn't fully reflect my analytical capabilities, and my 3.3 undergraduate GPA was earned while working full-time, but these numbers don't capture what I've actually accomplished or what drives me forward.

When I started at my current company four years ago, we had 12 people and no real product roadmap. I came in as a product manager and spent the first three months just understanding what we were building and why. We were hemorrhaging users—our churn rate was around 45% month-over-month. I did a lot of customer interviews, probably spoke to 60 or 70 logistics operators across Riyadh and Jeddah, and realized we were solving the wrong problem. Our customers didn't want another tracking tool; they wanted visibility that actually reduced their operational costs.

We rebuilt significant parts of our platform. I pushed hard for features that the engineering team thought were too complex, and I was wrong about some of them. One particular feature around predictive delivery windows cost us two months of development time and wasn't adopted the way I'd hoped. But we learned from it. By year two, churn dropped to 18%, and we grew to about 300 active logistics companies using our platform. Revenue scaled from roughly 2 million SAR annually to over 12 million.

What's interesting is that this growth happened largely because I started thinking like a business person, not just a product person. I began sitting in on finance calls, understanding our unit economics, thinking about customer acquisition costs versus lifetime value. I realized I had gaps in my business knowledge that were limiting my impact. I could optimize a product roadmap, but I couldn't strategically think about market expansion or how to position us for investment or acquisition.

I'm considering an MBA now because I want to move into a general management or strategy role, ideally within the tech ecosystem in the Middle East. I see companies here scaling rapidly, but they're often missing structured business thinking. The market is growing fast—maybe too fast—and there's real opportunity for someone who understands both product and business fundamentals.

American University in Dubai appeals to me specifically because it's positioned right here, where I actually want to work. Too many MBA programs position themselves globally, which is fine, but I want to be embedded in the regional ecosystem. I want to study alongside people building companies in Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Egypt. I want to understand the regulatory landscape, the investor landscape, the talent dynamics in a way that's relevant to where I'll actually be working. Your program's emphasis on regional business practice and your connections with GCC companies actually matter to me, not just as a checkbox.

I also need to be honest: my GMAT score frustrates me. I'm a strong analytical thinker, but I didn't prepare as systematically as I should have. I took the exam once, scored 601, and honestly, I probably could have spent another month preparing and gotten into the 650-700 range. I didn't, partly because I was managing a major product release at the time, but that's my own time management failure. I'm someone who tends to prioritize immediate work demands over long-term goals, and I'm aware that's something I need to address in business school and beyond.

What I want you to know is that I'm committed to this transition. I'm not taking an MBA as a credential check. I'm taking it because I've hit a ceiling in my current growth, and I genuinely believe structured business education will help me get to the next level—whether that's scaling my current company, moving to a strategy role, or eventually starting something of my own. I'm ready for this.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
When I think about five years from now, I see myself running a social enterprise that bridges the gap between multinational corporations and community development projects across Southeast Asia. Right now, I'm a Program Manager at a Madrid-based NGO focused on sustainable development, and I've spent the last three years managing small-scale initiatives in rural communities. It's been rewarding, but also frustrating—we're doing good work, but we operate at a small scale, and we're constantly chasing grants. I want to change that model.

The transition I'm planning requires a fundamental shift in how I think about business. I need to understand how to build sustainable revenue streams, manage larger teams, and negotiate with corporate partners. An EMBA makes sense because I'm not starting from zero in my career—I have real work experience and I've seen what works and what doesn't on the ground. But I lack the business fundamentals and the network to scale what I want to do.

CUHK's program appeals to me specifically because of its Asia-Pacific focus. I've worked in Central America and Sub-Saharan Africa through my NGO, but my next chapter needs to be in this region. The economics are different here, the corporate landscape is different, and the challenges facing communities are different. Learning alongside executives already embedded in Asian markets—whether they're from Hong Kong, Singapore, or mainland China—gives me access to exactly the kind of peer network I need to build my enterprise. I'm not interested in a generic MBA; I need one rooted in this specific context.

I'm also drawn to CUHK's emphasis on responsible leadership. This isn't just marketing speak for me—it's fundamental to what I want to build. A social enterprise only works if it's actually solving problems, not just extracting value. Your curriculum on governance and stakeholder management seems designed for exactly the kind of hybrid model I'm pursuing.

Five years out, I want to have launched this enterprise, secured initial corporate partnerships, and proven the model works in at least two countries. The EMBA won't make me an entrepreneur—I already know how to work in constrained environments and build relationships—but it will give me the financial literacy and strategic toolkit to think bigger than I currently can.

school_name: Zhejiang University School of Management EMBA
prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
# Classroom and Campus Contribution

When I moved to Milan from Rome twelve years ago, I made a deliberate choice to seek out industries and environments where I didn't already speak the language. Not Italian—that was fine. I mean the language of how business actually works in competitive, high-growth sectors. I've spent my career in Italian and European media companies, and while I've built real expertise in content strategy and audience analytics, I've realized my perspective has become somewhat provincial. An EMBA at Zhejiang would expose me to entirely different business models, different regulatory environments, and—most importantly—different ways of thinking about problems.

This awareness will make me someone who asks uncomfortable questions in class.

I've noticed that people who've worked in only one region, or one industry ecosystem, often don't realize their assumptions are assumptions. I've done this myself. For three years at Mediaset, I was convinced that our declining streaming subscriber numbers were a content quality issue. I kept pushing for more premium original series. My Korean colleague finally asked: had we considered that our pricing was simply too high relative to local purchasing power? It wasn't. We repositioned the entire product, and subscriber acquisition reversed within six months. I was humbled, but I learned that the best insight often comes from someone who isn't trapped by your industry's conventional wisdom.

Coming to Zhejiang, I expect to be that person frequently. My background in European media dynamics will occasionally be useful—I can probably speak intelligently about how regulation shaped our market development. But more valuable will be what I don't know. I want to push back on case studies that assume Western consumer behavior models. I want to ask questions about how Chinese competitors think about mobile-first business models that European companies still haven't figured out. I want to learn what I'm currently too unconscious to even know I'm missing.

I also know I work best in teams that include people genuinely different from me. I don't mean this in a performative way. I mean that the last three years, working on a remote team split between Italy, Poland, and Singapore, taught me that friction—real friction, not just different opinions—forces you to actually justify your thinking. My Polish colleague structured financial models completely differently than I did. Initially, I thought it was inefficient. Eventually, I realized his approach was more conservative and caught risks I would have missed.

I'm coming to Zhejiang not just to learn content about business strategy, but to be productively uncomfortable. I'll contribute by bringing European market context when relevant, by asking "why are we assuming that?" when I notice unconscious premises, and by actively seeking collaborations with classmates whose backgrounds challenge me most directly.

That discomfort is where I learn best, and I believe it's where good class discussions happen too.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
# Career Progression Essay

My journey in venture capital began eleven years ago when I graduated with a degree in business administration and decided to pursue a career in finance. Since then, I have worked in various roles within the investment industry, gaining valuable experience and developing a strong understanding of how businesses grow and succeed.

When I first started my career, I worked as an analyst at a mid-sized investment firm in São Paulo. This role taught me the fundamentals of financial analysis and due diligence. I spent my days reviewing business plans, building financial models, and preparing investment memos for senior partners. It was here that I learned the importance of attention to detail and thorough research when evaluating potential investments. After three years, I felt ready for a new challenge and decided to move to a larger firm.

My next position was as a senior analyst at a larger investment company where I was responsible for managing a portfolio of early-stage companies. During my time there, I worked with founders and CEOs to help them scale their businesses. I learned how to identify promising startups and understand market trends that could impact their success. This experience was instrumental in helping me develop a more strategic perspective on investment decisions. I spent approximately five years in this role before seeking new opportunities.

Currently, I work as an investment manager at a venture capital firm focused on technology startups. My responsibilities include sourcing deals, conducting due diligence, and supporting portfolio companies with their growth strategies. I have been in this position for three years and have contributed to the success of several companies that have gone on to achieve significant valuations and successful exits.

Throughout my career, I have developed strong skills in financial analysis, risk assessment, and stakeholder management. I have learned to work effectively with diverse teams and understand the importance of communication and collaboration in achieving business objectives. I have also gained exposure to different sectors and industries, which has broadened my perspective on market dynamics and investment opportunities.

One important lesson I have learned is that success in venture capital requires both analytical rigor and people skills. While financial models and data are important, the ability to build relationships and understand founder psychology is equally critical. I have learned this through my interactions with many talented entrepreneurs and through my own experiences evaluating and supporting their companies.

My decision to pursue an MBA at Tel Aviv University stems from my desire to deepen my knowledge of business strategy and leadership. While my eleven years of practical experience have given me valuable insights, I believe an MBA will provide me with a more comprehensive understanding of business fundamentals and help me develop my strategic thinking skills. Additionally, I am interested in expanding my professional network and learning from faculty and peers who bring diverse perspectives and experiences.

The MBA program at Tel Aviv University is an excellent fit for my goals because of its strong reputation in entrepreneurship and innovation. I am particularly interested in the courses on venture capital and startup strategy, which will complement my practical experience in the field. I also see value in the international student body and the opportunity to build connections with professionals from around the world.

In conclusion, my career in venture capital has been marked by progressive responsibility and increasing impact. I am confident that an MBA will equip me with the tools and knowledge necessary to take on more senior leadership roles and make meaningful contributions to the investment industry. I am excited about the prospect of studying at Tel Aviv University and am confident that I will be a valuable member of the MBA cohort.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
In five years, I want to be running a regional operations team for a retail or e-commerce company, probably somewhere in Southeast Asia. Not the CEO—I'm being honest about that. But someone who actually understands how a business works end-to-end, not just my corner of it. Right now at Savvy Co., I manage a small team handling supply chain coordination for our Australian online business. We're doing about $45 million in annual revenue, and I've learned a lot in two years, but mostly how much I don't know.

That's actually why I'm applying.

When I started at Savvy, I thought supply chain was just logistics. Move products, track inventory, hit delivery targets. My manager pulled me into a board presentation last year about our profit margins and suddenly I realized why our CEO cares so much about last-mile delivery costs. It wasn't an abstract business problem—it directly affected whether we could expand into new regions or invest in technology. I realized I was solving problems in isolation without understanding the financial and strategic implications.

I've done pretty well in my current role. We reduced our returns processing time by 30% over eighteen months, which sounds technical, but it freed up about $200K annually that our CFO actually redistributed to marketing. That's when it clicked for me—operations aren't separate from strategy. They're inseparable. And if I want to lead at a regional level, I need to understand accounting, how to read financial statements, strategic planning, the whole picture.

My GMAT score of 603 isn't exceptional. I know that. I'm a solid student but not naturally gifted at standardized tests. My undergraduate GPA was 3.8 because I actually care about understanding things properly—I don't do well with superficial learning. I think that matters more than my test score, and I hope you see it that way too.

Why University of Cyprus specifically? I've looked at several programs in Australia, Singapore, and Europe. Cyprus appeals to me because you sit in this interesting position between Europe and the Middle East and Asia. I grew up in Melbourne, but I'm genuinely considering where I want to work long-term, and Southeast Asia feels like where the energy is right now in retail and e-commerce. Your program has strong connections into that region—I saw the partnerships with companies in Dubai and your alumni working across the Middle East. But also, your focus on sustainable business practices actually matters to me. Savvy Co. has started thinking more seriously about our supply chain's environmental impact, and I want to learn how to embed that thinking into operations strategy, not just add it on top.

Your curriculum structure appeals to me too. I like that the program is intensive but doesn't stretch over two years. I can't justify leaving Australia for much longer than that—my family is here, and honestly, I want to get back into the workforce while what I've learned is fresh. The case study approach works for how I learn. I'm not someone who sits well in lectures just absorbing theory. I need to apply it.

The real goal is this: in five years, I want to lead a team that feels like they understand why they're doing what they're doing. I want to make decisions that balance operational efficiency with financial reality with strategic vision. I've seen enough of my current company's senior leaders operate in silos to know that's not the kind of leader I want to be. Your program strikes me as serious about teaching integrated business thinking, not just functional expertise.

I'm ready to invest a year intensively in understanding business at a level I can't reach on my own timeline at Savvy. Then I want to go back and actually do something with it.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I started my career at the wrong company and stayed for three years trying to prove otherwise. Out of college, I joined a mid-sized manufacturing firm in Ohio as a junior operations analyst. The work was solid—process optimization, supply chain basics, the fundamentals. But I kept waiting for the moment to feel invested, and it never came. Looking back, I think I was waiting for permission to care.

When my family's business needed help, I moved home. We run a specialty textiles company that's been around for 35 years, doing about $15M in annual revenue with a team of 40 across two facilities. My role started loosely—my dad needed someone who could talk to younger suppliers and understand their technology better. Within six months, I'd taken on operations fully, then expanded into sourcing and eventually business development.

The transition from an anonymous analyst at a large company to someone actually responsible for outcomes was uncomfortable. In my first year, I pushed hard to modernize our supplier relationships and cut costs. We saved $400K annually, which felt significant until I realized we'd damaged relationships with vendors who'd been with us for decades. I learned that scale is relative, and in a 40-person business, every decision has a face.

Over the next five years, I rebuilt those relationships while maintaining efficiency gains. More importantly, I started thinking beyond my specific areas. I realized our pricing model hadn't been updated in eight years. Our sales process was completely manual. We had zero data infrastructure. These weren't operations problems—they were business problems, and they required thinking across functions.

I've grown into a general manager role, though the title is still informal. I run operations, I'm involved in strategic planning, I oversee our financial planning process, and I work directly with my father on succession and growth strategy. But I'm hitting the limits of what I can do on my own. I understand the business deeply now, but I know I have significant gaps in financial strategy, organizational development, and how to scale beyond our current model.

That's why I'm pursuing the EMBA. I need structured perspective on the decisions I'm making and language to think about problems beyond my industry. I need to know what I don't know.

school_name: University of Tübingen MSc Management and Economics
prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
My first contribution to your classroom will be honest discomfort with many of the case studies we'll analyze.

Working in medical devices for the past two years at Stryker, I've learned that business school textbooks often strip away the human stakes embedded in real decisions.
