When we discuss pricing strategies or market entry, I'll likely push back—not to be difficult, but because I've sat in meetings where a 15% cost reduction on orthopedic implants meant we could expand access to underserved markets in West Africa. But that same reduction pressured our supply chain team and created quality control risks nobody really wanted to own. These aren't clean case scenarios.

I think this is valuable for discussions. Most of my classmates will have worked in consulting, finance, or tech—fields where the metrics are relatively straightforward. I can offer a different lens: one where "efficiency" sometimes conflicts with regulatory compliance, where innovation cycles are measured in years not quarters, and where your customer might be a 65-year-old woman in Lagos who will live another 25 years with your product inside her body.

Beyond classroom contributions, I want to be genuinely useful to the program's culture. I've noticed many international applicants come from similar backgrounds—finance or tech hubs, English-speaking countries, relatively privileged pathways. I'm not attempting false modesty when I say my background is different. I grew up in Ikoreja, Lagos. My father was a pharmacist. I came to university with a gap of nearly two years because my family couldn't afford tuition immediately after secondary school. These aren't hardship stories I lead with, but they do shape how I see problems.

What this means practically: I'll participate actively in supporting international students navigating the Germany transition. I've already moved countries twice for work—once to Dubai and now considering my options post-MBA. I understand the peculiar anxiety of bureaucratic systems in unfamiliar languages, the small humiliation of not understanding cultural references, the exhaustion of constantly translating your own background for others.

I'm also realistic about what I'll get wrong. My GMAT is strong, but my undergraduate record isn't—I was working 20 hours weekly during my first year, and it affected my grades. I sometimes speak too directly in meetings when I should listen more. I rushed my previous job transition and missed red flags that became problems later.

But I learn quickly. I ask for feedback, and I actually implement it. In my current role, I moved from product engineer to a commercial strategy position within 18 months by filling gaps I identified in market analysis. I did this partly because I wanted to understand the business better, and partly because staying in the same lane felt like slow death.

That's the energy I'll bring to Tübingen: genuine curiosity mixed with practical skepticism, an international perspective that's still grounded in specific context rather than abstraction, and a real commitment to showing up for classmates who need it.

school_name: University of Auckland Business School Executive MBA
prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
Three years ago, I was project manager at a solar installation company in Medellín. We were completing a 2.3 MW rooftop system for a manufacturing facility—a project that represented 18 months of planning and was our largest contract at the time. Two weeks before handover, our electrical engineer discovered that the inverter specifications didn't match what we'd promised in the contract. The unit we'd installed was slightly less efficient, though still compliant with local regulations.

The situation was straightforward financially: replacing it would cost roughly $40,000 and delay completion by four weeks. The client was already anxious about the timeline. Our CEO was pushing to avoid delays at any cost. He suggested we simply "accept the minimal difference" and move forward.

I couldn't do that.

What bothered me wasn't just the technical dishonesty—it was that I'd be asking the client to unwittingly accept less value than they'd paid for. They were a manufacturing company trying to reduce operating costs. That inverter difference would cost them approximately $8,000 annually over its lifespan. They deserved the choice.

I scheduled a meeting with the CEO and presented it differently. Instead of framing it as a sunk cost problem, I showed him the client's perspective: they'd budgeted for specific performance metrics, and we'd change those unilaterally without consent. I also outlined reputational risk. In Colombia's renewable energy sector, we depend on client references. One unhappy client could cost us far more than $40,000 in future business.

He disagreed initially but eventually agreed to disclose the issue to the client.

The conversation with the client was uncomfortable. But they appreciated the honesty. We negotiated a compromise—they accepted the existing inverter in exchange for a 12% price reduction, which they calculated was fair based on lifetime efficiency loss. We delivered on time using the original timeline, and they became one of our most reliable repeat clients.

I learned that ethical choices aren't usually binary. The resolution wasn't about suffering the full cost of correcting our error. It was about transparency, letting the client make an informed decision, and recognizing that trust is harder to rebuild than problems are to prevent.

This experience shaped how I approach leadership. I want to study at Auckland because your EMBA curriculum emphasizes stakeholder ethics and sustainable business practice—values that feel essential as our industry scales.

prompt: When thinking of your own personal successes, were they a result of your own personal skills or your ability to work with others? Please describe.
content:
I'd have to say it's genuinely both, though I didn't always understand that.

When I joined McKinsey four years ago, I was convinced my success would come from being the sharpest person in the room—fastest analysis, smartest recommendation, cleanest slide deck. I thought that's what got people promoted. So I worked obsessively. Stayed late to perfect analyses. Owned the technical work end-to-end.

Then I got staffed on a major transformation for a Middle Eastern telecom company, and everything changed. My senior partner—someone I'd only worked with once before—pulled me into a pre-engagement meeting and asked me directly: "Why are you so afraid of asking for help?" I was thrown. I said I wasn't. He said, "Your last project manager told me you rewrote her entire workstream because you thought you could do it better. You were probably right. But you made her feel useless."

That hit differently. Because he was right. I'd been the blockers to my own team's development, and I didn't even realize it.

I spent that engagement deliberately doing things differently. I asked my team for their insights before jumping to conclusions. I challenged myself to say "I don't know, what do you think?" at least once per day. When a junior analyst had an idea I initially disagreed with, I asked her to walk me through her logic instead of dismissing it.

The engagement went better than any I'd been on. The client noticed our team's cohesion. My partner noticed that I'd grown as a leader.

My real success has come from understanding that my technical skills don't matter much if I'm working in isolation. The best outcomes I've delivered have been because I finally learned how to get the best out of the people around me—to build with them, not around them. That's what I want to develop further at Haskayne.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
When I learned about Nord University Business School, I was immediately impressed by its reputation and commitment to excellence. I have spent considerable time researching your institution and I am confident that an MBA from Nord would be the perfect next step in my career journey.

I first discovered Nord through its website and was struck by the comprehensive curriculum and world-class faculty. The program's focus on practical business skills aligns perfectly with my professional goals. I have also read several articles about Nord's alumni network and the success stories of graduates working at major corporations around the world. This confirmed my belief that Nord is the right choice for me.

As an accountant with eight years of experience, I have developed strong analytical and technical skills. However, I recognize that to advance into senior leadership positions, I need to develop a broader business perspective. An MBA will provide me with the knowledge and credentials necessary to move into management roles and eventually become a CFO or corporate executive.

Nord's program stands out because of its excellent curriculum. The courses in financial management and strategic planning are particularly appealing to me. I am also excited about the international exposure the program offers, as I am Vietnamese and believe that studying in a global environment will enhance my professional development and help me build an international network.

I am particularly interested in the case-study method that Nord employs in its classes. This approach will help me develop critical thinking and problem-solving skills that are essential in today's business environment. Additionally, I would like to participate in the consulting club and attend networking events to connect with other talented professionals and business leaders.

The diversity of the student body at Nord is another factor that attracts me. Learning alongside students from different countries and backgrounds will broaden my perspective and prepare me for a career in international business. I am committed to contributing to the Nord community by sharing my accounting expertise and unique experiences as a Vietnamese professional.

Finally, I am drawn to Nord's strong commitment to ethical business practices and corporate social responsibility. These values align with my own beliefs about the importance of conducting business with integrity. I am eager to be part of an institution that emphasizes not just financial success, but also positive impact on society.

In conclusion, Nord University Business School represents an ideal fit for my academic and professional aspirations. I am confident that the MBA program will equip me with the skills, knowledge, and network necessary to achieve my career goals. I am excited about the opportunity to become part of the Nord community and contribute to its continued excellence.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
In five years, I want to be managing the supply chain operations for a mid-sized e-commerce company—ideally leading a team of eight to twelve people through the kind of growth that keeps me up at night in the best way possible. Right now, I'm a logistics coordinator at HomeGoods' Newark fulfillment center, and I've watched our peak season operations balloon from 40,000 to 120,000 daily shipments in two years. I know what it takes to scale. What I don't know yet is how to think like the person making decisions that affect the whole network.

That gap is exactly why I'm applying to Rutgers.

I grew up watching my parents run a small import business out of Jersey City—not glamorous, but they taught me that operations is where strategy meets reality. After college, I took the logistics coordinator role thinking I'd get my feet wet and figure things out. Two years in, I've gotten genuinely good at troubleshooting: I redesigned our receiving dock workflow and cut processing time by 18%, cut our damage rate from 2.8% to 1.1%. My manager nominated me for a supervisor track. But when I sat down with her to talk about what comes next, I realized I was thinking tactically about problems that need strategic solutions. Why are we making these trade-offs between speed and accuracy? What's the actual cost-benefit, and who should be deciding this?

That's when it hit me that I needed an MBA—specifically one that doesn't require me to leave my job or move. I'm not at a point in my career where I can pause for two years at Columbia or Wharton, and honestly, I'm not sure those schools are built for someone like me anyway. I have solid experience but I'm early. I have a 702 GMAT, not a 740. What I have is hunger and clarity about what I need.

Rutgers works because it's built for people in my exact position. Your executive format lets me stay in my role while actually deepening my understanding of supply chain management, operations strategy, and finance. Those aren't abstract interests for me—I'll use them Monday morning in my job. The Newark location matters too. I'm not trying to network my way into Goldman Sachs. I want to build relationships with other operations leaders in the tri-state area who are solving problems at scale.

In five years, I'll be that supply chain manager with a seat at the table where real decisions get made. I'll understand the numbers well enough to push back, build a team that trusts my judgment, and run operations that don't just work—that perform. An MBA from Rutgers fits because it doesn't ask me to become someone else or pretend I'm further along than I am. It asks me to level up from where I actually stand.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
When I tell people I'm applying to an MBA program in India while working full-time in Barcelona's retail sector, the first response is usually a confused smile. It's not the obvious path, which is probably why it's the right one for me.

Four years ago, I joined Retailnova—a mid-sized e-commerce platform focused on connecting Spanish and Portuguese independent brands to European consumers—as a merchandising analyst. I was 26, fresh out of my undergraduate degree with a decent GPA and zero business sophistication. My manager, Carmen, threw me into category management for our home goods division. Within six months, we were analyzing why a particular product line was underperforming, and I realized our inventory system was built on assumptions from 2015, not actual customer behavior data.

That realization changed everything for me. I started teaching myself SQL. I taught myself how to build dashboards in Tableau. And I convinced my boss to let me restructure how we tracked seasonal demand. The results were measurable: we reduced overstock by 23% and freed up €180,000 in working capital that first year.

But here's what I'm leaving out: it took me seven months of failures. Wrong database queries. Dashboards no one actually used. I presented to leadership twice and was completely ignored. What kept me going wasn't passion—that's a word I've learned to be skeptical of. It was stubborn curiosity and a genuine desire to understand why things work the way they do.

My GMAT score of 748 doesn't reflect where I started. I scored 680 on my first attempt and was genuinely angry about it. Not because the score was bad, but because I'd studied consistently and still felt unprepared. I enrolled in a structured program, not because I thought I could "fix" myself, but because I wanted to understand where my reasoning was breaking down. It took three months and a lot of humility.

This is relevant to why I'm applying to IIM Jammu specifically. I spent two weeks in India last year for a supply chain conference, and for the first time in my career, I was the person in the room who didn't understand the market. I couldn't predict why a particular e-commerce model would work in Delhi that would fail in Barcelona. I realized my framework for understanding retail was fundamentally European. At Retailnova, we're expanding into India next year—this is not theoretical for me anymore.

I'm not applying to an MBA to become a "better leader" or to check a box. I'm applying because I need to understand how business operates in a market that doesn't follow European rules. I need to work alongside people building companies in emerging economies. I need to understand consumer behavior, supply chains, and regulatory environments through a lens that isn't my own.

IIM Jammu's EPGP program appeals to me because it's not the obvious choice either—it's not ISB or IIMA, and that's exactly why I'm interested. The cohort is practicing professionals, not recent graduates. The program is smaller. The location isn't a major metro, which means students actually spend time in class instead of chasing the Mumbai job fair circuit. From what I've learned about your alumni, they're building things rather than optimizing existing structures.

I'm not a perfect applicant. My undergraduate GPA reflects someone who was figuring out what she cared about. But over the last four years, I've become someone who breaks complex problems into pieces, who gets comfortable with uncertainty, and who believes that the best learning happens when you're genuinely uncomfortable.

I'm ready to be uncomfortable again.

school_name: Yonsei School of Business Executive MBA
prompt: Describe a time when you led a team through a significant challenge. What was the outcome?
content:
# Leading Through Collapse

In early 2019, I was managing a team of seven customer service specialists at Zenith Telecom's Lagos operations center. We handled roughly 40,000 customer calls monthly—the lifeblood of the company's retail division. Then our primary billing system crashed during a software migration, and nothing worked the way it was supposed to.

The first 36 hours were chaos. Customers couldn't check balances, top-up credit, or verify charges. Our team had zero visibility into accounts. We were basically taking calls blindly, reading from outdated spreadsheets, promising callbacks we couldn't guarantee. My manager expected me to "figure it out," which is corporate speak for "this is your problem now." Churn started spiking. Senior management was calling every two hours.

I made a decision that probably saved us from total collapse: I shut down the normal call queue.

My team thought I was insane. But I redirected all incoming calls to a holding message and gave them our direct line. For 48 hours, we worked with IT to manually cross-reference the backup database against customer records—printouts, old transaction logs, whatever we could find. It was tedious and slow. A call that usually took three minutes now took twenty. But customers got real answers instead of guesses.

More importantly, I pulled my team into the strategy. I told them exactly what happened, why the system failed, and what we were trying to do. That's usually executive information you keep from frontline staff, but I needed them to understand this wasn't their fault and wasn't permanent. One person, Ifunanya, suggested we prioritize high-value customers first—the ones generating the most revenue. That actually made sense. Another, Chioma, realized we could validate accounts faster by calling the finance department directly instead of waiting for email responses. Small optimizations, but they added up.

Here's what I got wrong initially: I thought speed was the only metric that mattered. But speed without accuracy would have destroyed customer trust permanently. I had to tell my boss that our average handle time would spike 400%. He hated hearing it, but the alternative—putting incorrect account information back into the system—would have created weeks of problems. Sometimes leadership means defending your team's pace when everything above you is screaming for faster results.

By day four, IT restored partial system functionality. By day nine, we were mostly back to normal. We lost about 2% of our customer base—painful, but the industry average for a crisis like this is 8-10%. My team's accuracy during the crisis was 99.2%. Not a single customer reported billing discrepancies afterward.

The real outcome, though: that team stayed intact. Turnover in customer service at Zenith was usually 40% annually. My group had zero departures for the next two years. Ifunanya was eventually promoted to a supervisor role. When I left Zenith in 2021, three of my original seven had moved into leadership positions.

I learned that people perform best when you give them autonomy, real information, and purpose—not just marching orders. The crisis forced me to trust my team in ways normal operations never would have. I also learned that sometimes the hardest leadership decision is protecting your people from unrealistic external pressure, even when it's coming from above you.

That experience shaped how I've worked since. I moved to a larger role at Horizon Communications in 2021, where I managed operations across two cities and 34 people. The skills I developed during that billing system collapse—transparent communication, collaborative problem-solving, prioritizing accuracy over speed—became my management foundation.

An Yonsei EMBA appeals to me because I want to understand the strategic frameworks behind decisions I've been making intuitively. I want to learn how to scale these lessons across larger organizations and possibly back into the telecom sector in a more senior capacity. I'm ready for that next level.

school_name: SKEMA Business School MSc in Management
prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
# Career Aspirations and How SKEMA Will Help Me Succeed

I have always been passionate about working in the technology industry and making a meaningful impact on people's lives through innovation. Over the past eleven years, I have gained valuable experience in various tech roles, and I believe that pursuing an MBA at SKEMA Business School is the natural next step in my career journey.

My career goal is to become a leader in the tech sector. I want to move into a management position where I can oversee larger teams and have greater responsibility for strategic decisions. Working in startups has taught me a lot about adaptability and problem-solving, but I recognize that I need more formal business education to advance to the next level. An MBA will provide me with the technical knowledge and frameworks that I currently lack.

Throughout my eleven years in tech, I have worn many hats. I started as a junior developer and have progressed to more senior positions. I have worked on various projects that required me to collaborate with different departments. These experiences have shown me that I need a stronger understanding of finance, strategy, and organizational management. SKEMA's curriculum will fill these gaps and prepare me for executive roles.

I am particularly drawn to SKEMA because of its reputation for excellence and its focus on practical, real-world business applications. The school's emphasis on case studies and experiential learning aligns with how I learn best. Additionally, SKEMA's global network will be invaluable as I plan to potentially work internationally in the future. The connections I make with classmates from around the world will open doors that would otherwise remain closed.

The tech industry is constantly evolving, and I want to ensure I stay competitive and relevant. An MBA is increasingly expected for senior leadership positions, and I don't want to be left behind. I have seen peers with MBAs advance faster than those without, and I want to position myself for long-term success. Moreover, the financial benefits of an MBA are well-documented, and I expect a significant return on my investment.

In my current role, I have gained exposure to business operations, but I lack formal training in areas like financial modeling and strategic planning. SKEMA's MSc in Management program offers courses that will directly address these weaknesses. I am especially interested in the strategy and management modules, which will help me develop a more comprehensive understanding of how businesses operate at the enterprise level.

After graduating from SKEMA, I see myself moving into a general management or product management role at a larger tech company or established startup. I would like to manage a team of at least 20-30 people and contribute to high-level strategic decisions. Eventually, I hope to reach a VP or C-level position, though I recognize this will take time and continued hard work.

SKEMA's location and international focus also appeal to me. Being able to study in an environment with diverse perspectives and learning from classmates across different industries and geographies will enrich my education beyond just the classroom curriculum. This exposure to global business practices is something I cannot get in my current role.

I understand that an MBA is a significant investment of both time and money. However, I am fully committed to making the most of this opportunity. I am ready to engage deeply with the material, contribute meaningfully to class discussions, and build relationships with my peers and professors. SKEMA's program will equip me with the skills, knowledge, and network necessary to achieve my goal of becoming a respected leader in the technology industry.

In conclusion, my career aspirations are clear, and SKEMA Business School is the ideal institution to help me get there. I am excited about the opportunity to join your program and confident that it will serve as a catalyst for achieving my professional goals.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I started at the bottom of a struggling family business in Lagos, doing everything badly.

My father ran a distribution company moving FMCG products across Southwest Nigeria—a business that made money but never quite worked. I joined in 2015 as a logistics coordinator, which was partly genuine interest and partly the path of least resistance after university. I was 23 and convinced I'd move to strategy consulting within a year. Instead, I spent my first two years physically riding delivery trucks at 5 a.m., learning that our supply chain had maybe 40% reliability. We'd promise retailers Friday deliveries and show up Tuesday. Nobody was tracking this systematically.

What struck me wasn't the inefficiency—it was that my father knew it was broken but didn't have the language or framework to fix it. He'd been doing business on relationships and gut feel for thirty years. So I started mapping routes, created basic spreadsheets, and pushed him to implement tracking. It was clumsy, but deliveries improved to 78% on-time within six months. I wasn't strategic yet. I was just obsessive.

By 2017, I'd moved into operations manager and became the person who pushed change even when it made people uncomfortable. I digitized our entire order system, which cut processing time from four hours to thirty minutes. I negotiated with a tech vendor to build us a custom app—not because I'm technical, but because I learned enough to ask intelligent questions and wasn't satisfied with off-the-shelf solutions that didn't fit our model.

The real inflection happened in 2018. The business was stable but stalled. I wanted to expand into e-commerce logistics for other retailers, not just manage one company's distribution. My father thought I was mad. We fought about it for months. But I built a pitch deck anyway, approached a bank, and somehow got a line of credit to start a separate logistics unit. It failed initially—spectacularly. We signed on a major retailer, promised 48-hour delivery across Lagos, and couldn't execute. I lost sleep and nearly lost my confidence.

But we learned. We hired better people, rewrote our processes, and rebuilt that relationship over eighteen months of reliable service. By 2021, that unit was generating 35% of group revenue. I'd gone from coordinator to effectively running our expansion strategy, even though my official title was still "Operations Manager."

The gap in my growth became obvious around 2022. I was operational and tactical—excellent at execution, but increasingly frustrated that I couldn't think systematically about market positioning, unit economics, capital allocation. I'd taught myself most of what I knew. I'd read business books and completed online courses, but I kept hitting a ceiling. I could run operations in Nigeria, but I couldn't see the bigger strategic picture. I couldn't speak the language of finance or competitive strategy with any real depth.

That's when I started seriously considering an MBA. Not to escape operations or prove something to others. But because I actually want to build something beyond what I'm doing now—perhaps across West Africa, perhaps in logistics technology. And I know the foundation I have now, while strong, isn't enough.

Insper makes sense because it's where practical operators and strategic thinkers collide, and I need both. I don't want theory divorced from reality. I want to learn how to think bigger while staying rooted in execution.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
When I decided to pursue an MBA, I knew I wanted to study in a region that's experiencing rapid growth and transformation. That's when I discovered American University in Dubai, and I became immediately interested in what your program could offer me.

I've done quite a bit of research on your school. I visited your website multiple times and read through the program curriculum, which seems very comprehensive and focused on global business practices. I also looked at the faculty profiles and was impressed by the caliber of professors teaching at AUD. The fact that you're located in Dubai, one of the most dynamic business hubs in the world, is incredibly appealing to me as someone working in real estate.

Over the past eight years, I've built a career in real estate development and project management. I started as an associate and have worked my way up to managing portfolios worth over $200 million. However, I've realized that to advance to the executive level, I need to deepen my business acumen, especially in areas like finance, strategy, and international markets. An MBA from AUD would give me the credentials and knowledge I need.

What excites me about your program is the global perspective it offers. Real estate is becoming increasingly international, and having an MBA from a school in the Middle East would help me understand markets I'm less familiar with. I'm also interested in taking electives in international business and finance, which I know you offer.

Being part of the AUD community would be valuable for me in multiple ways. First, the networking opportunities would be substantial—connecting with classmates from different countries and industries would expand my professional network significantly. Second, the location itself is a learning opportunity. Living and studying in Dubai would expose me to different business cultures and practices that I could apply to my career back in the United States.

I'm confident that I would be a strong addition to your cohort. My professional experience combined with my commitment to learning would allow me to contribute meaningfully to class discussions and group projects. I'm ready to challenge myself academically and grow as a business leader, and I believe AUD is the right place for me to do that.

school_name: Universidad Torcuato Di Tella Executive MBA
prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# Challenging Conventional Thinking in Drug Development

When I joined Zenith Pharmaceuticals five years ago as a Senior Project Manager, our oncology division was hemorrhaging money on a promising but commercially problematic drug candidate. The compound showed strong efficacy in trials, but the patient population we were targeting was extremely small—perhaps 8,000 patients globally. Our CFO wanted to kill the program. My boss, the VP of R&D, suggested we push forward anyway because we'd already invested $45 million.

Both perspectives were conventional. One said: small market, no ROI. The other said: sunk cost fallacy aside, we've come this far. I questioned both.

I spent two weeks talking to oncologists, patient advocacy groups, and our clinical trial coordinators. What I learned changed how I saw the problem. Yes, the patient population with the exact genetic marker we'd been targeting was small. But there was a much larger population—roughly 60,000 patients—with three different genetic variants of the same cancer type. The variants had similar biology. No one had tried treating them together because the field had always segmented them as separate diseases.

I proposed something radical for our company: instead of advancing a narrow indication, we would redesign our clinical development strategy to test the drug across all three variants simultaneously. This meant changing our trial protocol midway through Phase II, renegotiating with regulatory bodies, and honestly, it was messy and politically unpopular. Our clinical team worried about trial complexity. Finance worried about timeline delays. Some senior leaders thought I was overcomplicating things.

But I brought data. I showed that the incremental cost of expanding the trial was roughly $3 million, versus the $40 million cost of developing a separate program for each variant. I worked with our regulatory affairs team to propose a novel statistical approach to the FDA. It took six months of internal debate and three regulatory meetings, but they approved it.

Three years later, the drug launched with an indication covering all three variants. Our year-one revenue was $78 million, compared to projections of $8-12 million under the original narrow indication. The drug now helps roughly 45,000 patients annually instead of 2,000. I was promoted to Director of Portfolio Strategy.

The lesson wasn't just commercial. I learned that conventional thinking in pharma often reflects organizational inertia, not actual constraints. We segment diseases because that's how we've always done it. We calculate markets based on historical precedent. We rarely ask: what if we're framing the problem wrong?

This experience made me realize I need deeper strategic training. I'm good at questioning assumptions within my company, but I lack formal frameworks for understanding broader business strategy, market dynamics, and how emerging technologies might reshape drug development entirely. I want to move beyond solving problems within existing systems—I want to help design better systems.

An MBA would give me the analytical rigor and business perspective I need to do that at a higher level. I'm drawn to Universidad Torcuato Di Tella because of your emphasis on strategic innovation in emerging markets, and your strong biotech network in Latin America, where I eventually want to help build more efficient pharmaceutical ecosystems.

school_name: University of Crete MSc Business Administration
prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
# Essay: A Creative Solution to Fund Our Programs

During my three years working at a nonprofit organization in Lima focused on youth education, I faced a significant challenge that taught me the importance of creative thinking. Our organization struggled with limited funding, which made it difficult to expand our programs to reach more underserved communities.

The problem was clear: we needed more money to serve more students, but traditional fundraising methods were not generating sufficient resources. We relied mainly on grant applications and occasional donations from individuals, but these sources were inconsistent and unpredictable. Our team of five people was stretched thin, and we could only reach about 200 students per year when we knew there were thousands of children in need.

I decided to implement a new approach. I thought about what we could do differently, and I realized that we should try something creative. I proposed to my director that we could organize a monthly marketplace event where local artisans and small business owners could sell their products, and we would take a small percentage of each sale. This would create a sustainable revenue stream while also supporting local entrepreneurs in our community.

We organized the first marketplace event in a borrowed community center with minimal promotion. We invited approximately fifteen vendors and hoped people would come. To our surprise, around 300 people attended the first event. We collected approximately $450 from vendor commissions, which may not sound like a lot, but it was money we did not have before.

Over the following months, we refined the concept. We improved our marketing by using social media and word-of-mouth. We increased the number of vendors and made the event more attractive with live music and food options. By the third month, we were earning approximately $1,200 per event. This additional revenue allowed us to hire one part-time staff member and expand our reach to serve an additional 100 students.

The marketplace events became a successful solution that addressed multiple problems at once. We generated revenue, supported local businesses, and created a community gathering space that strengthened relationships. This experience was valuable because it taught me that sometimes the best solutions come from questioning assumptions and trying something new.

I recognize that this success required persistence and willingness to learn from setbacks. Not every vendor wanted to participate initially, and we had to continuously adjust our strategy. However, I learned that creative problem-solving is about trying different approaches and being flexible enough to adapt.

This experience has motivated me to pursue an MBA because I want to develop stronger business skills that will help me solve problems more effectively in the nonprofit sector. I believe that nonprofit organizations need leaders who understand both the mission-driven aspect of this work and sound business principles. A degree from the University of Crete will provide me with the analytical tools and strategic knowledge I need to make a greater impact in my future career.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
In 2016, my property development company in Berlin was bleeding money on a mid-rise residential project in Friedrichshain. We'd already sunk €3.2 million into acquisition and preliminary design when the market shifted—suddenly, young professionals weren't interested in the 2-bedroom units we'd spent eighteen months planning. They wanted co-living spaces, open-plan offices, flexible arrangements. Our architect told us we were too far along to change course. Our CFO recommended we pivot to corporate housing. I did neither.

Instead, I spent three weekends interviewing sixty people in their twenties and thirties across four Berlin neighborhoods. What I discovered wasn't revolutionary—it was obvious, actually—but nobody in our company had bothered to ask. They didn't want smaller units; they wanted community. They wanted to know their neighbors. They wanted cheaper rent without sacrificing quality. They wanted flexibility: the ability to move out in six months if their startup failed or they got a job in Munich.

This contradicted everything we'd built our business on. Our entire model assumed people wanted privacy, permanence, and ownership. We were selling status. They were looking for belonging and optionality.

I pushed for us to completely redesign the project as a "flex-living" model—fifteen small private units (30 sqm) plus shared facilities: a kitchen on every floor, co-working spaces, event rooms, a courtyard garden. Shorter lease terms (six months minimum instead of two years). Shared furniture and services included in the rent. Essentially, I was suggesting we become a hospitality company disguised as a property developer.

My team thought I was insane. Our contractor said it would be logistically impossible. One board member walked out of the meeting.

But I had data. Not much, and certainly not scientific, but sixty conversations beat committee consensus.

We pushed forward. The redesign cost an additional €400,000 and delayed our opening by six months, but we filled the building in eight weeks. Today—eight years later—that project is our most profitable asset. The occupancy rate sits at 96%, turnover is low (people stay longer than expected once they're part of the community), and we've replicated the model across twelve buildings.

More importantly, it forced us to completely reimagine our business. We're no longer just developers; we're creating living ecosystems. We've brought in hospitality expertise. We've built relationships with co-working companies, language schools, and wellness providers. We employ twice as many people, and our revenue is up 340% since 2016.

What strikes me now is how close we came to ignoring an obvious insight because it required questioning our fundamental assumptions. Everyone around me was optimizing the original vision rather than questioning whether the vision itself was right.

I'm drawn to IIM Calcutta because I want to sharpen my instincts about when conventional thinking should be challenged—and when it shouldn't. At this stage in my career, I need structured frameworks for evaluating markets, consumer behavior, and organizational change. I need to learn from classmates and faculty who've navigated similar inflection points in different industries, different countries. Most importantly, I want to understand the difference between contrarianism and insight.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
What matters most to me is the moment when someone I've mentored finally understands something that previously seemed impossible to them—and then applies it to solve a real problem.

Three years ago, I was managing a portfolio of emerging market bonds at Andromeda Capital in Bogotá, making solid returns, moving up the ranks on schedule. My life looked good on paper. Then María, a junior analyst on my team, asked me to help her understand why a particular Colombian sovereign bond was widening when fundamentals hadn't deteriorated. Not the what—she could read the data. But the why. The actual reasoning.

I spent two hours with her that afternoon, walking through demand dynamics, FX carry positioning, geopolitical risk premia. By the end, she didn't just understand the bond. She understood how to think about the market differently. Two weeks later, she flagged a mispricing in Venezuelan debt that we exploited for about $400K in gains. More importantly, she started asking better questions.

That's when I realized that what actually drives me isn't the returns or the title. It's that moment of transmission—when someone's mental model shifts, and they become capable of things they weren't before. I've been chasing that feeling ever since.

The problem is that in asset management, there's only so much you can scale this. I can mentor a handful of junior analysts well. I can influence maybe two or three portfolio managers. But I'm fundamentally limited by the number of people I can sit down with. The structure of the business doesn't reward teaching. It rewards outperformance. And eventually, those two things can pull you in different directions.

That's what brought me to think seriously about an MBA, specifically one like SMU's Executive program. I came into asset management because I wanted to understand how capital flows and financial systems actually work—how decisions cascade through economies, how risk really gets priced. But I've come to understand that what I actually want is to shape how people think about these systems, not just make good bets on them.

I'm interested in roles that sit at the intersection of finance and education—whether that's in impact investing where I can shape capital allocation toward founders solving real problems, or in fintech where I can help build better infrastructure for retail investors, or even in development finance where financial literacy actually changes communities. In each case, the leverage comes from building systems and organizations, not from individual brilliance.

SMU appeals to me precisely because it's not a program designed for people trying to maximize their paychecks in New York or London. It attracts people who are thinking about what comes after climbing the first hill—what the next mountain actually is. The Asia focus matters too. My family is Colombian, but I've become increasingly convinced that the next decade of financial innovation and capital reallocation is happening in Asia, not the Atlantic.

What matters most to me, ultimately, is building something where the insight I have and the time I invest in other people actually compounds—where mentoring becomes embedded in the model, not a luxury I carve out. An MBA, particularly from SMU, feels like the right inflection point to make that transition real.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
Five years after completing my MBA at IIM Bangalore, I envision myself as a senior financial professional leading accounting and strategy initiatives in a multinational organization. I am passionate about leveraging my technical accounting background to drive meaningful impact across organizations in emerging markets, particularly in my home region of Latin America.

My career has been built on a foundation of strong analytical skills and attention to detail. During my four years as an accountant at a mid-size firm in São Paulo, I have developed expertise in financial reporting, audit processes, and regulatory compliance. I believe that an MBA will elevate my career to the next level by equipping me with the strategic thinking and leadership capabilities required for senior management roles.

In five years, I see myself in a role where I can make a difference. Whether this is as a Finance Manager, Controller, or CFO, I am committed to excellence and continuous improvement. I want to work at a company that values innovation and gives employees the opportunity to grow. I am particularly interested in industries such as technology, energy, or financial services, as these sectors are dynamic and offer interesting challenges.

IIM Bangalore EPGP is the perfect fit for my aspirations. The program's strong reputation and focus on developing leaders aligns perfectly with my goals. I am excited about the case-based learning method, which I believe will sharpen my problem-solving abilities. Additionally, the diverse cohort of international students will expose me to different perspectives and business practices from around the world. This global network is invaluable for someone like me who wants to eventually operate across Latin America and Asia.

The curriculum appears comprehensive, covering core areas like finance, operations, and strategy. I am particularly interested in courses focused on financial management and international business. These courses will provide me with the advanced knowledge I need to transition from an operational accounting role to a more strategic position.

Furthermore, the location of IIM Bangalore in India's Silicon Valley will provide me with insights into how companies operate in a fast-growing emerging market. I hope to apply these learnings to business opportunities in Brazil and other South American countries.

Beyond academics, I am eager to participate in consulting projects and case competitions, which I believe will help me apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios. I am also interested in networking with accomplished alumni who have built successful careers in finance and entrepreneurship.

My post-MBA goal is clear: to secure a position in a respected organization where I can contribute my accounting expertise while developing leadership and strategic planning skills. Within five years, I aim to be recognized as a trusted financial advisor who can guide companies through complex decisions and drive organizational growth.

I am confident that IIM Bangalore EPGP will provide me with the necessary tools, knowledge, and network to achieve these ambitions. The program's commitment to developing well-rounded leaders, combined with its international focus, makes it an ideal choice for my MBA journey.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
When I think about leadership, I think about someone who is not afraid to make difficult decisions and inspire others to follow. Throughout my career, I have tried to embody these qualities, and there is one experience that stands out as a defining moment in my leadership journey.

Two years ago, I joined my family's manufacturing business as an Operations Coordinator. The company has been in business for over thirty years and employs about 150 people. When I started, I quickly realized that many of our processes were outdated and inefficient. The production line was using manual systems that were prone to errors, and communication between departments was poor.

I saw an opportunity to make a real impact. I decided to lead a project to modernize our quality control procedures. This was challenging because many of the senior staff members had been with the company for a long time and were resistant to change. They were comfortable with the way things had always been done.

I worked hard to understand their concerns and address them. I organized meetings with the team to listen to their feedback and explain the benefits of the new system. I created presentations showing how the new processes would actually make their jobs easier, not harder. Over time, they began to trust me and see the value of what I was trying to accomplish.

After implementing the changes, we saw improvements in our production quality. The error rate decreased and we were able to process more items each day. This showed me that by being patient and inclusive in my approach, I could lead people through difficult transitions.

This experience taught me that leadership is not just about having a vision—it's about bringing people along with you. It's about listening, understanding, and communicating clearly. I learned that change is hard for people, and a good leader needs to be empathetic and help people understand why the change matters.

As I look toward my future, I know that an MBA will be crucial in developing my leadership skills further. I want to learn more about strategic management and how to lead larger teams. I want to understand global business better so that I can help my family's business expand internationally. Lyon University's program seems like a good fit for these goals.

I believe that the MBA will give me the tools I need to be a brave leader—someone who can make tough decisions, inspire teams, and create positive change. I am committed to this journey and excited about what I will learn.

school_name: SP Jain School of Global Management EMBA
prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I graduated from Tsinghua with my degree in Computer Science in 2020, at what turned out to be the worst possible time. The job market was frozen. I sent out 40 applications and got three offers, one of which was from a Series A SaaS startup in Beijing called CloudVault—basically unknown, run out of a co-working space, selling data management software to financial services companies.

I took it because I was terrified of unemployment, not because I believed in the mission.

For the first two years, I was an account executive supporting our enterprise segment. We were selling to banks and insurance companies across Asia-Pacific, which meant a lot of translating technical specs, managing expectations, and honestly, learning that I was pretty good at talking to people—something I never expected from myself as someone who coded through high school. I closed about $800K in ARR during those years, built relationships with three major accounts that are still with us today, and realized I actually loved the sales part more than I thought I would.

In 2022, our VP of Sales left suddenly, and I was asked to step into a lead role managing a team of six AEs. That's when things got real. I had to fire someone for the first time. I had to tell a senior team member they weren't getting promoted. I watched deals slip away because I misjudged our timeline. I also grew our segment revenue from $2.1M to $3.8M in 18 months, which felt significant at the time.

But last year, I hit a wall. I was managing people, managing processes, optimizing funnels—all the right moves technically—but I realized I didn't actually understand the financial mechanics of what I was doing. I was making decisions about headcount and pricing without really knowing the underlying unit economics. A director from our finance team explained why one of my expansion strategies would tank our margins, and I felt genuinely stupid.

That's when I knew I needed an MBA. Not to get the next promotion. Not because everyone says I should. But because I'm in a role where my ignorance is expensive, and I want to make better strategic decisions. SP Jain's EMBA appeals to me specifically because of the global cohort and Asia-Pacific focus—I want to understand how different markets actually work, not just theory. And frankly, I need to learn what I don't know.

content:

Several people responded positively and shared that the program provides excellent networking opportunities and exposure to Middle Eastern business practices. One alumna mentioned that the location in Dubai gave her unique advantages for understanding emerging markets, which I found particularly relevant given my background in the energy sector.

The presentation covered the curriculum structure, career services support, and alumni success stories. I appreciated learning about the emphasis on practical application and real-world case studies. Furthermore, I hope to engage with the faculty and leverage their research in areas like sustainable energy and market analysis.
