I created a more collaborative environment where people felt comfortable asking questions without fear of judgment. I even started monthly lunch meetings where we could discuss challenges and solutions together.

The results were significant. Within six months, my team's productivity increased by about 25%. More importantly, employee satisfaction improved, and people stopped leaving. My team became known as one of the most efficient in the firm. Several of my junior associates have since been promoted, which makes me proud.

This experience taught me that good leadership isn't about control—it's about empowerment and understanding people. I now believe that a manager's job is to create an environment where team members can grow and do their best work. This philosophy has guided me throughout my career, and I believe it will continue to serve me well in future leadership roles.

I'm excited about pursuing an MBA at IIM Ahmedabad because I want to deepen my understanding of management principles and learn how to lead at a broader organizational level. I'm confident this program will help me become a more effective leader.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
# Ethical Dilemma Essay

Two years ago, I was a senior producer at MediaWave Studios in Shanghai, managing a documentary series about rural education inequality. We had embedded ourselves in three villages for eighteen months, building trust with families and local officials. The final episode—which showed corruption in how government education subsidies were being distributed—was powerful and necessary.

Three days before our premiere at a major film festival, I received a call from our distribution partner. The provincial government had pressured them to pull our funding unless we removed specific names and figures from the episode. My director wanted to fight it. Our legal team said we could win in court, probably. But there was a catch: the village officials we'd documented were facing real consequences—potential criminal investigations. One had a teenage daughter applying to universities.

I realized I had a choice between the integrity of the story and the safety of the people who trusted us.

I didn't immediately know what to do. My first instinct was to protect the work—I'd spent nearly two years on this. But that instinct was about my ego, and I had to sit with that uncomfortable truth. These weren't abstract subjects. I'd eaten meals with these families. I'd watched them decide whether to speak on camera.

Over a week, I pushed our team to find a third path. We met with the implicated officials and offered them a choice: appear on camera to provide their perspective, or we would blur their identities while keeping the substantive allegations intact. One agreed to be interviewed; one declined. For the second, we kept the details but removed identifying information.

It wasn't what my director wanted. It wasn't what pure journalism might demand. But it was honest—we didn't hide wrongdoing, and we didn't weaponize individual people.

The episode still generated investigation and policy changes. It was messier than a purely adversarial approach would have been, and I've questioned whether I compromised too much. But I learned that ethical journalism isn't just about the story—it's about the relationship between storyteller and subject. That's stayed with me.

At HBKU, I want to deepen my understanding of how companies navigate these tensions between mission and responsibility to stakeholders. Your curriculum's emphasis on business ethics through real case studies resonates with me because I've lived the complexity firsthand.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
# Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.

Three years ago, I was a Major managing a logistics operation at RAAF Base Amberley with about sixty personnel. We were hemorrhaging money—our supply chain was fragmented across three separate systems that didn't talk to each other. Inventory discrepancies were costing us roughly $240,000 annually in wasted stock and emergency procurement premiums.

The frustrating part wasn't the numbers. It was watching capable people do redundant work. I'd walk past the stores section and see my team manually cross-referencing spreadsheets that should have been automated. They knew it was broken too.

I decided to push back against "this is just how the Air Force does it." I spent two months learning the systems properly—not just how to navigate them, but why they were designed that way historically. Then I proposed consolidating them into a single integrated platform. Predictably, I hit resistance. Other base commanders weren't keen on the hassle. My own supply officer was skeptical that the savings were real.

But I kept pushing. I brought in hard data, ran a proper pilot across one section, and made the business case concrete. We got approval. Implementation took eight months—messier than I expected, with some genuine teething problems—but we got there.

The result was $180,000 in annual savings. More importantly, I cut administration time by about 35%. My team went from managing compliance to actually solving logistics problems. Some people I'd worked with for years told me they finally had bandwidth to think again.

I'm most proud of it because it wasn't about being brilliant. It was about caring enough to push through bureaucratic inertia, being stubborn about something boring, and having the humility to learn systems I didn't understand before lecturing people about what was wrong with them. I made mistakes in how I communicated early on. I learned to listen to legitimate concerns rather than just dismiss resistance as obstruction.

That experience taught me something crucial: real leadership in organizations isn't about having all the answers. It's about creating the conditions where good people can do better work. That's why I'm pursuing this MBA. I want the tools to scale that impact beyond logistics—to understand how organizations actually change, and how to drive that change thoughtfully.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
I have always believed that great leaders are made, not born. Throughout my nine years working in the medical devices industry, I have learned valuable lessons about leadership that have shaped the way I approach management today.

My first real leadership opportunity came when I was promoted to senior operations coordinator at MediCare Solutions in Lagos. I was responsible for overseeing a team of five people and managing the daily operations of our distribution center. This was a significant moment in my career because it showed me that leadership was not just about having authority over others, but about inspiring them to do their best work.

One of the challenges I faced was that my team was not very motivated. They came to work, did their jobs, and left. There was no sense of passion or commitment to excellence. I realized that as a leader, I needed to change this dynamic. I started having regular meetings with my team members to understand their goals and aspirations. I also made sure to recognize their achievements and celebrate their successes. Over time, I noticed that the team became more engaged and productive.

This experience taught me that effective leadership is about communication and empathy. When you take the time to listen to your team and understand their needs, they will respond with loyalty and hard work. I also learned that a leader should lead by example. If you expect your team to be punctual and professional, you must be the same way.

Another important aspect of my leadership philosophy is accountability. I believe that as a leader, you must take responsibility for both the successes and failures of your team. When something goes wrong, it is important to address it immediately and work with your team to find solutions. This builds trust and shows that you are truly invested in their development.

In my current role at Advanced Biotech Services, I manage a larger team of eight people in our quality assurance department. I have continued to apply the principles I learned earlier in my career. I hold regular one-on-one meetings with each team member, provide constructive feedback, and create an environment where people feel valued and respected.

I also believe that continuous learning is essential for good leadership. The medical devices industry is constantly evolving, and leaders must stay informed about new regulations, technologies, and best practices. I have invested in my own professional development by completing several certifications and attending industry conferences. I believe that this commitment to learning sets a good example for my team.

Looking back at my journey, I realize that my leadership philosophy is centered on three key pillars: communication, empathy, and accountability. These principles have guided me through various challenges and have helped me build strong relationships with my team members. I believe that good leadership is not about being the smartest person in the room or having all the answers. Instead, it is about creating an environment where your team can thrive and grow.

As I consider the next phase of my career, I am excited about the opportunity to pursue an MBA. I believe that further education will help me develop into a more strategic and visionary leader. The Executive MBA program at Bilkent University is particularly appealing because of its focus on developing leaders who can make a positive impact in their organizations and communities.

In conclusion, my leadership experiences have shaped who I am as a manager and professional. I am committed to continuing to grow and develop as a leader, and I look forward to the opportunity to learn from faculty and peers at Bilkent University.

prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
Leadership without formal authority is an important skill in today's business environment. During my time at a SaaS company, I had the opportunity to demonstrate this capability.

I was working as a Senior Account Executive in the sales department when I noticed that our customer retention rates were declining. The product team and customer success team were siloed and not communicating effectively. I realized that I could play a bridging role between these teams, even though I had no direct authority over either one.

I took the initiative to organize weekly sync meetings between product and customer success. In these meetings, we discussed customer pain points and feature requests. I gathered feedback from my accounts and shared it with both teams. I also helped translate technical product language into customer-facing benefits, which improved communication between the groups.

Over time, the collaboration improved. Customer success began proactively reaching out to product with customer feedback. Product became more responsive to feature requests. This led to better customer satisfaction scores and improved retention metrics in my territory. Several colleagues told me they appreciated my efforts to bring the teams together.

This experience taught me several valuable lessons. First, I learned that leadership is not about having a title or formal authority. It's about identifying a problem and taking action to solve it. Second, I learned the importance of communication and collaboration across functional areas. When teams work together, they can achieve better results for the customer and the business. Third, I learned that being a bridge between different groups can create value, even if you don't have direct control over the outcomes.

Moving forward, I want to develop my leadership skills further and understand how to scale these kinds of cross-functional initiatives. An MBA will help me learn frameworks for organizational management and strategic thinking. I believe these tools will enable me to lead larger teams and drive bigger impact in my career.

I am interested in pursuing a general management track after my MBA, which is why I am applying to Goa Institute of Management. I hope to use my sales experience combined with business education to move into a leadership position where I can drive growth for a company.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
When I joined Arnott's in 2015 as a junior brand manager, the biscuit category was dying. Millennials weren't eating crackers like their parents did, and our market share was slipping. The obvious move was what every competitor was doing—reformulate for "health," cut calories, add fiber, rebrand with pastel packaging.

Instead, I pushed back.

I spent three months interviewing customers outside of typical focus groups—at university campuses, share houses, late-night kebab shops. What I found was that millennials didn't want a "healthy biscuit." They wanted permission to eat something indulgent without guilt, and they wanted it to mean something. Our brand had heritage and authenticity that newer competitors couldn't touch.

So we launched Arnott's Indulgence—a deliberately premium, unapologetically regular biscuit. The marketing was honest: "The biscuit you actually want." No health claims. No apologies. We positioned it as a moment of conscious indulgence rather than a daily staple.

My manager thought it was risky. The category was declining, and we were moving against the trend. But I had the data from my conversations, and I had conviction. We pitched it to the executive team with clear reasoning: the market wasn't shrinking because people stopped wanting biscuits; it was shrinking because we'd stopped giving them reasons to care.

The product launched in 2017 and grew 34% year-on-year for two years. It became our highest-margin offering and actually reversed some category decline in our segment. More importantly, it changed how the entire organization thought about consumer research. We started talking less about demographic data and more about genuine consumer tension points.

That experience taught me that innovation rarely comes from following the obvious playbook. It comes from asking better questions and being willing to look stupid for a while before you're proven right. It also taught me the limits of my current role—I was managing one product in one market. The insights I uncovered could work across categories and geographies, but I needed deeper training in strategic thinking and international business to pursue that.

That's why an MBA appeals to me. I want to move from defending individual innovations to shaping innovation strategy at a larger scale.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
# Ethical Dilemma Essay

Two months into my role as a regulatory affairs associate at Compugen, I discovered that our clinical trial protocol contained a flaw that could obscure serious adverse events in a subset of patients. I was reviewing safety data submissions when I noticed that the exclusion criteria for one patient population—older adults with existing kidney dysfunction—wasn't adequately justified in our statistical analysis plan. The issue wasn't fraud. It was worse, in some ways. It was the kind of thing that happens when smart people make small compromises without realizing where they lead.

Here's what complicated the situation: our trial was six months behind schedule and $2.3 million over budget. The company had already delayed two quarters of revenue. My manager—someone I genuinely liked and who had mentored me—explained that the exclusion criteria reflected standard practice and that flagging it now would trigger a costly protocol amendment. He wasn't telling me to ignore it. He was just making clear what an amendment would cost us. The implication hung in the air like a question mark.

I was twenty-six. I'd been in biotech for two years. I wasn't going to be the person who derailed a phase 2b trial.

But I kept coming back to the patients we were trying to recruit. The trial was investigating a treatment for a specific type of leukemia. These weren't abstract data points. They were people desperate enough to enter a clinical trial, trusting that we'd designed it honestly. I thought about my grandmother, who underwent experimental treatment for her cancer five years ago. She trusted the researchers. That trust felt sacred in a way that spreadsheets didn't capture.

I did something that felt both small and enormous: I emailed our Chief Medical Officer directly, with my manager copied. I didn't frame it as a crisis or an accusation. I simply laid out what I'd found—the statistical gap, the patient population affected, the alternatives for addressing it. I suggested three options, ranging from a limited amendment to a more comprehensive revision. I included language acknowledging the timeline and budget constraints, but I didn't let those constraints do my thinking for me.

The email sat in everyone's inbox for three days. Then the CMO called me into her office.

She said the kind thing first: "Thank you for having the courage to raise this." Then she said the hard thing: "You were right. And you were right to go around your chain of command, even though that probably feels terrible."

What followed was exactly what my manager had worried about. We amended the protocol. We spent an additional $480,000. We lost another eight weeks. But we redesigned the safety monitoring framework in a way that actually strengthened our data integrity. Two years later, that trial became the basis for a new indication that's currently in phase 3.

I learned something unexpected from that experience: most people actually want to do the right thing. My manager wasn't a villain. He was under immense pressure, and he made the familiar mistake of treating constraints as facts rather than choices. Once someone created space for the ethical option—space where doing the right thing didn't feel like career suicide—the outcome changed.

This is why I'm applying to Hebrew University's Executive MBA. I want to understand how to lead in ways that create that space consistently, not just in emergency moments. I want to study organizational ethics and strategy alongside Israelis navigating complex business environments. And honestly, I want to deepen my ability to see ethical issues before they become dilemmas.

The decision to raise that issue cost the company money. It also cost me some easy camaraderie with my manager for a while. But it didn't cost me my integrity, and that, I've learned, is the only currency that actually matters.

prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
I am passionate about understanding how businesses work and creating positive impact in the world. Since I was young, I have always been curious about different cultures and how people from different backgrounds can work together to achieve common goals.

In my current role at a manufacturing company, I have learned many valuable lessons about leadership and teamwork. I work with people from different countries and have developed strong communication skills. I believe that diversity is very important in business, and I try to bring people together despite our differences.

Outside of work, I enjoy reading business books and watching documentaries about successful entrepreneurs. I find it interesting to learn how companies grow from small startups to large multinational organizations. I also like traveling and exploring new places. I visited several countries in Europe and Asia, which has opened my mind to different perspectives and ways of thinking.

I am also interested in sustainability and environmental issues. I believe that companies have a responsibility to think about their impact on the environment and society. In my personal life, I try to be conscious about my carbon footprint and support companies that have good environmental practices.

Finally, I am someone who is always willing to learn and improve myself. I take feedback seriously and use it to become better at my job and as a person. I think that lifelong learning is essential in today's world, especially in business where things are changing rapidly.

I chose to apply to American University in Dubai because I want to develop my skills and knowledge in a global environment. The program seems like a good fit for my career goals, and I am excited about the opportunity to study in Dubai, which is a major business hub in the Middle East.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
When our biotech firm in Barcelona faced a manufacturing crisis two years ago, I learned that stakeholder management isn't about finding perfect solutions—it's about being honest about trade-offs.

We were six months into scaling production of a diagnostic platform for autoimmune diseases. Our manufacturing partner in Germany suddenly informed us they couldn't meet our Q3 targets due to equipment failures. This affected three major stakeholders with completely opposing interests: our investors who had committed €8 million and expected revenue projections, our hospital clients who had already begun patient screening programs, and our 40-person team whose jobs and equity packages depended on hitting milestones.

The pressure was intense. Our CFO wanted to immediately find alternative manufacturers, even if it meant 30% higher costs and burned through our runway. The hospital clients were calling daily asking about delays. Some of my team members were already updating their LinkedIn profiles. My instinct was to shield everyone from bad news and promise we'd fix it.

Instead, I did something harder: I called everyone together and told them the actual situation. Not the optimistic version. The real one.

With investors, I stopped leading with solutions I wasn't confident about. I said: "We've lost three months. Here are three paths forward, each with different costs and risks. Path A preserves our timeline but exhausts our capital. Path B stretches our runway but delays growth. Path C requires us to renegotiate with clients." I presented the financial impact of each option honestly. They hated the options, but they respected that I wasn't spinning a narrative. We landed on a hybrid approach where they injected another €3 million specifically for alternative manufacturing, but accepted a revised timeline.

For hospital clients, I visited three of them personally instead of managing through emails. I showed them exactly where production stood, what we could deliver by when, and what we couldn't. One client threatened to pull out—a €500k deal. Rather than fight, I asked what they actually needed. Turns out they needed 60% of our original commitment by October, not everything by August. We restructured the deal. We lost revenue visibility, but kept the relationship and proved we could be straight with them even when things broke.

For my team, I held a town hall and didn't sugarcoat the situation. I said the timeline was uncertain and some positions might be at risk. Then I gave them options: some could move to different projects, we'd accelerate equity vesting for those who wanted security, and we'd hire for new product lines if we got the additional funding. About 35 people stayed. More importantly, they stayed because they knew what they were choosing.

We recovered. Production restarted by month four, we hit modified targets by year-end, and we actually grew the team to 52 people.

Looking back, I managed this situation poorly in some ways. I was too reactive early on. I should have involved our clients in problem-solving rather than just updating them. My team probably carried more stress than necessary because I didn't delegate problem-solving to them sooner.

What I did right was recognizing that stakeholders aren't obstacles to manage around—they're people with legitimate, different needs. My job wasn't to make everyone happy. It was to be transparent about constraints and involve them in trade-offs.

prompt: What are your short-term career goals and how will the Full-Time MBA program at Michigan State University's Broad College of Business help you achieve them?
content:
I am passionate about advancing my career in the consumer goods industry and becoming a brand manager at a leading multinational corporation. An MBA from Michigan State University's Broad College of Business will provide me with the strategic skills and network necessary to achieve this goal.

Over the past twelve years, I have worked in various roles within the CPG sector, gaining valuable experience in marketing, sales, and product development. My current position has allowed me to develop a strong understanding of market dynamics and consumer behavior. However, I recognize that to move into senior management positions, I need to enhance my business acumen and develop a more comprehensive skill set.

The Full-Time MBA program at Broad College offers several features that align with my career objectives. First, the curriculum in strategic management and marketing will deepen my analytical capabilities and help me understand how to drive business growth. Second, the program's emphasis on data analytics will enable me to make better decisions based on quantitative insights rather than intuition alone. Third, networking opportunities with peers and alumni in the CPG industry will be invaluable as I transition into leadership roles.

I am particularly interested in how the program integrates real-world business cases into its teaching methodology. This approach resonates with my learning style, as I have always learned best through practical examples and problem-solving scenarios. Additionally, Michigan State's location in the Midwest provides access to numerous Fortune 500 companies in the consumer goods space, which could facilitate internship opportunities and industry connections.

My short-term goal is to secure a brand manager position within two years of graduation, preferably at a company like Procter & Gamble or Nestlé. I believe that an MBA will help me transition more smoothly into this role by providing the strategic framework and credibility that senior hiring managers seek. Furthermore, the program's career services will help me identify and pursue these opportunities more effectively.

Beyond my immediate career goals, I am committed to bringing diverse perspectives to the classroom. As a Pakistani non-binary professional, I understand the importance of representation in business leadership. I am excited to contribute to the diversity of thought at Broad College and to learn from classmates with different backgrounds and experiences.

In conclusion, Michigan State University's Broad College of Business is an excellent fit for my career aspirations. The combination of rigorous coursework, practical learning experiences, and strong industry connections will equip me with the tools needed to succeed as a brand manager and eventually advance into executive leadership. I am confident that this program will be a transformative step in my professional journey.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
In 2019, I was assigned as a Team Lead for a logistics coordination unit within Mexico's Ministry of Defense. I had three years of military background but this was my first formal leadership role, and I was inheriting a team of eight people who had worked under my predecessor for five years. Within the first month, I realized that morale was low—people weren't speaking up in meetings, deadlines were consistently missed by 3-5 days, and there was a general sense that "this is just how things work here."

I didn't immediately try to overhaul everything. Instead, I spent my first two weeks just listening. I sat with each team member individually and asked them what frustrated them about their work and what they thought could be better. What emerged was that the previous structure relied entirely on one person—my predecessor—making all decisions and holding all institutional knowledge. When that person left, the team felt abandoned. They weren't incompetent; they were disempowered.

I made two structural changes. First, I redistributed decision-making authority. Instead of having me or one senior person approve every logistics request, I identified each person's strongest skill area and made them the decision-maker for that domain. A person named Carlos, who had been quietly doing excellent analysis, became the owner of our supply chain forecasting. Another team member, Sandra, took control of vendor management. I kept the harder decisions for myself, but I was explicit about why and how we'd move to delegate them as people grew into their roles.

Second, I created what we called "Friday Stands"—fifteen-minute meetings where each person reported one thing they completed, one challenge they faced, and one thing they learned. It sounds simple, but it fundamentally changed how we operated. People started learning from each other. Mistakes became learning opportunities rather than reasons to hide. Within two months, I noticed team members answering each other's questions before coming to me.

The results were tangible. Our on-time delivery rate went from 82% to 94% within four months. More importantly, three team members volunteered for professional development courses, and two of them have since been promoted. When I eventually transferred to a different position last year, the team continued functioning well under a new leader—which to me is the real test of whether leadership stuck.

But honestly, I'm not sure I did anything particularly brave. I didn't make some bold strategic call or push through major organizational resistance. I mainly recognized a problem, tried to understand its root cause, and then addressed it in a straightforward way. The "bravery," if there was any, was in accepting that my predecessor's approach wasn't working and being willing to change it, even though change always feels risky in a government environment where tradition and hierarchy matter.

What I took from this experience is that leadership isn't always about charisma or making dramatic decisions. Sometimes it's about creating space for other people to lead within their areas and then trusting them to do it. It's about being willing to look foolish by asking questions and admitting when you don't know something.

I'm applying to Lyon because I want to deepen my understanding of how organizations actually function—not just the theory, but the practice of managing people and building systems that work. I've learned leadership by doing it, sometimes messily, and I think a structured program would help me become more intentional about the approaches I take and more confident in what works across different contexts.

school_name: University of Amsterdam Business School MSc
prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
Three years ago, I was leading a supply chain transformation for a FMCG conglomerate in Southeast Asia—about $2.8 billion in revenue. When I inherited the project, it was hemorrhaging money. The previous lead had implemented an ERP system that everyone hated. Adoption was at 34%. Managers were circumventing it, keeping spreadsheets as their real system of record. The company was losing roughly €1.2 million monthly to inefficiencies they couldn't even measure properly.

My accomplishment wasn't fixing the software. It was fixing why people wouldn't use it.

I spent my first month doing something most consultants skip: I sat with the operations team. Not in meetings where they perform for the client. I mean I sat with them during their actual work. A warehouse manager in Surabaya, a demand planner in Bangkok, a procurement officer in Manila. I asked them what they were doing with the spreadsheets. What they needed that the system wasn't giving them. What scared them about the new process.

What I found was that the ERP demanded they change how they worked—not because the new way was better, but because some vendor consultant designed it that way. The system required three approval steps that used to take one. It generated reports in a format nobody could read quickly. And nobody had bothered to explain why the change was happening at all.

I redesigned the implementation approach with the operations team itself. We customized workflows to match how they actually needed to work (not perfectly, but functionally). We rebuilt five key reports to the format they actually used. But the biggest shift was different: I organized monthly forums where operations leaders explained to their teams exactly why we were moving to integrated planning. Not abstract supply chain optimization. Real reasons: fewer stockouts for customers, more predictable workloads, less firefighting at 2 a.m.

Eight months later, adoption hit 87%. Eighteen months later, they'd optimized their inventory levels and reduced their working capital requirements by €4.3 million. The company reinvested most of that into their distribution network.

Why does this matter to me? Not because of the number or the efficiency gain, though those matter.

It matters because I learned that business problems aren't usually technical problems—they're human problems. The best strategy in the world fails if the people implementing it don't understand it or believe in it. I spent the first four years of my consulting career becoming excellent at analytics and process design. I could build a model or map a workflow as well as anyone. But this project taught me that impact requires a completely different skill: the ability to bring people along.

That realization changed my career. I started asking for projects where change management was the constraint, not process engineering. I got better at listening than presenting. I stopped trying to be the smartest person in the room and started trying to be the one who understood most clearly what mattered to people.

Now I want to move into a strategy role where I'm not advising companies—I'm running something. And I know that I need a deeper toolkit: more financial modeling, more business intuition, more exposure to how companies actually generate value. That's what draws me to Amsterdam. Your program's focus on strategy alongside behavioral aspects of business matches exactly where I need to develop. I've optimized processes. Now I want to optimize organizations.

prompt: Describe a time when you led a team through a significant challenge. What was the outcome?
content:
When our team at Aether Digital faced a potential shutdown in early 2022, I had to make a choice: accept the obvious path forward or push back on it.

We were a 12-person marketing automation startup in Dubai, and we'd grown quickly—maybe too quickly. We'd landed a major client, a regional e-commerce company doing about $40 million in annual revenue, and they represented 60% of our revenue. Then, three months in, their CFO demanded we rebuild our entire system architecture to integrate with their legacy infrastructure. The project would take four months and cost us roughly 200,000 AED in unexpected development work. We didn't have that cushion.

My co-founder wanted to decline. His reasoning was logical: we'd lose the client, yes, but at least we wouldn't hemorrhage cash. Two other team members agreed. Looking at the spreadsheet, I understood their position. But something didn't sit right with me.

I asked to spend a week analyzing whether there was a third option. I went back to the client and asked harder questions than we had in the initial sales process. What I learned was that their urgency wasn't really about the technical integration—it was about proving to their board that they'd made a smart vendor choice. They were nervous. Once I understood that, the conversation shifted.

I came back to my team with a proposal: instead of a full rebuild, we'd deliver a hybrid solution. We'd do the essential integration work (80,000 AED) but also assign one of our developers, Khalid, to be embedded with their team two days a week for three months. Khalid would work as an extension of their engineering team, not just a vendor. I told them we'd position it as a partnership model, not a cost.

The pitch to the client was different. We didn't lead with the technical specs. We said: "We're not just solving your integration problem—we're investing in your success." Their CFO pushed back initially, but their head of product, who'd been skeptical from the start, came around. Khalid ended up becoming a trusted part of their workflow. By month two, they were treating him like an internal hire.

The financial outcome was that we spent 80,000 AED instead of 200,000, kept the client, and—this was the real win—they extended the contract by another 18 months because they valued having Khalid embedded with them. We ended up keeping the client for three years total.

But the team outcome mattered more to me. When I explained the decision to my co-founder, he was skeptical. When I told the team we were going forward with this, there was real hesitation. People thought I was being naive. What changed their mind was when they saw Khalid actually deliver value and the client genuinely appreciated it. It wasn't about me convincing them with words—it was about the reality proving the strategy right.

The harder part came later, when scaling became an issue. We couldn't embed people with every client. This model had an expiration date, and I had to be honest with the team about that. We talked about what we'd learned and rebuilt our service offering around what actually worked: deep partnership rather than just software.

Looking back, what I learned wasn't just about problem-solving under pressure. It was that sometimes the people who report to you—or work alongside you—are right to be skeptical. My co-founder's concerns about cash were valid. Khalid's concern about bandwidth was real. The lesson wasn't that I had all the answers. It was that listening to their real concerns, then finding a path that addressed them honestly, mattered more than being right.

That experience shapes how I want to approach the next phase of my career, which is why I'm interested in studying business at Hebrew University. I want to deepen my understanding of how to build sustainable models, not just solve immediate crises.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
I'm most proud of rebuilding the audit practice at Deloitte Milan after we nearly lost a major pharmaceutical client three years ago.

When I took over the engagement in 2021, the relationship was in freefall. Our team had missed a critical compliance deadline, missed it badly, and the client's CFO was actively shopping for a replacement firm. I was 29 and had never run a client portfolio this size before—roughly €2.8 million in annual fees, twelve people reporting to me, months of trust to rebuild.

My first instinct was to apologize and promise faster turnarounds. But that felt hollow. So I actually spent a full week on-site at their manufacturing facility outside Milan, not doing audit work but understanding their calendar. I sat through their monthly close process, watched their IT team push updates, talked to their controller about what kept her up at night. I realized our audits were built around what was convenient for Deloitte's scheduling, not when they could actually absorb our findings.

We restructured everything. We moved our peak team deployments to fit their close cycle. I hired a senior manager specifically to be their quasi-embedded controller during busy weeks. We built a monthly findings dashboard instead of a wall of surprises at year-end. It sounds straightforward now, but it meant reorganizing three other client engagements and overloading my own hours for nearly six months.

The CFO called me after our first reformed audit cycle. She said, "We decided not to change firms." Small words, but I remember sitting in my car in the Deloitte parking lot feeling something I'd never felt before—the satisfaction of saving something through actual problem-solving instead of corporate platitudes.

By year two, we'd grown the engagement to €3.4 million. We won two additional projects from them. More importantly, that model became the template for how our entire Milan office approached pharmaceutical audits. I documented the process, trained other managers, and it's still how we operate there.

But what I'm actually most proud of is not the revenue. It's that I learned something essential about myself: I'm someone who gets energized by understanding what someone else actually needs, even when that's harder than what I'm trained to do. I was good at audit methodology, good with spreadsheets. But I was doing that work in isolation. Once I started designing around the client's reality instead of my processes, everything changed.

This is also why I'm pursuing the EMBA now. I've spent six years becoming technically proficient at accounting and finance. What I want to develop over the next two years is the broader capability to lead complex transformations—understanding supply chains, strategy, organizational behavior, not just debits and credits. Sasin's emphasis on case-based learning and your strong network across ASEAN appeals to me because I want to test my instincts against messier, real-world problems across different markets and industries.

I know my GMAT score doesn't scream top-tier analyst. But it reflects someone who's built competence through doing, not testing, and I believe an executive program is where I'll thrive.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
# What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?

Throughout my career in the non-profit sector, I have had the opportunity to contribute to meaningful work that has helped communities in need. My most significant accomplishment is my involvement in launching a community development initiative that aimed to improve living standards for underprivileged populations across multiple regions.

I have always believed that education and opportunity are fundamental human rights. When I joined my organization in 2012, I recognized that many communities lacked access to basic resources and support systems. This realization motivated me to take action and make a tangible difference in people's lives.

The initiative I led brought together various stakeholders and partners to create sustainable solutions. We worked to establish educational programs, provide vocational training, and support local entrepreneurs. Through this work, we were able to reach thousands of people and create positive change. The experience taught me that collective effort and collaboration are essential for achieving meaningful outcomes.

This accomplishment matters to me for several reasons. First, it demonstrated that I could take initiative and lead projects that directly impact people's welfare. Second, it showed me the importance of working with diverse teams and building partnerships across different sectors. Finally, it reinforced my passion for helping others and contributing to society in a meaningful way.

What makes this particularly significant is the lasting impact we achieved. Our programs have continued to grow and develop even after my direct involvement. We measured success not just in numbers—though we did reach over 5,000 direct beneficiaries—but in the stories of individuals whose lives were genuinely improved. I recall speaking with a young woman who completed our vocational training program and subsequently started her own small business. These personal stories remind me why this work matters.

However, I must acknowledge that success in non-profit work is complex. We faced numerous challenges including limited funding, difficulties coordinating across regions, and the challenge of measuring long-term impact. There were moments when I questioned whether our efforts were truly sustainable or merely addressing symptoms rather than root causes. These doubts pushed me to think more strategically about our approach and to seek continuous improvement.

This accomplishment has shaped my professional identity and my desire to pursue an MBA. I recognize that while my experience has given me valuable insights about community development and leadership, I need deeper knowledge in business strategy, financial management, and organizational development to maximize my impact in the future. An MBA will provide me with the analytical tools and strategic frameworks necessary to scale social impact initiatives and work more effectively with organizational leadership.

Looking forward, I want to continue building my expertise in this field and taking on increasingly senior roles where I can influence policy and strategy at a higher level. This accomplishment is not an endpoint but rather a foundation upon which I hope to build a career of sustained impact.

school_name: Fudan University School of Management (Master in Management)
prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
Throughout my ten years at Google and later at Meta, I've learned that the best ideas rarely come from individual brilliance. They emerge from diverse perspectives colliding in a room. This is why I'm drawn to Fudan's Master in Management program—not just for what I'll learn, but for what I can contribute to the collective intelligence of your cohort.

My background sits at an interesting intersection. I'm Japanese, educated in Tokyo, but I've spent the last decade working in the American tech ecosystem. At Google, I managed a team of eight engineers across three time zones on infrastructure projects. Later at Meta, I shifted into product strategy, working on localization initiatives for Southeast Asian markets. This experience has taught me how to translate between different cultural and technical languages—a skill I think is underutilized in business education.

In classroom discussions, I plan to bring concrete, real-world examples rather than theoretical abstractions. I've seen how companies make actual decisions under uncertainty, and how those decisions play out over quarters and years. When we discuss supply chain strategy or organizational design, I won't just reference case studies. I'll describe the time our infrastructure team at Google had to completely rebuild our approach to resource allocation because our initial model failed at scale. Or explain why Meta's organizational restructuring in 2022 made sense from the inside, even as it baffled external observers. These stories aren't always flattering—I've made mistakes and seen smart people make bad calls—but they're real.

Beyond the classroom, I want to be useful. At both companies, I found myself naturally becoming the person who helped others navigate ambiguity. When my teammates needed to understand stakeholder dynamics or wanted perspective on a difficult situation, they came to me. I'd like to do this at Fudan. I imagine helping Japanese classmates think through cross-cultural business challenges, or helping Chinese students understand how Western tech companies approach certain problems. I'm also interested in connecting classmates with people in my network—I know product leaders, engineers, and founders who might be valuable contacts for others' career transitions.

I should be honest: I'm coming to this program because I feel stuck. I've been a strong executor within large companies, but I haven't built anything from scratch. I haven't led a team toward a company-wide strategic shift. An MBA won't magically solve this, but the unstructured time, the peer learning, and the space to think differently might help me figure out what I actually want to build. That hunger to be a builder—I think that will make me an engaged, curious classmate.

I'm not looking to be the smartest person in the room. I'm looking to be the person who asks good questions, shares hard-won knowledge, and pulls others' thinking forward. That's how I work best, and that's what I want to bring to Fudan.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
Three years ago, I recommended we kill a deal I'd spent six months building.

At Zenith Ventures in São Paulo, I led the investment process for a Series B round in a logistics startup that was doing everything right on paper—$8M ARR, 40% MoM growth, expanding across five countries. The founding team was sharp. The market tailwind was real. By October, we were ready to commit our $5M check.

Then I noticed something in their CAC payback analysis. The numbers didn't quite add up. I asked their CFO to walk me through the unit economics again. He fumbled. When I dug deeper, I found that their customer retention rate had been declining for eight months—they were just covering it up with aggressive new customer acquisition. The growth was real, but it wasn't sustainable.

I told our investment committee we should pass. This wasn't a small call. My partners had already briefed our LPs. The founder had told his team they'd closed funding. I had to explain why I'd wasted six months.

But here's what mattered: six months later, that startup ran out of cash. Founders walked away, employees lost their jobs, our LPs dodged what would've been a $5M write-down. More importantly, I realized something about myself—I'd been afraid to speak up earlier because I didn't want to look foolish or derail a deal everyone else seemed excited about.

That experience changed how I approach my work. I started pushing back harder on assumptions, asking uglier questions, and accepting that being right sometimes means being unpopular. Over the next two years, I applied this discipline to our due diligence process. We missed out on some flashy deals, but we also avoided three more disasters and identified two breakout investments—Wayfleet and Ridetech—that returned 8x and 12x respectively.

This matters to me because I realized early success can be dangerous if it makes you overconfident. I'm applying to IIFT because I want to deepen my understanding of how to build sustainable businesses, not just spot fast-growing ones. I want to learn how global supply chains work, how emerging markets differ from what I've seen in LatAm, and how to think more systematically about the fundamentals underneath the hype.

It's humbling work. But it's honest work.

school_name: Poznan University of Economics MSc Management
prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
# Classroom Contributions and Student Life at Poznan University of Economics

I am excited about the opportunity to contribute meaningfully to the MSc Management program at Poznan University of Economics. Throughout my professional journey and academic experiences, I have developed a strong passion for learning and collaborating with diverse teams, which I believe will enable me to be a valuable member of your classroom community.

In my current role at Google, I work as a Senior Associate in the Sales Development team, where I have learned the importance of diverse perspectives and open communication. I believe these skills will translate directly into enriching classroom discussions. In every course, I plan to bring my real-world experience and contribute thoughtful insights that connect theoretical frameworks to practical business applications. My background working with global teams has taught me how to listen actively and engage respectfully with colleagues who have different viewpoints, which I think is crucial in an MBA program.

Additionally, I am passionate about making a difference in the student community. I have always been someone who enjoys helping others succeed, and I would like to contribute to student life by being a supportive peer. If there are opportunities to help organize study groups or mentor first-year students, I would be genuinely interested in participating. I believe that a strong community is built through mutual support and collaboration.

Throughout my three years at Google, I have developed strong quantitative skills and a deep understanding of the tech industry. I would be able to contribute to case discussions by bringing a data-driven approach to problem-solving. I am also interested in learning from my classmates who come from different industries and backgrounds. I think the diversity in an MBA cohort is one of the most valuable assets, and I am committed to fostering an inclusive environment where everyone feels comfortable sharing their perspectives.

Outside of formal coursework, I am interested in participating in clubs and activities that align with my professional interests. I would like to explore areas such as innovation management and entrepreneurship, and I believe connecting with peers who share these interests would be beneficial for my growth and for the broader student community.

I am also aware that moving to Poznan as an international student from Colombia will offer me unique experiences. I am excited to learn about European business practices and to share my perspective as someone from Latin America. I believe this cross-cultural exchange will enrich discussions and help create a more globally minded cohort.

In summary, I am committed to being an active and engaged member of the Poznan University of Economics community. I will contribute through thoughtful participation in classes, support for my peers, and a genuine curiosity about learning from others. My background at Google, combined with my desire to grow both professionally and personally, positions me to be a positive contributor to classroom discussions and student life. I look forward to the opportunity to be part of your program and to grow alongside my classmates.

school_name: Imperial College Business School MSc Management
prompt: Describe a time when you led a team through a significant challenge. What was the outcome?
content:
# Essay Response

During my time at True Communications, I have learned many important lessons about leadership. One experience that stands out is when our customer service team faced a significant challenge that required me to step up and lead.

In early 2023, our department was struggling with low customer satisfaction scores. The scores had been declining for several months, and management was concerned. As a senior executive assistant working in the customer service division, I was asked to help address this problem. I gathered my team of about 8 people and we decided to work together to improve our performance.

The main challenge was that our processes were inefficient. Customers were waiting too long for responses, and the quality of our service had decreased. I realized that we needed to make changes to how we worked. I organized several meetings with my team members to discuss what was wrong and how we could fix it.

During these meetings, I listened to their feedback and ideas. Many team members had good suggestions about what could be improved. For example, one team member suggested that we streamline our response process, and another suggested we provide more training. I encouraged everyone to contribute their thoughts, which I believe is an important part of being a good leader.

After collecting all the feedback, I created a plan to improve our customer service. The plan included implementing new response protocols, scheduling training sessions, and monitoring our progress weekly. I worked with my manager to get approval for the necessary resources.

Over the next two months, we implemented these changes. I held regular check-in meetings with the team to see how things were going. I also made sure to recognize and appreciate people's efforts during this difficult time. I believe that positive reinforcement is important for team motivation.

The results were very positive. Our customer satisfaction scores increased by 15% within two months, and our response time improved significantly. The team felt proud of what we accomplished together. This experience taught me that good leadership involves listening to your team, creating a clear plan, and supporting people through change.

This challenge has made me want to develop my leadership skills further through an MBA program. I know that Imperial College Business School has a strong reputation for developing leaders, and I believe that studying at Imperial would help me grow both professionally and personally. The MSc Management program would give me the knowledge and tools I need to lead larger teams and tackle bigger challenges in the future.

I am excited about the opportunity to study at Imperial and contribute to the program with my diverse background and experiences.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
My management philosophy crystallized the day I realized I'd made my team invisible.

I was running the curriculum development team at EduVenture Melbourne, a startup building online courses for regional schools. We were small—just four people—but ambitious. After eighteen months, we'd expanded from two courses to sixteen, and I'd started managing like I was scaling for fifty. I created processes, approval chains, weekly status reports. Everything felt professional and structured. Everything felt dead.

One Monday, our instructional designer Sarah didn't show up. No message. I was annoyed—we had a client deadline. When she returned Thursday, I pulled her aside expecting an explanation. Instead, she told me she'd had a panic attack because she'd missed a formatting detail in a module, and my feedback email had made her feel incompetent. She'd taken three days to convince herself to come back.

I'd spent so much energy building systems that I'd stopped seeing the people inside them.

That conversation forced me to confront something uncomfortable: I'd become the kind of manager I'd hated working for—someone who optimized for output without considering input. I didn't yell or micromanage, but I'd created an environment where people felt safer hiding mistakes than surfacing them.

I restructured how we worked. We moved from formal email feedback to daily fifteen-minute check-ins where people could air half-formed ideas. I started asking "What would help you succeed?" instead of "Why isn't this done?" I admitted when I didn't know something. Within two months, Sarah redesigned her entire workflow—her idea, not mine—and proposed a module structure that became our standard.

That experience taught me that management isn't about control or clever systems. It's about creating conditions where talented people trust enough to do their best work. It meant being vulnerable first, which felt counterintuitive as someone climbing a startup hierarchy.

Three years later, I still think about Sarah when I'm tempted to hide behind process. The best decisions I've made have come from teams willing to challenge me. I want to build organizations where that happens reflexively, not reluctantly. That's what brings me to IIM Shillong—to deepen my understanding of organizational psychology and leadership systems, and to learn from faculty and peers who've grappled with these questions at scale.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
My professional journey began seven years ago when I joined a venture capital firm in Moscow as an analyst. Since then, I have worked in the dynamic and fast-paced world of investment and have developed a passion for identifying promising startups and helping them grow.

In my first role, I was responsible for conducting market research and due diligence on potential investment targets. I learned quickly about financial modeling, pitch evaluation, and the entrepreneurial mindset. After two years, I was promoted to Senior Analyst, where I took on more responsibilities in portfolio management and investor relations. This was a significant step in my career as it allowed me to work more directly with founders and understand their vision for scaling their businesses.

After five years at this firm, I decided to pursue new opportunities and joined a second venture capital company based in Cyprus. Here, I have been working as an Investment Manager, focusing on early-stage technology startups in the Mediterranean region. My role involves evaluating business models, assessing market potential, and building relationships with entrepreneurs. I have been involved in several successful investments and have gained valuable experience in international business development.

Throughout my career in venture capital, I have developed strong analytical skills, business acumen, and an understanding of what makes companies succeed or fail. I have learned to work with diverse teams and stakeholders, and I have become comfortable with ambiguity and fast-paced decision-making. The venture capital industry has taught me the importance of continuous learning and adaptation.

However, I recognize that to advance further in my career and take on more strategic leadership roles, I need to deepen my knowledge of business management, finance, and corporate strategy. An MBA from University of Cyprus will provide me with the necessary foundation in these areas while also allowing me to network with peers and professors who share my interest in entrepreneurship and innovation.

I am particularly drawn to the MSc Management program because of its emphasis on practical learning and its location in a growing startup ecosystem. I believe that studying in Cyprus will expose me to diverse perspectives and help me develop a more comprehensive understanding of international business. Additionally, the program's flexibility will allow me to continue my professional work while pursuing my studies.

My goal after graduation is to move into a principal or partner-level role at a venture capital firm, where I can lead investment decisions and mentor junior team members. I am committed to using my education to make a meaningful impact in the startup ecosystem and to support the next generation of entrepreneurs. The MSc Management program at University of Cyprus is the right step to help me achieve these objectives and to become a more effective leader in the venture capital industry.

prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
When I joined SunPath Energy in Delhi as a Project Coordinator in 2019, I was the youngest person on our solar farm development team. My role was administrative—scheduling meetings, coordinating between our engineering and commercial teams, processing permits. I had no direct reports, no budget authority, and certainly no seat at the table where decisions were made.

But three months in, I noticed something that nobody else seemed to care about: we were losing money on every site survey we conducted. Our field teams were visiting locations multiple times because the initial surveys were incomplete. I estimated we were throwing away about £15,000 per month on redundant work.

I knew I couldn't just walk into my manager's office and demand we fix the process. Instead, I started documenting everything. I tracked survey forms, identified which questions were being missed, and mapped out the additional visits each incomplete survey triggered. I spent two weeks compiling this data in the evenings.

Then I did something I'm still not entirely sure was smart: I invited the survey team lead, Rajesh, out for chai. Not to present findings. Just to listen. I asked him about the actual constraints—what was making surveys incomplete? He told me the forms were poorly designed, the field conditions were chaotic, and the team had no incentive to get it right the first time. That last bit was crucial.

I went back and built a simple Excel dashboard showing the cost impact and proposed a solution: redesign the survey form, add a checklist, and implement a minor bonus for surveys with zero rework. I sent it to Rajesh first, not my manager. He refined it, suggested improvements, and then we presented it together to our manager and the commercial lead.

The proposal was approved. Within two months, repeat surveys dropped by 70%. We were saving roughly £10,000 monthly.

What surprised me most wasn't the financial outcome—it was what the process taught me about how organizations actually work. I had assumed influence came from authority. But I'd actually influenced people by doing unglamorous work—listening, collecting evidence, and most importantly, making it easy for Rajesh to say yes. I didn't frame this as fixing his team's incompetence. I framed it as solving a systemic problem together.

I learned that leading without authority requires genuine curiosity about other people's constraints, not just solutions. It requires patience. And it requires ego-management—I had to be comfortable with Rajesh getting credit alongside me.

Since then, I've taken on similar informal roles—I led our first corporate sustainability reporting initiative, managed the transition when we merged with a German partner company, and mentored three junior coordinators, one of whom was eventually promoted to my level. These weren't formal responsibilities. But they've shaped how I want to lead.

I'm applying to the GMP-MiM program because I'm ready to formalize the leadership skills I've developed informally. I want to understand organizational behavior, strategy, and finance at a deeper level—to move beyond solving operational problems and into shaping business direction. My background in renewable energy has given me real-world complexity; XLRI's program can give me the frameworks to operate at a higher level.

school_name: Rice University Jones Graduate School of Business (Executive MBA)
prompt: What are your short-term and long-term professional goals? How will our MBA program help you achieve them?
content:
When I first moved to Houston three years ago to lead the Asia-Pacific sales division for my company, I thought I'd found my final role. I had regional responsibility for $47 million in annual revenue across seven countries, a team of twelve account managers, and the autonomy I'd always wanted. But somewhere between managing distributor relationships in Tokyo and negotiating hospital contracts in Singapore, I realized I was solving the same problems repeatedly without really understanding the underlying business dynamics that created them.

Last year, we lost a major contract in South Korea—a $6 million deal that should have been ours. Our clinical product was superior, our pricing was competitive, and we had strong relationships with the hospital's procurement team. But we lost to a competitor who understood their hospital's capital expenditure constraints better than we did. They'd structured a lease-to-own model that aligned with the hospital's quarterly budget cycle, while we were trying to sell them our standard payment terms. I remember sitting in the debrief thinking: I could manage a sales team blindfolded, but I don't actually understand healthcare economics, reimbursement models, or how hospitals make financial decisions. That realization was humbling and clarifying in equal measure.

My short-term goal is to transition into a commercial operations or market access role within medical devices over the next two to three years. Sales has taught me how to influence and build relationships, but I need formal training in financial analysis, market dynamics, and strategic planning if I want to move into roles where I'm shaping company strategy rather than executing it. I'm not interested in leaving sales entirely—the customer interaction is what energizes me—but I want to be the person in the room who understands the financial architecture behind every decision.

My long-term goal is to lead a global commercial organization for a medical device company. Whether that's as a VP of Commercial Operations, Chief Commercial Officer, or similar role, I want to oversee how a company creates and captures value across markets. I want to work on the bigger strategic questions: Should we acquire a smaller competitor? How do we price our products differently in emerging versus mature markets? What regulatory changes are coming and how should we position ourselves? These questions excite me in a way that hitting a quarterly sales number no longer does.

The Jones Executive MBA is the natural next step for both goals. The program's focus on healthcare management appeals to me—I need that industry-specific perspective, not just generic business school content. But beyond that, I'm drawn to the format. I have a family and substantial responsibilities here in Houston; a traditional full-time program isn't realistic for me right now. The Executive MBA structure allows me to continue performing in my current role while building the analytical and strategic foundation I'm missing. That's pragmatic, but it also matters that Jones is here. I've been building my network in Houston intentionally—relationships with other medical device leaders, hospital administrators, physicians. I don't want to pause that network-building for two years at a program in another city.

Specifically, I want to take advantage of the Healthcare Management courses, the Financial Analysis sequence, and what I understand is strong access to industry practitioners and case study work. I'm also genuinely interested in the Organizational Behavior and Strategic Management classes, because I know my blind spots aren't just financial—they're also about how to navigate organizational change and lead through ambiguity. The international student perspective I bring, combined with my medical device experience, might add something to classroom discussions too.

I'm not coming to business school to figure out who I am professionally. I know who I am—I'm a sales leader who's grown confident in her technical abilities. I'm coming because I'm clear about where I want to go, and I know exactly what's missing in my toolkit. Jones can help me fill those gaps in a way that keeps my life intact while actually accelerating my career trajectory.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
Dear Admissions Committee,

When I realized I'd been in the same role for four years, running the same quarterly forecasts and attending the same brand reviews, I knew something had to give. I wasn't failing—my team respected me, my numbers were solid, and Nestlé's compensation was more than comfortable. But I was managing decline, not building something. That's when I started thinking seriously about an MBA, and more importantly, about what comes after.

I've spent eight years in CPG, starting as an associate brand manager at Unilever in Toronto and moving through increasingly complex roles—from managing a $15M yogurt portfolio to my current position as senior marketing manager overseeing innovation and go-to-market strategy across five product categories. I'm good at the mechanics of this business: I can run a regression analysis, build a P&L, navigate supply chain constraints. What I've discovered is that these skills, while valuable, aren't enough for the leap I want to make.

Here's what I'm wrestling with: I see the structural problems in how large organizations approach sustainability and resource efficiency, but I don't have the financial literacy or strategic toolkit to fix them. I watch our sustainability initiatives become compliance exercises rather than genuine competitive advantages. I can identify where we're leaving money on the table, but designing capital-efficient solutions requires knowledge I simply don't have. That gap is what's driven me toward Hebrew University specifically.

I'm drawn to your program for three concrete reasons. First, your emphasis on the Israeli innovation ecosystem and entrepreneurship model aligns with where I want to go—I'm not interested in climbing the corporate ladder at a multinational. I want to build something in the climate tech or sustainable food space. Second, your faculty's work in strategy and financial analysis for emerging markets is exactly the framework I need to evaluate and launch ventures in these sectors. Third, the international cohort and the location itself matter. I've realized I think differently when I'm outside my comfort zone, and two years in Israel, building relationships with founders and investors across the region, feels like the right environment for this transition.

I'm not the highest GMAT score you'll see, and my academic record isn't spectacular. But I'm also not coming in theoretically—I'm coming in with eight years of real P&L responsibility, specific problems I want to solve, and a genuine sense that this MBA isn't a credential to collect but a bridge to a different kind of career. I've succeeded in structured environments because I'm disciplined and I care about results. What I need now is exposure to frameworks I don't have, mentorship from people who've built rather than managed, and permission to try something uncomfortable.

I'm ready for that.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
# Achievement I'm Most Proud Of

Throughout my career in renewable energy, I have accomplished many things. However, the achievement I am most proud of is leading my team to develop a new solar panel efficiency initiative that helped reduce costs and improve performance across our organization.

I have been working in the renewable energy sector for 11 years, and I have seen the industry evolve significantly. When I joined my current company five years ago, I noticed that our solar panel production was not as efficient as it could be. The team was working hard, but there were inefficiencies in the process that nobody had really addressed. I believed that if we could improve the efficiency of our solar panels, we could make renewable energy more accessible to people and contribute to a more sustainable future.

So I decided to take action. I gathered my team, which consisted of about eight engineers and technicians, and I proposed an initiative to redesign our solar panel manufacturing process. I believed that by making changes to how we manufactured the panels, we could improve their efficiency by at least 15 percent.

My team and I worked very hard on this project. We analyzed the existing process, identified the problems, and came up with solutions. We experimented with different materials and techniques. After many months of hard work and dedication, we were able to achieve a 12 percent improvement in efficiency. This was close to our goal, though not exactly what we had targeted.

The result of this initiative was very positive. The company was able to reduce production costs by approximately 8 percent, which saved the company money. We were also able to produce higher quality solar panels, which meant more customers wanted to buy them. This helped the company grow, and it was very rewarding to see the positive impact of our work.

I am proud of this achievement because it shows that I can lead a team and make a real difference in the company. It also demonstrates my ability to identify problems and solve them in a practical way. Furthermore, this initiative helped advance renewable energy technology, which is something I care deeply about.

This experience taught me many important lessons. First, I learned that teamwork is essential for success. Second, I learned that persistence and hard work are necessary to achieve goals. Finally, I learned that even when you don't achieve exactly what you set out to do, you can still create value and make a positive impact.

Looking forward, I believe that an MBA from Renmin University Business School EMBA will help me develop the skills I need to take on more challenging projects and lead larger teams. I want to continue working in renewable energy and help make the world more sustainable. With the knowledge and skills I gain from this program, I hope to achieve even greater things in my career.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
When I first learned about WSB's emphasis on entrepreneurial mindset embedded across the curriculum, I didn't just read the program overview. I reached out to Khun Sarah Chen, a Thai alumna who founded a logistics startup in Bangkok after graduating, and asked her directly: "Did the program actually prepare you to build something from zero?" She spent an hour on the phone with me describing how the venture design module forced her to stress-test assumptions she'd never questioned, and how the consulting project with Nestlé Thailand taught her how to navigate corporate stakeholders—skills she now uses when pitching to institutional investors.

That conversation mattered because I've been running my own business for the past six years. I co-founded a B2B manufacturing platform connecting Thai suppliers to regional buyers, and we've grown to serve 300+ clients across Southeast Asia. But growth has exposed gaps in my thinking. I optimize for execution and sales, but I've never been taught to think systematically about organizational design or strategic positioning. I watch competitors scale faster and wonder if I'm missing frameworks I don't even know exist. WSB's program—particularly the strategy and operations modules—addresses exactly this blind spot.

What drew me deeper was your placement of Thailand as a regional hub case study. Most programs treat Southeast Asia as peripheral, but WSB recognizes this region as a laboratory for innovation. I attended your online workshop on "Supply Chain Resilience in Emerging Markets" in February and was struck by how lecturers referenced local context, not just Harvard case studies. When Professor Mathias discussed the Central World incident's impact on logistics networks, I realized he understood the actual constraints I navigate daily. That specificity matters.

I'm not seeking an MBA to leave entrepreneurship—I'm seeking one to deepen it. I plan to leverage the consulting project to test a strategic expansion into Vietnam. The alumni network, particularly mentors with regional expansion experience, will be invaluable. WSB's cohort-based learning model also appeals to me; at 35 and nine years into my career, I want peers wrestling with scaling challenges, not just early-career professionals figuring out career direction.

I've built something real, but I recognize its ceiling. WSB offers both the intellectual tools and the community of practitioners needed to push beyond it.

school_name: Koç University MSc in Management & Strategy
prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
When I think about balancing competing interests, my role at PT Mitra Investasi comes to mind. As a portfolio manager, I was constantly juggling the needs of different groups—clients who wanted returns, risk-averse compliance officers, and aggressive traders on my team.

One situation stands out. In 2019, we were managing a fund with about $45 million in assets.
