A major client, representing 30% of our AUM, wanted us to invest heavily in emerging market bonds to boost yields. Our compliance team flagged concerns about concentration risk and regulatory exposure. Meanwhile, my traders were pushing to deploy capital quickly before rates shifted.

I had to find a solution that satisfied everyone. I proposed a phased approach: we'd allocate 15% to the bonds the client wanted, but through a diversified strategy across three countries instead of one. This gave the client exposure and returns, addressed compliance's risk concerns, and gave traders a timeline to execute methodically.

The client was happy because they saw their strategy implemented. Compliance approved it because we managed the concentration risk. The traders got their capital deployment without the rushed execution that could have caused errors.

This situation taught me about stakeholder management. I realized that when you listen carefully to each party's actual underlying concerns—not just their surface demands—you can usually find a middle ground that works.

However, I know I could have done better. Looking back, I should have involved all stakeholders in the discussion from the start rather than proposing a solution after the fact. It would have been more collaborative and probably would have built more trust. I also could have done deeper analysis on the regulatory implications instead of just accepting compliance's initial pushback.

In my next role, I want to develop stronger skills in navigating complex organizational dynamics. An MBA will help me understand the strategic frameworks for managing stakeholders—not just in finance, but across different business contexts. Koç's program appeals to me because it emphasizes both analytical rigor and organizational behavior, which I think I need to grow as a leader.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
When I joined the Singapore Armed Forces three years ago as a logistics officer, I quickly realized that our supply chain operated almost entirely on legacy systems. We had spreadsheets managing inventory for critical equipment across seven bases, manual verification processes that took weeks, and no real-time visibility into stock levels. The standard approach was to maintain high buffer stocks to compensate for these inefficiencies—a solution that worked, but was costly and wasteful.

Around my second year, I noticed something that bothered me. During a routine inventory audit at Changi Naval Base, I found that 23% of our critical spare parts were duplicated across multiple warehouses simply because no one had visibility into what existed elsewhere. When I raised this in a meeting, the response was predictable: "This is how we've always done it. The manual system is reliable." That's when I decided to push back, even though I was relatively junior.

I started by documenting the exact costs of our inefficiency. I spent two months mapping our entire supply chain process, quantifying waste in both time and money. The numbers were striking—we were spending roughly SGD 400,000 annually on unnecessary buffer stock alone. I then researched commercial supply chain solutions, but quickly realized that off-the-shelf software wasn't designed for military logistics constraints like security protocols and equipment classifications.

Instead of accepting that limitation, I proposed we build our own lightweight system. This was unconventional—we weren't a tech organization, and the budget wasn't there. But I convinced my commanding officer by framing it differently: this wasn't about adopting trendy software, it was about solving a concrete operational problem with limited resources. I partnered with two colleagues and built a basic inventory management tool over four months using open-source frameworks and our existing IT infrastructure.

The results were tangible. Within six months of implementation, we reduced buffer stock by 31%, cut our physical inventory verification time from three weeks to four days, and improved our spare parts availability rate from 87% to 94%. More importantly, other bases asked to adopt the system. Today, five of our seven bases use it.

What I learned from this experience is that challenging convention doesn't require grand ideas—it requires patience and evidence. I didn't walk into meetings demanding change. I gathered data, understood the real constraints, and built something that worked within those constraints. The military culture respects results and stability, not rhetoric, which actually made my case stronger.

This experience is why I'm drawn to the Renmin EMBA program. I want to build on this foundation and learn how to scale these kinds of innovations across larger, more complex organizations. Singapore is a small market, and I'm interested in how businesses in China approach the same tensions between operational efficiency and growth that I encountered in the military. I believe studying alongside executives from the mainland will give me the perspective I need to move into operations leadership roles where I can drive similar systematic improvements—but in the private sector where the scale and impact are magnified.

prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
When I look back at my career in asset management, I realize that not everything has gone according to plan. In fact, some of my biggest challenges have taught me the most valuable lessons.

Three years ago, I was working as a junior analyst at a mid-sized asset management firm in Madrid. I was responsible for analyzing European equities and preparing reports for the portfolio managers. I was confident in my abilities and thought I understood the market well. However, I made a significant mistake that showed me I still had much to learn.

I recommended that we increase our position in a tech company that I believed would perform well in the coming quarters. I did extensive research and felt very confident about this decision. I presented my analysis to my manager and the team, and they approved the recommendation. We invested a substantial amount of capital based on my analysis.

Unfortunately, things did not go as I expected. The company announced disappointing earnings results, and the stock price fell significantly. Our position lost money, and I felt terrible about this situation. It was a difficult time for me because I realized that my analysis had been incomplete. I had focused too much on the positive aspects of the company and had not adequately considered the risks.

This experience was humbling. I learned that in asset management, it is not enough to be confident in your research. You must also be willing to challenge your own assumptions and consider alternative perspectives. I realized that I needed to improve my risk analysis skills and my ability to think critically about investment decisions.

After this incident, I took several steps to improve. First, I started reading more about risk management and behavioral finance. I wanted to understand not just what happened, but why my analysis had failed. Second, I began having more conversations with senior colleagues about their investment processes and how they think about risk. Third, I started using a more structured approach to my analysis, where I explicitly considered different scenarios and downside risks.

In the following year, my performance improved. I became more careful in my recommendations, and I was more willing to say "I don't know" or "I need more time to analyze this" rather than rushing to conclusions. My managers noticed this change, and I received positive feedback on my more thoughtful approach.

This experience has stayed with me and has shaped how I approach challenges in my career. I believe that the ability to learn from failures is critical for success in business. When things don't go as planned, it is important to reflect on what happened and make concrete changes.

Looking forward, I want to develop my skills further through an MBA program. I believe that Syracuse Whitman's curriculum will help me become a more well-rounded professional and will give me the tools to advance in my career. I am excited about the opportunity to study business strategy and analytics, which will complement my practical experience in asset management.

school_name: The University of Chicago Booth School of Business
prompt: Upon reflection, how has your perspective regarding your future, Chicago Booth, and/or getting an MBA changed since the time of your last application?
content:
When I applied to Booth two years ago, I was running on the assumption that an MBA was a box to check before moving into leadership. I'd hit the ceiling at my startup in Sydney—Senior Product Manager at a 200-person company—and thought a top-tier program would be the credential that unlocked the next chapter. I was wrong about what I actually needed.

The past two years forced a reckoning. After our Series B funding fell through in 2022, I took a step back. For the first time in my career, I wasn't immediately problem-solving or optimizing for the next promotion. I spent six months consulting, actually thinking about what I wanted to build rather than what looked good on a resume.

That reflection changed everything. An MBA suddenly isn't about validation—it's about filling specific gaps I now recognize. Running a failed fundraise taught me I don't understand cap tables or equity structures well enough. Leading through our Series A downsizing showed me I lack formal training in organizational design and compensation strategy. These aren't gaps a mentor or a book closes. They require rigor and peer learning I can only get in a program.

But here's what shifted most: I'm clearer about why Chicago specifically. Two years ago, I probably would have said something generic about top-tier faculty and recruiting. Now I know I need a program that treats entrepreneurship as a serious discipline, not a side interest. Booth's Polsky Center and the density of classmates building or leading early-stage companies is what draws me. I want to learn finance and strategy from people who actually understand the messiness of capital constraints.

I'm also more realistic about what an MBA can't do. It won't guarantee my next venture succeeds. It won't make me a natural leader. But combined with my 12 years in tech and a clearer sense of what I'm missing, it's actually the right move now—not the move I thought I needed two years ago.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
# Essay Response

Throughout my twelve years in the pharmaceutical industry, I have encountered numerous situations that required me to balance business objectives with ethical considerations. One particular incident stands out as a turning point in my professional development.

During my tenure at a mid-sized biotech company, I was leading a team responsible for regulatory compliance for a new drug candidate. We were approaching a critical milestone in our development timeline, and the commercial pressure to meet deadlines was significant. Our management had set aggressive targets, and the entire organization was focused on achieving them.

One day, while reviewing quality control data from our manufacturing partner, I noticed some anomalies in the test results. The data suggested potential issues with product consistency, though the results were still within acceptable ranges according to standard protocols. My initial instinct was to proceed as planned since technically, the results met regulatory requirements.

However, I began to question whether meeting the minimum standard was truly ethical. I realized that the anomalies, while not critical, could indicate deeper quality issues that might not be immediately apparent. I faced a dilemma: I could either proceed with the submission to regulators as planned, or I could flag the issue and delay our timeline significantly.

I recognized that this was not just a compliance issue, but a matter of patient safety and corporate integrity. I decided to escalate the findings to our quality assurance team and requested a deeper investigation. This decision meant potentially delaying our regulatory submission by several weeks, which would impact our financial projections and stakeholder expectations.

I communicated my concerns to senior management, explaining that while the data technically passed our standards, the anomalies warranted further investigation to ensure long-term product quality. The initial response was skeptical, as the delay would affect our timeline. However, I provided data-driven analysis showing the potential risks of proceeding without further verification.

After careful deliberation, the company decided to conduct additional testing. This investigation revealed that our manufacturing partner needed to adjust their processes to ensure consistent quality. The delay ultimately lasted three weeks, but it prevented what could have become a serious quality problem later.

This experience taught me that ethical decision-making often requires looking beyond minimum compliance requirements. It also showed me that integrity sometimes demands accepting short-term setbacks for long-term gains. The incident reinforced my belief that in pharmaceuticals, where patient health is at stake, we cannot compromise on quality standards.

The experience made me more conscious about the importance of ethical leadership and how leaders set the tone for organizational culture. I now actively advocate for thorough due diligence and encourage my team members to voice concerns without fear of repercussion. This is why I am seeking an MBA—to develop a deeper understanding of business ethics and leadership principles that will enable me to navigate complex situations with greater strategic insight.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
One of the biggest challenges I faced in my entrepreneurial journey was figuring out how to scale my online retail business without significantly increasing operational costs. This problem kept me up at night because I knew that if I didn't find a creative solution, my business would plateau and I would lose market share to larger competitors.

The problem was straightforward but difficult: my small team was overwhelmed with order fulfillment, and hiring additional staff would eat into my already thin profit margins. I needed to find a way to handle more orders while keeping costs manageable.

I decided to implement a creative solution by partnering with local universities to create an internship program. The idea was simple but innovative – I would offer undergraduate students the opportunity to work part-time in exchange for mentorship and professional experience. This would give them valuable work experience while providing me with flexible, affordable labor.

I reached out to three universities in the Manila area and presented my proposal to their business schools. The response was overwhelming. I received applications from over 200 students interested in the internship program. I carefully selected 15 interns and created a structured training program to ensure they understood my business values and operational standards.

The implementation was smooth because I invested time in creating comprehensive training materials and assigning experienced team members as mentors. Within the first month, my order fulfillment time decreased by 40%, and I was able to handle 50% more orders without hiring permanent staff. The interns gained real-world experience, and some of them eventually became full-time employees.

This solution was creative because it solved multiple problems at once. I reduced my labor costs, increased my operational capacity, and built a talent pipeline for future hiring. The interns also benefited tremendously from the experience, and I received thank-you emails from students saying that the internship changed their lives.

However, I also learned important lessons. Not all interns performed equally, and managing a large group of part-time workers required more supervision than I initially anticipated. I had to develop better systems for tracking productivity and ensuring quality control. By the third month, I had to let go of three interns who weren't meeting expectations, which was a difficult decision.

Looking back, this experience taught me that creativity isn't just about having a good idea – it's about execution, flexibility, and willingness to adapt when things don't go as planned. I also realized that I need to develop stronger people management skills to lead larger teams effectively.

This is why I want to pursue an MBA at IIM Kozhikode. I believe that a rigorous business education will help me develop the strategic thinking and leadership skills necessary to scale my business further and potentially expand into other markets. I am excited about the opportunity to learn from experienced faculty and collaborate with classmates from diverse backgrounds who will challenge my thinking and help me grow as an entrepreneur.

school_name: African Leadership University School of Business MiM
prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
# What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?

When I look back at my eight years in private equity, the accomplishment I'm most proud of isn't the one that made the biggest financial return or earned the most recognition from my partners. It's the transformation of our portfolio company's finance function, which taught me that impact isn't always measured in millions of dollars.

In 2019, we acquired a mid-market manufacturing company in Lahore for $47 million. On paper, it looked solid—strong margins, established customer base, decent management team. But within the first month, our CFO flagged a critical problem: the finance department was essentially non-functional. Records were fragmented across spreadsheets and outdated systems, cash flow projections were unreliable, and no one really understood the company's profitability by product line or customer segment.

The previous owner had never invested in building proper infrastructure. They'd grown the business through relationships and hustle, which works until it doesn't. Now we owned a company generating $85 million in annual revenue that couldn't accurately report its own performance.

I took this on personally. I knew we couldn't hire an experienced CFO immediately—the market for finance talent in Pakistan was tight, and frankly, any good CFO would want a bigger platform. So I spent the first six months embedded in Lahore, working alongside the existing finance team to rebuild everything from scratch. We implemented proper accounting systems, created cash flow models that actually tracked reality, and most importantly, I trained the existing staff instead of replacing them.

The team was skeptical at first. They'd been doing things a certain way for years, and suddenly this woman from our investment firm was reorganizing their entire world. There was resistance, honestly. Some people left. But I stayed patient. I worked weekends. I sat with the junior accountants and taught them how to read balance sheets. I brought in the plant managers to show them why understanding unit economics mattered for their bonuses.

By 2021, this company's EBITDA had grown to $18 million, and that growth was *real* because we could finally see where it was coming from. More importantly, we successfully recruited a strong local CFO who could run the function independently. When we exited in early 2023, the buyer was willing to pay a significant premium partly because of the financial infrastructure we'd built.

But here's why this matters to me more than other deals I've worked on: I realized I actually cared more about building something sustainable than hitting a return target. I cared about whether the 180 employees in that factory still had jobs and whether they understood how their work connected to the company's success. I cared about whether that CFO we hired had a real career path.

This accomplishment challenged my assumptions about what success looks like in private equity. The industry trains you to focus on value creation through operational improvements and financial engineering. Both matter. But I learned that the most meaningful value comes from actually developing people and institutions—especially in emerging markets like Pakistan where there's chronic underinvestment in talent and systems.

This realization is why I'm pursuing an MBA now. I want to formalize my understanding of how to scale impact, not just returns. I want to work in environments where business success and human development aren't seen as competing priorities. And honestly, I want to be intentional about where I focus my career next, rather than just following the traditional PE trajectory.

That experience in Lahore showed me what I'm actually energized by. And that matters more than any number on an exit memo.

school_name: Gordon Institute of Business Science (Executive MBA)
prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
I've spent the last decade learning how to read between the lines of financial statements, but I rarely talk about why that matters to me. It's easier to discuss audit methodologies or tax implications. Yet sitting in GIBS information sessions, I keep coming back to one question: what happens when I stop hiding behind spreadsheets?

I'm a senior manager at Deloitte's Tokyo office, leading a team of twelve across regulatory consulting. We work with major financial institutions on compliance frameworks—important work, technically sound work. But two years ago, I realized I'd become very good at solving problems I didn't fully understand. I could implement Basel III requirements without grasping how they shaped lending decisions that affected real people's access to capital. I was competent and disconnected.

That discomfort drove me to push for a client secondment in 2022. I spent four months embedded at Mizuho Financial Group, working directly with their lending strategy team instead of auditing them from a distance. The shift was uncomfortable. I wasn't the expert anymore. But I learned more about how financial institutions actually think—the tension between regulatory compliance and business growth, the politics of change management, the way quarterly pressures override long-term vision—than I had in eight years of compliance work.

That experience revealed something I can't unknow: I want to shape strategy, not just ensure it meets regulations. I want a seat at the table where decisions get made, not a reviewing position behind it.

The EMBA appeals to me precisely because I'm not starting from scratch. I'm not trying to figure out what business is; I understand technical depth in finance. What I lack is the breadth—the frameworks to think about operations, consumer behavior, organizational design, global strategy. And honestly, I lack confidence. I've been in analytical roles so long that I question whether I can lead differently. GIBS's intensive cohort model, where I'd work alongside leaders from across industries and geographies, is what I need. I can't get that from a distance learning program or part-time program scattered across semesters.

The South African context matters too. I've worked in Japanese banking regulation, the most risk-averse system I know. Seeing how GIBS engages with emerging market economics, entrepreneurship, and innovation across Africa would directly challenge my assumptions about what's possible. Japan's financial system is incredibly stable and incredibly cautious. I want to understand different approaches.

Non-binary identity shapes how I move through professional spaces—I've learned to observe before asserting myself, to listen carefully to unstated power dynamics, to find spaces where I can be authentic. These aren't weaknesses in a cohort environment; they're what let me absorb and contribute differently. I'm looking for a program that values different perspectives, not just diverse backgrounds.

I'm eleven years into my career with enough experience to know what I don't know and enough humility to be genuinely uncertain. GIBS's Executive MBA isn't a credential stamp for me. It's the specific environment where I can develop the strategic thinking, cross-functional fluency, and leadership confidence I need to move from managing compliance risk to shaping financial strategy. And more importantly, it's where I can finally stop hiding.

school_name: University of Queensland Business School Executive MBA
prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
# Leadership Experience Essay

When I joined Novagen's Clinical Operations team in Lima five years ago, I was the first woman in a management role within that division. The team of twelve—mostly men with 15+ years in the company—were skeptical. I knew I couldn't lead through credentials alone.

My first month, I noticed we were losing two weeks per trial cycle to miscommunication between regulatory and site teams. The process wasn't broken exactly, but it was slow. Instead of imposing changes, I started sitting with both teams separately, asking questions. What frustrated them? What did they wish they had? This took four weeks of my time, but I learned the regulatory team felt invisible—their concerns were dismissed by sites eager to enroll patients quickly.

I created a simple shared dashboard tracking both enrollment and compliance metrics. More importantly, I established weekly huddles where both teams presented their priorities. Nothing revolutionary, but it created visibility. Within two months, we cut cycle time by 35%.

The interesting part came six months later. One of my senior coordinators, Marco, wasn't hitting his targets. His files had errors that delayed approvals. I could have performance-managed him out, which was the company's usual approach. Instead, I asked why. He admitted he'd been overwhelmed and too proud to ask for help. We restructured his workload temporarily and paired him with a junior coordinator as a mentor—not because Marco needed training, but because teaching helped him regain confidence.

This changed how I thought about leadership. In Peru, I grew up watching my father—a factory supervisor—rule through authority. He never asked questions; he gave orders. His team respected him, but they feared him. I watched people leave. I told myself I'd be different, but I hadn't really understood what that meant until Marco.

What I learned is that my job isn't to have all the answers or to prove I deserve my seat at the table. It's to create conditions where good people do their best work. Sometimes that means systems and dashboards. Sometimes it means noticing when someone is drowning. The best insights came from listening more than talking, from understanding what motivated people individually rather than assuming everyone wanted the same things.

This philosophy has shaped everything since. When I moved to my current role managing a 28-person team across three countries, I've applied the same approach—less directive, more curious. We have the fastest trial recruitment rates in the Asia-Pacific region now, and our retention is 98%.

I'm drawn to an Executive MBA partly because I want to formalize this intuition. I want to understand the theory behind what's worked, and honestly, to identify where I'm still blind. I know good leadership goes beyond good intentions, and I'm ready to build on what I've learned in the field.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I started as an audit associate at a mid-sized firm in Lahore right after my BCom degree—the kind of entry-level role where you're drowning in spreadsheets and coffee, learning how to read a balance sheet like it's literature. For three years, I worked through various audit engagements, mostly with manufacturing and textile clients. The work was methodical, sometimes tedious, but I realized I had a knack for spotting discrepancies that others missed. More importantly, I started asking why things were done a certain way instead of just accepting the process.

That curiosity pushed me to take my CPA exams while working full-time, which honestly, I wouldn't recommend to anyone. But once I qualified, I moved into a senior auditor role and started leading smaller engagements. I was maybe 26, managing people older than me, which taught me quickly that competence matters more than age—though communication matters even more.

Five years ago, I shifted to financial consulting at a larger firm. This was the jump that changed how I thought about my career. Instead of just auditing what companies had done, I was advising them on what they should do. I worked with clients across energy, pharmaceuticals, and real estate, helping them restructure their finance functions, implement new systems, or prepare for due diligence before acquisitions. I led a team of four analysts, managed budgets around 2.5 crore rupees annually, and actually had to think about delivery timelines, client relationships, and staff development—not just technical accuracy.

The work has been genuinely fulfilling, but I've hit a ceiling. I'm strong at the details and the people side, but I feel the gaps in my strategic thinking. I haven't worked abroad, I haven't sat in boardrooms with C-suite executives, and I don't have the language to move into those spaces yet. An executive MBA feels like the bridge I need.

I chose Smith specifically because of its Canadian cohort diversity and the professional network that actually leverages South Asian talent. I'm ready to move beyond being a strong technical contributor and learn how to think like a business leader.

prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
When our operations director at OMV announced a three-month plant shutdown for maintenance in 2019, I was a mid-level engineer with no formal authority over the contractors. Yet the project timeline sat at our feet like a live wire.

The facility produced 12,000 barrels daily, and every day beyond ninety meant roughly €600,000 in lost revenue. I wasn't the project manager. I wasn't even the senior engineer. But I'd spent five years at that plant, and I knew something the new project manager didn't: our contractors had historically underestimated equipment lead times by weeks.

I started informally. Over coffee with the procurement lead, I shared historical data I'd compiled—showing three past projects where delays stemmed from ordering parts too late. I didn't present it as criticism. I framed it as pattern recognition I'd noticed. He listened, probably because I wasn't telling him what to do.

Then I organized a weekly coordination meeting. No one asked me to do this. I just sent invitations to the contractor leads, our internal teams, and the project manager. I brought coffee. I kept meetings to thirty minutes. The real work happened in between—quiet conversations where I'd flag potential bottlenecks before they became crises.

By week four, the contractor's equipment orders were three weeks ahead of schedule. The project manager later told me he'd noticed but didn't realize I'd orchestrated it. That was the point. I wasn't trying to be visible. I was trying to get the job done.

We finished the shutdown in eighty-six days, saving the company roughly €240,000. More important, I learned something about influence that no textbook had taught me: authority comes from credibility and follow-through, not titles. The contractors trusted my technical knowledge and my ability to problem-solve without making their lives harder. The internal teams appreciated that I was reducing their workload, not creating more bureaucracy.

The harder lesson was recognizing what I couldn't control. Two contractors still missed deadlines on smaller components. Despite my planning, external suppliers failed us. I couldn't will reality into a different shape, and trying harder doesn't overcome systematic bottlenecks outside your sphere.

This experience shaped how I now approach cross-functional projects. I've become the person who asks clarifying questions early, who sees risk before it crystallizes, who builds relationships before I need them. In an industry like oil and gas where projects involve dozens of competing interests and vendors, this skill matters more than formal authority ever could.

At GIM, I'm drawn to the cohort-based model because I want to develop this further—to understand influence not just operationally but strategically. I want to learn how to scale this approach as I move into roles where I'll have formal authority, and I need to understand the business and organizational dynamics I've only experienced from the engineer's vantage point.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
# What is Your Most Significant Accomplishment, and Why Does It Matter to You?

Three years ago, I was sitting in a conference room at Medtech Solutions in Shanghai when my manager asked me to lead the regulatory approval process for our new cardiac monitoring device in the EU market. I was 29, had been with the company for four years in a product development role, and had never managed a regulatory submission before. My first instinct was to say no.

The EU market represented €8 million in potential annual revenue—our biggest opportunity that year—but the regulatory landscape was something I barely understood. CE marking, MDR compliance, notified body engagement: these weren't concepts I'd worked with. I spent that evening reading through 200 pages of EU Medical Device Regulation documentation, feeling increasingly out of my depth. But I also felt something else: if I didn't do this, no one would. The regulatory team was stretched thin, and my manager believed I could figure it out.

I didn't figure it out alone. I called the heads of three notified bodies across Germany and Belgium. Most rejected me initially—they didn't have capacity. But I kept calling, refined my pitch, and eventually found one in Hamburg willing to take us on. I then assembled a cross-functional team: two quality engineers from Shanghai, an external consultant who'd done this before, and our clinical team lead. We met every Monday morning for eighteen months.

Those meetings were painful at first. I asked questions that probably made the consultant roll his eyes. Why did we need 500 patient data points instead of 300? What exactly was the difference between a risk assessment and a hazard analysis? But I listened, took notes, pushed back when something didn't make sense, and gradually the regulatory framework stopped being abstract and became real. Around month six, I realized I wasn't just managing a compliance checklist—I was learning to think like a regulator. I was asking: what could go wrong with this device? How do we prove it won't? How do we communicate that to someone who's never seen our product?

We hit a major obstacle in month fourteen. The notified body requested additional biocompatibility data that our original device design didn't fully support. This meant either a six-month delay to retest everything, or a hardware redesign that would push back our entire launch. I brought the team together and we spent a week modeling both scenarios. In the end, I recommended the redesign—faster, but it meant asking our design team to rework something they'd already finalized. That was tough. I had to go to my director and admit that part of my original timeline was wrong.

But we got it done. In September last year, we received CE marking approval. The device launched in Germany, France, and Italy in Q1. In our first year on the market, we've generated €6.2 million in revenue across those three countries. More importantly, we've learned enough from that submission that our next two device approvals took half the time.

Why does this matter to me? Because I discovered I can learn things I have no business knowing. I wasn't a regulatory expert, and I still wouldn't call myself one, but I became competent enough to lead a complex, high-stakes project across multiple countries and languages. I learned that admitting what you don't know is actually a strength, not a weakness. And I learned that the difference between a successful product launch and a failed one often comes down to someone being willing to sit in uncomfortable meetings and ask dumb questions until they become smart ones.

I want to grow beyond product development. I want to understand business strategy, financial modeling, and how to scale operations across markets. An Executive MBA at University of Hertfordshire appeals to me because I need both the technical knowledge and the perspective that comes from studying alongside working professionals from different industries. I'm ready for the next challenge.

prompt: Describe a time when you led a team through a significant challenge. What was the outcome?
content:
In 2021, our drug safety team at Vertex faced a crisis that forced me to lead differently than I ever had before.

We were preparing our quarterly report for the FDA—the kind of submission that, if delayed, could cost us millions and damage patient trust. Three days before deadline, I discovered that our adverse event database had corrupted during a system migration. Two weeks of reported side effects were compromised. We couldn't just resubmit; the FDA would flag it immediately.

My first instinct was to panic. I'd been a senior analyst for years, comfortable with the technical work. Leading a team of eight through a genuine disaster felt different.

I called an emergency meeting and did something I'd never done before: I told them the truth without sugarcoating it. I said, "This is bad. We might miss the deadline. But here's what I know we can do." Then I laid out the reality: we had 48 hours to manually reconstruct the lost data, validate it against our backup systems, and prepare documentation for the FDA explaining the incident transparently.

I split the team into three groups—reconstruction, validation, and FDA narrative. I assigned each person based on their strengths, not seniority, which meant I put two junior analysts in charge of validating our reconstruction work. Some people questioned that. I defended it because they were detail-oriented in ways the senior people weren't.

What surprised me was how much people stepped up when I stopped trying to be the expert. I spent 16 hours that week in that office, but not because I was working harder than everyone else—I was coordinating, unblocking obstacles, bringing coffee, and checking in on people who were hitting walls. When our validation lead broke down on day two from exhaustion, I told them to go home. We covered their work.

We submitted at 11:47 PM, thirteen minutes before deadline.

The FDA accepted our submission without requiring additional investigation. Our transparency about the incident actually built credibility with them.

That experience taught me that leadership isn't about having all the answers. It's about assembling people who do, trusting them enough to put them in uncomfortable positions, and then removing the barriers between them and success. It's something I want to build on at Ross—understanding how to scale that kind of team culture as I move into more complex management roles.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
Leadership is something I have always believed in. Throughout my career, I have tried to be a leader in everything I do, and I think this is important for success in business and in life.

At my current company, which is a large tech firm, I work in the product operations department. I have been there for three years now. During this time, I have had many opportunities to lead, and I have learned a lot about what it means to be a leader. One time that stands out is when I was asked to lead a project.

The project was about improving our internal processes. There were about eight people on my team, and we needed to figure out how to make things more efficient. This was a big responsibility, and I was nervous at first because I had never led such a large project before. But I knew that I could do it, and I wanted to prove myself.

I started by having meetings with all the team members to understand what they thought the problems were. Everyone had different ideas, and it was my job to listen to them and figure out what we should do. I created a spreadsheet to track all the ideas and then I prioritized them based on what I thought would have the most impact.

We ended up implementing three new processes that we thought would help. One of them was a new way to organize our files, one was a new meeting structure, and one was a new way to communicate with other departments. I made sure that everyone understood what they needed to do, and I held regular check-ins to see how things were going.

The result was that we saved about 10 hours per week across the team. This was good because it meant that people could focus on more important things. My manager was happy with the results, and the team seemed satisfied with the changes.

I think this experience taught me a lot about leadership. I learned that you need to listen to people and make them feel heard. I also learned that you need to be organized and keep track of things. And I learned that it is important to follow up and make sure that your plans are actually working.

I believe that I have the qualities that make a good leader. I am reliable, I am organized, and I care about my team. I think that an MBA from Kellogg would help me develop these skills even more. I want to be able to lead bigger projects and maybe even lead a team or a department in the future. I think that the education and training at Kellogg would help me achieve this goal.

In conclusion, leading the process improvement project showed me that I have what it takes to be a leader. I demonstrated my ability to manage a team, organize information, and achieve results. I am excited about the opportunity to study at Kellogg and continue developing my leadership abilities so that I can make a positive impact in the business world.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
I am writing to express my strong interest in the MSc Management program at the University of Bologna. Throughout my career in venture capital, I have developed a deep passion for understanding business and creating value, and I believe this program will be instrumental in helping me achieve my career goals.

For the past nine years, I have worked in venture capital, first as an analyst and now in a more senior capacity. During this time, I have been exposed to many different industries and business models. I have learned how to evaluate companies, understand financial statements, and work with entrepreneurs. This experience has been invaluable, but I recognize that I need a more formal educational foundation to progress further in my career.

My primary career aspiration is to become a venture capital partner. To achieve this, I need to develop stronger business acumen and a deeper understanding of management principles. I believe that the MBA program at the University of Bologna will provide me with the knowledge and skills necessary to succeed in this role. The program's curriculum, which includes courses in finance, strategy, and operations, will allow me to build on my practical experience with rigorous academic training.

Additionally, I am interested in eventually establishing my own investment fund focused on supporting African entrepreneurs and tech startups. This is a passion of mine because I believe that Africa has tremendous untapped potential, and venture capital can be a driver of economic growth and innovation on the continent. The Bologna MBA will help me develop the business skills and networks needed to launch and manage such a fund successfully.

I am particularly drawn to the University of Bologna because of its international reputation and strong emphasis on business fundamentals. The program's location in Europe will also provide me with exposure to different business practices and markets, which will be valuable given my ambitions to work across continents.

I also believe that my international background as a Kenyan applicant will add diversity to your cohort. I bring a unique perspective from East Africa to classroom discussions and case studies, and I am eager to learn from my classmates who come from different countries and industries.

In conclusion, the MSc Management program at the University of Bologna represents the next logical step in my career journey. By combining my nine years of practical experience with a rigorous MBA curriculum, I will be well-positioned to achieve my goal of becoming a venture capital partner and, ultimately, to establish my own investment fund focused on African tech startups. I am confident that this program will provide me with the tools necessary to succeed, and I am excited about the opportunity to contribute to the University of Bologna community.

Thank you for considering my application.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
# Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.

Throughout my eight years in investment banking, I have had the opportunity to work on several significant transactions and build strong relationships with clients. One achievement that stands out is my contribution to closing a major M&A deal that helped establish my position as a trusted advisor in my firm.

During my time at a top-tier investment bank in São Paulo, I was assigned to a complex cross-border transaction involving two large companies in the manufacturing sector. The deal was valued at approximately $200 million and required extensive coordination between multiple teams, including financial analysts, lawyers, and industry specialists. My primary responsibility was to build the financial models and prepare the pitch materials that would convince the client to move forward with our firm as their advisor.

What made this deal particularly challenging was the tight timeline. We had only three weeks to prepare comprehensive materials for the client presentation, and the scope of work was much larger than what we typically handled. Many of my colleagues thought it was impossible to complete everything on time, but I believed it could be done if we worked efficiently and stayed focused.

I took charge of organizing the workstreams and made sure everyone on the team knew exactly what they needed to deliver. I spent many late nights at the office, sometimes working until 2 or 3 in the morning, to ensure the financial models were accurate and the presentations were polished. I coordinated with the other departments to integrate their work into our materials. Through strong project management and dedication, we delivered a comprehensive pitch book that exceeded the client's expectations.

The client was impressed with our work and decided to appoint our firm as their exclusive financial advisor for the transaction. This was a significant win for our team and helped establish our reputation in the manufacturing sector. The deal eventually closed successfully, and I received recognition from senior management for my leadership and execution.

This achievement is something I am proud of because it demonstrated my ability to manage complex projects, lead teams effectively, and deliver results under pressure. It showed that I could handle responsibility and that clients trusted my work. It also taught me valuable lessons about what it takes to succeed in a demanding industry.

Beyond the immediate success of the deal, this experience reinforced my understanding of what I want from my career. I realized that I am most fulfilled when I am solving complex problems and working with talented people toward a common goal. The investment banking environment has given me excellent technical training and exposure to business strategy, but I believe I have grown as much as I can in this role.

An MBA from IIM Mumbai will help me transition into a leadership position where I can apply my technical skills and client management experience to drive strategic growth in an organization. The program's focus on developing business leaders and its strong network in India will be invaluable as I explore opportunities in emerging markets.

I am proud of what I have accomplished in banking, but I recognize that I need broader business knowledge and strategic thinking skills to take the next step in my career. An MBA will provide me with the tools and connections I need to become an effective business leader.

school_name: Stockholm School of Economics Executive MBA

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
# What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?

In 2019, I convinced the Vietnamese Ministry of Defense to restructure how we managed logistics across three regional commands. This doesn't sound dramatic, and at first, I didn't think it was significant. But the process of making it happen changed how I see myself as a leader.

I'd spent eight years in operations, watching the same problem repeat: our supply chains were fragmented. Each command operated independently, hoarding inventory, duplicating purchases, creating waste. A simple spare part for military vehicles might cost $40 in one region and $120 in another. We were hemorrhaging money we didn't have.

When I was assigned to the Strategic Planning Division, I had an opportunity to fix this, but it meant asking colonels and generals to give up autonomy. In the military hierarchy, that's not how things work. You don't ask your superiors to relinquish control. You follow orders.

I spent six months building a case. Not with spreadsheets—everyone had those. I traveled to each command, sat with logistics officers, and listened to their actual problems. I learned that the regional commander in Da Nang wasn't being stubborn about independence; he was terrified his supply lines would fail if he depended on a centralized system. The officers in Hanoi worried about political risk. These weren't operational obstacles. They were human ones.

So I designed the restructuring around their fears. I proposed a hybrid model: centralized purchasing and data, but regional control over deployment and timing. I built redundancies into the system. I created a governance council where each command had a vote. I wasn't asking them to surrender control—I was asking them to share it.

I presented this to the Deputy Minister myself. That part I was terrified about. I'm not from the capital. My family background is modest. I'd never given a briefing above brigadier general before. But I had something the brass didn't: I'd actually talked to the people who had to live with this system.

The restructuring was approved in late 2019. By 2021, we'd cut procurement costs by 34% across the three commands. We reduced redundant inventory by nearly $2 million. More importantly, we proved that centralized strategy and distributed execution could work in an organization built on command-and-control.

What matters to me about this isn't the money saved or even the operational efficiency, though both are real. It's that I learned something about myself: I'm most effective when I stop trying to be the smartest person in the room and start trying to understand what people actually need. I learned that in a hierarchical military context, the only way to drive change upward is to build trust downward first.

That experience is exactly why I'm pursuing the Executive MBA. I've realized I can operate well within military systems, but I want to understand how to lead across different structures entirely. EM Lyon's emphasis on organizational change and cross-cultural leadership appeals to me specifically because I need to develop capabilities beyond the military context. I spent twelve years mastering one environment. I'm ready to master others.

The accomplishment itself was meaningful. But understanding why it worked—and how it changed me—that's what matters.

school_name: Universidad Torcuato Di Tella Executive MBA
prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
When I told my partner at Deloitte that we should abandon the entire financial model our team had spent six months building, I could see the blood drain from his face. We were three weeks from presenting to the CFO of a major Argentine bank, and I was suggesting we throw out everything.

The engagement was straightforward in theory: help the bank modernize their credit risk assessment process. The conventional approach—the one we'd been following—was to overlay their legacy system with new analytics tools while keeping the existing approval workflow intact. It made sense on paper. It was low-risk, implementable in their timeframe, and it was what they'd asked for in the RFP.

But I'd spent the previous month embedded with their credit teams, watching them work. And I noticed something that didn't appear in any of their process documentation: they were ignoring the model's recommendations. Not always. But consistently, when the model flagged deals as risky, experienced loan officers would approve them anyway—and they were usually right. I started asking why.

The answer was simple: the model didn't know what the loan officers knew. It didn't account for long-term customer relationships, unwritten community networks, or how economic conditions in specific provinces affected different industries. These weren't gaps in the data. They were things the best credit people carried in their heads after twenty years in the business.

So I came back and said we needed to flip the entire approach. Instead of trying to make the system smarter, we should make it a tool that surfaced the officers' implicit knowledge. Build it so the model learns from their decisions, flags patterns they might have missed, and most importantly, gives them confidence they can trust it because they helped create it.

My partner thought I was insane. The bank had specifically asked for a quantitative solution. We'd already quoted them a fixed price based on the original scope. And honestly, I couldn't guarantee it would work.

But I also couldn't ignore what I'd seen. So we pushed back on the deadline—we lost the original timeline, which was painful—and completely restructured the proposal. Instead of handing them a black box, we designed a collaborative build process where the credit team would iterate with us on the model.

The bank's CFO was skeptical at first. But when we showed him our observation about the loan officers overriding the original recommendations, something clicked. He approved the new approach.

What happened next validated every instinct. During the build phase, the credit team actually engaged with the model instead of viewing it as an imposition. They caught problems we would have missed. When we deployed it, adoption was immediate—not because we'd forced it on them, but because they understood it. Within the first year, the bank reduced non-performing loans by 18% and accelerated their approval process by 40%.

The lesson I took wasn't really about being right (though that was nice). It was about having the courage to abandon what looks good on a PowerPoint when you have evidence it won't work in reality. Consulting teaches you to respect process and frameworks. That's valuable. But it also teaches you to defend your recommendations, even when they're inconvenient.

That's why I'm drawn to Di Tella's executive program. You're educating leaders in a region that's constantly reinventing itself economically and politically. That requires exactly this mindset—respecting tradition enough to understand it, but challenging it when the moment demands it. I want to deepen my ability to do that at scale, beyond one bank's credit process.

In seven years of consulting, I've learned I don't want to be the person who applies yesterday's solutions to today's problems. I want to build that instinct into everything I do next.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
Three years ago, I had to fire someone I hired. His name was Mehmet, and he reported directly to me as a sales engineer at our B2B SaaS company in Istanbul. He was technically competent—that wasn't the issue. The problem was that he treated junior team members like they were beneath him. In our weekly one-on-ones, I watched him dismiss ideas from recent graduates, cut people off in meetings, and send curt Slack messages that bordered on hostile.

I kept hoping it would fix itself. I gave feedback. Nothing changed. So I started avoiding the conversation, which was cowardly.

When I finally sat down with Mehmet, I didn't sugarcoat it. I told him directly that his behavior was damaging the team and that he had two months to show meaningful improvement or we'd part ways. He pushed back—hard. He said I was inexperienced, that I didn't understand how business worked, that the younger engineers needed toughening up. For a moment, I doubted myself. He was older. He had more industry experience. But then I realized that doubt was exactly the problem. I was prioritizing his comfort over my responsibility to the entire team.

He didn't improve. Two months later, he left.

The difficult part came after. I had to manage the guilt. I kept replaying conversations, wondering if I'd been too harsh, if I should have given more chances. But what stuck with me was something different: the immediate shift in the team's energy. People started volunteering for projects. Meetings became collaborative instead of performative. Junior team members began speaking up. I realized I'd been so focused on not wanting to hurt Mehmet's feelings that I was inadvertently hurting everyone else.

That experience taught me that leadership isn't about being liked—it's about being clear about expectations and honest about consequences. It's about protecting the culture you're trying to build, even when it's uncomfortable. But it also taught me something equally important: that clarity without empathy is just cruelty with a different name.

After that, I completely restructured how I managed. Instead of hoping people would "get it," I started having explicit conversations about what success looked like in their role. I began one-on-ones differently—asking people what they needed from me, not just reviewing their metrics. When someone wasn't performing, I addressed it immediately, but I also asked why. Sometimes it was a mismatch. Sometimes it was personal circumstances I didn't know about. Sometimes it was my failure to communicate what I expected.

Within a year, my team grew from eight to fourteen people. Retention improved from 65% to 89%. More importantly, the quality of work fundamentally shifted. We launched a new product vertical in Q2 that generated $2.3M in ARR by the end of the year—our fastest product launch ever.

I don't think any of that would have happened if I'd kept avoiding hard conversations. I also don't think it would have happened if I'd just become someone who fires people when things don't work out.

This is why I'm applying to Ivey. I want to formalize what I've learned through these messier, real-world experiences. I've picked up management philosophy through trial and error, but I want to understand the frameworks behind it. I want to study organizational behavior, strategic HR, and decision-making—not because I need to validate what I already know, but because I know how much I still don't understand. The MSc in Management appeals to me specifically because it's practical and rigorous at the same time. I'm not looking to unlearn what I've learned. I'm looking to build on it with deeper, more structured thinking. And honestly, I think I'd bring something real to the classroom—not just theories, but actual scars from trying to figure this stuff out.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
Two years ago, I discovered our team had been systematically underbilling a major client. Not through malice—through a technical flaw in our usage tracking system that underreported their API calls by roughly 8%. Over eighteen months, they'd been charged perhaps $180,000 less than they should have been.

I found this while auditing our billing infrastructure. My manager's first instinct was practical: "We're not obligated to tell them. They have their own cost monitoring. This is their visibility problem." The finance team agreed. We could quietly fix the system and move forward.

But I couldn't. Not because I'm virtuous—I'm not—but because the logic was fundamentally dishonest. We knew. We had an obligation once we knew.

What made this complicated was that the client was already frustrated with us over separate implementation delays. Raising a billing issue felt like adding salt to an open wound. I genuinely worried they'd terminate the contract entirely, which would hurt our team's numbers right before review season. I was up for a promotion. These were real stakes.

I pushed back with my manager. Not aggressively—I understood his position. Instead, I came with options. We could: (1) refund the difference immediately, accepting the hit; (2) refund with service credits to make it less painful to our P&L; or (3) propose they audit their own usage and we'd reconcile from there. I wanted to give our team a path that felt less like self-sabotage.

We chose option two—roughly $145,000 in refunds and $35,000 in service credits. I drafted the client communication myself, framing it as us investing in transparency because we valued the relationship.

The client's response surprised me. They appreciated the honesty. It actually strengthened the relationship. We expanded the contract six months later.

What I learned: The honest path isn't always the harder path. Sometimes it's just the one that requires you to sit with discomfort longer before acting. And there's usually a solution that doesn't require you to choose between integrity and your career—you just have to work harder to find it.

My time at IIM Calcutta would deepen this understanding. I want to study in an institution that takes seriously the connection between ethics and sustainable business decision-making. India's rapid technology sector growth creates exactly these dilemmas, and I want to understand them within a genuinely global context.

prompt: What are your short-term and long-term professional goals? How will our MBA program help you achieve them?
content:
When I started at Google in 2012 as a software engineer in São Paulo, I was focused on one thing: writing clean code. Twelve years later, I've moved through several roles—individual contributor, tech lead, program manager—and I've realized that the technical skills that got me here won't take me where I want to go.

My short-term goal is to move into product strategy within the next two years. I've seen too many talented engineers frustrated because we're building features nobody needs, or building them for the wrong market. I want to be the person in the room who bridges that gap—who understands both what's technically possible and what customers actually need. At Google, I've started gravitating toward these conversations, but I lack the formal framework to do it well. I can feel the gap in my understanding of market dynamics, business models, and how to actually think about trade-offs beyond engineering constraints.

Long-term, I want to lead product teams at a growth-stage company, probably outside the Bay Area. After twelve years in the FAANG bubble, I'm interested in the messier, more interesting problems that startups face—where resources are scarce and every decision has real financial consequences. I'd like to eventually move back to Brazil or take on a role with a company focused on emerging markets, but I need to understand the business side much better to do that credibly.

Here's what I'm honest about: I don't know if I'll land at a startup or stay in tech. I don't know if it's São Paulo or somewhere else. What I do know is that I need to think differently about business than I currently do. An MBA will give me the language, frameworks, and network I need.

Rutgers makes sense for me because of the location and flexibility. I'm working full-time and will be for the foreseeable future, and your part-time program fits my life. Your courses on product management and strategy appeal to me specifically, and the location in New Jersey keeps me close to New York tech networks without relocating.

I'm not sure this is the perfect fit, but it's the right next step.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I tell people I'm leaving Deloitte's London office, their first question is usually "where are you going?" The second is almost always "why not Harvard or London Business School?" The answer to that second question is why I'm applying to LUISS.

Three years into my consulting career, I was leading a team on a digital transformation project for a €2.2M retail client. We were building the roadmap. The work was technically sound—proper methodologies, stakeholder alignment, risk mitigation. And it was completely sterile. I remember sitting in the war room at our London office thinking: I have no idea if any of this actually matters to the people running this company. We were optimizing processes, not understanding context.

That realization sent me to Rome for a week—something I'd been meaning to do for years. I stayed in Testaccio, got slightly lost daily, ate too much cacio e pepe, and had coffee with my Italian university friend Marco who'd stayed in Rome after our Erasmus semester. He ran a small design consultancy with eight people. Over espresso, he was grappling with the same business problems I was—how do you grow strategically, how do you hire the right people, how do you maintain values as you scale—but he was doing it in a place where you can't hide behind process. Rome doesn't let you. Everything is relational, everything is about understanding the deeper context.

I came back to London knowing I needed to move. Not away from consulting—I still think the skillset is valuable—but toward a context where strategy connects to culture and place. Where I can work in an environment that actually cares about how decisions ripple through real communities.

LUISS appeals to me specifically because it sits in that intersection. Your program isn't just about equipping people with frameworks; it's rooted in Italy and Europe, where business success is inseparable from understanding complex stakeholder ecosystems, regulatory environments, and social dynamics. The electives in stakeholder capitalism and sustainable business strategy are genuinely what I want to study, not just programs designed to look good on a LinkedIn profile.

What I'll contribute: I bring five years of Big 4 rigor. I know how to build business cases, read financials, and manage complex projects. More importantly, I've learned what good consulting gets wrong—the ability to see the client's actual world beyond the spreadsheet. I'm curious and I ask uncomfortable questions. My cohort at Deloitte would tell you I'm the person who pushes back on assumptions and who genuinely wants to understand why something works, not just that it does.

I'm also genuinely excited to be part of a smaller program. I want to know my classmates. I want substantive conversations about business and purpose that extend beyond the classroom. I'm ready to be rooted somewhere again, and Rome—or at least a program deeply connected to Rome—feels like exactly where I should be.
