prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I decided to leave my position as Program Manager at Ankara Youth Initiative after four years, my colleagues thought I was walking away from impact. What I was actually doing was running toward it—just in a different direction, and with better tools.

At AYI, I managed community development projects across three districts, working with a budget of roughly €400,000 annually and coordinating a team of eight. We did good work: designed a vocational training program that placed 240 young people into jobs over two years, built partnerships with local businesses, created sustainable models. But by year three, I hit a wall. I was executing strategies designed by others, managing operations, but I wasn't equipped to think strategically about *why* our interventions worked—or didn't. When our job placement program underperformed in Ankara's eastern districts despite outperforming in central areas, I had no framework to diagnose whether it was a targeting problem, a curriculum issue, or something about local market dynamics. I could see the gap in my own thinking.

That gap is why I'm applying to Lyon's MSc Management Sciences, specifically your program's focus on data-driven decision-making and organizational strategy. Lyon's curriculum isn't abstract to me. When I read about your quantitative methods courses, I thought about the employment data we collected but barely analyzed. When I saw the organization strategy module, I thought about the five strategic plans I've helped implement without truly understanding how to evaluate trade-offs or measure competitive positioning. The NGO sector desperately needs people who can bridge operations and strategy—who can look at a problem, bring rigor to it, and actually *know* what they're doing instead of intuiting it.

I also chose Lyon specifically because of your location and network. France is where I want to anchor this next phase. Turkey's NGO ecosystem is growing but fragmented; France has more mature civil sector infrastructure and stronger partnerships between social enterprises and academic institutions. I want to understand how France structures social impact work differently. More practically, your program's strong industry connections in Western Europe matter to me. I'm not looking to jump to consulting or banking. I want to return to nonprofit work—ideally leading a regional program or eventually directing an organization—but I want to do it from a position of real strategic competence.

To your community, I'll bring operational realism. I've seen what breaks in ambitious nonprofit projects: misaligned incentives, measurement theater, partnerships that exist on paper. I can push back on ideas that sound good but won't survive contact with reality. I'm also someone who asks questions when things don't make sense, who gets frustrated with groupthink, and who will actually show up to study groups at 8 a.m. even though I'd rather sleep in. My colleagues have described me as reliable and stubborn—usually as compliments.

I'm ready to invest deeply in this program, not as a stepping stone, but as a necessary foundation for work I care about.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
I'm most proud of how I helped our client at Bain restructure their supply chain operations, but not for the reasons you might expect.

Going into the engagement, I was the most junior person on a team tasked with overhauling logistics for a €200M manufacturing company in Grenoble. The partner had already decided the answer: consolidate three regional warehouses into one. It made sense on paper. The math worked. It would save €3.2M annually.

But during my third week of site visits, I kept noticing something odd. The fastest delivery times weren't coming from the central hub—they were from the smallest, oldest warehouse in Lyon. I started pulling historical data, which is not glamorous work. I stayed late three nights comparing shipment patterns, customer locations, and actually spoke to the warehouse staff (which junior consultants on MBB projects often skip). The operations manager casually mentioned that their region's customers had unpredictable demand spikes because of seasonal automotive contracts.

I realized the centralization strategy would backfire for this segment. Instead of the clean one-warehouse model, I proposed keeping a distributed network but rebalancing inventory allocation using a dynamic model. The partner wasn't thrilled. McKinsey didn't hire me to suggest messy solutions.

But I pushed back respectfully, showed the financial modeling, and more importantly, I brought in data from the actual site rather than just theoretical optimization. We tested it on a pilot region for four weeks. It worked.

The client implemented the modified approach, and by quarter two they saved €2.8M instead of €3.2M. That half-million difference meant they kept the Lyon warehouse staffed and avoided layoffs in a region with limited job alternatives. For a 23-year-old consultant, admitting the textbook solution was wrong felt risky.

What I'm proud of isn't the marginal savings—it's that I trusted what the data actually showed rather than what prestigious consulting models predicted. I learned that being rigorous means questioning frameworks, not just executing them. It taught me to value lived experience alongside analytics.

This is partly why I'm drawn to Macquarie. The Master of Management appeals to me because it emphasizes adaptability across industries. I want to move beyond pure consulting optimization into roles where I can apply this lesson at scale—understanding that the best solution is often contextual, not universal. France's public sector modernization work interests me specifically because it requires exactly this kind of thinking.

prompt: Describe a difficult decision you made and the process that you went through to make it.
content:
# The Cost of Loyalty

Three years ago, I had to decide whether to leave Meridian Software, the SaaS company where I'd spent the last six years building the customer success team from scratch. I was Director of CS, managing 12 people and overseeing our largest 40 enterprise accounts. The decision kept me up at night for weeks.

The situation was straightforward on the surface: I had been recruited by a competitor, Apex Solutions, also in the enterprise data space. They offered me a VP of Customer Operations role, a 35% salary increase, and the chance to scale a function at a much larger company. It felt like the obvious next step. I was ready for VP-level responsibility, and my friends in tech said I'd be stupid to turn it down.

But leaving meant abandoning something I cared about. When I joined Meridian as the first CS hire in 2015, we had maybe five enterprise customers. I built our playbooks from nothing—how we onboarded, how we measured health, how we prevented churn. By 2021, those processes had helped us retain 95% of our customer base and grow ARR by $8 million. My team wasn't just competent; they were invested. I'd worked hard to create a culture where people actually wanted to show up.

The CEO asked me directly: "What would keep you?" That question was harder than I expected. It wasn't about money anymore. I realized that what I'd built at Meridian felt like mine in a way nothing else had. At Apex, I'd be inheriting systems, fixing someone else's problems. It was the safer, more prestigious choice.

I spent two weeks talking to people I trusted. My mom, who'd left her engineering job in Moscow to follow my father, told me something I hadn't expected: "Sometimes the best career move is not about the title. It's about being somewhere you believe in." My manager at Meridian didn't push me either way, which somehow made it harder. There was no external pressure forcing my hand.

What ultimately moved me was specific: our largest customer, Tyrell Manufacturing, was about to renew their contract. They'd had a terrible implementation under our previous vendor and were skeptical about everything. I'd spent eighteen months rebuilding their trust, personally. If I left, I worried that relationship would collapse. It sounds small, but it represented what felt broken about just optimizing for title and money.

I turned down Apex. I negotiated a promotion to VP at Meridian instead, and equity that reflected my stake in the company's future.

Looking back, I don't know if it was the right decision. Meridian hit some rough patches the next year—we missed our growth targets, and I watched people I hired get laid off during a restructuring. Apex went public. Sometimes I wonder if I was naive about loyalty in an industry where it's not reciprocated.

But I learned something about how I make decisions. I'm not purely rational. I care about the people I work with and the things I've built. That's probably a weakness in some contexts. For an MBA program, though, I think it matters. I need to understand not just financial models and strategy, but how to lead people through uncertainty and build cultures that last. Desautels' focus on executive leadership and organizational behavior is exactly where I need to deepen my thinking about these questions.

prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
In my second year at Crescent Ventures, I was assigned to evaluate a Series A round for a supply chain software company. The investment decision rested with our three senior partners, but the due diligence fell to me and two analysts who reported to a different partner.

We quickly discovered a problem. The startup's technology was solid, but their unit economics didn't work in the Saudi market, where logistics costs are significantly higher than in the US. I ran the numbers three times. The deal looked good on paper for their current markets, but scaling here would require a different pricing model entirely.

I had no authority to kill the deal. I wasn't a partner. I wasn't even the most senior analyst on the evaluation team. But I knew this was wrong, and I couldn't stay silent.

I scheduled separate meetings with each analyst first. I showed them my revised projections, asked for their thoughts, listened to their concerns. One agreed immediately. The other pushed back—he thought the partners' experience would matter more than our models. I didn't argue. I just asked him to review the calculations himself.

Then I wrote a memo. Not a recommendation, but a detailed analysis with scenarios. I was careful not to say "don't invest." I presented the facts: the market size, the cost structure, the assumptions baked into the founders' plan. I included what would need to change for this to work in our region.

I sent it to all three partners.

The deal didn't happen. One partner thanked me afterward; another asked why I hadn't raised this earlier. That stung. Looking back, I could have escalated sooner instead of hoping the obvious numbers would speak for themselves.

What I learned: influence without authority requires evidence, not certainty. I'd been waiting to be 100% sure before speaking up. But leadership in analysis means raising flags when something doesn't add up, even if you're not certain you're right.

It also taught me that being junior doesn't mean staying quiet. But it does mean approaching senior people with questions, not conclusions. I could have saved everyone time by reaching out earlier and framing it as "I'm seeing something that doesn't work—am I missing something?"

That experience shaped how I approach my current role. I speak up earlier. I ask better questions. I've learned that in VC, the people willing to push back on flawed thinking are more valuable than those who accept consensus.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
My professional journey began four years ago when I graduated with a degree in Business Administration. Like many graduates, I was eager to start my career and make an impact in the business world. I knew that I wanted to work in a dynamic industry where I could grow quickly and learn valuable skills that would serve me well throughout my career.

My first role was as a Junior Sales Associate at a large retail company. In this position, I was responsible for meeting sales targets and providing excellent customer service to our clients. I learned the importance of hard work and dedication during these early months. The retail environment taught me how to interact with diverse groups of people and manage my time effectively. After about eighteen months, I was promoted to Senior Sales Associate, which gave me more responsibility and a slight increase in salary.

Following this promotion, I decided to transition into the e-commerce space because I felt that the future of retail was moving online. This decision was driven by my passion for digital innovation and my desire to be part of the rapidly growing online retail sector. I secured a position as an E-commerce Coordinator at a mid-sized online retailer. This role involved coordinating between different departments to ensure that products were listed correctly on our website and that our inventory systems were functioning properly.

In my current role as an E-commerce Manager, I oversee a team of three coordinators and am responsible for managing the product database and ensuring that our online platform runs smoothly. We have seen steady growth over the past two years, and I believe that my contributions have played a part in this success. I manage budgets, liaise with suppliers, and help with strategic planning for new product launches.

Throughout my career, I have learned several important lessons. First, I have come to understand that attention to detail is critical in any business environment. Second, I have realized that effective communication with team members is essential for achieving goals. Finally, I have learned that continuous learning and self-improvement are necessary in today's competitive business landscape.

I have always been interested in furthering my education and developing my professional skills. An MBA would provide me with the theoretical knowledge and practical tools that I need to advance to a senior management position. I believe that studying at Insper would help me build on my existing experience and prepare me for future leadership roles. The program's curriculum is well-respected, and I am confident that it will equip me with the skills necessary to succeed in a competitive business environment.

My ultimate goal is to move into a General Manager position within the next five years, where I can oversee all aspects of a retail or e-commerce business. I am committed to my professional development and believe that an MBA is the right next step in my journey.

school_name: EM Lyon Business School MSc in Management
prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
I should note that there's a mismatch in your prompt—you've asked me to write for EM Lyon's MSc in Management, but the essay prompt references Kellogg's (Northwestern's business school). I'll write the essay using Kellogg's actual prompt since that's what was provided, and I'll make it authentic to an admitted applicant.

---

Three years ago, I was the only non-manager on a team of eight at Oi Telecomunicações tasked with launching our 4G infrastructure rollout across three states. I didn't have the title that would have made this easier. I had the problem.

Our timeline was crushing us—twelve months to deploy across São Paulo, Minas Gerais, and Rio Grande do Sul, or we'd miss our regulatory window and lose millions. The team was fractured. Engineering wanted to build from scratch. Operations wanted to retrofit existing towers. Finance kept moving the budget goalposts. My director was checking in every other week, increasingly frustrated.

I did something I'd never done before: I admitted I didn't know how to fix it alone, and I asked each person to teach me their constraints in detail. Not in meetings. Actual conversations. I spent six weeks sitting with our engineering lead in the telecom depot at 6 AM, learning why her timeline assumed no unforeseen complications. I drank terrible coffee with our operations manager and learned that retrofitting wasn't just faster—it was safer for field teams. I reviewed the Finance director's spreadsheets line by line and understood where he saw risk, not just "no."

Then I did something riskier: I proposed we were all right, and all wrong.

I built a hybrid model—something nobody had suggested because it seemed obvious that one approach would win. We'd retrofit the priority urban corridors (Operations was right about speed), but use new infrastructure in lower-density areas where we had time (Engineering's quality concerns made sense there). I modeled it out with real numbers and real timelines, flagging where we'd need contingency funds.

My director was skeptical. "You're creating more complexity," he said. I told him the complexity already existed; I was just naming it honestly.

He took the proposal up. It got approved, modified, and became our actual plan.

The launch succeeded. We hit 89% of our deployment targets that year—not perfect, but we launched on schedule. More importantly, that hybrid approach became our template for the next phase across the Northeast. We delivered on time and $4.2 million under budget. The team that had been fragmented became almost comfortable with each other.

I learned something uncomfortable about myself: I had been afraid to lead without a formal role. I thought leadership meant having the answer or the authority to make people implement it. What actually happened was messier—I had to sit in the discomfort of not knowing, ask better questions than I had answers for, and give credit to the people whose expertise I'd borrowed.

That's what changed. I stopped waiting for a title. I started noticing when teams were stuck because nobody felt safe saying what they actually thought. I started being the person who could sit with contradictions and ask: what if both are true?

I'm drawn to Kellogg because I want to understand leadership at scale—how to do this work in bigger, messier systems. At 8 years in telecommunications, I've learned I'm effective at fixing broken collaboration. I want to build real expertise in organizational dynamics and strategy so I can do this work more intentionally, and help teams do it themselves.

The bravery isn't always in being certain. Sometimes it's in saying you're not.

school_name: University of Bath School of Management (Executive MBA)
prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
# Career Progression Essay

Since graduating from university in 2021, I have been fortunate to work in the media and entertainment industry, which has always been a passion of mine. My career journey has been exciting and full of learning experiences that have shaped me as a professional.

I started my first role at a digital media company in Sydney as a Content Coordinator. In this position, I was responsible for creating content for various social media platforms and assisting the senior team with campaign management. During my two years there, I worked on multiple projects and developed skills in content creation, project coordination, and team collaboration. I learned a lot about how media companies operate and gained valuable experience in a fast-paced environment.

My main responsibilities included scheduling posts across platforms like Instagram, Facebook, and TikTok, managing editorial calendars, and conducting basic analytics to measure content performance. I also helped coordinate with external partners and vendors for content shoots and events. This role gave me a solid foundation in understanding audience engagement and the importance of data-driven decision-making in media.

Throughout my time at this company, I realized that I wanted to develop deeper expertise in business strategy and management. I enjoyed the collaborative aspects of my work and found myself increasingly interested in how business decisions were made at the senior level. I noticed that many of the leaders in my company had completed an MBA, and this sparked my interest in pursuing further education.

After two years, I decided to move into a new position as a Digital Marketing Assistant at another media firm, where I have been working for the past few months. In this role, I am involved in marketing campaign development and helping to coordinate digital initiatives. This has given me exposure to different aspects of the business and reinforced my desire to pursue an MBA.

I am drawn to the University of Bath School of Management's Executive MBA programme because I believe it will equip me with the strategic thinking and business acumen necessary to progress into management roles within the media industry. The programme's focus on practical, real-world business applications appeals to me, and I am excited about the opportunity to learn from experienced faculty and peers.

Through this programme, I hope to deepen my understanding of business strategy, financial management, and organisational leadership. I am particularly interested in how these principles can be applied to the media and entertainment sector, which is constantly evolving. I believe that an MBA will not only enhance my technical skills but also provide me with a broader perspective on business and management.

My career progression so far has been a journey of discovery, and I am ready to take the next step in my professional development. I am confident that the Executive MBA at University of Bath will provide me with the knowledge and network necessary to achieve my career goals and make meaningful contributions to the media industry.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
# Leadership Experience and Management Philosophy

Leadership is something I have always been passionate about. Throughout my career at Google, I have had the opportunity to lead teams and develop my own management philosophy. One experience that particularly shaped how I think about management was when I was promoted to Senior Manager of the Cloud Solutions team in 2019.

When I took on this role, I inherited a team of twelve people who were underperforming. The team had high turnover, low morale, and the previous manager had not set clear expectations. I knew I needed to make changes quickly. I immediately implemented several initiatives to improve the team's performance.

First, I held one-on-one meetings with each team member to understand their concerns and goals. I listened to what they had to say and tried to understand their perspectives. I also introduced a new performance management system that was more transparent and fair. This helped to create accountability and clarity around expectations.

Second, I focused on creating a positive team culture. I organized team building events and made sure to celebrate wins, no matter how small. I believed that a happy team is a productive team, so I tried to make the work environment more enjoyable.

Third, I invested in the professional development of my team members. I made sure they had access to training and mentorship opportunities. I also tried to create clear career paths so they could see how they could grow within the organization.

Within six months, the team's performance improved significantly. Turnover decreased by 40%, and we met our targets for the quarter. The team was much happier and more engaged.

This experience taught me that good leadership is about listening to your team, setting clear expectations, and investing in people. I believe that as a manager, my job is to create an environment where people can do their best work. It's not about being the smartest person in the room or making all the decisions. It's about empowering your team and helping them grow.

I also learned the importance of being authentic as a leader. I tried to be genuine with my team and share my own struggles and challenges. I think this helped them feel more comfortable being vulnerable with me.

Another important lesson from this experience is the need for continuous improvement. Even after the initial improvements, I kept looking for ways to make the team better. I was always asking for feedback and trying new approaches.

Going forward, I want to continue developing my leadership skills. I believe that an MBA from Rotterdam School of Management will help me understand management theory at a deeper level and learn from other experienced leaders. I am excited about the possibility of studying in a European business school and connecting with peers from different industries and countries.

My management philosophy is centered on people. I believe that investing in your team and creating a positive culture is the key to business success. I want to be the kind of leader who brings out the best in others and helps them achieve their full potential.

In conclusion, my experience at Google taught me that leadership is about serving your team and creating an environment where they can thrive. As I progress in my career, I want to apply these lessons to lead larger teams and eventually move into executive leadership. I believe the Executive MBA at Rotterdam will provide me with the tools and knowledge I need to take my career to the next level.

school_name: University of Leeds Business School MSc Management
prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
During my second year at Barclays in Singapore, I helped structure a leveraged buyout for a $450 million acquisition. The deal looked strong financially—clean multiples, solid management team, strategic fit. But two weeks before closing, our due diligence team flagged something uncomfortable: the target company had been consistently underreporting environmental liabilities in their manufacturing facilities across Malaysia.

The liabilities weren't material enough to kill the deal. They were buried in footnotes, technically disclosed. The client—a private equity firm I'd worked with before—was expecting us to close. My MD suggested we could flag it to the buyer without making a bigger issue of it. Legally sound. Commercially convenient.

But I kept thinking about the manufacturing workers, and about what "technically disclosed" actually meant.

I had three options: stay quiet, flag it quietly to the buyer and move on, or push back internally. The first was out. But I was genuinely torn between options two and three. My career was still building. The MD was influential. And there was a real possibility that pushing harder would be seen as naive—the kind of thing junior bankers do before they learn how the world works.

I requested a meeting with the MD and our compliance lead. I wasn't confrontational about it. I said: "I'm not comfortable with how we're positioning this, and I think there's actual risk for us here—reputational and otherwise." I presented what we knew, what the buyer should know more clearly, and why our credibility mattered more than a fast close.

The MD didn't love it. But he respected that I'd come to him directly rather than talking around it. We ended up sending a clearer memo to the buyer with fuller environmental context. The deal still closed. The buyer factored in the liabilities properly.

What struck me afterward wasn't that I'd been brave—I hadn't risked that much. It was realizing that ethical compromises rarely announce themselves. They come packaged as pragmatism. And every time you accept a small one, the next one becomes easier to rationalize.

That experience shaped how I approach work now. I'm genuinely drawn to Leeds because of your embedded focus on responsible business practice. I want to study somewhere that doesn't treat ethics as a compliance checkbox, and that values the kind of decision-making I learned is actually harder than it looks.

school_name: WHU — Otto Beisheim School of Management MiM
prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
Three years ago, I made a decision that cost my company nearly $200,000 and damaged relationships I'd spent years building.

I was a production manager at Huafeng Manufacturing, overseeing a team of 12 people on the plant floor. We'd just won a contract with a German automotive supplier—our biggest client at the time—to produce brake components. The specifications were tight, the timeline was aggressive, but I was confident. I'd managed similar projects before, and I didn't want to appear uncertain to my new leadership team.

The problem started small. During the design review phase, I noticed some ambiguity in the technical drawings. The German engineers and our sales team had interpreted the tolerances differently. Instead of flagging this immediately, I decided to move forward and figure it out during production. I told myself I could solve it faster than escalating the issue would take. I thought I was being efficient.

I was being reckless.

We started production in Month 2, and by Week 3, we realized that 40% of our output didn't meet specifications. The tolerances were wrong. Fixing the dies would take two weeks and cost roughly $180,000. Worst of all, our customer was now two weeks behind schedule, and they had their own deadlines to meet.

I remember sitting in the call with the German team, listening to their frustration, knowing it was entirely preventable. My plant manager asked why we hadn't caught this earlier. I didn't have a good answer. The truth was that I'd prioritized speed over rigor because I wanted to prove myself.

What made this worse was how I initially handled the fallout. For about a week, I kept trying to minimize the situation in reports to leadership. I focused on what we could recover rather than what we'd lost. I was more concerned with protecting my reputation than being honest about my role. It took a difficult conversation with my plant manager—who didn't yell at me, but simply asked why I thought that approach was helping—for me to realize how much I was making things worse.

I apologized to the entire team, to our customer, and to leadership. Specifically, I took responsibility for not raising concerns early, and I outlined exactly what I would do differently. Then I actually did it.

For the next three months, I implemented a checkpoint system before major production phases. Any technical ambiguity had to be documented and resolved in writing with all stakeholders—no verbal agreements, no assumptions. It slowed down our sales cycles slightly, but it prevented similar problems. We won back the customer's trust, though it took time. They stuck with us for another contract two years later.

The deeper lesson wasn't just about process rigor. It was about recognizing that my insecurity—wanting to prove my competence quickly—was making me less effective, not more. By trying to avoid looking uncertain, I created actual uncertainty that harmed people who depended on me. The irony was painful.

This experience fundamentally changed how I work. I now ask more questions earlier. I'm more comfortable saying "I don't know, but here's how we'll find out" rather than pretending confidence. And I've learned that taking responsibility isn't a career risk—avoiding it is.

I still think about that contract sometimes, particularly when I'm tempted to move fast at the expense of clarity. It's taught me that good leadership requires admitting when you're wrong and actually changing your behavior, not just apologizing and moving on. That's what I want to build on in my MBA: stronger analytical frameworks so my instincts are better informed, and the perspective to make decisions based on what's right rather than what makes me look good in the moment.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I tell people I work in tech startups, they usually assume I code. I don't. What I do is figure out how to make products that ten people want into products that ten thousand people want. After seven years bouncing between three different startups here in Southeast Asia, I've learned that scaling is less about technology and more about people, systems, and making hard decisions with incomplete information.

I'm applying to SKEMA because I've hit the ceiling of what I can do with pattern recognition and hustle alone. At my current company, a logistics SaaS startup in Manila, I run product strategy and partnerships. We've grown from $200K to $3M in annual recurring revenue over three years, which sounds great until you realize that growth without structure is just chaos with momentum. Last year, I watched a deal with a major regional distributor fall apart because I couldn't articulate our unit economics clearly enough. I had the numbers, but not the framework to tell the story. That moment made me realize I needed actual business training, not just startup survival skills.

SKEMA appeals to me for a few specific reasons. Your emphasis on international business resonates because my work is inherently regional—I'm constantly navigating different regulatory environments, market maturity levels, and business cultures across the Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam. I'm also drawn to your focus on digital transformation and entrepreneurship. The startup ecosystem here is growing but still fragmented. I want to understand best practices from more mature markets and bring them back in a way that actually fits our context, not just copy-pasted from Silicon Valley playbooks.

But here's what I'm still figuring out: I'm not sure if I want to build my own company again or if I'd rather work in strategy at a larger organization. That uncertainty actually pushed me toward a one-year program instead of the two-year options. I need focused time on fundamentals—finance, operations, data-driven decision-making—without the commitment of disappearing for two years while my industry evolves rapidly.

What I can offer SKEMA is perspective from the edges. Most of your cohort probably comes from developed markets or larger companies. I can bring ground-level knowledge about how tech actually gets adopted in emerging markets, where infrastructure is unreliable, where trust works differently, where innovation sometimes means solving problems that don't exist in the developed world. I'm also someone who doesn't just think about business in the abstract. I've fired people. I've negotiated contracts where I didn't know if we'd make payroll. I've sat through customer meetings where someone told me our product didn't work for them, and I had to figure out why.

I'm not the strongest test taker—my GMAT reflects that—but I've never been someone who operates only on the familiar. I'm applying to SKEMA because I'm ready to learn rigorously, and because I believe a business education should prepare you for the world as it actually is, not just as it appears in case studies.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
During my seven years at Deloitte Singapore, I have encountered many situations where I needed to balance competing interests. One particular experience stands out as a significant learning moment in my professional journey.

I was assigned to lead the financial audit of a mid-sized manufacturing company with approximately 200 employees. The client wanted us to complete the audit in four weeks to meet their year-end reporting deadline. However, my team of four junior auditors was simultaneously working on another engagement that was running behind schedule.

The challenge was clear: the client expected full dedication and timely delivery, while my team was stretched thin and at risk of burnout. My senior manager wanted us to prioritize the first client, as they were a major account. Meanwhile, my team members were frustrated and concerned about working excessive hours.

I realized I had to find a solution that satisfied everyone. First, I communicated transparently with both clients about realistic timelines and resource constraints. I explained to the manufacturing company that we could deliver quality work within their deadline, but we needed to be strategic about the audit scope. With the second client, I negotiated a phased approach where we could deliver interim findings while continuing with detailed testing.

Next, I reorganized our work plan. I identified tasks that could be outsourced to our offshore team in India, which freed up capacity for my Singapore-based team. I also implemented a rotating schedule so that no single team member worked more than fifty hours per week. This required careful planning, but it ensured sustainable workload distribution.

I held regular check-ins with my team to understand their concerns and adjusted our approach accordingly. I also maintained frequent communication with both clients, providing updates and managing expectations proactively.

The outcome was positive. We delivered the first audit on time and within quality standards. The second client appreciated our phased approach and actually extended our engagement. My team felt valued and supported, which improved morale. The senior manager was satisfied with how I handled the competing demands.

This experience taught me that balancing stakeholder interests requires clear communication, creative problem-solving, and putting people first. It also showed me that sometimes the best solution involves compromise from all parties rather than trying to satisfy everyone completely.

As I pursue my MBA at JBIMS, I am eager to develop my leadership and strategic thinking skills further. I believe this program will provide me with frameworks and perspectives to handle increasingly complex stakeholder management situations in my future career.

school_name: ICN Business School Master in Management
prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
# Professional Setback

During my time at Pharmatech Solutions, I faced a significant challenge that tested my resilience and taught me valuable lessons about leadership and accountability.

In 2019, I was leading a cross-functional team responsible for launching a new diagnostic product in the Latin American market. As the project manager, I believed we were on track to meet our Q3 deadline. However, due to poor communication between our regulatory affairs and commercial teams, we missed critical compliance milestones. When this became apparent, we were forced to delay the launch by six months, which resulted in lost revenue and damage to our team's credibility.

This was a difficult moment for me. I had to accept that I had not properly established clear communication channels, and I had failed to anticipate potential bottlenecks. Instead of making excuses, I took full responsibility for the oversight. I realized that as a leader, I needed to be more proactive in ensuring alignment across departments.

To recover from this setback, I implemented several changes. First, I created a detailed project management system with weekly check-ins between all stakeholders. Second, I introduced a risk assessment framework to identify potential issues before they became problems. Third, I invested time in building stronger relationships with the regulatory team to better understand their constraints and timelines.

The outcome was positive. The next product launch was completed on schedule, and the team's confidence in the project management process was restored. More importantly, this experience taught me the importance of communication, humility, and continuous improvement. I learned that setbacks are not failures—they are opportunities to grow as a leader.

This experience has shaped my approach to problem-solving and leadership. I now understand that success in business requires not just technical knowledge but also the ability to work effectively with others and adapt when things don't go as planned. I believe that an MBA will help me develop these soft skills further and prepare me for greater leadership responsibilities in the pharmaceutical industry.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
When I took over the English department at Saigon International School in 2016, we had a serious problem on our hands. Our students were passing standardized exams, sure—but they couldn't write. More specifically, they couldn't write anything original. Every essay followed the same five-paragraph formula. Every argument was generic. Teachers were assigning the same prompt year after year because it was easier to grade.

By that point I'd been teaching for eight years, so I knew the real issue wasn't the students. It was our system. We had no structured feedback mechanism. Teachers graded essays in isolation. No one was sharing what worked and what didn't. Students never saw examples of excellence or understood how to improve.

I decided we needed a creative writing laboratory—but I couldn't ask for a budget increase. Instead, I started small with what we had.

I created a rotating peer-review system where students from different grade levels (9-12) would review each other's drafts. I spent a month training them on specific feedback techniques—not "this is good" but "I didn't understand your argument in paragraph three because..." It took time to get them comfortable being critical of older students, but by the third round, the feedback became real.

The breakthrough came when I realized I needed to show students what excellence actually looked like. I compiled anonymous essays from previous years—with permission—that demonstrated different writing approaches. Some were five-paragraph classics. Others were experimental. Some had failed attempts at creativity. I organized them by technique rather than grade: "How to Use an Unexpected Metaphor," "Building an Argument With Questions," "When Breaking the Rules Works."

Then I added a monthly writing showcase where students could volunteer to read their work aloud. No grades. Just response. After the first one, I watched a quiet 10th grader named Linh present an essay comparing her grandmother's silence about the war to her own silence about anxiety. The classroom went quiet. Afterward, other students approached her asking how she'd done that—how she'd made something so personal also universal.

The results shifted over eighteen months. Our students' essays became more distinctive. College counselors started noticing. University submissions improved. More importantly, students started writing because they wanted to, not because they had to.

But I'm being honest—this wasn't revolutionary. It was just fundamentals: transparency, peer learning, models of excellence, and regular practice. Any good school does this. What surprised me was how long we'd gone without it, and how reluctant I initially was to implement something so basic because it required rethinking our entire workflow.

The real lesson wasn't that I'm particularly creative. It was that sometimes the best solutions don't require innovation—they require paying attention to what your people need and giving them the space to deliver it.

I'm interested in IIM Kozhikode because education in India faces similar systemic challenges, and I'm curious how management education approaches them.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:

I spent time on your website learning about the program structure and curriculum. The focus on entrepreneurship and innovation really stood out to me, as these are areas I'm passionate about. I also read about the international exchanges and the campus life, which seems vibrant and engaging. Throughout my 11 years in pharmaceutical and biotech companies, I've developed strong technical knowledge of drug development processes, regulatory frameworks, and market dynamics. However, I've come to realize that to advance into leadership roles, I need to strengthen my business acumen and strategic thinking. An MBA is the logical next step in my career progression. I also noticed that ESC Clermont has partnerships with various industries and organizations. Being part of this network would be valuable for my career transition. prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# IIM Calcutta Essay - Challenging Conventional Thinking

Three years ago, my team at Stellar Studios faced a problem that everyone in Indian media thought was unsolvable: how to produce serialized content for rural audiences without treating them as a lower-tier market segment.

The conventional approach was straightforward—and frankly, insulting. Production houses would either ignore rural audiences entirely or create watered-down versions of their urban content, assuming rural viewers wanted "simpler" stories. The data seemed to support this. Rural viewership metrics were fragmented across dozens of free-to-air channels, with no consolidated audience measurement. Advertisers and networks had largely written off the segment as unprofitable.

I wasn't convinced. While working as a content strategist, I'd spent time in four villages across Madhya Pradesh and Maharashtra, and what I observed didn't match the narrative. The audiences weren't less sophisticated—they were differently sophisticated. They wanted stories rooted in their lived experiences, but with the same production quality and narrative complexity as anything made for Mumbai.

I proposed something our leadership thought was reckless: let's produce a 40-episode drama series specifically for this audience, but with the same budget, crew, and storytelling rigor we'd normally reserve for our premium urban shows. The catch? We'd distribute it differently—partnerships with local cable operators and community viewing centers rather than traditional broadcast. It would be a network that *didn't* exist yet.

My director pushed back hard. "You're asking us to invest 8 crores in an audience we can't even measure properly. What if they don't watch? What if they do, but we can't monetize it?" He wasn't wrong to be skeptical.

But I had data he didn't expect. I'd conducted seventy informal interviews and run three test screenings. I showed him village-by-village demand curves. I modeled three different revenue scenarios, including a more conservative one where we'd break even in year two. Importantly, I also acknowledged what I *didn't* know—we were building the measurement infrastructure as we went.

We greenlit a pilot with half the original budget. The series launched in September 2021. By month four, we had 2.3 million concurrent viewers across 340 cable operators in Central India. More significantly, the audience retention rate was 68%—higher than our urban premium content. We didn't just reach them; they came back.

That success shifted how Stellar thinks about market segmentation entirely. What started as one series became a strategic division. We've since launched five additional shows using this model, generating revenue that now represents 23% of our content division's profit.

Looking back, the innovation wasn't really about production technique or distribution strategy. It was about rejecting the assumption that rural audiences were a different category of viewer requiring a different approach. They weren't. They were just an underestimated market that nobody had taken seriously.

IIM Calcutta appeals to me because I want to sharpen the analytical side of this instinct. I've learned to trust my observations and challenge industry assumptions, but I've often done it on intuition and limited data. An MBA would teach me to validate these hunches rigorously, build more robust business cases, and ultimately make bolder decisions with more confidence.

prompt: Select one prompt from group B and respond to your selected prompt using 100 words or fewer. I learned from a colleague (or someone I supervised) when... A practical way in which I have shown compassion towards a colleague or colleagues was... I have empowered a colleague (or teammate) to succeed in their own career/project by...
content: I learned from a colleague when I realized the importance of work-life balance. My senior manager showed me that taking time off actually makes you more productive. She was always calm and organized, never staying late like everyone else. I admired her approach and tried to implement it in my own life. This experience taught me that success is not just about working hard but also about working smart. I now make sure to disconnect after work hours and spend time with my family. This lesson has made me a better employee and a happier person overall.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
When I look back at my career, the accomplishment I'm most proud of is leading a team that increased our platform's user engagement by 40% in six months. This achievement matters to me because it demonstrates that I can deliver results and make a meaningful impact in a fast-paced tech environment.

I joined the company as a senior software engineer five years ago. The engagement metrics had plateaued, and leadership was concerned about our competitive position. I was asked to lead a cross-functional team to identify and solve the problem. I worked with product, design, and analytics to understand user behavior and pain points.

Through data analysis and user research, we discovered that our onboarding experience was causing new users to abandon the platform early. I led the initiative to redesign this critical flow. I coordinated with the design team to create a simpler, more intuitive journey. The engineering team, including myself, built the new experience over three months. We ran A/B tests and iterated based on feedback.

The results were significant. Within six months, new user retention improved from 35% to 50%, and overall engagement increased 40%. This translated to millions in additional revenue for the company. The CEO mentioned the project in an all-hands meeting, and I received a promotion to team lead.

This accomplishment matters to me for several reasons. First, it proved I could think strategically beyond just writing code. Second, it showed me the power of cross-functional collaboration and data-driven decision-making. Third, it gave me confidence that I could lead and influence people toward a common goal.

However, what really resonates with me is knowing that this project improved the product for millions of users. When I think about my career, I want to do more work that has this kind of scale and impact. The experience made me realize that technical skills alone are not enough—I need business acumen to understand market dynamics, strategic thinking to solve complex problems, and leadership skills to unite teams.

This is why I want to pursue an MBA. I want to deepen my understanding of business strategy and finance so I can take on more senior roles where I can drive organizational growth. The University of Hertfordshire Executive MBA appeals to me because I can continue working while I study, maintaining my career momentum while building the knowledge I need for the next chapter.

school_name: ESSEC Business School Master in Management
prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
**Kellogg's Purpose: A Time I Led**

I was twenty-six years old, three months into my analyst role at Cornerstone Partners, when my managing director asked me to lead the operational integration of our acquisition of Nordic Components, a Danish manufacturing company we'd bought for €47 million. I remember thinking: I've been here for a quarter. I can barely navigate the office kitchen without asking someone.

But I said yes.

The challenge was that Nordic Components operated in three countries, had 280 employees across fragmented systems, and the sellers had just left. There was no playbook—my MD just said, "We need a roadmap by next week. You'll own this." I had never managed a team before. I'd certainly never managed across language barriers and time zones.

My first week was humbling. I realized I didn't know what I didn't know. I didn't understand their supply chain deeply enough. I had no idea which processes were actually broken versus which just looked unfamiliar to me. I was trying to be the person with all the answers, and it was obvious to everyone that I wasn't.

So I stopped trying.

I went to Aarhus and spent three days on the factory floor. I shadowed people. I asked what frustrated them. I ate lunch with the finance team and listened to them complain about their invoice system. I talked to the sales director about why client retention was 94% instead of the 98% we needed. I learned that people knew exactly what was broken—they just hadn't been asked.

When I got back to Paris, I had something different: I had context. More importantly, I had credibility with the team because I'd shown up and cared enough to listen.

I structured the integration around what I'd learned. Instead of imposing changes, I formed working groups. The operations manager led the supply chain optimization. The finance controller owned the systems consolidation. I became the connector—I removed obstacles, helped them communicate across borders, and made sure we weren't duplicating work. I was leading by coordinating, not commanding.

There was a moment in month two when we faced a real conflict. The Danish team wanted to keep their legacy ERP system because they'd spent years customizing it. Our Paris office wanted to migrate everyone to our standard system to reduce costs. This felt like a fight I should win—the numbers were clear, the consolidation made sense. But I'd learned that winning an argument and getting genuine buy-in were different things.

I brought both sides together. I let the operations manager explain why they'd built their system the way they had. I asked the Paris tech lead to really listen to why disrupting that would cost them productivity in the short term. Then I proposed something in between: we'd migrate, but they'd lead the customization work to make sure it worked for their specific workflows. They'd own it. It took longer and cost more initially, but we got full participation instead of resentment.

Twelve weeks in, we delivered. Ninety percent of revenue was processing through unified systems. Employee turnover was zero—in an acquisition, that's remarkable. Client retention actually improved to 96% because the integration was clean and people felt stable.

But here's what I actually learned: leadership isn't about being the smartest person in the room. It's about creating space for people to do their best work, even when—especially when—you're uncertain yourself. I didn't have the answers when I started. What I had was a willingness to be honest about that, to learn from the people doing the actual work, and to trust them to solve problems with me rather than for me.

That's the kind of leader I want to become. And I think Kellogg's culture of building brave leaders—people willing to be vulnerable, curious, and collaborative—is exactly where I need to be.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
# What Matters Most to You, and Why?

What matters most to me is making a positive impact in the world while also achieving personal success. I believe that these two goals are not mutually exclusive, and throughout my life, I have tried to balance both.

Growing up in a family business environment, I learned the value of hard work and dedication from a young age. My parents instilled in me the importance of contributing meaningfully to society while also building something sustainable. This dual focus has shaped who I am today and continues to guide my decisions.

In my five years of professional experience, I have come to realize that success is not just about the numbers on a balance sheet. It is about creating value for all stakeholders—employees, customers, and the community. I have seen firsthand how a well-managed business can uplift people's lives and create opportunities for growth. This realization has deeply influenced my values and priorities.

What matters most to me is the ability to make decisions that benefit not just myself, but everyone involved. I want to work in an environment where ethical practices are non-negotiable and where we think about the long-term impact of our actions. In the family business, I have tried to champion initiatives that promote sustainability and employee welfare, even when they required short-term sacrifices.

I am also passionate about continuous learning and personal growth. Education has always been important to me, and I see an MBA as a stepping stone to achieve greater things. Through rigorous academic training and exposure to diverse perspectives, I want to build the skills and knowledge necessary to lead effectively in the future.

Additionally, what matters to me is authenticity. I believe in being true to myself and bringing my whole self to the table. In a professional setting, this means not compromising my values for convenience or profit. It also means being willing to challenge the status quo when necessary and advocating for what I believe is right.

The family business has taught me resilience and adaptability. We have faced challenges during economic downturns and market disruptions, but we have always found ways to innovate and survive. These experiences have shaped my belief that what matters most is not avoiding failure, but learning from it and bouncing back stronger.

Looking forward, I want to leverage my business background and combine it with formal management education to create more meaningful impact. I am interested in understanding global markets and bringing best practices from around the world to improve how we operate. I also want to mentor the next generation of business leaders and instill in them the same values that my parents taught me.

In conclusion, what matters most to me is making a positive difference while maintaining integrity and continuous growth. An MBA from a reputable institution will equip me with the tools and networks to achieve these goals more effectively. I am committed to being a responsible business leader who creates value for all stakeholders and contributes positively to society.

school_name: Montpellier Business School Master in Management
prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
When I first learned about Montpellier Business School, I was immediately impressed by its reputation in Europe and its focus on sustainability in business. As someone working in the energy sector, I felt drawn to a program that takes environmental responsibility seriously while preparing students for global careers.

To learn more about MBS, I visited the school's website and reviewed the Master in Management curriculum. I found that the program offers excellent courses in strategic management and international business, which align with my career goals. I also read several articles about the school's partnerships with companies across Europe and learned that alumni work in prestigious firms around the world. The campus in Montpellier seems like a vibrant place to study with good facilities and a diverse student body.

I also connected with a current student from my LinkedIn network who gave me insights into student life at MBS. She mentioned that the program is rigorous and that students are encouraged to participate in case competitions and consulting projects. This confirmed my belief that MBS would challenge me academically and help me develop practical skills beyond the classroom.

What appeals to me most about joining the Montpellier community is the opportunity to study in France while gaining an international perspective. Europe is an important market for the energy industry, and I believe studying here will help me build a strong network of professionals who understand both European business practices and global energy trends. Additionally, the school's location gives students access to internship opportunities across France and the EU, which is valuable for my career development.

I am particularly interested in the international business focus of the program. During my seven years in the oil and gas industry, I have worked with teams from different countries, but I want to deepen my understanding of how to lead effectively across cultures. An MBA at MBS would provide this knowledge while also helping me transition from technical roles into strategic management positions.

Furthermore, I plan to actively participate in student clubs and professional networks at MBS. I believe that being part of the business school community means more than attending classes—it means contributing to the learning environment and building relationships that will benefit my peers and myself. I am interested in joining groups related to energy and sustainability, and I would welcome the chance to share my industry experience with other students.

Looking ahead, I see an MBA from Montpellier as essential for my career progression. The combination of rigorous coursework, international exposure, and professional connections will prepare me to take on leadership roles in energy companies operating across Europe and Latin America. I am excited about the prospect of joining a school that values both academic excellence and real-world application.

In conclusion, I have taken time to research MBS thoroughly and I am confident that this program is the right choice for my professional development. I am ready to commit fully to the experience and to contribute positively to the Montpellier community.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
# Leadership and Responsibility

Leadership is something I have always believed is important in any organization. Throughout my four years working in the military and government sector, I have learned that being a leader means taking responsibility for your team and making sure everyone is working towards the same goal.

One example of when I demonstrated leadership was during a project at my current position in the Swedish Armed Forces. I was assigned to lead a team of eight people on a logistics coordination initiative. The project was important because it involved streamlining supply chain processes across different departments. I quickly realized that the team members had different communication styles and were not aligned on the project objectives.

I decided to take action by organizing weekly meetings where everyone could share their thoughts and concerns. I listened to what they had to say and tried to understand their perspectives. Through these conversations, I was able to identify some key issues that were preventing progress. I then worked with my supervisor to adjust the project timeline and resources to better suit the team's needs.

By the end of the project, we were able to complete it successfully. The team was more motivated and collaborative than before. I felt that I had made a real difference by bringing everyone together and making them feel heard. This experience taught me that good leadership is about communication and making sure your team feels valued.

I believe that an MBA from Kellogg's would help me develop my leadership skills further. The program's focus on educating brave leaders aligns with my career goals. I want to continue growing as a leader and eventually move into a more senior position where I can have greater impact on organizational strategy and decision-making.

I am excited about the opportunity to study at Kellogg's and learn from faculty and classmates who share my commitment to leadership excellence. I am confident that the program will provide me with the tools and knowledge I need to become a more effective leader in whatever role I take on next.

prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
When I was seven, my grandmother took me to the rooftop of our Istanbul apartment and pointed to the Marmara Sea. "The wind here could power a city," she said. I didn't understand then. Now, at 26, I'm helping make that happen.

What your application doesn't show is that my path to renewable energy wasn't the result of a childhood epiphany or environmental awakening. It was pure accident. After finishing my engineering degree at Boğaziçi, I took a summer internship at a solar installation company in Antalya because a friend worked there and offered me a place to stay. I was bored by the first week. The work felt repetitive—calculating panel angles, filing permits, watching installers climb roofs. I remember calling my mother and asking if this was what engineering actually was.

But then something shifted. We won a bid for a 12 MW solar farm for a factory in Bursa. Suddenly, I wasn't just doing calculations. I was the translator between the engineering team, the factory owners who'd never worked with solar before, and the local municipality that was skeptical about the project. I was 23 and completely out of my depth. The factory director didn't trust our projections. The municipality delayed approvals. The engineering team thought I was overcomplicating things by asking about economics and timelines.

That's when I realized I didn't actually want to be an engineer anymore. I wanted to understand why a perfectly viable clean energy project almost fell apart over miscommunication and financial misalignment. Why the factory director cared about IRR more than carbon offset numbers. Why the municipality was worried about grid stability instead of environmental impact.

Over the next three years at Zenith Renewables, I moved from engineer to project manager to operations lead. I now oversee a portfolio of 47 MW across six sites in Turkey and Bulgaria. But the core of what I do hasn't changed—it's translation. I translate between the financial objectives of our investors (who want 14% IRR), the technical constraints of our engineers (who know what's actually feasible), and the goals of our clients (who have their own timelines and risk tolerances). When these narratives don't align, projects die.

What my CV doesn't capture is that I'm obsessed with this problem in a way that feels almost personal. I stay late not because I'm trying to climb some corporate ladder, but because I'm genuinely frustrated when a 15 MW project sits delayed for six months because no one could bridge the gap between what the client thought they were buying and what our engineers knew they could build.

I'm also reasonably certain I'm not cut out to be a pure technical operator, but I'm not sure what comes next. I could move into development, or corporate strategy, or something I haven't even considered yet. What I know is that I need a framework for thinking about this—not more technical depth, but more strategic depth. I need to understand business models and capital structures and organizational strategy the way I understand engineering constraints.

The Cranfield EMBA appeals to me specifically because it's designed for people like me—engineers and specialists who've figured out that the technical part was never the bottleneck. You don't need another year learning finance theory. You need to sit with people from different industries, in a cohort model, and build new mental models for how business actually works. I want to come back to renewable energy with better tools, or maybe discover that my real contribution lies somewhere else entirely.

My grandmother was right about the wind. But building the future of energy isn't a technical problem anymore. It's an organizational one.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
Dear Admissions Committee,

I am writing to express my strong interest in the MBA Program at Deakin Business School. With 11 years of experience in the technology sector and a passion for business excellence, I believe I am an ideal candidate for your program.

Throughout my career, I have worked in various roles within tech startups, where I have gained valuable experience in different aspects of business operations. These experiences have shaped my understanding of what it takes to succeed in today's competitive business landscape.

I am particularly drawn to an MBA because I believe it will provide me with the theoretical knowledge and strategic frameworks needed to advance my career. I am passionate about learning and growing as a professional, and I see the MBA as the next logical step in my journey. Coming from Brazil, I bring a unique international perspective to the classroom. Sincerely,
