prompt: What is your motivation for pursuing the Executive MBA at the Gabelli School of Business? How does the Executive MBA fit into your short- and long-term goals?
content:
Over the past five years at NTT Communications, I have managed a team of eight engineers responsible for enterprise network solutions across our Tokyo and Singapore offices. During this time, I have become increasingly aware that my technical expertise, while valuable, has created a ceiling on my impact. I can solve complex infrastructure problems, but I struggle to translate those solutions into business strategy. I need the Executive MBA to bridge this gap.

My immediate goal is to transition into a product management or business development role within telecommunications within the next two years. Right now, I can identify market opportunities—I spotted a gap in managed security services for mid-market companies eighteen months ago—but I lack the financial and strategic frameworks to evaluate them properly. When I presented the opportunity to leadership, I couldn't articulate the addressable market size or unit economics convincingly. I watched someone with an MBA take that same idea and secure $2M in development budget. That moment clarified something: I need formal business training, not just more technical credibility.

The Gabelli School appeals to me specifically because of its location and cohort structure. Many of my industry contacts work in New York, and I can maintain my current position while attending evening and weekend classes. More importantly, I want to study alongside working professionals who have faced similar transitions. In my experience, Japanese companies value credentials, and an American EMBA—particularly from a Jesuit institution known for ethics and leadership—would help me advance in a traditionally hierarchical industry where I currently compete with people who studied at top business schools.

Long term, I want to move into general management. Within five to seven years, I aim to lead a business unit—whether that's a regional operations team or a dedicated service line within telecommunications. The EMBA will give me the strategic thinking and financial acumen to do that credibly. It will also strengthen my network in New York, which I believe will be central to my career growth.

I know that an EMBA alone won't guarantee this transition. But it's the missing piece. I have the work experience, the ambition, and the track record of impact. What I'm missing is the business language and framework to scale my contributions beyond engineering.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
When I took over the Southeast Asia operations team at PT Energi Maju in 2019, we were hemorrhaging talent. In eighteen months, we'd lost six senior engineers—people who understood our offshore platform maintenance protocols inside and out. Exit interviews told the same story: no career progression, unclear expectations, burnout.

As the newly promoted Operations Manager, I knew I had to act. But I also knew I didn't have all the answers.

I started by doing something unconventional for our industry: I asked. Not in formal surveys, but in actual conversations. I sat with welders on the platform, had coffee with our administrative staff, grabbed lunch with the engineers who were still there but probably job searching. What I heard wasn't surprising, but it was real. People didn't feel valued. They had ideas but no one asked for them. They saw foreign companies poaching their peers and wondered if they should follow.

I proposed a restructuring to leadership that felt risky at the time. Instead of the traditional hierarchical ladder, we created technical and management track paths—so engineers could advance without becoming managers if that wasn't their goal. We also built a monthly innovation forum where anyone could propose operational improvements, and we tied a percentage of bonuses to implementation of employee suggestions.

This required me to give up some control. I had to genuinely listen to ideas that sometimes contradicted my approach, and I had to be willing to say "you're right, let's do it your way." That was harder than I expected.

Within two years, our turnover rate dropped from 24% to 8%. More importantly, we implemented 34 suggestions from the forum that saved approximately $2.3 million annually—things like adjusting maintenance schedules based on actual equipment data rather than arbitrary timelines, which came directly from our technicians' observations.

But there's something else that happened that I'm still processing. Yuki, one of our senior engineers, told me during my exit interview that my approach had changed how he thought about leadership. He later left anyway—to start his own consulting firm—but he said he'd take the collaborative model with him. Sometimes I wonder if I'd failed as a leader, letting key people go. Other times I realize I helped them grow beyond what I could offer them at PT Energi Maju, and that matters too.

I think real leadership isn't about having all the answers or keeping your best people for yourself. It's about creating space for others to be brave—to voice ideas, to take risks, to outgrow their current roles if that's what they need. It's about being secure enough to admit when someone else's way is better.

This is why I'm drawn to Tampere's MSc Management program. I want to formalize what I've learned through experience, understand organizational behavior and change management more deeply, and explore how companies in different contexts approach leadership development. I'm particularly interested in how Nordic models of consensus-driven decision making might apply to Southeast Asian operations where hierarchy still dominates.

My next chapter isn't about managing bigger teams or chasing titles. It's about becoming more intentional about the impact I have on how others lead.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
What Matters Most to You, and Why?

Throughout my career in technology, I have come to realize that what matters most to me is making a positive impact on the world while advancing my professional goals. This dual commitment has shaped my journey and continues to drive my decisions as I pursue an MBA.

Growing up in Toronto, I was always taught that success meant more than just financial gain. My parents instilled in me values of integrity, hard work, and giving back to the community. These values have remained constant throughout my life, even as my career has evolved.

In my eleven years working at major technology companies, I have learned that success is not just about climbing the ladder or achieving promotions. Rather, it is about creating meaningful change and leaving a lasting legacy. At Google, where I spent five years, I worked on various projects that touched millions of users. At Meta, I have contributed to initiatives that connect people across the globe. These experiences have shown me the power of technology to make a difference.

What truly matters to me is the opportunity to lead teams and drive innovation. I have always been drawn to positions where I can mentor others and help them grow in their careers. Leadership is not about having authority; it is about inspiring others to reach their potential. Throughout my time in tech, I have seen how good leadership can transform a team and an organization.

As a non-binary person working in a male-dominated industry, I have faced unique challenges. These experiences have made me more empathetic and aware of diversity and inclusion issues in the workplace. I believe that creating inclusive environments where everyone feels valued is critical for both business success and social progress. This is something that matters deeply to me.

Additionally, I care about continuous learning and personal growth. The technology industry moves quickly, and staying relevant requires constant adaptation. I have always sought out new skills and knowledge, whether through online courses, certifications, or learning from colleagues. An MBA from Warwick Business School would allow me to deepen my understanding of business strategy, finance, and global markets.

Financial security also matters to me, I will admit. Growing up middle-class, I understand the value of money and the stability it provides. I want to build a comfortable life for myself and my family. However, money is not the only measure of success. I have turned down higher-paying roles because they did not align with my values.

Looking ahead, I want to transition into a leadership role where I can influence not just my team, but my entire organization. I am interested in product strategy and business development, areas where I can combine my technical knowledge with broader business acumen. The Executive MBA program at Warwick appeals to me because it will give me the strategic toolkit I need to make this transition successfully.

What matters most to me is the chance to be a leader who brings both technical excellence and human values to their work. I want to build organizations where innovation thrives and where people feel respected and heard. In a world that often feels divided, I believe that business can be a force for connection and positive change.

In conclusion, what matters most to me is making an impact, leading with integrity, and continuing to grow both professionally and personally. An MBA is the logical next step in this journey, and I am excited about the opportunities it will open for me.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
# Career Progression Essay

My career journey has been marked by a strong commitment to public service and continuous professional development. Since graduating in 2022, I have worked in the government sector, where I have developed valuable skills in operations, team management, and strategic planning.

After completing my undergraduate degree, I joined the Ministry of Defense in Seoul as a junior administrator. In this role, I was responsible for managing administrative tasks and supporting senior staff members. I learned the importance of attention to detail and maintaining organizational standards. During my time here, I gained exposure to how large government institutions operate and the complexity of managing multiple stakeholders.

After eight months, I was promoted to a coordinator position within the same organization. This promotion reflected my hard work and dedication to excellence. In this new role, I took on more responsibilities, including coordinating between different departments and assisting with project planning. I worked with a team of five people and helped them stay organized and on track with our departmental goals. This experience taught me the value of clear communication and the importance of strong interpersonal relationships in the workplace.

Currently, I am working as a senior coordinator in a different government department, where I have been for approximately one year. My responsibilities have expanded significantly, and I now oversee logistics and resource allocation for our team. I manage budgets and work closely with leadership to ensure that our operations run smoothly. Through this position, I have realized that I need to develop stronger analytical and strategic skills to advance further in my career.

Throughout my professional experience, I have learned several important lessons. First, I understand that effective leadership requires listening to team members and valuing their input. Second, I have seen how proper planning and organization can prevent problems and create efficiency. Third, I recognize that my technical skills in data management and basic financial analysis, while useful, need to be significantly strengthened for me to take on more senior roles.

The reason I am applying to the University of Galway MSc Management program is because I believe this degree will help me develop the skills I need to become a more effective leader. I am particularly interested in learning about modern management practices and gaining exposure to business perspectives that are different from the government sector. I believe that studying in Ireland will give me a fresh perspective and help me understand international approaches to management and organizational development.

I am committed to bringing my dedication and work ethic to the program, and I am excited about the opportunity to study in a new country and culture. After completing the program, I hope to return to the Korean government sector and apply my new knowledge to create positive change in my organization.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
# Essay Response

During my three years working in supply chain management at a logistics company in Milan, I have learned that balancing stakeholder interests is one of the most important skills a professional can develop. This experience has taught me valuable lessons about compromise and communication.

One situation that stands out involved managing a warehouse optimization project. We had multiple stakeholders with different goals. The operations team wanted to maximize efficiency, the finance department needed to control costs, and the sales team wanted faster delivery times. Each group had valid concerns and concerns that conflicted with the others.

I was assigned to lead a cross-functional team to address these competing priorities. I quickly realized that finding a solution that made everyone happy would be impossible. Instead, I focused on understanding what each stakeholder truly needed beneath their stated demands.

The operations team's real priority was reducing manual processes. Finance wanted to avoid expensive infrastructure upgrades. Sales needed to promise shorter lead times to clients. By talking to each group separately, I learned that we could redesign our current warehouse layout without major capital investment. This would streamline operations, keep costs down, and allow us to commit to slightly faster delivery windows.

I organized several meetings where I presented data showing how this approach would benefit everyone. I used metrics and projections to demonstrate that the layout redesign would improve efficiency by approximately 18 percent while requiring only 12,000 euros in software upgrades rather than the 200,000-euro infrastructure investment finance had feared.

The process took several months. There were moments of frustration, particularly when operations pushed for additional changes that would have exceeded budget constraints. However, I remained committed to finding middle ground. I documented everything we discussed and created a shared spreadsheet that all stakeholders could access and monitor.

In the end, we implemented the solution. Operations saw a 15 percent improvement in efficiency. Finance stayed within their budget parameters. Sales was able to reduce average delivery times by two days. While no group achieved everything they wanted, all stakeholders agreed the outcome was positive.

This experience showed me that balancing competing interests requires patience, data, and genuine listening. As an MBA student, I want to develop deeper skills in stakeholder management and strategic decision-making. I believe JBIMS will provide rigorous training in these areas, particularly through courses in organizational behavior and operations management.

prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
# Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?

In 2019, I managed a €2.3 million procurement initiative for the Nigerian Air Force that was supposed to overhaul our maintenance supply chain. On paper, it was straightforward: consolidate fragmented vendors, implement a new inventory system, and reduce costs by 40%. I'd spent three months designing the proposal. The military brass approved it. We had funding. I was confident—maybe too confident.

Six months in, everything fell apart.

The vendors we'd selected through the competitive bidding process couldn't meet our technical specifications. Our IT team, which I'd communicated with exactly twice before launch, discovered the inventory software wasn't compatible with our existing infrastructure. And the logistics partner I'd chosen based on their low bid had no experience with military procurement regulations. We were hemorrhaging money, operations were suffering, and I was the person responsible.

I remember sitting in my office after a particularly brutal meeting with the Finance Director. We'd burned through €600,000 with almost nothing to show for it. My first instinct was to blame the vendors, the IT team, the impossibly rigid military bureaucracy. I drafted a memo shifting responsibility, using careful language about "unforeseen challenges" and "external constraints." I never sent it.

Instead, I did something harder. I called a meeting with everyone involved—vendors, IT, logistics, operations teams, people I'd barely coordinated with properly. I went in and said: "I planned this wrong. I didn't include you. Walk me through what I missed."

Three hours of brutal honesty followed. The IT lead showed me how I'd misread their capacity report. The lead vendor explained how our specifications contradicted our timeline. The logistics partner had flagged regulatory concerns in an email I'd apparently skimmed. They'd all tried to communicate. I hadn't actually listened.

What I did differently from that point: I rebuilt the entire initiative with these teams, not around them. We extended the timeline to 18 months instead of rushing through 9. We cut vendor count from five to two, but spent six weeks vetting them properly. I sat with IT every two weeks—not to update them, but to understand their constraints before we designed solutions. I brought in the logistics partner's compliance specialist from week one.

The second version worked. By 2021, we'd achieved the original 40% cost reduction. More importantly, we'd reduced stockouts by 35% and cut maintenance downtime by half. But the numbers weren't why it mattered.

What surprised me most was how much better people worked when they felt heard. That vendor who'd been defensive in early meetings? By month twelve, they were proactively surfacing problems and suggesting innovations. The IT team went from a blocker to a real partner. That's not something I learned from the mistake itself—it's what I learned from changing how I operated after it.

This experience shifted how I think about leadership. For years, I'd conflated strong execution with solitary ownership—coming prepared, making decisions quickly, being the person who knew the answer. I was efficient. I was also brittle. The moment something deviated from my plan, the whole structure cracked because it had never been a real structure. It was just my plan, held together by confidence.

At Oulu Business School, I want to study how organizations actually make decisions at scale, how leaders build resilience in complex systems, and how to lead in environments where you genuinely don't have all the answers. The MSc Management program's emphasis on organizational behavior and strategic decision-making appeals to me precisely because it focuses on the questions I've learned matter most: How do you build trust across teams? How do you create systems robust enough to absorb failure? How do you lead people, not just projects?

I know I'll stumble again. The difference is now I'm looking for people who will tell me when I do.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I was promoted to Director of Network Operations at Telefónica Perú two years ago, I inherited a team of 47 engineers managing infrastructure across 12 million subscribers. Within six months, I realized I was good at optimizing systems—but terrible at optimizing for the people running them. Our turnover hit 28% that year. I was moving fast, solving problems, but not leading.

That failure is why I'm applying to Keio.

I grew up in Lima expecting to stay there. Telecommunications seemed like a stable career path, and in many ways it was—I built credibility, moved up quickly, and understood the technical side deeply. But by my late thirties, I could see the ceiling. Peru's telecom market is mature. Our innovation was incremental. And more pressingly, I'd reached a point where I couldn't bridge the gap between my technical expertise and the strategic, people-focused decisions that actually move organizations forward.

I spent the last year reading obsessively—Zenger Folkman on leadership presence, Edmondson on psychological safety—and I attended a week-long executive program in Madrid on organizational transformation. That helped, but it felt incomplete. I needed something more rigorous, more challenging, with peers who could genuinely push back on my thinking.

Japan matters to this equation in specific ways. Telefónica has been exploring a major partnership with a Japanese carrier for infrastructure sharing in Southeast Asia. Beyond the business case, though, I've watched how Japanese companies approach long-term value creation and employee development in ways that feel fundamentally different from what I've experienced in Latin America or Europe. It's not just the technology—it's the philosophy. When I visited Tokyo last spring for preliminary talks, I was struck by how much time senior leaders spent understanding their teams' aspirations. That resonated with the realization I'd had about my own blind spots.

Keio's Master of Management appeals to me precisely because it's not an MBA in the Anglo-American sense. The emphasis on general management, cultural intelligence, and what you call "developing the whole person" feels aligned with what I actually need. I'm not trying to jump to a startup or maximize my salary trajectory. I'm trying to become a genuinely thoughtful leader, particularly someone who can operate effectively across borders and cultural contexts. That's not abstract for me—it's already part of my job, and I'm underprepared for it.

What I'll bring to your community is perspective earned through real operational stakes. I've run P&Ls, managed crisis response during a major network outage affecting millions, and navigated regulatory environments that make many sectors look simple. I've also learned humility. I failed at something that mattered—my team's development—and I'm here because I want to fix that, not because I'm chasing prestige.

I know this program will challenge me. I'm counting on it. And I'm genuinely curious about learning from classmates with different industry backgrounds, different countries, different problems to solve. After 11 years in the same industry, I need that friction. Lima shaped who I am, but Tokyo is where I need to grow into who I want to become.

prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
# Essay: How I Will Contribute to Classroom Discussions and Student Life

I am excited about the opportunity to contribute to the Curtin Business School community. Throughout my career in the energy sector, I have learned that success depends on diverse perspectives and collaborative environments. I believe I can bring valuable insights to both classroom discussions and student life at Curtin.

In my nine years working in oil and gas, I have developed a strong understanding of operational efficiency and project management. These experiences will allow me to contribute meaningfully to case discussions and group work. When we discuss supply chain management or organizational structure, I will be able to share real-world examples from my work. I have managed teams and dealt with complex problems, which gives me practical knowledge that complements theoretical frameworks taught in MBA programs.

I am passionate about learning from my peers and helping others learn as well. I believe that an MBA program is not just about individual achievement, but about building relationships and sharing knowledge with classmates from different backgrounds. As an international student from Spain, I can offer a European perspective on business practices and global markets. This diversity of thought strengthens classroom discussions and helps everyone understand different business approaches.

Beyond academics, I am interested in participating in student clubs and social activities. I enjoy meeting new people and believe that strong friendships develop through shared experiences outside the classroom. I would be happy to join professional clubs related to energy and sustainability, and I would also participate in social events to build community. I think it is important to balance studies with activities that help students relax and connect with one another.

I am also committed to supporting my classmates who may struggle with certain material. If I can help explain concepts or work through problems with others, I am willing to do that. Peer support is crucial in an MBA program, and I want to be someone who contributes positively to the learning experience of those around me.

My background in the energy industry gives me unique insights that will enhance classroom discussions. I have worked on projects worth millions of euros and dealt with stakeholders across multiple countries. These experiences have taught me the importance of clear communication, problem-solving, and adaptability. I am confident that I can bring these lessons to the Curtin community.

I am excited about the possibility of joining Curtin Business School and becoming an active member of the student body. I am ready to contribute my knowledge, listen to others, and grow both personally and professionally. I believe that my work experience, international background, and commitment to building a strong community make me a valuable addition to the program. I look forward to the opportunity to learn alongside talented students and faculty at Curtin.

school_name: Singapore Management University Master in Management
prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
In my three years working in the French Ministry of Defence, I learned that the best decisions are made when people with different perspectives sit at the same table. I want to bring that conviction to SMU's classroom and community.

My background is unusual for an MBA program. I spent the last three years coordinating logistics and policy between military units across four regions. This wasn't glamorous work—much of it involved convincing skeptical commanders that restructuring supply chains could save €2 million annually, or explaining why civilian contractors needed the same security clearance protocols as uniformed personnel. But it taught me something valuable: technical expertise means nothing without the ability to understand someone else's constraints and communicate across ideological divides.

In classroom discussions, I plan to contribute perspective from an industry most MBAs never encounter. When we discuss organizational change, government contracts, or risk management, I can ground these concepts in real situations—budget freezes that lasted months, stakeholder conflicts that took a year to resolve, the peculiar inertia of institutions. But more importantly, I want to listen carefully to people from tech startups, finance, and consulting who see the world completely differently than I do. That friction, I believe, produces better thinking.

I'm also genuinely interested in challenging ideas I disagree with. During my time in the military bureaucracy, I noticed how easily groups default to protecting existing structures rather than questioning them. I've started pushing back against that instinct in myself. If someone in class proposes a business model that seems inefficient based on what I know, I want to ask real questions—not to show off, but to understand their reasoning. And I expect others will do the same to me.

Outside the classroom, I'm drawn to SMU's diversity initiatives. In France, we're still learning how to have honest conversations about representation in leadership. Seeing an MBA program that actively builds community across differences matters to me. I've never been involved in formal diversity work, but I'm committed to learning. I'd want to participate in student groups focused on inclusion, though I recognize I'm starting from behind on this.

I should be honest: I'm not naturally outgoing in social settings. I'm thoughtful, sometimes slow to speak up, and I prefer depth to breadth in conversations. But I've learned that quiet people can still contribute meaningfully. In my unit, I became the person colleagues consulted before making big decisions because I asked thorough questions and didn't pretend to have all the answers.

I'm applying to SMU because of its emphasis on Asian business and its commitment to developing leaders who think globally. I want to contribute by bringing a non-Western, non-corporate perspective to conversations, and by genuinely working to understand how business operates outside the rigid structures I've known.

school_name: The University of Chicago Booth School of Business
prompt: Is there any unclear information in your application that needs further explanation?
content:
I want to address my GMAT score of 611, which I know is below Booth's typical range. 

My academic background has always been practical rather than theoretical. In university in Tel Aviv, I focused on engineering and environmental science—subjects where I could apply knowledge directly. Standardized tests have never been my strength. I took the GMAT twice, improving from my first attempt, but I recognize the score still reflects a gap in my quantitative abilities.

However, I believe my 12 years of professional experience in renewable energy demonstrates my capability to succeed in an MBA program. I have worked in project management, business development, and operations across three continents. My GPA of 3.2 from university was solid given my courseload and work commitments at the time.

What I want to emphasize is that my career trajectory shows consistent growth and responsibility. I have managed teams, negotiated contracts worth millions, and contributed to the expansion of solar projects throughout Southeast Asia and Africa. These accomplishments required analytical thinking, strategic planning, and financial acumen—skills that the GMAT measures but that I have already demonstrated in real-world contexts.

I also want to note that I relocated from Israel to pursue my career in renewable energy, which required significant personal investment and adaptation. This reflects my commitment to continuous learning and my ability to thrive in challenging environments.

I understand that standardized test scores are important indicators of classroom readiness. I am willing to work hard in Booth's quantitative courses and have sought additional mathematics preparation since taking the GMAT. My professional experience and demonstrated success in complex business environments give me confidence that I will contribute meaningfully to classroom discussions and group projects, even if my test score suggests otherwise.

I am genuinely excited about Booth's curriculum and believe that an MBA from your institution will be the right next step in my career.

prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
Leading Without Authority

Throughout my career in the technology sector, I have learned that leadership is not always about having a title or formal position. One experience that particularly stands out is when I had to take charge of a cross-functional project at my current startup, despite not being the official project lead.

At my company, we were working on developing a new feature for our mobile application. The official project manager left the company suddenly, and the project was at risk of falling behind schedule. The team consisted of developers, designers, and product managers from different departments. Although I was just a senior developer, I recognized that someone needed to step up and ensure the project stayed on track.

I decided to take initiative and organized meetings with all stakeholders to understand their concerns and what needed to be done. I created a detailed timeline and assigned tasks to team members based on their strengths and availability. I made sure to communicate regularly with everyone involved and kept track of progress. I also helped resolve conflicts between team members when they disagreed about technical approaches or design decisions.

The experience taught me several important lessons. First, I learned that leadership is about taking responsibility rather than having a title. When you see something that needs to be done, you should not wait for someone else to do it. Second, I learned that effective communication is key. By keeping everyone informed and making sure they understood their roles, the team was able to work more efficiently. Third, I learned that people are more willing to follow someone who genuinely cares about their success and well-being, not just about completing tasks.

In the end, we successfully delivered the feature on time and it received positive feedback from users. The team members appreciated the effort I put in and some of them told me that my coordination really made a difference in the project's success.

This experience has shaped how I approach work now. I always try to be proactive and take on leadership responsibilities whenever I see an opportunity, even if I do not have the formal authority. I believe that in any organization, there are many informal leadership opportunities for those who are willing to look for them.

Now, as I think about pursuing an MBA, I am excited about the opportunity to learn more about leadership and management. I believe that formal business education will help me develop my leadership skills further and prepare me for more senior roles in the future. I want to be the kind of leader who can inspire and motivate others to achieve great things.

I am particularly interested in XLRI's GMP-MiM program because of its strong reputation in Asia and its focus on developing global business leaders. I think this program will give me the tools and knowledge I need to become an even more effective leader.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
Throughout my career in tech, I have always believed that innovation comes from challenging the status quo. This mindset has driven me to push boundaries and think differently about problems that others accept as normal.

At my current company, a major tech corporation, I noticed that our team was following traditional processes that had been in place for years. Everyone accepted these processes as the standard way of doing things. I saw an opportunity to challenge this conventional thinking and propose a new approach.

I decided to propose a new system that would streamline our workflow. My manager and I discussed the idea, and after some deliberation, we moved forward with implementing it. The new system was designed to make things more efficient and help our team work better together.

The results were positive. Our team saw improvements in our productivity and our processes became more streamlined. This showed me that challenging conventional thinking can lead to real innovation and positive outcomes.

This experience taught me that innovation is not just about having new ideas, but about having the courage to challenge the way things have always been done. In the future, I want to continue to push boundaries and think creatively about solutions to complex problems.

An MBA from EM Lyon would help me develop the strategic thinking and business acumen needed to drive innovation at a larger scale. I am excited about the opportunity to learn from experienced professors and diverse classmates who share my passion for innovation and pushing boundaries.

I believe that my background in tech, combined with my desire to challenge conventional thinking, makes me a strong candidate for the Executive MBA program. I am committed to bringing this innovative mindset to the program and contributing to the learning experience of my peers.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
I joined Google's São Paulo sales team in 2019, fresh off a role at Microsoft where I'd learned to respect process. At Google, I inherited a territory that looked respectable on paper—$4.2M in annual revenue—but felt hollow to me. Our top clients were renewing contracts on autopilot. We weren't actually solving their problems.

The conventional approach was clear: maximize contract value, hit renewal targets, move to the next deal. My manager appreciated consistency. My peers hit their numbers this way. But after talking to our biggest client—a retail chain with 200+ locations—I realized we'd been solving for the wrong metric entirely. They didn't need bigger ad budgets. They needed to prove ROI to their CFO so they could justify *keeping* digital spend when margins tightened.

I proposed something that made no sense by sales logic: we'd implement a custom attribution model before renewal talks even started. Free. For three months. Our product team would help their analysts connect Google Ads directly to in-store sales. This meant less focus on closing a deal quickly and more focus on proving undeniable business impact.

My sales director was skeptical. "That's not scalable," she said. "You're doing consulting work, not selling." She wasn't wrong by traditional metrics. But I kept coming back to a question: if we couldn't prove our value, why should they trust us with bigger budgets?

I got approval to pilot it with two accounts. I spent more time in their analytics systems than in sales calls. One of our engineers worked alongside their team. It was messy—our processes weren't designed for this. I was essentially doing work I wasn't hired to do, and my sales cycle stretched from 90 days to 150 days.

When renewals came, both accounts expanded. One doubled their investment. More importantly, they became advocates internally. Our renewal rate in that territory jumped from 82% to 97%.

I'd never expected this to scale, but within eighteen months, the approach became the template for our Latin America region. What started as one person pushing back against "this is how we do things" became organizational practice.

The friction I experienced wasn't a sign I was wrong—it was the cost of thinking differently in a system built around efficiency, not innovation. This matters to me beyond sales targets. I want to lead in environments where I can challenge assumptions and test new approaches, even when they're uncomfortable. Insper's emphasis on entrepreneurial thinking and its location in Brazil—a market where I've lived and worked—makes this the place where I can do that most effectively.

school_name: Excelia Business School Master in Management
prompt: Describe a time when you led a team through a significant challenge. What was the outcome?
content:
**Essay: Leading Through Challenge**

Throughout my career in education, I have learned that leadership is about bringing people together and motivating them to achieve common goals. One particular experience stands out as a defining moment in my professional journey where I had to lead my team through a significant challenge.

In 2019, I was working at a mid-sized education technology company in Johannesburg as a Programme Coordinator. Our team was responsible for implementing a new learning management system across five schools in the Gauteng province. The project was ambitious and had a tight deadline of three months. When we began, we quickly realized that the schools were resistant to change. Teachers were uncomfortable with the new technology, and there were concerns about how it would affect their daily teaching practices.

The challenge was multifaceted. We had limited budget, an inexperienced technical team, and school administrators who were skeptical about the entire initiative. As the person closest to the schools, I knew that I had to take charge and find solutions. I organized meetings with each school to understand their concerns. I listened to the teachers and principals, and I realized that they simply needed more support and training.

I decided to create a comprehensive training program that would address their fears. I worked with my team to develop workshop sessions that were practical and easy to understand. Instead of overwhelming people with technical jargon, we focused on showing real examples of how the system would make their work easier. I also assigned team members to each school to provide ongoing support during the transition period.

My approach was to be present and visible. I spent time in the schools, talking to teachers, answering questions, and showing empathy for their situation. I think this made a big difference because people felt heard and supported rather than just being told to implement something new.

After three months, all five schools had successfully adopted the learning management system. Teachers began using it regularly, and within six months, we received positive feedback about how it had improved their administrative tasks and student engagement. The schools requested that we expand the program to other institutions, which we did.

This experience taught me that leadership is not just about having a plan or making decisions. It is about understanding people, being flexible, and showing genuine care for those you work with. I learned that listening is as important as directing, and that sometimes the best solutions come from consulting with your team and the people affected by change.

This challenge has shaped how I approach problems today. I now understand that resistance to change often comes from fear or lack of understanding, and that good leaders take time to address these underlying issues. Moving forward in my career, I want to develop stronger strategic skills and learn how to manage larger initiatives with multiple stakeholders. An MBA will give me the tools and knowledge to scale these insights and become a more effective leader in the education sector.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
In five years, I see myself in a leadership role within the medical devices industry, ideally managing a team and driving innovation in product development. I am passionate about combining my technical background with business acumen to make a meaningful impact in healthcare. The SDA Bocconi MBA program is the perfect stepping stone to achieve these goals.

Currently, I work as a Product Engineer at a medical devices company in Australia. Over the past two years, I have been involved in various projects related to device manufacturing and quality assurance. This experience has given me insight into how products move from conception to market, and I have developed a genuine interest in understanding the business side of innovation. I know that to progress into management, I need formal business education.

My main motivation for pursuing an MBA is to gain the strategic thinking skills necessary for leadership. I believe that business school will teach me how to think about operations, finance, and marketing in ways that my engineering background has not. Additionally, I want to expand my professional network, particularly in Europe, as I am interested in potentially relocating after graduation.

SDA Bocconi appeals to me for several reasons. The program's strong reputation in Europe is attractive, and I appreciate that it offers courses in healthcare management and innovation. The location in Milan is also appealing as it is a major business hub. Furthermore, I have read that the program emphasizes practical experience through internships and consulting projects, which aligns well with my learning style.

After the MBA, my plan is to work for a multinational medical devices company, possibly in a product management or operations role. Within five years, I hope to be managing a small team—perhaps five to ten people—and contributing to strategic decisions about product lines. Eventually, I would like to move into senior management, but I understand that this requires building experience first.

I am also interested in the intersection of business and healthcare, and I believe that companies in this space need people who understand both the technical and commercial aspects of medical innovation. An MBA from SDA Bocconi will position me as someone with both engineering expertise and formal business training, which I believe is a valuable combination.

Honestly, I am still figuring out exactly which function within the industry excites me most—whether it is product management, operations, or strategy. However, I am confident that the MBA program will help me explore these options through electives and internships. I am looking forward to learning more about different career paths and making a more informed decision during the program.

In conclusion, SDA Bocconi's MBA program is an important part of my career development. The combination of strong academics, practical experience, and location in Europe makes it the right choice for me at this stage of my career. I am excited about the opportunity to grow as a business leader and bring my unique engineering perspective to a diverse cohort.

prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
# INCAE Business School Executive MBA Application Essay

When I was twenty-three, I moved to Colombia to work for a British oil company. I didn't speak Spanish fluently. I knew nothing about drilling operations. My manager had made the decision to hire me based on one thing: I was willing to go.

Eight years later, I've worked in four countries across Latin America and Africa. But this isn't an essay about how I'm adventurous or culturally adaptable—my CV already shows that. What it doesn't show is that I've spent most of my career feeling like an outsider, and I've only recently stopped seeing that as a weakness.

When I arrived in Bogotá, I was assigned to support the exploration division. The team had worked together for years. They spoke in technical jargon I didn't understand and made jokes about people I'd never met. During meetings, engineers would sketch diagrams on whiteboards, and I'd nod along, writing things down to research later in my hotel room. I was twenty-three, the only woman in the office, and visibly foreign. I felt like I was constantly performing competence I didn't possess.

I lasted six months before I considered asking for a transfer home.

What kept me in Bogotá wasn't confidence. It was stubbornness, honestly. And a moment where one of the senior engineers—a man named Carlos who barely spoke English—stopped me after a meeting and said, in slow Spanish, that my questions about stakeholder communication were the right ones, even though no one else was asking them. He said being outside the system sometimes let you see things the system couldn't.

That stuck with me. Not because it solved my insecurity, but because it suggested my outsider status might have a function.

Over the next eight years, I've leaned into that. In Angola, when I moved into supply chain management for a new project, I deliberately positioned myself as the person asking "why do we do it this way?" I challenged processes that everyone else assumed were fixed. Some pushback was just friction. But some of it led to genuine changes—we restructured our vendor agreements and saved the company approximately $2.8 million over two years.

But I also failed. Badly. In Nigeria, I proposed a local partnership model that leadership rejected, partly because my analysis wasn't thorough enough, and partly because I didn't build enough internal support. I learned that asking good questions only matters if you eventually provide good answers. I learned that being an outsider isn't an excuse for not doing the homework.

The real thing about myself that doesn't appear in my application is this: I still sometimes feel like I don't belong in rooms where decisions are made. The difference now is that I've learned to sit in that discomfort without either running away or pretending it isn't there. I've learned to say "I don't know yet" instead of either faking knowledge or assuming it's impossible for me to learn.

An MBA, particularly an executive program, feels like another one of those moments—a space where I'll be surrounded by people who have more certainty, more advanced credentials, clearer career trajectories. But I've learned that doubt isn't the opposite of qualification. It's actually what makes it possible to grow.

I'm applying to INCAE because I want to sharpen my strategic thinking in the energy transition. But I'm also applying because I work best in environments that push me to prove I belong. That might sound negative, but it's been the engine of every meaningful development I've had.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I walked into Temasek's trading floor in 2019 for my first role as a portfolio manager, I was the only woman in a room of fifteen analysts. I'd spent the previous eight years building my technical skills in equities at Goldman—studying earnings models, memorizing sector dynamics, climbing the ranks in a fairly linear way. But something shifted when I moved into asset allocation. I realized I'd been optimizing for the wrong thing. Everyone around me was brilliant at analyzing financial statements, yet our portfolio construction decisions were being made by consensus in a room that looked identical to rooms everywhere else in finance.

That was four years ago. Today, I lead a team of seven managing a $2.8bn emerging markets allocation. The composition of my team—four women, three men—wasn't an accident. I actively recruited differently. Our portfolio has outperformed the benchmark by 3.2% annually, and I'd argue it's partly because we ask different questions. When everyone assumes Chinese tech stocks are uninvestable post-regulation, someone at your table needs to actually push back instead of nod along.

But I've hit a wall. My firm has been generous, my compensation is strong, and the work is interesting. What I'm missing is intellectual friction outside my bubble and a structured space to question my own assumptions. I've been managing money for a decade in Asia; I've barely spent time seriously studying markets through a European or North American lens. More importantly, I've learned enough about myself to know that I can't grow further without stepping outside the functional silo of asset management and understanding how capital flows across the world differently than I've assumed.

LBS's Executive MBA appeals to me precisely because it's diverse in a way that matters. I don't need another program full of finance people who think like me. Your cohort—your faculty from five continents, your class structure designed for experienced practitioners—is what actually addresses my growth gap. I want to sit in strategy sessions with operators, entrepreneurs, and strategists who see the world differently. And I want to be the person in those sessions who brings a more inclusive version of how we think about allocating capital.

I believe I'll bring something concrete to your community too: a point of view shaped by a decade in Asian finance, a track record of building high-performing diverse teams, and an intellectual humility that comes from recognizing how much I still have to learn.

school_name: Prague University of Economics Executive MBA
prompt: Describe a time when you led a team through a significant challenge. What was the outcome?
content:
**Leadership Through Adversity**

Throughout my career in retail and e-commerce, I have always believed that a strong leader is someone who can guide their team through difficult times. One experience that stands out as a defining moment in my professional journey involves leading a team through a significant operational challenge at my previous company.

In 2019, I was working as a Operations Manager at a mid-sized e-commerce company in Madrid. My team consisted of about fifteen people responsible for warehouse logistics and fulfillment. One day, we discovered that our inventory management system had failed due to a software glitch. This was a critical situation because we had thousands of orders pending and no accurate way to locate products in the warehouse. The company was at risk of losing customers and damaging its reputation.

I immediately called a meeting with my team. I explained the situation clearly and told everyone that we would work through this together. I divided the team into smaller groups and assigned each group a specific section of the warehouse. We decided to manually count and verify all inventory using spreadsheets and notebooks. It was not the most efficient method, but it was necessary.

For two weeks, my team worked overtime. I worked alongside them, often staying late into the evening. During this time, I made sure to check in with team members regularly to see how they were doing. I brought coffee and snacks to keep morale up. I also communicated daily updates to upper management so they knew we were making progress.

The outcome was successful. We managed to reconcile all inventory within two weeks and prevented major order delays. The system was eventually fixed by our IT department, and we implemented better backup procedures. My team felt proud of what they accomplished together. Several team members told me afterward that they appreciated how I handled the situation and that it made them feel valued as part of the company.

This experience taught me the importance of clear communication, teamwork, and perseverance. I learned that when facing challenges, it is essential to remain calm and composed. I also realized that taking care of your team's wellbeing during stressful times is crucial for maintaining morale and productivity.

Since then, I have continued to apply these lessons in my subsequent roles. I believe that leadership is not just about making decisions from behind a desk, but about being present with your team and showing them that you care about their success.

As I consider my next steps in my career, I am excited about the opportunity to pursue an MBA at Prague University of Economics. This program will provide me with the strategic skills and global perspective I need to advance into senior management positions. I am particularly interested in supply chain management and would like to eventually lead larger teams across multiple locations.

In conclusion, the inventory crisis was a pivotal moment that shaped how I approach leadership. It reinforced my belief that challenges are opportunities to build stronger teams and develop as a leader. I am confident that the skills I have developed through these experiences, combined with the knowledge I will gain from an MBA, will prepare me to take on greater responsibilities in the future.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
Two years ago, I discovered our customer data was being used in ways our clients never explicitly consented to. We were a Series B fintech startup in Bogotá with 45 employees, and our data practices—while technically compliant with Colombian privacy law—felt wrong to me.

I had just moved into a product leadership role, which meant sitting in strategy meetings where revenue projections depended on aggressive data monetization. The business model was straightforward: improve our core product for free, then sell anonymized user behavior insights to larger financial institutions. Our CEO saw it as clean revenue. Most of the team didn't question it.

But I kept thinking about my abuela, who would use our app to manage her small pension. She trusted us. The compliance officer kept saying we were in the clear legally, and technically, she was right. That's what made it harder—I couldn't point to a rule we were breaking.

I spent a week researching what companies like Wise and Banco Falabella were doing around user trust and data. Then I drafted a memo to our leadership team. I didn't write it as an accusation. Instead, I framed it around competitive advantage: startups that lead on transparency build stronger customer loyalty and weather regulatory scrutiny better. I included specific numbers—customer churn rates at fintech companies that had privacy incidents, and research showing that 67% of Latin American users would pay slightly higher fees for better data protection.

The conversation was tense. Our CEO felt blindsided. Our Head of Finance worried we'd lose projected revenue. But I had data, and I had conviction.

We compromised. We didn't abandon data monetization entirely, but we built an opt-in program instead of opt-out. Users could choose whether to participate, with real incentives—cashback, lower fees. Within six months, 34% of our user base opted in, which meant we lost theoretical revenue but kept our integrity intact.

It didn't feel like a victory. We still sell data, just more honestly. But walking through Bogotá and seeing our ads, I don't feel that knot in my stomach anymore.

What I learned is that doing the right thing rarely means winning. It usually means accepting that messy middle ground where everyone's slightly uncomfortable.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
When I was promoted to Network Operations Manager at SK Telecom's Seoul hub three years ago, I inherited a team of fourteen people managing infrastructure for 2.3 million residential customers. The achievement I'm proudest of isn't the promotion itself, but what happened next—and honestly, what almost didn't happen.

Our network was bleeding money. We had the highest maintenance costs in the company, and our Mean Time To Repair was running 40% above the target. I could see the problem immediately: the team was reactive, fighting fires instead of preventing them. But more importantly, they didn't trust each other. The senior technicians hoarded knowledge. Junior staff were afraid to speak up. I watched two separate incidents where someone knew about an issue but didn't report it because they thought it wasn't "their job."

I spent my first two months just listening. I met individually with each team member, and I didn't ask about metrics or processes. I asked what frustrated them, what made them proud, what they wanted to learn. One technician, Min-jun, told me he'd been suggesting a preventive maintenance schedule for two years and nobody had listened. Another, Ji-woo, mentioned she'd been assigned to the graveyard shift as punishment for a mistake, even though she'd just had a baby.

So we rebuilt. We implemented Min-jun's system with proper testing. We restructured shift rotations and actually asked people what times worked for them. But the real change was cultural. I created a weekly meeting where anyone could surface issues without judgment—no manager filter, no blame. I started giving credit publicly, constantly. When someone made a mistake, we treated it as a learning moment for the whole team.

Within eighteen months, our MTTR dropped to 22% below target. Maintenance costs fell by 31%. We went from having one person express interest in professional development to nine people completing advanced certifications.

What matters to me most about this accomplishment is that it proved something I'd been uncertain about: that I could lead through trust instead of authority. I'm not naturally charismatic. I'm reserved. My first instinct as a manager was to be the smartest person in the room and direct everything. But watching Min-jun's face when we actually implemented his idea, or seeing Ji-woo get promoted because she finally had time to upskill—that changed how I see leadership.

I want my MBA to build on this foundation. I know my technical skills have limits. I want to understand organizational dynamics better, to learn strategy beyond just optimizing one team. University of Hertfordshire's executive program appeals to me because I'm still working full-time, and I need a program that treats my experience as relevant rather than something to be reset.

This accomplishment matters because it's shifted what I measure myself against. Not just operational metrics, but whether people around me feel genuinely capable.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
I believe there is more to my profile than what the numbers might initially suggest. While my GMAT score of 623 does not reflect my true analytical capabilities, my nine years of professional experience in the non-profit sector have taught me invaluable lessons about leadership, problem-solving, and impact that standardized tests cannot measure.

Throughout my career, I have been passionate about making a difference in communities that need support the most. Working in the NGO space has shown me that business principles and social good are not mutually exclusive. This realization sparked my desire to pursue an MBA and bridge the gap between my non-profit background and the business world.

My GMAT score reflects a difficult personal period. I took the test while managing several ongoing projects at my organization, and I did not prepare as thoroughly as I should have. However, I have always been a strong academic performer, as evidenced by my 3.7 GPA, and I am confident that I have the intellectual capacity to succeed in a rigorous graduate program. The GMAT does not define my ability to think critically or engage with complex business concepts.

In my role at my organization, I have worked on various initiatives aimed at improving operational efficiency and expanding our reach. I have collaborated with teams across different departments and learned how to communicate effectively with diverse stakeholders. These experiences have prepared me well for the collaborative nature of an MBA program.

I am specifically interested in the University of Bayreuth MSc Management program because I believe it will provide me with the tools and knowledge I need to advance in my career. The program's focus on sustainable business practices aligns with my values and my commitment to social responsibility. I am eager to learn from experienced faculty and my peers, and I am confident that I will make meaningful contributions to the classroom discussion.

My non-profit background brings a unique perspective to business education. I have seen firsthand how limited resources require creative thinking and strategic planning. I understand the importance of accountability and measurable outcomes. These lessons will allow me to bring practical insights to case discussions and group projects.

As I move forward in my career, I hope to leverage the skills and knowledge gained from this program to help my organization scale its impact. I am excited about the opportunity to study at Bayreuth and to become part of a community of forward-thinking professionals committed to positive change.

I understand that my GMAT score may raise questions, but I hope you will consider my overall profile and the unique value I can bring to your program. Thank you for considering my application.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
When I was promoted to Senior Portfolio Manager at Schrodinger Capital in Frankfurt four years ago, I realized that being a good leader meant inspiring my team to achieve excellence. This experience fundamentally shaped my management philosophy.

Prior to my promotion, I was a dedicated individual contributor focused on delivering strong investment returns. However, when I was given responsibility for a team of five analysts, I quickly understood that my role had changed. I needed to think about how to motivate people, set clear expectations, and create an environment where everyone could do their best work.

One of the first things I did was implement weekly team meetings where we discussed our portfolio strategy and market outlook. I believed that by communicating more frequently with my team, I could ensure everyone was aligned on our objectives. I also started providing regular feedback to my analysts on their performance and areas for improvement. I found that people responded well to this approach and became more engaged in their work.

Over the next two years, my team grew to eight people, and our assets under management increased by approximately 15%. I was proud of these results because they demonstrated that my leadership approach was working. The team seemed happy, and we were delivering strong returns for our clients.

However, in 2022, two of my senior analysts left the company within a few months of each other. Both cited personal reasons for their departures, but I was surprised and disappointed. When I reflected on what happened, I realized that I had been so focused on achieving investment performance that I had not paid enough attention to the individual development needs of these analysts. One of them had expressed interest in learning more about ESG investing, but I had not made this a priority. The other wanted more exposure to client meetings, but I had not created these opportunities.

This experience taught me that effective leadership requires balancing business objectives with genuine investment in people's development and career growth. It also showed me that I needed to listen more carefully to what my team members wanted and needed, rather than assuming I knew what was best.

Since then, I have worked to improve my leadership approach. I now spend time at the beginning of each quarter discussing career aspirations with my team members and identifying projects that align with their interests. I have also made an effort to involve analysts in client presentations and strategy discussions, which has increased their sense of ownership and engagement.

My management philosophy is now centered on the idea that people do their best work when they feel valued, supported, and given opportunities to grow. This requires creating an environment of trust and open communication, and being willing to invest time in people's development.

The EMBA program at Macquarie will provide me with the tools and frameworks to further develop my leadership capabilities and become a more effective manager. I am particularly interested in the organizational behavior curriculum, which I believe will deepen my understanding of how to build high-performing teams.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
My vision for the next five years is to become a successful manager and leader in the international business world. I believe that the MSc Management program at University of Bayreuth will provide me with the necessary skills and knowledge to achieve this goal.

Throughout my career as an entrepreneur, I have learned many important lessons about business, teamwork, and innovation. I have worked on several startup projects and have gained valuable experience in different areas of management. However, I recognize that my education has gaps, and I need formal training in management theory and practice to advance to the next level.

In five years, I see myself working in a management position at a medium to large company, preferably in Germany or another European country. I want to apply the skills I have learned as an entrepreneur to help established companies innovate and grow. I am passionate about making a difference in the business world and contributing to companies that create value for their stakeholders.

The MSc Management program at University of Bayreuth is the perfect fit for my aspirations. The curriculum offers comprehensive courses in strategic management, organizational behavior, and international business, which are exactly what I need to develop my management capabilities. I am particularly interested in the courses on innovation and entrepreneurship, as these will allow me to leverage my existing startup experience while gaining deeper theoretical understanding.

Additionally, I appreciate that the program is located in Germany, a country known for its strong industrial base and business culture. Studying in Germany will give me the opportunity to expand my professional network in Europe and to better understand the German business environment, which is important for my career plans.

The program's focus on practical learning and case studies appeals to me because I believe that management education should be grounded in real-world applications. The opportunity to work on projects with actual companies will help me bridge the gap between my entrepreneurial background and traditional corporate management.

Furthermore, the diverse international student body at Bayreuth will enrich my learning experience. Working with classmates from different countries and cultural backgrounds will prepare me to manage teams in a globalized world, which is essential for my future career.

After completing the MSc Management, I plan to work for 3-4 years in a management role to consolidate my learning and gain practical experience in a structured corporate environment. Following this, I might consider pursuing further education or starting another venture, but I will make this decision based on the opportunities and insights I gain during my first years after graduation.

In conclusion, the MSc Management program at University of Bayreuth aligns perfectly with my career objectives and will provide me with the tools I need to transition successfully from entrepreneurship to corporate management. I am confident that this program will help me achieve my vision of becoming an effective and respected manager in the European business landscape.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
In 2019, I was a mid-level product manager at Amazon's London office, managing a portfolio of logistics optimization tools. The team was stagnant. We'd shipped the same features for two years, our user retention had flatlined at 62%, and I remember sitting in a 1-1 with my manager thinking: either this changes, or I'm going to feel mediocre for the next decade.

I started by doing something my boss explicitly told me not to do—I spent two weeks shadowing actual warehouse operations managers instead of relying on customer interviews. What I found was humbling. They didn't care about half the features we'd built. They were losing dozens of hours per week to a manual spreadsheet export workaround we didn't even know existed.

I wrote a business case—badly, honestly—but the insight was solid. We pivoted the entire roadmap. I hired a junior PM (our first team member who'd actually worked in a warehouse), we scrapped four projects, and we committed entirely to automating that export workflow and rebuilding the underlying data infrastructure. My manager was skeptical. Our director was skeptical. I was terrified.

Eighteen months later, we'd reduced manual data entry time by 73% across 200+ facilities. But here's what mattered more: user retention jumped to 89%. We started getting emails—actual emails—from operations managers saying the tool had changed how they staffed their teams. One manager wrote that he could now spend Saturday mornings with his kids instead of in the office processing data.

We also grew the team from 4 to 12 people, launched in three new markets, and the tool became one of Amazon's three most-adopted logistics products that year. I got promoted to senior PM.

Why does this matter? It matters because I learned that significant impact isn't about being the cleverest person in the room. It's about having the humility to question your own assumptions, the discipline to sit with uncomfortable truths, and the persistence to convince skeptics when you're genuinely uncertain yourself. I was scared I was wrong. I probably made mistakes in how I communicated the vision. But I moved anyway.

The other reason it matters is that it showed me I'm most energized when I'm building something *for* people, not just building something. I cared about those warehouse managers' Saturday mornings. That sounds sentimental, but it's true—and it's what drew me to FGV EAESP.

Brazil's tech ecosystem is moving fast, but it's still underpenetrated compared to the US and Europe. The problems are different here: regulatory complexity, infrastructure gaps, different consumer behaviors. I want to apply nine years of product experience in a context where I can actually shape how Brazilian companies scale globally. An MBA from FGV isn't a credential pause for me—it's my entry point into understanding this market deeply enough to build something meaningful in it.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
When I joined Medicore Health Solutions as a Regional Manager in 2018, the standard practice for hospital equipment procurement was straightforward: identify needs, issue RFPs, evaluate bids, negotiate with the lowest cost vendor. It worked, but it was slow—typically taking 4-5 months from initial request to delivery.

I noticed something during my first month. A hospital in Jakarta needed urgent upgrades, but our vendor was backlogged. Rather than wait in the queue like every other client, I asked: what if we approached this differently?

Instead of purchasing outright, I proposed a lease-to-own model that would have been unthinkable in our industry at the time. The vendor had excess inventory gathering dust; hospitals had cash flow constraints. I brought them together. The vendor got immediate revenue and inventory turnover. Hospitals got equipment without massive capital outlay. We structured it so hospitals could upgrade to newer models every three years instead of being locked into outdated technology for a decade.

My leadership was skeptical. The CFO said it would cannibalize our equipment sales. The procurement team worried about contract complexity. But I had numbers. Over 18 months, we could deploy equipment to 12 hospitals that otherwise would have waited 2-3 years. I pushed through.

Within two years, the lease-to-own program generated $2.3 million in revenue and became our fastest-growing business segment. More importantly, we reduced procurement timelines from 4-5 months to 6-8 weeks. Hospitals upgraded their capabilities faster. Our vendor relationships deepened. It sounds simple now, but in 2019 Singapore, suggesting hospitals lease medical equipment instead of buying it was genuinely radical.

What I learned wasn't just about business models. I learned that challenging conventional thinking requires patience and evidence, not just conviction. I had to understand why the old way existed before I could credibly propose an alternative. And I had to be willing to be wrong—if the numbers hadn't worked, I would have admitted it.

Today, as I consider an MBA, I'm drawn to problems where the "right answer" isn't obvious. I want to deepen my strategic thinking beyond my healthcare bubble. IIM Ahmedabad's approach to experiential learning and focus on building adaptive leaders appeals to me. I believe the program will push me to question even more fundamental assumptions—and that's exactly what I need for my next chapter.

prompt: Select one prompt from group A and respond to your selected prompt using 100 words or fewer. The COVID-19/pandemic taught me the importance of...
content: I chose to self-teach data analytics when our hospital's EMR migration exposed a gap in my leadership capabilities. As Operations Manager, I was directing decisions with gut feel instead of evidence. I spent six months working through SQL tutorials at 5 AM before my shifts, then built dashboards tracking OR efficiency and patient flow. My first analysis revealed we were losing $40K monthly to scheduling conflicts I couldn't see before. The CFO used my findings in a board presentation. That project taught me I could master technical skills when the stakes felt real. Now I'm hungry to deepen that capability—I know the healthcare leaders I respect most combine clinical judgment with analytical rigor.

school_name: University of Auckland Business School Executive MBA
prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
Two years ago, I discovered that one of our top-performing property agents at my firm in Singapore had been misrepresenting square footage on listings. Nothing dramatic—we're talking 5 to 8 percent overstatement—but enough to inflate prices by SGD 30,000 to 50,000 per unit. He'd done it for about eighteen months before I caught it during a compliance audit.

The easy path was clear: fire him quietly, issue corrections, and move on. He was closing deals faster than anyone else on our team, bringing in roughly 15 percent of our annual revenue. Our CEO hinted at this approach during our first conversation about it.

But I couldn't ignore what bothered me. These weren't abstract transactions. I kept thinking about the young couple who'd stretched themselves thin buying one of his properties, or the retiree who'd liquidated part of her portfolio. They'd made financial decisions based on false information. That felt different from bending rules or cutting corners—it was deception that had real consequences for people's lives.

I spent two days wrestling with this before deciding to do three things: First, I recommended we terminate him, which we did. Second, I pushed to contact every buyer from his listings over the eighteen-month period—about forty families—to disclose the discrepancies and offer remediation. My CEO and our legal team resisted. They were worried about liability and didn't think we had a legal obligation.

I did have a legal obligation, I argued. We're licensed professionals. And morally, we had a bigger one. We ended up offering price adjustments to affected buyers, which cost us close to SGD 400,000. Some buyers refused the adjustment; others accepted. A few pursued complaints with the regulator anyway.

It wasn't a clean resolution. I didn't feel heroic about it. What I felt was that we'd at least done the thing that let me sleep at night.

The experience taught me that ethical compromises feel justified in the moment—the revenue argument is always compelling. But they corrode trust, inside your organization and with clients. Since then, I've tried to build systems that make the right choice the easier choice: better audits, clearer incentive structures, hiring for values fit.

This is partly why I'm drawn to the Auckland Executive MBA. I want to sharpen my thinking on corporate governance and ethics—not as abstract concepts, but as practical tools for leading through these moments.

prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
