Two weeks before launching our new distribution network across Southern Africa, our software provider went bankrupt. We were three months into the implementation at our logistics company in Johannesburg, and suddenly, the platform we'd redesigned our entire operation around simply ceased to exist.

I'd championed this project aggressively. We'd spent $1.2 million, restructured two teams, and I'd convinced our CFO that this was non-negotiable for competing with DHL and Aramex in the region. When the provider's CEO called to deliver the news, I remember the silence afterward—my operations director was staring at me across the table, and I could see the thought forming: we're about to miss our Q3 targets, and this one's on you.

My first instinct was to blame the vendor. I spent an hour compiling a document on their risk management failures, their lack of financial disclosure, all the ways I couldn't have predicted this. But that didn't matter. We had 47 employees trained on software that no longer existed, three major clients expecting faster deliveries, and seven days until launch.

What I should have done from the beginning was build redundancy into our planning. I'd treated this implementation as binary—it was either going to work, or it wasn't. I hadn't seriously war-gamed the downside scenarios. I'd been confident because I'd done my homework on the vendor, but confidence isn't the same as risk management.

So we pivoted. I contacted our old logistics platform provider—the one we were replacing—and asked if we could extend our contract by three months while we found an alternative. It cost us an extra $180,000 and was embarrassing. I also got honest with our team: I'd made an assumption that cost us money and time, and I was accountable for it. Not in a self-flagellating way, but directly.

We implemented a modified version of the launch with a smaller team using the legacy system, bought us time, and brought in a new vendor—a more established one—by Q4. The delays did impact revenue that quarter, but we recovered faster than the timeline suggested we would.

This experience taught me that leadership isn't about being right; it's about being prepared for being wrong. I now build decision trees with explicit failure modes. I ask harder questions about dependencies. And I've learned that admitting mistakes early actually builds more trust with your team than maintaining an illusion of control.

An Executive MBA appeals to me because I'm at a stage where I need to understand the financial and strategic implications of decisions more deeply. I want to learn frameworks that help me anticipate risk without paralysis. And honestly, I want to learn from peers who've made bigger mistakes than I have.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
After completing my undergraduate degree in Economics from Waseda University in Tokyo, I embarked on my professional journey in investment banking, which has been a transformative and enriching experience that has shaped my understanding of finance and business.

I started my career in 2016 as an Analyst at a major Japanese investment bank in Tokyo. During this period, I worked on various M&A transactions and capital markets deals, which provided me with a strong foundation in financial modeling and deal structuring. I was responsible for preparing pitch books, conducting due diligence, and supporting senior bankers throughout the transaction lifecycle. This role taught me the importance of attention to detail and the ability to work under pressure in a fast-paced environment.

In 2019, I was promoted to Senior Analyst, where I took on more responsibility in managing junior team members and leading certain aspects of deals. I worked on several cross-border transactions that exposed me to different markets and regulatory environments. This experience broadened my perspective on global finance and helped me develop my leadership capabilities.

Most recently, I have been working as an Associate since 2021, focusing on technology and telecommunications sector deals. In this role, I have developed stronger client relationships and have been more involved in business development activities. I have worked on transactions ranging from $50 million to over $500 million in value, which has deepened my expertise in sector-specific dynamics and valuation methodologies.

Throughout my career, I have gained valuable insights into how businesses operate, grow, and transform. I have developed strong analytical skills and have learned how to communicate complex financial concepts to various stakeholders. The investment banking industry has been instrumental in my professional development.

However, I recognize that my experience has been primarily focused on deal execution rather than strategy and decision-making at the corporate level. I believe that an MBA from Rotterdam School of Management will provide me with the broader business acumen and strategic perspective that I need to progress into more senior roles, potentially in corporate development or investment strategy.

prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
When I look back at my career in real estate, the biggest setback came in 2018 when I lost a major deal that I had been working on for almost eight months. I was working as a project manager at a mid-sized development company in Bangkok, and we were trying to secure a commercial property in the Silom district. The asking price was around 450 million baht, and I was confident we would close it.

However, due to some miscommunication with the legal team and some delays in getting the proper documentation from the seller, we missed the deadline. Another competitor came in with a better offer and they secured the property. At that time, I felt very disappointed and blamed the legal team for not moving faster. I thought the deal was lost because of their mistakes, not because of anything I did.

After this happened, I realized that I needed to look at myself more carefully. I had not communicated clearly with the legal team about the timeline. I had assumed they understood the urgency without explaining it properly. I also did not follow up regularly with the seller to make sure all documents were being prepared on schedule. These were my responsibilities as the project manager, and I had not done them well enough.

From this experience, I learned that in real estate, communication is everything. You need to make sure all parties are aligned and understand the timeline and expectations. I also learned that as a project manager, I cannot blame others when things go wrong – I need to take responsibility for the entire process.

After this setback, I made some important changes in how I work. I now use a project management system to track all deadlines and communications. I send weekly updates to all stakeholders. I have also learned to be more proactive in identifying potential problems before they happen.

Over the next few years, I have successfully closed several deals, including a residential project worth 800 million baht. I believe this experience made me a better professional, and I am now ready to advance my career with an MBA from IIM Mumbai.

school_name: University of Leeds Business School MSc Management
prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
Two years ago, I made a call that nearly destroyed a friendship and cost me a promotion I desperately wanted.

I was working as a content director at Vega Media, a mid-sized entertainment production house in Ho Chi Minh City. We'd been contracted to produce a documentary series about Vietnamese migrant workers in Southeast Asia—important work that genuinely mattered to me. My best friend Linh, whom I'd known since university, was the executive producer. We'd built Vega's documentary division together from nothing.

Halfway through production, I discovered that Linh had been padding crew expenses. Not egregiously—maybe 15-20% inflated. She later admitted she was covering personal debts from a failed business venture. The money wasn't coming from our pockets; it was coming from the client, a Japanese foundation. I found out because I was reviewing the final budget before submission.

My first instinct was to protect her. We'd been through everything together. I convinced myself that the client had deep pockets, that this was a victimless situation, that reporting it would be betrayal. For three weeks, I said nothing.

But every time I looked at footage of these migrant workers talking about exploitation and being cheated out of wages, I felt sick. I was sitting on documentation of exactly what they were describing—just at a different scale, with different victims. The hypocrisy became unbearable.

I didn't go to HR immediately. Instead, I asked Linh to meet me at a coffee shop near our office. I told her I knew, and that I couldn't submit false expenses. I wasn't threatening her; I was telling her I needed her to fix it. I gave her two days to come clean to our boss.

She was furious. She called me self-righteous, accused me of betraying our friendship for my career. Some of that stung because it contained a grain of truth—I knew my honesty would make me look good. She didn't come forward. So I went to our finance director.

The aftermath was complicated. Linh was let go. Our friendship fractured for about eighteen months. I didn't get the promotion—our boss felt the whole situation reflected poorly on my judgment for not catching it earlier. There was awkwardness in the office, whispers about whether I'd been a good friend.

What I learned wasn't a neat lesson. It wasn't that "doing the right thing always works out"—it didn't, not immediately. But I learned that integrity isn't situational. I can't claim to value honesty when reviewing a documentary about exploitation while tolerating dishonesty from someone I love. That inconsistency was actually the most unethical position.

Since then, I've been more careful about framing difficult conversations. With Linh, I eventually reached out and apologized for how I handled certain things—I could have given her more time, been less accusatory. We're not close anymore, but we're civil. She's since rebuilt her career.

At Leeds, I'm drawn to the ethics module in the Management MSc. I want to deepen my thinking about these gray areas. Working in media, I face constant decisions about representation, truth-telling, and competing loyalties. I want the frameworks and the vocabulary to make better choices—and to help my future teams do the same.

prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
Last year, I led a product launch at my company that was supposed to be our biggest release of Q2. We had twelve months to prepare, a budget of $2.3M, and what I thought was a solid plan. I was the director overseeing a team of eight engineers and three designers. We were launching a new API for enterprise clients—something the market wanted, and something we had the technical chops to deliver.

By month eight, we were already three weeks behind. I didn't see it coming because I wasn't looking closely enough. In retrospect, the warning signs were there: our standups had become performative, nobody was flagging blockers honestly, and I was so focused on the launch deadline that I stopped asking the hard questions about whether we were actually on track. When I finally pulled the engineering leads aside individually, they admitted the original timeline was unrealistic. We'd all known it but hadn't said it.

The launch happened, but late. We shipped in June instead of May. We lost two enterprise deals to a competitor who moved faster. That hurt.

What I'd do differently, first: I'd establish psychological safety earlier. I created an environment where people felt pressured to agree with the plan rather than challenge it. That's on me. Second, I'd implement earlier checkpoints with honest assessments. We had monthly reviews, but they were cursory. I should have done deep dives at month three and five to actually validate our progress.

What surprised me most was how much this taught me about my own leadership gaps. I'm naturally optimistic and deadline-driven, which can be strengths, but without enough skepticism built into the process, they become liabilities. I've spent the last year being more deliberate about creating space for dissent.

For Bath Spa's program, I'm drawn to the emphasis on reflective practice in your curriculum. That resonates with where I am right now. I'm not looking to come back to business school to learn frameworks—I've done that. I want to step back and examine my leadership philosophy more deeply, and I think your approach to teaching management through case studies and peer dialogue will push me to confront assumptions I've been making for years.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I started my career at 22, fresh out of university in Manila, as a junior product analyst at a fintech startup called Paystream. I remember being terrified during my first week—not because of the work, but because I realized how little I actually knew. The team was maybe twelve people, and everyone seemed to have strong opinions about product direction. I mostly stayed quiet and asked what probably seemed like obvious questions.

But I noticed something: our user onboarding flow had a massive dropout rate. Like, 40% of users never completed registration. I spent a month analyzing session recordings and user feedback, then proposed a complete redesign. My manager was skeptical—we were a bootstrap company with zero budget for major changes—but the CEO gave me two weeks to build a prototype. We shipped it, and conversions improved to 68%. That project taught me that you don't need resources; you need to care enough to dig into the problem.

I stayed at Paystream for four years, eventually leading the analytics team. We grew from 3 million to 50 million pesos in annual revenue. It was scrappy, intense work—I was running SQL queries at midnight, presenting to investors on Tuesday, then debugging dashboard issues on Wednesday. I loved it, but I also learned my limits. I'd make decisions without enough context and have to backtrack. I'd optimize for the wrong metrics. I'd avoid difficult conversations with stakeholders. These weren't failures that destroyed the company, but they cost us time and team trust.

In 2017, I moved to Barcelona to work for Voxel, a B2B data visualization platform. The role was "Senior Product Manager," which sounded impressive until I realized I was competing against PMs from Google and Stripe. My first six months were humbling. The product roadmap was bloated. We had twelve features in progress and no clear prioritization. I kept pushing for focus, but I didn't have enough credibility to shift how we worked. After eighteen months, I realized I needed to either become a director—which meant moving into pure management—or step back.

I chose step back, which sounds like failure but felt honest. I moved to a strategy role at Voxel, working on market expansion into Southeast Asia. That decision changed things. It got me out of the execution hamster wheel and forced me to think systematically about markets, pricing, hiring, go-to-market. I led two successful market entries into Vietnam and Thailand, and we scaled regional revenue from zero to 2.8 million euros in two and a half years. The work was different—longer feedback loops, more ambiguity, more stakeholder complexity.

Now, after nine years in startups, I want to become a GM or eventually run my own company. But I also know that my product management experience, while deep, is narrow. I've built teams in scrappy environments, but I haven't managed P&Ls at scale. I've done strategy on a regional level, but not global. I haven't navigated organizational politics or managed through external shocks. ESADE's Executive MBA appeals to me precisely because it's designed for people like me—operating at mid-to-senior level but needing breadth before the next step. I want to study business fundamentals properly, learn from classmates running much larger operations, and build networks across industries. Startups taught me to move fast and learn by doing. An MBA will teach me to be intentional about it.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
When I joined Medtronic's Barcelona facility five years ago as a junior quality engineer, I was convinced that technical excellence was the entire equation. I could optimize manufacturing processes, reduce defect rates by 0.3%, and get praised for it. I thought that was enough.

It wasn't.

The breaking point came during a routine production issue in early 2022. We'd discovered a batch of cardiac stents with a coating inconsistency—nothing that violated specifications, but something felt off. My team wanted to ship it. The numbers looked fine. The risk assessment was acceptable. I recommended we proceed.

A week later, a surgeon in Madrid called our quality hotline. One of those stents had failed in a patient. The patient survived, but barely. I remember staring at that incident report, realizing that my comfort with "acceptable risk" had almost cost someone their life. I hadn't asked the right questions. I hadn't pushed back. I'd been a technician, not a leader.

That experience fractured something in me—in a necessary way. I started reading about medical device companies that had failed ethically (Theranos, certain insulin pump manufacturers), and I realized my five-year career trajectory was too narrow. I could become an excellent quality engineer, sure. But I wanted to become someone who understood the business side of healthcare—the decisions that determine which products get made, which get funded, how companies balance innovation with safety.

That's when I knew I needed an MBA.

But here's what I'll be honest about: my GMAT score doesn't reflect my actual analytical capabilities. I scored 638, which I know is below Nirma's typical range. I'm a deliberate test-taker—I overthink, I second-guess. In my actual work, I can build complex root-cause analyses and navigate ambiguous technical problems. The GMAT felt like taking an exam in a language I understood but didn't speak natively. It's a limitation I'm aware of, and it's something I've had to accept about myself rather than pretend away.

What I'm confident about is my track record: I've led cross-functional teams in manufacturing optimization, I've successfully navigated regulatory approval processes in the EU market, and I've developed enough business intuition to recognize when quarterly targets can't trump patient safety. I've also become fluent in both English and German in addition to my native Spanish and Catalan, which has been essential in coordinating across our European operations.

I'm drawn to Nirma specifically because of your strong placement in healthcare and pharmaceutical sectors, combined with your emphasis on ethical leadership. The recent focus on sustainability and responsible innovation in your curriculum resonates with where my thinking has evolved. I want to learn from faculty and peers who take seriously the idea that business decisions have human consequences.

I'm not applying to an MBA to escape engineering. I'm applying because I want to get better at the parts of my job that engineering doesn't teach.

prompt: Describe a difficult decision you made and the process that you went through to make it.
content:
# A Difficult Decision

Three years ago, I had to decide whether to stay at Nexus—a Series B fintech startup where I'd spent six years building our data infrastructure team from scratch—or leave to start something of my own.

The timing felt urgent in the way only startups can make things feel urgent. I'd just been promoted to VP of Engineering, managing 14 people across three time zones. We'd grown revenue 340% year-over-year. I had equity that was actually worth something. My parents, who'd always been skeptical of my tech career, were finally proud. Walking away felt like ingratitude.

But I was burnt out in a way that felt irreversible.

I wasn't sleeping well. I'd started having panic attacks before all-hands meetings. More tellingly, I'd stopped caring about the problems we were solving—helping small businesses access credit faster. I used to care about that. I genuinely did. Now I was just executing, managing up, managing burnout.

The tempting narrative was that I needed a break. Take three months off, travel in Southeast Asia, come back refreshed. I almost did that. It would have been easy and comfortable.

Instead, I forced myself to sit with the actual question: Did I want to keep building infrastructure at other people's companies, or did I want to build something where I controlled the vision?

I spent two months meeting with founders, VCs, and engineers I respected. I wasn't looking for validation—I was looking for honest feedback about whether I had a real idea or just a case of founder-envy. One person, an investor I trust, told me bluntly: "Your idea is solid but underbaked. You're not ready yet. You're just tired and looking for an escape hatch."

That conversation hurt, but it also clarified something. I realized I'd been conflating burnout with clarity. I hadn't actually spent time thinking deeply about what I wanted to build. I had a problem I wanted to solve—fragmented data pipelines in mid-market companies losing millions annually—but I hadn't tested whether customers actually cared enough to pay for it.

So I made a counterintuitive choice: I stayed at Nexus for another year, but I negotiated a compressed schedule. Four days a week managing the team, one day a week exploring the idea with potential customers. It was harder than leaving, honestly. It meant constant code-switching and saying no to promotions and recognition.

But that year changed everything. I talked to 47 companies. I built a rough prototype. I discovered the problem was real but different than I'd imagined. And I also discovered that I actually loved parts of my job at Nexus again—specifically, building things with my team, not the politics or the growth theatre.

When I finally left Nexus to start BuildCore last year, I did it from a place of confidence, not escape. We've raised a seed round and acquired our first 12 customers. I'm not claiming I made the "right" choice—it's too early to know. But I made it deliberately, with data, not emotion.

That process taught me that the hardest decisions aren't usually about choosing between good and bad. They're about choosing between two things you actually want and being honest about why.

school_name: University of Porto FEP Executive MBA
prompt: Describe a time when you led a team through a significant challenge. What was the outcome?
content:
# Essay: Leadership Through Challenging Times

Throughout my nine years working in asset management, I have encountered numerous challenges that required strong leadership skills. One particular situation stands out as a defining moment in my career when I had to lead my team through significant adversity.

In 2018, I was managing a team of five analysts at my firm when we faced a major market downturn. The broader economic conditions created uncertainty, and our clients were anxious about their investments. This was a difficult period for everyone involved, as the pressure from senior management increased while market conditions deteriorated.

As the team leader, I recognized that my responsibility was to maintain morale and ensure that we continued delivering quality work despite the challenging environment. I held regular meetings with my team members to discuss the situation and listen to their concerns. I believed it was important to be transparent about what was happening in the markets and what it meant for our business going forward.

I also implemented new processes to help us work more efficiently. We streamlined our reporting procedures and created better communication channels between departments. This helped reduce redundancy and allowed us to focus on what mattered most: delivering value to our clients.

The outcome of this leadership experience was positive. My team remained focused and motivated throughout the downturn. We did not lose any team members during this period, which I believe is a testament to the supportive environment I tried to create. Client retention remained strong, and we actually managed to bring in some new business despite the difficult market conditions.

This experience taught me that good leadership is about being present for your team when things are difficult. It is not just about making strategic decisions but also about understanding the human element of business. My team appreciated that I was willing to listen and work with them to find solutions.

Looking back, I realize that this challenge helped me develop as a leader. It showed me that when you invest in your people and create an environment of trust and transparency, they will perform well even in adverse circumstances. This is a lesson I have carried with me throughout my career.

I am pursuing an MBA at University of Porto because I want to deepen my understanding of leadership and business strategy. I believe that an Executive MBA will provide me with the tools and frameworks to become an even more effective leader in the asset management industry. The program's focus on practical application aligns well with my goal of continuing to grow as a professional.

prompt: Prepare the Essay as directed on the first page of the format.
content:
When I think about why I want to pursue an MBA, the answer is clear: I want to advance my career and become a better leader. Throughout my three years working in accounting at a mid-sized firm in London, I have come to realise that my current qualifications are limiting my potential for growth.

My journey in accounting has taught me many valuable lessons. I started as a junior accountant and have since progressed to a more senior role. This progression has shown me that I have the ability to succeed in a professional environment. However, I am aware that without an MBA, I may not be able to reach the highest levels of management that I aspire to achieve.

I chose accounting because I have always been good with numbers and enjoyed working on spreadsheets. It seemed like a logical career path. But over time, I realised that accounting alone is not enough. I need to understand business strategy, finance, and how to manage people effectively. An MBA from Hitotsubashi would provide me with these skills and help me transition into a leadership role.

What appeals to me about Hitotsubashi is its reputation as a prestigious institution. I know it is highly ranked and respected in Asia, which is important to me because I am considering expanding my career options internationally. The EMBA programme is designed for working professionals, which suits my situation perfectly since I need to continue working while studying.

I am particularly drawn to the focus on global business education. In my current role, I work with international clients, and I believe having a stronger understanding of global markets would be invaluable. I also appreciate that the programme emphasises practical learning and case studies, which aligns with how I learn best.

My biggest challenge has been my academic performance, which I acknowledge was not strong during my undergraduate years. My GPA of 2.6 reflects a period when I was not focused on my studies and did not fully appreciate the importance of education. However, I have matured significantly since then and am now much more committed to academic pursuits. My GMAT score of 629 shows that I am capable of improving when I set my mind to it.

I believe that an MBA from Hitotsubashi will be a turning point in my career. It will equip me with the knowledge and credentials to move into senior management positions and ultimately achieve my goal of becoming a finance director or CFO. I am excited about the prospect of joining a cohort of talented international professionals and learning from world-class faculty.

In conclusion, pursuing this EMBA is the logical next step in my career trajectory. I am committed to making the most of this opportunity and am confident that I will contribute meaningfully to the Hitotsubashi community.

school_name: SKOLKOVO Moscow School of Management EMBA
prompt: Share an example where you had to lead without formal authority. What did you learn?
content:
When my uncle took ill two years ago, I inherited responsibility for our ceramic tile distribution network in Lima without the title to go with it. My father, who owns the company, was traveling; my uncle was the operational manager. I was the finance guy—numbers, spreadsheets, quarterly reports. I had never managed people.

The first week was chaos. Our largest client, a construction firm handling three major projects, threatened to switch suppliers because delivery times had slipped 40%. Our warehouse staff were demoralized—they'd respected my uncle, and they saw me as the number-cruncher coming down from the office. I could have waited for my father to return and told him we had a crisis. Instead, I did something uncomfortable: I admitted I didn't know what I was doing.

I called a meeting with the warehouse manager, two logistics coordinators, and our delivery drivers. I told them I didn't have operational experience, but I could help remove obstacles they faced. I asked them to walk me through every step of their process—not to critique, but to understand. They were skeptical at first, but something shifted when they realized I wasn't there to blame anyone.

What emerged was clear: we had a scheduling problem compounded by communication gaps between warehouse and delivery. My finance background actually became useful here. I created a simple dashboard tracking order-to-delivery timelines, and we used the data to show clients realistic delivery windows—honest ones. I negotiated a two-week ramp period with our largest client rather than promising immediate fixes we couldn't deliver.

I learned that leading without authority isn't about proving you belong in charge. It's about credibility built through competence, honesty, and genuine respect for people doing the actual work. These drivers and warehouse staff understood our operation far better than I did. My job was to listen, connect dots they couldn't see from their level, and make their jobs easier.

We recovered that client relationship. More importantly, my father saw something in how I approached the problem—not with hierarchical force, but with problem-solving and humility. He's now involving me more in strategic decisions.

Skolkovo appeals to me because I'm inheriting a business facing significant changes as Peru's economy shifts. I need to lead modernization and expansion without alienating people who've built this company. An EMBA with international peers will help me navigate this transition with better tools than I currently have.

prompt: Describe a difficult decision you made and the process that you went through to make it.
content:
**Describe a difficult decision you made and the process that you went through to make it.**

In 2019, I had to decide whether to leave SunEnergy Solutions, where I'd spent six years climbing from a junior engineer to Senior Operations Manager overseeing a 40-person team across three solar farms in Colombia. The company was growing, the work felt meaningful, and I'd built real relationships. My boss was already hinting at a director-level promotion within eighteen months.

But I was stuck.

The promotion would have come with more money and a fancier title. What it wouldn't have come with was the skillset I desperately needed. I'd been managing operations—schedules, budgets, compliance reports. Important work, but I'd watched colleagues who understood finance, strategy, and business modeling advance into truly influential roles. I understood solar panel efficiency curves better than I understood how to read a balance sheet. I could optimize a maintenance schedule but couldn't evaluate whether an acquisition made financial sense.

I spent three months wrestling with this. I made a spreadsheet (yes, really) listing what staying offered versus what leaving demanded. But the numbers didn't help. The real question was uncomfortable: Was I afraid of starting over at a new company where I wouldn't be the most experienced person in the room? Was I overestimating how much my background mattered? Could I actually do an MBA while working, or was I romanticizing the idea?

I talked to my mentor, Carlos, who'd left a comfortable job at Ecopetrol ten years earlier. He told me something I didn't want to hear: "Your fear is exactly why you should go. You're not questioning whether you can do the promotion—you're questioning whether the promotion will make you better."

That landed hard.

I decided to leave, but not to start over from scratch. I negotiated a transition role with a smaller wind energy company in Medellín, taking a pay cut and a step back in title. The role was designed to expose me to project finance and M&A work—exactly the gaps I needed to fill. It was humbling. I was one of the few women in the finance discussions, and I often felt underprepared.

That decision led directly to my current role at a renewable energy development fund in Toronto, where I evaluate investment opportunities for utility-scale projects. I'm back in Latin America now on deals, but I'm doing it with an understanding of the financial and strategic dimensions that I didn't have before.

I'm applying to Desautels because I'm ready to formalize what I've learned through this painful, excellent education. I want to work with people who've made difficult decisions and come out stronger. I want to study business strategy and corporate finance in a cohort that understands that growth often means giving up what you're good at to become competent at what matters more. 

Most importantly, I'm ready to connect my technical background in renewable energy with the business acumen required to actually scale solutions to climate change. Desautels' integrated approach to strategy and sustainability, combined with Montreal's positioning as a hub for clean tech investment, makes this the right next step.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
# Essay: My Most Significant Accomplishment

Three years ago, I stood in front of a room of forty solar technicians in Mombasa, most of whom had finished secondary school but never received formal technical training. I was terrified, not because I'm afraid of public speaking, but because I was about to tell them that their company—the one that had employed them for years—was about to collapse. And that I was responsible for rebuilding it.

I'd been brought in as Operations Manager at Mwanzo Solar, a mid-sized distributed solar company serving off-grid communities across coastal Kenya. The business model was solid: install rooftop systems and partner with microfinance institutions to handle payments. The execution was broken. In six months, I discovered that our installation teams had no standardized procedures, our quality control was non-existent, and we were losing money on every single project. Our debt was spiraling. The founder wanted to sell to a larger competitor and walk away.

I asked for six months instead.

My most significant accomplishment is that I took that company from the brink of insolvency to profitability, and more importantly, I did it in a way that kept all forty technicians employed and invested in the outcome. That matters to me because it forced me to confront what I actually care about beyond spreadsheets and revenue targets.

Here's what I did: First, I implemented a modular training program for our technicians. I spent three weeks documenting every installation step—from site assessment to final testing—and broke it into reproducible units. I trained twenty of our most experienced technicians to become trainers. This wasn't outsourced to some consulting firm; it was built from within. Within four months, our installation time dropped by 32% and our warranty claim rate fell from 11% to 2.3%.

Second, I restructured our payment partnerships. Our microfinance partners were taking 35% of revenue on collections they barely managed. I renegotiated contracts and built a simple SMS-based payment reminder system that we managed ourselves. This alone recovered $80,000 annually in lost payments and improved our cash flow dramatically.

But here's the part that actually matters: when we hit profitability at month seven, I created a performance bonus scheme tied to customer satisfaction scores rather than pure installation volume. I did this because I realized that during those first three months, when I was in pure survival mode, I'd nearly become the person I didn't want to be. I was squeezing every inefficiency out of the system without asking whether we were building something worth being part of.

One of our technicians—a woman named Amara who became my lead trainer—pulled me aside after we announced the new bonus structure. She told me that she'd started looking for other jobs because she thought I was just going to extract value and leave, like every other manager before me. "Now I actually want to stay," she said. That conversation rewired something in me.

I grew the business to three locations and increased team size to sixty people. But I measure that accomplishment differently now. Yes, the margins matter. Yes, the revenue matters. But what actually matters is that I proved to myself—and to the people who trusted me with their livelihoods—that you can build a sustainable business without burning people out, that you can innovate without losing your integrity, and that the people doing the work are not obstacles to profitability; they're the only reason it exists.

An Executive MBA from Nanyang appeals to me precisely because I'm at a point where I want to make this scale. I've proven I can turn around operations. Now I want to understand how to replicate this model across regions, how to attract capital to energy access in emerging markets, and how to build the kind of organizational culture that doesn't fade when you're not in the room. Your program's focus on strategy, emerging market finance, and leadership through complexity is exactly where I need to go.

That's why this accomplishment matters. It's not the P&L. It's who I became in the process.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
**Ethical Dilemma in My Professional Journey**

During my time working as an accounting analyst at a mid-sized firm in Bogotá, I encountered an ethical dilemma that has shaped my understanding of professional responsibility and integrity.

In my second year at the company, I was assigned to audit the expense reports for one of our major clients, a retail distribution company. While reviewing their quarterly expenses, I noticed something unusual. Several invoices from a vendor showed amounts that seemed inflated compared to the previous quarters. The pattern was subtle—nothing obviously wrong at first glance—but my instinct told me to investigate further.

As I dug deeper, I realized that the client's operations manager had been approving invoices from this vendor at higher prices than what was actually being charged. The difference was being pocketed by the manager himself. This was clearly fraudulent, and it represented a significant breach of financial integrity.

The dilemma I faced was complex. The operations manager was well-liked in the company and had been there for many years. I knew that reporting this would damage his career and possibly his family. On the other hand, my job as an auditor is to ensure that financial records are accurate and that fraud is prevented. Ignoring this would be betraying my professional responsibility and the trust that the client had placed in our firm.

I thought about this for several days, weighing the consequences of both action and inaction. I considered talking to the manager directly to understand his motivation, but I realized that this could be seen as confronting him about illegal activity, which was not my place. I also considered whether I should speak with my supervisor first before going to the client, and I decided that this was the appropriate course of action.

I scheduled a meeting with my supervisor and presented the evidence I had gathered. I explained my findings clearly and objectively, without making accusations. I let the data speak for itself. My supervisor was appreciative of my diligence and agreed that we needed to report this to the client's management.

The client conducted their own investigation and confirmed my findings. The operations manager was eventually dismissed from his position, and the firm implemented stronger controls to prevent similar situations in the future. It was difficult to see someone lose their job because of my discovery, but I knew that I had made the right choice.

This experience taught me that ethical decisions are rarely easy or simple. They often involve choosing between loyalty to individuals and loyalty to principles. However, I learned that integrity cannot be compromised, no matter how difficult the circumstances. As someone who comes from Colombia, where corruption is a persistent problem in many sectors, I have a strong belief in the importance of ethical business practices.

This dilemma reinforced my commitment to pursuing an MBA. I want to develop a deeper understanding of business ethics and corporate governance so that I can be a leader who promotes integrity in whatever organization I work for. I believe that business schools have a responsibility to train the next generation of professionals to think critically about ethical issues and to have the courage to do the right thing.

Moving forward, I plan to be more proactive in identifying and addressing potential ethical issues before they become major problems. I want to use my skills in accounting and financial analysis to help organizations build stronger ethical cultures.

school_name: EM Lyon Business School MSc in Management
prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
In 2016, our organization in Dubai had a serious problem: we were reaching maybe 200 families a month with our financial literacy programs, but our waiting list had 2,000 names. I was running the programs back then, and I remember sitting in our tiny office thinking that we'd built something that worked, but it didn't scale.

So I spent three months mapping what was actually happening. Not the version we told donors, but the real process. I found that 60% of our time was spent on manual data entry and tracking. We were using spreadsheets cobbled together from five different sources. Our team of four was drowning in administrative work that could've been automated.

I pitched an idea to our director: let me rebuild our entire system. Not hire more people—redesign the workflow. This wasn't technically difficult, but it was organizationally messy. I had to convince the finance team to change how they tracked expenses, push back on some of the program coordinators' comfort with how things had always been done, and convince our director that a "tech project" was the right priority when we could've just hired another person.

I spent four months learning Airtable and building custom workflows. I trained the team myself on a Saturday afternoon over coffee. By early 2017, we'd eliminated most of the data entry. But more importantly, we'd created visibility—every coordinator could see real-time participant data, completion rates, demographics. That information made them better at their jobs.

Within a year, we were reaching 650 families monthly. Same staff size. Same budget. The waiting list got much smaller.

But what actually matters to me about this isn't the numbers, though those matter. What matters is that I discovered something about how I want to work. I'm most energized when I can see a problem clearly, break it down into pieces, and figure out how to solve it systematically. I'm not naturally a people-person in the way a great program director is. I'm not a visionary who dreams up new initiatives. But I'm someone who takes something that's working at half-speed and makes it work properly.

The frustration is that I've spent nine years in an organization where this skill set—improvement, process design, operational thinking—isn't the main career path. The main path is director, executive director, raising money. Those are crucial, but they're not where my strengths are.

An MBA, especially in the MSc in Management at EM Lyon, feels like the right moment to formalize this interest. I want to move into operations or business development roles in non-profits—places where I can help organizations like mine work smarter, not just bigger. I want to learn about organizational design, analytics, and strategic operations from professors and classmates who've worked in mature, well-managed organizations.

I know my GMAT score isn't what it should be. But I also know exactly why I'm here and what I want from it.

prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
Growing up in Cairo, I learned to navigate paradoxes. The city is chaotic yet organized, ancient yet constantly building. This shaped how I think about problems—I look for systems within apparent disorder.

At Arada Developments, a Cairo-based real estate firm, I spent my first two years managing projects that ranged from 50,000 to 300,000 square meters. On paper, this sounds straightforward. In practice, it meant coordinating between Egyptian government offices, European architects, and contractors who operated on what I call "flexible timelines." I became obsessed with process efficiency—not because I'm naturally organized, but because inefficiency cost us three to four months on a single permitting cycle, which translated directly to millions in carrying costs.

I built a checklist system that broke down the government approval process into 47 discrete steps, identifying which steps could overlap and which ones genuinely had to wait. It reduced our approval timeline by six weeks on average. My manager appreciated it. My colleagues joked about it. But what struck me was simpler: I realized I enjoyed solving operational problems more than I enjoyed the architecture or the glamorous side of real estate.

This matters because it conflicts with what people might assume about me. My background is in a prestigious Cairo family involved in development. My path seemed predetermined—grow in the company, eventually take leadership, repeat the family story. Yet somewhere between Excel spreadsheets and government office lobbies, I discovered that what energizes me is systematic problem-solving and building scalable processes, not necessarily staying in real estate.

I'm still figuring out exactly where this leads. That uncertainty bothers me less than it used to. I know an MBA should clarify direction, but I'm applying because I want to challenge my own assumptions more rigorously. I want to work with people from different contexts—not just different countries, but different industries and problem types. Real estate taught me one system. I want to become fluent in others.

I suspect ESSEC's international environment and general management focus will push me productively out of my industry silo. That matters more to me than finding a "perfect fit." I'm looking for friction, actually.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
I want to pursue an MBA because I believe it will help me achieve my career goals and become a better leader in the manufacturing industry. I have worked in manufacturing for 11 years and have learned a lot, but I know there are gaps in my knowledge that an MBA can fill.

My career aspiration is to eventually move into senior management or operations leadership. I think about this often and know that an MBA from a reputable institution will give me the credibility and skills I need. In my current role, I manage a team of eight people and oversee production scheduling and quality control. This has been rewarding but I want to do more. I want to impact business strategy and help companies become more efficient and profitable.

The manufacturing industry is changing rapidly with automation and new technologies. I have seen how companies that adapt succeed and those that don't struggle. I want to be the kind of leader who understands these changes deeply and can guide organizations through them. An MBA will help me understand business strategy better and also give me knowledge about finance and analytics that I currently lack.

Temple's Fox Executive MBA appeals to me for several reasons. The program is designed for working professionals which is important because I cannot leave my job. The location in Philadelphia is convenient for me since I live in the region. I also know that Fox has a good reputation and the curriculum seems comprehensive. I particularly appreciate that the program offers courses in operations management and supply chain management, which align with my current work and future goals.

I also want to develop my professional network. In manufacturing, relationships matter and I know many of my classmates will become colleagues and contacts throughout my career. This is one of the key reasons an MBA makes sense now.

As a Nigerian non-binary professional, I have navigated different cultural contexts and identity challenges. This has made me adaptable and resilient. I bring a unique perspective to teams and I believe diverse leadership is important in business. An MBA program with diverse students will enhance my learning and I hope I can bring value to my peers as well.

Beyond management, I am also interested in potentially consulting or starting my own business someday. An MBA would help me develop the business acumen and confidence for either path. I am not sure which direction yet, but I know that an MBA from Fox will give me the foundation to pursue these opportunities.

In conclusion, I am committed to advancing my career in manufacturing leadership. Temple's Fox Executive MBA is the right program for me at the right time in my career. I am ready to invest in myself and become the leader I aspire to be.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
What matters most to me is the ability to help people navigate systems that were never built with them in mind.

I realized this three years ago while representing a small manufacturing company in Rayong through a complicated customs dispute. The owner, Mr. Somchai, had built his business from nothing over twenty years, but he was completely lost in the bureaucracy. Thai customs regulations are deliberately opaque—written in language only lawyers understand, enforced inconsistently, and often contradictory. He'd already spent 400,000 baht in fees to another firm and was considering shutting down. When I untangled the actual requirements and helped him understand what was legitimate versus what was just procedural theater, he cried. I remember feeling something shift in me that day.

Before that moment, I treated law like a puzzle to solve. I'd built a respectable career at Dharasastra & Co., worked on deals worth billions, and earned partnership track credentials. But I was solving puzzles for other lawyers and multinational corporations. I wasn't actually helping anyone change their situation.

That realization has made me restless in ways I didn't expect. I started volunteering with a legal aid organization in Bangkok, and that's where I discovered something uncomfortable: I don't actually know how to communicate legal concepts to people without legal training. I'd explain securities law and watch clients' eyes glaze over. I could recite regulations, but I couldn't make them actionable. I realized that my seven years of legal expertise was built on assumptions about access and literacy that don't exist for most people.

This is why I need an MBA. It's not because I want to leave law—I'm too committed to that uncertainty I just described. But I need to understand business fundamentals, operations, and how organizations actually function if I'm going to build something that bridges the gap between complex legal systems and the people navigating them. I need to understand capital structures and unit economics to design solutions that are sustainable, not just well-intentioned. I need to think like an entrepreneur, not just an attorney.

FGV EAESP appeals to me specifically because of your emphasis on business with social responsibility. I've been looking at programs across Southeast Asia, but you're actually serious about it—not just as a mission statement, but in how you structure coursework and bring in practitioners doing this work. The fact that you're based in São Paulo, teaching Brazilian case studies alongside Southeast Asian contexts, matters too. The problems I want to solve aren't unique to Thailand. I want to understand how systems fail people across different economies and what levers actually move in practice.

I know I'm taking an unconventional path for someone at my level in law. But I'd rather be genuinely useful to one small business owner who's struggling than competent at advising large ones who don't need my help.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
When our refinery in the Eastern Province hit a critical bottleneck in 2019, we were losing $2.3 million per day. The problem seemed impossible to solve within our existing framework, so I had to think differently.

The issue was inventory management across three separate production units that never communicated with each other. Each unit optimized for its own efficiency, which meant crude oil would pile up in one area while another area sat idle waiting for feedstock. Our planning team ran monthly reconciliations—actual meetings where people printed spreadsheets and compared numbers. It was absurd.

I proposed something radical for a conservative industry: we'd build a real-time dashboard that pulled data from all three units simultaneously. But here's where it got creative—instead of asking IT to build another black box, I embedded myself in their team for two weeks. I learned their language, their constraints, their fears. The infrastructure group thought this was impossible without a complete system overhaul that would take eighteen months. The operations team said they'd never trust automated data feeds.

So I didn't ask them to trust it. I asked them to test it.

We started with just one unit and one metric—crude throughput. I worked with a junior developer to build something intentionally simple and visible: a single screen showing real-time numbers that anyone could verify manually. No fancy algorithms. No black boxes. Just transparency. We put it in the control room where operators could see it, check it, and challenge it. Within three weeks, operators were correcting data quality issues because they felt ownership.

That's when something unexpected happened. Once operators had visibility, they started asking questions. "Why does Unit 2 always back up at 3 PM?" "Can we reroute this stream differently?" These weren't questions we were asking them to ask. These were questions they generated themselves because they could finally see the whole picture.

We expanded the dashboard across all three units over the next six months. By month three, we'd already improved throughput by 8%, recovering nearly $600,000 in daily losses. But the real gain was something I didn't anticipate: we'd created a culture where operations and IT started speaking the same language. Engineers stopped waiting for perfect systems and started iterating. Operations teams stopped resisting technology.

The solution wasn't technically sophisticated. Any decent developer could have built it in a weekend. What made it work was understanding that the technical problem was actually a human problem. I had to convince skeptics that incremental progress mattered more than perfect solutions. I had to sit in uncomfortable meetings where senior engineers questioned my competence. I had to admit that I didn't know how to solve this alone.

This experience crystallized something for me: I'm at my best when I'm translating between worlds—between business and technology, between what is and what could be. That's why I'm pursuing an MSc in Management at Galway. I want to deepen my strategic thinking and understand organizational behavior more rigorously. Your program's focus on innovation management and change leadership directly addresses what I see as my next growth edge. I'm not looking to escape the energy sector; I'm looking to lead transformation within it. Ireland's emergence as a renewable energy hub fascinates me, and I want to contribute to that transition.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
Five years from now, I see myself running a legal consulting firm in Lagos, one that bridges the gap between Nigerian businesses and international commerce. Not just another law practice grinding through contracts, but a firm that actually helps companies navigate the complexity of doing business across borders—something I've realized is desperately needed here.

I've spent the last decade working in corporate law, first with Aluko & Associates and then in-house at a manufacturing company. The work was solid, but I kept hitting the same wall: I could advise on legal compliance, but when clients asked about market entry strategy, supply chain optimization, or whether they should restructure their operations, I was out of depth. I watched partners make critical business decisions based on incomplete information, and I realized the lawyers weren't the problem—we lacked business acumen. That gap costs companies millions.

My current role managing the legal department of a 200-person manufacturing firm crystallized this. I oversee contracts worth $15 million annually, but I'm constantly second-guessing strategic decisions because I don't have formal training in finance, operations, or business strategy. When the CEO asked me to evaluate whether we should integrate vertically or outsource logistics, I could tell him what was legal. I couldn't tell him what made business sense.

That's when I decided an MBA wasn't optional—it was essential to the person I want to become.

University of Cyprus appeals to me specifically because of the program's location and structure. The Mediterranean context and European framework give me exposure to markets beyond Nigeria, which I need. Your emphasis on strategic management and operations aligns directly with my gaps. But more importantly, I want to study in a place where I can learn from classmates running businesses across Africa, the Middle East, and Europe. That diversity feels non-negotiable for someone building an international practice.

I'm not going to pretend I'm applying to prove something to doubters—I've already built credibility in my field. I'm applying because I know the ceiling of what I can achieve without this foundation. Within five years, I want to be the person other companies call first when they're taking a major step forward. The MBA is the tool that gets me there. Your program, specifically, is where I can build it.

prompt: Select one prompt from group A and respond to your selected prompt using 100 words or fewer. I had my preconceived ideas about a person or group of people challenged at work and I responded by... I took initiative to self-teach/learn a new skill to enhance my career/skill set by... The COVID-19/pandemic taught me the importance of...
content: I had preconceived ideas about sales challenged when I joined a fintech startup as a non-technical founder. I assumed sales teams were just order-takers, disconnected from product vision. My first week, I shadowed our top performer and realized she understood our API architecture better than half our engineers. She translated technical complexity into client problems we actually solved. I started weekly deep-dives into our product documentation, asking "why" constantly. Within months, I was closing enterprise deals I'd previously thought impossible. That experience rewired how I lead—I stopped assuming expertise and started asking questions. Now I pair our sales team with engineers monthly. Revenue grew 40%, but more importantly, I learned that the best leaders listen first, judge second.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
When I left my role as Operations Manager at Broughton Manufacturing last year, colleagues asked if I'd finally found my calling elsewhere. I hadn't. I'd found something harder to admit: that I'd plateaued.

Over eleven years, I've built a solid career. I moved from quality control supervisor to managing a team of eighteen across three production facilities. I negotiated a £2.3m supplier contract that cut material costs by 18%. My team consistently hit targets. By traditional measures, I was progressing. But somewhere around year nine, I realised I was executing the same playbook on an increasingly larger scale, and no one—myself included—was asking why we were doing things the way we were doing them.

The wake-up call came during a restructuring project. Our plant in Birmingham was hemorrhaging efficiency, and leadership asked me to fix it. I did what I'd always done: streamlined workflows, renegotiated shift patterns, squeezed more output from the same resources. It worked. Productivity went up 12%. But as I was presenting the results, I found myself thinking: this is process optimisation, not reinvention. We'd made the system more efficient without questioning whether the system itself made sense.

That's when I started reading about operations differently—not just through the lens of cost reduction, but through strategy. I realised how little I actually understood about how manufacturing operations connect to broader business strategy. Why do some factories thrive as digital transitions happen while others implode? How do you plan operations when supply chains are fragmenting? I'd been so focused on executing operations that I'd never learned to *think* about them.

I've spent the past eight months doing temporary contract work while taking online courses in supply chain management and strategic planning. It's clarified what I need: formal training in how business actually works beyond my function. Not just operations tools, but how finance shapes decisions, how markets drive strategy, how data and technology are fundamentally changing what operations can be.

UTS appeals to me specifically because of your focus on technology-enabled operations and your connections with Australian manufacturing—an industry rethinking itself right now. I'm drawn to studying in Sydney rather than back home because I want to challenge my assumptions in a new context. Australian manufacturing faces different pressures than the UK, and I think there's real value in understanding an unfamiliar market.

I'm not coming to UTS because I've mastered my field and want to crown my career with an MBA. I'm coming because I've realised how much of my field I don't actually understand, and I'm ready to think differently about operations before I reach a point where change feels too risky. I've always been someone who solves problems, but I think I'll be more useful—to employers and to myself—if I first learn to ask better questions.

prompt: Reflect on a core value that defines you. Share a specific example of how it has guided your decisions and actions, and explain how it will shape your contributions and growth as a McCombs MBA student. Highlight how this value has influenced your leadership style and life path, and how it will shape your MBA experience.
content:
Honesty—not the polite kind, but the kind that makes people uncomfortable—is what I've built my life around.

Three years ago, I was leading a 12-person team at a solar development firm in Hamburg. We were pitching our largest project to date: a 45-megawatt installation that would've secured the company's position in the commercial market. Two weeks before presenting to the investor, our structural engineer flagged a potential foundation issue that our initial designs hadn't fully addressed. It would cost €200,000 and delay the project by six months.

My boss wanted me to present the original timeline anyway. "We can resolve it during construction," he said. "We've done it before."

I didn't. Instead, I presented the problem directly to the investor, explained our mitigation plan, and asked for a revised timeline. I lost the project in its original form, but not my integrity. The investor came back eight months later with a different site and asked specifically to work with our team because of that conversation.

That experience crystallized something I'd been learning: dishonesty always costs more than transparency, even when transparency feels riskier in the moment.

This value shapes how I lead. I give direct feedback, admit when I'm wrong, and expect the same from my team. It's not always comfortable, but it builds trust faster than anything else. It's also why I'm pursuing an MBA now—I'm honest enough with myself to know my analytical and financial skills aren't as sharp as they need to be for senior positions.

At McCombs, I want to contribute this perspective to classroom discussions and group projects, especially in cases where the "easy" answer wins over the right one. I'm drawn to McCombs' emphasis on leadership ethics, and I'm ready to challenge myself alongside people who value clarity over convenience.

school_name: UCL School of Management MSc Management
prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
When I joined Google's Lagos office in 2019 as a Product Marketing Associate, I remember feeling simultaneously out of place and exactly where I needed to be. I was one of the few women in my cohort, and definitely the only one from my hometown of Ibadan. My first project was launching the Google for Small Business initiative across West Africa—a program designed to teach local entrepreneurs how to use Google's tools. I spent three months visiting markets, shops, and call centers, collecting feedback that would shape our entire go-to-market strategy. It was unglamorous work, but it taught me something crucial: understanding your audience means getting your hands dirty.

That initiative grew into something I didn't expect. Within eighteen months, we'd onboarded over 15,000 small businesses across Nigeria, Ghana, and Senegal. Google promoted me to Product Manager, and suddenly I was leading a team of four, managing a $2.3 million annual budget, and reporting directly to our Regional Head of Partnerships. I was twenty-six years old and completely terrified.

The terrifying part wasn't the promotion itself—it was realizing I didn't know how to lead. I'd been a strong individual contributor, but managing people required a different skill set entirely. I made mistakes. I gave feedback that was too harsh, missed signs that team members were struggling, and took on too much while delegating too little. Around month three, one of my best engineers left for another company, and her exit interview made it clear it was partially my fault. That was painful, but it was also clarifying.

I became deliberate about my development. I started meeting with my manager's manager quarterly. I read Kim Scott's "Radical Candor" and actually implemented it—not just the framework, but the philosophy. I learned that being a good leader meant caring enough about your team to be honest with them, and being strong enough to admit when you're wrong. The next year, my team's engagement scores improved significantly, and we shipped features that increased user retention by 23%.

By 2022, I was managing partnerships for Google's cloud division across Africa. It was a natural progression, but it also made something clear to me: I'd built expertise in a specific area, but I hadn't built the broader business perspective I needed to eventually lead at a strategic level. I know how to manage products and teams. What I don't know well enough is how to think about organizational strategy, capital allocation, financial modeling, and how technology intersects with macroeconomic forces.

UCL's MSc Management appeals to me precisely because it's designed for people who need both depth and breadth. I don't want an MBA focused purely on tech or strategy consulting—I want to understand how all the pieces fit together. I want to return to Africa with the ability to think like a general manager, not just a product person. I'm at a point where I need to step back and build the foundational knowledge that will actually enable the impact I want to have in the next decade.

prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
I am writing to provide additional context that I believe is important for your review of my application.

Throughout my academic journey, I have faced numerous challenges that have shaped who I am today. My GPA of 3.0 does not fully represent my capabilities and potential as a student. During my undergraduate years, I struggled with time management and personal issues that affected my performance. However, I have learned from these experiences and grown significantly.

In my professional career, I have worked in the non-profit sector for six years. This experience has been incredibly meaningful and has given me a deep sense of purpose. I am passionate about making a difference in the world and helping those in need. Working in non-profits has taught me the importance of collaboration, innovation, and thinking outside the box to solve complex problems.

My GMAT score of 647 does not reflect my true potential either. I found the standardized test format challenging and did not have adequate time to prepare due to my work commitments. However, I am confident that I have the analytical and quantitative skills necessary to succeed in an MBA program. I have taken on increasingly complex projects and responsibilities in my role, which demonstrates my ability to handle rigorous academic work.

As a Filipino and non-binary individual, I bring a unique perspective to the classroom. Diversity is important, and I believe my background and identity will contribute to a more inclusive learning environment at Macquarie Business School. I am committed to being an active participant in student organizations and campus life.

The non-profit sector has shown me that there are many ways to create positive change in society. I am interested in pursuing an MBA to deepen my understanding of business strategy and management. I want to learn how to scale social impact and make non-profits more sustainable and efficient. An MBA will give me the tools and knowledge I need to advance in my career and contribute more meaningfully to my organization and community.

I am particularly interested in Macquarie Business School because of its strong reputation and commitment to developing leaders. I am excited about the opportunity to learn from experienced faculty and engage with my peers from around the world.

Thank you for considering my application. I hope this additional context helps you understand my qualifications and commitment to pursuing an MBA. I am confident that I will be a valuable addition to your program and community.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
# Essay Response

The achievement I am most proud of is successfully leading the expansion of my real estate company into three new markets over the past five years. This accomplishment demonstrates my ability to drive growth and manage complex projects at scale.

When I joined our organization in 2013 as a junior agent, we were operating only in Ho Chi Minh City with a small team of eight people. I recognized early on that there was significant untapped potential in Hanoi and Da Nang, where urbanization and foreign investment were creating new opportunities. I proposed a market entry strategy to our CEO and was given the responsibility to execute it.

The expansion required careful planning and coordination across multiple departments. I had to identify suitable locations for new offices, recruit and train local sales teams, establish relationships with key property developers, and build brand awareness in unfamiliar markets. Over the course of three years, we successfully opened offices in both cities and grew our overall headcount from 8 to 45 employees.

What makes me most proud is not just the numerical growth, but the positive impact we created. By entering these new markets, we helped many Vietnamese families find quality housing options and enabled foreign investors to participate in Vietnam's real estate boom. Our company became a trusted brand in these regions, and we contributed to the local economy through job creation and tax revenue.

This experience taught me the importance of strategic thinking and resilience. There were many obstacles along the way – local competitors tried to block us from entering their markets, some of my team members quit during the difficult early months, and we faced unexpected regulatory challenges in Hanoi. However, I remained committed to the vision and worked through each problem systematically.

I am particularly proud of how I handled the cultural differences between the three cities. What worked in Ho Chi Minh City didn't automatically work in Hanoi or Da Nang. I had to adapt our business model, adjust our marketing messaging, and learn to work with different stakeholders in each region. This experience helped me develop a more nuanced understanding of Vietnam's diverse business landscape.

Today, the three offices we established generate approximately 40% of our total company revenue. This success has positioned me well within the organization and has given me confidence that I can lead larger initiatives in the future.

I believe an Executive MBA from Sasin will help me build on this foundation. I want to develop stronger strategic and financial management skills that will enable me to lead at a higher level, perhaps even start my own real estate development company. I am ready to bring my experience and learning mindset to the classroom and contribute to discussions about business expansion and emerging markets in Southeast Asia.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
# Most Significant Accomplishment

Throughout my career in retail and e-commerce, I have had the opportunity to grow both professionally and personally. My most significant accomplishment is leading a team that achieved a 15% increase in sales performance over one year.

This accomplishment matters to me because it represents my ability to drive results and inspire others. In my role as a Senior Associate at a medium-sized online retailer, I was tasked with improving our conversion rates, which had been stagnant for several quarters. I knew I needed to make a meaningful impact.

I approached this challenge by implementing new strategies. First, I reorganized the team structure to better align with our goals. Second, I introduced new training programs to improve employee engagement. Third, I worked with the marketing department to optimize our customer experience. These initiatives combined to create the positive results we achieved.

The process was not easy. There were obstacles and setbacks along the way. However, my determination and commitment to excellence helped us push through. I learned valuable lessons about perseverance and leadership during this time.

This accomplishment matters deeply to me because it shows that I can make a real difference in an organization. It demonstrates my technical skills and my ability to lead teams toward success. It also reflects my passion for the retail and e-commerce industry and my desire to continue growing as a professional.

As I pursue my MBA at Alliance Manchester Business School, I want to build on this foundation. I believe that an advanced degree will help me develop the strategic thinking and business acumen necessary to take on greater responsibilities in the future. I am committed to my professional development and to making valuable contributions to any organization I work with.

In conclusion, my accomplishment of leading a team to achieve significant sales growth is something I am proud of. It has shaped my career trajectory and influenced my decision to pursue an MBA. I am excited about the opportunity to bring my experience and dedication to Alliance Manchester Business School and to continue my journey of professional excellence.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
I've worked in retail for six years, and somewhere along the way, I stopped understanding my own industry.

When I started at MediaShop Berlin as a junior buyer in 2018, the job made sense. I managed inventory for 40 SKUs, built supplier relationships, attended trade shows. It was straightforward. But by 2021, when I moved into our e-commerce division, everything fragmented. We were suddenly tracking customer acquisition costs, lifetime value, conversion funnels—metrics I could see in dashboards but didn't really understand. My team would debate whether to invest in personalization algorithms or expand our logistics network, and I'd listen without a framework to evaluate either option.

I realized I was managing without comprehending the business. I could tell you how much we spent on Facebook ads or what our margin looked like, but I couldn't explain *why* those numbers should matter relative to each other, or what they meant for strategy.

That's when I knew I needed an MBA.

Over the last year, I've done what I could. I completed Google's Business Analytics certificate. I built a financial model comparing our three warehouse locations. I read quarterly earnings reports from Zalando and Amazon. But I'm learning things in fragmented pieces, without system. I'm preparing myself through YouTube when I should be learning with structure, with peers who understand strategic thinking, with instructors who can challenge my assumptions.

Imperial's EMBA appeals to me specifically because it's designed for people like me—working managers who need to close gaps while staying in our careers. I can't step away for two years. I need to keep building at MediaShop while getting the foundation I lack. Your curriculum focuses on analytical decision-making and strategic management in ways that directly address what I'm missing. And frankly, studying in London, in a program cohort of working professionals from across industries, is exactly the accelerator I need.
