The CFO wanted cost-cutting. The Chief Commercial Officer wanted to preserve headcount in his division. The CTO thought we were ignoring technical debt. Nobody trusted each other, and frankly, nobody trusted us either. By week three, stakeholders were actively withholding information, and our data quality was terrible.

I realized we couldn't solve this with better analysis. We needed to rebuild trust first.

So I did something unconventional. Instead of presenting findings to the leadership team as a bloc, I spent three weeks meeting each executive individually in their offices—not to convince them, but to listen. The CFO was terrified of recession. The CCO had been burned by failed initiatives before and feared his team would be scapegoated. The CTO felt ignored in previous transformations. These weren't personality conflicts. They were legitimate fears dressed up as politics.

I brought these themes back to the team and restructured our entire approach. We built the new operating model with explicit safeguards: transparent cost-benefit calculations so nobody could twist numbers, explicit protections for workforce transitions, and a technology roadmap that addressed the CTO's concerns upfront. We presented it not as "consulting recommendations" but as "a model we built *with* you, addressing your specific concerns."

Did everyone get everything they wanted? No. But something shifted. The CFO committed to the plan. The CCO actually advocated for it in steering meetings. The CTO contributed ideas instead of throwing obstacles.

We delivered the transformation on time. More importantly, the client implemented it, which is rare. What I learned wasn't just about managing stakeholders—it's that leadership often means stepping back from being the expert in the room. It means admitting that I didn't have all the answers, and that the people closest to the problem usually do. That vulnerability, paradoxically, made me more effective.

I'm drawn to INSEAD's GEMBA program because it brings together leaders from radically different industries and countries. I want to learn how peers from banking, manufacturing, nonprofits, and government navigate complexity and build trust across difference. That's the edge case where my experience feels thin, and where I want to grow.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
Leadership means taking responsibility and inspiring others to achieve common goals. Throughout my career in the medical devices industry, I have tried to demonstrate these qualities in various situations.

At my current company, PT Medika Solutions in Jakarta, I work as a Senior Product Manager. In this role, I have led several projects and managed teams of different sizes. One particular experience stands out as a moment when I was able to show leadership.

Last year, our department was tasked with launching a new product line for orthopedic devices. This was a challenging project because it involved multiple departments and stakeholders. The timeline was aggressive—we had only six months to go from concept to market. I was assigned to lead this initiative.

From the beginning, I realized that communication would be critical. I scheduled regular meetings with all teams involved, including R&D, regulatory, manufacturing, and sales. I made sure everyone understood the timeline and their responsibilities. I also tried to create an environment where people felt comfortable sharing concerns and ideas.

The project faced several obstacles. There were delays in regulatory approval, and the manufacturing team encountered some technical issues with the production process. These setbacks could have derailed the entire project. However, I remained calm and focused on finding solutions. I worked closely with the manufacturing team to troubleshoot the issues, and I also coordinated with our regulatory team to expedite the approval process.

I also made it a point to recognize the hard work and dedication of my team members. I believed that motivation was important, so I regularly acknowledged their contributions and celebrated small wins along the way. This helped keep morale high even during difficult periods.

Ultimately, we successfully launched the product on schedule. The product has been well-received in the market, and it has contributed significantly to the company's revenue. This experience showed me that leadership is not just about making decisions, but about bringing people together and creating a shared vision.

Through this experience, I learned that a good leader must be able to handle pressure and uncertainty. I also learned the importance of listening to others and being open to different perspectives. These lessons have shaped how I approach leadership in my current role and will continue to guide me in my career.

I believe that an MBA education will help me develop these leadership skills further. I am interested in learning about different management approaches and strategies that I can apply in my future career. I am particularly interested in how to lead in increasingly complex business environments.

Kellogg's commitment to developing brave leaders aligns well with my career aspirations. I am excited about the opportunity to learn from experienced faculty and my peers. I believe that the MBA program will equip me with the knowledge and skills needed to become a more effective leader.

In conclusion, my experience leading the orthopedic devices project demonstrates my ability to take on responsibility and inspire others. I am confident that an MBA from Kellogg will help me achieve my career goals and become the kind of leader who can make a positive impact in the business world.

school_name: Asian Institute of Management Executive MBA
prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
Five years post-MBA, I want to be the supply chain director for a mid-sized manufacturing company in Southeast Asia—probably Thailand or Vietnam. Not the regional head, not chasing the C-suite title everyone assumes you're after. The director level is where the real decisions happen. That's where you can reshape how 400 people approach their work, redesign operations without committee approval slowing you down, and actually see the impact of your decisions within a reasonable timeframe.

I've spent eleven years in logistics, starting as a coordinator in Hamburg and working my way through roles in procurement, operations, and now as a senior manager at a €180 million distributor. I'm good at optimizing networks—I cut our lead times by 28 percent and reduced inventory carrying costs by €2.3 million last year. But I've hit a ceiling. My company is stable, traditional, and frankly, risk-averse. The decisions I want to make—digitizing our supply chain, pushing into Southeast Asian markets, restructuring our entire procurement model—aren't on their agenda.

What I actually need isn't just technical knowledge about supply chains. I have that. What I'm missing is the ability to think like a business leader instead of a functional expert. I need to understand finance well enough to build a real case for major capital investments. I need to know how to navigate corporate politics and board dynamics. I need frameworks for decision-making under ambiguity, not just optimization problems with clear parameters. And honestly, I need the confidence that comes from peer learning—from working alongside people who've already made these transitions successfully.

AIM's program fits because it's not trying to turn me into a generalist consultant. You understand that people entering an executive MBA already have industry depth. Your curriculum actually builds on that—you're designing for people who need broader business perspective, not people learning their functional area for the first time. The Southeast Asia focus is essential for me. I'm not interested in returning to Europe. The growth is happening here, the problems are more complex, and the business environment is fundamentally different.

I'm also drawn to your cohort model. Eleven years in logistics means I'm comfortable being the supply chain person in the room. But at AIM, I'll be alongside people from banking, manufacturing, tech, strategy—people who can challenge my assumptions and show me what I don't know. That peer learning is what makes this investment make sense.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
My career has been a journey of continuous growth and development within the military and government sectors. Since graduating in 2016, I have worked to build a strong foundation in leadership and organizational management.

My first position was as a Junior Administrative Officer at the Turkish Ministry of Defense in Ankara. During this time, I gained valuable experience in understanding bureaucratic processes and how large organizations operate. I worked with various teams to manage documentation and support senior officials. This role taught me the importance of attention to detail and working within established hierarchies.

After two years, I moved into a more challenging position as a Mid-Level Coordinator for a government agency focused on strategic planning. This was where I began to understand the bigger picture of how decisions are made at higher levels. I was responsible for coordinating between different departments and ensuring that information flowed smoothly. I managed approximately 15 people and oversaw several projects related to policy implementation. During this period, I realized that I wanted to develop stronger skills in business strategy and decision-making.

In my current role as Senior Officer for Policy Development, which I have held for the past three years, I have taken on more responsibility. I work on developing policies that affect multiple departments and have been involved in several important initiatives. My work involves analyzing data, meeting with stakeholders, and presenting recommendations to senior leadership. I have learned how to communicate complex ideas in a clear manner and how to influence others through careful preparation and presentation.

Throughout these positions, I have developed a strong understanding of how organizations function. I have worked with people from different backgrounds and cultures, which has given me a global perspective. I believe my experience in the public sector has prepared me well for business leadership roles.

However, I recognize that my background in government work may be different from the private sector, and this is one reason I am interested in pursuing an Executive MBA. The program will help me bridge the gap between public sector management and business management principles. I want to gain knowledge in areas such as financial management, strategic planning, and business innovation that are not always emphasized in government work.

I am committed to using my MBA education to advance my career and potentially transition into a role where I can apply business principles to improve organizational efficiency and effectiveness. I believe that Toulouse Business School's Executive MBA program will provide me with the necessary tools and knowledge to achieve these goals.

prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
**Professional Setback**

Throughout my career in media and entertainment, I have faced many challenges that have shaped me into the professional I am today. One particular setback stands out as a defining moment in my personal and professional development.

Two years ago, I was working as a content manager at a mid-sized production company in London. I was responsible for overseeing a team of five people and managing multiple projects simultaneously. During this period, I was tasked with leading the development of a new digital platform that was meant to revolutionize how our company distributed content to audiences. This was a significant opportunity, and I was excited about the prospect of taking on such a large responsibility.

Unfortunately, things did not go as planned. The project faced numerous delays due to various factors, both within and outside of my control. There were technical issues with the development team, budget constraints, and changing client requirements. As a result, the project ended up missing its deadline by several months, and the budget exceeded projections significantly. This was a major setback for me personally, as I felt that I had failed in my role as a leader.

At first, I was quite upset about the situation. I questioned my own abilities and wondered if I was capable of managing such large-scale projects. However, I quickly realized that dwelling on the situation would not help me move forward. I decided to take a step back and analyze what had gone wrong.

Upon reflection, I identified several key issues that had contributed to the project's failure. First, I had not communicated effectively with all stakeholders from the beginning. I had assumed that everyone understood the project timeline and requirements, but this was not the case. Second, I had not allocated enough resources to the project early on. I had underestimated the complexity of the technical aspects and had not hired additional staff when needed. Third, I had not established clear milestones or checkpoints to monitor progress.

After identifying these issues, I took concrete action to improve my management skills. I began attending project management workshops and reading books on leadership. I also implemented new systems within my team, including weekly status meetings, clearer documentation of requirements, and more frequent communication with stakeholders. These changes made a significant difference in how our team operated.

The experience taught me valuable lessons about leadership and project management. I learned that communication is paramount in any project, and that it is important to set clear expectations early on. I also learned that it is critical to be realistic about timelines and resources, and to ask for help when needed. Most importantly, I learned that setbacks are not failures, but rather opportunities to learn and grow.

Since this experience, I have become a more effective leader and manager. I have successfully overseen several projects that were completed on time and within budget. My team respects my leadership, and I have received positive feedback from colleagues about my improved communication skills.

I believe that this experience has prepared me well for the challenges of an MBA program. The setback taught me resilience and the importance of continuous learning and self-improvement. I am confident that I can bring these lessons to my studies at EDHEC and to my future career in the media and entertainment industry.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
When I walked into Rabin's office in Tel Aviv three years ago, I had no idea I'd be sitting across from the managing partner of a 200-person firm within two years. What I thought would be a five-year trajectory compressed itself dramatically—partly through luck, mostly through the kind of work that left my laptop open at midnight more nights than not.

But here's what nobody tells you about climbing quickly: you hit a ceiling made of things you can't learn from staying late. I'm good at the technical work—contract analysis, due diligence, closing deals. I can read a balance sheet and spot issues most associates miss. What I can't do yet is run a practice. I can't structure a firm for growth. I don't know how to build a business model that scales beyond "hire smart people and work them hard." And I'm increasingly aware that I shouldn't.

At 28, I have maybe three more years before the question stops being "can you be a partner?" and starts being "why aren't you a partner?" I need to use this window deliberately, not reactively.

The gap I'm trying to close isn't theoretical. Last year we lost a $2M client because I misread the economics of retaining them—I charged what the work was worth, not what it was worth to them. I spent six months thinking about that mistake. I started reading about pricing psychology, value delivery, margin management. I realized I was trying to learn business strategy from articles and podcasts, which is like trying to learn law from Medium posts.

I also realized I don't want to be a traditional legal partner. The firms I respect most—firms that have clients for decades, that people actually want to work for—they think differently about client relationships, leverage, and what "running a practice" means. They're more business than law, even though law is their product. I want that skillset before I'm the one responsible for a practice.

London Business School's Executive MBA appeals to me for specific reasons. Your program's focus on strategy and general management is obvious, but what matters more is your cohort. I've looked at the profiles: CFOs from Dubai, consultants from Mumbai, a woman running a healthcare startup in Stockholm. That's not a network—that's a business school. These are people solving real problems in real time, not case studies. I need to think alongside people doing things at scale I haven't attempted yet.

The 18-month structure works for my constraints. I can't leave the firm for two years, but I can block off weekends and three weeks per year. And honestly, at this point in my career, being in a classroom with 40-year-old operations directors seems more valuable than being the youngest person in the room.

I'm not trying to escape law. I'm trying to become the kind of lawyer who builds something that lasts beyond my own billable hours.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
When I first learned about ESC Clermont Master in Management, I was immediately drawn to the program's reputation for excellence and innovation in business education. As someone from Egypt with a decade of experience in management consulting, I knew that this program could be the perfect next step in my career trajectory.

To learn more about the school, I visited the website multiple times and read through the program structure carefully. I was impressed by the curriculum, which covers all the essential topics needed for a successful career in management. The faculty bios were informative, and I noticed that many professors have significant industry experience, which I believe is crucial for quality business education. I also looked at the student testimonials and found them very encouraging. The fact that ESC Clermont has a strong network and alumni presence across Europe was particularly appealing to me.

I also reached out to a few current students through LinkedIn and learned more about their experiences. They spoke positively about the community and the quality of education they were receiving. One student mentioned that the program has strong connections with companies like LVMH and other major corporations, which excited me because networking and industry connections are important to me.

What I found most compelling about ESC Clermont is the emphasis on developing well-rounded business leaders. Coming from a consulting background at one of the top firms, I have learned that technical skills alone are not sufficient. You need creativity, emotional intelligence, and cultural awareness. The program's focus on these softer skills, combined with rigorous training in business fundamentals, aligns perfectly with my career goals.

Being part of the ESC Clermont community would allow me to expand my professional network significantly. During my ten years in consulting, I have worked primarily within the consulting bubble. A diverse cohort of students from different countries and industries would expose me to fresh perspectives and new ways of thinking. I am particularly interested in learning from classmates who come from entrepreneurial or family business backgrounds, as these experiences differ greatly from the corporate consulting environment I know.

Additionally, I am eager to take advantage of the school's experiential learning opportunities. I understand that the program includes consulting projects and case competitions, which would allow me to apply theoretical knowledge to real business problems. This is something I value highly given my consulting background—the ability to see theory in action.

I am also interested in the school's international opportunities and the chance to study abroad or participate in exchange programs. Having spent most of my career in the Middle East and Europe, I am keen to expand my perspective further and understand emerging markets better. This global exposure would be invaluable as I transition from execution-focused consulting work to more strategic leadership roles.

In conclusion, I believe that ESC Clermont Master in Management is an excellent fit for my professional development. The combination of strong academics, a diverse community, and practical learning opportunities is exactly what I am seeking as I move forward in my career. I am confident that I would be a valuable member of your cohort, and I am excited about the possibility of joining your community.

prompt: Statement of Purpose (SOP)
content:
When I joined Vodafone Italia's network operations team in 2012, I had one job: monitor outages. Instead, I found myself in the middle of a crisis—a cascading infrastructure failure across three regions that took down service for nearly 200,000 customers. I was twenty-six, speaking almost no English, and suddenly in a war room with executives arguing in rapid-fire Italian about whose fault it was.

That day changed how I saw my career. Not because I solved the problem—I didn't. But because I realized the gap between technical competence and the ability to drive real change. I could read network diagnostics perfectly, but I couldn't influence the vendor selection decision that might prevent this from happening again. I couldn't push back on cost-cutting measures that weakened our infrastructure. I couldn't speak the language of strategy that seemed to matter most.

Over the last decade, I've deliberately built toward that missing language. I moved from operations into network planning, then into business case development for our 5G rollout. I led a team of eight engineers through a digital transformation project that reduced our network provisioning time by 42 percent—not because I coded better than anyone, but because I learned to connect technical reality to business outcomes. I got comfortable presenting to the board, negotiating with vendors, and building the kind of three-year roadmap that actually gets funded.

But there's a ceiling. At Vodafone, my career path as a woman in telecom is narrower than it should be. More importantly, I've started to realize that much of what I'm learning on the job is scattered—disconnected from deeper frameworks about organizational leadership, financial strategy, and how to drive transformation across an industry that's being completely upended by 5G and AI. I'm learning through doing, but I'm missing the why.

I want an MBA now because I'm at the point where intuition and experience need to be sharpened by real theory. I want to understand not just how to execute a transformation initiative, but how to anticipate market shifts and position my company—or eventually lead a company—to capitalize on them. Great Lakes appeals to me specifically because your EMBA is designed for people like me: experienced professionals in technical industries who need to make a sharp pivot toward general management. Your program's emphasis on financial management and strategic planning, combined with the flexible schedule that lets me keep my role at Vodafone, feels like exactly what I need.

I'm not interested in an MBA to leave telecom. I'm interested because I want to lead in it. After twelve years of learning the technical and operational foundation, I'm ready to learn what it takes to set strategy.

prompt: Statement of Purpose (SOP)
content:
# Statement of Purpose

I've spent the last eleven years in real estate development, watching cities transform one project at a time. What started as an analyst role at a mid-sized Moscow firm became senior project manager overseeing $40+ million portfolios across residential and mixed-use developments. This experience has been fulfilling, but it's also exposed the gaps in my understanding of the business side—the strategy, finance, and operations that separate successful companies from ones that plateau.

I'm applying to Great Lakes EMBA because I need to sharpen my business acumen while staying rooted in my career.

In real estate, I've learned that technical execution isn't enough. I've watched projects succeed because someone understood the market dynamics, negotiated with lenders effectively, or structured deals cleverly—not because the buildings were built well. A few years ago, our firm decided to enter the Moscow hospitality sector. I led the project management side and it was well-executed, but the deal ultimately underperformed because we didn't adequately stress-test our financial assumptions or fully understand the tourism market dynamics. That project cost us $2.3 million in unexpected losses. It taught me that I can manage timelines and teams, but I need deeper expertise in financial modeling, market analysis, and strategic planning.

The last three years have been transitional for me. I relocated to Toronto in 2022 and shifted into asset management for a REIT managing $300 million in commercial property. It's been an intentional reset—moving away from pure project execution toward understanding how capital allocation decisions get made. This role has clarified what I want next: I want to move into development leadership where I combine my operational experience with real business strategy.

An MBA would close the gap between where I am and where I need to be. I need formal training in corporate finance, strategic management, and data-driven decision-making. More importantly, I need to think more systematically about how real estate companies scale, how capital flows in this industry, and how to lead through complexity.

I've been selective about programs. Great Lakes appeals to me because of the flexibility of the EMBA format—I can continue working while building these skills—and because your curriculum includes real estate finance and development case studies that directly apply to my trajectory. The cohort diversity and the Canadian business context also matter to me. I'm interested in understanding how institutional capital works differently in North America than in Russia or Europe, and how that shapes development strategy.

I'm not coming to business school to pivot entirely or discover a new passion. I'm coming with clear eyes about my limitations and what I need to learn. I want to stay in real estate but lead more strategically. That requires this credential and this structured learning environment.

school_name: Poznan University of Economics MSc Management
prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
What matters most to me is making a positive impact on society through my work in the non-profit sector. Throughout my career, I have been committed to creating meaningful change in the lives of vulnerable populations, and this passion has driven every decision I have made professionally.

I began my career nine years ago with a desire to contribute to development work. Working in the NGO space has given me the opportunity to engage with communities across East Africa and understand the complexities of social challenges. Whether working on health initiatives, educational programs, or economic empowerment projects, I have seen firsthand how strategic management and proper resource allocation can transform lives. This realization has been central to my professional identity.

My journey in the non-profit sector has taught me that good intentions alone are insufficient. To create lasting impact, organizations need strong operational systems, financial management, and strategic planning. I have worked on various programs where I observed gaps in how resources were distributed and how projects were monitored. These experiences made me realize that I need deeper knowledge of business management to be more effective in my role.

Pursuing an MSc in Management is the logical next step in my career. I believe that developing advanced skills in organizational management, financial analysis, and strategic planning will enhance my ability to lead projects and organizations toward greater impact. The skills I will gain at Poznan University of Economics will equip me to tackle the management challenges I face in my current work and prepare me for future leadership positions within the non-profit sector.

Beyond my professional aspirations, what matters to me is integrity and ethical leadership. In my experience working with vulnerable populations, I have seen how easily power can be misused and how important it is to maintain transparent and accountable practices. I am committed to being a leader who puts the needs of the communities we serve first, rather than personal gain or organizational growth for its own sake.

I am also passionate about continuous learning. The complexity of development work means that there are always new challenges to address and new approaches to consider. I see the MBA program as an opportunity to expand my thinking and learn from diverse peers and instructors who bring different perspectives to management challenges.

Finally, what matters most to me is using my education to give back to my community. As a Kenyan woman working in development, I feel a responsibility to use whatever advantages I gain through education to benefit others facing similar challenges to those I have overcome. I want to eventually lead an organization or initiative that directly addresses poverty and inequality in East Africa, and I am confident that the management skills I gain at Poznan will help me achieve this goal.

In conclusion, what matters most to me is impact, integrity, and the commitment to lifelong learning. The MSc in Management at Poznan University of Economics represents an important investment in these values, and I am excited about the opportunity to grow as a leader and professional.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
In 2019, I suggested we stop using trucks to deliver medicine to rural clinics in Kenya. My logistics manager laughed. He'd been in the industry for thirty years and said I was either naïve or looking to get fired.

I was neither. I was frustrated.

We were losing 12-15% of our pharmaceutical shipments to spoilage on the roads between Nairobi and the Rift Valley. The heat damage was predictable. The delays were consistent. And every month, we were writing off hundreds of thousands of shillings while clinics went without stock. The industry standard was simple: pack better, drive faster, hire better drivers. Everyone did it. Everyone accepted the losses.

But I'd noticed something while reviewing our route data. The motorcycle taxis that moved between towns—the ones we dismissed as unreliable—actually had better delivery times and zero temperature failures. They were faster because they didn't wait for a full truck. They navigated congestion differently. And they operated on smaller margins, so they moved with urgency.

The idea seemed insane: fragment our consolidated shipments and use motorcycle couriers for time-sensitive medical goods. We'd lose economies of scale. We'd increase per-unit costs. I couldn't even explain it clearly at first.

What I did instead was run a pilot. Without formal approval—which in retrospect was reckless—I redesigned our packaging to fit motorcycle storage and worked with three independent couriers to move 2% of our weekly volume. I tracked everything obsessively. Temperature data, delivery times, cost per unit, spoilage rates.

Six weeks in, the numbers were undeniable. Zero spoilage. Average delivery time cut by 40%. Per-unit cost was higher, but when we factored in spoilage savings and reduced inventory holding costs from faster turns, we were actually ahead by 18%.

My manager circulated my pilot results reluctantly. The response was mixed. One director called it a "nice experiment that doesn't scale." Another wanted to understand our supplier relationships with the couriers. Someone asked if we were now in the taxi business.

But the clinics started requesting the faster service. One rural health center reduced their emergency stock by 30% because they could count on consistent deliveries. That mattered—it meant capital freed up for other supplies.

We formally launched the hybrid model six months later. Within two years, we'd scaled it across three regions and reduced our pharmaceutical spoilage to under 3%. I won't claim I revolutionized Kenyan logistics. I'll claim I questioned why we were doing something the way we'd always done it.

That's what Exeter's program attracts me most: the focus on questioning systems across cultures and industries. I want to study how established supply chains function globally, but more importantly, I want to learn the frameworks to challenge them thoughtfully. I'm ready to think beyond my sector—to see where else conventional wisdom is just habit.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
Three years ago, I discovered our warehouse in Lyon was systematically underreporting safety incidents. Nothing catastrophic—a few unreported near-misses, a minor fall classified as "training time," pressure from the site manager to keep our safety metrics pristine for a corporate audit.

I wasn't directly involved, but I oversaw the logistics operations. The temptation to stay quiet was real. We were six months from a major contract renewal, the team was understaffed, and raising this would mean investigations, potential fines, and probably having to replace our site manager—someone I'd worked with for five years. The easy choice was looking the other way.

But I couldn't stop thinking about the warehouse workers. One of them had a two-year-old daughter. If something actually happened and we'd hidden safety issues, I'd have to live with that.

I reported it to our compliance officer, which immediately made things complicated. Our parent company launched an audit, and yes, we were fined €80,000. The site manager was let go, which felt terrible—he'd cut corners because targets were unrealistic, not because he was reckless. But he also needed to be held accountable for creating a culture where people hid incidents.

What surprised me was the outcome. We invested in proper safety training, hired additional site supervisors, and revised our incident reporting system. Within eighteen months, we actually reduced our serious incidents by 40%. The workers felt safe reporting problems because they knew management would respond rather than punish them.

I learned that ethical decisions aren't binary. It wasn't "honesty versus loyalty." It was about what loyalty actually meant—not protecting individuals from consequences, but protecting the people who depended on the system to keep them safe. And it forced me to recognize my role: as an operations leader, I set the tone. If I excuse safety shortcuts, everyone below me knows what's acceptable.

This experience shaped how I approach leadership. I want to study at IAE partly because I'm drawn to supply chain sustainability—not just environmental sustainability, but ethical practices across complex global networks where corners are constantly being cut. I want to lead companies that do the right thing even when it costs more, even when it's complicated.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
I started as a junior network technician at PT Telkom in Jakarta in 2013, right after my undergraduate degree. I was twenty-two, living with my parents, and honestly terrified. My job was to monitor telecommunications infrastructure across Central Java—sitting in a control room, watching systems, troubleshooting when something broke. I was one of maybe fifteen technicians in the regional office, and I was the only non-binary person anyone there had ever worked with openly. Those first two years were about survival: learning the systems, proving myself technically competent, and figuring out how to exist professionally in a conservative workplace.

By 2015, I'd been promoted to senior technician and started leading small teams—initially four people, then eight. I realized I was better at explaining complex problems to non-technical staff than at the technical work itself. I had a manager who noticed this and encouraged me to move into operations. That transition in 2016 was harder than I expected. I was starting over in some ways, learning project management and business processes instead of just technical troubleshooting. But I was also building something: I helped streamline our network maintenance scheduling, reducing unplanned downtime by 23% over eighteen months. That felt real.

From 2017 to 2019, I worked as operations manager for a regional division covering four provinces. My team grew to forty people. This is where I first confronted how unprepared I was. We had significant budget constraints, aging infrastructure, and staff retention was terrible. I made mistakes—I pushed too hard on cost cutting in year one and nearly lost our best engineers. I learned to listen more. I also realized I needed to understand the business side better. I was managing operations in isolation, not thinking strategically about how our decisions affected customers, revenue, or stakeholder relationships.

In 2019, I moved to PT Indosat as senior operations manager, overseeing similar functions but at a larger scale—twelve million customers across their network in Sumatra and Kalimantan. The scope was immediately overwhelming. We had older systems, higher operational costs than competitors, and I was inheriting a team that didn't trust the previous leadership. For two years, I focused on stabilizing operations and building trust. We implemented new monitoring systems, reduced critical incidents by 31%, and improved our incident response time from 4.5 hours to 2.1 hours average. More importantly, I built a team that actually talked to each other.

By 2021, I was promoted to regional director—my current role—managing both operations and customer relations across those same territories. This is when I realized how much I don't know. I'm responsible for P&L decisions, stakeholder management, strategic planning, and I'm constantly out of my depth. I've read dozens of business books, taken online courses, but I'm learning from mistakes instead of from deliberate study. Last year we lost a major corporate contract—not because of our execution, but because I didn't understand the full sales cycle and didn't prepare our team soon enough.

That experience is exactly why I'm applying. I've built something meaningful in telecommunications, learned how to lead people, and developed an intuition for operations. But my progression has been reactive, problem-driven. I need structured frameworks for strategic thinking, financial analysis, and business decision-making. Deakin's executive MBA appeals to me because I can bring eleven years of real operational experience while developing the broader business perspective I'm clearly missing. Melbourne's proximity to the Asia-Pacific telecommunications hub is also a significant practical advantage.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
# Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.

Eight years ago, I was managing a portfolio of three oncology drugs at Cristália Farmacêutica in São Paulo, none of which were performing above market expectations. The division was bleeding money—we were losing market share to competitors who had better distribution networks and more aggressive pricing strategies. My director asked me to "fix it," which was code for "figure out why we're failing."

Instead of cutting costs and laying people off—the obvious play—I decided to investigate why our sales team couldn't close deals with hospital networks. I spent six weeks visiting 15 major hospitals across São Paulo and Rio, sitting in procurement meetings, asking uncomfortable questions. The answer was sobering: our product quality was fine, but our supply chain was unreliable. Hospitals didn't trust us to deliver on time. One procurement director told me, "Your competitor gets here on Tuesday. You might get here Tuesday or Thursday."

I restructured our entire distribution model. We shifted from consolidated warehouse drops to weekly direct-to-hospital deliveries, partnered with a more expensive but reliable logistics company, and brought down our lead time from 7-10 days to 3-4 days. On paper, it looked terrible—operational costs increased by 18%. My CFO wasn't happy.

But within 18 months, market share for all three drugs grew. We went from dead last in our category to second. Revenue jumped 34 percent. More importantly, we kept our jobs—the division stayed alive while competitors were consolidating and cutting staff. I learned that sometimes the right solution costs more upfront.

What I'm most proud of isn't the revenue number. It's that I chose to listen first instead of cutting first. In pharma, where I've spent nine years, there's enormous pressure to show quick wins. I could have implemented the standard cost-reduction playbook and looked like a hero for two quarters. Instead, I spent time understanding the actual problem.

That mindset—staying curious, asking hard questions before implementing solutions—is what I want to develop further in an EMBA. I'm at a stage where I'm moving toward senior leadership roles, and I know my biggest gaps are strategic thinking and exposure to different industries' approaches to similar problems. Monash's program attracts accomplished professionals from diverse sectors, and that environment is exactly what I need to grow.

prompt: Why are you interested in earning an MBA? What specific business knowledge, skills or insights do you hope to further develop? Briefly describe one business lesson/insight AND one people leadership (direct/indirect) lesson/insight you have gained from your current or previous roles that may be unique to your industry or would add value to an Executive MBA classroom discussion. Describe the most challenging team you have been on (either as a leader or contributor). What did you learn about yourself from that experience? Engaging and contributing to your EMBA team and classroom learning, as well as successfully completing classes will require significant effort and time. Given your busy work and personal schedules, how have you prepared to dedicate the time required?
content:
Last year, I made a decision that cost our company $2.3 million in lost pipeline.

We were six months into launching our analytics module for Salesforce users—something our product team had spent eighteen months building. I was VP of Product at the time, and the initial metrics looked terrible. Adoption was under 8%, and customers kept telling us it was overcomplicated. My instinct was to keep pushing, to believe the product was right and the market just needed more education.

My VP of Sales disagreed. She showed me three customer recordings where power users actually gave up trying to use the feature. One quote I still remember: "I don't have time to figure this out." We held off launching it broadly and instead rebuilt it from scratch, which meant shelving the module entirely for six months. Revenue projections got cut as a result.

But eighteen months later, when we finally relaunched it, adoption hit 34% in the first quarter. That same feature became our highest-retention driver. The lesson wasn't particularly novel—listen to customers, fail fast, iterate. But living through the cost of ignoring it taught me something deeper: my job as a leader isn't to be right. It's to build the conditions where the right answer surfaces, even if it contradicts me. That's changed how I operate.

I've been in Enterprise SaaS for twelve years, and that industry-specific blind spot—the trap of falling in love with your own solution—is something I see destroy products constantly. I'm interested in understanding the business frameworks that help leaders avoid this. I suspect behavioral economics has a lot to offer here, and I want to study decision-making under uncertainty in ways that go beyond my current, mostly intuition-based approach.

The second lesson came from managing through my worst team experience: our go-to-market restructuring in 2022. We had just acquired a smaller company whose product overlapped with ours. I was asked to integrate the teams, keep morale intact, and deliver a consolidated platform launch in nine months.

I was terrible at it initially. I tried to be the translator between the legacy team and the new team, thinking if I understood both perspectives deeply, I could bridge them. Instead, I became a bottleneck. People didn't trust that I was representing their interests fairly. Two strong leaders left because they felt unheard. I was acting like a mediator when I should have been acting like a leader—being clear about the direction, even if not everyone agreed.

I restructured how we communicated. I started having separate one-on-ones where I was explicitly NOT trying to be neutral. I asked people directly: "What do you need from me?" Instead of solving tensions, I facilitated the teams in solving their own tensions. It felt uncomfortable at first—less diplomatic—but it worked. We hit the launch date, and more importantly, both teams felt like they had agency in the outcome.

That experience taught me I have a tendency toward conflict avoidance that masquerades as thoughtfulness. I'm still working on it.

As for managing the EMBA workload: I'm not going to pretend this will be easy. I have two kids, ages six and eight, and I travel one week a month for work. But I've built my schedule around protected study time before, and I know what I'm capable of when something matters to me.

I've also had to make tradeoffs. I'm stepping back from a mentoring program I run at my company—something I love—because I can't do both well. I'm being more ruthless about saying no to coffee meetings. And I'm being honest with my family about what the next two years looks like.

Foster's EMBA is specifically designed for working leaders, and that's not a program feature I'm working around—it's why I'm applying. I want to test my intuitions against evidence. I want frameworks. I want to learn from people running companies in industries completely different from mine, where my assumptions don't apply. That's exactly what the cohort-based model offers, and I'm ready for it.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
I came to IIM Shillong's website on a Tuesday evening after a frustrating board meeting at Zimmer Biomet's facility in Warsaw. We'd spent three hours debating why our orthopedic implant division wasn't gaining traction in Eastern European hospitals, and the answer was sitting in front of me the whole time: we didn't understand the ecosystem. We had the technology, the manufacturing excellence, the certifications—but we kept thinking like Americans selling to Europeans, not like business builders solving actual hospital problems.

That's when I realized I'd spent nine years becoming very good at one thing: execution within existing structures. At Stryker before this, I managed a 12-person supply chain team across three countries. At my first company in Saint Petersburg, I helped establish regulatory compliance for our cardiovascular device line. The work was solid. I was promoted twice. But I was always operating inside someone else's playbook.

What I haven't done is think like a founder. Haven't sat in a boardroom where I was actually responsible for strategy, not just implementation. Haven't learned how to read markets that don't look like Western Europe. And I haven't learned these things from people who've actually built something in India and Southeast Asia.

IIM Shillong's EPGP stands out to me precisely because of this gap. I'm not looking for another standard MBA where we case-study Apple and Google. I need to sit next to people who've navigated emerging markets with real constraints—capital limitations, regulatory unpredictability, talent shortages. I need to learn from professors and peers who understand how to build competitive advantage when you can't just throw resources at a problem. That's the India advantage, and it's not something I can find in Europe or North America.

My medical device background isn't a constraint here—it's actually valuable. The sector is global, heavily regulated, and increasingly competitive in Asia. I want to work at the intersection of healthcare innovation and emerging market strategy, possibly helping established companies like mine think differently about India and ASEAN expansion, or eventually starting something myself. But first, I need to see how this actually works from the inside.

What I'll bring to your cohort: I'm someone who asks uncomfortable questions. In Warsaw, I pushed back on our "one-size-fits-all" approach with data and persistence until we finally got real conversations about localization happening. I also genuinely enjoy mentoring—I've supervised early-career professionals in Poland and Russia, and I'm genuinely interested in learning from people with different perspectives, not just teaching them what I know.

And honestly, I bring some intellectual humility. My GMAT score isn't stellar—719 reflects that I'm a strong thinker but not a test-taking machine. My GPA from twenty-five years ago doesn't define me. But I know what I don't know, and I'm here to fill those gaps seriously, not just collect credentials.

I'm betting on IIM Shillong because I think this is where I'll learn to think like a business leader, not just a functional expert. And I think my nine years in a highly regulated, global industry will make me a richer conversation partner for people navigating India's healthcare and manufacturing challenges.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I think about my career trajectory, I realize that education has always been the common thread. I am applying to IESE because I want to pursue an MBA that will help me grow professionally and personally while learning from diverse perspectives in a global environment.

I have worked in the education sector for seven years now, starting as a teacher in South Africa and moving into curriculum development and educational technology roles. Throughout this journey, I have come to understand that education is the key to unlocking human potential. This is why I am passionate about making a difference in this field.

IESE's program stands out to me because of its reputation for excellence and its commitment to developing business leaders who will have a positive impact on society. The school's emphasis on ethics and responsible business practices aligns with my values. Additionally, IESE's global network and diverse student body will expose me to different ways of thinking and problem-solving, which I believe is essential in today's interconnected world.

I am particularly drawn to IESE's location in Spain and Europe, as it provides an opportunity to expand my horizons beyond what I've experienced in South Africa. Europe represents new markets and new challenges in education that I am eager to explore. The program will equip me with the analytical and strategic tools I need to transition from operations and curriculum work into a business leadership role.

In terms of what I will contribute to the IESE community, I believe my background in education is valuable. I can bring a different perspective, having come from a developing country context where educational challenges are quite different from those in Europe. I will actively participate in classroom discussions and group work, and I will be a supportive and collaborative teammate.

As a non-binary person from South Africa, I also bring diversity to your cohort in terms of both identity and geographic origin. I am committed to fostering an inclusive environment where all voices are heard and valued.

Looking forward, I hope to use my MBA to transition into a more strategic role within education technology or educational policy. I want to work on projects that have real impact at scale. The tools and network I gain from IESE will be instrumental in achieving these goals.

I am genuinely excited about the possibility of joining IESE and contributing to its vibrant community. I am confident that the program will be transformative for my career and that I will be a dedicated member of the class who brings both enthusiasm and commitment to learning and growth.

Thank you for considering my application.

prompt: What are your short-term and long-term career goals, how will an EMBA from USC Marshall help you achieve those goals?
content:
I have always been passionate about building companies and creating value in the startup ecosystem. My short-term goal is to move into a leadership role at my current VC firm, where I can lead deal sourcing and portfolio company support. In the long-term, I aspire to start my own investment fund focused on early-stage technology companies in emerging markets.

An EMBA from USC Marshall will be instrumental in helping me achieve these goals. The program's rigorous curriculum in finance, strategy, and entrepreneurship will give me the technical skills I need to succeed as a fund manager. I will gain deeper knowledge of financial modeling, investment analysis, and risk management—all critical competencies for someone looking to launch their own fund.

Additionally, the network at Marshall is unparalleled. Being in Los Angeles, the heart of the startup ecosystem, I will have access to entrepreneurs, limited partners, and other investors who can help me build relationships that are essential for fundraising. The EMBA cohort will be composed of accomplished professionals, and I believe these connections will be invaluable as I progress in my career.

I chose USC Marshall specifically because of its strong reputation in entrepreneurship and its location on the West Coast. The program's focus on real-world application through case studies and projects resonates with my learning style. I am also excited about the opportunity to learn from professors and classmates who have experience in venture capital and private equity.

My background as an Egyptian woman in venture capital has taught me the importance of persistence and adaptability. These qualities, combined with the education and network I will gain at Marshall, will position me to achieve my goal of launching my own investment fund within the next five to seven years. I am committed to making a meaningful impact in the venture capital space and helping entrepreneurs bring their ideas to life.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
During my fourth year at TechVenture, a Series B marketing automation startup in San Francisco, I faced a situation that tested my ability to navigate genuinely conflicting interests with no obvious right answer.

We were three months away from launching our European expansion, with significant resources already committed. Our VP of Sales wanted to prioritize enterprise customers in the UK and Germany—high-value deals that would look impressive to our investors. Meanwhile, our Head of Product was advocating for a focus on mid-market companies across Scandinavia, arguing this segment would provide sustainable, repeatable revenue. And then there was my own team, the eight customer success managers I managed, who were exhausted from supporting our rapid US growth and worried about being stretched even thinner.

I wasn't in a formal decision-making role, but I was trusted to give input because I sat between Sales, Product, and Customer Success. So I couldn't just pick a side.

I spent two weeks gathering data—not to delay the decision, but because I genuinely didn't know the answer. I analyzed our churn rates by customer segment, looked at sales cycles for different customer profiles, and ran projections on the operational overhead my team could actually handle without burning out people I cared about. The numbers told an uncomfortable story: the enterprise-focused approach would probably hit quarterly targets faster, but our churn data suggested mid-market customers had lower CAC and stayed longer. The Scandinavian market was less sexy but more aligned with our actual strengths.

What I realized, though, was that the tension between these perspectives wasn't just a math problem. Our VP of Sales had legitimate pressure from the board to show aggressive growth. Our Product leader was right that unsustainable growth would eventually hurt us. And my team's wellbeing wasn't some soft concern—burned-out customer success managers means customers don't get supported, which tanks retention.

I recommended we start with a hybrid approach: pursue a smaller number of high-touch enterprise deals in the UK while building repeatable processes for the mid-market Scandinavian segment. It wasn't what Sales wanted, and it wasn't the scaling approach Product ideally wanted either. But it created breathing room—enough enterprise revenue to satisfy investor appetite while proving our mid-market thesis without destroying my team.

The VP of Sales was initially frustrated, but he adapted his pitch to investors around this narrative of "profitable growth over hockey-stick growth." Product actually liked it because they got real data from an underserved segment. My team got sustainable hiring plans and clearer priorities.

Eighteen months later, Scandinavia became our most profitable market by unit economics. It taught me something important: the stakeholders with the loudest voices often have real constraints I don't immediately see. My job isn't to find the "correct" answer everyone should want, but to understand what's actually driving their position and whether there's a path that honors the legitimate needs underneath.

I'm drawn to SSE's program partly because of this—I want to deepen my thinking about business strategy beyond startup speed. Europe is where I want to build next, and I think I'm ready to understand these markets and these kinds of complex decisions at a deeper level.

school_name: EM Lyon Business School MSc in Management
prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
# Leadership Essay

Three years ago, I was managing a portfolio of four manufacturing companies at Stratton Capital Partners in Mexico City, and I realized I had no idea what I was doing.

This wasn't imposter syndrome—it was a legitimate problem. Our largest investment, a mid-sized auto parts supplier, was hemorrhaging cash. The operations team blamed procurement. Procurement blamed the plant managers. The plant managers blamed everyone. Meanwhile, our investment thesis was deteriorating by 200 basis points per quarter.

What I hadn't done was actually step into the facility and talk to people.

I spent the next month traveling between our three plants in Monterrey, Guadalajara, and Querétaro. I sat with line workers during their shifts, ate lunch in the cafeteria, and asked questions that probably made me sound naive. Why was a component being ordered from Spain when a local supplier could deliver in half the time? Why were we running machines at 60% capacity? Why did talented engineers leave after two years?

The pattern became clear: the previous ownership had treated these plants as cash machines, not living businesses. People had stopped caring because nobody had asked them to care.

When I returned to Mexico City, I didn't present a turnaround deck. Instead, I pushed back on my managing director's suggestion to replace the plant managers. I argued we keep them, but restructure their compensation around operational improvements. I also convinced him that I needed to spend 40% of my time on-site—unconventional for a PE analyst.

I created quarterly forums where plant managers, procurement, and operations sat in a room together without hierarchy. Not touchy-feely stuff; we just forced them to solve problems collectively. We also launched a cost reduction competition between plants with actual incentives. It sounds simple, but it worked because people suddenly had clarity on what mattered and could see the direct connection between their work and the outcome.

Eighteen months later, that company was profitable. We cut procurement costs by 18%, reduced machine downtime by 35%, and cut employee turnover from 28% to 14%. We were eventually able to sell it above our original investment multiple.

What struck me more than the numbers was the shift in how people talked about their work. During my last visit, a maintenance supervisor in Monterrey—a guy named José who'd been threatening to leave when I first met him—told me, "Finally, someone is asking us what we think." That felt more important than anything in the investment committee memo.

This experience fundamentally changed how I approach problems. I learned that smart analysis means nothing if you can't bring people along. That fear of looking stupid in front of your peers is usually the thing preventing real leadership. That the people doing the work usually know the solution—you just have to create the space for them to say it.

I also learned what I still need to develop. I relied heavily on my MD's support, and I wasn't great at managing my own boss's expectations during the messy middle phase. I want to build those executive communication and stakeholder management skills.

An MBA, particularly at an institution that emphasizes leadership development, is how I get there. I'm not trying to become a better analyst. I'm trying to become someone who can lead teams through ambiguity, shape culture, and eventually build value in ways that go beyond spreadsheets. That's what got me excited about this program.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
Why Bilkent University Executive MBA

This diverse background has given me a comprehensive understanding of how businesses operate in the healthcare industry. I am now at a point where I feel ready to formalize my business education and gain the strategic skills necessary to move into executive-level positions.

The medical devices field is dynamic and constantly evolving. New technologies are emerging, regulations are changing, and companies must adapt quickly to remain competitive. I want to be better equipped to navigate these challenges and contribute more meaningfully to my organization. An MBA will provide me with the knowledge and frameworks I need to make better decisions and lead teams more effectively.

I chose Bilkent University because it is a reputable institution with a strong reputation in business education. The Executive MBA program is known for its quality curriculum and experienced faculty. Additionally, the program's location in Istanbul is appealing to me as it provides access to a vibrant business community and a region that is increasingly important for the medical devices industry. The flexible format of the executive program also aligns well with my career, allowing me to continue working while pursuing my degree.

What I will bring to the Bilkent community is my extensive industry experience and my commitment to learning. In my classes, I will contribute practical insights from my work in medical devices and help other students understand real-world business challenges. I am someone who values collaboration and believes that learning happens through dialogue with others. I will actively participate in discussions and group projects, and I will share my perspectives based on what I have seen work—and not work—in my industry.

Beyond the classroom, I am committed to being an engaged member of the program community. I hope to build relationships with my classmates and participate in networking events that Bilkent offers. These connections will be valuable not only during the program but throughout my career.

I am excited about the opportunity to study at Bilkent and confident that this program will help me achieve my career goals. I look forward to contributing my experience and learning from both the faculty and my peers. Thank you for considering my application.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
When I graduated from Universidad de los Andes in 2016, I thought I knew what my career would look like. A structured path in finance, probably at a bank in Bogotá or maybe a multinational in Miami. My parents had done well in traditional business, and I figured I would follow a similar trajectory.

I lasted eight months at Deloitte before I quit.

The catalyst was a project we did pro bono for a microfinance NGO in rural Cundinamarca. I was supposed to do financial modeling for three weeks. Instead, I found myself sitting across from a woman who'd taken a 2 million peso loan to start a small textile business. She showed me pictures of her daughter's school (the one she could finally afford), and suddenly the spreadsheet in front of me meant something completely different.

I left Deloitte and joined that NGO, Fundación Esperanza, as a Program Officer in 2017. Honestly, the first year was humbling. I was making a third of what I'd made at Deloitte, and I was terrible at grant writing. My boss told me bluntly that I wrote like an accountant, not a storyteller. But I was also learning something that my previous job never taught me: how to think across constraints. When you have $400,000 to serve 500 families, every decision has weight.

Over five years at Fundación Esperanza, I moved into a Regional Coordinator role, overseeing three branch offices across the Caribbean coast. We grew our annual budget from $1.2 million to $3.8 million and expanded from serving 500 families to nearly 2,000. I managed a team of twelve and worked on everything from donor relations to program design. I learned how to write a credible grant proposal. I learned how to have hard conversations with board members about sustainability. I also learned that good intentions aren't enough—you need systems, accountability, and sometimes you need to make cuts that hurt.

In 2022, I moved to a Management role at a larger international NGO, CARE International's Colombia office, as Operations Manager. The organization is more established, the budget is bigger (we work with roughly $18 million annually), and the problems are more complex. I've been involved in restructuring our supply chain, implementing new financial controls, and leading a team through a difficult transition when we closed two regional offices.

This progression hasn't been linear, and it's certainly not what I imagined at twenty-two. But each step has clarified what I actually want to do: improve how development organizations function. I'm good at operations and financial management, but I want deeper expertise in organizational strategy. I want to understand how to scale impact without losing it. An MBA, particularly one with a strong focus on nonprofit management and organizational leadership, feels like the right next step to have more influence in this sector.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
# Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.

Three years ago, I inherited a dying product line. Revenue had flatlined at $2.3M annually, churn was running at 18% per quarter, and the team—five engineers and two customer success reps in our Toronto office—was visibly demoralized. My VP told me bluntly: either turn it around in eighteen months or we'd sunset it.

My first instinct was to immediately cut costs and simplify the product. Instead, I did something that felt riskier: I spent my first month just listening. I sat with customers on calls, read every piece of feedback we'd collected, and asked my team what they actually wanted to build. What emerged wasn't a grand strategy—it was something simpler and more human. The product had drifted. We'd stopped talking to customers. We'd stopped believing in what we were making.

I made a decision that broke internal precedent. Rather than headquarter all decisions at our San Francisco office, I gave our Toronto team real autonomy to reshape the product roadmap based on what our customers needed. This meant pushing back against pressure from leadership to hit arbitrary metrics. It meant saying no to revenue-generating features that felt dishonest to what the product should be.

The team responded. One engineer who'd been quietly interviewing elsewhere suddenly started staying late. Our customer success lead began personally onboarding new accounts—something she'd stopped doing years prior. Within six months, churn dropped to 8%. By month eighteen, we'd grown revenue to $5.7M with a healthier margin and a waiting list of customers wanting to implement.

But the number I'm actually proudest of? All seven original team members are still here, and we've hired four more. Two were promoted into leadership roles.

I learned that brave leadership isn't always about bold vision statements. Sometimes it's about trusting your people enough to get out of their way, and being willing to disappoint executives in the short term because you believe in doing right by your customers. Kellogg's emphasis on developing future leaders who can inspire others—not just direct them—resonates deeply with how I approach my work. I'm ready to deepen that philosophy and bring it into whatever industry challenge comes next.

prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
Two years into my VC role at Crescent Capital in Lagos, I led due diligence on what I was certain would be our flagship Series A investment. The company, a B2B logistics platform, had traction in Nigeria and Ghana—exactly the kind of regional play our €50M fund loved. I pushed hard for the investment. My partner was skeptical about the founding team's go-to-market strategy, but I convinced him we could work through it post-investment. We closed a €3.2M round.

Within eight months, the company collapsed. The CEO had been understating customer churn. The go-to-market issues my partner flagged weren't fixable with capital alone. We lost nearly the entire investment.

I was devastated. More than that, I was embarrassed. I'd let my confidence override my rigor. I'd treated a red flag as a minor concern because I wanted the deal to work.

What saved me wasn't moving on quickly—it was sitting with that failure for a while. I asked my partner directly: where did my diligence break down? His answer: I'd fallen in love with the thesis instead of the business. I'd asked affirming questions instead of hard ones. I'd assumed that Lagos's market gap meant this particular team could fill it.

Over the next six months, I rebuilt my investment approach from scratch. I created a checklist of founder red flags—specifically around historical execution and capital efficiency—that I applied to every deal. I started leading smaller checks first, partnerships on larger rounds, building conviction through staged commitments rather than all-in bets. I read every post-mortem publicly available from other firms' failures.

The harder change was psychological. I accepted that my instinct could be wrong. That conviction without evidence is expensive. That the best investors are comfortable saying "I don't know yet."

Since then, I've sourced and led three investments that have returned 4.2x, 2.8x, and currently on track for 5x. But honestly, the learning matters more than the returns. I'm a more thorough investor now. And I know my limitations.

INSEAD-Tsinghua's emphasis on learning through real-world complexity, not just theory, is exactly why I need an MBA at this stage. I'm ready to push beyond VC—to understand how to scale across markets and teams, not just capital allocation. This program's combination of Asian and European perspectives, its case-driven rigor, and its focus on emerging markets leader-building will prepare me to move into operational leadership where I can apply these hard-won lessons.

prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
I am excited about the opportunity to contribute meaningfully to Grenoble Ecole de Management's community. Throughout my nine years in the legal profession, I have developed a deep appreciation for collaborative problem-solving and diverse perspectives, which I believe will enrich both classroom discussions and student life.

In my current role as a legal associate, I work with teams on complex cases that require careful analysis and creative thinking. This experience has taught me the importance of listening to different viewpoints and synthesizing them into workable solutions. I plan to bring this mindset to classroom discussions, where I can offer insights from my legal background while remaining open to learning from my peers.

My work in the legal field has exposed me to various international contexts, which has given me a global perspective. I understand that business operates across borders and cultures, and I am committed to contributing this awareness to class discussions about international business and cross-cultural management. As someone from Japan, I can share perspectives on Asian markets and business practices that might be valuable to my classmates.

I am also a strong believer in the power of teamwork. During my time in law firms, I have collaborated with people from different backgrounds and expertise levels. I see the classroom as an extension of this collaborative environment. I plan to actively participate in group projects and study groups, ensuring that everyone's voice is heard and valued. I believe that learning is most effective when students support each other and work toward common goals.

Outside of formal class time, I am interested in contributing to student life through meaningful engagement. I would be eager to participate in case competitions, student clubs, or any initiatives that bring the community together. My experience working on legal matters has developed strong organizational and project management skills, which I could apply to help organize student events or contribute to club leadership if appropriate.

I also recognize the importance of diversity in creating a rich learning environment. As a non-binary person and as someone from a non-Western background, I bring perspectives that might be underrepresented in many business schools. I am committed to being an ally to other students from underrepresented groups and to fostering an inclusive community where everyone feels welcome to contribute.

In legal practice, I have encountered many situations where understanding human behavior and motivation is crucial. This has made me interested in organizational behavior, leadership, and management theory. I plan to engage thoughtfully with these topics in the classroom, drawing on real-world examples from my legal career to illustrate concepts and challenge assumptions.

I understand that every classroom benefits from students who are willing to prepare thoroughly and engage authentically with the material. I am committed to doing the reading, thinking deeply about case studies, and coming to class ready to discuss. I will not simply offer opinions; instead, I will ground my contributions in evidence and reasoning, skills that are fundamental to legal practice.

Finally, I believe that the best classroom experiences happen when students are generous with their time and knowledge. I plan to be accessible to classmates who might want to discuss course material, study together, or simply get to know each other better. Building a strong community is something I value deeply.

Grenoble Ecole de Management's emphasis on sustainable business and corporate responsibility aligns well with my values. I am particularly interested in how legal and ethical frameworks can drive positive business outcomes, and I look forward to exploring these questions with my classmates.

I am confident that my professional experience, diverse background, and commitment to community engagement will make me a valuable member of Grenoble's student body. I am genuinely excited about the prospect of contributing to your classroom and contributing to an inclusive, intellectually vibrant community.
