school_name: SKEMA Business School MSc in Management
prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# Essay Response

Innovation is about challenging the status quo and finding better ways to do things. In my role at my current company, I had the opportunity to do exactly this.

When I joined the team as a product manager, our sales cycle was incredibly long—around 6 months for enterprise deals. Everyone accepted this as normal for the SaaS industry. I noticed that a lot of time was wasted in meetings between sales and engineering teams because they weren't aligned on customer requirements. I thought there had to be a better way.

I proposed implementing a new process where product, sales, and engineering would meet weekly instead of waiting for formal requirements documents. This was unconventional because traditionally, these departments worked in silos. Management was skeptical at first, but I pushed for a 3-month trial.

The results were significant. We reduced our sales cycle from 6 months to 4 months on average. This meant faster revenue for the company and faster implementation for customers. The teams also reported better communication and fewer misunderstandings. What started as a small experiment became standard practice across the organization.

The key to driving this innovation was not accepting that "this is how we've always done it." I listened to frustrations from all teams and identified the root cause. By bringing people together, we created a culture of collaboration that benefited everyone.

This experience taught me that innovation doesn't always require complex solutions. Sometimes it's about rethinking how we work together. I realized that my strength lies not just in executing tasks, but in questioning processes and pushing for improvements.

Moving forward, I want to deepen my business acumen and learn how to scale these ideas across larger organizations. An MBA will give me the strategic framework to think beyond my current role and contribute more meaningfully to my company's growth. I'm particularly interested in SKEMA because of its focus on global business practices and innovation, which aligns with my desire to become a leader who drives positive change.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
Why University of Bayreuth MSc Management

I am writing to express my strong interest in the MSc Management program at University of Bayreuth. After ten years working in the renewable energy sector, I have come to realize that I need formal business education to advance my career and make a greater impact in this field.

My background is in renewable energy, where I have held various positions in project management and operations. I started my career in the Philippines and have worked on several solar and wind projects. Throughout my career, I have gained practical experience in managing teams, budgets, and timelines. However, I feel that without an MBA or Master's degree, I cannot move into senior leadership positions where I can truly drive change in the renewable energy industry.

I chose University of Bayreuth because it is a well-respected program in Europe and offers high-quality education. The program's focus on management fundamentals appeals to me because I need to strengthen my understanding of finance, strategy, and organizational behavior. I believe that studying at your institution will provide me with the knowledge and skills necessary to become an effective leader in my field. Additionally, Germany is a leader in renewable energy and environmental sustainability, which aligns perfectly with my career goals and passion for making a difference in the world.

My experience in the renewable energy sector will allow me to contribute meaningfully to the student community at Bayreuth. I can share my insights about working in developing markets and managing complex technical projects with limited resources. Many of my classmates will likely come from banking, consulting, or manufacturing backgrounds, so I think my perspective from a growing industry like renewables will be valuable. I am eager to learn from diverse classmates and I believe I can offer them practical knowledge about project execution and operations management.

Throughout my career, I have developed strong interpersonal skills and a collaborative mindset. I work well in teams and enjoy mentoring junior staff members. I am confident that I will be an engaged and active participant in group projects and classroom discussions. My diverse background as a Filipino professional working across Asia gives me a unique cultural perspective that I can bring to classroom conversations about global business and international markets.

I am particularly interested in the program's courses on strategic management and operations, as these directly align with my professional goals. After graduation, I plan to return to Asia and pursue a management position with a multinational renewable energy company or possibly start my own consulting business focused on helping developing countries transition to clean energy. A Master's degree from University of Bayreuth will give me the credential and knowledge base necessary to achieve these ambitions.

In conclusion, I am excited about the opportunity to study at University of Bayreuth and contribute to your vibrant academic community. I am a dedicated professional with substantial industry experience, and I am committed to being a successful student and a positive influence on my peers. Thank you for considering my application.

prompt: Why are you interested in joining Schulich?
content:
When I tell people I'm considering an MBA, the first question is usually "Why now?" Fair question. I'm comfortable in my role at Databricks—good salary, interesting problems, a team I respect. But for the past year, I've felt like I'm executing a playbook someone else wrote, and I need to learn how to write my own.

I joined Databricks five years ago as an Enterprise Account Executive and have spent the last two years leading a team of six selling data analytics solutions to Fortune 500 companies. I've hit quota consistently and helped grow our Toronto office from two people to eighteen. But here's what's been bothering me: I'm genuinely good at selling, but I don't understand the economics beneath what I'm selling. When a prospect asks why our platform costs 40% more than Snowflake's, I can recite the value prop, but I can't walk them through the unit economics or model out their total cost of ownership with confidence. I'm selling the sizzle, and I want to understand the steak.

More fundamentally, I'm realizing that leadership in tech requires a broader aperture than my current toolkit provides. I've built intuition about sales strategy and team dynamics through doing, but I've noticed gaps when decisions move beyond my domain. Last year, we were trying to decide whether to pursue a PLG motion to complement our sales-led model. I advocated hard for it based on feel and competitive pressure, but I couldn't articulate the financial case or market sizing with any rigor. The decision got made without input that would've actually moved the needle because I couldn't speak the language.

An MBA would fix that, but more importantly, Schulich specifically makes sense for my path. I'm not trying to jump industries or reinvent myself—I actually love enterprise software. What I need is a program that will deepen my business acumen without asking me to step back from tech entirely. Schulich's focus on technology and innovation entrepreneurship aligns with where I want to build my career, whether that's moving into a Chief Revenue Officer role, running a P&L at a scaling startup, or eventually starting something myself.

I've looked at programs in both Canada and the US. US schools come with a premium price tag and the expectation that I'll relocate permanently—something my wife and I aren't willing to do right now. More pragmatically, I'm not convinced I need a San Francisco network for where I want to go. But Schulich has something different. You're embedded in the Canadian tech ecosystem in a way that matters. Your alumni are building companies in Toronto, Vancouver, and Montreal—cities I actually want to be in. When I spoke with a few Schulich grads at my firm, they talked about the program with genuine respect, not just credential respect. They mentioned specific courses and alumni connections that actually shaped how they think about business.

The cohort structure also appeals to me. At this stage of my career, learning from classmates with 10+ years of experience across different industries would be more valuable than learning from people fresh out of undergrad. I'm not interested in being the oldest guy in the room, but I also don't want to be in a program designed primarily for career switchers.

I'm not going to pretend I'm a perfect fit—my GMAT score of 696 is solid but not outstanding, and I know I'm bringing a fairly tech-heavy background to a diverse cohort. But I'm also someone who's serious about this. I've mapped out why it matters, I'm planning to stay in Canada, and I'm genuinely trying to close real gaps rather than chase a credential. That's the guy sitting in your classroom.

school_name: University of Bath School of Management (Executive MBA)
prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
When I joined Axiom Capital Partners as an Associate in 2018, I was tasked with identifying operational inefficiencies across our portfolio companies. One of our major holdings, a mid-sized manufacturing firm, was struggling with supply chain delays that were impacting margins by approximately 12-15% annually.

The problem was complex. The company had multiple suppliers across different regions, and there was no centralized system to track inventory or forecast demand. Communication between procurement, production, and logistics teams was siloed, leading to frequent stockouts and overordering. I knew that solving this would require creative thinking and cross-functional collaboration.

I decided to implement a solution that leveraged technology and process improvement. Working with the operations team, I designed an integrated dashboard that consolidated real-time data from all suppliers, internal inventory systems, and production schedules. This involved using existing software platforms more effectively rather than purchasing expensive new systems.

The implementation process required significant change management. I conducted workshops with the procurement, production, and logistics teams to understand their pain points and get their buy-in. I created training sessions to ensure everyone could use the new dashboard effectively. Within three months, we saw a 20% reduction in supply chain lead times and a 35% decrease in excess inventory.

This experience taught me that creative solutions don't always mean inventing something entirely new. Sometimes they mean reimagining how existing tools can work together to solve problems in better ways. By bringing different teams together and creating visibility across silos, we transformed how the company operated.

Since then, I have applied similar principles across our portfolio. At another holding company in the consumer goods space, I identified that the sales and customer service teams were not aligned on customer data. I created a shared customer analytics dashboard that both teams could access and update. This simple intervention improved customer retention by 8% and increased cross-selling opportunities by 15%.

What I appreciate about my role in private equity is that it has given me exposure to multiple industries and business functions. This broad perspective has helped me think creatively about problems. I don't just see challenges; I see opportunities to drive change.

Looking forward, I want to deepen my strategic thinking and business acumen through an MBA. The Executive MBA at Bath would provide me with the academic rigor and peer network to accelerate my growth. I am particularly drawn to your program's emphasis on leadership and innovation, which aligns with my career aspirations to eventually transition into a leadership role focused on portfolio strategy and value creation.

I am confident that the combination of my operational experience and formal business education will position me to make meaningful contributions to the program and drive even greater impact in my career.

school_name: University of Münster School of Business MSc
prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
I'm a numbers person who never thought numbers would limit me, until my GMAT score came back at 694. I stared at that screen in a Cairo internet café, genuinely disappointed in myself. But here's what that moment taught me: I've spent eight years at Deloitte solving problems that don't reduce to a single test, and I'd be doing the admissions team a disservice if I didn't explain why I belong at Münster despite a standardized metric that doesn't capture how I actually think.

I joined Deloitte's Cairo office fresh out of university precisely because I didn't want a linear career. In consulting, you're forced to operate across disciplines—one quarter I'm mapping supply chain inefficiencies for a manufacturing client, the next I'm restructuring a family office's governance. That restlessness isn't a bug; it's shaped how I approach problems. When a Middle Eastern pharmaceutical company came to us three years ago facing regulatory fragmentation across six markets, I led the cross-functional team that synthesized those disparate requirements into a unified compliance framework. We saved them €2.8 million in duplicate processes, but more importantly, it proved that complexity isn't an obstacle—it's the real work.

My undergraduate GPA of 3.3 reflected something I've become clearer about: I never optimize for grades, I optimize for understanding. I'd rather spend four hours designing a real client proposal than memorizing another framework. That approach has served me well in practice—I've been promoted faster than peers with higher credentials—but it means my academic metrics don't tell the whole story.

What draws me to Münster specifically is your program's integration of strategy consulting with European business context. I want to transition from advising on problems to building something myself, likely returning to the MENA region. The MSc gives me two crucial things: legitimacy in German-speaking markets where I'm considering growth opportunities, and time in a cohort that forces me to challenge my consulting orthodoxies. Your faculty research in emerging markets governance is exactly where I want my thinking to evolve.

I'm aware I'm not a statistically perfect candidate. But I've learned that the best decisions—both for companies and for admissions committees—aren't made by reducing people to their most convenient metrics. I'm excited to prove that conviction right at Münster.

prompt: Tell us about a creative solution you implemented to solve a complex problem.
content:
Three years ago, I was managing quality operations for a cardiovascular device manufacturer in Lyon, and we had a critical problem: our sterilization validation process took fourteen weeks. That's fourteen weeks before a single unit could reach a hospital. We were hemorrhaging time, losing contracts, and our competitors were lapping us.

The obvious solution was throwing more bodies at it—hire more inspectors, run parallel batches. But we didn't have the budget, and frankly, more people meant more variation. Instead, I spent a month just observing the process. I watched technicians repeat the same manual checks obsessively. I noticed they'd validate identical test runs multiple times because the previous results "felt" incomplete. It wasn't negligence—it was fear. One failed sterilization batch could kill patients.

So I proposed something unconventional: we'd build a predictive model using our historical data. Not to replace validation, but to pre-screen batches and flag which ones genuinely needed full testing versus which were routine based on raw material lot, humidity, temperature logs, and past outcomes. Our quality director was skeptical. "We can't gamble with patient safety," he said. He was right, of course. So we didn't gamble.

I partnered with our data analyst and a local engineering school to develop the model conservatively. We tested it on six months of archived data. When it showed 87% accuracy at identifying which batches would pass standard validation, we ran it in parallel with our existing process for three months—no shortcuts, just dual-track. The data convinced people.

We reduced validation time to nine weeks. Not revolutionary, but real. More importantly, we reduced it safely and without hiring. The process became more reproducible because technicians stopped making judgment calls based on intuition.

What surprised me was the resistance I encountered from my own team initially. I'd expected pushback from leadership, not from the people doing the work daily. That taught me that process improvements aren't just technical—they're about bringing people along. I had to show them I wasn't replacing their expertise; I was freeing them from tedious, repetitive work so they could focus on actual quality judgment.

That experience is exactly why I want to study at Technion. I want to sharpen my ability to blend technical problem-solving with organizational change. The Executive Program's focus on innovation and operations seems built for people who've done this work and want to do it more strategically.

prompt: Please use this space to share with the Admissions Committee how you have reflected and grown since your previous application and discuss any relevant updates to your candidacy (e.g., changes in your professional life, additional coursework, and extracurricular/volunteer engagements).
content:
Since my first application to Wharton, I have taken significant steps to strengthen my candidacy and clarify my career vision. I now understand where my previous application fell short, and I am committed to addressing these gaps.

Most importantly, I have pursued additional professional development. I completed a Google Analytics certification course and attended several industry conferences in the media sector. These experiences have deepened my understanding of digital transformation in entertainment, which is critical to my career goals. I also volunteered with a local nonprofit that focuses on media literacy in schools, which has given me perspective on the social impact of our industry.

In my current role at a major entertainment company, I have taken on expanded responsibilities. I now lead a small team of three analysts and have become more involved in strategic planning decisions. This has reinforced my belief that business education is essential to my growth as a leader. The work is challenging, and I realize that my technical skills, while solid, need to be complemented by broader business acumen that an MBA would provide.

I also recognize that my GMAT score of 715 was not as competitive as I would have liked. While I considered retaking the exam, I decided instead to focus on demonstrating my commitment through action. The courses and volunteer work represent genuine interest in continuous improvement, not just test preparation.

Looking back at my first application, I see that I was somewhat unfocused about why I specifically wanted an MBA and what I hoped to accomplish. I have spent considerable time since then reflecting on my five-year plan and how Wharton fits into that vision. I am now ready to contribute meaningfully to your program and to maximize the opportunity that an MBA would provide.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
# Five Years Forward

Two years ago, I was sitting in the Deloitte Toronto office on my first day, convinced that audit was my path. I had done everything right: strong undergrad, landed the Big 4 offer, was tracking toward the Partner track everyone kept mentioning. But after eighteen months of rotating through energy sector clients, I realized I was becoming competent at something that didn't actually interest me.

That's when everything shifted. I moved to the advisory side, and suddenly the work felt different. We weren't just auditing; we were diagnosing problems and designing solutions. I spent three months embedded with a mid-market manufacturing firm in Southern Ontario restructuring their supply chain. We cut their logistics costs by $2.3M annually, and I watched the CEO's face change when she understood what that meant for her workforce and margins. That's what I want to do for the next decade—move beyond analysis and become someone who actually shapes business outcomes.

In five years, I see myself leading operations or strategy for a mid-sized company, ideally in the Mediterranean region. I'm drawn to the region for personal reasons—my mother's family is from Cyprus, and I've spent summers there since I was eight—but also because I see genuine opportunities. European small-to-medium enterprises are caught between the efficiency demands of larger competitors and the legacy constraints of traditional management. They need people who understand both the data and the people side. They need bridge-builders.

I don't expect to be a CEO in five years. That's not the goal. I want to be the person who sits between the board and the operations floor, who can read a balance sheet and understand why morale dropped in the warehouse. I want to lead teams that are 20-40 people, not 200. And frankly, I want to actually know my team members' names and what matters to them.

The University of Cyprus program is the right vehicle for this, for several concrete reasons. First, being in Nicosia puts me physically close to the business environment I want to understand. I'm not studying Europe from Toronto. I'm embedded in it. The two-year structure also matters—I'm ready to step out of Big 4 consulting for a sustained period. Two years isn't long enough to lose my technical skills, but it's enough to develop the strategic perspective I'm missing.

What genuinely appeals to me is the program's emphasis on family business and Mediterranean enterprise. That's not abstract theory for me. My uncle runs a shipping logistics firm in Limassol, and I've watched him navigate some of the exact challenges the program highlights—competing against larger European firms, managing generational leadership transitions, staying profitable in a fragmented market. Being able to study those dynamics rigorously, with faculty who understand the regional context, changes the entire value proposition of the degree.

I also appreciate that this isn't a top-10 global MBA program that costs $200K and positions everyone for consulting or finance. That's not what I want. I want a program that takes my analytical foundation and reorients it toward building something real—whether that's restructuring operations at a real company or eventually starting something. The caliber of classmate I'll find here—people with regional business experience and ambitions grounded in reality—appeals to me more than a classroom full of people all optimizing for the same three exit options.

In five years, I want to email the admissions director and tell her I'm now the Chief Operations Officer at a 150-person manufacturing firm in Limassol, and that the frameworks from the International Management course genuinely shaped how I approached the role. That's the version of this story I'm betting on.

That's what I want to build toward. University of Cyprus is where that becomes possible.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
In 2019, I was leading the M&A advisory team at Rothschild & Cie when we landed what seemed like a straightforward engagement: a €450 million acquisition of a mid-market logistics company by a German industrial group. Straightforward, it turned out, was naive.

Three weeks into the process, everything fractured. The German buyer wanted to move aggressively—they saw market consolidation happening and didn't want to lose the asset. The French target company, family-owned for sixty years, had a board that included the founder's three adult children who disagreed fundamentally on the deal structure. One wanted to exit entirely; another wanted to stay on as operational partner; the third was essentially checked out and worried only about tax optimization. Meanwhile, the German group's CFO was threatening to walk if we couldn't lock in a February close, which was physically impossible given French employment law requirements around change-of-control consultation periods.

I was thirty-four, relatively junior to handle something this complicated, and my senior partner was traveling for six weeks. I had to choose whose interests I prioritized—but there was no clean answer.

What I didn't do was hide behind process. I sat down separately with each stakeholder and actually listened to what they feared, not just what they stated in meetings. The German buyer wasn't just chasing speed; they were genuinely worried about market window closing and competitive pressure. The family wasn't just being sentimental; they had legitimate anxiety about whether the buyer would preserve their legacy brand. The CFO's ultimatum masked real execution risk on their end.

I proposed something unconventional: a three-tranche close structure that gave us a February close on core assets (satisfying the German timeline), but staggered earn-out payments tied to the subsidiary's performance over two years. This created a real incentive for the incoming operational partner (the middle sibling) to drive integration successfully rather than extract value. I built in governance mechanisms that preserved brand autonomy while consolidating supply chain. It was messier than either buyer or seller initially wanted, but it was honest about competing realities.

The pushback was immediate. The German CFO said the earn-out structure was "too French" and created liability. The founder's eldest child felt I was compromising on valuation by not pushing pure cash upfront. My own partner, when he returned, questioned whether I was abusing my position to be conciliatory rather than aggressive.

Here's what I learned: I wasn't skilled at that moment. I was uncomfortable with conflict. I wanted everyone to feel heard because I didn't want anyone to be angry with me. That's not the same thing as actually balancing interests—it's just people-pleasing dressed up in professional language.

But I also realized something important: the deal closed in March, not February, and it held. Two years later, the subsidiary exceeded its earn-out targets by 18%. The family maintained the brand identity they cared about. The German buyer achieved the consolidation they needed. And I learned that sometimes the harder job isn't finding the elegant solution—it's sitting with the discomfort of disappointing people in the short term to serve the actual long-term structure.

I'm applying to ESSEC because that's exactly where I want to sharpen this skill. Your curriculum's focus on stakeholder governance and your emphasis on practical, cross-cultural problem-solving speaks to where I've been struggling. Investment banking taught me technical skills, but it didn't teach me the systems thinking I need to handle truly ambiguous situations. I want to study alongside people who see business as something more than transactions—people who understand that real leadership is about holding space for competing goods, not just managing competing bads.

prompt: How, considering your career progress to date, does an EMBA fit in with your future career goals? Please describe.
content:
Over the last four years in venture capital, I have learned that success requires continuous growth and deep expertise in both technology and business fundamentals. An EMBA from Haskayne would be the natural next step in my career trajectory.

In my current role as an investment associate, I evaluate early-stage companies across multiple sectors. This work has shown me the importance of strong business acumen and strategic thinking. An EMBA would deepen my understanding of financial analysis, operations, and leadership—skills essential for advancing to a principal or partner level within five years.

The venture capital industry is competitive, and most senior professionals have advanced degrees. I believe an executive MBA would strengthen my credentials and demonstrate my commitment to the field. Additionally, the program would help me develop a more sophisticated understanding of how to scale companies and create value across portfolios.

Beyond technical skills, I see the EMBA as an opportunity to build a strong network. The diverse cohort at Haskayne would expose me to different perspectives and business practices that will be invaluable throughout my career. Meeting other professionals from various industries would broaden my understanding of business problems and potential solutions.

Finally, as someone who has always believed in the importance of continuous learning, pursuing an EMBA aligns with my personal values. It represents a commitment to self-improvement and professional development that I think is critical for long-term success in venture capital.

I am confident that Haskayne's program would provide the tools, knowledge, and network necessary to advance my career and become a more effective investor and leader in the technology ecosystem.

prompt: Tell us about a time you had to balance competing interests of different stakeholders.
content:
During my time at a major tech company, I encountered a complex situation where I needed to balance the interests of multiple stakeholders. This experience taught me valuable lessons about leadership and compromise.

I was working in the product team and was tasked with managing a project that involved significant changes to our platform. The engineering team wanted to implement new features that would require three months of development time. The sales team, however, was pressuring us to deliver results quickly to meet quarterly targets. Meanwhile, the finance department was concerned about budget constraints and wanted us to minimize costs.

This was a challenging situation. I had to think carefully about how to satisfy everyone. I organized several meetings with representatives from each department to understand their concerns and priorities. Through these discussions, I realized that the key was finding common ground.

I proposed a phased approach to the project. We would deliver a minimum viable product in the first month that would allow sales to start closing deals and generating revenue. Then, we would add more features in subsequent phases based on customer feedback and market demands. This way, the engineering team could work at a sustainable pace, sales could meet their targets, and we could manage the budget more effectively.

The solution worked well. Sales was happy because they had something to show customers earlier than expected. Engineering appreciated the more realistic timeline. Finance was satisfied with the budget management. The project ultimately succeeded and we were able to deliver value to the company.

This experience showed me the importance of stakeholder management in a business setting. I learned that successful outcomes often require compromise and finding creative solutions that address everyone's core needs. I believe this skill will be valuable in an MBA program where collaboration and teamwork are essential.

Looking forward, I want to develop my strategic thinking and business acumen further. ESSEC's diverse student body and focus on practical business skills align well with my goals. I am excited about the opportunity to learn from professors and peers who have different perspectives and experiences. This will help me become a more well-rounded business leader.

prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
# Professional Setback Essay

During my time at my tech company, I faced a significant challenge that taught me valuable lessons about resilience and teamwork. I was working as an Account Executive in the enterprise SaaS space, and I had been consistently performing well in my role. However, in my third year, I experienced a major setback that shook my confidence.

I had been working on a very large deal with a Fortune 500 company for several months. This was the biggest opportunity in my career at that point, and I was excited about closing it. I had invested significant time and effort into building the relationship with the client, understanding their needs, and customizing our solution for them. The deal was worth around $500K annually, which would have been a huge win for our team and for me personally.

Unfortunately, the deal fell through at the last moment. The client decided to go with a competitor who offered a slightly lower price point. I was devastated. I felt like I had failed, and I questioned my abilities as a sales professional. I wondered if I was cut out for this career path.

After taking some time to process the disappointment, I decided to learn from this experience. I scheduled a meeting with my manager to discuss what went wrong. We analyzed the situation carefully, and I realized that I had focused too much on the product features and not enough on the client's true business outcomes and ROI. I also learned that I should have been more proactive in addressing their budget concerns earlier in the process.

Following this experience, I made significant changes to my approach. I started spending more time understanding each client's business strategy and financial constraints from the beginning of the sales process. I also began working more closely with our finance team to create better ROI models for prospects.

Over the next year, my performance improved dramatically. I closed several large deals and became one of the top performers in my region. More importantly, I learned that setbacks are not failures—they are opportunities for growth.

This experience has made me more resilient and taught me the importance of continuous learning and improvement. I believe these qualities will serve me well in my MBA journey and beyond.

school_name: Henley Business School Reading MSc Management
prompt: As we review your application, what more would you like us to know?
content:
When I joined Volkov & Partners as a junior associate in Moscow seven years ago, I thought I'd found my place. The firm was prestigious, the work was intellectually demanding, and I was good at it. But by year five, I realized I was solving the same problems for different clients, and the problems themselves weren't changing much. I was competent but static.

That realization led me to do something unusual for a Russian law firm: I proposed creating a legal operations department. Not a practice group. Not a new service line. Operations. I spent three months researching how other firms managed this, sat through some uncomfortable conversations with partners who saw it as administrative overhead, and eventually built the case using data—specifically, metrics around partner time utilization, client budgeting accuracy, and associate retention.

They gave me six months and a budget for two hires.

By year six, I'd restructured how our firm tracked billable hours, standardized client intake processes, and implemented a knowledge management system. It wasn't flashy work, but it cut our unbilled time by 18% and gave associates actual visibility into career progression. I also discovered I didn't love being in that role, and I think that's important to say: I'm not here because I found my true passion in legal operations. I'm here because that project showed me something about myself I couldn't ignore.

I'm effective when I can think about systems—how things connect, where they break, what's possible if you change how the pieces interact. But I was operating within a single function at a single firm, solving problems incrementally. The legal industry moves slowly, which has value, but I wanted to work somewhere with different constraints, different possibilities.

I'm also someone who's spent my entire career in hierarchical, traditional environments. As someone who is non-binary, navigating Russian corporate culture has required a kind of constant translation—finding the space between who I am and what the context allows. It's given me perspective on organizational culture that I wouldn't have otherwise. But I've started to wonder what I could accomplish in environments that didn't require that translation, where I could show up as myself while still being taken seriously.

That's pulled me toward Henley. Reading's MSc Management isn't a lateral move into another degree; it's a deliberate step toward leadership roles where I can apply what I've learned about operations and systems thinking to larger strategic questions. I've looked at programs in Moscow and Western Europe, and Henley appeals to me specifically because it's pragmatic. The curriculum prioritizes real business problems over theory for its own sake, and the cohort model—studying alongside people from completely different industries and backgrounds—is something I can't replicate in Russia.

I also can't pretend my background doesn't matter here. Russia is changing, and I'm not sure what that means for my future there. I'm considering where I want to build that future, and a degree from a respected UK institution opens possibilities I need to explore seriously.

I'm ready to stop being competent within boundaries and start thinking bigger about what I'm capable of. Henley feels like the right place to do that.

prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
# Achievement and Why It Matters

I am most proud of the time when I led a team to complete a major audit project ahead of schedule at my current company, Deloitte Madrid. This achievement was meaningful to me because it demonstrated my capacity to manage people and deliver results under pressure.

When I started at Deloitte three years ago, I was assigned to work on various audit engagements. Over time, I was given more responsibility. Last year, a senior manager asked me to lead a team of four junior auditors on a financial audit for a mid-sized manufacturing company. The project was scheduled for eight weeks and involved reviewing compliance with international accounting standards across multiple business units.

I decided early on that I wanted to make this project run smoothly. I created a detailed timeline and assigned tasks to my team members based on their skill levels. I also made sure to communicate regularly with the client and with my manager to ensure everyone was informed about progress. I implemented weekly meetings where the team could discuss any challenges and I could provide guidance.

Throughout the project, we faced some difficulties. There were some areas where the client's records were incomplete, which made the audit more difficult. However, my team worked hard and we managed to find solutions. I also worked late many nights to review the work and ensure quality was high.

In the end, we completed the project in six weeks instead of eight. The client was very satisfied with our work and gave us positive feedback. My manager praised me for the strong leadership and told me that my performance was exceptional.

This achievement matters to me because it shows several important things. First, it shows that I can lead a team effectively and motivate people to do their best work. Second, it demonstrates my technical abilities in accounting and auditing. Third, it shows that I am committed to delivering quality work and going above and beyond expectations.

For my career, this experience was very important. It made me realize that I enjoy managing people and working on complex projects. It also gave me confidence that I can handle greater responsibility in the future. This is one reason why I want to pursue an MBA—to develop my leadership skills further and to prepare myself for senior management roles.

In conclusion, I am proud of leading this audit team successfully and delivering exceptional results. This achievement represents my dedication to excellence and my potential to grow as a leader in the accounting and finance field.

prompt: How will you enhance the MSx community?
content:
# How will you enhance the MSx community?

When I joined Helios as a Sales Development Rep in Nairobi in 2019, I was 22 and had no idea what enterprise software actually did. I knew we sold cloud infrastructure solutions to mid-market companies across Africa, but the technical details blurred together. What I did know was that I was one of maybe three people in our Lagos and Nairobi offices who could code-switch between Sheng, English, and technical jargon without breaking a sweat—and that turned out to matter more than I expected.

Over five years, I've moved into a customer success role managing a $2.3M portfolio of accounts, and I've learned that success in SaaS isn't just about the product or the process. It's about understanding what your customer actually needs versus what they think they need, and having the credibility to be honest about gaps.

I want to bring this perspective to Stanford's MSx program specifically because of how you've structured the cohort model. The compressed format means you're building a tight community fast, and I've noticed—from talking to current students and alumni—that the diversity of backgrounds matters intensely. When everyone arrives with the same consulting exit story or finance background, the conversations stay safe. You're already doing this well, but I think my path could push that further.

Here's what I mean: I didn't come through the traditional MBA pipeline. I'm Kenyan, non-binary, from a startup that's still bootstrapped and probably always will be. Most of my colleagues at Helios are engineers or founders; most of my friends didn't go to Stanford or MIT. When we discuss go-to-market strategy in class, I'm thinking about how you actually launch in markets where credit card penetration is 40% and payment infrastructure doesn't exist yet. When we talk about organizational design, I'm thinking about teams where one person is simultaneously an engineer, a salesperson, and on the board. These aren't edge cases for me—they're my baseline.

I also know what it's like to feel like an outsider in spaces designed for people who look nothing like you. I've been the only non-binary person in rooms of 50 people; I've been the only person of color in enough conference calls to stop counting. I want to make sure that when MSx admits someone five years from now who grew up in Dar es Salaam or São Paulo or somewhere in rural Punjab, they don't have to question whether they belong the way I sometimes do. I want to actively create space for people's full selves to show up.

Practically, I'd love to help build out the international/emerging markets angle on some of the class projects. I've also thought about starting an informal peer mentoring group for underrepresented founders and operators—not another formal club, but just people who get it gathering regularly to talk through recruitment, fundraising, whatever.

I know MSx attracts people who've already done impressive things, and that's part of what makes it special. But I think there's also real value in people who've built things that don't show up in TechCrunch, who've solved problems in ways that traditional MBA curricula might not emphasize. I want to contribute that view, and I want to keep learning from people who see the world completely differently than I do.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
I am proud of launching my first e-commerce business called TechHub, which sells mobile phone accessories online. This accomplishment is significant because it shows I have what it takes to be an entrepreneur and create value in the market.

Starting TechHub in Shanghai in 2021 was challenging. I invested my own savings of 50,000 RMB and worked with two friends to build the business from scratch. We sold phone cases, screen protectors, and chargers primarily through Taobao and WeChat. In the first year, we generated approximately 800,000 RMB in revenue and hired four employees.

What makes this accomplishment matter to me is that it proved I could take initiative and execute a business idea. Before TechHub, I worked as a junior sales manager at a telecommunications company for two years. The job was stable but I felt I was not growing or learning new skills. I realized that if I wanted to develop myself further, I needed to take risks and try something new. Starting a business was the way to do that.

The hardest part of running TechHub was managing cash flow and dealing with inventory problems. In our second year, we purchased too much stock and faced serious cash shortages. I had to negotiate with suppliers to extend payment terms and reduce our marketing spending significantly. This taught me valuable lessons about financial planning and the importance of forecasting demand accurately. Without this experience, I would not understand the real challenges of business operations.

Through TechHub, I also learned the importance of teamwork. I could not have succeeded without my co-founders and our small team. We had disagreements about strategy and product direction, but we learned to communicate better and make decisions together. This experience has made me a better leader.

I believe my accomplishment with TechHub demonstrates my entrepreneurial mindset and my ability to overcome obstacles. I am proud of what we built and the revenue we generated, even though we faced setbacks along the way.

This is why I want to pursue a Master in Management at Lagos Business School. The program will give me the business fundamentals and strategic thinking skills I need to scale TechHub or start new ventures. I want to learn from experienced professors and classmates, and I believe LBS is the best place to develop my potential as an entrepreneur and business leader.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
When I think about leadership, I immediately think about the time I led a team project at the Department of Defence. This experience showed me what it really means to be a brave leader and how important it is to inspire others.

I joined the DoD three years ago as a junior analyst in the strategic planning unit. My role was to support senior officials with research and analysis on defence policy matters. About two years into my position, I was given the opportunity to lead a small team on an important initiative. This was significant because I had never led a team before, and I knew this would be a defining moment in my career.

The project involved coordinating between different departments to streamline our intelligence gathering processes. There were about eight people on the team from various backgrounds and departments. Some had been working at the DoD longer than I had, so I knew I would need to establish credibility quickly while also being respectful of their experience.

I started by having individual meetings with each team member to understand their perspective on the current processes and what they thought could be improved. This helped me understand the landscape and build relationships. I then organized a kickoff meeting where I laid out the goals clearly and explained why this work mattered to the broader mission of the department. I emphasized that we were all in this together and that I valued their input.

Throughout the project, I made sure to communicate regularly with the team. We had weekly meetings where people could raise concerns and suggest improvements. I tried to create a safe environment where people felt comfortable speaking up. I also made it a point to acknowledge people's contributions publicly, which I think helped build morale.

There were definitely challenges along the way. At one point, two team members disagreed strongly about how to approach one aspect of the project. Rather than just making a decision myself, I facilitated a discussion where both sides could present their reasoning. Ultimately, we found a compromise that incorporated elements from both viewpoints. I think this showed the team that I valued diverse perspectives.

After about four months, we completed the project and presented our recommendations to senior leadership. The feedback was positive, and our recommendations were implemented. More importantly, the team told me they appreciated the collaborative approach and that they felt heard throughout the process.

This experience taught me that brave leadership is about more than just making tough decisions. It's about creating an environment where people feel valued and motivated to do their best work. It's about being willing to listen and to admit when you don't have all the answers. It's about inspiring people to work together toward a common goal, even when they might have different opinions.

I know that leadership is a journey, and this was just the beginning for me. As I continue my career, I want to develop these skills further. An MBA from Kellogg would help me understand leadership on a deeper level and give me frameworks to lead more effectively. I'm excited about the prospect of joining a community of leaders who are committed to making a positive impact in the world.

school_name: University of Auckland Business School MiM
prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
Five years from now, I see myself running a regional commercial function for a major FMCG player, likely based here in Asia-Pacific. Not the glamorous "dream job" everyone talks about, but something more specific: I want to be the person who bridges strategy and execution—the one who can walk into a market where we're underperforming, understand why, and actually fix it with a team I've built and trained myself.

I've spent twelve years in CPG, and I've learned that knowing *what* to do and knowing *how to get it done* are completely different skills. I started as a brand manager at Nestlé in Italy, moved through sales leadership at Unilever across three countries, and most recently spent five years at a regional distributor in Southeast Asia. In that last role, I rebuilt our commercial team in Vietnam and grew our market share from 8% to 14% in three years. That was fulfilling—but it also showed me how much I don't know.

Here's the honest part: I'm not someone with a grand vision to "transform the industry." I'm not starting a DTC brand or pivoting into sustainability full-time. Those are valid paths, but they're not mine. What I actually want is to get better at the fundamentals—deeper commercial strategy, real financial acumen, building high-performing organizations—and then apply those skills in emerging markets where there's genuine room to grow. The CPG space is consolidating everywhere. The real opportunities are here in Asia.

I chose Auckland's MiM program specifically because I'm at an unusual inflection point. Most twelve-year career people do an MBA after ten years to stay relevant or climb the last rung. I'm doing it because I want to move laterally—from operations/sales into strategy and general management. That's a bigger shift than my career trajectory suggests, and it requires structured learning I can't get from internal leadership programs or self-study.

What attracted me to Auckland was actually the combination of geographic positioning and program design. I've worked across Italy, UK, Belgium, Vietnam, and Thailand. New Zealand and Sydney are logical next steps for regional FMCG roles—the market is growing, labor costs are manageable, and it's become a real test market for Asian distribution strategies. Being based here, I'll have genuine access to those opportunities while studying.

But more importantly, your program has something specific: the Asia-Pacific focus isn't just marketing—it's embedded. The exchange options with Singapore and Melbourne, the case-based learning in emerging markets, the strong recruitment from regional companies—that's not window dressing. When I looked at the electives, I noticed you require strategy alongside finance, and that operations-finance integration matters for someone like me. I also noticed your teaching method leans heavily on group projects and real consulting engagements. That's intentional for me. I've managed teams before, but I've never had to *study* how to do it rigorously, and I've definitely never had to present strategic analysis to people smarter than me on it.

After the program, I'm realistic about the timeline. Year one, I'll probably take a senior commercial manager role—maybe leading Asia sales for a mid-sized brand, or a regional business analyst position if I want to test the strategy track more. By year three, I should be at general manager or director level for a market or category. Year five, I want to be running a P&L, probably $40-60 million revenue, with a real team structure I've built. That's not huge, but it's real, and it's where I can actually drive change.

I know what I want to improve about myself: my financial modeling, my ability to influence without authority, my understanding of supply chain economics. I know where I want to go: building commercial excellence in Asian markets, on my own terms. And I know why Auckland fits: it's positioned exactly where I need to be, teaches exactly what I need to learn, and sits in a region where the rest will follow naturally.

prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
I believe that diversity of perspective is crucial to the learning experience, and I am committed to bringing my unique background to enrich classroom discussions at Grenoble.

Throughout my career in the tech startup space, I have learned that innovation thrives when people from different cultures and experiences come together. As a Japanese woman working in a male-dominated industry, I have always felt responsible for sharing perspectives that might otherwise be overlooked. I plan to do the same at Grenoble by actively participating in class discussions and contributing my viewpoint on global business challenges.

In my current role as a product manager, I work with teams across different regions and bring cultural sensitivity to product development decisions. This experience has taught me that understanding different markets and consumer behaviors is essential for success. I want to apply this to my MBA studies by engaging thoughtfully in case discussions and group projects, particularly those involving international expansion or emerging markets.

I am also passionate about supporting my peers and building community. I believe that student life is not just about what happens in the classroom, but also about the relationships we build with one another. I would like to participate in student clubs and social activities to get to know my classmates better and create a supportive environment for everyone. In my experience, the best teams are built when people trust each other and feel comfortable sharing ideas.

Additionally, I recognize that I may have gaps in certain areas—particularly in finance and accounting—and I plan to ask questions and learn from my classmates who have backgrounds in these fields. I believe that admitting what you don't know is just as important as sharing what you do know. This humble approach to learning will help me contribute meaningfully to discussions where others have more expertise.

Finally, I am excited about the opportunity to learn from diverse classmates and faculty at Grenoble. The school's international reputation and focus on global business practices align with my professional goals. I am committed to being an active participant in the MBA community and to making the most of this transformative experience.

I believe that my combination of startup experience, cultural background, and genuine interest in learning from others will allow me to be a valuable member of the Grenoble community. I am ready to contribute not just academically, but also to the vibrant student life that makes an MBA program truly special.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
What matters most to me is the ability to see potential where others see only constraints.

I realized this about myself three years ago while sitting in a conference room in Mumbai, staring at a spreadsheet that represented the worst investment decision our firm had made in a decade. We'd backed a mid-sized manufacturing company, poured $47 million into operational improvements, hired consultants, installed new systems—and watched margins contract anyway. The CEO was convinced the business was unsalvageable. My colleagues were already running the numbers on exit scenarios. I couldn't shake the feeling that we were looking at this wrong.

So I did something unconventional. I spent six weeks on the plant floor in Gujarat, not in the corporate office. I worked the morning shift, watched the production line, talked to machine operators and supervisors nobody had bothered to interview. What I found wasn't a broken business model—it was broken incentives. The workers had no visibility into how their decisions affected costs. The production team and quality team operated like separate companies. A supervisor had invented a workaround on a critical machine three years ago, but nobody had documented it, so every new operator had to rediscover it.

I came back and convinced the leadership team to try something different: instead of more top-down consulting, we'd run rapid experiments with the people actually doing the work. We gave the floor teams transparency into costs and helped them redesign their workflows. We standardized the undocumented workarounds. Within eight months, we'd improved margins by 340 basis points. That company is now one of our strongest assets, and I learned something that changed how I approach everything: constraints are often information poorly distributed.

This appetite for unlocking potential has become central to how I work. In my current role, I lead the post-acquisition integration team across a portfolio of eight companies. It's technically a finance function, but what I actually do is help leadership teams see their business differently. I've pushed back against two proposed exits simply because I believed we hadn't yet found the right configuration of the business—and in both cases, we found operational levers that transformed value creation. I mentor younger analysts specifically on this: learning to ask "what are we missing?" instead of accepting the first narrative.

But here's what troubles me, and why I'm applying to Insper now: I've been operating largely within the private equity framework, which means my canvas is limited. I can unlock potential within a company or across a portfolio, but I'm constrained by the PE playbook—which is ultimately about extraction within a defined timeframe. I'm hungry to work in environments where potential creation isn't just about operational efficiency or financial engineering. I want to understand how to build businesses, not just optimize them. How to think about scalability, market creation, technology integration, and talent development in ways that go beyond the PE toolkit.

I'm drawn to Insper specifically because of your focus on entrepreneurship and innovation alongside rigorous business fundamentals. Brazil's entrepreneurial ecosystem is exploding—there's genuine business creation happening, not just consolidation and optimization. I want to place myself in that energy. I want to learn from founders and operators who are building from scratch, not from PE professionals refining existing models. The program's emphasis on both quantitative rigor and creative problem-solving aligns with how I already think, but it will expand what I'm capable of building.

What matters most to me is growth—the growth of businesses, yes, but more fundamentally, the growth of my own capacity to create value in ways I haven't yet imagined. That requires stepping outside what I know works and into spaces where I'll need to think differently.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
When I started at Lakewood Industries in 2011, I had no idea I'd spend the next dozen years untangling the mess my family had built and rebuilding it into something I could actually be proud of. That's the career arc I'm living—not the polished "founded a startup at 22" narrative, but something messier and more real.

I joined the company as an Operations Coordinator, fresh out of undergrad in Vancouver, mostly because my parents needed someone who could use spreadsheets without breaking them. The business was doing fine on the surface—$8 million in annual revenue, 35 employees scattered across two facilities—but there were problems nobody wanted to acknowledge. Inventory was chaos. Decision-making happened in kitchen conversations rather than actual meetings. The family assumed I'd follow the traditional path: get some experience, wait my turn, inherit the title.

I decided early on that wasn't enough.

My first three years were about learning the actual business rather than learning family politics. I moved between departments deliberately—supply chain, sales, quality control—mapping out where things were broken. By 2014, I had enough credibility to suggest something radical: we needed systems. Real ones. I led a project to implement new inventory management software, which cut our carrying costs by 23% in the first year and opened up the capital we needed to actually grow. It wasn't glamorous. I spent six months hand-entering 15 years of data. But it worked.

The bigger tension came around 2017. My parents wanted to grow the business, but they wanted to do it the way they always had—conservatively, by feel, without any real strategic planning. I was 28, increasingly frustrated, and convinced that growing 5% annually while sitting on untapped market opportunities was inexcusable. We fought about it for two years. Real fights. The kind where family dinners are tense and nobody wins.

What changed was the transition to a formal management structure. In 2019, my parents finally agreed to bring in external advisors and create actual job descriptions. I was promoted to Operations Director and later VP of Business Development. That role revealed something important: I'm better at building and systematizing than I am at making peace. For someone in a family business, that's complicated information to have.

Over the last five years, I've restructured our supply chain, opened a third facility in Toronto, grown revenue to $22 million, and brought in two external board members. But I've also had to sit with the reality that I can't do this forever exactly as it is. I'm not cut out to be the traditional family business successor, managing legacy relationships and keeping everyone comfortable. I'm better when I'm challenging assumptions and building something new.

That's where the MBA comes in. I need to understand business at a strategic level beyond what I've learned through trial and error. I need to learn whether my instincts about growth, systems, and organizational design are actually sound, or whether I've just been lucky. And honestly, I need to figure out what comes next—whether that's taking Lakewood to the next level, or building something else entirely.

RMIT's MBA appeals to me because of its focus on practical business application and its global network. I'm not looking for theory divorced from reality. I need rigor, but grounded in the kind of complexity I'm actually facing.

prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
When I was twelve, my father sat down with me and my two brothers at our kitchen table in Bogotá and told us he was starting a business. He'd been working in corporate finance for fifteen years, and he was terrified. I remember him saying, "I don't know if this will work, but I know I have to try." That conversation stayed with me.

Five years later, I started my first company with a friend from university. We built software for supply chain optimization in the agricultural sector—nothing revolutionary, but we were solving a real problem for farmers in Colombia and Peru. We got to about $400K in annual revenue before we realized we didn't have the capital or expertise to scale properly. We sold it to a larger logistics company in 2022.

That failure, if I can call it that, taught me something important about myself: I don't need to succeed at everything, but I do need to understand why things happen the way they do. After we sold the company, instead of immediately jumping into another startup, I spent six months working in operations at a consumer goods company. I wanted to see how larger organizations moved, how they made decisions differently than we did with six people in a cramped office.

What's not obvious in my resume is that I'm genuinely interested in understanding the "why" behind business decisions, not just the "what." My GMAT score reflects this—I scored well on the analytical sections but lower on the verbal component, which frustrated me because I can articulate complex ideas in Spanish more easily than in English. English is my second language, and sometimes the nuance I want to express doesn't make it to the page. I'm working on that.

I'm applying to Smith's Executive MBA because I've hit a point where I need structure and rigor that a startup environment doesn't provide. I've learned to move fast and make decisions with incomplete information—that's been invaluable. But I'm aware of my blindspots. I don't have a deep background in financial modeling. I've never led a team larger than eight people. I haven't worked in a corporate environment long enough to understand how to navigate politics, budgets, and organizational change at scale.

What I do have is genuine curiosity and a track record of learning quickly. I'm bilingual in business—comfortable in English and Spanish. I understand the Latin American market from personal experience. And I'm hungry to understand how to build something that lasts, not just something that exits.

My father's business is still running today, nearly twenty years later. That matters to me more than I expected it would.

prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
Three years into my role as operations coordinator at Rafha Industrial Products in Riyadh, I watched our manufacturing floor operate the same way it had for decades. We produced textile machinery components—solid products, reliable processes, zero innovation. The factory ran on shift schedules established in the 1990s, with quality checks happening only at the end of production lines. No one questioned it. This was how things worked.

Then I noticed something: our defect rate was running at 3.2%, which seemed acceptable to everyone. Except to me, it meant roughly 950 faulty components monthly, and the cost of warranty replacements was eating 2% of our operating margin. When I suggested implementing mid-line quality inspections, I got polite pushback. Our production manager said it would "slow everything down" and "complicate an already-tight schedule." The engineering director mentioned that this approach worked fine for their competitors.

I decided to test it anyway.

I pulled data from six months of production records and discovered something interesting: 67% of defects originated from three specific stations where operators were working 10-hour shifts without breaks. The conventional thinking was that breaks interrupted workflow. I proposed something different: shorter, staggered breaks every 90 minutes plus basic quality checkpoints at those three stations. I calculated that the setup would cost roughly 180,000 SAR in equipment and training, but even a 1% reduction in defects would save us 420,000 SAR annually.

My manager approved a one-month pilot on the graveyard shift—partly because it was the lowest-risk option, partly because they wanted to prove me wrong.

What happened surprised us both. Defect rates dropped to 1.8% during that month. Operator fatigue dropped too—measurable through quality metrics and informal feedback. Production speed actually didn't suffer; if anything, it improved slightly because we were catching errors before they cascaded into larger problems. There was less rework downstream.

Rolling it out company-wide required getting skeptics on board. I prepared detailed comparisons, showed the data, and didn't oversell it. I acknowledged the upfront costs and admitted I couldn't predict every outcome. But I could show numbers, and numbers speak louder than "this is how we've always done it."

We implemented the full program over six months. By the time I was promoted to supervisor last year, our defect rate had stabilized at 1.5%, and we'd recovered the initial investment plus 340,000 SAR in net savings.

What I learned wasn't just about operations efficiency. It was that questioning established practices requires evidence, not just intuition. It requires humility about what you don't know and courage to test ideas even when experienced people doubt them. An MBA program like Bilkent's appeals to me precisely because it teaches you to think systematically about these kinds of challenges—how to challenge convention responsibly, grounded in data and respect for organizational context. That's the kind of leader I want to become.

prompt: Imagine yourself at the completion of your MBA journey. Why was pursuing your MBA at Texas McCombs the right decision for your personal and professional growth? Reflect on how you made the most of your time in the program—academically, through hands-on learning opportunities, and within the McCombs community. Highlight how you contributed to the McCombs community and used its resources to grow personally and professionally. Consider sharing how you applied what you learned in the program to your work.
content:
Looking back, I can see exactly when I needed an MBA: it was the moment our supply chain nearly collapsed in Q3 last year.

I was leading merchandising operations at Lazada Indonesia—managing inventory across 50,000 SKUs—when COVID disruptions hit our suppliers. I had the data, the hustle, and seven years in retail, but I didn't have the financial literacy to model scenarios, the strategic framework to think beyond the next quarter, or the confidence to challenge my VP's assumptions in that boardroom.

That's when McCombs' EMBA felt inevitable. Not someday, but now.

The program forced me to stop being the person who just executes and become someone who thinks structurally. In Operations Strategy, I analyzed our entire supply chain through the lens of cost of capital and risk management—concepts that had felt abstract suddenly explained why our buffering strategy was actually bleeding cash. I brought those frameworks straight back to my role, renegotiating vendor contracts and reducing safety stock by 18%, saving roughly 2.1 billion rupiah annually.

But the real value wasn't in the coursework alone. It was in the cohort.

Studying alongside fellow operational leaders meant I could test ideas with people who genuinely understood my constraints. When I was struggling to build a business case for supply chain digitization, my study group didn't let me hide behind "too risky"—they pushed me to quantify the risk and present it honestly. That conversation, more than any lecture, changed how I approach advocating for change internally.

I've also started mentoring two younger merchandisers, walking them through the same financial thinking that transformed my own decision-making. Teaching them has made me realize that professional growth isn't just about moving up—it's about bringing people with you.

McCombs proved that the right environment doesn't just teach you content. It changes how you think, and more importantly, how you see your responsibility to others.

school_name: University of Ljubljana School of Economics MSc
prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
When I was twenty-three, I opened a small pop-up shop in Bangkok's Thonglor district selling secondhand designer bags. I named it "Redo" — partly because I was giving clothes a second life, partly because I was giving myself one too. My family wasn't thrilled. My parents expected me to take the management trainee position at the department store chain where my uncle was a director. It was the practical path, the one that made sense to people.

But the pop-up taught me something that eight years in retail still hasn't fully explained: I think better when I'm building something from nothing.

What you won't see in my CV is that Redo lasted exactly fourteen months. We had decent foot traffic, decent margins. The problem was me. I was managing inventory in spreadsheets and handling customer emails at midnight because I couldn't delegate. I told myself I was passionate about the product, about sustainability, about our brand story. What I was actually passionate about was control. I didn't know how to let people help me.

I closed the shop in 2017 and joined Zalora's Bangkok operations team as a category manager. That decision — choosing a structured environment after betting everything on independence — felt like failure at the time. But it wasn't. It was necessary.

The last eight years have been less about entrepreneurship and more about understanding how businesses actually work at scale. I've built demand forecasting models that reduced our overstock by 18%. I've restructured our vendor relationships across Southeast Asia. I've sat in meetings where we decided to exit markets, and I've seen firsthand how those decisions affect real people — the warehouse staff in Penang, the delivery partners in Jakarta.

What those experiences taught me is that my skills are real, but my instincts alone are incomplete. I'm an executor. I see inefficiencies and I fix them. I see an opportunity and I move fast. But I can be impatient with process, dismissive of risk mitigation, and I push my teams harder than I probably should. I know this about myself now because I've learned it the hard way, not from an article about leadership styles.

I'm applying to Ljubljana because I'm at a specific inflection point. Southeast Asian retail is changing faster than I can learn on the job. The market consolidation is real. The shift to omnichannel is real. And honestly, I've peaked in my current role — I can improve the metrics, but I can't expand the strategy. I need to study the theory behind what I've been doing intuitively. I need frameworks. I need to understand supply chain architecture at a level that reading industry reports hasn't given me.

But there's something else, something smaller and maybe more important. I grew up in a culture that valued fitting in, and I've spent my career in industries — retail, fashion, e-commerce — where "different" is often just marketing. At thirty-one, I'm less interested in fitting in anywhere. I'm looking for an environment where I can learn rigorously, where people are serious about ideas and evidence, where my gender or nationality or weird career path is simply irrelevant to whether I'm capable.

Ljubljana appeals to me because it feels different in the right way. It's not a prestige play. It's thoughtful. The curriculum seems built around actual problems rather than just teaching you how to sound smart at cocktail parties. Your alumni network in retail and supply chain management is real. And frankly, studying in Europe for two years appeals to me as a reset — a chance to sit in seminars and struggle through case studies without worrying about whether I'm being a good enough manager or team lead.

I've built things. I've managed people. I've made decisions with real consequences. What I haven't done yet is stop and actually study. I think I'm ready for that now.

prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
When I think about a time things didn't go as planned, the first thing that comes to mind is my experience leading a marketing campaign at my previous company. This was a difficult period for me, and it taught me valuable lessons about planning and execution.

I was working as a junior marketing manager at a mid-size e-commerce company in Jakarta. My boss asked me to oversee a major promotional campaign for the holiday season. I was excited about this opportunity because it was my first time leading such a significant project. I immediately began working on the campaign strategy and creative materials.

Unfortunately, the campaign did not perform well. The sales numbers were much lower than expected, and we lost a lot of money. I realized that I had made many mistakes. I did not do enough market research beforehand. I did not communicate clearly with the design team about what we needed. I did not set realistic timelines for the project. These were all problems that could have been avoided with better planning.

At first, I felt very discouraged. I wondered if I was capable of handling bigger responsibilities. However, I decided to take this as a learning opportunity. I met with my boss and the team to discuss what went wrong. We analyzed the data and identified the key issues. I apologized to the team and took responsibility for my failures.

After this experience, I made significant changes to how I approach projects. I now spend more time on planning and research before starting any campaign. I create detailed timelines and communicate them clearly to all team members. I also schedule regular check-in meetings to ensure everyone is on the same page. These new habits have helped me become a better manager.

The following year, I led another campaign and it was much more successful. The sales increased by 40% compared to the previous year. This showed me that failure can be a good teacher if you are willing to learn from it.

If I could go back and do things differently, I would definitely spend more time planning. I would also be more careful about setting realistic expectations with my boss and the team. I think communication is very important in any project, and I should have focused more on this from the beginning.

This experience has shaped how I approach challenges now. I have learned that setbacks are normal and that they provide opportunities for growth. I am excited to bring these lessons to business school, where I hope to learn even more about leadership and management. I believe that Fudan's MBA program will help me develop the skills I need to become a successful leader in the retail and e-commerce industry.
