prompt: Share a time when things didn't go as planned. What would you do differently?
content:
# Essay: When the Client Didn't Buy In

I was twenty-six, two years into my Bain engagement at a pharmaceutical company's Paris headquarters, and convinced I had the answer to their supply chain problem. Our team had spent four months modeling a network redesign that would save the company €12 million annually. The analysis was solid—I'd triple-checked the assumptions. The recommendation was clean: consolidate three distribution centers into two. We presented to the CFO and his operations leadership on a Tuesday morning in January.

They said no.

Not "let us think about it." Not "we need to revisit some details." Just no. The CFO explained that closing the Marseille facility would eliminate 200 jobs, and politically, the company couldn't absorb that blow in the current climate. It was a three-sentence rejection of four months of work.

I remember walking back to the Bain office feeling gutted. I was frustrated—not at them, but at myself. I'd optimized purely for financial return. I'd built the perfect solution to the wrong problem.

What I would do differently is obvious in hindsight: I should have started with a conversation. Before modeling, before spreadsheets, I should have spent a day at each distribution center. I should have talked to the operations director off the record about what actually mattered to him—and understood that "save €12 million" was never going to compete with "avoid laying off 200 people in your hometown." I should have asked better questions before building.

But the insight that actually shaped me came after that initial disappointment. Rather than move on to the next project, I went back to the CFO and asked if we could rework the recommendation. This time, I understood the real constraints. We came back with a hybrid approach: automate the Marseille center rather than close it, redeploy headcount into higher-skill roles, and still capture €7 million in savings. They implemented it. It wasn't the elegant answer I'd originally wanted, but it was the right answer for their business.

That experience taught me something crucial about consulting that I didn't fully grasp in school: the best recommendation means nothing if stakeholders won't own it. Analysis is just half the job. The other half is understanding what people actually care about—what keeps your CFO up at night, what the operations team fears losing, what the local government needs to hear.

I've carried this lesson through subsequent engagements. When I led the growth strategy work for a logistics startup in Berlin last year, I spent my first week just listening. I sat in on customer calls. I went to their main warehouse at 4 AM. I asked the CEO what worried her most, not what she thought I wanted to hear. That work was messier and less "consultant-clean," but when we landed on the recommendation to expand into benelux instead of scaling domestically, she implemented it within six months. She'd bought in early because she'd helped shape the thinking.

I'm applying to Nanyang because I'm ready for the next chapter—one where I build something rather than advise on it. But I'm also drawn to your program specifically because of your emphasis on stakeholder engagement and organizational behavior alongside strategy. At Bain, I learned that being right isn't enough. At Nanyang, I want to learn how to be right *and* lead people through change.

The supply chain recommendation that failed taught me more than a dozen successful projects. I don't regret it for a second.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
Dear Admissions Committee,

I am writing to express my strong interest in Imperial College Business School's Executive MBA program. Over the past nine years working in asset management, I have come to recognize that my technical expertise in portfolio analysis and risk management, while valuable, needs to be complemented by the strategic business thinking and global perspective that your program offers.

My career in asset management began as a junior analyst at Turkmen Capital Partners in Istanbul, where I analyzed equity portfolios for high-net-worth clients. I quickly progressed to managing a team of four analysts and eventually overseeing a €120 million fund focused on emerging market equities. This progression taught me the mechanics of the business—how to read markets, structure investments, and deliver returns. However, it also revealed significant gaps in my understanding of how asset management firms actually operate as businesses.

Two years ago, I moved to London to join Meridian Asset Management as a Senior Portfolio Manager. The move was partly career ambition, but honestly, it was also escape. I had hit a ceiling in Istanbul. I could manage money well, but I couldn't see the bigger picture. When our firm faced pressure from institutional investors demanding better governance and clearer risk frameworks, I realized I was solving symptoms, not causes. I didn't understand regulatory strategy, organizational scaling, or how to translate investor demands into business models. My director would mention UCITS regulations or discuss client acquisition costs, and I would nod along while feeling fundamentally out of my depth.

That moment crystallized something I'd been sensing for years: I need to understand business at a deeper level to progress meaningfully in this industry. I'm not interested in jumping ship to finance tech or private equity. I want to become a leader in asset management—perhaps eventually running a firm or starting my own. But right now, my toolkit is incomplete. I can analyze securities. I can't analyze industries or competitive dynamics. I understand portfolio construction but not business unit economics.

Imperial's Executive MBA appeals to me for specific reasons. The program's emphasis on finance and asset management through courses like Advanced Corporate Finance and Financial Strategy aligns directly with my field. But more importantly, the cohort model—studying alongside executives from diverse industries—offers something my current environment cannot. Most of my peers at Meridian are also from finance. We validate each other's thinking rather than challenge it. I want to learn how a manufacturing executive thinks about competition, or how a healthcare manager builds organizational culture, and then apply those perspectives to asset management.

I also recognize that as someone from Turkey working in London, I bring a distinct perspective to a classroom discussion. I've navigated emerging markets, worked across different regulatory environments, and managed the professional transition from Istanbul to London. This isn't exotic value—it's practical experience that could enrich case discussions and group projects.

I should be honest: I'm not the typical MBA candidate. My GMAT score reflects the rusty test-taking skills of someone who hasn't sat exams in fifteen years, not my capability to engage with complex analytical work. I've spent my career solving real problems with real stakes—not case studies. What I bring instead is maturity, clarity about my goals, and genuine readiness to apply what I learn immediately. I'm not exploring myself through an MBA. I'm building capabilities I need to execute a specific vision.

I'm excited about the possibility of joining Imperial's Executive MBA cohort and contributing thoughtfully to it. I'm ready to invest the time and rigor required, and I'm confident that the program's practical, business-focused approach is precisely what will accelerate my development as a leader in asset management.

Sincerely,
[Applicant Name]

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
# Cover Letter for MBA Admission

Dear IIM Ahmedabad Admissions Committee,

When I first moved to Tokyo five years ago to join Deloitte's management consulting practice, I thought I had a clear career trajectory mapped out. I was wrong—and I'm grateful for it.

My role as a senior consultant has exposed me to the complexities of organizational transformation across financial services, retail, and manufacturing sectors. I've led engagements for clients like Sumitomo Banking Group and a mid-sized automotive supplier, managing teams of five to eight people and delivering recommendations that impacted budget decisions in the $50-100 million range. This experience has been valuable, but it's also shown me exactly what I'm missing.

The problem is that I'm executing someone else's strategy, not building one. At Deloitte, I'm skilled at analyzing what's broken and proposing fixes within existing frameworks. But I want to understand why companies make strategic bets, how markets shift, and why some leaders see opportunities others miss. I want to sit across the table from a CEO and debate market entry decisions in India, not just execute the due diligence afterward.

This is where IIM Ahmedabad fits specifically into my plan. India's business landscape—the growth trajectories, regulatory environment, and entrepreneurial ecosystem—represents something fundamentally different from what I've experienced in Japan or my client work across Asia-Pacific. I'm not looking for a one-year break before returning to my current path. I want to be positioned to either move into strategy and corporate development roles, or potentially start something in India's growing logistics or fintech sectors. IIM Ahmedabad's regional expertise, alumni network, and curriculum in strategic management will be critical for both paths.

I recognize my GMAT score of 629 is below your median. I'm not going to pretend otherwise. I spent three months studying when I should have spent four. But my work performance and the decisions I've made—pushing into complex client situations, taking on international assignments, learning Hindi on my own—demonstrate the capability to succeed in your program. I learn faster when the stakes are real.

I'm ready to move from analysis to strategy. I'm ready to engage deeply with Indian business. I'm ready for IIM Ahmedabad.

Sincerely,
[Applicant Name]

prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
# Essay Response: A Failure That Taught Me an Important Lesson

Failure is something everyone experiences, and I believe it is one of the most valuable teachers in both personal and professional life. Throughout my eleven years working in the renewable energy sector, I have encountered several setbacks that have shaped who I am today. One particular failure stands out as a turning point in my career and has taught me crucial lessons about leadership and communication.

In 2018, I was leading a team project at my company focused on implementing a new solar panel installation process. The goal was to streamline our workflow and reduce costs by approximately 15%. I was confident in my technical knowledge and believed I had developed a solid plan. However, I made a critical mistake: I failed to adequately involve my team members in the planning process. I simply presented them with the new process and expected them to execute it.

The result was disappointing. Team members felt disconnected from the project and did not understand the rationale behind the changes. There was resistance to implementation, and several team members even requested transfers to other departments. The project fell behind schedule by two months, and we did not achieve the cost reduction we had targeted. It was a humbling experience.

What I learned from this failure was profound. I realized that technical competence alone is not enough to be a good leader. I needed to develop better communication and collaborative skills. I had to understand that my team members were not simply resources to implement my ideas, but valuable contributors with their own perspectives and expertise.

After this experience, I committed to changing my approach. I started to actively listen to my team members and involve them early in the decision-making process. I took courses in leadership and communication to improve my skills. I also made it a point to explain the "why" behind decisions, not just the "what." These changes made a significant difference in my subsequent projects.

In the years following this failure, I successfully led three additional major projects. My team became more engaged and motivated. Project completion rates improved, and we achieved better results. More importantly, several team members who had initially wanted to leave ended up staying with the company and became my strongest collaborators.

This experience taught me that failure is not something to be ashamed of, but rather an opportunity for growth. It showed me that even with years of experience, there is always room for improvement. It also reinforced the importance of humility and the willingness to admit mistakes.

As I consider pursuing an MBA, I believe this lesson will be particularly relevant. Business school will present new challenges and opportunities to learn from others. I recognize that I still have much to learn about management, strategy, and business operations. This failure has taught me to approach these learning opportunities with an open mind and a collaborative spirit.

The renewable energy industry is growing rapidly, and I am excited about the opportunity to contribute to this sector in more senior roles. An MBA from KAIST College of Business will provide me with the knowledge and skills I need to take on greater responsibilities. More importantly, I hope to continue learning from my failures and using them as stepping stones for personal and professional development.

In conclusion, my failure in 2018 was a difficult but valuable lesson. It taught me that leadership is not just about technical expertise, but about building relationships, communicating effectively, and valuing the contributions of others. As I move forward in my career, I carry this lesson with me and remain committed to continuous improvement and growth.

prompt: What is your most significant accomplishment, and why does it matter to you?
content:
# What is Your Most Significant Accomplishment, and Why Does It Matter to You?

Throughout my career in venture capital, I have been fortunate to work on many meaningful projects and investments. However, if I had to identify my most significant accomplishment, it would be my role in sourcing and closing a Series A investment in a fintech startup that has since grown to become a leader in its space.

When I first encountered this company in 2019, it was just an idea with a small team of two founders. The market opportunity was clear, but the execution risk was high. Most of my colleagues were skeptical. I saw potential, though. I spent several months conducting due diligence, meeting with the founders repeatedly, and analyzing their go-to-market strategy. Eventually, I convinced my investment committee to move forward with a $2.5M check, which represented our fund's largest check size at that time.

The deal closed in Q3 2019. Since then, the company has raised three additional rounds of funding and is now valued at over $150M. The founders have been extremely successful in building out their team and scaling their operations. Our initial investment has returned approximately 8x, which is well above our fund's average return. Beyond the financial returns, I take pride in knowing that this company has helped thousands of customers manage their finances more effectively.

What makes this accomplishment meaningful to me is not just the monetary return, but the validation that my instincts and research were correct when others doubted. In venture capital, you make investment decisions with incomplete information, and most investments fail. The fact that this one succeeded, and succeeded spectacularly, is deeply satisfying.

Additionally, this experience taught me valuable lessons about conviction and persistence. It reinforced that in this industry, you must be willing to back your thesis even when your peers disagree. The founders themselves have also become friends and mentors to me. I regularly advise them on strategic decisions, and they have helped me understand the challenges of building a company from the ground up.

This accomplishment matters to me because it demonstrates my ability to identify opportunities that others overlook and to execute at a high level in a competitive environment. It has given me confidence in my judgment and my ability to add value beyond just deploying capital. As I pursue an Executive MBA at Nanyang Business School, I hope to build on these strengths and develop new capabilities in areas where I feel less confident, particularly in general management and operations.

The venture capital industry has been incredibly rewarding, and I believe an EMBA will help me transition into a leadership role where I can apply these lessons on a broader scale.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
I grew up watching my mum stretch a $40 grocery budget across a week for three kids. She worked at a community health centre in Western Sydney, and I remember her bringing home stories about families who couldn't afford their medications. At twelve, I didn't fully understand poverty—I just knew it seemed unfair that some people had to choose between eating and staying healthy.

That feeling has never left me.

For the past eleven years, I've worked in the non-profit sector—first as a program officer at a youth homelessness service in Melbourne, then coordinating health initiatives across rural Queensland. Last year, I was promoted to Operations Director at Safe Haven Australia, managing a $3.2 million budget and a team of eighteen across four locations. On paper, it's been a successful climb. But increasingly, I've realized that success in my field doesn't scale the way it should.

We help maybe 400 people a year. There are 116,000 Australians experiencing homelessness on any given night.

The gap between what we do and what's needed is crushing sometimes. I've become excellent at writing grant proposals and managing stakeholder relationships, but I've hit a ceiling. I know how to run a program well. I don't know how to fundamentally change how resources get allocated in this sector, or how to help other organizations do more with what they have. I've become a very competent person within a broken system, rather than someone who can fix the system.

That's what's driving me to business school now. I need to understand finance, strategy, and organizational design in ways my non-profit experience hasn't taught me. I want to learn how to build sustainable models, how to think about scaling impact, how to build coalitions between sectors. I want to come back to this work—I can't imagine doing anything else—but I want to come back armed with tools I don't currently have.

What matters most to me is reducing needless suffering. I've spent eleven years doing that incrementally. ESC Clermont's emphasis on creating value for society while developing business acumen feels like the right place to prepare for the next chapter—one where I can actually move the needle on this.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
When I joined FinFlow as a product manager two years ago, we had seven people in our Milan office and a rough product-market fit. Today, we're at 45 people across three offices, and I helped build the metrics dashboard that now tracks user engagement across our platform. But honestly, I'm realizing I don't know how to scale the way I need to.

The frustration hit me during our Series A fundraising round last year. I could see where the product should go, and I understood our users well enough. But when investors asked about market sizing, unit economics, or how we'd structure our team to hit growth targets in Asia, I fumbled. I had answers that were mostly intuition. I needed frameworks.

That's when I started reading about operations, finance, and strategic management—areas I'd avoided since my undergraduate days at Politecnico di Milano where I studied engineering. The MBA started to make sense not as a nice-to-have, but as something I actually needed to become the founder I want to be. And frankly, it's something I should have done before jumping into a startup.

I chose NUS because of three things. First, Singapore's position in Southeast Asia made sense for what we're building. Our next market expansion is probably going to be here rather than in Europe, and I wanted to study somewhere that would give me real exposure to how business works in this region. Second, the program's focus on practical strategy and operations—not just case studies—felt right for where I am. I'm not trying to rebrand myself; I'm trying to deepen what I can actually do. Third, honestly, the class composition matters to me. I've read about your students coming from 30+ countries, and I didn't want to do an MBA in a bubble of people who all think like European tech founders.

I know I'm not coming in with the strongest numbers—my GMAT was a 684, and that still frustrates me—but I've learned I'm someone who pushes harder when I actually care about the outcome. I care about this.

I'd bring a perspective on early-stage scaling to your cohort, not theoretical knowledge but actual experience of what breaks when you try to grow too fast. And honestly, I'd probably be that person asking uncomfortable questions in class about how the frameworks apply when you're trying to survive, not optimize.

prompt: Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.
content:
# Tell us about a failure that taught you an important lesson.

Three years into my consulting career at Deloitte, I led a supply chain optimization project for a manufacturing client in Valencia. On paper, it looked straightforward: redesign their distribution network, reduce costs by 15%, implement in six months. I was confident. Maybe overconfident.

I spent the first month building models in isolation. Complex algorithms, elegant solutions. I barely involved the operations team. When I finally presented, they didn't push back politely—they pushed back hard. The plan ignored how their facilities actually worked. Seasonal staffing wasn't accounted for. Equipment constraints I'd treated as variables were actually fixed. A director said, "You've optimized something that doesn't exist."

It stung. I thought I'd failed the client. Worse, I thought I'd failed the team that trusted me to lead.

I could have defended the model or pushed harder, but instead I asked: what am I missing? The answer was humbling—I'd been solving the problem alone because I thought that proved competence. I'd avoided early client conversations because I was afraid of being challenged. Looking back, it was about ego, not analysis.

So we restarted. I spent two weeks on the factory floor, watching shifts change, talking to logistics coordinators, understanding the actual constraints. The revised model was simpler but infinitely more useful because it was built on reality, not assumptions. We delivered 12% cost reduction instead of 15%, but it actually stuck. The client extended the engagement another six months.

The lesson wasn't just "involve stakeholders early"—it was about understanding why I didn't. I'd grown up valuing individual achievement. In consulting, you're taught to be the smartest person in the room. These aren't wrong instincts, but they're incomplete. Real problem-solving requires intellectual humility and genuine curiosity about how systems actually work beyond my models.

This shapes how I approach work now. I actively seek perspectives that challenge me. I'm direct about uncertainty. I ask more questions before recommending answers.

An MBA isn't about learning new frameworks—I can read research papers anywhere. It's about surrounding myself with people who think differently, who'll challenge my blind spots, and who'll help me solve real problems, not perfect ones. That's why IIM Calcutta appeals to me: the peer-driven culture and the emphasis on practical application rather than theory divorced from reality.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
**Career Aspirations and How EM Normandie Will Help Me Achieve Them**

Throughout my seven years working in supply chain and logistics, I have developed a strong passion for this industry and a desire to advance my career to the next level. I believe that pursuing an MBA at EM Normandie will be the perfect stepping stone to help me reach my professional goals and become a leader in the field.

My current role as a logistics coordinator has given me valuable experience in managing inventory, coordinating shipments, and working with various teams. I have learned a lot about how companies operate and how important it is to have efficient supply chain processes. However, I realize that to move forward in my career, I need to gain more strategic knowledge and develop my business acumen. An MBA is the natural next step for me.

My main career aspiration is to become a supply chain manager or director within the next five to ten years. I want to take on more responsibility and lead teams to improve operational efficiency and reduce costs. I know that supply chain is vital to any organization's success, and I want to be someone who contributes meaningfully to this area. By getting my MBA, I will be equipped with the tools and knowledge necessary to achieve this goal.

EM Normandie's Master in Management program is an excellent fit for my goals because it offers strong courses in operations and supply chain management. The curriculum seems well-designed to prepare students for leadership roles in international business. Additionally, being a French person, studying at a French business school makes sense for my career development in Europe. The program's international perspective will help me understand global markets and international trade, which are increasingly important in logistics.

The school's emphasis on practical learning and case studies will help me apply theoretical concepts to real-world situations I have already encountered in my work. This will make my learning more meaningful and directly applicable. Furthermore, the networking opportunities at EM Normandie will allow me to connect with other professionals and potentially find mentors who can guide my career progression.

I am confident that after completing the MBA, I will be better prepared to take on managerial positions and eventually move into senior leadership. The combination of rigorous coursework, real-world experience I already have, and the connections I will make at EM Normandie will position me perfectly to achieve my goals.

In conclusion, an MBA from EM Normandie is more than just a degree for me—it is an investment in my future and a crucial step toward becoming the supply chain leader I aspire to be. I am excited about the prospect of joining your program and contributing to the vibrant community at EM Normandie.

content:
