prompt: Describe a time when you challenged conventional thinking to drive innovation.
content:
# Challenging the Billable Hour Model

Three years into my consulting career at Deloitte, I was staffed on a digital transformation project for a mid-sized manufacturing client in the Philippines. The engagement was structured as a traditional time-and-materials contract, which meant we billed based on hours spent, regardless of actual outcomes. About six weeks in, I noticed something that bothered me: our team was optimizing for duration, not impact.

We'd schedule meetings that could have been emails. We'd run parallel workstreams instead of consolidating them. Nobody was explicitly told to work slowly, but the incentive structure made it rational. The longer the project, the higher the revenue. I watched our project manager defend a three-week timeline for something that probably needed two, and I realized nobody was going to push back because nobody benefited from efficiency.

I scheduled a conversation with the engagement lead and floated an idea: what if we restructured the remaining four months as a fixed-fee outcome model, with shared upside if we delivered early? The pushback was immediate. "That's not how we do things," my manager said. The risk department had concerns. Finance wanted to model the downside scenarios.

But the client was also frustrated—they could feel the inefficiency, and their CFO had mentioned budget concerns during a steering committee. So I built a proposal: we'd convert the remaining scope to fixed-fee at a 15 percent discount, but if we delivered in under 18 weeks, we'd split the savings 60-40. If we exceeded 18 weeks, we absorbed the cost.

It took three weeks of internal negotiation. I had to convince the partnership that this wasn't a race to the bottom but a market differentiator. Eventually, we got approval, and I rewrote the contract.

What happened next validated the model. Freed from billable hour constraints, the team consolidated meetings, automated repetitive tasks, and made faster decisions. We finished in 15 weeks, well under the threshold. The client paid less but felt better served. We made slightly less revenue than the original contract would have generated, but the client became a reference for future work and signed a larger engagement with us the following year.

I was promoted to senior consultant partly on the strength of that project, and it's become a case study we reference in our digital delivery practice. But here's the honest part: it wasn't actually that courageous. The client was already unhappy. The risk was manageable. And I only escalated because I happened to notice the inefficiency.

What I learned is that challenging convention matters less when you're pointing out something obvious. The harder work is building the business case, securing buy-in from people with competing incentives, and being willing to be wrong. That's what I want to develop at Nord—the ability to push back on not just what we do, but how the incentive structures around our work actually shape behavior. I want to learn frameworks for identifying when conventional thinking is actually limiting growth, not protecting it.

prompt: Tell us about a leadership experience that shaped your management philosophy.
content:
When I think about leadership, the first experience that comes to mind is my time managing a small team at my previous company. I was promoted to team lead after working there for about a year, and this role really changed how I think about being a manager.

Before this promotion, I didn't have much management experience. I was just focused on doing my own work well. But when I became a team lead, I realized that my job was completely different. I had to think about other people's careers and development, not just my own performance. This was a big shift for me.

One thing I learned is that communication is really important. My manager didn't communicate well with our team, and people were often confused about what we were supposed to be doing. So when I became a team lead, I made sure to have regular meetings with my team members to explain our goals and answer any questions. I think this made people feel more confident and motivated.

I also learned that it's important to be fair. When I was assigning work, I tried to give everyone equal opportunities to work on interesting projects, not just the same people every time. I wanted people to feel like they had a chance to grow and learn new things.

Another important lesson was about listening to feedback. I made mistakes as a new manager, and when team members told me what I was doing wrong, I tried to actually listen instead of getting defensive. This helped me improve and also showed my team that I cared about their opinions.

Through this experience, I developed my management philosophy: I believe that a good manager should focus on communication, fairness, and listening to their team. I think when people feel heard and have opportunities to develop, they work better and are more engaged. This is what I want to bring to my future career.

I'm hoping that the MBA program at Peking Guanghua will help me build on these foundational leadership skills and teach me more advanced management techniques that I can apply in more complex business environments.

school_name: University of Zagreb Faculty of Economics MSc
prompt: Tell us something about yourself that is not captured elsewhere in your application.
content:
When I was 16, my high school physics teacher handed me a failing grade and told me I wasn't "a math person." I believed him for exactly three weeks.

What's not in my application is how that moment shaped everything after. I spent the next month working through every problem set twice—once to understand it, once to prove I understood it. I went from 4/20 on that test to 18/20 on the final exam. My teacher apologized. I didn't care about the apology. I cared that I'd learned something about myself: that I was stubborn enough to reject someone else's ceiling on my potential.

This pattern shows up everywhere in my career, but the numbers can't quite capture it. Yes, I've spent eight years in tech startups. Yes, I helped build our last company from a team of three to 47 people across Paris, Barcelona, and Berlin. But what doesn't appear anywhere is the fact that I spent six months completely lost, convinced I was in the wrong role.

Three years into that company, I was promoted to VP of Operations. On paper, it was a clear win. In reality, I was managing process when I wanted to be building products. For months I told myself to just accept it, get better at spreadsheets, grow up. One Tuesday I realized I'd become someone who optimized expense reports and that was genuinely all I had to say about my week.

So I did something stupid: I told my CEO I'd made a mistake. That I wasn't the right person for the role and would rather take a step back. He didn't fire me. Instead, we spent two weeks figuring out what I actually wanted. I moved into a hybrid role—still operational, but with real product involvement. Within a year, we'd restructured how the entire tech team interfaced with operations.

I'm not mentioning this because I want credit for being "self-aware." I'm mentioning it because I know that an MBA will challenge me in ways I can't predict. And I want to study at a school where that's not just tolerated—where it's actually valued. Zagreb's program appeals to me precisely because it's smaller, more selective, and honest about what it asks of students. I'm not looking for a place to coast on my GMAT score. I'm looking for friction.

prompt: Why have you chosen to apply to our program? What will you contribute to our community?
content:
I chose to apply to SKEMA Business School because I believe it's time for me to take a deliberate step toward leadership, and your program offers exactly what I need at this stage of my career.

For the past twelve years, I've worked in accounting and finance across three organizations in Thailand and two years in Vietnam. I started as a junior accountant at a mid-sized firm in Bangkok, progressed to senior roles managing teams of 4-6 people, and most recently completed a two-year assignment as Finance Manager at a regional manufacturing company. My work has been solid—I've implemented cost control systems that reduced operational expenses by 12%, trained junior staff, and earned consistent promotions. But I've hit a ceiling. I'm capable of more than what my current path offers, and I can feel it.

What's missing is a structured framework for strategic thinking. In my accounting roles, I solve problems within my domain. I optimize processes. I make sure numbers are right. But I don't sit at tables where business strategy is discussed. I don't understand how to move from being excellent at execution to driving business decisions. An MBA gives me that language and that access.

SKEMA appeals to me specifically because of your international focus. Thailand's economy is changing—we're seeing more foreign direct investment, more competition from regional firms, and more demand for finance professionals who think beyond local accounting standards. I've felt this shift in my own work. Two years ago, I was asked to help a Vietnamese subsidiary align its reporting with IFRS standards, and I realized how much I didn't know about global business operations. Your program's emphasis on international business strategy, combined with your network across Europe and Asia, directly addresses where I want to develop expertise. I want to understand how accounting and finance connect to broader business transformation.

The global classroom matters to me too. I've worked with international teams, but mostly in operational contexts. Being in a classroom with peers from different countries, studying business through different lenses, will push me beyond the Thai accounting perspective I've inherited. I want to challenge my assumptions and build relationships that extend my thinking.

As for what I'll contribute: I bring practical experience that many of your peers may not have. I've worked in resource-constrained environments where you can't hire consultants—you solve problems yourself. I've trained teams with varying education levels and taught technical concepts to non-finance people. I've navigated business operations across Southeast Asia during periods of real instability. I'm also someone who asks direct questions when I don't understand something, and I'm comfortable saying "I don't know" before learning. These aren't glamorous contributions, but they're real. I show up prepared, I work hard, and I'm genuinely interested in how other people think.

I'm not applying to SKEMA because I believe an MBA will transform me into a different person or unlock some hidden genius. I'm applying because I've maxed out what I can achieve on my own, and I need a structured program, excellent professors, and a cohort that will challenge me to think bigger. Your program fits that need, and I believe I'll be a reliable, engaged member of your community.

prompt: What matters most to you, and why?
content:
What matters most to me is making a positive impact in the world through my work in manufacturing. I believe that business has the power to create meaningful change, and I want to be part of that transformation.

From a young age, I was taught the importance of hard work and dedication. Growing up in Singapore, a country built on strong work ethic and innovation, I learned that success comes from commitment and perseverance. These values have shaped who I am today and continue to drive my decisions in my career and personal life.

In my current role at a mid-sized manufacturing company, I have been exposed to many aspects of business operations. I work with different teams and have learned about supply chain management, quality control, and production efficiency. Through these experiences, I have come to understand that manufacturing is not just about producing goods—it is about creating value for customers and contributing to the economy.

What matters most to me is being able to make a difference in people's lives. Whether it is through improving product quality, reducing costs, or creating jobs, I believe that business is a force for good. I want to work in an industry where I can see the direct impact of my efforts. Manufacturing provides this opportunity because the products we make are used by millions of people every day.

I also value continuous learning and self-improvement. In my five years of work experience, I have learned a great deal about different aspects of manufacturing. However, I realize that I still have much to learn. This is one of the reasons why I want to pursue an MBA. I want to deepen my knowledge of business fundamentals and develop the skills needed to become a better leader.

Leadership is something that matters to me. I believe that good leaders inspire their teams and create a positive work environment. In my current position, I have had the opportunity to lead small teams and have found this to be very rewarding. I want to continue developing my leadership abilities so that I can eventually manage larger teams and drive bigger initiatives.

Another thing that matters to me is integrity and ethical business practices. I believe that companies should conduct their business in a responsible and transparent manner. In my industry, there are sometimes pressures to cut corners or prioritize short-term profits over long-term sustainability. I want to be a leader who stands up for what is right and encourages my organization to adopt ethical practices.

Family is also very important to me. I come from a close-knit family, and my parents have always supported my ambitions. They taught me the importance of education and have encouraged me to pursue my goals. I want to make them proud by achieving success in my career, and an MBA from Stanford would be a significant step toward that goal.

Finally, what matters most to me is the opportunity to grow both professionally and personally. I want to work in an environment where I can challenge myself, learn new things, and become the best version of myself. I believe that Stanford's MBA program can provide me with the education, network, and experiences needed to achieve my goals.

In conclusion, what matters most to me is making a positive impact through business, continuous learning, leadership development, ethical practices, and personal growth. I am committed to these values and believe that an MBA will help me pursue them more effectively. I am excited about the opportunity to study at Stanford and contribute to the business world in meaningful ways.

prompt: Briefly summarize your career progression since you started your professional career.
content:
My professional journey began in 2017 when I joined McKinsey & Company as a Business Analyst in the Paris office. I was eager to apply my analytical skills and contribute to solving complex business problems for leading organizations across Europe.

During my first two years, I worked on various projects spanning strategy, operations, and digital transformation. These experiences exposed me to different industries and helped me develop a solid foundation in management consulting. I collaborated with senior consultants and learned how to break down complex issues, gather data, and present findings to C-level executives. This period was crucial for building my consulting toolkit.

In 2019, I was promoted to Senior Associate, reflecting my growing expertise and ability to manage larger project components. In this role, I took on more responsibility for client relationships and began leading smaller teams of analysts. I continued working on strategy projects, but increasingly focused on implementation and helping clients execute their transformation roadmaps. This shift from pure strategy to execution was meaningful because I realized I enjoyed seeing tangible results from our recommendations.

Over the next few years, my work continued to evolve. I became more involved in business development, helping to pitch new projects to existing and prospective clients. I also mentored junior consultants, which I found rewarding. The consulting environment was fast-paced and demanding, requiring constant learning and adaptation. I worked with companies in manufacturing, retail, financial services, and technology sectors.

However, by 2023, after seven years in consulting, I began to question whether I wanted to continue on the traditional partnership track. The travel, the long hours, and the project-based nature of the work started to feel less fulfilling. I realized I wanted to develop deeper expertise in a specific function or industry rather than being a generalist problem-solver. I also wanted to spend more time in one organization, building something over the long term rather than recommending solutions and moving to the next engagement.

This realization led me to consider an MBA. I believe an executive program would help me transition into a strategic leadership role within an organization where I can combine my consulting background with deeper functional expertise. I am particularly interested in operations, supply chain, or business development roles where I can apply my analytical skills while having more direct control over implementation.

An MBA from IIM Amritsar would provide me with the skills and network needed to make this transition successfully. I am committed to bringing my consulting experience to the classroom and learning from peers who have built different types of careers. I am ready for the next chapter.

school_name: University of Navarra School of Economics MiM
prompt: Describe the achievement you are most proud of and explain why.
content:
I am most proud of leading the expansion of our sales team at CloudSync from 3 to 12 people over the course of two years. This achievement represents the culmination of my hard work and dedication to the company's growth.

When I joined CloudSync in 2016, we were a small startup struggling to meet our revenue targets. The sales department was understaffed and overwhelmed. Management asked me to take on a leadership role and build out the team. I accepted this challenge immediately because I knew it was an opportunity to make a real difference.

I started by identifying the gaps in our sales process. We were losing deals because we didn't have enough people to follow up with leads. I created a recruitment strategy and hired several talented salespeople from competitors. I trained them on our product and our sales methodology. Within the first year, we had doubled our team size and increased revenue by 40%.

In the second year, I continued to build the team strategically. I hired people with different backgrounds and experiences. I created a mentorship program where senior salespeople could help junior salespeople develop their skills. I also implemented a new CRM system that helped us track leads more effectively.

By the end of two years, our team had grown to 12 people and we were hitting all of our targets. Revenue had increased significantly and we were closing deals faster than ever before. The company was able to expand into new markets because we had the sales capacity to support that growth.

This achievement is important to me because it shows that I can take on a big challenge and execute. I was given a problem and I solved it through strategic thinking and hard work. I didn't just hire people randomly – I thought carefully about what we needed and I built a team that could deliver results.

I also learned a lot about leadership during this time. I realized that it's not just about hitting numbers – it's about developing people and creating a culture where they can succeed. My team members respected me because I was invested in their growth, not just in the bottom line.

This experience has shaped who I am as a professional. I understand what it takes to scale a business. I know how to identify talent and recruit it. I know how to build systems and processes that allow teams to work more effectively.

I want to bring this experience to business school and continue to develop as a leader. An MBA from University of Navarra School of Economics will give me the tools and knowledge I need to take on even bigger challenges in the future. I'm excited about the opportunity to learn from professors and classmates who have diverse backgrounds and perspectives.

This achievement means a lot to me, and I'm proud of what I accomplished at CloudSync.

prompt: Please provide any additional information that will enhance our understanding of your candidacy for the program.
content:
When I decided to pursue consulting after my undergraduate degree in Stockholm, I was driven by the appeal of problem-solving and the chance to work across industries. Six years at Deloitte have delivered on that promise. I've led engagements for Nordic financial services clients, managed a team of four analysts, and contributed to proposals that generated roughly €2M in new business. By any traditional measure, I've progressed well.

Yet I've hit a ceiling I didn't anticipate. My technical skills are solid, but I lack the strategic business foundation to move into senior leadership roles. At Deloitte, advancement increasingly requires an MBA from a top-tier program—it's become the unspoken requirement. More importantly, I realize I want to build something, not just advise others on how to build it. The consulting model has shown me what I'm good at; now I need to learn what I'm capable of creating.

An executive MBA appeals to me for this reason. I'm not looking to hit pause on my career or relocate. I'm looking for an intensive program that fits into the reality of my life right now: continuing to work, staying in Stockholm with my partner, and gaining the strategic toolkit I'm missing. Marshall's cohort model and evening schedule align with what I need.

What concerns me is my GMAT score of 619. I'm a stronger candidate on paper than that number suggests—my work experience and track record speak to analytical capability. I know this essay won't overcome that gap entirely, but I wanted to acknowledge it directly rather than hope it goes unnoticed. I'm committed to proving I belong in this program.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
I first learned about Rotterdam through a conversation I almost didn't have. Last March, I was at a tech conference in Barcelona, and I spotted someone wearing an RSM hoodie at the coffee station. Instead of letting the moment pass—which is exactly what I would have done two years ago—I introduced myself. That person was Paula, an EMBA student who spent forty-five minutes telling me about her cohort, the city, and why she'd chosen to study in Europe rather than pursue programs closer to home in the US where she'd been working.

That conversation was a turning point. Paula's honesty about the program's intensity and the deliberate pace of Rotterdam's curriculum resonated with me in a way that polished websites never could. She wasn't selling me on the school; she was describing a real choice she'd made that aligned with what she actually needed at that stage of her career. I wanted to understand that choice better.

I spent the next two months doing what feels like genuine research to me—not just checking boxes, but trying to understand whether this program matched where I am now. I read through recent case studies on the website and noticed RSM's emphasis on systems thinking and complex problem-solving. That mattered to me because my last two years at Google working on supply chain optimization have taught me that technical solutions mean almost nothing without understanding organizational and market dynamics. I've seen brilliant engineers build features nobody wanted, and I've seen mediocre technology succeed because the business model worked.

I also contacted three alumni directly—two who stayed in Europe and one who returned to Latin America—and asked them unprompted questions about what they wish they'd known before starting. The honest answers were useful: one told me the program doesn't coddle you, another said the international cohort is legitimately diverse, and a third mentioned that Rotterdam's location forces you to think beyond US-centric business problems. That last point hit home. I'm Peruvian, and I've spent most of my career in American tech companies where strategy discussions happen as if the rest of the world is an afterthought. I want to work somewhere that takes that seriously.

What I'll do with being part of your community: I plan to be specific about this. I want to join the consulting club because I'm seriously exploring a transition toward strategy work, and I learn best by doing actual projects, not just discussing them in classes. I want to find peers who understand the gap between technical excellence and business impact—something I've felt acutely and want to address head-on. I also want to contribute to conversations about technology's role in emerging markets, particularly Latin America, which I suspect isn't foregrounded enough in typical MBA curricula.

I'm not coming to Rotterdam to discover myself or to pivot completely. I'm coming because I've hit a ceiling in my current trajectory, and I'm specific about why this program addresses that. I need to think bigger about systems, markets, and strategy—not because it sounds impressive, but because I've realized that's what makes me curious again about work.

prompt: Describe your career aspirations and how our MBA will serve as a catalyst for achieving them.
content:
When I tell people I work in education, they often assume I teach in a classroom. I don't. For the past eleven years, I've been building educational infrastructure—first as a curriculum developer in Cairo, then as a program manager for an NGO focused on vocational training across the Middle East, and most recently as a learning operations director for a tech-enabled education startup in Dubai. What ties these roles together isn't pedagogy; it's the business side of education. I've learned how to scale programs, manage budgets, and navigate the complex landscape where social impact meets financial sustainability.

This is also where I've hit a ceiling.

Two years ago, my organization expanded to serve 45,000 students across eight countries. On paper, this was a success—enrollment targets were met, donor relations were strong, and our cost-per-learner metrics improved by 23%. But internally, I realized I was managing operations tactically rather than driving strategy. When our leadership team debated market entry decisions for Vietnam and Indonesia, I contributed implementation timelines and cost breakdowns. I couldn't contribute to the competitive analysis or the business model questions underneath the expansion. That gap has bothered me ever since.

I want to move into a director-level strategy role within the next five years, ideally leading business development for an education organization that serves emerging markets. To do this credibly, I need formal training in areas where I've picked up knowledge piecemeal: financial modeling, strategic market analysis, organizational behavior at the executive level. My experience gives me the domain expertise; what I'm missing is the business fundamentals that would let me think at the strategic level rather than execute at the operational level.

Fudan's MBA appeals to me for specific reasons. First, your location. Shanghai is the natural hub for anyone building education businesses in Asia. Being based there for twelve months would give me direct exposure to the region's ed-tech ecosystem at a moment when investment and consolidation are accelerating. The school itself has been aggressive about internationalization—roughly 40% of your cohort is non-Chinese, and your faculty recruitment from top programs is visible. This matters to me because my work is fundamentally cross-cultural. I've managed teams in Egypt, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, UAE, and online. I need classmates and professors who can help me think through how business strategy translates across contexts where regulatory environments, consumer behaviors, and funding mechanisms are radically different.

Second, Fudan's strength in Chinese business practices and language training is unique among MBA programs I'm considering. My organization has discussed entering the Chinese market for three years. We've never committed because none of our leadership team understands the regulatory landscape, the relationship-based nature of Chinese business, or frankly, how to operate at scale in a managed economy. If I do this MBA, I want to come back with not just analytical frameworks, but actual insight into how business strategy works in this region.

I also recognize what I'm not bringing to this program. My GMAT score isn't exceptional. My undergraduate GPA was decent but not impressive. I'm not applying at 28 with a start-up exit or an offer from a MNC. What I do bring is a clear sense of where I am, where I want to go, and why this particular MBA fits that trajectory. I've spent eleven years in one sector, building competence incrementally. I'm ready for a step change in capability.

The education sector will continue to expand globally. My bet is that the organizations that win over the next decade will be those that understand both pedagogy and business—that can build sustainable models in markets where traditional ed-tech assumptions don't apply. I want to be the person building those organizations. An MBA won't teach me education; it will teach me the business side well enough to lead.

prompt: Please submit a cover letter seeking a place in our MBA Program. Tell us why you are an ideal candidate.
content:
I have always believed that education is the key to unlock doors of opportunity. Coming from a family business background in Vietnam, I have learned the importance of hard work and dedication. After nine years of working in our family business, I have realized that I need to pursue an MBA to take my career to the next level.

My family business started many years ago and has grown significantly. I am responsible for managing various aspects of the operations, including sales, marketing, and general management. Through this experience, I have developed strong leadership skills and a deep understanding of business fundamentals. However, I recognize that formal business education will provide me with the strategic knowledge I need to expand our business internationally and implement modern management practices.

Renmin University Business School is an excellent choice for me because of its reputation and strong curriculum. The MBA program will equip me with the necessary tools and frameworks to make better business decisions. I am particularly interested in the courses offered and the experienced faculty members who can guide me through my learning journey.

In my current role, I have faced many challenges that have shaped my perspective on business. I have worked with my family to navigate difficult market conditions and I understand the complexities of running a business in Vietnam. I am confident that an MBA from Renmin will help me tackle these challenges more effectively and position our family business for sustainable growth.

I am committed to being an active participant in the classroom and bringing my practical experience to contribute to discussions. I believe that my diverse background and industry experience will enrich the learning environment for my classmates. Additionally, I am eager to build a professional network that will help me understand different industries and business practices.

I am passionate about making a difference in my family business and in the broader business community. An MBA degree will help me achieve my goals and become a more effective leader. I am confident that I am an ideal candidate for your program because of my work experience, my dedication to continuous learning, and my commitment to excellence.

Thank you for considering my application.

prompt: Reflect on a core value that defines you. Share a specific example of how it has guided your decisions and actions, and explain how it will shape your contributions and growth as a McCombs MBA student. Highlight how this value has influenced your leadership style and life path, and how it will shape your MBA experience.
content:
Integrity is the value that has shaped my entire career and personal journey. I believe that doing the right thing, even when no one is watching, is fundamental to who I am and how I lead.

Throughout my six years in the pharmaceutical industry, I have faced situations where integrity was tested. In my current role as a Senior Analyst at a mid-sized biotech company, I work on regulatory compliance and clinical trial documentation. This work demands absolute honesty because lives depend on accurate data. I have always made sure that my reports and submissions reflect the truth, nothing more and nothing less.

One moment that stands out is when I discovered a discrepancy in our trial data that could have been overlooked. Rather than ignore it or report it minimally, I immediately flagged it to my supervisor and the compliance team. This meant delays in our timeline and additional work for everyone, but it was the right call. My integrity in that moment reinforced my commitment to ethical practices in healthcare.

This value has defined my leadership approach. I lead by example, holding myself to high standards and expecting the same from my team. I believe people follow leaders they trust, and trust comes from consistent ethical behavior.

At McCombs, I want to deepen my understanding of business ethics and corporate responsibility. I know that my integrity will guide me through the program, helping me contribute meaningfully to class discussions and group projects. I am committed to building relationships with classmates based on honesty and mutual respect. As I progress into senior leadership roles, I will carry this value forward, ensuring that my decisions impact not just my organization but the broader healthcare industry positively.

My integrity is not just a value—it is my foundation.

prompt: Describe a professional setback. How did you recover and what did you learn?
content:
In my first month at Meridian Capital Partners, I completely botched a deal analysis. We were evaluating a 45 million EUR acquisition of a mid-market logistics company in Northern France, and I was tasked with building the financial model and conducting preliminary due diligence. I worked through two weekends straight, convinced that sheer effort would compensate for my inexperience. I didn't ask for help. I didn't validate my assumptions with anyone senior. I just pushed forward.

When I presented my findings to the investment committee, the holes became immediately obvious. My revenue projections had no basis in industry comps. I'd missed an entire segment of the company's customer concentration risk. Worse, my working capital assumptions contradicted the management team's own guidance—something I would have caught if I'd actually read the management presentation carefully instead of just skimming it. One partner asked me a clarifying question, and I realized I couldn't answer it. The meeting lasted twelve minutes. We passed on the deal.

I remember sitting at my desk afterward feeling genuinely sick. Not just embarrassed—I felt incompetent. And the worst part was that nobody had to tell me I'd failed. I knew it.

That evening, I did something I probably should have done before: I called my direct manager, Sophie, and asked if we could grab coffee. I told her the truth about how I'd approached the work—the overconfidence, the isolation, the sloppy execution. I didn't make excuses. I asked her what I needed to do differently.

Sophie said something I didn't expect: "You're not the first analyst to make this mistake, and you won't be the last. What matters now is whether you're the kind of person who learns from it." She spent the next two weeks teaching me her actual methodology—not just the mechanics of building a model, but the mindset behind it. Ask questions first. Validate assumptions with the team. Understand what you don't know before you're in front of the partners.

More importantly, she assigned me to shadow her on the next three deals. For six months, I watched how she synthesized information, asked the right questions, and knew when to escalate uncertainty rather than pretend certainty.

That failed analysis cost the firm nothing materially, but it taught me more than any successful deal could have. I learned that intelligence matters less than intellectual humility. I learned that collaboration beats heroic effort. And I learned that growth happens after failure, not despite it.

Now, two years later, I lead diligence on deals in the 30-100 million range. Last year, I identified a critical margin pressure that saved us from a bad investment. That discovery came not from working harder, but from asking better questions and listening to people smarter than me.

An EMBA appeals to me precisely because I'm still in that learning mode—ready to be challenged, ready to be wrong, and ready to extract everything I can from the experience.

prompt: How do you plan to contribute to classroom discussions and student life?
content:
I've spent the last four years in PwC's Sydney office running operational transformation projects for mid-market Australian businesses. The work has been rewarding—I've led teams through complex change initiatives, managed stakeholder expectations, and delivered millions in cost savings. But lately, I've noticed something: I'm good at solving structured problems within defined parameters, and I'm much less comfortable in spaces where there's genuine ambiguity about what the right question even is.

This is why an MBA matters to me, and why I think I can add something real to Macquarie's classroom environment.

In group work, I naturally gravitate toward process. On my current engagement, I insisted on weekly structured debriefs where we mapped dependencies and flagged risks early. It prevented rework and kept us on timeline. But I also remember a moment when a junior team member suggested a completely different approach to our data analysis—something unconventional that didn't fit our original project structure. I initially dismissed it because it wasn't in our scope. Turns out, she was onto something valuable that we missed.

That gap between competence and openness is where I want to grow, and where I think I can contribute something to an MBA cohort. I don't come in with the startup energy or the "I'm going to change the world" narrative that fills a lot of MBA programs. I come in with specific technical skills—I can facilitate a structured discussion, I can push on flawed logic, I can help a team get unstuck on execution problems. But I'm also coming in humbled. I know what I don't know, which is how to think bigger about business strategy, how to evaluate markets and opportunities in ways that go beyond operational efficiency, and honestly, how to lead in environments without clear best practices.

I'm genuinely excited about learning from people who think differently than me. At PwC, most of my cohort came through similar pathways. An MBA cohort—especially at Macquarie, with its mix of Australian and international backgrounds—will push me to defend my assumptions. I want to be the person in class who asks follow-up questions when something doesn't add up, but also the person who's willing to sit with discomfort when someone challenges my thinking.

Outside formal discussions, I'm interested in getting involved with the Macquarie consulting club or case competition team. I'd likely focus on the logistics and execution side initially, but I'm also excited about the chance to work through cases with people from finance, tech, and other industries. I think that's where some of the real learning happens—not in the classroom discussion, but at 10 PM on a Thursday when your team realizes your initial hypothesis was wrong and you have to start over.

I'm not here to transform the classroom with brilliant insights. I'm here to learn, to have my perspectives challenged, and to help others think through hard problems more clearly. That's what I plan to bring.

prompt: Through your research, what actions have you taken to learn about our school? How will you take advantage of being part of our community?
content:
When I first started considering IIMK, I did what most applicants do—I visited the website, looked at rankings, and checked the placement statistics. But after my initial research, I realized I was approaching this the way I approach client work: surface-level analysis without understanding the actual story. So I went deeper.

I reached out to three alumni working in management consulting in Southeast Asia—one at Deloitte in Jakarta, another at McKinsey in Bangkok, and a third who recently moved to Singapore. What struck me most wasn't what they said about the academics (though they all praised the analytical rigor), but what they said about the culture. All three mentioned the collaborative environment and how people genuinely helped each other navigate recruiting and career decisions, even across batches. That felt different from the competitive atmosphere I'd heard about at some other top programs.

I also attended the virtual information session in December and the Q&A for international applicants last month. During the second session, I asked about the school's approach to building networks in Asia-Pacific, since that's where I plan to work. The response was honest—they acknowledged they're not as established as ISB or XLRI in some markets, but that the school is actively investing in alumni chapters and corporate partnerships across the region. That candor actually made me trust the program more.

I spent a few hours looking through the student blogs and LinkedIn profiles of recent IIMK graduates. I noticed several had moved into strategy and operations roles at companies I've worked with—Unilever, Infosys, and ICICI Bank. I also found that quite a few had made the consulting-to-corporate transition, which is exactly what I'm thinking about. One person's post about moving from Accenture to a Product Manager role at a fintech startup in Bangalore was particularly helpful because they detailed what skills the MBA actually helped them develop.

The more I learned, the more I understood what I'm actually looking for: a rigorous program where I won't be coasting, but also a place where people actually want to help each other succeed rather than just compete for grades. IIMK seems to offer that balance, especially the collaborative project-based learning I read about in the curriculum.

Here's where I'm honest about my profile: my GMAT score of 660 and GPA of 3.4 aren't what they could have been. I was working full-time during my engineering degree and didn't prioritize academics the way I should have. For the GMAT, I studied for about eight weeks while managing a demanding client engagement, and I didn't give myself the prep time I needed. These are my limitations, and I own them. But in my eight years at Accenture, I've built a different kind of track record—I've grown from analyst to senior manager, led a 12-person team, and consistently delivered projects for clients in the financial services and retail sectors across India, Indonesia, and Malaysia. I've learned how to think systematically, adapt quickly, and push myself in environments where it actually matters.

What I want from IIMK is the chance to consolidate what I've learned in consulting and position myself for a move into corporate strategy or product leadership. The school's focus on analytical thinking combined with practical business applications aligns with that goal. Beyond the curriculum, I'm excited about the chance to be part of a community that's building something in Asia—not just following a set playbook that's already proven successful. I think I can contribute to that, and I'm genuinely interested in being part of the next generation of IIMK alumni building businesses and leading teams across this region.

prompt: Kellogg's purpose is to educate, equip, and inspire brave leaders. Tell us about a time you have demonstrated leadership.
content:
Leadership means taking responsibility and inspiring others to follow your vision. Throughout my career in supply chain management, I have had many opportunities to demonstrate this quality.

At my previous company, a mid-sized logistics firm in Manila, I was working as a Senior Coordinator in the warehouse operations department. Our team was struggling with efficiency issues, and management was concerned about our performance metrics. I realized that something needed to change, so I took the initiative to step up and lead.

I organized a meeting with my team members and we discussed the problems we were facing. Together, we identified several areas where we could improve. I created a new system for organizing our inventory and scheduling our daily tasks. I also encouraged my team to communicate more openly and to share their ideas about how we could work better.

After implementing these changes, our team saw significant improvements. Our efficiency increased, and the number of errors decreased. Management noticed our progress and commended our work. This experience taught me that good leaders listen to their team and work together to find solutions.

Another example of my leadership was when I helped train new employees at the company. I understood that new workers often felt lost and confused during their first few weeks. I made it my responsibility to mentor them and help them integrate into the team. I spent extra time explaining processes and answering their questions. The new employees felt supported and were able to become productive team members more quickly.

These experiences have shown me that leadership is not just about being in charge. It is about caring for your team, being willing to work hard, and inspiring others through your actions. I believe that a good leader should be humble and always willing to learn from others.

I am excited about pursuing an MBA at Kellogg because I want to develop my leadership skills further. I want to learn frameworks and strategies that will help me become a more effective leader in the future. I believe that Kellogg's program will equip me with the tools I need to make a bigger impact in the business world.

prompt: Where do you see yourself five years after completing your MBA? How does our program fit into this vision?
content:
Five years from now, I see myself as a successful management consultant leading a team of talented professionals at a top consulting firm. I want to make a real impact in the business world and help companies achieve their strategic goals. An MBA from Amity Business School will give me the tools and knowledge I need to reach this vision.

Throughout my 12 years in consulting at a Big 4 firm, I have gained valuable experience in business strategy and operations. I have worked with many clients across different industries and learned how to solve complex problems. However, I realize that without an MBA, my career growth has limitations. I am currently at a point where I need deeper expertise in finance, strategy, and leadership to advance to a senior level.

Amity Business School's program is well-known for its strong curriculum in strategic management and business analytics. The core courses in finance and operations will help me build a stronger foundation in areas where I feel I need improvement. Additionally, your program's emphasis on case study method and real-world problem solving aligns perfectly with my consulting background. I believe this teaching method will allow me to apply academic concepts directly to my work.

The global network of Amity graduates is also very attractive to me. In consulting, relationships and networks are extremely important. Having access to alumni working in different countries and companies will be valuable for my career development. I am particularly interested in the opportunity to work on international consulting projects through the program, which will help me expand my cross-border business knowledge.

Beyond my current consulting role, my five-year vision includes potentially moving into strategy or operations roles at large corporations. An MBA will position me well for these transitions, as it will signal to employers my commitment to professional development and give me the credentials that many of these positions require. I am also considering starting my own consulting boutique firm in the future, and an MBA will give me the business fundamentals needed to successfully launch and manage a company.

As a Thai national, I am also interested in bringing international perspectives and insights to Amity's classroom. During my consulting work, I have gained experience in Southeast Asian markets, which I believe is valuable for my classmates. I am committed to contributing actively to classroom discussions and group projects.

In conclusion, Amity Business School is the right choice for me at this stage of my career. Your program offers the rigorous curriculum, experienced faculty, and valuable network that I need to achieve my goals. I am confident that after graduation, I will be well-prepared to take on leadership roles and make meaningful contributions to the consulting industry and beyond.

prompt: Describe an ethical dilemma you faced. How did you resolve it?
content:
Ethical Dilemma in My Legal Career

Throughout my twelve years working in the legal sector, I have encountered numerous situations that have challenged my professional ethics and personal values. One particular experience stands out as a turning point in my career and has shaped how I approach difficult decisions in the workplace.

During my time at a mid-sized law firm in Johannesburg, I was assigned to work on a significant corporate transaction. The case involved two companies, and our firm represented one of them. As I reviewed the documentation, I discovered what appeared to be a material fact that, if disclosed, would likely derail the entire deal. This information was not explicitly requested by the opposing party, and my senior partner suggested that we were under no obligation to volunteer it.

This situation presented a clear ethical dilemma. On one hand, my firm stood to earn substantial fees from this transaction. On the other hand, withholding relevant information felt wrong, even if it was legally permissible. I found myself torn between my professional responsibilities to my firm and my personal sense of integrity.

I spent considerable time thinking about this situation. I consulted with colleagues and read through our firm's ethics guidelines. I reflected on what kind of professional I wanted to be and what values I wanted to uphold throughout my career. Ultimately, I decided that I could not participate in a transaction where I was knowingly withholding material information, regardless of whether it was technically legal to do so.

I approached my senior partner and expressed my concerns. I explained that while the information might not have been explicitly requested, I believed that transparency was fundamental to ethical legal practice. I suggested that we could disclose the information and still move forward with the transaction, or we could withdraw from the matter. The conversation was difficult because I was junior at the time and challenging a senior partner is never easy.

To my surprise, my partner appreciated my perspective. After further consideration, he agreed that disclosure was the right course of action. We informed the opposing party about the material fact, and while it did complicate the transaction, the deal ultimately proceeded. Both parties felt confident moving forward because all relevant information had been shared.

This experience taught me several important lessons. First, it reinforced that ethical decisions are often not straightforward, and they require careful thought and consideration. Second, I learned that speaking up about ethical concerns, even when it is uncomfortable, is important for maintaining professional integrity. Third, I discovered that doing the right thing is often rewarded, even if it is not immediately profitable.

Since that experience, I have continued to prioritize ethics in my work. I have taken on more complex matters where ethical questions frequently arise, and I have tried to approach each situation with the same commitment to integrity that guided me through that early dilemma.

I believe that this experience demonstrates my character and my commitment to ethical principles. As I pursue my MBA at Bayes Business School, I hope to further develop my understanding of business ethics and how to navigate complex ethical challenges in a corporate environment. I am committed to becoming a leader who values integrity and who encourages ethical behavior in all professional interactions.
